content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γέροντος_1">. Sophocles marks the length of interval which he supposes between the <ref n="Soph. OT"> <title>O. T.</title> </ref>and the <ref n="Soph. OC"> <title>O. C.</title> </ref>by <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 395" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg007:395">v. 395,</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">γέροντα δ’ ὀρθοῦν φλαῦρον ὃς νέος πέσῃ</quote> </cit>. In the <ref n="Soph. OT"> <title>O. T.</title> </ref>Oedipus cannot be imagined as much above 40,—his two sons being then about 15 and 14, his two daughters about 13 and 12 respectively. It was "long" after his fall when Creon drove him into exile ( <ref n="Soph. OC 437"> 437</ref> , <ref n="Soph. OC 441">441</ref> ). It would satisfy the data of both plays to suppose that about 20 years in the life of Oedipus have elapsed between them. </gloss>

data.quote:
γέροντα δ’ ὀρθοῦν φλαῦρον ὃς νέος
									πέσῃ

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Ἀντιγόνη_1">. An anapaest can hold only the first place in a tragic trimeter, unless it is contained in a proper name, when it can hold any place except the sixth. Soph. has the name <foreign xml:lang="grc">Ἀντιγόνη</foreign> only four times in iambics. Here, in <ref n="Soph. OC 1415">1415</ref> , and in <ref n="Soph. Ant. 11"> <title>Ant.</title> 11</ref>the anapaest holds the fifth place; in <ref n="Soph. OC 507"> <title>O. C.</title> 507</ref>, the 4th. But Eur. prefers the anapaest of <foreign xml:lang="grc">Ἀντιγόνη</foreign> in the 4th place: see <ref n="Eur. Phoen. 88"> <title>Ph.</title> 88</ref>, <ref n="Eur. Phoen. 757">757</ref> , <ref n="Eur. Phoen. 1264">1264</ref> , <ref n="Eur. Phoen. 1323">1323</ref> , <ref n="Eur. Phoen. 1465">1465</ref> , <ref n="Eur. Phoen. 1636"> 1636</ref> (4th place) as against <ref n="Eur. Phoen. 58">58</ref> , <ref n="Eur. Phoen. 1476">1476</ref> , <ref n="Eur. Phoen. 1588">1588</ref> (5th). The anapaest must be wholly in the proper name: hence <cit> <bibl n="Eur. IA 1570" ref="Eur. IA 1570"> Eur. <title>I. A.</title> 1570</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔλεξε δ’, ὦ θηροκτόν’ Ἄρτεμι παῖ Διός</quote> </cit>was amended by Porson, <cit> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔλεξε δ’, ὦ θηροκτόν’ Ἄρτεμις Διός</quote> <bibl n="Eur. IA 1570" ref="Eur. IA 1570">Eur. IA 1570</bibl> </cit> . </gloss>

data.quote:
ἔλεξε δ’, ὦ θηροκτόν’ Ἄρτεμι παῖ
									Διός

ἔλεξε δ’, ὦ θηροκτόν’ Ἄρτεμις
									Διός

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="χώρους_2">, like <foreign xml:lang="lat">loca</foreign> , vaguely, "region" (so <ref n="Soph. OT 798"> <title>O.T.</title> 798</ref>): but sing. <foreign xml:lang="grc"> χῶρος</foreign> below ( <ref n="Soph. OC 16">16</ref> , <ref n="Soph. OC 37">37</ref> , <ref n="Soph. OC 54">54</ref> ), of a definite spot. Oed. already knows that they are near <emph>Athens</emph> ( <ref n="Soph. OC 25">25</ref> ), but it is time that the day's journey was ended ( <ref n="Soph. OC 20">20</ref> ); will this rural region—or town—supply their needs if they halt? The exordium has something of a Homeric tone,—due not merely to the form of the question (like that of Odysseus on awakening in Phaeacia, <ref n="Hom. Od. 6.119"> <title>Od.</title> 6.119</ref>, and in Ithaca, <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Od. 13.200" ref="Hom. Od. 13.200"> <title>Od.</title> 13.200</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τέων αὖτε βροτῶν εἰς γαῖαν ἱκάνω;</quote> </cit>), but also to the epic phrase <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἀνδρῶν πόλιν</foreign> ( <ref n="Hom. Il. 17.737"> <title>Il.</title> 17.737</ref>etc.). </gloss>

data.quote:
τέων αὖτε βροτῶν εἰς γαῖαν
									ἱκάνω;

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πλανήτην_3">: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Heraclid. 878" ref="Eur. Heraclid. 878"> Eur. <title>Heracl.</title> 878</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ξένοι πλανήτην εἴχετ’ ἄθλιον βίον</quote> </cit>. The word is not in itself opprobrious: in <ref n="Soph. OC 123">123</ref> it is merely opp. to <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἔγχωρος</foreign> : cp. <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Rep. 371D" ref="Plat. Rep. 371D"> Plat. <title>Rep.</title> 371D</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">καλοῦμεν...τοὺς...πλανήτας ἐπὶ τὰς πόλεις, ἐμπόρους</quote> </cit>. In <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 1029" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg004:1029"> <title>O. T.</title> 1029</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">πλάνης</quote> </cit>, said by Oed. to the Corinthian, takes its colour from the added <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἐπὶ θητείᾳ</foreign> , "a vagrant hireling." </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σπανιστοῖς_4">, made scanty, given scantily: so Philostratus ( <date when="0235">circ. 235 A.D.</date> ) p. 611 <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἄρωμα...σπανιστόν</foreign> , "rare." This implies <foreign xml:lang="grc">σπανίζω τι</foreign> as="to make a thing scanty" or rare, which occurs in Greek of the <date from="-0200" to="-0100">2nd cent. B.C.</date> ( <ref> Philo Byzant. <title>De septem mirabil.</title> 4</ref>): cp. <cit> <bibl n="Shak. LR 1.1" ref="Shak. LR 1.1"> Shaksp. <title>Lear</title> 1. 1. 281</bibl> <quote xml:lang="eng">"you have obedience scanted."</quote> </cit>For a different use see <ref n="Strab. 15.3.1">Strabo 15. 727</ref> (a land) <foreign xml:lang="grc">σπανιστὴ καρποῖς</foreign> , "poor in...," implying <foreign xml:lang="grc">σπανίζω τινά</foreign> as="to make one needy," whence the perf. pass. <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἐσπανίσμεθ’ ἀρωγῶν</foreign> ( <ref n="Aesch. Pers. 1024"> Aesch. <title>Pers.</title> 1024</ref>): and here again cp. <cit> <bibl n="Shak. MV 2.1" ref="Shak. MV 2.1"> Shaksp. <title>Merch.</title> 2. 1. 17</bibl> <quote xml:lang="eng">"if my father had not scanted me."</quote> </cit> </gloss>

data.quote:
"if my father had not scanted
									me."

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δέξεται_4">: <cit> <bibl n="Xen. Anab. 5.5.24" ref="Xen. Anab. 5.5.24"> Xen. <title>Anab.</title> 5.5.24</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ξενίοις... δέχεσθαι</quote> </cit> : <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Laws 919A" ref="Plat. Laws 919A"> Plat. <title>Legg.</title> 919A</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">καταλύσεσιν ἀγαπηταῖς δεχόμενος.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δωρήμασιν_4">, food, and shelter for the night: <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Od. 14.404" ref="Hom. Od. 14.404"> <title>Od.</title> 14.404</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐς κλισίην ἄγαγον καὶ ξείνια δῶκα</quote> </cit>(whereas <foreign xml:lang="grc">δῶρα</foreign> , or <foreign xml:lang="grc">ξεινήϊα δῶρα</foreign> , in Hom. usu.=special presents, as of plate or the like, <ref n="Hom. Od. 24.273"> <title>Od.</title> 24.273</ref>).</gloss>

data.quote:
ἐς κλισίην ἄγαγον καὶ ξείνια
									δῶκα

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐξαιτοῦντα_5">, "asking <emph>earnestly.</emph> " This compound has a like force in <ref n="Soph. OT 1255"> <title>O. T.</title> 1255</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Trach. 10"> <title>Trach.</title> 10</ref>; and so the midd. below, <ref n="Soph. OC 586">586</ref> , <ref n="Soph. OC 1327">1327</ref> . Cp. <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἐξεφίεται</foreign> , <emph>straitly</emph> enjoins, <ref n="Soph. Aj. 795"> <title>Ai.</title> 795</ref>. In prose, the special sense of <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἐξαιτεῖν</foreign> was "to demand the <emph>surrender</emph> of" a person, answering to <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἐκδιδόναι</foreign> : <cit> <bibl n="Antiph. 6.27" ref="Antiph. 6.27">Antiph. or. 6 § 27</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ...θεράποντας ἐξαιτοῦσι μὴ ἤθελον ἐκδιδόναι.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σμικροῦ_5">is better than <foreign xml:lang="grc">μικροῦ</foreign> , since the rhetorical <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἐπαναφορά</foreign> (cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 610">610</ref> , <ref n="Soph. OT 25"> <title>O.T.</title> 25</ref>) needs the same form in both places. <foreign xml:lang="grc">μικρός</foreign> having prevailed in later Attic (as in Xen. and the orators), our MSS. in the tragic texts often drop the <foreign xml:lang="grc">ς</foreign> . But, metre permitting, tragedy preferred <foreign xml:lang="grc">σμικρός</foreign> . In <cit> <bibl>Soph. fr. 38</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ μικρὸς ὢν τὰ φαῦλα νικήσας ἔχω</quote> </cit>, the word="of short stature," in which sense <ref n="Hom. Il. 5.801"> <title>Il.</title> 5.801</ref>too has <quote xml:lang="grc">Τυδεύς τοι μικρὸς μὲν ἔην δέμας</quote> , though in <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Il. 17.757" ref="Hom. Il. 17.757">17. 757</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">σμικρῇσι</quote> </cit> . <ref> Curtius ( <title>Etym.</title> p. 622)</ref>, comparing <foreign xml:lang="grc">σμυκτήρ</foreign> and <foreign xml:lang="grc">μυκτήρ</foreign> , remarks that analogy speaks for the antiquity of the <foreign xml:lang="grc">ς</foreign> in <foreign xml:lang="grc">σμικρός</foreign> , while it is possible that the <foreign xml:lang="grc">μ</foreign> was not original, but arose from some other sound. </gloss>

data.quote:
εἰ μικρὸς ὢν τὰ φαῦλα νικήσας
									ἔχω

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φέροντα_6">= <foreign xml:lang="grc">φερόμενον</foreign> : <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 590" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg004:590"> <title>O. T.</title> 590</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">πάντ’ ἄνευ φόβου φέρω</quote> </cit>: cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 1411">1411</ref> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καὶ_τόδ_6">. As <foreign xml:lang="grc">καὶ οὖτος</foreign> (like <foreign xml:lang="lat">et is</foreign> , <foreign xml:lang="lat">isque</foreign> ), or <foreign xml:lang="grc">καὶ ταῦτα</foreign> , introduces a strengthening circumstance ( <cit> <bibl n="Hdt. 6.11" ref="Hdt. 6.11">Her. 6. 11</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἶναι δούλοισι, καὶ τούτοισι ὡς δρηπέτῃσι</quote> </cit>), so here <foreign xml:lang="grc">καὶ τόδε</foreign> marks the last step of a climax. Some edd. point thus, <foreign xml:lang="grc">φέροντα·</foreign> taking <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἐξαρκοῦν</foreign> as= <foreign xml:lang="grc"> ἐξαρκεῖ</foreign> , "and that suffices me": but this ( <emph>a</emph> ) supposes a very harsh ellipse of <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἐστί</foreign> , ( <emph>b</emph> ) maims the rhythm, ( <emph>c</emph> ) weakens the force of the series <foreign xml:lang="grc">σμικρὸν—μεῖον—ἐξαρκοῦν</foreign> . </gloss>

data.quote:
εἶναι δούλοισι, καὶ τούτοισι ὡς
									δρηπέτῃσι

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐμοί_6"> after <foreign xml:lang="grc">Οἰδίπουν</foreign> : cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 1329">1329</ref> : as <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 535" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg004:535"> <title>O.T.</title> 535</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τῆς ἐμῆς</quote> </cit> after <foreign xml:lang="grc">τοῦδε τἀνδρός</foreign> (like <ref n="Soph. Trach. 1073"> <title>Tr.</title> 1073 f.</ref>): <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 865" ref="Soph. Aj. 865"> <title>Ai.</title> 865</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">μυθήσομαι</quote> </cit> after <foreign xml:lang="grc">Αἴας θροεῖ</foreign> : <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Euthyph. 5A" ref="Plat. Euthyph. 5A"> Plat. <title>Euthyphro</title> 5 A</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδέ τῳ ἂν διαφέροι Εὐθύφρων τῶν πολλῶν...εἰ μὴ εἰδείην</quote> </cit> . </gloss>

data.quote:
οὐδέ τῳ ἂν διαφέροι Εὐθύφρων τῶν
									πολλῶν...εἰ μὴ εἰδείην

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="στέργειν_7">, absol., cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 519">519</ref> , <cit> <bibl n="Dem. 18.112" ref="Dem. 18.112"> Dem. <title>De Cor.</title> § 112</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ δέ φησιν οὖτος, δειξάτω, κἀγὼ στέρξω καὶ σιωπήσομαι</quote> </cit>: usu. with accus., as <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Phil. 538" ref="Soph. Phil. 538"> <title>Ph.</title> 538</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνάγκῃ προὔμαθον στέργειν κακά</quote> </cit>. Like <foreign xml:lang="grc">στέργειν</foreign> , <foreign xml:lang="grc">αἰνεῖν</foreign> is sometimes absol. in this sense ( <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Supp. 388" ref="Eur. Supp. 388"> Eur. <title>Suppl.</title> 388</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">κἂν μὲν θέλωσιν αἰνέσαι</quote> </cit>), but <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἀγαπᾶν</foreign> almost always takes a clause with <foreign xml:lang="grc">ὅτι, εἰ</foreign> or <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἐάν</foreign> ( <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Od. 21.289" ref="Hom. Od. 21.289"> <title>Od.</title> 21.289</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="hexameter"> <l part="I">οὐκ ἀγαπᾷς ὃ ἕκηλος ...</l> <l>δαίνυσαι</l> </lg> </quote> </cit>), or an accus. </gloss>

data.quote:
εἰ δέ φησιν οὖτος, δειξάτω, κἀγὼ
									στέρξω καὶ σιωπήσομαι

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αἱ_πάθαι_7"> : <cit> <bibl n="Hdt. 1.207" ref="Hdt. 1.207">Her. 1 207</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰ δέ μοι παθήματα ἐόντα ἀχάριτα μαθήματα γέγονε</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. Ag. 177" ref="Aesch. Ag. 177"> Aesch. <title>Ag.</title> 177</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="lyric"> <l>τὸν πάθει μάθος</l> <l>θέντα κυρίως ἔχειν.</l> </lg> </quote> </cit> </gloss>

data.quote:
τὰ δέ μοι παθήματα ἐόντα ἀχάριτα
									μαθήματα γέγονε

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὁ_χρόνος_7">, the time (through which I live), attending on me ( <foreign xml:lang="grc">ξυνών</foreign> ) in long course ( <foreign xml:lang="grc">μακρός</foreign> ). Cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 963"> <title>O. T.</title> 963</ref>(Polybus died of disease) <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ τῷ μακρῷ γε συμμετρούμενος χρόνῳ</quote> , <quote xml:lang="eng">"and of the long years which he had told."</quote> For <foreign xml:lang="grc">ξυνών</foreign> cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 863" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg004:863"> <title>O. T.</title> 863</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἴ μοι ξυνείη...μοῖρα</quote> </cit> : <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 622" ref="Soph. Aj. 622"> <title>Ai.</title> 622</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">παλαιᾷ... ἔντροφος ἁμέρᾳ</quote> </cit> : <cit> <bibl n="Pind. P. 4" ref="Pind. P. 4"> Pind. <title>Pyth.</title> 4.157</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="lyric"> <l>ἤδη με γηραιὸν μέρος ἁλικίας</l> <l>ἀμφιπολεῖ</l> </lg> </quote> </cit> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="διδάσκει_8">, verb agreeing with nearest subject: cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 830"> <title>Ant.</title> 830</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1133">1133</ref> : <cit> <bibl n="Ps. Xen. Const. Ath. 1.2" ref="Ps. Xen. Const. Ath. 1.2"> [Xen.] <title>Resp. Athen.</title> ( <date when="-0420"> <foreign xml:lang="lat">circ.</foreign> 420 B.C.</date>) 1 § 2</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">δικαίως αὐτόθι καὶ οἱ πένητες καὶ ὁ δῆμος πλέον ἔχει</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Sym. 190C" ref="Plat. Sym. 190C"> Plat. <title>Symp.</title> 190C</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">αἱ τιμαὶ γὰρ αὐτοῖς καὶ ἱερὰ τὰ παρὰ τῶν ἀνθρώπων ἠφανίζετο</quote> </cit> : <cit> <bibl n="Cic. Att. 9.10.2" ref="Cic. Att. 9.10.2"> Cic. <title>Ad Att.</title> 9. 10, 2</bibl> <quote xml:lang="lat">"nihil libri, nihil litterae, nihil doctrina prodest."</quote> </cit> </gloss>

data.quote:
δικαίως αὐτόθι καὶ οἱ πένητες καὶ ὁ
									δῆμος πλέον ἔχει

αἱ τιμαὶ γὰρ αὐτοῖς καὶ ἱερὰ τὰ παρὰ
									τῶν ἀνθρώπων ἠφανίζετο

"nihil libri, nihil litterae, nihil
									doctrina prodest."

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τρίτον_8">, as completing the lucky number: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 1174" ref="Soph. Aj. 1174"> <title>Ai.</title> 1174</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">κόμας ἐμὰς καὶ τῆσδε καὶ σαυτοῦ τρίτου</quote> </cit>: <ref n="Soph. OT 581"> <title>O. T.</title> 581</ref>(where see n.). </gloss>

data.quote:
κόμας ἐμὰς καὶ τῆσδε καὶ σαυτοῦ
									τρίτου

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θάκησιν_9">is in itself a correct form. <foreign xml:lang="grc">θάκησις</foreign> ( <foreign xml:lang="grc">θακέω</foreign> ) is (1) the act of sitting, (2) the means of sitting, as <foreign xml:lang="grc">οἴκησις</foreign> ( <foreign xml:lang="grc">οἰκέω</foreign> ) is (1) the act of dwelling, (2) the house. It is not found elsewhere, but cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Phil. 18" ref="Soph. Phil. 18"> Soph. <title>Ph.</title> 18</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="trimeter"> <l>ἡλίου διπλῆ</l> <l>πάρεστιν ἐνθάκησις</l> </lg> </quote> </cit>, a twofold means of sitting in the sun. With the MS. reading <foreign xml:lang="grc">θάκοισιν</foreign> construe: — <foreign xml:lang="grc">στῆσόν με ἢ πρὸς θάκοις βεβήλοις, εἴ τινα (θᾶκον) βλέπεις</foreign> , etc. (We could not render <foreign xml:lang="grc">εἴ τινα βλέπεις</foreign> "if thou seest any <emph> man</emph> ," since the need for a halt did not depend on that condition.) This is a construction much less clear and simple than that with <foreign xml:lang="grc">θάκησιν</foreign> . <foreign xml:lang="grc"> βεβήλοις</foreign> may have induced the change of <foreign xml:lang="grc">θάκησιν</foreign> into <foreign xml:lang="grc"> θάκοισιν</foreign> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="βεβήλοις_10">, neut. plur. (cp. <cit> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀβάτων ἀποβάς</quote> <bibl n="Soph. OC 167" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg007:167">, 167</bibl> </cit>), places which may be trodden, <foreign xml:lang="lat">profana</foreign> , opp. to <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἱερά, ἄθικτα</foreign> : cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. fr. 86.6" ref="Soph. fr. 86.6">fr. 86. 6</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="trimeter"> <l>δεινὸς γὰρ ἕρπειν πλοῦτος ἔς τε τἄβατα</l> <l>καὶ πρὸς βέβηλα</l> </lg> </quote> </cit>(Vater's correction of <foreign xml:lang="grc">καὶ πρὸς τὰ βατά</foreign> ): <cit> <bibl> Bekker <title>Anecd.</title> 325. 13</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀβέβηλα τὰ ἄβατα χωρία καὶ ἱερὰ καὶ μὴ τοῖς τυχοῦσι βάσιμα, μόνοις δὲ τοῖς θεραπεύουσι τοὺς θεούς. βέβηλα δὲ ἐλέγετο τὰ μὴ ὅσια μηδὲ ἱερά· οὕτω Σοφοκλῆς</quote> </cit>. (This ignores the classical use of <foreign xml:lang="grc">ὅσιος</foreign> as opp. to <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἱερός</foreign> : in <cit> <bibl n="Aristoph. Lys. 743" ref="Aristoph. Lys. 743"> Aristoph. <title>Lys.</title> 743</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅσιον χωρίον</quote> </cit> = <foreign xml:lang="grc">βέβηλον</foreign> .) In <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Her. 404" ref="Eur. Her. 404"> Eur. <title>Her.</title> 404</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="trimeter"> <l part="F">καὶ βέβηλα καὶ κεκρυμμένα</l> <l part="I">λόγια</l> </lg> </quote> </cit>= oracles to which <emph>access</emph> was easy, as opp. to those hidden in temple-archives. </gloss>

data.quote:
ἀβέβηλα τὰ ἄβατα χωρία καὶ ἱερὰ καὶ μὴ
									τοῖς τυχοῦσι βάσιμα, μόνοις δὲ τοῖς θεραπεύουσι τοὺς
									θεούς. βέβηλα δὲ ἐλέγετο τὰ μὴ ὅσια μηδὲ ἱερά· οὕτω
									Σοφοκλῆς

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἢ_ἄλσεσιν_10">does not necessarily imply entrance on the <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἄλση</foreign> . But the contrast with <foreign xml:lang="grc">πρὸς βεβήλοις</foreign> is unmeaning unless Oed. thinks of a seat <emph>on</emph> sacred ground, and not merely <emph>near</emph> it. So Antigone, who recognises the grove as sacred <ref n="Soph. OC 16">(16)</ref> , seats him within it <ref n="Soph. OC 19">(19)</ref> . This grove at Colonus was <cit> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀστιβές</quote> <bibl n="Soph. OC 126" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg007:126">(126)</bibl> </cit>because the cult of the Eumenides so prescribed. Sacred groves were often open to visitors, as was the <foreign xml:lang="grc">κυκλοτερὲς ἄλσος</foreign> of the Nymphs, with an altar "whereon all wayfarers were wont to make offerings," <cit> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅθι πάντες ἐπιρρέζεσκον ὁδῖται</quote> <bibl n="Hom. Od. 17.208" ref="Hom. Od. 17.208"> ( <title>Od.</title> 17.208)</bibl> </cit>. Hence Pausanias sometimes mentions that a particular <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἄλσος</foreign> was <emph>not</emph> open to the public. At Megalopolis, in the precinct of Zeus Philios, there was an <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἄλσος</foreign> of which he says, <cit> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐς μὲν δὴ τὸ ἐντὸς ἔσοδος οὐκ ἔστιν ἀνθρώποις</quote> <bibl n="Paus. 8.31.5" ref="Paus. 8.31.5">(8. 31. 5)</bibl> </cit>. At Pellene, again, there was a walled <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἄλσος</foreign> of Artemis Soteira; <cit> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔσοδός τε πλὴν τοῖς ἱερεῦσιν ἄλλῳ γε οὐδενὶ ἔστιν ἀνθρώπων</quote> <bibl n="Paus. 7.27.3" ref="Paus. 7.27.3">(7. 27. 3).</bibl> </cit> </gloss>

data.quote:
ἐς μὲν δὴ τὸ ἐντὸς ἔσοδος οὐκ ἔστιν
									ἀνθρώποις

ἔσοδός τε πλὴν τοῖς ἱερεῦσιν ἄλλῳ γε
									οὐδενὶ ἔστιν ἀνθρώπων

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐξίδρυσον_11">, place me in a seat; cp. <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἐκ</foreign> in <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἐξορθόω</foreign> (to render <foreign xml:lang="grc"> ὀρθόν</foreign> ). <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἐξίδρυσον</foreign> , without addition, could hardly mean, <foreign xml:lang="eng"> seat me <emph> apart</emph> ,</foreign> <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>out of the path. In <ref>Eur. fr. 877</ref> (the only other example of <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἐξιδρύω</foreign> ) it is the context which fixes this sense, <foreign xml:lang="grc">τηλοῦ γὰρ οἴκων βίοτον ἐξιδρυσάμην</foreign> , <quote xml:lang="eng">"I fixed the seat of my life far apart from men's homes."</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πυθώμεθα_11"> . <foreign xml:lang="grc">πυθοίμεθα</foreign> is impossible here. After a primary tense, the optative in a final clause with <foreign xml:lang="grc">ὡς, ὅπως</foreign> , etc., occurs only:—(1) in Homeric Greek, where the case is merely imaginary: <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Od. 17.250" ref="Hom. Od. 17.250"> <title>Od.</title> 17.250</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="hexameter"> <l>τόν ποτ’ ἐγὼν …</l> <l>ἄξω τῆλ’ Ἰθάκης, ἵνα μοι βίοτον πολὺν ἄλφοι</l> </lg> </quote> </cit> : <quote xml:lang="eng"> "him <emph>some day</emph> I will take far from Ithaca,—so that (if I should do so) he might bring me large gain,"</quote>—implying, <foreign xml:lang="grc">εἰ ἄγοιμι, ἄλφοι ἄν</foreign> . (2) After words expressing an <emph>aspiration</emph> or <emph>prayer</emph> (and not, like <foreign xml:lang="grc">στῆσον</foreign> here, a simple order): <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. Eum. 297" ref="Aesch. Eum. 297"> Aesch. <title>Eum.</title> 297</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="trimeter"> <l>ἔλθοι, κλύει δὲ καὶ πρόσωθεν ὢν θεός,</l> <l>ὅπως γένοιτο … λυτήριος</l> </lg> </quote> </cit> : <quote xml:lang="eng">"may she come—and a god hears e'en afar— that [so] she might prove my deliverer."</quote> <ref n="Aesch. Supp. 670"> Aesch. <title>Suppl.</title> 670 ff.</ref>, by which Campb. defends <foreign xml:lang="grc">πυθοίμεθα</foreign> , would come under (2), if the text were certain, but there <foreign xml:lang="grc">τώς</foreign> is a <foreign xml:lang="lat">v.l.</foreign> for <foreign xml:lang="grc">ὡς</foreign> . (3) More rarely, where the primary tense implies a secondary: <cit> <bibl n="Dem. 22.11" ref="Dem. 22.11"> Dem. <title>In Androt.</title> § 11</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῦτον ἔχει τὸν τρόπον ὁ νόμος...ἵνα μηδὲ πεισθῆναι μηδ’ ἐξαπατηθῆναι γένοιτ’ ἐπὶ τῷ δήμῳ</quote> </cit>: <quote xml:lang="eng"> "the law <emph>stands</emph> thus [= <emph>was made</emph> thus], that the people <emph>might</emph> not even have the power"</quote> etc.: <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἔχει</foreign> implies <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἐτέθη</foreign> . </gloss>

data.quote:
τοῦτον ἔχει τὸν τρόπον ὁ νόμος...ἵνα
									μηδὲ πεισθῆναι μηδ’ ἐξαπατηθῆναι γένοιτ’ ἐπὶ τῷ
									δήμῳ

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μανθάνειν_ἥκομεν_12">, we have come to learning, = are in such plight that we must learn: the infin. as after verbs of duty or fitness ( <foreign xml:lang="grc">ὀφείλω, προσήκει</foreign> , etc.). Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 1158" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg004:1158"> <title>O. T.</title> 1158</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰς τόδ’ ἥξεις</quote> </cit> ( <foreign xml:lang="lat">sc.</foreign> <foreign xml:lang="grc">εἰς τὸ ὀλέσθαι</foreign> ). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ξένοι_ἀστῶν_13">: cp. the address of Oedipus the King to the Theban elders ( <ref n="Soph. OT 216"> <title>O. T.</title> 216ff.</ref>), esp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 222" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg004:222">vv. 222 f.,</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="trimeter"> <l>νῦν δ’, ὕστερος γὰρ ἀστὸς εἰς ἀστοὺς τελῶ,</l> <l part="I">ὑμῖν προφωνῶ κ.τ.λ.</l> </lg> </quote> </cit> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Οἰδίπους_14">, the more frequent voc. (cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 405"> <title>O. T.</title> 405</ref>crit. n.): but <quote xml:lang="grc"> Οἰδίπου</quote> below, <ref n="Soph. OC 557">557</ref> , <ref n="Soph. OC 1346">1346</ref> . Athens is a little more than a mile S.E. of Colonus. The picture which Sophocles meant <foreign xml:lang="grc">πύργοι</foreign> to suggest probably included both the Acropolis—a beautiful feature in the view—and the line of city-walls with their towers. So the city-walls of Thebes are <cit> <quote xml:lang="grc">πύργοι,</quote> <bibl n="Soph. Ant. 122" ref="Soph. Ant. 122"> <title>Ant.</title> 122</bibl> </cit> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οἳ_14">at the end of the verse: cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 298"> <title>O. T.</title> 298</ref>, <ref n="Soph. El. 873"> <title>El.</title> 873</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Trach. 819"> <title>Tr.</title> 819</ref>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="στέγουσιν_15">, the reading of all MSS., is probably right. It is true that in class. Greek <foreign xml:lang="grc">στέγω</foreign> usually means either (1) "cover, conceal," as <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 1118" ref="Soph. El. 1118"> <title>El.</title> 1118</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄγγος... σῶμα...στέγον</quote> </cit>, or (2) "keep out," as <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. Seven 216" ref="Aesch. Seven 216"> Aesch. <title>Theb.</title> 216</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">πύργον στέγειν εὔχεσθε πολέμιον δόρυ</quote> </cit>. But the first sense — "cover" — might easily pass into "protect," and <ref n="Xen. Cyrop. 7.1.33"> Xen. <title>Cyr.</title> 7.1.33</ref>has <quote xml:lang="grc">αἱ ἀσπίδες ...στεγάζουσι τὰ σώματα</quote> . Wakefield's <foreign xml:lang="grc">στέφουσιν</foreign> ("girdle") is specious; we have <foreign xml:lang="grc">στεφάνωμα</foreign> or <foreign xml:lang="grc">στεφάνη πύργων</foreign> ( <ref n="Soph. Ant. 122"> <title>Ant.</title> 122</ref>, <ref n="Eur. Hec. 910"> Eur. <title> Hec.</title> 910</ref>), <cit> <quote xml:lang="grc">Βαβυλῶνα...τείχεσιν ἐστεφάνωσε</quote> <bibl>(Dionys. Periegetes 1006)</bibl> </cit> , <cit> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅπλοισιν Μεγάλη πόλις ἐστεφάνωται</quote> <bibl n="Paus. 9.15" ref="Paus. 9.15">(Paus. 9. 15)</bibl> </cit>. But it does not follow that <foreign xml:lang="grc">πύργοι πόλιν στέφουσιν</foreign> could stand. <foreign xml:lang="grc">στέφω</foreign> never occurs as = "to be set around," but either as (1) "to set around" — <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἄνθη περὶ κεφαλὴν στέφεις</foreign> , or (2) "to crown" — <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἄνθεσι κεφαλὴν στέφεις</foreign> ,—sometimes in the fig. sense of "honouring," as with libations or offerings ( <ref n="Soph. Ant. 431"> <title>Ant.</title> 431</ref>etc.). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὡς_ὀμμάτων_15"> , <foreign xml:lang="lat">sc.</foreign> <foreign xml:lang="grc"> εἰκάσαι</foreign> , to judge from sight (alone), without exact knowledge: <cit> <bibl n="tlg5037.tlg006" ref="tlg5037.tlg006">schol.</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς ἔστιν ἐκ προόψεως τεκμήρασθαι</quote> </cit>: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Thuc. 1.10" ref="Thuc. 1.10">Thuc. 1. 10</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰκάζεσθαι ἀπὸ τῆς φανερᾶς ὄψεως</quote> </cit>, to be estimated by the mere external aspect. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="χῶρος_ἱρός_16">. Cp. <ref n="Plat. Phaedrus 230B"> Plat. <title>Phaedr.</title> 230B</ref>, where Socrates recognises the sacred character of the spot by the Ilissus: <quote xml:lang="grc">Νυμφῶν τέ τινων καὶ Ἀχελῴου ἱερὸν ἀπὸ τῶν κορῶν τε καὶ ἀγαλμάτων</quote> (the votive dolls and images) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔοικεν εἶναι</quote> . There, too, <foreign xml:lang="grc">τὸ σύσκιον</foreign> was a feature. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὡς_εἰκάσαι_16">, A's reading, is preferable to <foreign xml:lang="grc">ὡς ἀπεικάσαι</foreign> , which would imply a more diffident guess. The poet of Colonus intends that the sacred character of the grove should at once impress the Theban maiden; and <foreign xml:lang="grc">σάφα</foreign> is confirmed by the emphasis of <foreign xml:lang="grc">δάφνης, ἐλαίας, ἀμπέλου</foreign> . It has been objected that <foreign xml:lang="grc">σάφα</foreign> is inconsistent with <foreign xml:lang="grc">εἰκάσαι</foreign> . But it merely expresses the speaker's own belief that her guess is right; as we can say, "a certain conjecture." In L's reading, <foreign xml:lang="grc">ὡς ἀφεικάσαι</foreign> , it seems more likely that a second <foreign xml:lang="grc">ς</foreign> should have been lost than that <foreign xml:lang="grc">π</foreign> should have become <foreign xml:lang="grc">φ</foreign> . For the constr. with <foreign xml:lang="grc">ὡς</foreign> , cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Trach. 1220" ref="Soph. Trach. 1220"> <title>Tr.</title> 1220</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὥς γ’ ἐπεικάζειν ἐμέ.</quote> </cit> <foreign xml:lang="grc">ὡς</foreign>is omitted below, <ref n="Soph. OC 152"> 152</ref> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="βρύων_16">takes a dat. in its literal sense of "sprouting" ( <cit> <quote xml:lang="grc">βρύει ἄνθεϊ</quote> <bibl n="Hom. Il. 17.56" ref="Hom. Il. 17.56"> <title>Il.</title> 17.56</bibl> </cit>), but either a dat. (as <ref n="Aristoph. Cl. 45"> Aristoph. <title>Nub.</title> 45</ref>) or a gen. in its figurative sense of "being full." <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Ax. 371c" ref="Plat. Ax. 371c"> [Plat.] <title>Axiochus</title> 371 C</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄφθονοι μὲν ὧραι παγκάρπου γονῆς βρύουσι</quote> </cit>(evidently pieced together from some poet). </gloss>

data.quote:
ἄφθονοι μὲν ὧραι παγκάρπου γονῆς
									βρύουσι

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀμπέλου_17">. <ref> Cyril ( <title>Jerem. Homil.</title> 4. 41)</ref>, speaking of the later pagan practice, says, <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰς ἄλση ὅταν φυτεύωσι ξύλα, φυτεύουσιν οὐ τὰ καρποφόρα, οὐ συκῆν οὐδ’ ἄμπελον, ἀλλὰ μόνον τέρψεως χάριν ἄκαρπα ξύλα</quote> . But in earlier times, at least, <foreign xml:lang="grc">τὰ καρποφόρα</foreign> were not rare in sacred groves; cp. <ref n="Xen. Anab. 5.3.12"> Xen. <title>Anab.</title> 5.3.12</ref>(referring to the shrine of the Ephesian Artemis at Scillus) <quote xml:lang="grc">περὶ δ’ αὐτὸν τὸν ναὸν ἄλσος ἡμέρων δένδρων ἐφυτεύθη, ὅσα ἐστὶ τρωκτὰ ὡραῖα</quote> . <ref n="Paus. 1.21.7">Paus. 1.21.7</ref> (in an <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἄλσος</foreign> of Apollo at Athens) <quote xml:lang="grc">δένδρων καὶ ἡμέρων καὶ ὅσα τῶν ἀκάρπων ὀσμῆς παρέχεταί τινα ἢ θέας ἡδονήν</quote> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πυκνόπτεροι_17">, poet. for <foreign xml:lang="grc">πυκναί</foreign> , the second element being equivalent to a separate epithet, <foreign xml:lang="grc"> πτεροῦσσαι</foreign> : cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 717" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg007:717">717</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἑκατομπόδων Νηρῃδων</quote> </cit> , <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 1055" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg007:1055">1055</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">διστόλους</quote> </cit> , <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 846" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg004:846"> <title>O. T.</title> 846</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οἰόζωνος ἀνήρ</quote> </cit>, a lonely way farer (where see n.). Such an epithet as "thickly-feathered" would be unmeaning here. The <emph>many</emph> nightingales, heard to warble from the thick covert, argue the undisturbed sanctity of the inner grove. Antigone notices an indication which her blind father can recognise. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δ_17">is elided at the end of the verse, as <ref n="Soph. OT 29"> <title>O.T.</title> 29 (n.)</ref>, so also <foreign xml:lang="grc"> τ’</foreign> , as <ref n="Soph. OT 1184"> <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 1184</ref>etc., and once <foreign xml:lang="grc">ταῦτα</foreign> , <ref n="Soph. OT 332"> <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 332</ref>: cp. <ref target="cl1164">below, 1164</ref> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὡς_γέροντι_20"> with <foreign xml:lang="grc">μακρὰν</foreign> : cp. <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Soph. 226C" ref="Plat. Soph. 226C"> Plat. <title>Soph.</title> 226C</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ταχεῖαν, ὡς ἐμοί, σκέψιν ἐπιτάττεις</quote> </cit>( <quote xml:lang="eng">"a rapid process of thought for such as I am"</quote> ): <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Rep. 389D" ref="Plat. Rep. 389D"> <title>Rep.</title> 389 D</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">σωφροσύνης δέ, ὡς πλήθει, οὐ τὰ τοιάδε μέγιστα</quote> </cit>; <quote xml:lang="eng">"for the mass of men, are not the cardinal points of temperance such as these?"</quote> Cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 76">76</ref> . </gloss>

data.quote:
σωφροσύνης δέ, ὡς πλήθει, οὐ τὰ τοιάδε
									μέγιστα

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="προὐστάλης_20">, hast fared forward: a compound not found elsewhere in Trag., except in <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. Seven 415" ref="Aesch. Seven 415"> Aesch. <title>Theb.</title> 415</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">Δίκη...νιν προστέλλεται</quote> </cit>, sends him forth as her champion. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Χρόνου_οὕνεκ_22"> . <ref n="Soph. OT 857"> <title>O.T.</title> 857 f.</ref>: <cit> <bibl n="Hdt. 3.122" ref="Hdt. 3.122">Her. 3. 122</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἵνεκέν τε χρημάτων ἄρξεις ἁπάσης τῆς Ἑλλάδος</quote> </cit>(if it is merely a question of money): <cit> <bibl n="Antiph. 5.8" ref="Antiph. 5.8">Antiphon or. 5 § 8</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">κἂν ἀνωμότοις ὑμῖν...ἐπιτρέψαιμι..., ἕνεκά γε τοῦ πιστεύειν</quote> </cit> , <quote xml:lang="eng">"I would leave the verdict to you, though you were unsworn, if it were only a question of confidence."</quote> </gloss>

data.quote:
εἵνεκέν τε χρημάτων ἄρξεις ἁπάσης τῆς
									Ἑλλάδος

κἂν ἀνωμότοις ὑμῖν...ἐπιτρέψαιμι...,
									ἕνεκά γε τοῦ πιστεύειν

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὅποι_23">, since <foreign xml:lang="grc">καθέσταμεν</foreign> implies <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἥκομεν</foreign> : cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 227">227</ref> , <ref n="Soph. OC 476">476</ref> : on the same principle, <quote xml:lang="grc">Ὀλυμπίαζε</quote> (not <foreign xml:lang="grc"> Ὀλυμπίασι</foreign> ) <quote xml:lang="grc">παρεῖναι</quote> , <ref n="Thuc. 3.8">Thuc. 3. 8</ref> .</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γοῦν_24">: <quote xml:lang="eng"> "well ( <foreign xml:lang="grc">οὖν</foreign> ), I know <emph>Athens</emph> ( <foreign xml:lang="grc">γε</foreign> ), but not this place."</quote>Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 233" ref="Soph. El. 233"> <title>El.</title> 233</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλ’ οὖν εὐνοίᾳ γ’ αὐδῶ</quote> </cit> , <quote xml:lang="eng">"well, it is in kindness that I speak."</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἡμὶν_25">as a trochee is frequent in Soph. (Ellendt counts 26 instances), but does not occur in Eur., nor in Aesch., except in <ref n="Aesch. Eum. 347"> <title>Eum.</title> 347</ref>, where Porson's <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἁμίν</foreign> for <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἁμῖν</foreign> seems necessary. Modern edd., with Dind., usu. write <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἡμίν</foreign> : others, as Nauck and Ellendt, would always write <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἧμιν</foreign> , for which the old grammarians afford some warrant (cp. <ref> Chandler, <title>Accent.</title> 2nd ed. § 673</ref>): while others, again, would distinguish an emphatic <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἡμίν</foreign> from a non-emphatic <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἦμιν</foreign> (cp. <ref> Hadley and Allen, <title>Greek Gram.</title> § 264</ref>). !-- need brevia for those carets --> <!-- The verse with the "tribrach" (i. e. resolved longum) seems to be hypothetical, Jebb's own --> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀλλ_τόπος_26">. The tribrach is divided like that in <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Phoen. 511" ref="Eur. Phoen. 511"> Eur. <title>Phoen.</title> 511</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐλθόντ|α^ συ^ν ο^πλ|οις</quote> </cit>, where <foreign xml:lang="grc">σὺν</foreign> coheres closely with <foreign xml:lang="grc">ὅπλοις</foreign> , as <foreign xml:lang="grc">ὁ</foreign> with <foreign xml:lang="grc">τόπος</foreign> . But even where no such cohesion exists, a tribrach may be broken after the second syllable if it is also broken after the first: <foreign xml:lang="lat">e.g.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">δέσποινα, σὺ τάδ’ ἔπραξας οὐ γνώμης ἄτερ</quote> is correct: cp. n. on <ref n="Soph. OT 537"> <title>O. T.</title> 537</ref>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἦ_μάθω_26">, deliberative subjunct., of which the aor. is more frequent than the pres.: so <ref n="Soph. OT 364"> <title>O. T.</title> 364</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἴπω</quote>: see on <ref n="Soph. OT 651"> <title>O. T.</title> 651</ref>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐξοικήσιμος_27">, capable of being made into a dwelling-place, "habitable," here implying "inhabited." Adjectives with the suffix <foreign xml:lang="grc">σιμο</foreign> properly denote adaptability. They were primarily formed from substantives in <foreign xml:lang="grc">‐σι‐ς</foreign> , as <foreign xml:lang="grc">χρήσι‐μο‐ς</foreign> , fitted for use, from <foreign xml:lang="grc">χρῆσις</foreign> . The noun <foreign xml:lang="grc"> ἐξοίκησις</foreign> is found only in the sense of "emigration," <ref n="Plat. Laws 704C"> Plat. <title>Legg.</title> 704C</ref>, 850 B. But as from <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἱππάζομαι</foreign> was formed <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἱππά‐σιμος</foreign> , though no <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἵππασις</foreign> occurs, so <foreign xml:lang="grc"> ἐξοικήσιμος</foreign> here is taken directly from <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἐξοικεῖν</foreign> as="to make into a dwellingplace" ( <cit> <bibl n="Thuc. 2.17" ref="Thuc. 2.17">Thuc. 2.17</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐξῳκήθη</quote> </cit> ). <foreign xml:lang="grc">οἰκήσιμος</foreign> as="habitable" occurs in later Greek. Just as <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἐξοικήσιμος</foreign> is practically equivalent to <foreign xml:lang="grc">οἰκητός</foreign> here, so Silius speaks of the Capitoline as <quote xml:lang="lat"> " <emph> superis</emph> habitabile <emph>saxum</emph> ,"</quote>alluding to the actual shrines on it ( <ref n="Sil. Ital. 1.541">1. 541</ref> ). Cp. <cit> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἁλώσιμος βάξις</quote> <bibl n="Aesch. Ag. 10" ref="Aesch. Ag. 10"> (Aesch. <title>Ag.</title> 10)</bibl> </cit>, tidings of an <emph>actual</emph> , not merely <emph>possible</emph> , capture. This poet. use is the converse of that by which <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄρρητος</quote> could mean "unspeakable," or <foreign xml:lang="lat">invictus</foreign> , "unconquerable." </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀλλ_μὴν_28">, " <emph>nay, but</emph> it <emph>is</emph> inhabited." <ref n="Aesch. Pers. 233"> Aesch. <title>Pers.</title> 233</ref>(in a reply) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλὰ μὴν ἵμειῤ</quote> , " <emph>nay, but</emph> he was eager" (to take this very city). Especially in rejecting an alternative: <ref n="Eur. Hel. 1047"> Eur. <title>Helen</title> 1047</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλ’ οὐδὲ μὴν ναῦς ἔστιν</quote>, "nay, but neither is there a ship." </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καὶ_δὴ_31">, "already": <cit> <bibl n="Aristoph. Birds 175" ref="Aristoph. Birds 175"> Aristoph. <title>Av.</title> 175</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ΠΕΙ. βλέψον κάτω. ΕΠ. καὶ δὴ βλέπω.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

data.quote:
ΠΕΙ. βλέψον κάτω. ΕΠ. καὶ δὴ
									βλέπω.

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μὲν_οὖν_31">, "nay rather" ( <foreign xml:lang="lat">imo</foreign> ); <cit> <bibl n="Aristoph. Kn. 13" ref="Aristoph. Kn. 13"> Aristoph. <title>Eq.</title> 13</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ΝΙ. λέγε σύ. ΔΗ. σὺ μὲν οὖν λέγε</quote> </cit>. </gloss>

data.quote:
ΝΙ. λέγε σύ. ΔΗ. σὺ μὲν οὖν
									λέγε

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὦ_ξεῖν_33">. The Ionic voc. occurs even without metrical necessity, <cit> <bibl n="Eur. IT 798" ref="Eur. IT 798"> Eur. <title>I. T.</title> 798</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ξεῖν’, οὐ δικαίως</quote> </cit>: Soph. rarely uses <foreign xml:lang="grc">ξεῖνος</foreign> except in voc.: <ref target="cl1014">1014 n.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὑπέρ_ἐμοῦ_33"> = <foreign xml:lang="grc">ὑπὲρ ἐμοῦ τε</foreign> : as <ref n="Soph. OT 258"> <title>O. T.</title> 258</ref>(where see n.), <quote xml:lang="grc">κυρῶ τ’ ἐγώ</quote> = <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἐγώ τε κυρῶ</foreign> : <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1294"> <title>Ph.</title> 1294</ref>. Cp. Tennyson's lines <title>"To the Princess Frederica"</title> : <quote xml:lang="eng"> <lg type="Eng"> <l>O you that were eyes and light to the King till he past away</l> <l>From the darkness of life.</l> </lg> </quote> <ref n="Soph. Ant. 989"> <title>Ant.</title>989</ref>(of the blind Teiresias and his guide) <quote xml:lang="grc">δὔ ἐξ ἑνὸς βλέποντε</quote> .</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὕνεχ_φράσαι_34">: that thou hast come near, <foreign xml:lang="grc">αἴσιος σκοπὸς ὧν</foreign> (= <foreign xml:lang="grc">τούτων ἃ</foreign> ) <foreign xml:lang="grc"> ἀδηλοῦμεν</foreign> , an opportune inquirer into our doubts, <foreign xml:lang="grc">φράσαι</foreign> , so as to explain (epexegetic infin., cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 50">50</ref> ). <foreign xml:lang="grc"> σκοπὸς</foreign> has its ordinary sense of "scout" (cp. <ref target="cl297">n. on 297</ref> ). Oedipus supposes that the man has been sent to make inquiry. <foreign xml:lang="grc">τούτων</foreign> is objective gen. after <foreign xml:lang="grc">σκοπός</foreign> .</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὧν_35">, by attract.: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 788" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg004:788"> <title>O. T.</title> 788</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὧν … ἱκόμην</quote> </cit>= ( <foreign xml:lang="grc">τούτων</foreign> ) <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἃ ἱκόμην.</foreign> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀδηλοῦμεν_35">. Since <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἀδηλέω</foreign> =to be <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἄδηλος</foreign> , (as <foreign xml:lang="grc"> ἀπειθέω</foreign> to be <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἀπειθής, ἀκοσμέω</foreign> to be <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἄκοσμος</foreign> ,) the form strictly implies that <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἄδηλος</foreign> could mean, "not <emph>seeing</emph> clearly": but an act. sense nowhere occurs, for in <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Orest. 1318" ref="Eur. Orest. 1318"> Eur. <title>Or.</title> 1318</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">χρόᾳ δ’ ἀδήλῳ τῶν δεδραμένων πέρι</quote> </cit>means, "faces wherein the deeds cannot <emph>be read</emph> " (not, <quote xml:lang="eng">"which seem to know nought of them"</quote> ). Cp. the verbs formed from the active use of verbal adjectives which were primarily passive, as <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἀλαστέω</foreign> , to be unforgetting, <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἀτλητέω</foreign> , to be impatient ( <ref n="Soph. OT 515"> <title>O. T.</title> 515</ref>). Conversely, <foreign xml:lang="grc">δηλόω</foreign> , "to <emph>make</emph> <foreign xml:lang="grc">δῆλος</foreign> ," sometimes verges on the sense, "to <emph> be</emph> <foreign xml:lang="grc">δῆλος</foreign> " ( <ref n="Soph. Ant. 20"> <title>Ant.</title> 20</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Ant. 242">242</ref> ). </gloss>

data.quote:
χρόᾳ δ’ ἀδήλῳ τῶν δεδραμένων
									πέρι

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὰ_πλείον_36">, "the" details foreshadowed by the preamble. <ref n="Isoc. 5.63">Isocr. or. 5 § 63</ref> (in a rapid sketch of Conon's career) <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ τί δεῖ τὰ πλείω λέγειν</quote> ; "and why dwell on the details?" So in <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Phil. 576" ref="Soph. Phil. 576"> Soph. <title>Ph.</title> 576</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">μή νύν μ’ ἔρῃ τὰ πλείον’</quote> </cit> , <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Trach. 731" ref="Soph. Trach. 731"> <title>Tr.</title> 731</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">σιγᾶν ἂν ἁρμόζοι σε τὸν πλείω λόγον</quote> </cit>, the art. denotes "the" sequel which the previous discourse promises. In <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Med. 609" ref="Eur. Med. 609"> Eur. <title>Med.</title> 609</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς οὐ κρινοῦμαι τῶνδέ σοι τὰ πλείονα</quote> </cit>, the gen. brings this out: "Enough —I will not dispute with thee on the further aspects of this matter." </gloss>

data.quote: μή νύν μ’ ἔρῃ τὰ πλείον’

σιγᾶν ἂν ἁρμόζοι σε τὸν πλείω
									λόγον

ὡς οὐ κρινοῦμαι τῶνδέ σοι τὰ
									πλείονα

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐχ_πατεῖν_37">. The poets can use <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἁγνός</foreign> either like <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἱερός</foreign> ( <foreign xml:lang="lat">e.g.</foreign> <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Andr. 253" ref="Eur. Andr. 253"> Eur. <title>Andr.</title> 253</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἁγνὸν τέμενος</quote> </cit>), or, as here, like <foreign xml:lang="grc">ὅσιος</foreign> . For the infin. active, cp. <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Phaedo 62B" ref="Plat. Phaedo 62B"> Plat. <title>Phaedo</title> 62B</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">λόγος οὐ...ῥᾴδιος διϊδεῖν</quote> </cit> , <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Phaedo 90C" ref="Plat. Phaedo 90C">90 C</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">λόγου ...δυνατοῦ κατανοῆσαι</quote> </cit> : <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Med. 316" ref="Eur. Med. 316"> Eur. <title>Med.</title> 316</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">λέγεις ἀκοῦσαι μαλθάκ’</quote> </cit> ( <foreign xml:lang="lat">auditu mollia</foreign> ): <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 792" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg004:792"> Soph. <title>O. T.</title> 792</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄτλητον...ὁρᾶν</quote> </cit>, and n. on <ref n="Soph. OT 1204"> <title>O. T.</title> 1204</ref>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοῦ_νομίζεται_38"> ; <quote xml:lang="eng">"to which of the gods is it deemed to belong?"</quote> After verbs of being thought, called, etc., the gen. expresses "belonging" (1) to a possessor, as here and <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Ant. 738" ref="Soph. Ant. 738"> <title>Ant.</title> 738</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ τοῦ κρατοῦντος ἡ πόλις νομίζεται</quote> </cit>; or (2) to a class, as <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Andr. 12" ref="Eur. Andr. 12"> Eur. <title>Andr.</title> 12</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="trimeter"> <l part="F">τῶν ἐλευθερωτάτων</l> <l part="I">οἴκων νομισθεῖσ’</l> </lg> </quote> </cit>. With (1) here cp. the gen. of the deity after <foreign xml:lang="grc"> ἱερός</foreign> ( <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Phaedo 85B" ref="Plat. Phaedo 85B"> Plat. <title>Phaedo</title> 85B</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἱερὸς τοῦ αὐτοῦ θεοῦ</quote> </cit> ). </gloss>

data.quote:
οὐ τοῦ κρατοῦντος ἡ πόλις
									νομίζεται

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄθικτος_οἰκητός_39"> , <foreign xml:lang="lat">sc.</foreign> <foreign xml:lang="grc"> ἐστιν</foreign> , answering <foreign xml:lang="grc">τίς ἔσθ’ ὁ χῶρος</foreign> ; cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 1274" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg007:1274">1274</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄναυδος οὐδ’ ἃμηνίεις φράσας</quote> </cit> , <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Phil. 2" ref="Soph. Phil. 2"> <title>Ph.</title> 2</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄστειπτος οὐδ’ οἰκουμένη</quote> </cit>. The second question, <foreign xml:lang="grc">τοῦ θεῶν νομίζεται</foreign> ; is answered by <foreign xml:lang="grc">αἱ γὰρ ἔμφοβοι κ.τ.λ.</foreign> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Γῆς_κόραι_40">: as in <ref n="Aesch. Eum. 416"> Aesch. <title>Eum.</title> 416</ref>they call themselves <quote xml:lang="grc">Νυκτὸς αἰανῆς τέκνα</quote> , and invoke <quote xml:lang="grc">μᾶτερ Νύξ</quote> <ref n="Aesch. Eum. 844">(844)</ref> : Aesch. does not name the other parent. In <ref n="Hes. Th. 184"> Hesiod. <title>Theog.</title> 184</ref>the mother is Earth, impregnated by the blood of Uranus,—the idea being that the Erinyes were called into life by the crime of a son (Zeus) against a father. Other versions made them daughters of Euonymè (a name for Earth) and Cronus ( <ref> Epimenides <foreign xml:lang="lat">ap.</foreign> Tzetzes on Lycophron 406</ref>), or of Earth and Phorkys ( <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> the sea): cp. <ref> Welcker <title>Griech. Götterl.</title> 3. 81</ref>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τίνων_κλύων_41">; of whom hearing the august name might I make a prayer? <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="eng">"who may they be, whose name I am to hear, and to invoke?"</quote> The optat. with <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἄν</foreign> gives a reverential tone to the question: <foreign xml:lang="grc">εὐξαίμην ἄν</foreign> refers to such propitiatory words of invocation as were uttered on approaching a shrine. The description has left the Theban stranger in doubt as to the particular deities meant. He might think of other "Daughters of Darkness," — as of the <foreign xml:lang="grc">Κῆρες</foreign> ( <ref n="Hes. Th. 217"> Hes. <title>Theog.</title> 217</ref>), or of the <foreign xml:lang="grc">Μοῖραι</foreign> ,—whom the Eumenides of Aeschylus address as <foreign xml:lang="grc">ματροκασιγνῆται</foreign> , children of the same mother, <foreign xml:lang="grc">Νύξ</foreign> ( <ref n="Aesch. Eum. 961"> <title>Eum.</title> 961</ref>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πάνθ_ὁρώσας_42">, because no crime escapes their ken: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 835" ref="Soph. Aj. 835"> <title>Ai.</title> 835f.</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="trimeter"> <l part="F">τὰς ἀεί τε παρθένους</l> <l>ἀεί θ’ ὁρώσας πάντα τἀν βροτοῖς πάθη,</l> <l>σεμνὰς Ἐρινῦς τανύποδας.</l> </lg> </quote> </cit> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Εὐμενίδας_42">, the title of the Erinyes at Sicyon ( <ref n="Paus. 2.11.4">Paus. 2.11.4</ref> ), was not used by Aeschylus in his play of that name, unless with Herm. we assume that it was in a part of Athena's speech which has dropped out after <ref n="Aesch. Eum. 1028">v. 1028</ref> . When Harpocration says that the Athena of Aeschylus, <quote xml:lang="grc">πραΰνασα τὰς Ἐρινύας, Εὐμενίδας ὠνόμασεν</quote> , he perh. refers to such epithets as <cit> <quote xml:lang="grc">εὔφρονες</quote> <bibl n="Aesch. Eum. 992" ref="Aesch. Eum. 992"> ( <title>Eum.</title> 992)</bibl> </cit> , <cit> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἵλαοι, εὐθύφρονες</quote> <bibl n="Aesch. Eum. 1040" ref="Aesch. Eum. 1040">(1040)</bibl> </cit> , <cit> <quote xml:lang="grc">Σεμναί</quote> <bibl n="Aesch. Eum. 1041" ref="Aesch. Eum. 1041">(1041)</bibl> </cit>. Demosthenes ( <ref n="Dem. 23.66">or. 23 § 66</ref> ) uses the name in referring to the trial of Orestes. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄλλα_καλά_43"> : <ref>schol.</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄλλα ὀνόματα παρ’ ἄλλοις καλὰ νομίζεται</quote> . Wunder and others quote <cit> <bibl n="Plut. Them. 27" ref="Plut. Them. 27"> Plut. <title>Them.</title> 27</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὦ ξένε, νόμοι διαφέρουσιν ἀνθρώπων· ἄλλα δ’ ἄλλοις καλά</quote> </cit>. This is against rendering, "but otherwhere [the folk would give them] other fair names." Near Megalopolis, on the road to Messene, there was a shrine of the <foreign xml:lang="grc">Μανίαι</foreign> : <quote xml:lang="grc">δοκεῖν δέ μοι, θεῶν τῶν Εὐμενίδων ἐστὶν ἐπίκλησις</quote> , <ref n="Paus. 8.34.1">Paus. 8.34.1</ref> . Aeschines gives the attributes of the Erinyes to the <foreign xml:lang="grc">Ποιναί</foreign> ( <cit> <quote xml:lang="grc">τοὺς ἠσεβηκότας...ἐλαύνειν καὶ κολάζειν δᾷσὶν ἡμμέναις,</quote> <bibl n="Aeschin. 1.190" ref="Aeschin. 1.190">.or. 1 § 190</bibl> </cit>). As at Athens they were <foreign xml:lang="grc">Σεμναί</foreign> , at Thebes they were <foreign xml:lang="grc">Πότνιαι</foreign> (cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 84">84</ref> ). Another name was <foreign xml:lang="grc">Ἀραί</foreign> ( <ref n="Aesch. Eum. 417"> <title>Eum.</title> 417</ref>). </gloss>

data.quote:
ὦ ξένε, νόμοι διαφέρουσιν ἀνθρώπων·
									ἄλλα δ’ ἄλλοις καλά

τοὺς ἠσεβηκότας...ἐλαύνειν καὶ
									κολάζειν δᾷσὶν ἡμμέναις,

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μὲν_44">seems right. It implies a thought answering, rather than opposed, to <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἵλεῳ δεξαίατο</foreign> : <foreign xml:lang="lat"> i.e.</foreign> "gracious on their part may be the welcome, (as, on mine, the duty to remain is clear)": <emph>not</emph> , "gracious, indeed, may be their welcome, ( <emph>but</emph> , even if they should be stern, I must stay)." Cp. the <foreign xml:lang="grc">μέν</foreign> , without a following <foreign xml:lang="grc">δέ</foreign> , which lightly emphasises rather than contrasts: <cit> <bibl n="Xen. Cyrop. 1.4.12" ref="Xen. Cyrop. 1.4.12"> Xen. <title>Cyr.</title> 1.4.12</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐγὼ μὲν οὐκ οἶδα</quote> </cit>(as others, perhaps, may). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸν_ἱκέτην_44">, without <foreign xml:lang="grc">με</foreign> (which I should at least prefer to <foreign xml:lang="grc">)μὲ</foreign> or <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἐμὲ</foreign> , if <foreign xml:lang="grc">μὲν</foreign> were changed), is more solemn: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 284" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg007:284">284</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλ’ ὥσπερ ἔλαβες τὸν ἱκέτην.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δεξαίατο_44">, Ionic: so <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 921" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg007:921">921</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">πυθοίατο</quote> </cit> , <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 945" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg007:945">945</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">δεξοίατο</quote> </cit> , <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 1274" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg004:1274"> <title>O. T.</title> 1274</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὀψοίατο, γνωσοίατο</quote> </cit>, where see n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὡς_45">is clearly right. The <foreign xml:lang="grc">ὥστ’</foreign> of the MSS. would mean, " <emph>and so</emph> " ( <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> since they are the Eumenides). It could not mean, "and in that case," <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> "if they prove kind." <foreign xml:lang="grc">ὡς</foreign> is best taken as simply causal, "for" ( <cit> <bibl n="tlg5037.tlg006" ref="tlg5037.tlg006">schol.</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐγὼ γὰρ οὐκ ἀναστήσομαι</quote> </cit>), rather than as="know that" ( <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Phoen. 1664" ref="Eur. Phoen. 1664"> Eur. <title>Phoen.</title> 1664</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ΚΡΕΩΝ. ὡς οὔτις ἀμφὶ τῷδ’ ὑγρὰν θήσει κόνιν</quote> </cit>). </gloss>

data.quote:
ΚΡΕΩΝ. ὡς οὔτις ἀμφὶ τῷδ’ ὑγρὰν θήσει
									κόνιν

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γῆς_45">: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 668" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg007:668">668</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="lyric"> <l part="F">τᾶσδε χώρας</l> <l part="F">… ἔπαυλα</l> </lg> </quote> </cit> . <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Hel. 797" ref="Eur. Hel. 797"> Eur. <title>Helen</title> 797</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁρᾷς τάφου τοῦδ’ ἀθλίους ἕδρας ἐμάς;</quote> </cit> </gloss>

data.quote:
ὁρᾷς τάφου τοῦδ’ ἀθλίους ἕδρας
									ἐμάς;

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἂν_ἐξέλθοιμ_45">: the optat. with <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἄν</foreign> calmly expresses a fixed resolve: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 343" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg004:343"> <title>O. T.</title> 343</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐκ ἂν πέρα φράσαιμι</quote> </cit> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τί_τοῦτο_46">"What means this?" (cp. <foreign xml:lang="grc">τί δ’ ἔστι;</foreign> "what now?" <ref n="Soph. OT 319"> <title>O. T.</title> 319</ref>n.). <quote xml:lang="eng">"What has this sudden resolve to do with the mention of the Eumenides?"</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ξυμφορᾶς_ἐμῆς_46"> . <foreign xml:lang="grc">σύνθημα</foreign> =something agreed upon ( <foreign xml:lang="grc">συντίθεμαι</foreign> ), as <foreign xml:lang="lat">e.g.</foreign> a military watchword ( <ref n="Hdt. 9.98">Her. 9.98</ref> ). Apollo had told Oedipus that, when he reached a shrine of the <foreign xml:lang="grc">Σεμναί</foreign> , then he should find rest <ref n="Soph. OC 90">(90)</ref> . This was the <foreign xml:lang="grc">σύνθημα</foreign> , the sign preconcerted between them, which Oedipus has now recognised at Colonus (cp. <cit> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔγνωκα,</quote> <bibl n="Soph. OC 96" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg007:96">96</bibl> </cit>). He calls <emph>his own prayer</emph> <ref n="Soph. OC 44">(44 f.)</ref> the <foreign xml:lang="grc">σύνθημα</foreign> of his fate, because it embodies the two points of the <foreign xml:lang="grc">σύνθημα</foreign> , — "Here are the Eumenides, — here I stay." Campbell renders, " <emph>the word that sums</emph> my destiny," and seems to regard the notion of "sign" as blended with that of "summary." But the two notions are distinct. <foreign xml:lang="grc">σύνθημα</foreign> is always parallel in sense with <foreign xml:lang="grc">συντίθεμαι</foreign> as="to concert" ( <foreign xml:lang="grc">βουλήν</foreign> , etc.), never with <foreign xml:lang="grc">συντίθημι</foreign> as="to put briefly together." </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐμοί_47">is indispensable, while <foreign xml:lang="grc">οὐδὲ μέντοι</foreign> would be weak. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοὐξανιστάναι_47">: the art. with the infin. (whether subject or object) is esp. frequent in the dramatists, for the simple reason that it was often metrically convenient: <ref n="Soph. OC 442">442</ref> : <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 114" ref="Soph. Aj. 114"> <title>Ai.</title> 114</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τέρψις ἥδε σοι τὸ δρᾶν</quote> </cit> : <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Ant. 78" ref="Soph. Ant. 78"> <title>Ant.</title> 78</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="trimeter"> <l part="F">τὸ γὰρ</l> <l>βίᾳ πολιτῶν δρᾶν ἔφυν ἀμήχανος</l> </lg> </quote> </cit> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δίχ_48">, like <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἄνευ</foreign> or <foreign xml:lang="grc"> χωρίς</foreign> , "without the sanction of": <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 768" ref="Soph. Aj. 768"> <title>Ai.</title> 768</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="trimeter"> <l part="F">καὶ δίχα</l> <l part="I">κείνων</l> </lg> </quote> </cit> , <quote xml:lang="eng">"e'en without the gods' help."</quote> Nauck objects to the position: but not less bold, at least, is <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 1084" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg004:1084"> <title>O.T.</title> 1084</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="trimeter"> <l>ἔτι</l> <l>ποτ’ ἄλλος</l> </lg> </quote> </cit> , <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 986" ref="Soph. Aj. 986"> <title>Ai.</title> 986</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="trimeter"> <l part="F">οὐχ ὅσον τάχος</l> <l part="I">δῆτ’ αὐτὸν ἄξεις …; </l> </lg> </quote> </cit> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐνδείξω_δρῶ_48">, indicate what I am doing: <foreign xml:lang="grc">δρῶ</foreign> is pres. indic.: <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Gorg. 488A" ref="Plat. Gorg. 488A"> Plat. <title>Gorg.</title> 488A</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἱκανῶς μοι ἔνδειξαι τί ἔστι τοῦτο</quote> </cit>. <cit> <bibl n="Antiph. 6.37" ref="Antiph. 6.37">Antiphon or. 6 § 37</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐνδεῖξαι τῷ δικαστηρίῳ τὰ ἀδικήματα</quote> </cit>. The technical <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἔνδειξις</foreign> was an information laid against usurpers of public functions, or, in certain cases, against <foreign xml:lang="grc">κακοῦργοι</foreign> . Schneidewin and Wecklein take <foreign xml:lang="grc">δρῶ</foreign> as subjunct., understanding, —"report the matter ( <emph>and ask</emph> ) what I am to do": but the idea of <emph> asking</emph> could not be supplied. </gloss>

data.quote:
ἱκανῶς μοι ἔνδειξαι τί ἔστι
									τοῦτο

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ξεῖνε_49">: <ref n="Soph. OC 33">33</ref> . !-- this isn't obvious: what's the lemma here? --> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μὴ_ἀτιμάσῃς_49"> <foreign xml:lang="lat">tou/twn</foreign>(genit. as after verbs of depriving) <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἅ σε προστρέπω</foreign> (cp. <ref n="Soph. Aj. 831"> <title>Ai.</title> 831</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τοσαῦτά σε... προστρέπω</quote>), <foreign xml:lang="grc">φράσαι</foreign> (epexegetic infin.): deny me not the grace of the things for which I supplicate thee, that thou shouldest declare them. Cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 35">35</ref> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τίς_ἔσθ_52"> , <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="eng"> "what is it <emph>called?</emph> "</quote>In answer to the same query at <ref n="Soph. OC 38">v. 38</ref> he had only learned that part of it was <emph>sacred.</emph> Cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 26">26</ref> .</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κἀγώ_53">. We say: — "What <emph>I</emph> know, <emph>you also</emph> shall know" ( <foreign xml:lang="grc">ὅσ’ οἶδ’ ἐγώ, καὶ σὺ ἐπιστήσει</foreign> ). The Greeks could say: — "What <emph>I also</emph> (=I on my part) know, you (also) shall know." The second "also" ( <foreign xml:lang="grc">καί</foreign> ) is absent here, since <foreign xml:lang="grc">σύ</foreign> is wanting. <cit> <bibl n="Xen. Sym. 2.25" ref="Xen. Sym. 2.25"> Xen. <title>Symp.</title> 2. 25</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">δοκεῖ μέντοι μοι καὶ τὰ τῶν ἀνδρῶν συμπόσια ταὐτὰ πάσχειν ἅπερ καὶ τὰ ἐν γῇ φυόμενα</quote> </cit> . <cit> <bibl n="Antiph. 5.23" ref="Antiph. 5.23">Antiphon or. 5 § 23</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐζητεῖτο οὐδέν τι μᾶλλον ὑπὸ τῶν ἄλλων ἢ καὶ ὑπ’ ἐμοῦ</quote> </cit>. So <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 1146" ref="Soph. El. 1146"> Soph. <title>El.</title> 1146</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="trimeter"> <l part="F">οὔτε γάρ ποτε</l> <l>μητρὸς σύ γ’ ἦσθα μᾶλλον ἢ κἀμοῦ φίλος</l> </lg> </quote> </cit>. Cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 870">below, 870</ref> ( <quote xml:lang="grc"> κἀμὲ</quote> ): <ref n="Soph. Aj. 525"> <title>Ai.</title> 525</ref>: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 927"> <title>Ant.</title> 927</ref>. </gloss>

data.quote:
δοκεῖ μέντοι μοι καὶ τὰ τῶν ἀνδρῶν
									συμπόσια ταὐτὰ πάσχειν ἅπερ καὶ τὰ ἐν γῇ φυόμενα

ἐζητεῖτο οὐδέν τι μᾶλλον ὑπὸ τῶν ἄλλων
									ἢ καὶ ὑπ’ ἐμοῦ

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Ποσειδῶν_55"> . <cit> <bibl n="Paus. 1.30.4" ref="Paus. 1.30.4">Paus. 1.30.4</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> δείκνυται δὲ καὶ χῶρος καλούμενος Κολωνὸς Ἵππιος...καὶ βωμὸς Ποσειδῶνος Ἱππίου καὶ Ἀθηνᾶς Ἱππίας <note anchored="true" place="inline" resp="RCJ"> <bibl n="Soph. OC 1069" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg007:1069">(1069)</bibl> </note>, ἡρῷον δὲ Πειρίθου καὶ Θησέως <note anchored="true" place="inline" resp="RCJ"> <bibl n="Soph. OC 1593" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg007:1593">(1593)</bibl> </note>, Οἰδίποδός τε καὶ Ἀδράστου</quote> </cit>. This altar of Poseidon ( <cit> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπιστάτης Κολωνοῦ</quote> <bibl n="Soph. OC 889" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg007:889">889</bibl> </cit>) lies beyond the stage-scene <ref n="Soph. OC 888">(888)</ref> . </gloss>

data.quote:
δείκνυται δὲ καὶ χῶρος καλούμενος Κολωνὸς
							Ἵππιος...καὶ βωμὸς Ποσειδῶνος Ἱππίου καὶ Ἀθηνᾶς Ἱππίας  ,
							ἡρῷον δὲ Πειρίθου καὶ Θησέως  ,
							Οἰδίποδός τε καὶ Ἀδράστου

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐν_δ_55"> (adv.), <foreign xml:lang="lat">sc.</foreign> <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἐστίν</foreign> : Prometheus did not belong to Colonus itself (as Poseidon did), but to the neighbouring Academy (see <ref target="cl56">on 56</ref> ): he is named as one of several divine presences in the vicinity. So <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἐν δ’</foreign> adds a new member to a group, <ref n="Soph. OT 27"> <title>O. T.</title> 27</ref>(where the same words <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἐν δ’ ὁ π. θεός</foreign> refer to the plague), <ref n="Soph. Aj. 675"> <title>Ai.</title> 675</ref>. If, instead of <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἐν δ’</foreign> we read <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἠδ’</foreign> (which Soph. sometimes used in dialogue, <ref>fr. 345</ref> and <ref> 493</ref> ), this would rather link the two deities as holding Colonus. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Προμηθεύς_56">is a "Titan" as son of the Titan Iapetus ( <ref n="Hes. Th. 510"> Hes. <title> Theog.</title> 510</ref>). <ref> Welcker ( <title>Griech. Götterl.</title> 2. 254)</ref>thinks that "Titan," instead of "Titanid," is used here only because, like the Titans, Prometheus rebelled against Zeus: but this seems strained. Cp. <ref n="Cic. Tusc. 2.10.23"> Cic. <title>Tusc.</title> 2. 10.23</ref>(from the <title xml:lang="grc">Προμ. Λυόμενος</title> of Aesch., Prometheus speaking) <foreign xml:lang="lat">Titanum suboles, socia nostri sanguinis, Generata caelo.</foreign> <foreign xml:lang="grc">πυρφόρος</foreign> <ref n="Soph. OC 55">(55)</ref> , because represented with a torch in the right hand: <ref n="Eur. Phoen. 1121"> Eur. <title>Phoen.</title> 1121</ref>(on the shield of Tydeus) <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="trimeter"> <l part="F">δεξιᾷ δὲ λαμπάδα</l> <l>Τιτὰν Προμηθεὺς ἔφερεν ὡς πρήσων πόλιν</l> </lg> </quote>. So <foreign xml:lang="grc">πυρφόρος</foreign> of Artemis ( <ref n="Soph. OT 207"> <title>O. T.</title> 207</ref>), and Capaneus ( <ref n="Soph. Ant. 135"> <title>Ant.</title> 135</ref>). Cp. <ref>Philostratus p. 602</ref> (quoting the Athenian rhetorician Apollonius, <date when="0225">circ. 225 A.D.</date> ) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἰὼ Προμηθεῦ δᾳδοῦχε καὶ πυρφόρε</quote> . His altar was in the Academy, just S. of Colonus, and this was the starting-point of the <foreign xml:lang="grc">λαμπαδηφορία</foreign> (to the acropolis) at the three torch-festivals. <cit> <bibl>Harpocrat. 184</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τρεῖς ἄγουσιν Ἀθηναῖοι ἑορτὰς λαμπάδας, Παναθηναίοις καὶ Ἡφαιστείοις καὶ Προμηθείοις</quote> </cit>. <cit> <bibl> Schol. Aristoph. <title>Ran.</title> 131</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">λαμπαδηφορίαι δὲ γίγονται τρεῖς ἐν τῷ Κεραμεικῷ, Ἀθηνᾶς, Ἡφαίστου, Προμηθέως</quote> </cit>. Aesch. wrote both a <title xml:lang="grc">Πρ. Πυρφόρος</title> (the 1st play of his trilogy) and a satyric <title xml:lang="grc">Πρ. Πύρκαεύς</title> . </gloss>

data.quote:
τρεῖς ἄγουσιν Ἀθηναῖοι ἑορτὰς
									λαμπάδας, Παναθηναίοις καὶ Ἡφαιστείοις καὶ
									Προμηθείοις

λαμπαδηφορίαι δὲ γίγονται τρεῖς ἐν τῷ
							Κεραμεικῷ, Ἀθηνᾶς, Ἡφαίστου, Προμηθέως

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τόπον_56">by inverse attraction: <cit> <bibl n="Lys. 19.47" ref="Lys. 19.47">Lys. or. 19 § 47</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὴν οὐσίαν ἣν κατέλιπε τῷ υἱεῖ οὐ πλείονος ἀξία ἐστίν κ.τ.λ.</quote> </cit>: cp. on <ref n="Soph. OT 449"> <title>O. T.</title> 449</ref>. </gloss>

data.quote:
τὴν οὐσίαν ἣν κατέλιπε τῷ υἱεῖ οὐ
									πλείονος ἀξία ἐστίν κ.τ.λ.

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὀδός_57">. Somewhere near the grove of the Eumenides, but not within the stagescene, was a spot called "the threshold" of Hades,—a steeply-descending rift or cavern in the rock, at the mouth of which some brazen steps had been made (see <ref target="cl1590">on 1590 f.</ref> ),—in accordance with the epic notion that Hades had a <foreign xml:lang="grc">χάλκεος οὐδός</foreign> ( <ref n="Hom. Il. 8.15"> <title>Il.</title> 8.15</ref>). From this <emph>spot</emph> , the immediately adjacent <emph>region</emph> (including the grove) was known as " <emph>the brazen threshold</emph> ," — <foreign xml:lang="grc">χαλκόπους</foreign> , borrowed from the literal <foreign xml:lang="grc">χαλκᾶ βάθρα</foreign> <ref n="Soph. OC 1591">(1591)</ref> , taking the general sense of "adamantine." As <quote xml:lang="eng">"rooted on the nether rock"</quote> ( <foreign xml:lang="grc">γῆθεν ἐρριζωμένον</foreign> <ref n="Soph. OC 1591">1591</ref> ), and also as linked by mystic sanctities with the Powers of the Under-world, this region of the "brazen threshold" is called <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἔρεισμ’ Ἀθηνῶν</foreign> , the <emph>stay</emph> of Athens: a phrase in which the idea of physical basis is joined to that of religious safeguard. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="χαλκόπους_57">, with feet of brass ( <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 491" ref="Soph. El. 491"> <title>El.</title> 491</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">χ. Ἐρινύς</quote> </cit>, untiring), <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> furnished with brazen steps: not, putting brass under the foot, as some have taken it: so <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἀργυρόπους, χρυσόπους</foreign> etc. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀρχηγός_60">, or <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἀρχηγέτης</foreign> ,=esp. the founder of a family or clan, or (like <foreign xml:lang="grc">κτίστης, οἰκιστής</foreign> ) of a city. <cit> <bibl> Bekker <title>Anecd.</title> I. 449</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀρχηγέται· ἡγεμόνες οἱ ἐπώνυμοι τῶν φυλῶν</quote> </cit>, quoting from the <title xml:lang="grc">Γῆρας</title> of Ar. <foreign xml:lang="grc">παρὰ τοὺς ἀρχηγέτας</foreign> ,=by the statues of the ten <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἐπώνυμοι ἥρωες</foreign> of the Attic tribes. <cit> <bibl>Arist. fr. 85 (Berl. ed. p. 1491 a 20)</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀρετὴ τοῦ γένους, καὶ εὐγενεῖς οἱ ἀπὸ τούτου τοῦ γένους, οὐκ ἐὰν ὁ πατὴρ εὐγενὴς ᾖ ἀλλ’ ἐὰν ὁ ἀρχηγός</quote> </cit>. <cit> <bibl n="Isoc. 3.28" ref="Isoc. 3.28">Isocr. or. 3 § 28</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">Τεῦκρος μὲν ὁ τοῦ γένους ἡμῶν ἀρχηγός</quote> </cit>. <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Tim. 21E" ref="Plat. Tim. 21E"> Plat. <title>Tim.</title> 21E</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τῆς πόλεως θεὸς ἀρχηγός τίς ἐστιν</quote> </cit>(of Saïs in Egypt, which claimed origin from the goddess Neith). </gloss>

data.quote:
ἀρχηγέται· ἡγεμόνες οἱ ἐπώνυμοι τῶν
									φυλῶν

ἀρετὴ τοῦ γένους, καὶ εὐγενεῖς οἱ ἀπὸ
									τούτου τοῦ γένους, οὐκ ἐὰν ὁ πατὴρ εὐγενὴς ᾖ ἀλλ’ ἐὰν ὁ
									ἀρχηγός

Τεῦκρος μὲν ὁ τοῦ γένους ἡμῶν
									ἀρχηγός

τῆς πόλεως θεὸς ἀρχηγός τίς
									ἐστιν

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σοι_62">, ethic dat.: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 761" ref="Soph. El. 761"> <title>El.</title> 761</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="trimeter"> <l>τοιαῦτά σοι ταῦτ’ ἐστίν, ὡς μὲν ἐν λόγῳ</l> <l>ἀλγεινά, κ.τ.λ. </l> </lg> </quote> </cit> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λόγοις_62">, "story," legend, generally, but esp. poetry, in which Colonus had not yet figured: the <title>Iliad</title> <ref n="Hom. Il. 23.679">(23. 679)</ref> buries Oedipus at Thebes: cp. <ref n="Paus. 1.30.4">Paus. 1.30.4</ref> (of the Oedipus-myth at Colonus) <quote xml:lang="grc">διάφορα μὲν καὶ ταῦτα τῇ Ὁμήρου ποιήσει</quote> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τῇ_ξυνουσίᾳ_63">, "by the dwelling with them": <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> those who live at Colonus feel the charm of its holy places grow upon them. So the Thucydidean Pericles describes the Athenians as <cit> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὴν τῆς πόλεως δύναμιν καθ’ ἡμέραν ἔργῳ θεωμένους καὶ ἐραστὰς γιγνομένους αὐτῆς</quote> <bibl n="Thuc. 2.43" ref="Thuc. 2.43">(2. 43)</bibl> </cit>: cp. <cit> <bibl>the schol. here,</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τῷ ἔργῳ καὶ τῇ πείρᾳ πλέον τιμώμενα, οὐ τοῖς λόγοις</quote> </cit>. </gloss>

data.quote:
τὴν τῆς πόλεως δύναμιν καθ’ ἡμέραν
									ἔργῳ θεωμένους καὶ ἐραστὰς γιγνομένους αὐτῆς

τῷ ἔργῳ καὶ τῇ πείρᾳ πλέον τιμώμενα,
									οὐ τοῖς λόγοις

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἦ_γάρ_64"> <foreign xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</foreign>The eager interest of Oed. in this question depends on his knowledge, derived from the oracle, that he brought <cit> <quote xml:lang="grc">κέρδη τοῖς δεδεγμένοις</quote> <bibl n="Soph. OC 92" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg007:92">(92)</bibl> </cit>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καὶ_κάρτα_65">: cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 301">301</ref> : <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Hipp. 89" ref="Eur. Hipp. 89"> Eur. <title>Hipp.</title> 89</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ΘΕ. ἆρ’ ἄν τί μου δέξαιο...; ΙΠ καὶ κάρτα γ’</quote> </cit>. </gloss>

data.quote:
ΘΕ. ἆρ’ ἄν τί μου δέξαιο...; ΙΠ καὶ
									κάρτα γ’

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θεοῦ_65">, the <emph>hero</emph> Colonus. Though the distinction had lost nothing of its clearness at this date (cp. <cit> <bibl n="Antiph. 1.27" ref="Antiph. 1.27">Antiphon or. 1 § 27</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὔτε θεοὺς οὔθ’ ἥρωας οὔτ’ ἀνθρώπους δείσασα</quote> </cit>), <foreign xml:lang="grc">θεός</foreign> is sometimes the generic term for beings who receive divine honours: so Amphion and Zethus, the Theban heroes, are <foreign xml:lang="grc">τὼ σιώ</foreign> ( <ref n="Aristoph. Ach. 905"> Aristoph. <title>Ach.</title> 905</ref>), and Eupolis says ( <ref> <title xml:lang="grc">Ἀστράτευτοι</title> fr. 3</ref>) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν εὐσκίοις δρόμοισιν Ἀκαδήμου θεοῦ</quote> (the <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἐπώνυμος</foreign> of the <foreign xml:lang="grc">Ἀκαδήμεια</foreign> ). </gloss>

data.quote:
οὔτε θεοὺς οὔθ’ ἥρωας οὔτ’ ἀνθρώπους
									δείσασα

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἢ_λόγος_66">"or does <emph>power of discussion</emph> rest with the people?" <foreign xml:lang="grc">πλήθει</foreign> , the popular <emph>assembly</emph> , as oft. <foreign xml:lang="grc">τὸ ὑμέτερον πλῆθος</foreign> in the Attic orators. <ref n="Thuc. 2.40">Thuc. 2.40</ref> (Pericles, on the Athenian democracy) <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ τοὺς λόγους τοῖς ἔργοις βλάβην ἡγούμενοι</quote> . The schol. paraphrases, <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἢ ἐν τῷ πλ. ἐστὶν ἡ ἰσχύς</foreign> ; and <foreign xml:lang="grc">κράτος</foreign> is a conject. instead of <foreign xml:lang="grc">λόγος</foreign> . Elmsley and others cp. <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Cycl. 119" ref="Eur. Cycl. 119"> Eur. <title>Cycl.</title> 119</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τίνος κλύοντες; <note anchored="true" place="inline" resp="RCJ" xml:lang="eng">(under what king?)</note> ἢ δεδήμευται κράτος;</quote> </cit>There is no evidence for <foreign xml:lang="grc">λόγος</foreign> as (1) the commanding word, "sway": (2) the deciding word, "arbitrament": or (3) the "principle" ( <foreign xml:lang="lat">ratio</foreign> ) of government. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐκ_67">, of the head and fount of power: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 264" ref="Soph. El. 264"> <title>El.</title> 264</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">κἀκ τῶνδ’ ἄρχομαι</quote> </cit> : <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Ant. 63" ref="Soph. Ant. 63"> <title>Ant.</title> 63</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀρχόμεσθ’ ἐκ κρεισσόνων</quote> </cit> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὗτος_κρατεῖ_68">; = <foreign xml:lang="grc">τίς ἔστιν οὗτος ὃς κρατεῖ;</foreign> <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Hec. 501" ref="Eur. Hec. 501"> Eur. <title>Hec.</title> 501</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="trimeter"> <l>τίς οὗτος σῶμα τοὐμὸν οὐκ ἐᾷς</l> <l part="I">κεῖσθαι;</l> </lg> </quote> </cit> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λόγῳ_σθένει_68">, word (counsel) and might (of deeds): <ref n="Hom. Od. 16.242"> <title>Od.</title> 16.242</ref>(Odysseus) <quote xml:lang="grc">χεῖράς τ’ αἰχμητὴν ἔμεναι καὶ ἐπίφρονα βουλήν</quote> : <ref n="Pind. P. 5"> Pind. <title>Pyth.</title> 5.111</ref>(may Cyrene's king be blest) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπ’ ἔργοισιν ἀμφί τε βουλαῖς</quote> : <ref n="Soph. OT 884"> Soph. <title>O. T.</title> 884</ref>(of a <foreign xml:lang="grc">τύραννος</foreign> ) <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ δέ τις ὑπέροπτα χερσὶν ἢ λόγῳ πορεύεται</quote> . So Theseus is described by <ref n="Thuc. 2.15">Thuc. 2.15</ref> as <quote xml:lang="grc">γενόμενος μετὰ τοῦ ξυνετοῦ καὶ δυνατός</quote> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἆρ_μόλοι_70">"I wonder if any one would go?" = I wish that some one would go. <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Il. 10.303" ref="Hom. Il. 10.303"> <title>Il.</title> 10.303</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="hexameter"> <l>τίς κέν μοι τόδε ἔργον ὑποσχόμενος τελέσειε</l> <l part="I">δώρῳ ἔπι μεγάλῳ;</l> </lg> </quote> </cit> Cp. <ref target="cl1100">infra 1100</ref> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αὐτῷ_70">, poet. after the verb of motion: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Il. 12.374" ref="Hom. Il. 12.374"> <title>Il.</title> 12.374</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπειγομένοισι δ’ ἵκοντο</quote> </cit> : <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. PB 358" ref="Aesch. PB 358"> Aesch. <title>PV</title> 358</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἦλθεν αὐτῷ Ζηνὸς...βελος</quote> </cit>: cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 711"> <title>O. T.</title> 711</ref>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πομπός_70">, one sent to bring a person, <ref n="Soph. OT 288"> <title>O.T.</title> 288</ref>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὡς_τί_71">goes with both participles, <foreign xml:lang="grc">μολεῖν</foreign> with the second only. The Chorus are uncertain whether Oedipus has merely some <emph> message</emph> for Theseus, or wishes to bring him in person to the spot (as <foreign xml:lang="grc">πομπός</foreign> might imply). Our pointing is better than <foreign xml:lang="grc">ὡς πρὸς τί; λ. ἢ κ. μολεῖν</foreign> ; The query turns more on the motive of the appeal than on a sharp contrast between its possible forms. <foreign xml:lang="grc">λέξων</foreign> should not be joined with <foreign xml:lang="grc">μολεῖν</foreign> ( <quote xml:lang="eng"> " <emph>bid</emph> him come,"</quote>Blaydes). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καταρτύσων_μολεῖν_71">, to prepare things (to work upon his mind, directly or indirectly), so that he shall come: for the inf. cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 1286">1286</ref> : <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Rep. 562C" ref="Plat. Rep. 562C"> Plat. <title>Rep.</title> 562C</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὴν πολιτείαν... παρασκευάζει τυραννίδος δεηθῆναι</quote> </cit>: and for <foreign xml:lang="grc">καταρτύω</foreign> of mental or moral influence, <cit> <bibl n="Plut. De Recta 38d" ref="Plut. De Recta 38d"> Plut. <title>Mor.</title> 38 D</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἂν...μὴ λόγοις χρηστοῖς ἀφαιρῶν ἢ παρατρέπων καταρτύῃ τὴν φύσιν</quote> </cit> . </gloss>

data.quote:
ἂν...μὴ λόγοις χρηστοῖς ἀφαιρῶν ἢ
									παρατρέπων καταρτύῃ τὴν φύσιν

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μὴ_73"> <foreign xml:lang="grc">βλέποντος</foreign>, not <foreign xml:lang="grc">οὐ</foreign> , since the blindness is a condition: "if he has not sight." </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὁρῶντα_74">: the blind man's words will be instinct with mental vision. (Cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 747"> <title>O.T.</title> 747</ref>.) The insight is ascribed to the words themselves, not to the speaker, as at <ref n="Soph. OC 267">267</ref> <foreign xml:lang="grc">πεπονθότα</foreign> and <foreign xml:lang="grc"> δεδρακότα</foreign> are epithets of the <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἔργα</foreign> , not of the agent. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. Lib. 854" ref="Aesch. Lib. 854"> Aesch. <title>Cho.</title> 854</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">φρέν’...ὠμματωμένην</quote> </cit> , <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. Supp. 467" ref="Aesch. Supp. 467"> <title>Suppl.</title> 467</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὠμμάτωσα...σαφέστερον</quote> </cit> ( <foreign xml:lang="grc">λόγον</foreign> ). <cit> <bibl> Milton, <title>Par.
