Lost.</title> 3. 51</bibl> <quote xml:lang="eng"> <lg type="english"> <l>So much the rather thou, Celestial Light,</l> <l>Shine inward, and the mind through all her powers</l> <l part="I">Irradiate; there plant eyes.</l> </lg> </quote> </cit> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οἶσθ_σφαλῇς_75">dost thou know (how to act), — that thou mayest not come to harm? A modification of the phrase <foreign xml:lang="grc">οἶσθ’ ὡς ποίησον</foreign> , in which <foreign xml:lang="grc">ποίησον</foreign> is abruptly substituted for <foreign xml:lang="grc">δεῖ σε ποιῆσαι</foreign> . So, here, <foreign xml:lang="grc">οἶσθα</foreign> eagerly bespeaks attention to the advice: see on <ref n="Soph. OT 543"> <title>O. T.</title> 543</ref>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὡς_ἰδόντι_76"> : <foreign xml:lang="grc">ὡς</foreign> has a limiting force (as above, <ref n="Soph. OC 20">20</ref> ), <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Ant. 1161" ref="Soph. Ant. 1161"> <title>Ant.</title> 1161</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἦν ζηλωτός, ὡς ἐμοί</quote> </cit>(cp. on <ref n="Soph. OT 763"> <title>O. T.</title> 763</ref>). The dat. is that of the person <emph> interested</emph> by the perception, as in <cit> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς μὲν συνελόντι εἰπεῖν</quote> <bibl n="Xen. Anab. 3.1.38" ref="Xen. Anab. 3.1.38"> (Xen. <title>An.</title> 3. 1 § 38)</bibl> </cit> , <quote xml:lang="grc">πολλὰ καὶ ἄλλα παραλιπόντι</quote> ( <ref n="Thuc. 2.51">Thuc. 2.51</ref> ), <quote xml:lang="grc">συλλαμβάνοντι κατὰ τὸ ὀρθόν</quote> (for one who rightly comprehends, <ref n="Hdt. 7.143">Her. 7.143</ref> ), <quote xml:lang="grc">τῷ ἁπτομένῳ οὐ θερμὸν ἦν</quote> ( <ref n="Thuc. 2.49">Thuc. 2.49</ref> ), etc. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δαίμονος_76"> , <foreign xml:lang="lat">sortis</foreign> : so <ref n="Soph. OC 1337">1337</ref> , and oft.: boldly in <cit> <bibl>fr. 587</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">μὴ σπεῖρε πολλοῖς τὸν παρόντα δαίμονα</quote> </cit>, sow not the rumour of thy fate abroad. </gloss>

data.quote:
μὴ σπεῖρε πολλοῖς τὸν παρόντα
									δαίμονα

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μὴ_ἄστυ_78">is a comforting parenthesis. <foreign xml:lang="grc">μὴ</foreign> is due to the preceding imperative <foreign xml:lang="grc">μέν’</foreign> : cp. <cit> <bibl n="Thuc. 1.124" ref="Thuc. 1.124">Thuc. I. 124</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ψηφίσασθε τὸν πόλεμον, μὴ φοβηθέντες τὸ αὐτίκα δεινόν</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Xen. Cyrop. 3.1.37" ref="Xen. Cyrop. 3.1.37"> Xen. <title>Cyr.</title> 3.1.37</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπάγου τὴν γυναῖκα καὶ τοὺς παῖδας, μηδὲν αὐτῶν καταθείς</quote> </cit>: but it has, in itself, almost the effect of a reassuring injunction, "do not suppose that I mean." We could not make <foreign xml:lang="grc">οἱ ἐνθάδ’ αὐτοῦ μὴ κατ’ ἄστυ δημόται</foreign> a single phrase, as=such of the folks as are not in the town, but here. </gloss>

data.quote:
ψηφίσασθε τὸν πόλεμον, μὴ φοβηθέντες
									τὸ αὐτίκα δεινόν

ἀπάγου τὴν γυναῖκα καὶ τοὺς παῖδας,
									μηδὲν αὐτῶν καταθείς

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐνθάδ_αὐτοῦ_78"> : <cit> <bibl>Solon fr. 36. 11</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τοὺς δ’ ἐνθάδ’ αὐτοῦ</quote> </cit>(in Attica, as opp. to abroad): so <ref>Eupolis fr. inc. 1. 4</ref> (where Bothe after Meineke badly points <foreign xml:lang="grc">τῶν ἐνθάδ’, αὐτοῦ</foreign> ), etc. The word <foreign xml:lang="grc">δημότης</foreign> in <ref n="Soph. Ant. 690"> <title>Ant.</title> 690</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Aj. 1071"> <title>Ai.</title> 1071</ref>=a common man as opp. to a chief. Here, as in Eur. (Aesch. has not the word) and Pind. ( <ref n="Pind. N. 7"> <title>Nem.</title> 7. 65</ref>), <foreign xml:lang="grc">δημόται</foreign> are the "citizens" generally; though in this place the term is tinged with the notion of "demesmen."</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἰ_χρή_80">. All our MSS. have <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἢ χρή</foreign> (which Campbell retains); but, as between <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἢ</foreign> and <foreign xml:lang="grc">εἰ</foreign> in such a case, their authority is small: thus in <ref n="Aesch. Lib. 994"> Aesch. <title>Cho.</title> 994</ref>, where <foreign xml:lang="grc">εἴτ’</foreign> is certain, L gives the senseless <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἤτ’</foreign> . <emph>Epic</emph> usage allows <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἠὲ</foreign> ( <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἢ</foreign> ), answered by <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἦε</foreign> ( <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἦ</foreign> ), in an indirect question: <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Il. 2.299" ref="Hom. Il. 2.299"> <title>Il.</title> 2.299</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="hexameter"> <l part="F">ὄφρα δαῶμεν</l> <l>ἢ ἐτεὸν Κάλχας μαντεύεται, ἦε καὶ οὐκί</l> </lg> </quote> </cit>. But is there any Attic example of this construction? Three instances are indeed alleged from Aesch. ( <ref n="Aesch. PB 780"> <title>P. V.</title> 780</ref>, <ref n="Aesch. Lib. 756"> <title>Cho.</title> 756</ref>, <ref n="Aesch. Lib. 890">890</ref> ), but they are most doubtful: see Appendix. Attic usage prescribed <foreign xml:lang="grc">εἰ</foreign> (or <foreign xml:lang="grc">εἴτε</foreign> ) as = <emph>whether</emph> , introducing the indirect question: the correlative <emph>or</emph> was usu. <foreign xml:lang="grc">εἴτε</foreign> , but sometimes, as here, <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἤ</foreign> . </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἡμὶν_81">, ethic dat.: do we find ourselves alone? Cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 62">62</ref> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐν_ἡσύχῳ_82">, in quiet case, nearly= <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἡσύχως</foreign> , as <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 1675" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg007:1675">1675</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν πυμάτῳ</quote> </cit>= "at the last": cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 384" ref="Soph. El. 384"> <title>El.</title> 384</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">νῦν γὰρ ἐν καλῷ φρονεῖν</quote> </cit> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μόνης_πέλας_83"> , <foreign xml:lang="lat">sc.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc"> οὔσης</quote> , a gen. absol. (we could not understand <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς ὄντι πέλας ἐμοῦ μόνης</quote> ): cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 1588">1588</ref> : <ref n="Soph. OT 966"> <title>O. T.</title> 966</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὧν ὑφηγητῶν</quote> , <foreign xml:lang="lat">sc.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc"> ὄντων</quote> . </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πότνιαι_84">, fitting in his mouth, as being esp. their name at Thebes <ref n="Soph. OC 43">(43)</ref> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δεινῶπες_84">: as looking sternly on sin <ref n="Soph. OC 42">(42)</ref> . The face of the Avengers is still terrible to his inner eye. Sophocles nowhere portrays the lineaments of the Furies, as Aesch. does ( <ref n="Aesch. Eum. 46"> <title>Eum.</title> 46-54</ref>), but he leaves on the mind an impression not less awful. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εὖτε_84">: <foreign xml:lang="grc">νῦν ἔκαμψα ἐπὶ ἕδρας</foreign> ( <emph>gen. sing.</emph> ) <foreign xml:lang="grc">ὑμῶν πρώτων</foreign> (possess. gen.) <foreign xml:lang="grc">τῆσδε γῆς</foreign> (partitive gen.). <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἐπί</foreign> can be so placed since <foreign xml:lang="grc">ὑμῶν</foreign> is possessive gen. (= <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑμετέρας</quote> ): cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 126">126</ref> , <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 177" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg004:177"> <title>O. T.</title> 177</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀκτὰν πρὸς ἑσπέρου θεοῦ.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔκαμψα_84">( <foreign xml:lang="lat">sc.</foreign> <foreign xml:lang="grc">γόνυ</foreign> ) absol., as <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Hec. 1079" ref="Eur. Hec. 1079"> Eur. <title>Hec.</title> 1079</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">πᾶ βῶ, πᾶ στῶ, πᾶ κάμψω;</quote> </cit> </gloss>

data.quote: πᾶ βῶ, πᾶ στῶ, πᾶ κάμψω;

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀγνώμονες_86">, without <foreign xml:lang="grc">γνώμη</foreign> , hence, <emph> inconsiderate</emph> ; and so, <emph>unfeeling</emph> : <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Trach. 473" ref="Soph. Trach. 473"> <title>Tr.</title> 473</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">φρονοῦσαν θνητὰ κοὐκ ἀγνώμονα</quote> </cit> , <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> not refusing to make allowance for human frailty. <cit> <bibl n="Xen. Mem. 2.8.5" ref="Xen. Mem. 2.8.5"> Xen. <title>Mem.</title> 2.8.5</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀγνώμονι κριτῇ περιτυχεῖν</quote> </cit>, to fall in with a judge who makes no allowance. But <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀγνώς</quote> = "undiscerning," <ref n="Soph. OT 677"> <title>O. T.</title> 677</ref>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐξέχρη_87">, since in Attic <foreign xml:lang="grc">χράω</foreign> contracts in <foreign xml:lang="grc">η</foreign> : <cit> <bibl n="Tyrt. 3.3" ref="Tyrt. 3.3">Tyrtaeus 3. 3 </bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="elegiac"> <l part="F">Ἀπόλλων</l> <l>χρυσοκόμης ἔχρη πίονος ἐξ ἀδύτου</l> </lg> </quote> </cit> : <ref n="Pind. O. 7"> Pind. <title>Ol.</title> 7.92</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔχρεον</quote>( <foreign xml:lang="lat">v.l.</foreign> <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἔχραον</foreign> ): <cit> <bibl n="Lucian Alex. 22" ref="Lucian Alex. 22"> Lucian <title>Alex.</title> 22 </bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔχρα καὶ ἐθέσπιζε</quote> </cit>(common dialect). <foreign xml:lang="grc"> <emph>τὰ</emph> πόλλ’</foreign>, cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 564" ref="Soph. El. 564"> <title>El.</title> 564</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰ πολλὰ πνεύματ’</quote> </cit>, those frequent winds. The prophecy was made to Oedipus at Delphi when he went thither in his youth from Corinth, to ask whether he was indeed the son of Polybus, the Corinthian king, and Meropè. The god did not solve his doubt,— <cit> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄλλα δ’ ἄθλια καὶ δεινὰ καὶ δύστηνα προὔφηνεν λέγων</quote> <bibl n="Soph. OT 789" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg004:789"> ( <title>O. T.</title> 789)</bibl> </cit>. Eur. makes Oedipus, while still at Thebes, tell Antigone of a <foreign xml:lang="grc">χρησμός</foreign> which doomed him to die at <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἱερὸς Κολωνός</foreign> ( <ref n="Eur. Phoen. 1705"> <title>Phoen.</title> 1705 ff.</ref>). Far more poetical is the conception of Sophocles, that Apollo had appointed the <emph>sign</emph> , but not named the <emph>place.</emph> </gloss>

data.quote:
ἄλλα δ’ ἄθλια καὶ δεινὰ καὶ δύστηνα
									προὔφηνεν λέγων

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ταύτην_παῦλαν_88">: spoke of this as a rest. The pronominal object of the verb, instead of being <foreign xml:lang="grc">τοῦτο</foreign> , is assimilated to the gender of the predicate <foreign xml:lang="grc">παῦλαν</foreign> : cp. <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Crat. 433E" ref="Plat. Crat. 433E"> Plat. <title>Crat.</title> 433E</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">λέγει... εἶναι ταύτην ὀρθότητα ὀνόματος, ξυνθήκην</quote> </cit>, he says that in <emph>this</emph> consists the correctness of a word,—convention: <cit> <bibl n="Lys. 12.37" ref="Lys. 12.37">Lysias or. 12 § 37</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ταύτην γὰρ ἐσχάτην δίκην δυνάμεθα παρ’ αὐτῶν λαβεῖν</quote> </cit>, <emph> this</emph> (death) is the extreme penalty which we can exact from them. </gloss>

data.quote:
λέγει... εἶναι ταύτην ὀρθότητα
									ὀνόματος, ξυνθήκην

ταύτην γὰρ ἐσχάτην δίκην δυνάμεθα παρ’
									αὐτῶν λαβεῖν

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐν_μακρῷ_88">: so <ref n="Soph. El. 330"> <title>El.</title> 330</ref>: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 422"> <title>Ant.</title> 422</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Phil. 235"> <title>Ph.</title> 235</ref>, etc.: but <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 1648" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg007:1648">1648</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">χρόνῳ βραχεῖ</quote> </cit> (without <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἐν</foreign> ). The general Attic rule was to use <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἐν</foreign> in such phrases as <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἐν πολλῷ, μακρῷ, ὀλίγῳ, βραχεῖ χρόνῳ, ἐν ὀλίγαις ἡμέραις, ἐν πολλοῖς ἔτεσιν</foreign> . The instances in which <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἐν</foreign> is omitted are comparatively rare in poetry, and very rare (usu. doubtful) in prose, with the exception of the phrase <foreign xml:lang="grc">ὑστέρῳ χρόνῳ</foreign> which in prose usu. lacks <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἐν</foreign> : it takes it, however, below at <ref n="Soph. OC 614">614</ref> and <ref n="Soph. Trach. 18"> <title>Tr.</title> 18</ref>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐλθόντι_βίον_89">. Apollo said: <quote xml:lang="grc">αὕτη παῦλά σοι ἔσται, ἐλθόντι χώραν τερμίαν, ὅπου ἂν λάβῃς θ. ς. ἕδραν καὶ ξενόστασιν· ἐνταῦθα κάμψεις κ.τ.λ.</quote> In the orat. obliqua, if the tense of the principal verb were primary (as <foreign xml:lang="grc">λέγει</foreign> ), <foreign xml:lang="grc">ὅπου ἂν λάβῃς</foreign> would become <foreign xml:lang="grc">ὅπου ἂν λάβω</foreign> : since it is secondary ( <foreign xml:lang="grc"> ἔλεξε</foreign> ), we have <foreign xml:lang="grc">ὅπου λάβοιμι</foreign> . The part. <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἐλθόντι</foreign> expresses the first condition to be fulfilled before the <foreign xml:lang="grc">παῦλα</foreign> can be attained. <foreign xml:lang="grc">ταύτην</foreign> is explained by <foreign xml:lang="grc"> <emph>ἐνταῦθα</emph> κάμψειν</foreign>. <foreign xml:lang="grc"> τερμίαν</foreign> is proleptic: in whatever land he should find the Semnae, that land was to be for him <foreign xml:lang="grc">τερμία</foreign> , <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> was to contain the goal of his wanderings. The word occurs elsewhere only in <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1331"> <title>Ant.</title> 1331</ref>, <quote xml:lang="grc">τερμίαν ἁμέραν</quote> , one's last day. It fits the metaphor of <foreign xml:lang="grc">κάμψειν</foreign> , from rounding the post in the <foreign xml:lang="grc">δίαυλος</foreign> ( <quote xml:lang="grc">κάμψαι διαύλου θάτερον κῶλον πάλιν</quote> , <ref n="Aesch. Ag. 344"> Aesch. <title> Ag.</title> 344</ref>), since <foreign xml:lang="grc">τέρμα</foreign> oft.= <foreign xml:lang="grc">νύσσα</foreign> or <foreign xml:lang="grc"> καμπτήρ</foreign> , the turning-post ( <ref n="Hom. Il. 23.466"> <title>Il.</title> 23.466</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">εὖ σχεθέειν περὶ τέρμα</quote>). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σεμνῶν_90">: see on <ref target="cl43">43</ref> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ξενόστασιν_90">, quarters for strangers. <cit> <bibl>Pollux 9. 50</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> μέρῃ δὲ καὶ πόλεως καὶ πανδοκεῖον καὶ ξενὼν καὶ ὡς ἐν Ἰνάχῳ Σοφοκλέους <note anchored="true" place="inline" resp="RCJ" xml:lang="eng">(a satyric drama, fr. 253)</note> , πανδόκος ξενόστασις</quote> </cit>. The word occurs only in these two places of Soph.: so <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἱππόστασις, βούστασις</foreign> .</gloss>

data.quote:
μέρῃ δὲ καὶ πόλεως καὶ πανδοκεῖον καὶ ξενὼν
							καὶ ὡς ἐν Ἰνάχῳ Σοφοκλέους   , πανδόκος ξενόστασις

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κέρδη_κτλ_92">: with advantages, through my having settled there ( <foreign xml:lang="grc">οἰκήσαντα</foreign> ), for my entertainers, and ruin for the Thebans. The conjecture <foreign xml:lang="grc">οἰκίσαντα</foreign> , "having founded," deserves to be carefully weighed. Cp. the poet. use of <foreign xml:lang="grc">κτίσας</foreign> below <ref n="Soph. OC 715"> (715)</ref> in regard to the invention of the curb: also <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. PB 250" ref="Aesch. PB 250"> Aesch. <title>PV</title> 250</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τυφλὰς ἐν αὐτοῖς ἐλπίδας κατῴκισα</quote> </cit>. On the other hand, the blessing to Attica turned on the <emph>personal residence</emph> of Oed. therein at the close of his life: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 626" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg007:626">626</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="trimeter"> <l part="F">κοὔποτ’ Οἰδίπουν ἐρεῖς</l> <l part="I">ἀχρεῖον οἰκητῆρα δέξασθαι</l> </lg> </quote> </cit>. This favours <foreign xml:lang="grc">οἰκήσαντα</foreign> . <foreign xml:lang="grc">κέρδη</foreign> and <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἄτην</foreign> , accusatives in appos. with the sentence <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἐνταῦθα κάμψειν τὸν βίον</foreign> : the participle <foreign xml:lang="grc"> οἰκήσαντα</foreign> (in antithesis with <foreign xml:lang="grc"> δεδεγμένοις</foreign> , cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 13" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg007:13">13</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ξένοι πρὸς ἀστῶν</quote> </cit>) serves to bring out the point on which the <foreign xml:lang="grc">κέρδη</foreign> and <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἄτη</foreign> depend. For the <emph>plur.</emph> acc. in appos. cp. <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Alc. 6" ref="Eur. Alc. 6"> Eur. <title>Alc.</title> 6</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="trimeter"> <l part="F">καί με θητεύειν πατὴρ</l> <l part="F">...τῶνδ’ ἄποιν’ ἠνάγκασεν</l> </lg> </quote> </cit>. This is better than to refer <foreign xml:lang="grc">κέρδη</foreign> and <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἄτην</foreign> to the person of Oed. ( <quote xml:lang="eng">"having dwelt there as a blessing"</quote> etc.), which would suit <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἄτην</foreign> , but hardly the plur. <foreign xml:lang="grc">κέρδη</foreign> ,—used here instead of <foreign xml:lang="grc">κέρδος</foreign> (cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 579">579</ref> ) because the "blessings" were to be felt in many ways and on many occasions (see <ref n="Soph. OC 1524">1524 ff.</ref> ). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοῖς_πέμψασιν_93">is supplemented by <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἀπήλασαν</foreign> , since <foreign xml:lang="grc">πέμπειν</foreign> can be said of those who "speed the parting guest": <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Od. 15.74" ref="Hom. Od. 15.74"> <title>Od.</title> 15.74</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">χρὴ ξεῖνον παρεόντα φιλεῖν ἐθέλοντα δὲ πέμπειν</quote> </cit>. </gloss>

data.quote:
χρὴ ξεῖνον παρεόντα φιλεῖν ἐθέλοντα δὲ
									πέμπειν

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="παρηγγύα_94">cannot mean "pledged,""promised" ( <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἠγγυᾶτο</foreign> ), but only "passed the watchword to me," <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> " <emph>told</emph> me, as <emph>a sign.</emph> " <cit> <bibl n="Xen. Cyrop. 3.3.58" ref="Xen. Cyrop. 3.3.58"> Xen. <title>Cyr.</title> 3.3.58</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">παρηγγύα ὁ Κῦρος σύνθημα, Ζεὺς σύμμαχος καὶ ἡγεμών</quote> </cit>, <quote xml:lang="eng">"C. proceeded to pass the watchword, 'Zeus'"</quote> , etc. <foreign xml:lang="grc">παρεγγυάω</foreign> regularly has this sense (which sometimes passes into that of "exhorting," "encouraging" one another); or else that of "putting something into another's hand," "entrusting" it to him. The omission of the temporal augment in L and other MSS. is not a sufficient ground for adopting Herwerden's <foreign xml:lang="grc">φερέγγυα</foreign> ("trusty"). </gloss>

data.quote:
παρηγγύα ὁ Κῦρος σύνθημα, Ζεὺς
									σύμμαχος καὶ ἡγεμών

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἢ_τιν_95">, some such sign as earthquake or thunder ( <foreign xml:lang="grc">τινά</foreign> with both): thunder is the sign given at <ref n="Soph. OC 1606">1606</ref> . <foreign xml:lang="grc">τιν’</foreign> suggests that the god spoke merely of "signs": Oed. interprets. Cp. <cit> <bibl> schol. Aristoph. <title>Ach.</title> 171</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">διοσημία δέ ἐστιν ὁ παρὰ καιρὸν χειμών</quote> </cit>. <cit> <bibl n="Plut. De Defect. 419F" ref="Plut. De Defect. 419F"> Plut. <title>Mor.</title> 419 F</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">σύγχυσιν μεγάλην περὶ τὸν ἀέρα καὶ διοσημίας πολλὰς γενέσθαι</quote> </cit> . </gloss>

data.quote:
διοσημία δέ ἐστιν ὁ παρὰ καιρὸν
									χειμών

σύγχυσιν μεγάλην περὶ τὸν ἀέρα καὶ
									διοσημίας πολλὰς γενέσθαι

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔγνωκα_μὲν_96">is answered <ref n="Soph. OC 101">(101)</ref> by <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἀλλά μοι...δότε</foreign> . <foreign xml:lang="grc">νυν</foreign> , "then," seems better than <foreign xml:lang="grc">νῦν</foreign> , (though this could stand,) since the oracle is the basis of his belief. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τήνδε_ὁδὸν_96">: acc. of extension in space (with <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἐξήγαγε</foreign> ), denoting the ground traversed: cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 1686">1686</ref> : <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Phil. 1223" ref="Soph. Phil. 1223"> <title>Ph.</title> 1223</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">κέλευθον ἕρπεις</quote> </cit> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐκ_οὐ_97">, which in grammatical order immediately follows <foreign xml:lang="grc"> ὡς</foreign> , can be thus placed because felt as one adverbial expression=" <emph>assuredly</emph> ": so often <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἔστιν ὅτε</foreign> (="sometimes"), <foreign xml:lang="grc">οὐκ ἔστιν ᾗ</foreign> ("in no wise"), <foreign xml:lang="grc">οὐδεὶς ὅστις οὐ</foreign> ("everybody"), etc. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πτερὸν_97">: no outward sign had been given. The "omen" was in the leading of his will. Cp. the feeling in the <title>Odyssey</title> (more spiritual here than the <title>Iliad</title> ) that the gods sometimes act directly on the human mind by inspiring a thought at a crisis. <ref n="Hom. Od. 16.282"> <title>Od.</title> 16.282</ref>(Odysseus to his son, when planning to slay the suitors) <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="hexameter"> <l>ὁππότε κεν πολύβουλος ἐνὶ φρεσὶ θήσει Ἀθήνη,</l> <l part="I">νεύσω μέν τοι ἐγὼ κεφαλῇ</l> </lg> </quote>: which anticipates such a <foreign xml:lang="grc">πτερόν</foreign> as is meant here. For <foreign xml:lang="grc">πτερόν</foreign> as= <foreign xml:lang="grc">οἰωνός</foreign> or <foreign xml:lang="grc">ὄρνις</foreign> (= <cit> <quote xml:lang="grc">πάνθ’ ὅσαπερ περὶ μαντείας διακρίνει</quote> <bibl n="Aristoph. Birds 719" ref="Aristoph. Birds 719"> Aristoph. <title>Av.</title> 719</bibl> </cit>), Schneidewin cp. <cit> <bibl n="Call. Lav.Pall. 124" ref="Call. Lav.Pall. 124"> Callimachus <title>Lav. Pall.</title> 124</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ποίων <note anchored="true" place="inline"> ( <quote xml:lang="grc">ὀρνίθων</quote> )</note>οὐκ ἀγαθαὶ πτέρυγες</quote> </cit>, <cit> <bibl n="Prop. 4.10.11" ref="Prop. 4.10.11">Propert. 4. 10. 11</bibl> <quote xml:lang="lat">felicibus edita pennis</quote> </cit>(with happy auguries). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐξήγαγ_98">, <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> "to my goal" ( <foreign xml:lang="grc"> ἐξ‐</foreign> ), not, "aside from the highway." <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Phaedo 66B" ref="Plat. Phaedo 66B"> Plat. <title>Phaedo</title> 66B</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">κινδυνεύει τοι ὥσπερ ἀτραπός τις ἐκφέρειν ἡμᾶς</quote> </cit>(and so <ref n="Soph. Aj. 7"> Soph. <title>Ai.</title> 7</ref>). </gloss>

data.quote:
κινδυνεύει τοι ὥσπερ ἀτραπός τις
									ἐκφέρειν ἡμᾶς

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐ_ἄν_98">, "for <emph>else</emph> ," etc., the suppressed protasis being <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ μὴ ἐξήγαγε</quote> : so <ref n="Soph. OC 125">125</ref> : <ref n="Soph. OT 82"> <title>O. T.</title> 82</ref>, and Appendix to <ref n="Soph. OT"> <title>O. T.</title> </ref>p. 221. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="νήφων_ἀοίνοις_100">; the austere wanderer lights first on the shrine of the austere goddesses ( <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς αἰεὶ τὸν ὁμοῖον ἄγει θεὸς ὡς τὸν ὁμοῖον</quote> ); <foreign xml:lang="grc">νήφων</foreign> implying the thought that he has been in a manner consecrated to suffering. Water, and honey mixed with milk ( <quote xml:lang="grc">μελίκρατον</quote> ), formed the <cit> <quote xml:lang="grc">χοὰς ἀοίνους, νηφάλια μειλίγματα</quote> <bibl n="Aesch. Eum. 107" ref="Aesch. Eum. 107"> (Aesch. <title>Eum.</title> 107)</bibl> </cit>of the Furies. <cit> <bibl n="Poll. 6.26" ref="Poll. 6.26">Pollux 6. 26</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ γὰρ νηφαλιεύειν τὸ νηφάλια θύειν ἔλεγον, ὅπερ ἐστὶ τὸ χρῆσθαι θυσίαις ἀοίνοις, ὧν τὰς ἐναντίας θυσίας οἰνοσπόνδους ἔλεγον</quote> </cit>. Photius <foreign xml:lang="lat">s. v.</foreign> <foreign xml:lang="grc">νηφάλιοι θυσίαι</foreign> , <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν αἷς οἶνος οὐ σπένδεται, ἀλλὰ ὕδωρ καὶ μελίκρατον</quote> . </gloss>

data.quote:
τὸ γὰρ νηφαλιεύειν τὸ νηφάλια θύειν
									ἔλεγον, ὅπερ ἐστὶ τὸ χρῆσθαι θυσίαις ἀοίνοις, ὧν τὰς
									ἐναντίας θυσίας οἰνοσπόνδους ἔλεγον

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀσκέπαρνον_101"> (cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 19">19</ref> ), not shaped by the adze ( <cit> <quote xml:lang="grc">σκέπαρνος,</quote> <bibl>fr. 724</bibl> </cit>): so Soph. is quoted by Hesychius <ref n="Hsch. 1.90">(1. 90)</ref> for <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀδρέπανον</quote> (from <quote xml:lang="grc">δρεπάνη</quote> ). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="βίου_καταστροφήντινα_102">, some ending of life,—some close to my course. <foreign xml:lang="grc">βίου πέρασις</foreign> is <foreign xml:lang="grc">τὸ περᾶν τὸν βίον</foreign> , a passing through life to its end, a concluding of it ( <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Andr. 101" ref="Eur. Andr. 101"> Eur. <title>Andr.</title> 101</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὴν τελευταίαν...περάσας ἡμέραν</quote> </cit>): <foreign xml:lang="grc">καταστροφή</foreign> adds the notion of a career which approaches its goal. <ref n="Thuc. 2.42">Thuc. 2.42</ref> (of those who had fallen in the war) <quote xml:lang="grc">δοκεῖ δέ μοι δηλοῦν ἀνδρὸς ἀρετὴν πρώτη τε μηνύουσα καὶ τελευταία βεβαιοῦσα ἡ νῦν τῶνδε καταστροφή</quote> (the closing scene of their lives). <cit> <bibl n="Plb. 5.54" ref="Plb. 5.54">Polyb. 5. 54</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὴν αὐτὴν ἐποιήσαντο τοῦ βίου καταστροφήν</quote> </cit>. </gloss>

data.quote:
τὴν αὐτὴν ἐποιήσαντο τοῦ βίου
									καταστροφήν

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὀμφὰς_102">: see on <ref target="cl550">550</ref> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μειόνως_ἔχειν_104"> = <quote xml:lang="grc">μείων εἶναι</quote> . This euphemistic mode of expression with the comparative adverb is often found where censure or disparagement is to be conveyed less bluntly. <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Phaedo 75A" ref="Plat. Phaedo 75A"> Plat. <title>Phaedo</title> 75A</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὀρέγεται μὲν πάντα ταῦτα εἶναι οἷον τὸ ἴσον, ἔχει δὲ ἐνδεεστέρως</quote> </cit>(repeated just afterwards thus, <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐτοῦ ἐνδεέστερά ἐστιν</quote> ): <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Apol. 34C" ref="Plat. Apol. 34C"> <title>Apol.</title> 34 C</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τάχ’ ἂν οὖν τις ταῦτα ἐννοήσας αὐθαδέστερον ἂν πρός με σχοίη</quote> </cit>, = <foreign xml:lang="grc">αὐθαδέστερος ἂν εἴη</foreign> : <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Laws 932A" ref="Plat. Laws 932A"> <title>Legg.</title> 932 A</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐάν τις ἐν τῇδε τῇ πόλει γονέων ἀμελέστερον ἔχῃ τοῦ δέοντος</quote> </cit> = <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἀμελέστερος ᾖ</foreign> . Oedipus says to the Furies: "Grant me rest, unless haply ( <foreign xml:lang="grc">τι</foreign> , adv., as <ref n="Soph. OT 969"> <title>O. T.</title> 969</ref>, here with bitter irony) I seem <emph>to be beneath such grace</emph> ,—I, who have suffered so much and so long." <foreign xml:lang="grc">μειόνως ἔχειν</foreign> means here to be <foreign xml:lang="grc">μείων</foreign> in the sense of " <emph>too insignificant</emph> ," " <emph>of too little account</emph> ," in respect of suffering: <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> , one who <emph>has not yet suffered enough.</emph> Thus we arrive at the same <emph>sense</emph> which the scholiast extorts by a <emph>method</emph> which seems impossible. He explains <foreign xml:lang="grc">μειόνως ἔχειν</foreign> as = <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἐλαττόνως ἔχειν τὰ κακά</foreign> , "to <emph>have ills</emph> in too small a degree." But (1) as Herm. said, this would be <foreign xml:lang="grc">μεῖον</foreign> or <foreign xml:lang="grc">μείω ἔχειν</foreign> , and (2) it is impossible to <emph>understand</emph> <foreign xml:lang="grc">τὰ κακά</foreign> . Campbell thinks that <foreign xml:lang="grc">μειόνως ἔχειν λατρεύων</foreign> = <foreign xml:lang="grc">μειόνως ἔχειν λατρευμάτων</foreign> : which is open to objection (1), and also to this, that the partic. <foreign xml:lang="grc">λατρεύων</foreign> could not do duty for a partitive gen. after <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἔχειν</foreign> . Wecklein (who follows the schol.) suggests <foreign xml:lang="grc">μειόνως ἔχειν κακῶν</foreign> , and <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἀεί</foreign> for <foreign xml:lang="grc"> βροτῶν</foreign> in <ref n="Soph. OC 105">105</ref> . </gloss>

data.quote:
ὀρέγεται μὲν πάντα ταῦτα εἶναι οἷον τὸ
									ἴσον, ἔχει δὲ ἐνδεεστέρως

τάχ’ ἂν οὖν τις ταῦτα ἐννοήσας
									αὐθαδέστερον ἂν πρός με σχοίη

ἐάν τις ἐν τῇδε τῇ πόλει γονέων
									ἀμελέστερον ἔχῃ τοῦ δέοντος

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μόχθοις_λατρεύων_105"> : <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. Ag. 217" ref="Aesch. Ag. 217"> Aesch. <title>Ag.</title> 217</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνάγκας ἔδυ λέπαδνον</quote> </cit> : <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Supp. 877" ref="Eur. Supp. 877"> Eur. <title>Suppl.</title> 877</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">χρημάτων ζευχθεὶς ὕπο</quote> </cit>(in bonds to lucre). <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Trach. 357" ref="Soph. Trach. 357"> <title>Tr.</title> 357</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">πόνων λατρεύματα</quote> </cit> (servitude <emph>in</emph> toils) is not similar. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἴτ_106">, in urgent petition, as <ref n="Soph. OC 248">248</ref> , <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 46" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg004:46"> <title>O. T.</title> 46</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἴθ’...ἀνόρθωσον</quote> </cit> : <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 1413" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg004:1413">1413</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἴτ’, ἀξιώσατ</quote> </cit> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γλυκεῖαι_106">, with blandishment, as <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Trach. 1040" ref="Soph. Trach. 1040"> <title>Tr.</title> 1040</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὦ γλυκὺς Ἅιδας</quote> </cit>. No other poet of the class. age (I think) ventures on this use of <quote xml:lang="grc">γλυκύς</quote> in addressing deities, which, indeed, is somewhat apt to recall the Aristophanic <quote xml:lang="grc">ὦ γλύκων, ὦ γλυκύτατε</quote> . </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Σκότου_106">: on <ref target="cl40">40</ref> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Παλλάδος_107">, possessive gen. with <foreign xml:lang="grc">καλούμεναι</foreign> : Athens, thou that art said to belong to Pallas, of all cities most honoured: <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Ion 8" ref="Eur. Ion 8"> Eur. <title>Ion</title> 8</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="trimeter"> <l>ἔστιν γὰρ οὐκ ἄσημος Ἑλλήνων πόλις,</l> <l>τῆς χρυσολόγχου Παλλάδος κεκλημένη</l> </lg> </quote> </cit> : <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Ion 311" ref="Eur. Ion 311"> <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 311</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">Λοξίου κεκλήμεθα</quote> </cit>, I am called (the servant) of Apollo. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἴδωλον_110"> (cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 393">393</ref> ), a mere wraith, with the semblance and speech of the man, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀτὰρ φρένες οὐκ ἔνι πάμπαν</quote> , but the living heart is not therein (as Achilles says of the <quote xml:lang="grc">εἴδωλον</quote> of Patroclus, <ref n="Hom. Il. 23.104"> <title>Il.</title> 23.104</ref>). So the wraith of Helen is <cit> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἴδωλον ἔμπνουν,</quote> <bibl n="Eur. Hel. 34" ref="Eur. Hel. 34"> Eur. <title>Helen</title> 34</bibl> </cit> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐ_γ_110">. After <quote xml:lang="grc">τόδ’</quote> in <ref n="Soph. OC 109">109</ref> a second <foreign xml:lang="grc">τόδ’</foreign> here would be very awkward: and the article, if not necessary, is at least desirable. <foreign xml:lang="grc">οὐ γὰρ δή</foreign> is esp. used in rejecting an alternative to something already stated, and <foreign xml:lang="grc">γε</foreign> is often added with the force of "at any rate"; below, <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 265" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg007:265">265</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="trimeter"> <l part="F">οὐ γὰρ δὴ τό γε</l> <l part="I">σῶμ’</l> </lg> </quote> </cit> : <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 1020" ref="Soph. El. 1020"> <title>El.</title> 1020</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ γὰρ δὴ κενόν γ’ ἀφήσομεν</quote> </cit> : <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Phil. 246" ref="Soph. Phil. 246"> <title>Ph.</title> 246</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ γὰρ δὴ σύ γ’ ἦσθα ναυβάτης</quote> </cit>. On the other hand <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ γὰρ δή</quote> <emph>without</emph> <quote xml:lang="grc">γε</quote> occurs <ref n="Soph. OT 576"> <title>O. T.</title> 576</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Ant. 46"> <title>Ant.</title> 46</ref>. </gloss>

data.quote: οὐ γὰρ δὴ κενόν γ’ ἀφήσομεν
οὐ γὰρ δὴ σύ γ’ ἦσθα ναυβάτης

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="χρόνῳ_112">, dat. of circumstance with <foreign xml:lang="grc">παλαιοί</foreign> , old in respect of their years, <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> "aged." The phrase (an unusual one) does not seem to be intensive, as Campbell makes it, "very old" ( <quote xml:lang="grc">γονῇ γενναῖε</quote> in <ref n="Soph. OT 1469"> <title>O. T.</title> 1469</ref>is not similar), but simply pleonastic, as in <ref n="Hom. Od. 13.432"> <title>Od.</title> 13.432</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">παλαιοῦ... γέροντος</quote>, an old man of many years. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐπίσκοποι_112"> here= <foreign xml:lang="lat">speculatores</foreign> , explorers, but in <ref n="Soph. Ant. 217"> <title>Ant.</title> 217</ref>overseers, watchers, and <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1148"> <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 1148</ref>of Dionysus, "master" (of mystic rites). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καὶ_κρύψον_113">all MSS. (1) This is usu. explained by partitive apposition ( <foreign xml:lang="grc">σχῆμα καθ’ ὅλον καὶ μέρος</foreign> ), the part <foreign xml:lang="grc">πόδα</foreign> being in appos. with the whole <foreign xml:lang="grc">με</foreign> : "Hide me,—that is, my foot,—apart from the road." The construction is common ( <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Phil. 1301" ref="Soph. Phil. 1301"> <title>Ph.</title> 1301</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">μέθες με...χεῖρα</quote> </cit> , <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Il. 11.240" ref="Hom. Il. 11.240"> Hom. <title>Il.</title> 11.240</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸν δ’ ἄορι πλῆξ’ αὐχένα</quote> </cit>): the question here is as to the sense. <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἄγαγέ με πόδα</foreign> could bear such a sense: but <foreign xml:lang="grc"> <emph>κρύψον</emph> με πόδα</foreign>cannot do so, unless we grant that <foreign xml:lang="grc">κρύπτειν πόδα</foreign> could mean "to <emph> guide</emph> another's steps <emph>to</emph> a hiding place." Wholly different is <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Hec. 812" ref="Eur. Hec. 812"> Eur. <title>Hec.</title> 812</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ποῖ μ’ ὑπεξάγεις πόδα;</quote> </cit>"whither art thou withdrawing thy steps from me?" = <foreign xml:lang="grc">ποῖ με φεύγεις</foreign> ; (2) Paley thinks that <foreign xml:lang="grc"> πόδα</foreign> is "quite redundantly used," as if <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἄγουσα</foreign> had been part of the sentence. The evidence cited for a "redundant" use of <foreign xml:lang="grc">πόδα</foreign> consists in ( <emph>a</emph> ) the phrase <foreign xml:lang="grc">βαίνω πόδα</foreign> , <ref n="Eur. El. 1173"> Eur. <title>El.</title> 1173</ref>etc., where <foreign xml:lang="grc">βαίνω</foreign> is trans.: ( <emph>b</emph> ) one place, <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Alc. 1153" ref="Eur. Alc. 1153"> Eur. <title>Alc.</title> 1153</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλ’ εὐτυχοίης, νόστιμον δ’ ἔλθοις πόδα</quote> </cit>: where, <emph>if</emph> right, <foreign xml:lang="grc">π.</foreign> is a bold cognate acc., come with returning foot: but <foreign xml:lang="grc">ὁδόν</foreign> and <foreign xml:lang="grc">δόμον</foreign> are <foreign xml:lang="lat">vv. ll.</foreign> (3) Campbell takes <foreign xml:lang="grc">με</foreign> as governed, <foreign xml:lang="grc">πρὸς τὸ σημαινόμενον</foreign> , by <foreign xml:lang="grc">κρύψον πόδα</foreign> as= <foreign xml:lang="grc"> ὑπέξαγε</foreign> : but this involves the difficulty noticed under (1). I regard as probable H. Keck's <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἐκποδὼν ὁδοῦ</foreign> . Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Phoen. 978" ref="Eur. Phoen. 978"> Eur. <title>Phoen.</title> 978</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">χθονὸς τῆσδ’ ἐκποδών</quote> </cit>. No substitute for <foreign xml:lang="grc">πόδα</foreign> is satisfactory: among the conjectures are <foreign xml:lang="grc">κόρα, μέ ποι, πάλιν, πέλας, πέρα, πρόσω, τάχα, τόδε, τόδ’ ἄψ</foreign> . </gloss>

data.quote:
ἀλλ’ εὐτυχοίης, νόστιμον δ’ ἔλθοις
									πόδα

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τῶνδ_λόγουςἐροῦσιν_114">, learn <emph>in regard to these men</emph> what they will say; not, learn <emph>from</emph> them (by speaking to them), since his present object is only to overhear them, unseen. This gen. of connection often goes thus with verbs of perceiving, etc.: <cit> <bibl n="Xen. Mem. 3.6.17" ref="Xen. Mem. 3.6.17"> Xen. <title>Mem.</title> 3.6.17</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐνθυμοῦ τῶν εἰδότων ὅτι λέγουσι</quote> </cit>. <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Gorg. 517C" ref="Plat. Gorg. 517C"> Plat. <title>Gorg.</title> 517C</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀγνοοῦντες ἀλλήλων ὅτι λέγομεν</quote> </cit>. Distinguish <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 593" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg007:593">593</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅταν μάθῃς μου νουθέτει,</quote> </cit>when thou hast learnt <emph>from</emph> me. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐν_μαθεῖν_115"> : <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> "for in learning (how the people of the place are disposed) consists the caution of (proper for) all that we are doing": we are poor strangers, who must be prepared to shape our course according to the mood of the <cit> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀστοί</quote> <bibl n="Soph. OC 13" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg007:13">(13)</bibl> </cit>. The spondee can stand in the 5th place, since <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἐν</foreign> , to which <foreign xml:lang="grc">γάρ</foreign> adheres, itself coheres closely with <foreign xml:lang="grc">τῷ μαθεῖν</foreign> : so <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 376" ref="Soph. El. 376"> <title>El.</title> 376</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ γὰρ τῶνδέ μοι</quote> </cit>(where, as here, Elms. proposed <foreign xml:lang="grc">δὲ</foreign> instead of <foreign xml:lang="grc">γὰρ</foreign> ): <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 409" ref="Soph. El. 409"> <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 409</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τῷ τοῦτ’ ἤρεσεν</quote> </cit>; cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 664">664</ref> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τῶν_ποιουμένων_116">: so <ref n="Soph. El. 84"> <title>El.</title> 84</ref>(just before an exit, as here): <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="trimeter"> <l part="F">ταῦτα γὰρ φέρει</l> <l>νίκην τ’ ἐφ’ ἡμῖν καὶ κράτος τῶν δρωμένων</l> </lg> </quote>. The <foreign xml:lang="grc">γνώμη</foreign> here, though perhaps meant to mark the caution taught by bitter experience (cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 273">273</ref> ), has the tone of Periclean Athens: cp. <ref n="Thuc. 2.40">Thuc. 2.40</ref> (it is a mischief) <quote xml:lang="grc">μὴ προδιδαχθῆναι...λόγῳ πρότερον ἢ ἐπὶ ἃ δεῖ ἔργῳ ἐλθεῖν</quote> : <ref n="Thuc. 3.42">3. 42</ref> (Diodotus answering Cleon) <quote xml:lang="grc">τοὺς...λόγους... διδασκάλους τῶν πραγμάτων</quote> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὅρα_117">: cp. <ref n="Aesch. Eum. 255"> Aesch. <title>Eum.</title> 255</ref>(the Furies hunting Orestes): <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="lyric"> <l>ὅρα, ὅρα μάλ’ αὖ λεῦσσέ τε πάντα, μὴ</l> <l>λάθῃ φύγδα βὰς ματροφόνος ἀτίτας</l> </lg> </quote>: cp. also the scene in which the Chorus of the <title>Ajax</title> are seeking the hero ( <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 867" ref="Soph. Aj. 867">867</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="lyric"> <l>πᾶ πᾶ</l> <l>πᾶ γὰρ οὐκ ἔβαν ἐγώ;</l> </lg> </quote> </cit> ). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τίς_ἦν_117">imperf. of previous mention (not implying that he <emph>is</emph> not still trespassing): who was he of whom our informant spoke? <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Crito 47D" ref="Plat. Crito 47D"> Plat. <title>Crito</title> 47D</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὃ τῷ μὲν δικαίῳ βέλτιον ἐγίγνετο <note anchored="true" place="inline" resp="RCJ" xml:lang="eng"> (is, <emph>as we agreed</emph>as we agreed</note>, τῷ δὲ ἀδίκῳ ἀπώλλυτο</quote> </cit>. Slightly different is the imperf. of a truth newly seen: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Phil. 978" ref="Soph. Phil. 978"> <title>Ph.</title> 978</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="trimeter"> <l part="F">ὅδ’ ἦν ἄρα</l> <l part="I">ὁ ξυλλαβών με</l> </lg> </quote> </cit>, "so (all the time) this was he who has seized me." </gloss>

data.quote: ὃ τῷ μὲν δικαίῳ βέλτιον ἐγίγνετο  , τῷ δὲ ἀδίκῳ ἀπώλλυτο

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ναίει_117">, of mere <emph>situation</emph> (not habitation), as <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Il. 2.626" ref="Hom. Il. 2.626"> <title>Il.</title> 2.626</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">νήσων αἳ ναίουσι πέρην ἁλός</quote> </cit>: so <ref n="Soph. Aj. 597"> <title>Ai.</title> 597</ref>(of Salamis), and <ref n="Soph. Trach. 99"> <title>Tr.</title> 99</ref>(of a wanderer). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐκτόπιος_119">instead of <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἐκ τόπου</foreign> : <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 716" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg007:716">716</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἁλία...πλάτα θρώσκει</quote> </cit> : <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 1340" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg004:1340"> <title>O. T.</title> 1340</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπάγετ’ ἐκτόπιον</quote> </cit> : <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 1411" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg004:1411">1411</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">θαλάσσιον ἐκρίψατ’</quote> </cit> : <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Ant. 785" ref="Soph. Ant. 785"> <title>Ant.</title> 785</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">φοιτᾷς δ’ ὑπερπόντιος</quote> </cit> : <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 419" ref="Soph. El. 419"> <title>El.</title> 419</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐφέστιον πῆξαι</quote> </cit> : <cit> <bibl n="Eur. IT 1424" ref="Eur. IT 1424"> Eur. <title>I. T.</title> 1424</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">παράκτιοι δραμεῖσθε</quote> </cit> . <cit> <bibl n="Plut. Dio 25" ref="Plut. Dio 25"> Plut. <title>Dion</title> 25</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">πελάγιοι πρὸς τὴν Σικελίαν ἔφευγον</quote> </cit>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀκορέστατος_120">, "most insatiate" ( <foreign xml:lang="grc">κόρος</foreign> ); hence, reckless of due limit,— shameless: cp. <cit> <quote xml:lang="lat"> <lg type="hexameter"> <l part="F">improbus annis</l> <l part="I">atque mero fervens</l> </lg> </quote> <bibl n="Juv. 3.282" ref="Juv. 3.282">(Iuv. 3. 282)</bibl> </cit> . <ref n="Eur. Her. 926"> Eur. <title>Her.</title> 926</ref> (deprecating <foreign xml:lang="grc">ὕβρις</foreign> ), <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="lyric"> <l>μήποτ’ ἐμὸν φρόνημα</l> <l>ψυχά τ’ ἀκόρεστος εἴη</l> </lg> </quote>. A positive <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἀκορής</foreign> is found in later Greek ( <ref>Themistius, or. 90 D</ref> , <date from="0300" to="0400">4th cent. A.D.</date> ): and as <foreign xml:lang="grc">διακορής</foreign> and <foreign xml:lang="grc">κατακορής</foreign> are classical (Plato, etc.), it may be a mere accident that <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἀκορής</foreign> has no earlier warrant. If referred to <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἀκόρεστος</foreign> , the superl. would be a poet. form like <foreign xml:lang="grc">νέατος, μέσατος</foreign> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="προσπεύθου_122">(only here) ought to mean "ask, or learn, <emph>further</emph> " (the reg. sense of <foreign xml:lang="grc">προσπυνθάνεσθαι, προσερωτᾶν</foreign> ), but this is weak: here, it seems rather to mean, " <emph>press</emph> the inquiry," inquire <emph>assiduously:</emph> cp. <foreign xml:lang="grc">προσαιτεῖν, προσλιπαρεῖν</foreign> . <foreign xml:lang="grc">προσφθέγγου</foreign> ("speak to him"), a <foreign xml:lang="lat">v.l.</foreign> for <foreign xml:lang="grc">προσπεύθου</foreign> , is plainly unsuitable. Hermann transposed <foreign xml:lang="grc"> προσδέρκου</foreign> and <foreign xml:lang="grc">προσπεύθου</foreign> : but the "looking" naturally precedes the "asking," and <foreign xml:lang="grc">πανταχῇ</foreign> suits both. The conjectures <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="lyric"> <l>λεῦσσ’ αὐτόν, προσδρακοῦ,</l> <l>προσπεύθου</l> </lg> </quote> (or <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="lyric"> <l>προσπυθοῦ,</l> <l>προσδέρκου</l> </lg> </quote>) are open not only to the objection from the sense of <foreign xml:lang="grc">λεύσσειν</foreign> <ref n="Soph. OC 121">(121)</ref> , but also to this, that the aor. is less fitting here. As to <foreign xml:lang="grc">προσδρακοῦ</foreign> , <foreign xml:lang="grc"> ἐδρακόμην</foreign> in <ref> <title>Anth. Pal.</title> 7. 224</ref>is a very rare example of that form. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πλανάτας_123">, one who has wandered hither from beyond our borders, and so = <foreign xml:lang="grc">ξένος</foreign> : cp. on <ref target="cl3">3</ref> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="προσέβα_ἄν_125">: cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 98">98</ref> : for the place of <foreign xml:lang="grc">οὐκ</foreign> , <ref n="Soph. Ant. 96"> <title>Ant.</title> 96</ref>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄλσος_ἐς_126">: see on <ref target="cl84">84</ref> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀμαιμακετᾶν_127">: used by the poets of any violent force, divine or elemental, with which men cannot cope (as the Chimaera, <ref n="Hom. Il. 6.179"> <title>Il.</title> 6.179</ref>; Artemis in her wrath, <ref n="Pind. P. 3"> Pind. <title>Pyth.</title> 3.33</ref>; the sea, <ref n="Pind. P. 1"> <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 1.14</ref>; fire, <ref n="Soph. OT 177"> <title>O. T.</title> 177</ref>), and probably <emph>associated</emph> with <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἄμαχος</foreign> . But the reduplication recalls <foreign xml:lang="grc">μαι‐μά‐ω</foreign> (cp. <foreign xml:lang="grc">πορ‐φύρ‐ω, ποι‐πνύ‐ω</foreign> ), —the <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἀ</foreign> being intensive: and if we suppose a secondary development of √MA as <foreign xml:lang="grc">μακ</foreign> ( <ref> Fennell on Pind. <title>P.</title> 1. 14</ref>), the proper sense of <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἀμαιμάκετος</foreign> would be " <emph>very furious.</emph> " The word being of epic coinage, it is conceivable that associations with <foreign xml:lang="grc">μάχομαι</foreign> may have influenced the formation as well as the usage. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καὶ_κτλ_130">In approaching or passing a shrine, it was usual to salute ( <foreign xml:lang="grc">προσκυνεῖν</foreign> ), and to invoke the deity audibly. But in passing the grove of the Eumenides the people of Colonus avoid looking towards it. No <emph>sound</emph> , no articulate word, escapes them. Their lips only <emph>move</emph> in sign of the prayer which the mind conceives. Cp. on <ref target="cl489">489</ref> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸ_φροντίδοςἱέντες_130">= "moving the lips of (in) reverently-mute thought"; <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἱέναι</foreign> (instead of <foreign xml:lang="grc">οἴγειν, λύειν, διαίρειν</foreign> ) <foreign xml:lang="grc">στόμα</foreign> has been suggested by the phrases <foreign xml:lang="grc">φωνὴν</foreign> (or <foreign xml:lang="grc">γλῶσσαν</foreign> ) <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἱέναι</foreign> : cp. <ref>fr. 844. 3</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πολλὴν γλῶσσαν ἐκχέας μάτην</quote> . This is better than to make <foreign xml:lang="grc">στόμα</foreign> purely figurative (like <quote xml:lang="eng">"the still, small voice"</quote> ), when the sense would be, "giving a (still) voice to our reverent thought," <foreign xml:lang="grc">εὐφάμου</foreign> (= <emph> silent</emph> ) qualifying the metaphor as when discord is called <quote xml:lang="grc">πῦρ ἀνήφαιστον</quote> , <ref n="Eur. Orest. 621"> Eur. <title>Or.</title> 621</ref>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀφώνως_131">. The ancient custom was to pray aloud, partly from a feeling that one ought not to make any prayer which might not be heard by all <emph> mortals.</emph> Pythagoras in <ref> Clemens Alex. <title>Strom.</title> 4. 26 § 173</ref>(it is usual <foreign xml:lang="grc">μετὰ φωνῆς εὔχεσθαι</foreign> ) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐμοὶ δοκεῖ, οὐχ ὅτι τὸ θεῖον ᾤοντο μὴ δύνασθαι τῶν ἡσυχῇ φθεγγομένων ἐπαΐειν, ἀλλ’ ὅτι δικαίας ἐβούλοντο εἶναι τὰς εὐχάς, ἃς οὐκ ἄν τις αἰδεσθείη ποιεῖσθαι πολλῶν συνειδότων</quote> . <cit> <bibl n="Pers. 2.6" ref="Pers. 2.6">Persius 2. 6</bibl> <quote xml:lang="lat"> <lg type="hexameter"> <l>Haud cuivis promptum est murmurque humilesque susurros</l> <l>Tollere de templis et aperto vivere voto.</l> </lg> </quote> </cit> <cit> <bibl n="Luc. 5.104" ref="Luc. 5.104">Lucan 5. 104</bibl> <quote xml:lang="lat">tacito mala vota susurro Concipiunt.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐδὲν_134">, <foreign xml:lang="lat">sc.</foreign> <foreign xml:lang="grc">αὐτάς</foreign> : <foreign xml:lang="grc">οὐδὲν ἅζονθ’</foreign> as="reverencing <emph> nothing</emph> " would be at least unusual. The act. of <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἅζομαι</foreign> occurs only here; but that fact scarcely seems to warrant a change. If any were made, the simplest would be <foreign xml:lang="grc">οὐδὲν ἄγονθ’</foreign> (in the sense of <foreign xml:lang="grc">θεοὺς ἄγειν</foreign> ), with <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἔχεις</foreign> in <ref n="Soph. OC 166">166</ref> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὃν_135">with <foreign xml:lang="grc">γνῶναι</foreign> only: <foreign xml:lang="grc"> λεύσσων</foreign> absol.: see on <ref target="cl121">121</ref> . </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μοι_137">ethic dat. ( <ref n="Soph. OC 62">62</ref> , <ref n="Soph. OC 81">81</ref> ) </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ναίει_137"> <ref n="Soph. OC 117">117</ref>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐκεῖνος_138">, of whom ye were speaking: <ref n="Soph. Ant. 384"> <title>Ant.</title> 384</ref>: <ref n="Aristoph. Ach. 41"> Aristoph. <title>Ach.</title> 41</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῦτ’ ἐκεῖν’ οὑγὼ )λεγον</quote>: <ref n="Aristoph. Cl. 1167"> <title>Nub.</title> 1167</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅδ’ ἐκεῖνος ἀνήρ</quote>: <ref n="Soph. El. 665"> <title>El.</title> 665</ref>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φωνῇ_ὁρῳ_138">: (I appear to you), <emph>for</emph> in sound is my sight ( <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> I know your presence by your voices). To this announcement of his blindness a certain gentle pathos is added by <foreign xml:lang="grc">τὸ φατιζόμ</foreign> . (acc. in appos.), "as they say of us the blind": alluding generally, perh., to the fig. use of <foreign xml:lang="grc">ὁρᾶν, βλέπειν</foreign> in ref. to mental sight (as <ref n="Soph. OT 747"> <title>O. T.</title> 747</ref>, of the blind seer, <quote xml:lang="grc">δέδοικα μὴ βλέπων ὁ μάντις ᾖ</quote> ), rather than to any special proverb. So <ref n="Thuc. 7.87">Thuc. 7.87</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πανωλεθρίᾳ δή, τὸ λεγόμενον,...οὐδὲν ὅτι οὐκ ἀπώλετο</quote> , referring merely to the phrase. <ref n="Dem. 25.89">[Dem.] or. 25 § 89</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὥσπερ τὸ τῆς παροιμίας, ὁρῶντας μὴ ὁρᾶν καὶ ἀκούοντας μὴ ἀκούειν</quote> . We must not render (1) with the schol., "I understand by sound what ye mean," <foreign xml:lang="grc">τὸ λεγόμενον παρ’ ὑμῶν</foreign> , nor (2) with Ellendt, <quote xml:lang="eng">"I perceive what is uttered by your voice."</quote> The pause saves the short final of <foreign xml:lang="grc">φατιζόμενον</foreign> from being a breach of synaphea: cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 143">143</ref> ( <quote xml:lang="grc"> πρέσβυς</quote> ;): <ref n="Soph. Ant. 932"> <title>Ant.</title> 932</ref>( <quote xml:lang="grc">ὕπερ</quote> ). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὁρᾶν_κλύειν_141">, epexegetic inf., like <foreign xml:lang="grc">χαλεπὸς συζῆν</foreign> ( <ref n="Plat. Stat. 302B"> Plat. <title>Polit.</title> 302B</ref>). The cry which bursts from the Chorus merely utters their horror at first <emph> seeing</emph> and <emph>hearing</emph> the wretch who has dared so great an impiety;—they have not yet had time to scan the traces of misery which the blind man's form exhibits (cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 286">286</ref> ). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="προσίδητ_ἄνομον_142">, regard <emph>as</emph> lawless: schol. <foreign xml:lang="grc">λείπει τὸ ὡς</foreign> . The omission is remarkable. Doederlein cp. <ref n="Thuc. 2.72">Thuc. 2.72</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">δέχεσθε δὲ ἀμφοτέρους φίλους</quote> , which is less bold: so, too, is <ref n="Soph. OT 412"> <title>O. T.</title> 412</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τυφλόν μ’ ὠνείδισας</quote>(where see n.). In modern Greek, however, (and the use doubtless goes far back,) <foreign xml:lang="grc">θεωρεῖν</foreign> regularly= <quote xml:lang="eng"> "to consider <emph>as</emph> "</quote> (without <foreign xml:lang="grc">ὡς</foreign> ). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀλεξῆτορ_143"> : <ref n="Aristoph. Wasps 161"> Aristoph. <title>Vesp.</title> 161</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἄπολλον ἀποτρόπαιε, τοῦ μαντεύματος</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐ_πρώτης_144">not wholly of the best fortune, <foreign xml:lang="grc">εὐδαιμονίσαι</foreign> (epexeg. inf., <foreign xml:lang="grc">εἰς τὸ εὐδαιμονίσαι</foreign> schol.) so that men should call him happy. The gen. is a poet. form of the possessive, <quote xml:lang="eng">"belonging to"</quote> the best fortune (as to a category); cp. <ref n="Pind. P. 3"> Pind. <title>Pyth.</title> 3.60</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οἵας εἰμὲν αἴσας</quote>, of what estate we (mortals) are: <ref n="Plut. Num. 2"> Plut. <title>Num.</title> 2</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">κρείττονος ἦν μοίρας</quote>. The <emph> place</emph> of <foreign xml:lang="grc">εὐδαιμ</foreign> . has been influenced by its common constr. with a causal gen.: but we could not say, <foreign xml:lang="grc">οὐκ εἰμὶ εὐδαιμονίσαι</foreign> , I am not to be congratulated. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πρώτης_144">, not <quote xml:lang="eng">"from his birth,"</quote> but <quote xml:lang="eng">"best"</quote> : <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1347"> <title>Ant.</title> 1347</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="trimeter"> <l part="F">τὸ φρονεῖν</l> <l>εὐδαιμονίας πρῶτον ὑπάρχει</l> </lg> </quote>: a sense associated with the idea of first prize ( <ref n="Hom. Il. 23.275"> <title>Il.</title> 23.275</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰ πρῶτα λαβών</quote> ), <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰ πρωτεῖα</quote> : cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 1313">1313</ref> ; and so <ref n="Soph. OC 1228">1228</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πολὺ δεύτερον</quote> . </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐ_πάνυ_144"> oft <emph>means</emph> "not at all," but prob. as a result of the primary ironical sense, "not altogether." </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔφοροι_145">: since the stranger had said <foreign xml:lang="grc">κρινοῦσι</foreign> <ref n="Soph. OC 79">(79)</ref> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δηλῶ_δ_146"> (like <foreign xml:lang="grc">σημεῖον δέ, τεκμήριον δέ</foreign> ), <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> , and this is plain from my being guided by yonder maiden: cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 1145">1145</ref> : <ref n="Soph. OT 1294"> <title>O. T.</title> 1294</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">δείξει δὲ καὶ σοί</quote>(sc. <foreign xml:lang="grc">Οἰδίπους</foreign> ): <ref n="Aristoph. Eccl. 936"> Aristoph. <title>Eccl.</title> 936</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">δείξει τάχ’ αὐτός</quote>: <cit> <bibl n="Lys. 10.20" ref="Lys. 10.20">Lys. or. 10 § 20</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">δηλώσει δέ· οἰχήσεται γὰρ ἀπιών.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀλλοτρίοις_ὄμμ_146">. (instrumental dat.): <ref n="Soph. Ant. 989"> <title>Ant.</title> 989</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="trimeter"> <l part="F">τοῖς τυφλοῖσι γὰρ</l> <l>αὕτη κέλευθος ἐκ προηγητοῦ πέλει</l> </lg> </quote> : <ref n="Eur. Phoen. 834"> Eur. <title>Phoen.</title> 834</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="trimter"> <l>ἡγοῦ πάροιθε, θύγατερ, ὡς τυφλῷ ποδὶ</l> <l>ὀφθαλμὸς εἶ σύ</l> </lg> </quote>. (In <ref n="Plat. Phaedo 99B"> Plat. <title>Phaedo</title> 99B</ref>, quoted by Blaydes, read <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἀλλοτρίῳ ὀνόματι</foreign> , not <foreign xml:lang="grc">ὄμματι</foreign> .) </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σμικροῖς_148">: for the allusive (masc.) plur., instead of <foreign xml:lang="grc"> σμικρᾷ</foreign> , cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 366"> <title>O. T.</title> 366</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">σὺν τοῖς φιλτάτοις</quote>(with Iocasta): for the sense, below, <ref n="Soph. OC 957">957 </ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐρημία με … σμικρὸν τίθησι</quote> . The antithesis of <emph>persons</emph> suggests that <foreign xml:lang="grc">σμικροῖς</foreign> is masc. rather than neut.: so below <ref n="Soph. OC 880">880</ref> : <ref n="Soph. Aj. 158"> <title>Ai.</title> 158</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">σμικροὶ...μεγάλων χωρίς</quote>, <ref n="Soph. Aj. 160">160</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="anapest"> <l>μετὰ γὰρ μεγάλων βαιὸς ἄριστ’ ἂν</l> <l>καὶ μέγας ὀρθοῖθ’ ὑπὸ μικροτέρων</l> </lg> </quote>. If <foreign xml:lang="grc">σμικροῖς</foreign> were neut., it could mean: ( <emph>a</emph> ) like the masc., weak <emph>persons:</emph> cp. <ref n="1 Corinthians 1.27">1 Cor. i. 27</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰ μωρὰ τοῦ κόσμου ἐξελέξατο ὁ θεός, ἵνα καταισχύνῃ τοὺς σοφούς</quote> : ( <emph>b</emph> ) <emph>fig.</emph> , <quote xml:lang="eng"> "weak <emph>things</emph> ,"</quote>frail supports. But the neut. plur. <foreign xml:lang="grc">σμικρά</foreign> in such antitheses usu.= <quote xml:lang="eng"> "lowly <emph>fortunes</emph> "</quote>: <ref n="Pind. P. 3"> Pind. <title>P.</title> 3. 107</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="lyric"> <l>σμικρὸς ἐν σμικροῖς, μέγας ἐν μεγάλοις</l> <l>ἔσσομαι</l> </lg> </quote> : <ref n="Eur. El. 406"> Eur. <title>El.</title> 406</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="trimeter"> <l>εἴπερ εἰσὶν εὐγενεῖς</l> <l>οὐκ ἔν τε μικροῖς ἔν τε μὴ στέρξουσ’ ὁμῶς;</l> </lg> </quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὥρμουν_148">: usu. <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἐπί τινος</foreign> : <ref n="Dem. 18.281"> Dem. <title>De Cor.</title> § 281</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐκ ἐπὶ τῆς αὐτῆς</quote>( <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἀγκύρας</foreign> ) <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁρμεῖ τοῖς πολλοῖς</quote> : but also <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπί τινι</quote> : <ref n="Plut. Sol. 19"> Plut. <title> Solon</title> 19</ref>(he added the <foreign xml:lang="grc">Βουλή</foreign> to the Areopagus) <quote xml:lang="grc">οἰόμενος ἐπὶ δυσὶ βουλαῖς ὥσπερ ἀγκύραις ὁρμοῦσαν ἧττον ἐν σάλῳ τὴν πόλιν ἔσεσθαι</quote> . For the metaphor cp. <ref>Soph. fr. 619</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλ’ εἰσὶ μητρὶ παῖδες ἄγκυραι βίου</quote> . <ref>Eur. fr. 858</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="trimeter"> <l>ἥδε μοι τροφός,</l> <l>μήτηρ, ἀδελφή, δμωίς, ἄγκυρα, στέγη</l> </lg> </quote> . <ref n="Eur. Orest. 68"> <title>Or.</title> 68</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="trimter"> <l>ὡς τά γ’ ἄλλ’ ἐπ’ ἀσθενοῦς</l> <l>ῥώμης ὀχούμεθ’</l> </lg> </quote> : <ref n="Eur. Med. 770"> <title>Med.</title> 770</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκ τοῦδ’ ἀναπτόμεσθα πρυμνήτην κάλων</quote>. Campbell understands — "Nor, being a prince ( <foreign xml:lang="grc">μέγας</foreign> ), as I am, should I have taken up my rest here to crave a small boon." But (1) <foreign xml:lang="grc">μέγας</foreign> in this sense ill suits the present tone of Oed.: cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 110">110</ref> , <ref n="Soph. OC 393">393</ref> . (2) This version of <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἐπὶ σμικροῖς ὥρμουν</foreign> is impossible: the scholium <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπὶ εὐτελέσιν αἰτήμασιν οὐκ ἂν σφόδρα ἱκέτευον</quote> evades the point.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐή_149">. L has <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἒ ἒ·</foreign> which should metrically answer to <foreign xml:lang="grc">ὅρα</foreign> <ref n="Soph. OC 117"> (117)</ref> . It is possible that in an exclamation, followed by a momentary pause, the second <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἔ</foreign> should stand here: but it is more prob. that, as in <ref n="Aesch. Seven 966"> Aesch. <title>Theb.</title> 966</ref>etc., we should write <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἐή</foreign> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀλαῶν_ὀμμάτων_149">. Oedipus has spoken of his own ill fortune as if it consisted primarily in his blindness. The Chorus then ask: — "Ah! and wast thou blind <emph>from thy birth?</emph> Thy life has been long, as well as unhappy, one may judge." The gen. could depend on <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἐή</foreign> , as oft. on <foreign xml:lang="grc">φεῦ, ὦ, οἴμοι</foreign> , etc., but is better taken with <foreign xml:lang="grc">φυτάλμιος</foreign> , of which the sense (with <foreign xml:lang="grc">αὐτῶν</foreign> <emph> understood</emph> ) would else be obscure. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φυτάλμιος_150"> = <quote xml:lang="eng">"generator"</quote> : <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> didst thou bring them with thee into life? <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἔφυσας τυφλὰ ὄμματα</foreign> ;= <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἦσθα τυφλὸς ἐκ γενετῆς</foreign> ; <ref n="Soph. Aj. 1077"> <title>Ai.</title> 1077</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">κἂν σῶμα γεννήσῃ μέγα</quote>though one <emph>grow</emph> a great body (=though his frame wax mighty). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μάν_153">strengthens the adversative force of <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἀλλά</foreign> (as in <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἀλλὰ μήν, ἀλλ’ οὐδὲ μήν</foreign> ): <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἔν γ’ ἐμοί</foreign> = <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἐν ἐμοί γε</foreign> . Cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 247">247</ref> : <ref n="Soph. OT 314"> <title>O. T.</title> 314</ref>(n.): <cit> <bibl n="Xen. Ec. 7.14" ref="Xen. Ec. 7.14"> Xen. <title>Oec.</title> 7.14</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τίς ἡ ἐμὴ δύναμις; ἀλλ’ ἐν σοὶ πάντα ἐστίν</quote> </cit>. The thought is like that of <ref n="Soph. Ant. 556"> <title>Ant.</title> 556</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλ’ οὐκ ἐπ’ ἀρρήτοις γε τοῖς ἐμοῖς λόγοις</quote>(sc. <foreign xml:lang="grc">θανεῖ</foreign> ). </gloss>

data.quote:
τίς ἡ ἐμὴ δύναμις; ἀλλ’ ἐν σοὶ πάντα
									ἐστίν

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="προσθήσει_153">, make thine own, bring on thyself: <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. Pers. 531" ref="Aesch. Pers. 531"> Aesch. <title>Pers.</title> 531</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">μὴ καί τι πρὸς κακοῖσι προσθῆται κακόν</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Her. 146" ref="Eur. Her. 146"> Eur. <title>Her.</title> 146</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἴδια προσθέσθαι κακά</quote> </cit> : <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Andr. 394" ref="Eur. Andr. 394"> <title>Andr.</title> 394</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="trimeter"> <l>τί δέ με καὶ τεκεῖν ἐχρῆν</l> <l>ἄχθος τ’ ἐπ’ ἄχθει τῷδε προσθέσθαι διπλοῦν;</l> </lg> </quote> </cit>The MSS. have <foreign xml:lang="grc">προσθήσεις</foreign> : but the active word would require either ( <emph>a</emph> ) the reflexive pronoun, as in <cit> <bibl>fr. 323</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="trimeter"> <l>ταῦτ’ ἐστὶν ἄλγιστ’, ἢν παρὸν θέσθαι καλῶς</l> <l>αὐτός τις αὑτῷ τὴν βλάβην προσθῇ φέρων</l> </lg> </quote> </cit> : <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 819" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg004:819"> <title>O. T.</title> 819</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="trimeter"> <l part="F">οὔτις ἄλλος ἦν</l> <l>ἢ )γὼ )π’ ἐμαυτῷ τάσδ’ ἀρὰς ὁ προστιθείς</l> </lg> </quote> </cit>: or ( <emph>b</emph> ) some dat. such as <foreign xml:lang="grc">τοῖς σοῖς κακοῖς</foreign> : and we cannot legitimately supply either. So, again, the version "thou shalt not bring <emph>on us</emph> " ( <foreign xml:lang="grc">οὐκ ἐμοὶ προσθήσεις τὴν σὴν ἀράν</foreign> schol.) could stand only if <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἐμοί</foreign> or <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἡμῖν</foreign> were expressed. </gloss>

data.quote:
μὴ καί τι πρὸς κακοῖσι προσθῆται
									κακόν

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="περᾷς_154">, absol.: "thou art going too far" (into the grove): Oed., not reassured by their cry <ref n="Soph. OC 141">(141)</ref> , has moved some steps back.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀλλ_προπέσῃς_156">is answered by <foreign xml:lang="grc">μετάσταθ’</foreign> <ref n="Soph. OC 162">162.</ref> <!-- not sure which "ethics" this refers to; need to check --> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="προπέσῃς_νάπει_156">, advance blindly in the grove, till he stumble (so to say) on its inmost mystery. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Aristot. Nic. Eth. 1116a" ref="Aristot. Nic. Eth. 1116a"> Arist. <title>Eth.</title> 3.7.12</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οἱ μὲν θρασεῖς προπετεῖς</quote> </cit> . <cit> <bibl n="Isoc. 5.90" ref="Isoc. 5.90">Isocr. or. 5 § 90</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <note anchored="true" place="inline" resp="RCJ" xml:lang="eng"> (the Greeks, when conquering the Persians at Cunaxa, <date when="-0401">401 B.C.</date>401 B.C.</note>διὰ τὴν Κύρου προπέτειαν</quote> </cit>, his precipitancy in rushing at his brother Artaxerxes ( <cit> <bibl n="Xen. Anab. 1.8.26" ref="Xen. Anab. 1.8.26"> Xen. <title>Anab.</title> 1.8.26</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰπών, Ὁρῶ τὸν ἄνδρα, ἵετο ἐπ’ αὐτόν</quote> </cit>). </gloss>

data.quote:
εἰπών, Ὁρῶ τὸν ἄνδρα, ἵετο ἐπ’
									αὐτόν

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀφθέγκτῳ_156">: see on <ref target="cl130">130 ff</ref> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὗ_ποτῶν_158">, where the bowl filled with water is used along with the stream of <emph> sweetened</emph> drink-offering: <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> where libations are poured, first, of <emph>water alone</emph> , and then of <emph>water mingled with honey;</emph> see on <ref target="cl472">vv. 472-479</ref> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μειλιχίων_π_158">: schol. <quote xml:lang="grc">γλυκέων ποτῶν, ὅ ἐστι, μέλιτος, οἷς μειλίσσουσι τὰς θεάς</quote> (see on <ref target="cl100">100</ref> ). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="συντρέχει_158">, is combined with: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Trach. 295" ref="Soph. Trach. 295"> <title>Tr.</title> 295</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">πολλή )στ’ ἀνάγκη τῇδε <note anchored="true" place="inline" resp="RCJ"> ( <foreign xml:lang="lat">σξ.</foreign> τῇ πράξει )</note>τοῦτο συντρέχειν</quote> </cit>, this joy of mine must needs attend on this good fortune of my husband. While <foreign xml:lang="grc">κρατήρ</foreign> points to the figurative use of <foreign xml:lang="grc">συντρέχει, ῥεύματι</foreign> <emph> suggests</emph> its literal sense. Others understand:— <quote xml:lang="eng"> "where the basin ( <quote xml:lang="grc">κρατήρ"</quote> ) runs together in a stream ( <quote xml:lang="grc">ῥεύματι</quote> modal dat.) of sweetened waters,</quote> <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="eng">"is filled by the confluence of sweetened waters"</quote>; but ( <emph>a</emph> ) <quote xml:lang="grc">κρατήρ</quote> is the bowl from which the <quote xml:lang="grc">χοαί</quote> are poured, not a basin which receives them: ( <emph>b</emph> ) such an inversion is impossible.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐρατυει_164"> , <foreign xml:lang="lat">arcet</foreign> , keeps (thee) off (from us), separates: <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Phoen. 1260" ref="Eur. Phoen. 1260"> Eur. <title>Phoen.</title> 1260</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐρήτυσον τέκνα | δεινῆς ἁμίλλης</quote> </cit>. This is said to themselves rather than to Oed.: they are not sure that he has heard their cry, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπόβαθι</quote> . To Musgrave's <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἐρατύοι</foreign> the objections are:—(1) the opt. where we should expect the imperat. The opt. is sometimes joined with the imper. in good wishes or counsels ( <cit> <bibl n="Pind. O. 13" ref="Pind. O. 13"> Pind. <title>O.</title> 13.25</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀφθόνητος γένοιο...καὶ τόνδε λαὸν εὔθυνε</quote> </cit>): but here, where <emph>peremptory command</emph> is given ( <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 162" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg007:162">162</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">μετάσταθ’, ἀπόβαθι</quote> </cit> — <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 169" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg007:169">169</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">φώνει</quote> </cit>), the opt. is quite out of place. (2) The sense would be weak, after <ref n="Soph. OC 161">161</ref> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οἴσεις_166">, written in L over the vulgate <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἔχεις</foreign> , cannot be a correction of the latter, but must represent a distinct reading (whether conjectural or not). <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἴσχεις</foreign> would suit the metre (= <quote xml:lang="grc">ἅζονθ’</quote> <ref n="Soph. OC 134">134</ref> , where <ref target="cl134">see n.</ref> ) equally well: but the language slightly favours <quote xml:lang="grc">οἴσεις.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φέρειν_λέσχαν_166">=to bring forward something <emph>to be discussed with us</emph> (cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Ant. 159" ref="Soph. Ant. 159"> <title>Ant.</title> 159</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="lyric"> <l>σύγκλητον</l> <l>τήνδε γερόντων προὔθετο λέσχην</l> </lg> </quote> </cit>), not, "in answer to <emph>our address</emph> ," a sense which <foreign xml:lang="grc">λέσχη</foreign> never has. For <foreign xml:lang="grc">φέρειν</foreign> cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Trach. 122" ref="Soph. Trach. 122"> <title>Tr.</title> 122</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὧν ἐπιμεμφομένα σ’ ἁδεῖα <note anchored="true" place="inline" resp="RCJ" xml:lang="eng"> ( <quote xml:lang="grc">αἰδοῖα</quote>ai)doi=a</note>μὲν ἀντία δ’ οἴσω</quote> </cit>: for fut. indic. with <foreign xml:lang="grc">εἰ</foreign> of <emph>immediate</emph> purpose, with an imperat. in apodosis, <cit> <bibl n="Aristoph. Birds 759" ref="Aristoph. Birds 759"> Aristoph. <title>Av.</title> 759</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">αἶρε πλῆκτρον εἰ μαχεῖ</quote> </cit> . </gloss>

data.quote:
ὧν ἐπιμεμφομένα σ’ ἁδεῖα  μὲν ἀντία δ’
							οἴσω

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀβάτων_167">: see on <ref target="cl10">10</ref> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἵνα_νόμος_168">, where use suffers all (to speak): for the omission of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐστί</quote> cp. <cit> <bibl n="Hdt. 1.90" ref="Hdt. 1.90">Her. 1.90</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπειρωτᾶν...εἰ ἀχαρίστοισι νόμος εἶναι τοῖς Ἑλληνικοῖσι θεοῖσι</quote> </cit> . </gloss>

data.quote:
ἐπειρωτᾶν...εἰ ἀχαρίστοισι νόμος εἶναι
									τοῖς Ἑλληνικοῖσι θεοῖσι

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀπερύκου_169"> , <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπέχου τοῦ φωνεῖν</quote> : schol. <quote xml:lang="grc">πρότερον δὲ μὴ διαλέγου</quote> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ποῖ_ἔλθῃ_170">; Such phrases present <emph>thought</emph> , <emph>speech</emph> , or the <emph> mind</emph> itself, as a <emph>region</emph> in which the wanderer is bewildered; cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 310">310</ref> : <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 922" ref="Soph. El. 922"> <title>El.</title> 922</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐκ οἶσθ’ ὅποι γῆς οὐδ’ ὅποι γνώμης φέρει</quote> </cit>: <quote xml:lang="eng">"thou knowest not whither or into what fancies thou art roaming"</quote> : <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 1174" ref="Soph. El. 1174"> <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 1174</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ποῖ λόγων … ἔλθω;</quote> </cit> <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 390" ref="Soph. El. 390"> <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign>390</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ποῦ ποτ’ εἶ φρενῶν;</quote> </cit> <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Trach. 705" ref="Soph. Trach. 705"> <title>Tr.</title>705</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐκ ἔχω...ποῖ γνώμης πέσω</quote> </cit> . </gloss>

data.quote:
οὐκ οἶσθ’ ὅποι γῆς οὐδ’ ὅποι γνώμης
									φέρει

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔλθῃ_170">, delib. subjunct., in third pers., as <cit> <bibl n="Dem. 18.124" ref="Dem. 18.124"> Dem. <title>De Cor.</title> § 124</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">πότερόν σέ τις, Αἰσχίνη, τῆς πόλεως ἐχθρὸν ἢ ἐμὸν εἶναι φῇ;</quote> </cit>L has <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἔλθοι</foreign> , which might be defended as = "whither can one <emph>possibly</emph> turn?" — a more despairing form of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔλθῃ</quote> . Mr. A. Sidgwick has pointed out ( <ref> Aesch. <title>Cho.</title> Append. p. 122</ref>) that the Attic examples of such an optat. without <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄν</quote> are always directly or indirectly interrogative (as <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Ant. 604" ref="Soph. Ant. 604"> <title>Ant.</title> 604</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τίς...κατάσχοι;</quote> </cit>), and are akin to the interrogative or "deliberative" subjunctive, not to the conditional optat. with <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄν</quote> . The principle is (I think) true. But here, at least, the genuinely "deliberative" <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔλθῃ</quote> seems best. See Appendix. </gloss>

data.quote:
πότερόν σέ τις, Αἰσχίνη, τῆς πόλεως
									ἐχθρὸν ἢ ἐμὸν εἶναι φῇ;

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀστοῖς_μελετᾶν_171">, we must practise the same customs which they practise. <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Ba. 890" ref="Eur. Ba. 890"> Eur. <title>Bacch.</title> 890</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="lyric"> <l>οὐ</l> <l>γὰρ κρεῖσσόν ποτε τῶν νόμων</l> <l>γιγνώσκειν χρὴ καὶ μελετᾶν</l> </lg> </quote> </cit>: we must never set our theory, or practice, above the laws. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καὶ_δή_173">: see on <ref target="cl31">31</ref> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μὴ_ἀδικηθῶ_174">. The prohibitive subjunct. (esp. aor.) is freq. in the 1st pers. <emph> plur.</emph> , but the 1st pers. <emph>sing.</emph> is very rare: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Trach. 802" ref="Soph. Trach. 802"> <title>Tr.</title> 802</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">μηδ’ αὐτοῦ θάνω</quote> </cit> : <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Il. 1.26" ref="Hom. Il. 1.26"> <title>Il.</title> 1.26</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">μή σε κιχείω</quote> </cit> : <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Il. 21.475" ref="Hom. Il. 21.475">21. 475</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">μή σευ ἀκούσω</quote> </cit> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σοὶ_175">(the coryphaeus) after <quote xml:lang="grc">ὦ ξεῖνοι</quote> (the Chorus): cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 208" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg007:208">208</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὦ ξένοι,...μή μ’ ἀνέρῃ</quote> </cit> : <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 242" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg007:242">242 ff.</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὦ ξένοι, οἰκτείρατ’</quote> </cit>, followed by <quote xml:lang="grc">ὄμμα σόν</quote> . Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 1111" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg004:1111"> <title>O. T.</title> 1111</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">πρέσβεις</quote> </cit> , <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 1115" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg004:1115">1115</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">σύ</quote> </cit>. <foreign xml:lang="grc">καὶ</foreign> was omitted by Herm., to make a paroemiac (when the sound and rhythm become extremely unpleasing); <foreign xml:lang="grc">σοὶ</foreign> was omitted, with the same object, by Brunck and Elmsley. Both words are genuine. A paroemiac is neither needful nor desirable here, when another follows so closely <ref n="Soph. OC 177">(177).</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τῶνδ_ἑδράνων_176"> , <quote xml:lang="eng">"these seats,"</quote> the resting-place, generally, in front of the grove, rather than the particular rocky seat pointed out at <ref n="Soph. OC 192">192 f.</ref> : cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 233">233 f.</ref> (Hardly " <emph>abodes</emph> ," <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> Colonus, as <ref n="Aesch. Pers. 4"> Aesch. <title>Pers.</title> 4</ref>.) </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄξει_177">was altered to <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἄρῃ</foreign> by Elmsley on the ground that <foreign xml:lang="grc">οὐ μή</foreign> with the fut. indic. <emph> forbids;</emph> with the subjunctive, <emph>denies.</emph> But, besides the passages in which <foreign xml:lang="grc">οὐ μή</foreign> stands with the <emph>2nd pers. fut. ind.</emph> , and forbids (as <cit> <bibl n="Aristoph. Frogs 462" ref="Aristoph. Frogs 462"> Aristoph. <title>Ran.</title> 462</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ μὴ διατρίψεις</quote> </cit>, "don't dawdle"), there are others in which it stands with the <emph>1st or 3rd pers. fut. ind.</emph> , and denies. In some of these our MSS. are doubtless corrupt; but there are others in which the correction, if any, must be bold. Thus: (1) <emph>with 1st pers.:</emph> <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 1052" ref="Soph. El. 1052"> Soph. <title>El.</title> 1052</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὔ σοι μὴ μεθέψομαί ποτε</quote> </cit> : <cit> <bibl n="Aristoph. Frogs 508" ref="Aristoph. Frogs 508"> Aristoph. <title>Ran.</title> 508</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ μή σ’ ἐγὼ | περιόψομἀπελθόντ’</quote> </cit>. (2) <emph>with 3rd pers.:</emph> <cit> <bibl n="Xen. Hell. 1.6.32" ref="Xen. Hell. 1.6.32"> Xen. <title>Hellen.</title> 1.6.32</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἶπεν ὅτι ἡ Σπάρτη οὐδὲν μὴ κάκιον οἰκιεῖται αὐτοῦ ἀποθανόντος</quote> </cit> : <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Phoen. 1590" ref="Eur. Phoen. 1590"> Eur. <title>Phoen.</title> 1590</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="trimeter"> <l>σαφῶς γὰρ εἶπε Τειρεσίας οὐ μήποτε</l> <l>σοῦ τήνδε γῆν οἰκοῦντος εὖ πράξειν πόλιν</l> </lg> </quote> </cit>(oblique of <foreign xml:lang="grc">οὐ μὴ πράξει</foreign> ). On the whole the evidence points to the conclusion that <foreign xml:lang="grc">οὐ μή</foreign> could be used with the 1st or 3rd pers. fut. indic., as with the aor. or pres. subjunct., in giving a strong assurance. !-- e)/ti_ wants a macron over the iota, similarly to_ --> </gloss>

data.quote:
εἶπεν ὅτι ἡ Σπάρτη οὐδὲν μὴ κάκιον
									οἰκιεῖται αὐτοῦ ἀποθανόντος

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μάν_182">(a stronger <quote xml:lang="grc">μέν</quote> , "verily") may here be simply hortative ("come!") as it oft. is with the imperat.: <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Il. 1.302" ref="Hom. Il. 1.302"> <title>Il.</title> 1.302</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ δ’ ἄγε μὴν πείρησαι</quote> </cit> : <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Il. 5.765" ref="Hom. Il. 5.765">5. 765</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄγρει μάν</quote> </cit> : <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. Supp. 1018" ref="Aesch. Supp. 1018"> Aesch. <title>Suppl.</title> 1018</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἴτε μάν</quote> </cit>.
