If the lost words of Oed. uttered a complaint, then <foreign xml:lang="grc">μάν</foreign> may have had an adversative force, "yet": but this is more oft. <foreign xml:lang="grc">γε μήν</foreign> than <foreign xml:lang="grc">μήν</foreign> alone: cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 587">587.</ref> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὧδ_182">, in this direction: see on <ref n="Soph. OT 7"> <title>O. T.</title> 7</ref>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀμαυρῷ_κώλῳ_182"> = <cit> <quote xml:lang="grc">τυφλῷ ποδί</quote> <bibl n="Eur. Hec. 1050" ref="Eur. Hec. 1050"> (Eur. <title>Hec.</title> 1050)</bibl> </cit>: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 1639" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg007:1639">1639</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀμαυραῖς χερσίν</quote> </cit>. In <ref n="Eur. Her. 123"> Eur. <title>Herc. Fur.</title> 123</ref>, however, <quote xml:lang="grc">ποδὸς ἀμαυρὸν ἴχνος</quote> = merely "my <emph>feeble</emph> steps" (for Amphitryon is not <emph>blind</emph> ). That might be the meaning here too. But in choosing between the literal sense of <quote xml:lang="grc"> ἀμαυρός</quote> , "dim," and the fig. sense, "feeble," we must be guided by the context of each passage; and the context here favours the former. Cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 1018">1018.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τόλμα_σέβεσθαι_184">. These four vv. are wrongly given by the MSS. to Antigone. Her gentle counsel in <ref n="Soph. OC 171">171 ff.</ref> may have prompted the attribution. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ξεῖνος_ξένης_184"> : <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Phil. 135" ref="Soph. Phil. 135"> <title>Ph.</title> 135</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="lyric"> <l>τί χρή με, δέσποτ’, ἐν ξένᾳ ξένον</l> <l>στέγειν, ἢ τί λέγειν...;</l> </lg> </quote> </cit> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὦ_τλάμων_185">: the nom. can thus stand for the voc. even in direct address, as <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Med. 1133" ref="Eur. Med. 1133"> Eur. <title>Med.</title> 1133</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">μὴ σπέρχου, φίλος</quote> </cit>: but is sometimes rather a comment, as <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Med. 61" ref="Eur. Med. 61"> <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 61</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὦ μῶρος, εἰ χρὴ δεσπότας εἰπεῖν τόδε</quote> </cit>. Cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 753">753</ref> , <ref n="Soph. OC 1471">1471.</ref> </gloss>

data.quote:
ὦ μῶρος, εἰ χρὴ δεσπότας εἰπεῖν
									τόδε

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τέτροφεν_ἄφιλον_186">, holds in <emph>settled</emph> dislike:—the perfect tense marking how the sentiment which forbids impiety towards the Eumenides has interwoven itself with the life of the place. <quote xml:lang="grc">τρέφω τί ἄφιλον</quote> =to hold a thing (in one's thoughts) as unloveable: cp. <cit> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν ἐλπίσιν τρέφω τι</quote> <bibl n="Soph. Ant. 897" ref="Soph. Ant. 897"> ( <title>Ant.</title> 897)</bibl> </cit>. For the perfect, denoting a <emph>fixed</emph> view, cp. <cit> <bibl n="Hdt. 3.38" ref="Hdt. 3.38">Her. 3.38</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὕτω νενομίκασι τὰ περὶ τοὺς νόμους</quote> </cit>(and so <ref n="Hdt. 7.153">7. 153</ref> , <ref n="Hdt. 8.79">8. 79</ref> ): <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Laws 8.837c" ref="Plat. Laws 8.837c"> Plat. <title>Legg.</title> 8. 837 C</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁ...ἐρων τῇ ψυχῇ...ὕβριν ἥγηται τὴν περὶ τὸ σῶμα τοῦ σώματος πλησμονήν</quote> </cit> : <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Prot. 348e" ref="Plat. Prot. 348e"> <title>Prot.</title> 348 E</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὕτω πεπίστευκας σαυτῷ</quote> </cit>. The perf. act. of <quote xml:lang="grc">τρέφω</quote> occurs in <cit> <bibl> <title>Anthol.</title> Append. 111. 2 (Jacobs vol. II. p. 795)</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄνδρας ἀγακλειτοὺς τέτροφε Κεκροπίη</quote> </cit>: in Polybius ( <ref>12. 25 h</ref> in the later form <quote xml:lang="grc">τέτραφα</quote> ), etc.: but in older Greek only in the Homeric use, as <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Od. 23.237" ref="Hom. Od. 23.237"> <title>Od.</title> 23.237</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">περὶ χροῒ τέτροφεν ἅλμη</quote> </cit>(the brine has hardened on their flesh): whence Nauck here, <quote xml:lang="grc"> <emph>πόλει</emph> τέτροφεν</quote>(as= <quote xml:lang="grc"> πέφυκεν</quote> ) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄφιλον</quote> , (whate'er) hath <emph> grown</emph> unpleasing to the city. </gloss>

data.quote:
οὕτω νενομίκασι τὰ περὶ τοὺς
									νόμους

ὁ...ἐρων τῇ ψυχῇ...ὕβριν ἥγηται τὴν
									περὶ τὸ σῶμα τοῦ σώματος πλησμονήν

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄν_189">with the optat. verbs, not with <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἵνα</foreign> : "(to a place) where I may speak on the one hand, and hear on the other": <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ μὲν … τὸ δέ</quote> are adverbial: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Xen. Anab. 4.1.14" ref="Xen. Anab. 4.1.14"> Xen. <title>Anab.</title> 4.1.14</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰ μέν τι μαχόμενοι, τὰ δὲ καὶ ἀναπαυόμενοι.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

data.quote:
τὰ μέν τι μαχόμενοι, τὰ δὲ καὶ
									ἀναπαυόμενοι.

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἴποιμεν_ἀκούσαιμεν_189"> , <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> "arrive at a mutual understanding," — a regular phrase: <cit> <bibl n="Thuc. 4.22" ref="Thuc. 4.22">Thuc. 4.22</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ξυνέδρους δὲ σφίσιν ἐκέλευον ἑλέσθαι οἵτινες λέγοντες καὶ ἀκούοντες περὶ ἑκάστου ξυμβήσονται</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Theoc. 25" ref="Theoc. 25">Theocr. 25. 48</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="hexameter"> <l part="F">αἰσυμνήτης</l> <l>ᾧ κε τὸ μὲν εἴποιμι, τὸ δ’ ἐκ φαμένοιο πυθοίμην</l> </lg> </quote> </cit>(a head-man, <quote xml:lang="eng">"who to shrewd questions shrewdly can reply,"</quote> Calverley). </gloss>

data.quote:
ξυνέδρους δὲ σφίσιν ἐκέλευον ἑλέσθαι
									οἵτινες λέγοντες καὶ ἀκούοντες περὶ ἑκάστου
									ξυμβήσονται

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄν_189_2">with the optat. in the relative clause just as in apodosis; so <cit> <bibl n="Theoc. 25" ref="Theoc. 25">Theocr. 25. 61</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="hexameter"> <l>ἐγὼ δέ τοι ἡγεμονεύσω</l> <l>… ἵνα κεν τέτμοιμεν ἄνακτα</l> </lg> </quote> </cit>(to a place where <emph>we are likely to find</emph> him): <cit> <bibl n="Xen. Anab. 3.1.40" ref="Xen. Anab. 3.1.40"> Xen. <title>Anab.</title> 3.1.40</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐκ οἶδα ὅ τι ἄν τις χρήσαιτο αὐτοῖς</quote> </cit>(I know not what use one <emph>could make</emph> of them). </gloss>

data.quote:
οὐκ οἶδα ὅ τι ἄν τις χρήσαιτο
									αὐτοῖς

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εὐσεβίας_ἐπιβαίνοντες_189"> , <emph>entering on piety</emph> , placing ourselves within its pale: but this figurative sense is here tinged with the notion of "entering on lawful <emph>ground</emph> " (schol. <quote xml:lang="grc">εὐσεβῶς πατοῦντες</quote> ). For the fig. sense cp. <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Od. 23.52" ref="Hom. Od. 23.52"> <title>Od.</title> 23.52</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="hexameter"> <l>ὄφρα σφῶϊν ἐϋφροσύνης ἐπιβῆτον</l> <l>ἀμφοτέρω φίλον ἦτορ</l> </lg> </quote> </cit> , <quote xml:lang="eng"> "that ye may both <emph>enter into</emph> your heart's delight"</quote>(Butcher and Lang): <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Phil. 1463" ref="Soph. Phil. 1463"> <title>Ph.</title> 1463</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">δόξης οὔποτε τῆσδ’ ἐπιβάντες</quote> </cit>, though we had never <emph>entered on</emph> that hope (dared to entertain it). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καὶ_πολ_191"> : <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Ant. 1106" ref="Soph. Ant. 1106"> <title>Ant.</title> 1106</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνάγκῃ δ’ οὐχὶ δυσμαχητέον</quote> </cit> . <cit> <bibl>Simonides fr. 5. 21</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνάγκᾳ δ’ οὐδὲ θεοὶ μάχονται</quote> </cit> . <cit> <bibl>Eur. fr. 709</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">χρεία διδάσκει, κἂν βραδύς τις ᾖ, σοφόν</quote> </cit>. </gloss>

data.quote:
χρεία διδάσκει, κἂν βραδύς τις ᾖ,
									σοφόν

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αὐτοῦ_192">. Oed. has now advanced to the verge of the grove. Here a low ledge of natural rock forms a sort of threshold, on which his feet are now set. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αὐτοπέτρου_βήματος_192">, a "step," <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> ledge, <emph>of natural rock</emph> , not shaped by man (as was the ordinary <quote xml:lang="grc">βῆμα</quote> or raised place for speakers, etc.), distinct, of course, from the <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄξεστος πέτρος</quote> of <ref n="Soph. OC 19">19</ref> , which was <emph>within</emph> the grove. So <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐτόξυλος</quote> (of rough wood, <ref n="Soph. Phil. 35"> <title>Ph.</title> 35</ref>), <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐτοπόρφυρος</quote> (of natural purple), <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐτόποκος</quote> (of simple wool), <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐτόπυ—ρος</quote> (of unbolted wheaten flour), <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐτόκομος</quote> (with natural hair, <ref n="Aristoph. Frogs 822"> Aristoph. <title>Ran.</title> 822</ref>), <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐτόροφοι πέτραι</quote> (rocks forming a natural roof, <ref> Oppian <title>Halieut.</title> 1. 22</ref>). The <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἀντιπέτρου</foreign> of the MSS. could mean:—(1) "A ledge <emph>like</emph> rock"; cp. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀντίπαις</quote> ( <ref n="Aesch. Eum. 38"> Aesch. <title>Eum.</title> 38</ref>)= " <emph>weak as</emph> a child": and so the schol. in L, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἰσοπέτρου, χαλκοῦ</quote> ,— <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> "a ledge of material firm as rock," "of brass," meaning the <quote xml:lang="grc">χαλκόπους ὀδός</quote> understood <emph>literally</emph> : see, however, <ref target="cl57">on 57</ref> . (2) "A ledge <emph>serving as</emph> a rock": cp. ( <quote xml:lang="grc">ὀνείδη</quote> ) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀντίκεντρα</quote> ( <ref n="Aesch. Eum. 136"> Aesch. <title>Eum.</title> 136</ref>), <quote xml:lang="grc">λίθος ἀντιθύρετρος</quote> ( <ref>Nonnus 11. 140</ref> ), <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀντίπυργος πέτρα</quote> ( <ref n="Eur. Ba. 1097"> Eur. <title>Bacch.</title> 1097</ref>). (3) "A seat of rock <emph>fronting thee</emph> ": cp. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀντίπρῳρος</quote> , with <quote xml:lang="grc">πρῴρα</quote> facing one. This does not fit the data. (4) Bellermann: "a (stone) seat over against a rock," <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> "behind which the stone wall rises" (?).—Campb. renders first by " <emph>rocky</emph> ," then by " <emph> rock-like</emph> ," and refers it to <quote xml:lang="eng">"some peculiarity in the basement of the low seats."</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πόδα_κλίνῃς_193">(aor.) like <quote xml:lang="grc">πόδα τρέπειν</quote> ( <ref n="Eur. Supp. 718"> Eur. <title>Suppl.</title> 718</ref>), since, the seat being now at his side, he <emph>turns away</emph> from it if he moves forward. Wecklein explains it as = <quote xml:lang="grc">γόνυ κάμψῃς</quote> ("sit down"), but (1) <quote xml:lang="grc">πόδα</quote> could not here stand for <quote xml:lang="grc">γόνυ</quote> , and (2) the question is now of <emph> halting</emph> , not yet of sitting down (see <ref n="Soph. OC 195">195</ref> ). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἦ_ἑσθῶ_195">"am I to sit down?" deliberative aor. subj. of <foreign xml:lang="grc"> ἕζομαι</foreign> . This aor. of the simple verb occurs nowhere else: but <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἐκαθέσθην</foreign> is used in later Greek (as <foreign xml:lang="grc">καθεσθέντα</foreign> <ref n="Paus. 3.22.1">Paus. 3.22.1</ref> ). Since <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἑ</foreign> is the radical vowel, it seems better to suppose a synizesis ( <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἦ ἑσθῶ;</foreign> ) than an aphaeresis ( <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἦ )σθῶ;</foreign> ): the <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἦ</foreign> , though not necessary, is prob. genuine. I have left this questionable <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἑσθῶ</foreign> in the text, on the strength of <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἐκαθέσθην</foreign> : but the <foreign xml:lang="lat"> v.l.</foreign> <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἦ στῶ</foreign> ("am I to halt?"), preferred by the schol. in L, seems more defensible than it has been thought by recent edd. The answer of the Chorus, no doubt, refers to sitting down. So, however, it could do after <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἦ στῶ;</foreign> He has already been told to go no further <ref n="Soph. OC 191">(191 f.)</ref> : but, in his anxiety to avoid further offence, it is conceivable that he should repeat his question in the clearest form. (Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Hec. 1079" ref="Eur. Hec. 1079"> Eur. <title>Hec.</title> 1079</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">πᾶ βῶ, πᾶ στῶ, πᾶ κάμψω</quote> </cit> ...;) </gloss>

data.quote: πᾶ βῶ, πᾶ στῶ, πᾶ κάμψω

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λέχριός_ὀκλάσας_195">, "yes, moving sideways," — the rocky seat being near his side — "(sit down), crouching low on the top of the rock." <foreign xml:lang="grc"> ὀ‐κλάζω</foreign> (cp. <foreign xml:lang="grc">ὀ‐δάξ</foreign> , from <foreign xml:lang="grc">√δακ</foreign> ), from <foreign xml:lang="grc">κλά‐ω</foreign> , to bend the hams in crouching down; <cit> <bibl n="Xen. Anab. 6.1.10" ref="Xen. Anab. 6.1.10"> Xen. <title>Anab.</title> 6.1.10</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ Περσικὸν ὠρχεῖτο,...καὶ ὤκλαζε καὶ ἐξανίστατο</quote> </cit>, <quote xml:lang="eng">"he danced the Persian dance, sinking down and rising again by turns"</quote> (there was a dance called <foreign xml:lang="grc">ὄκλασμα</foreign> ): so <foreign xml:lang="grc">ὀκλαδίας</foreign> =a folding campstool. </gloss>

data.quote:
τὸ Περσικὸν ὠρχεῖτο,...καὶ ὤκλαζε καὶ
							ἐξανίστατο

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="βραχὺς_195">, <quote xml:lang="eng">"low,"</quote> (as <foreign xml:lang="grc">μέγας</foreign> = <quote xml:lang="eng">"tall,"</quote> ) because the seat is near the ground. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄκρου_195">, on the outer edge of the rocky platform ( <foreign xml:lang="grc">βῆμα</foreign> <ref n="Soph. OC 192">192</ref> ). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λᾶος_195">, gen. of <foreign xml:lang="grc">λᾶας</foreign> , as <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Od. 8.192" ref="Hom. Od. 8.192"> <title>Od.</title> 8.192</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">λᾶος ὑπὸ ῥιπῆς</quote> </cit>. No part of <foreign xml:lang="grc">λᾶας</foreign> occurs in trag., except here and <ref n="Eur. Phoen. 1157"> Eur. <title>Phoen.</title> 1157</ref>acc. <quote xml:lang="grc">λᾶαν</quote> . The MSS. have <foreign xml:lang="grc">λάου</foreign> , and the schol. in L quotes Herodian ( <date when="0160">160 A.D.</date> ), <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν τῷ ε— τῆς καθόλου</quote> ( <ref> =bk 5 of his lost work <title xml:lang="grc">ἡ καθόλου προσῳδία</title> </ref>), as taking it from a nom. <foreign xml:lang="grc">λάος</foreign> : but Herodian had perhaps no warrant besides this passage, and no other trace of such a form occurs. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐμὸν_τόδ_197"> : <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> the office of placing him in his seat (cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 21" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg007:21">21</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">κάθιζέ... με</quote> </cit>). Hermann changes the <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἐν ἡσυχίᾳ</foreign> of the MSS. to <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἐν ἁσυχαίᾳ</foreign> , joining it with <foreign xml:lang="grc">βάσει</foreign> . The corresponding strophic verse is lost (see on <ref target="cl181"> 181</ref> ): but the metre confirms the emendation (see Metrical Analysis). The words <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἐν ἡσυχαίᾳ … ἐμάν</foreign> are said as she helps him to sit down. He has to make one step sideways <ref n="Soph. OC 195">(195)</ref> to the seat. Taking his arm, she says: "Lean on me, and join step to quiet step" ( <foreign xml:lang="grc"> ἅρμοσαι</foreign> aor. imper. midd.): <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> "advance one foot to the resting-place, bring the other up beside it, and then (supported by my arm) sit down." Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Orest. 233" ref="Eur. Orest. 233"> Eur. <title>Or.</title> 233</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἦ κἀπὶ γαίας ἁρμόσαι πόδας θέλεις;</quote> </cit> <quote xml:lang="eng">"wouldest thou set thy feet together (plant thy feet) on the ground?"</quote> <cit> <bibl>Pseudo-Simonides 182</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="elegiac"> <l>ὅπᾳ ποδὸς ἴχνια πρᾶτον</l> <l>ἁρμόσαμεν</l> </lg> </quote> </cit>, where we first planted our feet (on the battlefield,—there we fell). Campbell takes <foreign xml:lang="grc">βάσιν</foreign> as the foot of Oed., and <foreign xml:lang="grc">βάσει</foreign> as a "stone support for the foot attached to the seat," rendering, <quote xml:lang="eng">"fit thy foot into its quiet resting place."</quote> This seems improbable. "Time thy step to <emph>my</emph> quiet step" is unsuitable, since they are close to the seat already. The interjection <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἰώ μοί μοι</foreign> , given by the MSS. to Antigone, but by Herm. (rightly) to Oed., need not, with Herm., be placed after <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἅρμοσαι</foreign> . </gloss>

data.quote:
ἦ κἀπὶ γαίας ἁρμόσαι πόδας
									θέλεις;

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δύσφρονος_202">, as the work of a mind clouded by the gods: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Ant. 1261" ref="Soph. Ant. 1261"> <title>Ant.</title> 1261</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἰὼ φρενῶν δυσφρόνων ἁμαρτήματα</quote> </cit>. The gen. after the exclamation <foreign xml:lang="grc">ὤμοι</foreign> : cp. on <ref target="cl149">149</ref> . </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τλάμων_203">, see on <ref target="cl185">185</ref> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="χαλᾷς_203">, hast ease (alluding to his words betokening pain and exhaustion): <emph> not</emph> , <foreign xml:lang="grc">εἴκεις καὶ οὐκ ἀντιτείνεις τῷ ἐξελθεῖν</foreign> (from the grove), as the schol. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τίς_πολ_205">: cp. on <ref target="cl68">68</ref> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τίν_πατρίδ_205">. For the twofold question, cp. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 220"> <title>Ph.</title> 220</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="trimeter"> <l>τίνες ποτ’ ἐς γῆν τήνδε κατέσχετ’; …</l> <l>ποίας πάτρας ἂν ἢ γένους ὑμᾶς ποτε</l> <l part="I">τύχοιμ’ ἂν εἰπών</l> </lg> </quote> ; <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Hel. 86" ref="Eur. Hel. 86"> Eur. <title>Helen</title> 86</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀτὰρ τίς εἶ; πόθεν; τίν’ ἐξαυδᾶν σε χρή</quote> </cit>; (Dind. <foreign xml:lang="grc">τίνος σ’ αὐδᾶν χρέων</foreign> ;) <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Od. 1.170" ref="Hom. Od. 1.170"> <title>Od.</title> 1.170</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <note anchored="true" place="inline" resp="RCJ" xml:lang="eng">(and in five other places)</note>τίς πόθεν εἶς ἀνδρῶν; πόθι τοι πόλις ἠδὲ τοκῆες;</quote> </cit> </gloss>

data.quote:
ἀτὰρ τίς εἶ; πόθεν; τίν’ ἐξαυδᾶν σε
									χρή

τίς πόθεν εἶς ἀνδρῶν; πόθι τοι πόλις ἠδὲ
							τοκῆες;

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μή_ἀνέρῃ_210">. As the verses from <ref n="Soph. OC 207">207</ref> onwards are <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἀνομοιόστροφα</foreign> (see on <ref target="cl117"> 117</ref> ), the strophic test is absent, but <foreign xml:lang="grc">μή, μή μ’</foreign> is metrically preferable to <foreign xml:lang="grc">μὴ μὴ μή μ’</foreign> here (see Metr. Analysis). And, after the preceding <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἀλλὰ μή</foreign> , a <emph>threefold</emph> iteration would rather weaken than strengthen. !-- this is not, in fact, ionics --> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φύσις_212">=origin, birth: <ref n="Soph. OC 270">270</ref> : <ref n="Soph. Trach. 379"> <title>Tr.</title> 379</ref>(Iolè) <quote xml:lang="grc">λαμπρὰ … φύσιν, | πατρὸς μὲν οὖσα γένεσιν Εὐρύτου, κ.τ.λ.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γεγώνω_214">, delib. perf. subjunct. from <foreign xml:lang="grc">γέγωνα</foreign> : whence, too, the imper. <foreign xml:lang="grc">γέγωνε</foreign> , <ref n="Soph. Phil. 238"> <title>Ph.</title> 238</ref>. Both these could, indeed, be referred to a pres. <foreign xml:lang="grc">γεγώνω</foreign> , which is implied by other forms, as <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἐγέγωνε</foreign> ( <ref n="Hom. Il. 14.469"> <title>Il.</title> 14.469</ref>): cp. <ref> Monro <title>Hom. Gr.</title> § 27</ref>. Poetry recognised, in fact, three forms,—a perf. <foreign xml:lang="grc">γέγωνα</foreign> , a pres. <foreign xml:lang="grc"> γεγώνω</foreign> , and a pres. <foreign xml:lang="grc">γεγωνέω</foreign> ( <foreign xml:lang="grc">γεγωνεῖν</foreign> , <ref n="Hom. Il. 12.337"> <title>Il.</title> 12.337</ref>). Cp. <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἄνωγα</foreign> with impf. <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἤνωγον</foreign> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τίνος_σπέρματος_214">; possessive gen., denoting the stock, country, etc., to which one <emph> belongs:</emph> cp. on <ref target="cl144">144</ref> : <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Sym. 203A" ref="Plat. Sym. 203A"> Plat. <title>Symp.</title> 203A</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">πατρὸς τίνος ἐστὶ καὶ μητρός;</quote> </cit> <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Meno 94D" ref="Plat. Meno 94D"> <title>Meno</title>94 D</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οἰκίας μεγάλης ἦν</quote> </cit> : <cit> <bibl n="Dem. 57.57" ref="Dem. 57.57">Dem. or. 57 § 57</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅσοι τῶν μεγάλων δήμων ἐστέ.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πατρόθεν_214"> with <foreign xml:lang="grc">εἶ</foreign> : the Chorus, whose uneasy curiosity is now thoroughly roused, presses for an explicit answer, and first (as usual) for the <emph>father's</emph> name. <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Laws 753C" ref="Plat. Laws 753C"> Plat. <title>Legg.</title> 753C</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰς πινάκιον γράψαντα τοὔνομα πατρόθεν καὶ φυλῆς καὶ δήμου</quote> </cit>. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 547" ref="Soph. Aj. 547"> <title>Ai.</title> 547</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐμὸς τὰ πατρόθεν</quote> </cit> . </gloss>

data.quote:
εἰς πινάκιον γράψαντα τοὔνομα πατρόθεν
									καὶ φυλῆς καὶ δήμου

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τί__216">"what is to become of me?" <ref n="Soph. Trach. 973"> <title>Tr.</title> 973</ref>(Hyllus, in his wild grief for his father) <quote xml:lang="grc">τί πάθω; τί δὲ μήσομαι; οἴμοι</quote> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐπ_βαίνεις_217">, "thou art <emph>coming to</emph> the verge" (not, "thou treadest <emph> on</emph> the verge," which would require gen. or dat.), since, after the hint <cit> <quote xml:lang="grc">αἰνὰ φύσις</quote> <bibl n="Soph. OC 212" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg007:212">(212)</bibl> </cit>, the full truth cannot long be withheld. Cp. <ref>fr. 658</ref> (Orithyia was carried) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπ’ ἔσχατα χθονός</quote> : <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Ant. 853" ref="Soph. Ant. 853"> <title>Ant.</title> 853</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">προβᾶσ’ ἐπ’ ἔσχατον θράσους</quote> </cit> : <cit> <bibl n="Hdt. 8.52" ref="Hdt. 8.52">Her. 8.52</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐς τὸ ἔσχατον κακοῦ ἀπιγμένοι</quote> </cit> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μακρὰ_219">, neut. <emph>plur.</emph> as adv.: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 883" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg004:883"> <title>O. T.</title> 883</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑπέροπτα</quote> </cit> (n.): <cit> <bibl n="Aristoph. Lys. 550" ref="Aristoph. Lys. 550"> Aristoph. <title>Lys.</title> 550</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὔρια θεῖτε</quote> </cit> : <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Orest. 152" ref="Eur. Orest. 152"> Eur. <title>Or.</title> 152</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">χρόνια...πεσὼν...εὐνάζεται</quote> </cit> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Λαΐου_τιν_220">; The word <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἀπόγονον</foreign> , seemingly a gloss, which follows <foreign xml:lang="grc">τιν’</foreign> in the MSS., is against the metre, which requires <foreign xml:lang="meter">-uuu-</foreign> after <foreign xml:lang="grc">τιν’</foreign> : it also injures the dramatic force. Each word is wrung from Oed.; the gen. <foreign xml:lang="grc">Λαΐου</foreign> tells all. The long syll. after <foreign xml:lang="grc">τιν’</foreign> could be,—(1) <foreign xml:lang="grc"> ὤ</foreign> , which Herm. supplies,—giving it, however, to the Chorus,—whereas the rhythm will be better if it is an interjection by Oed.: (2) <foreign xml:lang="grc">ὄντ’</foreign> ; (Dindorf): or (3) <foreign xml:lang="grc">οὖν</foreign> ; (Elmsley). The two latter are somewhat tame.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ποῖ_καταθήσεις_227">fig. from the payment of a debt in money. If you will not pay it here and now, to what place will you bring the payment for it? <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> when, and in what form, can your promise of a safe refuge <ref n="Soph. OC 176">(176 f.)</ref> be redeemed, if I am driven from Colonus? <foreign xml:lang="grc">ποῖ</foreign> with a verb pregnantly used, as <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 476" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg007:476">476</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ δ’ ἔνθεν ποῖ τελευτῆσαί με χρή;</quote> </cit> <emph>to</emph>what end am I to <emph>bring</emph> it? Cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 383">383</ref> . For <foreign xml:lang="grc">καταθήσεις</foreign> cp. <cit> <bibl n="Dem. 21.99" ref="Dem. 21.99"> Dem. <title>In Mid.</title> § 99</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ γάρ ἐστιν ὄφλημα ὅ τι χρὴ καταθέντα ἐπίτιμον γενέσθαι τουτονί</quote> </cit>, there is no debt (to the Treasury), by paying which he can recover the franchise. <cit> <bibl n="Pind. N. 7" ref="Pind. N. 7"> Pind. <title>Nem.</title> 7.75</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">νικῶντί γε χάριν … οὐ τραχύς εἰμι καταθέμεν</quote> </cit>. </gloss>

data.quote:
τὸ δ’ ἔνθεν ποῖ τελευτῆσαί με
									χρή;

οὐ γάρ ἐστιν ὄφλημα ὅ τι χρὴ καταθέντα
									ἐπίτιμον γενέσθαι τουτονί

νικῶντί γε χάριν … οὐ τραχύς εἰμι
									καταθέμεν

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐδενὶ_ἔρχεται_228">, to no one comes punishment from fate, <foreign xml:lang="grc">ὧν</foreign> (= <foreign xml:lang="grc">τούτων ἃ</foreign> ) <foreign xml:lang="grc"> προπάθῃ</foreign> , for things (caus. gen.) which he has already suffered, <foreign xml:lang="grc">τὸ τίνειν</foreign> (acc.), in respect of his requiting them. <foreign xml:lang="grc">συγγνωστόν ἐστιν ἐὰν τίνῃ τις ἃ ἂν προπάθῃ</foreign> . <"Thou didst deceive <emph>us</emph> by getting our promise before telling thy name; we may requite thy deceit by deeming our promise void." <foreign xml:lang="grc">τίνειν</foreign> (with <foreign xml:lang="grc">τὸ</foreign> added, see on <ref target="cl47">47</ref> ) further explains the causal gen. <foreign xml:lang="grc">ὧν</foreign> : "no one is punished <emph>for</emph> deeds which have first been done to <emph>him</emph> —that is, <emph>for repaying</emph> them to the aggressor." Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 1203" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg007:1203">1203</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">πάσχειν, παθόντα δ’ οὐκ ἐπίστασθαι τίνειν</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Orest. 109" ref="Eur. Orest. 109"> Eur. <title>Or.</title> 109</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τίνοι...τροφάς</quote> </cit> , <emph>repay</emph> care. </gloss>

data.quote:
πάσχειν, παθόντα δ’ οὐκ ἐπίστασθαι
									τίνειν

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὧν_228">for <quote xml:lang="grc">ὧν ἄν</quote> , as <ref n="Soph. OC 395">395</ref> , <ref n="Soph. OT 1231"> <title>O. T.</title> 1231</ref>(n.). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀπάτα_δ_230">: guile on the one part ( <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἑτέρα</foreign> ), matching itself against deeds of guile on the other ( <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἑτέραις</foreign> ), makes a recompense of woe, not of grace (as in return for <emph>good</emph> deeds): <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἔχειν</foreign> , epexeg. "(for the deceiver) to enjoy" (cp. <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Il. 1.347" ref="Hom. Il. 1.347"> <title>Il.</title> 1.347</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">δῶκε δ’ ἄγειν</quote> </cit> ). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀπάτα_ἑτέρα_230">, not another <emph>kind</emph> of guile, but another <emph>instance</emph> of it, as <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Phil. 138" ref="Soph. Phil. 138"> <title>Ph.</title> 138</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τέχνα <note anchored="true" place="inline" resp="RCJ" xml:lang="eng">(a king's skill)</note> τέχνας ἑτέρας προὔχει</quote> </cit>, excels skill <emph>in another man.</emph> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="παραβαλλ_230">., as <cit> <bibl n="Eur. IT 1094" ref="Eur. IT 1094"> Eur. <title>I. T.</title> 1094</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐγώ σοι παραβάλλομαι θρήνους</quote> </cit> , <emph>vie with thee</emph> in dirges: <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Andr. 290" ref="Eur. Andr. 290"> <title>Andr.</title> 290</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">παραβαλλόμεναι</quote> </cit>, abs., "in rivalry." For the sentiment cp. <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Crito 49B" ref="Plat. Crito 49B"> Plat. <title>Crito</title> 49B</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδὲ <note anchored="true" place="inline" resp="RCJ"> ( <quote xml:lang="grc">δεῖ</quote> )</note>ἀδικούμενον ἄρα ἀνταδικεῖν, ὡς οἱ πολλοὶ οἴονται</quote> </cit> : <cit> <bibl>Archil. fr. 67</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="iambics"> <l>ἓν δ’ ἐπίσταμαι μέγα,</l> <l>τὸν κακῶς με δρῶντα δεινοῖς ἀνταμείβεσθαι κακοῖς</l> </lg> </quote> </cit> . <cit> <bibl n="Pind. P. 2" ref="Pind. P. 2"> Pind. <title>Pyth.</title> 2.83</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="lyric"> <l>φίλον εἴη φιλεῖν·</l> <l>ποτὶ δ’ ἐχθρὸν ἅτ’ ἐχθρὸς ἐὼν λύκοιο δίκαν ὑποθεύσομαι,</l> <l>ἄλλ’ ἄλλοτε πατέων ὁδοῖς σκολιαῖς.</l> </lg> </quote> </cit> </gloss>

data.quote:
οὐδὲ  ἀδικούμενον
							ἄρα ἀνταδικεῖν, ὡς οἱ πολλοὶ οἴονται

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἑδράνων_233"> with <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἔκτοπος</foreign> (cp. on <ref target="cl119">119</ref> ), <foreign xml:lang="grc">χθονὸς</foreign> with <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἄφορμος</foreign> , which adds force to <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἔκθορε</foreign> ; cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 430" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg004:430"> <title>O. T.</title> 430</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="trimeter"> <l>οὐκ εἰς ὄλεθρον; οὐχὶ θᾶσσον; οὐ πάλιν</l> <l>ἄψορρος οἴκων τῶνδ’ ἀποστραφεὶς ἄπει;</l> </lg> </quote> </cit> <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Hipp. 155" ref="Eur. Hipp. 155">Eur. <title>Hipp.</title> 155 has</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ναυβάτας τις ἔπλευσεν | Κρήτας ἔξορμος</quote> </cit>, "from an anchorage in Crete," cp. <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἐξορμεῖν</foreign> to be (or go) out of port: but <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἄφορμος</foreign> belongs to <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἀφορμᾶν</foreign> (there is no <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἀφορμεῖν</foreign> ), "rushing from" ( <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἀφορμηθείς</foreign> , schol.). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="χρέος_προσάψῃς_235"> (like <foreign xml:lang="grc">κῦδος, τιμάς, αἰτίαν προσάπτειν</foreign> ), fix a debt or <emph>obligation</emph> on the city, <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> make it liable to expiate a pollution. But <foreign xml:lang="grc">χρέος</foreign> = simply "matter" in <ref n="Soph. OT 155"> <title>O. T.</title> 155</ref>, n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αἰδόφρονες_237">: as ye have <foreign xml:lang="grc">αἰδώς</foreign> for the Eumenides, so have <foreign xml:lang="grc">αἰδώς</foreign> for the suppliant. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Dem. 37.59" ref="Dem. 37.59">Dem. or. 37 § 59</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἂν ἑλών τις ἀκουσίου φόνου...μετὰ ταῦτ’ αἰδέσηται καὶ ἀφῇ</quote> </cit>(with ref. to the kinsman of a slain man <emph>pardoning</emph> the involuntary slayer). </gloss>

data.quote:
ἂν ἑλών τις ἀκουσίου φόνου...μετὰ
									ταῦτ’ αἰδέσηται καὶ ἀφῇ

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀλλ_237">, "Nay," opening the appeal: cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 14"> <title>O. T.</title> 14</ref>. The second <foreign xml:lang="grc"> ἀλλ’</foreign> in <ref n="Soph. OC 241">241</ref> = "at least." </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γεραὸν_ἐμὸν_238">: the text of this verse is doubtful, and there is no strophic test, but it seems most likely that <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλαὸν</quote> was an interpolation: see crit. n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀκόντων_240">, epithet of the agent, instead of that proper to the act ( <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀκουσίων</quote> ): <ref n="Soph. OC 977">977</ref> : <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 1229" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg004:1229"> <title>O. T.</title> 1229</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">κακὰ ἑκόντα κοὐκ ἄκοντα</quote> </cit>. Cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 74">74</ref> , <ref n="Soph. OC 267">267.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀΐοντες_αὐδάν_240">, "perceiving," <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> "being aware of,""having heard," the report of his involuntary deeds. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 792" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg007:792">792</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">κλύω</quote> </cit> : <cit> <bibl n="Thuc. 6.20" ref="Thuc. 6.20">Thuc. 6.20</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς ἐγὼ ἀκοῇ αἰσθάνομαι</quote> </cit>.—Not: (1) "on hearing (from him) the mention of his deeds" — as implied in his name: nor (2) "on hearing his first utterance," as if <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἀκόντ</foreign> . </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔργων_240">could be caus. gen. with <foreign xml:lang="grc">οὐκ ἀνέτλατε</foreign> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀλλ_241">, "at least," cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 1276">1276</ref> : <cit> <bibl>fr. 24</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">κἂν ἄλλο μηδέν, ἀλλὰ τοὐκείνης κάρα</quote> </cit>. </gloss>

data.quote:
κἂν ἄλλο μηδέν, ἀλλὰ τοὐκείνης
									κάρα

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐκ_ἀλαοῖς_244">, as <emph>his</emph> are. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="προσορωμένα_244">: for the midd. cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 1059" ref="Soph. El. 1059"> <title>El.</title> 1059</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐσορώμενοι</quote> </cit>. The midd. of the simple <foreign xml:lang="grc">ὁράω</foreign> is poet. only ( <ref n="Soph. Ant. 594"> <title>Ant.</title> 594</ref>): but the midd. of <foreign xml:lang="grc">προοράω</foreign> and <foreign xml:lang="grc"> περιοράω</foreign> occurs in Attic prose. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὥς_τις_245"> <foreign xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</foreign>: as if I were a young kinswoman of your own, appealing to you, the eldest of my house, for protection. The words are hardly so strong as "like daughter to father"; and though <foreign xml:lang="grc">σὸν</foreign> is addressed to the coryphaeus (cp. on <ref target="cl175">175</ref> ), this sense would be less fitting. So Creon imagines his niece Antigone appealing to the sacred ties of kinship ( <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Ant. 487" ref="Soph. Ant. 487"> <title>Ant.</title> 487</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">Ζηνὸς ἑρκείου</quote> </cit> ; <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Ant. 658" ref="Soph. Ant. 658">658</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐφυμνείτω Δία | ξύναιμον</quote> </cit> ). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐν_κείμεθα_247">, "we are <emph>situated</emph> " ( <emph>not</emph> , "prostrate") "in your power": <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἐν ὔμμι</foreign> , <foreign xml:lang="lat">penes vos</foreign> , cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 392">392</ref> , <ref n="Soph. OC 422">422</ref> , <ref n="Soph. OC 1443">1443</ref> , <ref n="Soph. OT 314"> <title>O. T.</title> 314</ref>(n.), <cit> <bibl n="Dem. 18.193" ref="Dem. 18.193"> Dem. <title>De Cor.</title> § 193 </bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν γὰρ τῷ θεῷ τοῦτο τὸ τέλος ἦν, οὐκ ἐν ἐμοί</quote> </cit>. The epic forms <foreign xml:lang="grc">ὔμμες</foreign> (nom.), <foreign xml:lang="grc">ὔμμι</foreign> (dat.), <foreign xml:lang="grc">ὔμμε</foreign> (acc.), freq. in Hom., belonged esp. to the Lesbian Aeolic: the acc. occurs in <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. Eum. 620" ref="Aesch. Eum. 620"> Aesch. <title>Eum.</title> 620</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">βουλῇ πιφαύσκω δ’ ὔμμ’ ἐπισπέσθαι πατρός</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Ant. 846" ref="Soph. Ant. 846"> Soph. <title>Ant.</title> 846</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ξυμμάρτυρας ὔμμ’ ἐπικτῶμαι.</quote> </cit> <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἐν ὑμῖν γὰρ</foreign>(MSS.) is unmetrical: and if <foreign xml:lang="grc">γὰρ</foreign> is omitted, <foreign xml:lang="grc"> ὑμῖν</foreign> still mars the metre, which requires a dactyl. </gloss>

data.quote:
ἐν γὰρ τῷ θεῷ τοῦτο τὸ τέλος ἦν, οὐκ
									ἐν ἐμοί

βουλῇ πιφαύσκω δ’ ὔμμ’ ἐπισπέσθαι
									πατρός

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κείμεθα_247">, of a critical situation, as <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Trach. 82" ref="Soph. Trach. 82"> <title>Tr.</title> 82</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="trimeter"> <l>ἐν οὖν ῥοπῇ τοιᾷδε κειμένῳ, τέκνον,</l> <l>οὐκ εἶ ξυνέρξων;</l> </lg> </quote> </cit>(when his fate is thus trembling in the balance). Cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 1510"> 1510</ref> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="νεύσατε_248">with acc. of the boon, as <ref n="HH 5.445"> <title>Hom. Hymn.</title> 5.445</ref>, <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Alc. 978" ref="Eur. Alc. 978"> Eur. <title>Alc.</title> 978</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">Ζεὺς ὅ τι νεύσῃ</quote> </cit>(more oft. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπι‐</quote> or <quote xml:lang="grc"> κατανεύειν</quote> ). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὰν_χάριν_248">, the unlooked-for grace, <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> for which, after your stern words (226), we can scarcely dare to hope,—but which, for that very reason, will be the more gracious. <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Med. 1417" ref="Eur. Med. 1417"> Eur. <title>Med.</title> 1417</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="anapests"> <l>καὶ τὰ δοκηθέντ’ οὐκ ἐτελέσθη,</l> <l>τῶν δ’ ἀδοκήτων πόρον εὗρε θεός</l> </lg> </quote> </cit> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πρός_ς_250">: in supplications the poets often insert the enclitic <foreign xml:lang="grc">σε</foreign> between <foreign xml:lang="grc">πρός</foreign> and the gen. of that by which one adjures: <ref n="Soph. OC 1333">1333</ref> : <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Trach. 436" ref="Soph. Trach. 436"> <title>Tr.</title> 436</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">μή, πρός σε τοῦ κατ’ ἄκρον κ.τ.λ.</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Phil. 468" ref="Soph. Phil. 468"> <title>Ph.</title> 468</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="trimeter"> <l>πρός νύν σε πατρός, πρός τε μητρός, ὦ τέκνον,</l> <l>πρός τ’ εἴ τί σοι κατ’ οἶκόν ἐστι προσφιλές,</l> <l>ἱκέτης ἱκνοῦμαι.</l> </lg> </quote> </cit> <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἐκ σέθεν</foreign>could go with <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἄντομαι</foreign> only if <foreign xml:lang="grc">πρός σ’</foreign> were <quote xml:lang="grc">πρός τ’</quote> or <quote xml:lang="grc">πρὸς δ’</quote> and even then would be harsh. Join, then, <foreign xml:lang="grc">ὅ τι σοι φίλον ἐκ σέθεν</foreign> , "whatever, <emph>sprung from thyself</emph> , is dear to thee"; the next words repeat this thought, and add to it: "yea, <emph>by child</emph> —or wife, or possession, or god." Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 530" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg007:530">530</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐξ ἐμοῦ</quote> </cit> . <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἐκ σέθεν</foreign> could not mean simply, "on thy part," as = "in thy home." Against Elmsley's tempting <foreign xml:lang="grc">οἴκοθεν</foreign> (cp. <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Med. 506" ref="Eur. Med. 506"> Eur. <title>Med.</title> 506</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῖς οἴκοθεν φίλοις</quote> </cit>) it may be remarked that the alliteration <quote xml:lang="grc">πρός σ’ … σοι — ἐκ σέθεν</quote> seems intentional (cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 370"> <title>O. T.</title> 370</ref>n.). </gloss>

data.quote:
μή, πρός σε τοῦ κατ’ ἄκρον
									κ.τ.λ.

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἢ_θεός_251">: a designed assonance ( <foreign xml:lang="grc">παρομοίωσις</foreign> ): cp. <cit> <bibl n="Isoc. 5.134" ref="Isoc. 5.134">Isocr. or. 5 § 134</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ τὴν φήμην καὶ τὴν μνήμην</quote> </cit> : <cit> <bibl n="Isoc. 4.45" ref="Isoc. 4.45">or. 4 § 45</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀγῶνας...μὴ μόνον τάχους· καὶ ῥώμης ἀλλὰ καὶ λόγου καὶ γνώμης.</quote> </cit> <foreign xml:lang="grc"> <emph>χρέος</emph> </foreign>here= <quote xml:lang="grc">χρῆμα</quote> , "thing," any cherished possession (cp. <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Il. 23.618" ref="Hom. Il. 23.618"> <title>Il.</title> 23.618</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">καί σοι τοῦτο, γέρον, κειμήλιον ἔστω</quote> </cit>), rather than "business," "office." </gloss>

data.quote: καὶ τὴν φήμην καὶ τὴν μνήμην

ἀγῶνας...μὴ μόνον τάχους· καὶ ῥώμης
									ἀλλὰ καὶ λόγου καὶ γνώμης.

καί σοι τοῦτο, γέρον, κειμήλιον
									ἔστω

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀθρῶν_252">, if thou look closely. <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Rep. 577C" ref="Plat. Rep. 577C"> Plat. <title>Rep.</title> 577C</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὴν ὁμοιότητα ἀναμιμνησκόμενος τῆς τε πόλεως καὶ τοῦ ἀνδρὸς οὕτω καθ’ ἕκαστον ἐν μέρει ἀθρῶν τὰ παθήματα ἑκατέρου λέγε</quote> </cit> . </gloss>

data.quote:
τὴν ὁμοιότητα ἀναμιμνησκόμενος τῆς τε
									πόλεως καὶ τοῦ ἀνδρὸς οὕτω καθ’ ἕκαστον ἐν μέρει ἀθρῶν
									τὰ παθήματα ἑκατέρου λέγε

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄγοι_253">, <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> draw on to evil: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Ant. 623" ref="Soph. Ant. 623"> <title>Ant.</title> 623</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅτῳ φρένας | θεὸς ἄγει πρὸς ἄταν</quote> </cit>. Oedipus was led on to his unwitting deeds by a god. Cp. <cit> <bibl>fr. 615</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="trimeter"> <l>οὐδ’ ἂν εἷς φύγοι</l> <l>βροτῶν ποθ’, ᾧ καὶ Ζεὺς ἐφορμήσῃ κακά</l> </lg> </quote> </cit>: so, too, <ref n="Soph. El. 696"> <title>El.</title> 696</ref>. For the hiatus after <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄγοι</quote> , cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 1202" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg004:1202"> <title>O. T.</title> 1202 f.</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">καλεῖ | ἐμός</quote> </cit> . </gloss>

data.quote:
ὅτῳ φρένας | θεὸς ἄγει πρὸς
									ἄταν

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὰ_θεῶν_256">, euphemistic: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. Pers. 373" ref="Aesch. Pers. 373"> Aesch. <title>Pers.</title> 373</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ γὰρ τὸ μέλλον ἐκ θεῶν ἠπίστατο</quote> </cit>. For <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἐκ</foreign> cp. also <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Phil. 1316" ref="Soph. Phil. 1316"> <title>Ph.</title> 1316</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰς … ἐκ θεῶν | τύχας</quote> </cit> : <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Phoen. 1763" ref="Eur. Phoen. 1763"> Eur. <title>Phoen.</title> 1763</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰς ἐκ θεῶν ἀνάγκας</quote> </cit>. Similarly <cit> <bibl n="Eur. IA 1610" ref="Eur. IA 1610"> <title>I. A.</title> 1610</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰ τῶν θεῶν</quote> </cit>(=their dispensations). </gloss>

data.quote:
οὐ γὰρ τὸ μέλλον ἐκ θεῶν
									ἠπίστατο

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ῥεούσης_259">, when it flows away, perishes, <foreign xml:lang="grc">μάτην</foreign> , "vainly," without result: <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> issues in no corresponding deeds. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Trach. 698" ref="Soph. Trach. 698"> <title>Tr.</title> 698</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ῥεῖπᾶν ἄδηλον</quote> </cit> : <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 1000" ref="Soph. El. 1000"> <title>El.</title> 1000</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <note anchored="true" place="inline" resp="RCJ" xml:lang="eng">(our fortune)</note>ἀπορρεῖ κὰπὶ μηδὲν ἔρχεται</quote> </cit> : <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 1267" ref="Soph. Aj. 1267"> <title>Ai.</title> 1267</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">χάρις διαρρεῖ</quote> </cit>. Cp. Lat. <foreign xml:lang="lat">futilis, fluere</foreign> ( <cit> <bibl n="Cic. Fin. 2.32" ref="Cic. Fin. 2.32"> Cic. <title>Fin.</title> 2. 32. 106</bibl> <quote xml:lang="lat">fluit voluptas corporis et prima quaeque avolat</quote> </cit>). For <foreign xml:lang="grc">μάτην</foreign> cp. <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. Lib. 845" ref="Aesch. Lib. 845"> Aesch. <title>Ch.</title> 845</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg> <l>λόγοι</l> <l>... θνήσκοντες μάτην</l> </lg> </quote> </cit>. (Not, "when the fame <emph>is current</emph> without good ground.") </gloss>

data.quote:
fluit voluptas corporis et prima
									quaeque avolat

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἰ_260">with ind. <foreign xml:lang="grc">φασί</foreign> ( <foreign xml:lang="lat">siquidem dicunt</foreign> ) introduces the actual case which has suggested the general question, <foreign xml:lang="grc">τί δῆτα κ.τ.λ.</foreign> : cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 823" ref="Soph. El. 823"> <title>El.</title> 823</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="lyric"> <l>ποῦ ποτε κεραυνοὶ Διός, ἢ ποῦ φαέθων</l> <l>Ἅλιος, εἰ ταῦτ’ ἐφορῶντες</l> <l>κρύπτουσιν ἕκηλοι;</l> </lg> </quote> </cit> <foreign xml:lang="grc">γε</foreign>oft. follows <foreign xml:lang="grc">εἰ</foreign> (and <foreign xml:lang="grc">εἴπερ</foreign> ) in such cases, but here is better taken with <foreign xml:lang="grc">τάς</foreign> : it slightly emphasises the name of Athens. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θεοσεβεστάτας_260">. Athens is pre-eminently (1) religious, (2) compassionate towards the oppressed. <cit> <bibl n="Paus. 1.17.1" ref="Paus. 1.17.1">Paus. 1.17.1</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἀθηναίοις δὲ ἐν τῇ ἀγορᾷ καὶ ἄλλα ἐστὶν οὐκ ἐς άπαντας ἐπίσημα καὶ Ἐλέου βωμός, ᾧ μάλιστα θεῶν ἐς ἀνθρώπινον βίον καὶ μεταβολὰς πραγμάτων ὅτι ὠφέλιμος, μόνοι τιμὰς Ἑλλήνων νέμουσιν Ἀθηναῖοι. τούτοις δὲ οὐ τὰ ἐς φιλανθρωπίαν μόνον καθέστηκεν, ἀλλὰ καὶ ἐς θεοὺς εὐσεβοῦσιν ἄλλων πλέον· καὶ Αἰδοῦς σφίσι βωμός ἐστι καὶ Φήμης καὶ Ὁρμῆς</quote> </cit> . </gloss>

data.quote:
Ἀθηναίοις δὲ ἐν τῇ ἀγορᾷ καὶ ἄλλα
									ἐστὶν οὐκ ἐς άπαντας ἐπίσημα καὶ Ἐλέου βωμός, ᾧ μάλιστα
									θεῶν ἐς ἀνθρώπινον βίον καὶ μεταβολὰς πραγμάτων ὅτι
									ὠφέλιμος, μόνοι τιμὰς Ἑλλήνων νέμουσιν Ἀθηναῖοι. τούτοις
									δὲ οὐ τὰ ἐς φιλανθρωπίαν μόνον καθέστηκεν, ἀλλὰ καὶ ἐς
									θεοὺς εὐσεβοῦσιν ἄλλων πλέον· καὶ Αἰδοῦς σφίσι βωμός
									ἐστι καὶ Φήμης καὶ Ὁρμῆς

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μόνας_261">, not strictly <quote xml:lang="eng">"alone,"</quote> but <quote xml:lang="eng">"more than all others"</quote> : cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 299"> <title>O. T.</title> 299</ref>n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸν_ξένον_261">. The two standard instances were subsequent, in mythical date, to the time of Oedipus. (1) Theseus, at the prayer of Adrastus king of Argos, compels Creon and his Thebans to permit the burial of the Argive warriors who had fallen in the war of Eteocles and Polynices. This is the subject of the <ref n="Eur. Supp."> <title>Supplices</title> of Eur.</ref>, which continues the story of the <ref n="Soph. Ant."> <title>Antigone</title> </ref>and the <ref n="Eur. Phoen."> <title>Phoenissae.</title> </ref>(2) Demophon, the son of Theseus, protects the children of Hercules against the Argive Eurystheus. This is the subject of the <ref n="Eur. Heraclid."> <title>Heracleidae</title> of Eur.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σῴζειν_262">, to give him a safe refuge: <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἀρκεῖν</foreign> , to come to his rescue ( <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 322" ref="Soph. El. 322"> <title>El.</title> 322</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐσθλός, ὥστ’ ἀρκεῖν φίλοις</quote> </cit>), if anyone seeks to take him thence by force. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οἵας_τε_262">, <foreign xml:lang="lat">sc.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἶναι</quote> , here synonymous with <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἔχειν</foreign> . After <quote xml:lang="grc">οἷός τε</quote> this ellipse of <quote xml:lang="grc"> εἰμί</quote> is frequent. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κἄμοιγε_ποῦ_263">. The thought of the whole passage is,— <quote xml:lang="grc"> τί δόξα μάτην ῥέουσα ὠφελεῖ, <emph>εἰ</emph> τὰς Ἀθήνας <emph>φασὶ</emph> ( μὲν ) θεοσεβεστάτας εἶναι, <emph>ἐμοὶ δὲ</emph> ταῦτα μηδαμοῦ ἐστιν</quote>; Instead however, of a clause <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐμοὶ δὲ...κ.τ.λ.</quote> , thus depending on <foreign xml:lang="grc">εἰ</foreign> , a new sentence is opened by the direct question,— <foreign xml:lang="grc">καὶ ἔμοιγε ποῦ ταῦτά ἐστιν;</foreign> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καί_263">, <emph> prefixed</emph> to interrogative words (as <quote xml:lang="grc">ποῦ, πῶς, ποῖος, τίς</quote> ), makes the query an indignant comment on a preceding statement: <ref n="Soph. El. 236"> <title>El.</title> 236</ref>: <cit> <bibl n="Dem. 18.232" ref="Dem. 18.232"> Dem. <title>De Fals. Legat.</title> § 232</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ τίς, ὦ ἄνδρες Ἀθηναῖοι, τοῦτ’ ἰδὼν τὸ παράδειγμα δίκαιον αὑτὸν παρασχεῖν ἐθελήσει;</quote> </cit> </gloss>

data.quote:
καὶ τίς, ὦ ἄνδρες Ἀθηναῖοι, τοῦτ’ ἰδὼν
									τὸ παράδειγμα δίκαιον αὑτὸν παρασχεῖν ἐθελήσει;

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οἵτινες_263">, causal, as if <quote xml:lang="grc">παρ’ ὑμῖν</quote> had preceded: hence= <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπεὶ ὑμεῖς</quote> . Cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 427">427</ref> , <ref n="Soph. OC 866">866</ref> . <cit> <bibl n="Thuc. 4.26" ref="Thuc. 4.26">Thuc. 4.26</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> ἀθυμίαν τε πλείστην ὁ χρόνος παρεῖχε παρὰ λόγον ἐπιγιγνόμενος, <emph>οὓς</emph> ( ὅτι αὐτοὺς ) ᾤοντο ἡμερῶν ὀλίγων ἐκπολιορκήσειν</quote> </cit>, <emph>since</emph> they had thought to reduce them in a few days. <cit> <bibl n="Thuc. 1.68" ref="Thuc. 1.68">1. 68</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> νῦν δὲ τί δεῖ μακρηγορεῖν, <emph>ὧν</emph> ( = ἐπεὶ ἡμῶν ) τοὺς μὲν δεδουλωμένους ὁρᾶτε</quote> </cit> ...; <cit> <bibl n="Thuc. 6.68" ref="Thuc. 6.68">6. 68</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> πολλῇ μὲν παραινέσει...τί δεῖ χρῆσθαι, <emph> οἳ</emph> πάρεσμεν ἐπὶ τὸν αὐτὸν ἀγῶνα</quote> </cit> ; <ref n="Thuc. 8.76.6">8. 76 § 6</ref> ( <quote xml:lang="grc">οἵ γε</quote> ). <cit> <bibl n="Aristoph. Cl. 1225" ref="Aristoph. Cl. 1225"> Aristoph. <title>Nub.</title> 1225</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="trimeter"> <l>ἵππον; οὐκ ἀκούετε</l> <l>ὃν ( = ὅτι ἐμὲ ) πάντες ὑμεῖς ἴστε μισοῦνθ’ ἱππικήν</l> </lg> </quote> </cit> . <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 457" ref="Soph. Aj. 457"> <title>Ai.</title> 457</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τί χρὴ δρᾶν; <emph>ὅστις</emph> ἐμφανῶς θεοῖς | ἐχθαίρομαι</quote> </cit>. Cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 1228"> <title>O. T.</title> 1228</ref>n.: <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1364"> <title>Ph.</title> 1364</ref>. </gloss>

data.quote:
ἀθυμίαν τε πλείστην ὁ χρόνος παρεῖχε παρὰ
							λόγον ἐπιγιγνόμενος,   ( ὅτι αὐτοὺς ) ᾤοντο ἡμερῶν ὀλίγων
							ἐκπολιορκήσειν

νῦν δὲ τί δεῖ μακρηγορεῖν,   (
							= ἐπεὶ ἡμῶν ) τοὺς μὲν δεδουλωμένους ὁρᾶτε
πολλῇ μὲν παραινέσει...τί δεῖ χρῆσθαι,   πάρεσμεν ἐπὶ τὸν αὐτὸν ἀγῶνα

τί χρὴ δρᾶν;   ἐμφανῶς θεοῖς
							| ἐχθαίρομαι

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τάδε_βάθρα_264">denote, generally, the seats afforded by the natural rock in or near the grove: here he is thinking specially of the <quote xml:lang="grc">βάθρον ἀσκέπαρνον</quote> <ref n="Soph. OC 101">(101)</ref> within its precincts. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐ_γε_265">: see on <ref target="cl110">110</ref> . For the art. <foreign xml:lang="grc">τό</foreign> , followed only by <foreign xml:lang="grc">γε</foreign> at the end of the v., with its noun <quote xml:lang="grc">σῶμα</quote> in the next v., cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Ant. 67" ref="Soph. Ant. 67"> <title>Ant.</title> 67</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ γὰρ | … πράσσειν</quote> </cit> , <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Ant. 78" ref="Soph. Ant. 78"> <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 78</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ δὲ | … δρᾶν</quote> </cit> : <ref n="Soph. Trach. 92"> <title>Tr.</title> 92</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Trach. 742">742</ref> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐπεὶ_ἔξοιδα_266">. I am <cit> <quote xml:lang="eng">"a man more sinned against than sinning"</quote> <bibl n="Shak. LR 3.2" ref="Shak. LR 3.2"> ( <title>Lear</title> 3. 2. 60)</bibl> </cit>,—as would appear, could I unfold to you <emph>my relations with</emph> my parents ( <foreign xml:lang="grc">τὰ μητρὸς καὶ πατρός</foreign> ), <emph>on account of which</emph> relations (the parricide and the incest— <foreign xml:lang="grc">ὧν</foreign> neuter) ye dread me. Of that I am sure. (For those relations began with their casting out their new-born son to perish. That first wrong led to the rest: hence it was that I knew not the face of my assailant in the pass, or of my bride at Thebes.) </gloss>

data.quote:
"a man more sinned against than
									sinning"

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πεπονθότ_δεδρακότα_267">. The agent's activities ( <foreign xml:lang="grc">τὰ ἔργα μου</foreign> ) here stand for the agent himself; and so, instead of <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῖς ἔργοις πεπονθώς εἰμι</quote> (cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 873">873</ref> ), we have <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰ ἔργα μου πεπονθότ’ ἐστί</quote> . (Cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 74">74</ref> , <ref n="Soph. OC 1604">1604</ref> .) <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 1214" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg004:1214"> <title>O. T.</title> 1214</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">γάμος· τεκνῶν καὶ τεκνούμενος</quote> </cit>= one in which <emph>the son</emph> has become <emph>the spouse.</emph> So a particular activity of a person's mind is sometimes expressed by the active participle (neut.) of a verb to which the person himself would properly be subject: <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ βουλόμενον, τὸ ὀργιζόμενον τῆς γνώμης</quote> ( <ref n="Thuc. 1.90">Thuc. 1.90</ref> , <ref n="Thuc. 2.59">2. 59</ref> ): <cit> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ δεδιός, τὸ θαρσοῦν αὐτοῦ</quote> <bibl n="Thuc. 1.36" ref="Thuc. 1.36">(1. 36)</bibl> </cit> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἱκόμην_ἱκόμην_273">: cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 336">336</ref> , <ref n="Soph. OC 974">974</ref> ; <ref n="Soph. OT 1376"> <title>O. T.</title> 1376</ref>(n.) <quote xml:lang="grc">βλαστοῦσ’ ὅπως ἔβλαστε</quote> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὑφ_ἔπασχον_274">, impf. of <emph>attempted</emph> act, cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 1454" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg004:1454"> <title>O. T.</title> 1454</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οἵ μ’ ἀπωλλύτην</quote> </cit> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔπασχον_274">: when the iron pin was driven through the babe's feet and he was exposed on Cithaeron, <ref n="Soph. OT 718"> <title>O. T.</title> 718</ref>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὥσπερ_κἀνεστής_276"> .: <emph>as</emph> ye caused me to leave my seat in the grove, <emph>so</emph> give me the safety which ye then promised: see on <ref target="cl264">264</ref> : for <foreign xml:lang="grc">καί</foreign> , on <ref target="cl53">53</ref> . For <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἀνιστάναι</foreign> , of causing <quote xml:lang="grc">ἱκέται</quote> to leave sanctuary, cp. <cit> <bibl n="Thuc. 1.126" ref="Thuc. 1.126">Thuc. 1.126</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <note anchored="true" place="inline" resp="RCJ" xml:lang="eng">(cylon and his adherents)</note> καθίζουσιν ἐπὶ τὸν βωμὸν ἱκέται τὸν ἐν τῇ ἀκροπόλει. ἀναστήσαντες δὲ αὐτοὺς οἱ τῶν Ἀθηναίων ἐπιτετραμμένοι,... ἐφ’ ᾧ μηδὲν κακὸν ποιήσουσιν, ἀπαγαγόντες ἀπέκτειναν</quote> </cit> . </gloss>

data.quote:
καθίζουσιν ἐπὶ τὸν βωμὸν ἱκέται τὸν ἐν τῇ
							ἀκροπόλει. ἀναστήσαντες δὲ αὐτοὺς οἱ τῶν Ἀθηναίων ἐπιτετραμμένοι,... ἐφ’
							ᾧ μηδὲν κακὸν ποιήσουσιν, ἀπαγαγόντες ἀπέκτειναν

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θεοὺς_θεοὺς_277">: the art. with the repeated word, as <ref n="Soph. OC 5">5</ref> , <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Phil. 992" ref="Soph. Phil. 992"> <title>Ph.</title> 992</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">θεοὺς προτείνων τοὺς θεοὺς ψευδεῖς τίθης</quote> </cit>. </gloss>

data.quote:
θεοὺς προτείνων τοὺς θεοὺς ψευδεῖς
									τίθης

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μοίραις_ποεῖσθε_278">could not stand for <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν μοίραις ποιεῖσθε</quote> . The prep. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν</quote> is indispensable. See the discussion of this passage in the Appendix. The gentlest remedy would be <foreign xml:lang="grc">μοίρας</foreign> ( <emph>as gen. sing.</emph> ), which two MSS. have. As <cit> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν οὐδενὶ λόγῳ ποιεῖσθαι</quote> <bibl n="Hdt. 3.50" ref="Hdt. 3.50">(Her. 3.50)</bibl> </cit> and <cit> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν οὐδεμιᾷ μοίρᾳ ἄγειν</quote> <bibl n="Hdt. 2.172" ref="Hdt. 2.172">(2. 172)</bibl> </cit>are parallel phrases, so <cit> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδενὸς λόγου ποιεῖσθαι</quote> <bibl n="Hdt. 1.33" ref="Hdt. 1.33">(1. 33)</bibl> </cit>might suggest <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδεμιᾶς μοίρας ποιεῖσθαι</quote> . For the two negatives cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 336" ref="Soph. El. 336"> <title>El.</title> 336</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ μὴ δοκεῖν μὲν δρᾶν τι πημαίνειν δὲ μή</quote> </cit>, "and <emph>not</emph> to seem active yet do <emph>no</emph> harm." It is hollow, Oed. says, to insist so strictly on the sanctity of a grove ( <quote xml:lang="grc">θεοὺς τιμῶντες</quote> ), and then to refuse the gods their <quote xml:lang="grc">μοῖρα</quote> , their due tribute of practical piety. You treat the gods as if they were not, when at their shrines you do <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνόσια ἔργα</quote> <ref n="Soph. OC 283">(283)</ref> by violating your pledge to a suppliant. </gloss>

data.quote:
καὶ μὴ δοκεῖν μὲν δρᾶν τι πημαίνειν δὲ
									μή

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ποεῖσθε_278">. Numerous Attic inscriptions of the <date from="-0500" to="-0400">5th</date> and <date from="-0400" to="-0300">4th cent. B.C.</date> show that in this verb <quote xml:lang="grc">ι</quote> was regularly omitted before <quote xml:lang="grc">ει</quote> or <quote xml:lang="grc">η</quote> ( <quote xml:lang="grc">ποεῖ, ποήσει</quote> ), though never before <quote xml:lang="grc">ου, οι</quote> , or <quote xml:lang="grc">ω</quote> ( <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐποίουν, ποιοίη, ποιῶν</quote> : Meisterhans, p. 27). L generally, but not always, omits the <quote xml:lang="grc">ι</quote> before <quote xml:lang="grc">ει</quote> or <quote xml:lang="grc">η</quote> if the syll. is short. Forms from <quote xml:lang="grc">ποιῶ</quote> , with the 1st syll. short, occur below in <ref n="Soph. OC 459">459</ref> , <ref n="Soph. OC 584">584</ref> , <ref n="Soph. OC 652">652</ref> , <ref n="Soph. OC 1018">1018</ref> , <ref n="Soph. OC 1033">1033</ref> , <ref n="Soph. OC 1037">1037</ref> , <ref n="Soph. OC 1144">1144</ref> . In <ref n="Soph. OC 584">584</ref> and <ref n="Soph. OC 652">652</ref> , as here, L keeps the <quote xml:lang="grc">ι</quote> : in the other five places it omits it. In <ref n="Soph. OC 1517">1517</ref> , where the quantity is different, L has <quote xml:lang="grc">ποεῖν</quote> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μήπω_281">, not <quote xml:lang="grc">οὔπω</quote> , because of the imperat. <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἡγεῖσθε</foreign> <ref n="Soph. OC 278">(278)</ref> . After verbs of <emph>thinking</emph> , the negative with the inf. is ordinarily <foreign xml:lang="grc">οὐ</foreign> ( <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Prot. 317A" ref="Plat. Prot. 317A"> Plat. <title>Prot.</title> 317A</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἡγοῦμαι γὰρ αὐτοὺς οὔ τι διαπράξασθαι</quote> </cit>): though <foreign xml:lang="grc">μή</foreign> is used in asseveration (as with <quote xml:lang="grc">ὄμνυμι</quote> ), and sometimes in strong expressions of personal conviction: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 1455" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg004:1455"> <title>O. T.</title> 1455</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οἶδα μήτε μ’ ἂν νόσον | μήτ’ ἄλλο πέρσαι μηδέν</quote> </cit>, where see n. <cit> <bibl n="Thuc. 6.102" ref="Thuc. 6.102"> Thuc. 6.102 <foreign xml:lang="lat">ad fin.</foreign> </bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">νομίσαντες μὴ ἂν...ἱκανοὶ γενέσθαι</quote> </cit>(and <ref n="Thuc. 4.18">id. 4. 18</ref> ): <cit> <bibl n="Xen. Cyrop. 7.5.59" ref="Xen. Cyrop. 7.5.59"> Xen. <title>Cyr.</title> 7.5.59</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐνόμισε δὲ μὴ ἂν γενέσθαι ποτὲ πιστόν.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

data.quote:
ἡγοῦμαι γὰρ αὐτοὺς οὔ τι
									διαπράξασθαι

οἶδα μήτε μ’ ἂν νόσον | μήτ’ ἄλλο
									πέρσαι μηδέν

ἐνόμισε δὲ μὴ ἂν γενέσθαι ποτὲ
									πιστόν.

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φωτὸς_βροτῶν_281">, no wight <emph>among mortals</emph> , no one <emph>in the world.</emph> In <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 1358" ref="Soph. Aj. 1358"> <title>Ai.</title> 1358</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τοιοίδε μέντοι φῶτες ἔμπληκτοι βροτῶν</quote> </cit>, though <quote xml:lang="grc">βροτοῖς</quote> is a specious variant, <quote xml:lang="grc">βροτῶν</quote> has the better authority: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Od. 17.587" ref="Hom. Od. 17.587"> <title>Od.</title> 17.587</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="hexameter"> <l>οὐ γάρ πού τινες ὧδε καταθνητῶν ἀνθρώπων</l> <l>ἀνέρες ὑβρίζοντες</l> </lg> </quote> </cit> : <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Od. 23.187" ref="Hom. Od. 23.187">23. 187</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνδρῶν δ’ οὔ κέν τις ζωὸς βροτός</quote> </cit>. </gloss>

data.quote:
ἀνδρῶν δ’ οὔ κέν τις ζωὸς
									βροτός

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ξὺν_οἷς_282">, <quote xml:lang="grc">σὺν τοῖς θεοῖς</quote> (schol.), <quote xml:lang="eng">"with whose help,"</quote> since the gods strengthen men to refrain from evil, as well as to do good. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μὴ_κάλυπτε_282">, as with a veil ( <quote xml:lang="grc">κάλυμμα</quote> ) of dishonour cast over her bright fame: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Il. 17.591" ref="Hom. Il. 17.591"> <title>Il.</title> 17.591</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸν δ’ ἅχεος νεφέλη ἐκάλυψε μέλαινα</quote> </cit>. <cit> <bibl n="Thuc. 7.69" ref="Thuc. 7.69">Thuc. 7.69</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀξιῶν...τὰς πατρικὰς ἀρετάς, ὦν ἐπιφανεῖς ἦσαν οἱ πρόγονοι, μὴ ἀφανίζειν</quote> </cit> . <cit> <bibl n="Plut. Cor. 31" ref="Plut. Cor. 31"> Plut. <title>Cor.</title> 31</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἠμαυρωμένος τῇ δόξῃ.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

τὸν δ’ ἅχεος νεφέλη ἐκάλυψε
									μέλαινα

ἀξιῶν...τὰς πατρικὰς ἀρετάς, ὦν
									ἐπιφανεῖς ἦσαν οἱ πρόγονοι, μὴ ἀφανίζειν

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὰς_εὐδαίμονας_282"> : <cit> <bibl n="Hdt. 8.111" ref="Hdt. 8.111">Her. 8.111</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">λέγοντες ὡς κατὰ λόγον ἦσαν ἄρα αἱ Ἀθῆναι μεγάλαι τε καὶ εὐδαίμονες</quote> </cit> . </gloss>

data.quote:
λέγοντες ὡς κατὰ λόγον ἦσαν ἄρα αἱ
									Ἀθῆναι μεγάλαι τε καὶ εὐδαίμονες

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔλαβες_284">, since Oed. put himself into their hands when he left sanctuary <ref n="Soph. OC 174">(174 f.).</ref> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸν_ἱκέτην_284">, cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 44">44</ref> , <ref n="Soph. OC 487">487</ref> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐχέγγυον_284">, having received your <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐγγύη</quote> , pledge, that I should not be wronged <ref n="Soph. OC 176">(176)</ref> . Elsewhere <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἐχέγγ.</foreign> = "having a good <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἐγγύη</foreign> to <emph>give</emph> ," trustworthy (like <quote xml:lang="grc">φερέγγυος</quote> ); <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Med. 388" ref="Eur. Med. 388"> Eur. <title>Med.</title> 388</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">γῆν ἄσυλον καὶ δόμους ἐχεγγύους</quote> </cit>(= <cit> <quote xml:lang="grc">πύργος ἀσφαλής</quote> <bibl n="Eur. Med. 389" ref="Eur. Med. 389"> <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 389</bibl> </cit>). But Oed. could call himself <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐχέγγυος</quote> in <emph> this</emph> sense only as coming with credentials from Apollo; and that is not the point here. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Hdt. 5.71" ref="Hdt. 5.71">Her. 5.71</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνιστᾶσι ( τοὺς ἱκέτας ) … οἱ πρυτάνιες, … ὑπεγγύους πλὴν θανάτου</quote> </cit>, under a pledge that they should stand their trial, but not suffer death. </gloss>

data.quote:
ἀνιστᾶσι ( τοὺς ἱκέτας ) … οἱ
									πρυτάνιες, … ὑπεγγύους πλὴν θανάτου

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐκφύλασσε_285">, till I am out of peril: only here, and twice in Eur. as = "to watch <emph> well</emph> " ( <ref n="Eur. Orest. 1259"> <title>Or.</title> 1259</ref>, <ref n="Eur. Ion 741"> <title>Ion</title> 741</ref>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δυσπρόσοπτον_286">, since the sightless orbs bore traces of his dreadful act ( <ref n="Soph. OT 1268"> <title>O. T.</title> 1268</ref>): cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 577">577</ref> . Continue <foreign xml:lang="grc">με</foreign> with <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἀτιμάσῃς</foreign> . </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἱερός_287">, as now formally the <quote xml:lang="grc">ἱκέτης</quote> of the Eumenides <ref n="Soph. OC 44">(44)</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εὐσεβής_287">, since he has come thither <cit> <quote xml:lang="grc">κατ’ ὀμφὰς τὰς Ἀπόλλωνος</quote> <bibl n="Soph. OC 102" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg007:102">(102)</bibl> </cit> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φέρων_ὄνησιν_287">: the first hint, to the Chorus, of the <quote xml:lang="grc">κέρδη</quote> mentioned in the prayer which only his daughter witnessed <ref n="Soph. OC 92">(92)</ref> . Cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 72">72</ref> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὁ_τις_288">: the master— whoever he be. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 107" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg004:107"> <title>O. T.</title> 107</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τοὺς αὐτοέντας ...τιμωρεῖν τινας</quote> </cit>the murderers—whoever they be. <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Laws 716A" ref="Plat. Laws 716A"> Plat. <title>Legg.</title> 716A</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁ μὲν εὐδαιμονήσειν μέλλων...ὁ δέ τις ἐξαρθείς κ.τ.λ.</quote> </cit>The art. implies that the person exists; the indef. pron., that his name is unknown. </gloss>

data.quote:
ὁ μὲν εὐδαιμονήσειν μέλλων...ὁ δέ τις
									ἐξαρθείς κ.τ.λ.

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὰ_τούτου_290">, in the space between (the present time) and that event ( <foreign xml:lang="lat">sc.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῦ παρεῖναι αὐτόν</quote> ) </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὰ_290">as in <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰ νῦν, τὸ αὐτίκα, τὸ ἐκ τοῦδε</quote> , etc. Dem. <title>De Cor.</title> § 26 <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸν μεταξὺ χρόνον...τῶν ὅρκων</quote> , the interval between (that time, and) the oaths: <cit> <bibl n="Aristoph. Birds 187" ref="Aristoph. Birds 187"> Aristoph. <title>Av.</title> 187</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν μέσῳ...ἀήρ ἐστι γῆς</quote> </cit>, between (heaven and) earth: <cit> <bibl n="Aristoph. Ach. 433" ref="Aristoph. Ach. 433"> <title>Ach.</title> 433 </bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="trimeter"> <l>ἄνωθεν τῶν Θυεστείων ῥακῶν,</l> <l>μεταξὺ τῶν Ἰνοῦς</l> </lg> </quote> </cit>, between (them and) Ino's. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τἀπὸ_σοῦ_293">, coming from thee, urged on thy part: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Trach. 844" ref="Soph. Trach. 844"> <title>Tr.</title> 844</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰ δ’ ἀπ’ ἀλλόθρου | γνώμας μολόντ’</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Ant. 95" ref="Soph. Ant. 95"> <title>Ant.</title> 95</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὴν ἐξ ἐμοῦ δυσβουλίαν</quote> </cit> . </gloss>

data.quote:
τὰ δ’ ἀπ’ ἀλλόθρου | γνώμας
									μολόντ’

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὠνόμασται_294"> , <quote xml:lang="eng">"expressed"</quote> (rather than <quote xml:lang="eng">"mentioned"</quote> ): cp. <cit> <bibl n="Dem. 18.35" ref="Dem. 18.35"> Dem. <title>De Cor.</title> § 35</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ γὰρ τὰ ῥήματα τὰς οἰκειότητας ἔφη βεβαιοῦν, μάλα σεμνῶς ὀνομάζων</quote> </cit>(expressing himself in very stately language). </gloss>

data.quote:
οὐ γὰρ τὰ ῥήματα τὰς οἰκειότητας ἔφη
									βεβαιοῦν, μάλα σεμνῶς ὀνομάζων

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="βραχέσι_294">, not "short," but "light," "trivial": <cit> <bibl n="Thuc. 1.78" ref="Thuc. 1.78">Thuc. 1.78</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">βουλεύεσθε οὖν βραδέως ὡς οὐ περὶ βραχέων</quote> </cit>. </gloss>

data.quote:
βουλεύεσθε οὖν βραδέως ὡς οὐ περὶ
									βραχέων

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄνακτας_295">, <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> Theseus: <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. Lib. 53" ref="Aesch. Lib. 53"> Aesch. <title>Cho.</title> 53</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">δεσποτῶν θανάτοισι</quote> </cit>(Agamemnon's death). Cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 146">146</ref> , <ref n="Soph. OC 814"> 814</ref> , <ref n="Soph. OC 970">970</ref> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="διειδέναι_295">, here, <foreign xml:lang="lat">diiudicare:</foreign> usu.= <foreign xml:lang="lat">dignoscere;</foreign> <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Phaedrus 262a" ref="Plat. Phaedrus 262a"> Plat. <title>Phaedr.</title> 262 A</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὴν ὁμοιότητα...καὶ ἀνομοιότητα ἀκριβῶς διειδέναι</quote> </cit>. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 394" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg004:394"> <title>O. T.</title> 394</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">διειπεῖν</quote> </cit> ( <quote xml:lang="grc">αἴνιγμα</quote> ), to solve it.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πατρῷον_γῆς_297">, not for <quote xml:lang="grc">πατρῴας γῆς ἄστυ</quote> , but simply <quote xml:lang="eng">"his father's city in the land"</quote> (the gen. <foreign xml:lang="grc">γῆς</foreign> as <ref n="Soph. OC 45">45</ref> ), <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> the city from which Aegeus (69) had swayed Attica. The poets can use <quote xml:lang="grc">πατρῷος</quote> as= <quote xml:lang="grc">πάτριος</quote> ; but in the mouth of Oed. ( <ref n="Soph. OT 1450"> <title>O. T.</title> 1450</ref>) <quote xml:lang="grc">πατρῷον ἄστυ</quote> means the city of Laïus, and in that of Ant. ( <ref n="Soph. Ant. 937"> <title>Ant.</title> 937</ref>) the city of Oedipus: on the other hand, <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰ πάτρια...δώματα</quote> ( <ref n="Soph. OT 1394"> <title>O. T.</title> 1394</ref>), his <quote xml:lang="eng"> "ancestral"</quote> home. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔχει_297">= "is in," cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 37">37</ref> . Isocrates conceives the line of hereditary Attic kings as having been unbroken from Erichthonius down to Theseus ( <ref n="Isoc. 12.126"> <title>Panath.</title> § 126</ref>). The greatness of Athens as the centre of government was reputed to date from Theseus; but the royal seat of his predecessors was supposed to have been a lesser Athens (the acropolis and the part south of it. <ref n="Thuc. 2.15">Thuc. 2.15</ref> ), from which they swayed Attica while its communes were still independent ( <quote xml:lang="grc">σποράδην καὶ κατὰ κώμας οἰκοῦσαν</quote> , <ref n="Isoc. 10.35"> Isocr. <title>Encom. Helen.</title> § 35</ref> ). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σκοπὸς_297">refers to the quality in which the man of Colonus had presented himself to Oed. <ref n="Soph. OC 35">(35)</ref> , and so helps him at once to know who is meant. The word can mean "messenger" only in the sense of "one sent to obtain news"; but we need not change it, as Wecklein does, to <foreign xml:lang="grc">πομπός</foreign> .</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κἀμὲ_298">: see on <ref target="cl53">53</ref> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔπεμψεν_298">is better here than <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔπεμπεν</quote> , which could only mean, <quote xml:lang="eng">"was our summoner."</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="στελῶν_298">, to make him set forth, to fetch him: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 860" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg004:860"> <title>O. T.</title> 860</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">πέμψον τινὰ στελοῦντα</quote> </cit> . </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καὶ_κάρθ_301">: cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 65">65</ref> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κέλευθος_303">: cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 164">164</ref> . Some wayfarers, passing by Colonus towards Athens, may have heard the prolonged tumult of horror which greeted the name of Oedipus <ref n="Soph. OC 222">(222)</ref> . As the distance to the city is more than a mile, there will be many chances for the news to be caught up from their lips, and carried to Theseus. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πλανᾶσθαι_304">: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Cic. Rep. 1.17" ref="Cic. Rep. 1.17"> Cic. <title>Rep.</title> 1. 17</bibl> <quote xml:lang="lat">speremus nostrum nomen volitare et vagari latissime.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

data.quote:
speremus nostrum nomen volitare et
									vagari latissime.

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τῶν_304">refers to <quote xml:lang="grc"> <emph>ἔπη</emph> . ἀΐω</quote>and like verbs can take a gen. either of the <emph>person</emph> , or (as <ref n="Soph. OC 1187">1187</ref> ) of the <emph>thing</emph> , heard: though the latter is more often in the acc. (as <ref n="Soph. OC 240">240</ref> ). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θάρσει_παρέσται_305">: the same words (in another context) <ref n="Soph. OC 726">726</ref> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πολὺ_305">, with strong rumour: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 786" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg004:786"> <title>O. T.</title> 786</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑφεῖρπε γὰρ πολύ</quote> </cit> . <cit> <bibl n="Aeschin. 1.166" ref="Aeschin. 1.166">Aeschin. or. 1 § 166</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> πολὺς μὲν γὰρ ὁ Φίλιππος ἔσται <note anchored="true" place="inline" resp="RCJ" xml:lang="eng">(we shall hear a great deal of him),</note> ἀναμιχθήσεται δὲ καὶ τὸ τοῦ παιδὸς ὄνομα Ἀλεξάνδρου</quote> </cit>.</gloss>

data.quote:
πολὺς μὲν γὰρ ὁ Φίλιππος ἔσται   ἀναμιχθήσεται δὲ καὶ τὸ τοῦ
							παιδὸς ὄνομα Ἀλεξάνδρου

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κεἰ_εὕδει_306">, even if he is reposing (from affairs), and is unwilling to move. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εὕδω_306">, in the <emph>fig.</emph> sense ( <ref n="Soph. OT 65"> <title>O. T.</title> 65</ref>), is more often said of things (as <quote xml:lang="grc">εὕδει πόντος</quote> , etc., cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 621">621</ref> ) than of men: but <quote xml:lang="grc"> καθεύδω</quote> , at least, was often thus used: <cit> <bibl n="Plut. Pomp. 15" ref="Plut. Pomp. 15"> Plut. <title>Pomp.</title> 15</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὥρα μέντοι σοι μὴ καθεύδειν ἀλλὰ προσέχειν τοῖς πράγμασιν</quote> </cit>. The conjectures <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἕρπει</foreign> and <foreign xml:lang="grc">σπεύδει</foreign> (the latter referring, not happily, to <quote xml:lang="grc">σπεῦδε βραδέως</quote> ) both suppose that Theseus lingers by the way. But, if he started, he would scarcely loiter. </gloss>

data.quote:
ὥρα μέντοι σοι μὴ καθεύδειν ἀλλὰ
									προσέχειν τοῖς πράγμασιν

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="βραδύς_306">here=indisposed to exertion (as <quote xml:lang="grc">βραδύς</quote> is joined with <quote xml:lang="grc">μαλακός</quote> in <ref n="Plat. Stat. 307A"> Plat. <title> Polit.</title> 307A</ref>, and <quote xml:lang="grc">βραδύτης</quote> with <quote xml:lang="grc">ἡσυχιότης</quote> in <ref n="Plat. Charm. 160B"> <title>Charm.</title> 160 B</ref>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κλύων_σοῦ_307">(gen. of connection), hearing <emph>about</emph> thee, <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 317" ref="Soph. El. 317"> <title>El.</title> 317</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῦ κασιγνήτου τί φής</quote> </cit> ; <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Phil. 439" ref="Soph. Phil. 439"> <title>Ph.</title> 439</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀναξίου μὲν φωτὸς ἐξερήσομαι</quote> </cit> : <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Od. 11.174" ref="Hom. Od. 11.174"> <title>Od.</title> 11.174</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰπὲ δέ μοι πατρός τε καὶ vἱέος</quote> </cit>. Cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 355">355.</ref> </gloss>

data.quote:
εἰπὲ δέ μοι πατρός τε καὶ
									vἱέος

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τίς_ἐσθλός_309">. Oedipus has hinted to the Chorus that he brings <quote xml:lang="grc">ὄνησιν ἀστοῖς τοῖσδε</quote> , but has reserved all explanation of his meaning until Theseus shall arrive <ref n="Soph. OC 288">(288)</ref> . His exclamation here again touches on his secret; but, instead of interpreting <foreign xml:lang="grc">εὐτυχής</foreign> , he turns it off, for the present, by a quickly-added commonplace. "Does not experience, indeed, teach us that the benefactor of others is often his own?" The generous man, though he acts from no calculation of self-interest, actually serves himself by making zealous friends. Like thoughts are found in many popular shapes elsewhere: <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Il. 13.734" ref="Hom. Il. 13.734"> <title>Il.</title> 13.734</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <note anchored="true" place="inline" resp="RCJ" xml:lang="eng">(of the man with <foreign xml:lang="grc">νόος ἐσθλός</foreign>no/os e)s1qlo/s</note>καί τε πολέας ἐσάωσε, μάλιστά τέ κ’ αὐτὸς ἀνέγνω</quote> </cit>, "he saveth many, yea, and he himself best recognises (the worth of wisdom)": <cit> <bibl> Menander <title>Sentent.</title> 141</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐσθλῷ γὰρ ἀνδρί [ γ’ ] ἐσθλὰ καὶ διδοῖ θεός</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl> <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 391</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ξένοις ἐπαρκῶν τῶν ἴσων τεύξῃ ποτέ</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl> <title>Hortat.</title> 23</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁ χρηστός, ὡς ἔοικε, καὶ χρηστοὺς ποιεῖ</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl> pseudo - Philem. <foreign xml:lang="lat">ap.</foreign> Boissonad. <title>Anecd.</title> 1. 147</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">μετέρχεται τὸ δίκαιον εἰς πλεονεξίαν</quote> </cit>. Conversely, <cit> <quote xml:lang="grc">οἷ αὐτῷ κακὰ τεύχει ἀνὴρ ἄλλῳ κακὰ τεύχων</quote> <bibl n="Hes. WD 265" ref="Hes. WD 265"> (Hes. <title>Opp.</title> 265)</bibl> </cit> , <cit> <quote xml:lang="grc">λίαν φιλῶν σεαυτὸν οὐδ’ ἕξεις φίλον</quote> <bibl> (Men. <title>Sent.</title> 310)</bibl> </cit>. We should not suppose a suppressed clause: ("I do not say, to <emph> himself</emph> ,") "for what good man is <emph>not</emph> a friend to himself?" The interest of the king is identified with that of his realm. To distinguish them so sharply is unfitting here. Cp. 1124, 1496, 1553. The conjecture <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἔσθ’ ὃς</foreign> (for <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἐσθλὸς</foreign> ) makes Oed. apologise for the selfishness of <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἐμοί τε</foreign> : "for who is not his own friend?" (!) </gloss>

data.quote: καί τε πολέας ἐσάωσε, μάλιστά τέ κ’ αὐτὸς ἀνέγνω

ἐσθλῷ γὰρ ἀνδρί [ γ’ ] ἐσθλὰ καὶ διδοῖ
									θεός

ξένοις ἐπαρκῶν τῶν ἴσων τεύξῃ
									ποτέ

ὁ χρηστός, ὡς ἔοικε, καὶ χρηστοὺς
									ποιεῖ

οἷ αὐτῷ κακὰ τεύχει ἀνὴρ ἄλλῳ κακὰ
									τεύχων

λίαν φιλῶν σεαυτὸν οὐδ’ ἕξεις
									φίλον

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τίλέξω_310">, here prob. fut. ind. rather than aor. subj. (though <cit> <bibl n="Soph.
