OC 315" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg007:315">315</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τί φῶ;</quote> </cit>): cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 1419" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg004:1419"> <title>O. T.</title> 1419</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οἴμοι, τί δῆτα λέξομεν πρὸς τόνδ’ ἔπος;</quote> </cit> <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Phil. 1233" ref="Soph. Phil. 1233"> <title>Ph.</title>1233</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὦ Ζεῦ, τί λέξεις;</quote> </cit>For fut. ind. combined with aor. subj., cp. <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Ion 758" ref="Eur. Ion 758"> Eur. <title>Ion</title> 758</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἴπωμεν ἢ σιγῶμεν ἢ τί δράσομεν;</quote> </cit> </gloss>

data.quote:
οἴμοι, τί δῆτα λέξομεν πρὸς τόνδ’
									ἔπος;

εἴπωμεν ἢ σιγῶμεν ἢ τί
									δράσομεν;

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ποῖ_φρενῶν_310">: see on <ref target="cl170">170</ref> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τί_ἔστι_311"> (cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 46">46</ref> ) marking surprise, as <ref n="Soph. OT 319"> <title>O. T.</title> 319</ref>(n.), <ref n="Soph. OT 1144">1144</ref> etc. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Αἰτναίας_πώλου_312">, not seen, of course, by the spectators: Ismene leaves it with her servant <ref n="Soph. OC 334">(334)</ref> , and enters on foot <ref n="Soph. OC 320">(320)</ref> . Sicily having a reputation both for its horses ( <ref> Oppian <title>Cyneg.</title> 1. 170</ref>) and for its mules ( <ref>Photius 366. 12</ref> ), some understand a <emph>mule</emph> here, as that animal (with an easy saddle, <foreign xml:lang="grc"> ἀστράβη</foreign> ) was much used for such journeys. But though <quote xml:lang="grc">πῶλος</quote> , <emph>with a defining word</emph> (as <cit> <quote xml:lang="grc">τῶν καμήλων</quote> <bibl n="Aristot. HA 1.1.47" ref="Aristot. HA 1.1.47"> Arist. <title>Hist. An.</title> 1.1.47</bibl> </cit> , <cit> <quote xml:lang="grc">κύνεοι</quote> <bibl> <title>Anthol.</title> 12. 238</bibl> </cit>) could denote the young of animals other than the horse, <quote xml:lang="grc">πῶλος</quote> <emph>alone</emph> would always mean a young horse. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Αἰτναίας_312">implies some choice breed, as in <ref n="Thphr. Char. 21"> Theophr. <title> Char.</title> XXI</ref>(=VII in my 1st ed.) the <quote xml:lang="grc"> μικροφιλότιμος</quote> buys <quote xml:lang="grc">Λακωνικὰς κύνας, Σικελικὰς περιστεράς</quote> , etc. In <ref n="Aristoph. Peace 73"> Aristoph. <title> Pax</title> 73</ref>the <quote xml:lang="grc">Αἰτναῖος μέγιστος κάνθαρος</quote> is not a mere joke on the Etna breed of <emph>horses</emph> , but alludes to a species of beetle actually found there (cp. <ref>Aesch. fr. 229</ref> , <ref> Plato Com. <title xml:lang="grc">ἑορτ.</title> fr. 13</ref>, quoted by schol. ad loc.).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κρατὶ_313">: locative dat., "on her head," rather than dat. of interest with <quote xml:lang="grc">ἡλιοστερὴς</quote> , "for her head." The <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἡλιοστερής</foreign> of the MSS. is a very strange word. It ought to mean "deprived of the sun": cp. <cit> <quote xml:lang="grc">βιοστερής</quote> <bibl n="Soph. OC 747" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg007:747">747</bibl> </cit>, <cit> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὀμματοστερής</quote> <bibl n="Soph. OC 1260" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg007:1260">1260</bibl> </cit>. Even with an active sense, "depriving of the sun," it is awkward. It could not mean "sun- <emph>averting.</emph> " In <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. Supp. 1063" ref="Aesch. Supp. 1063"> Aesch. <title>Suppl.</title> 1063</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">Ζεὺς...ἀποστεροίη γάμον</quote> </cit>is not, "may he avert from us," but "may he take away (from our foes)": Hartung would read <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀποστρέφοι μοι</quote> . (1) <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἡλιοσκεπὴς</foreign> (Nauck) is supported by <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Il. 16.224" ref="Hom. Il. 16.224"> <title>Il.</title> 16.224</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">χλαινάων ἀνεμοσκεπέων</quote> </cit>, and (2) <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἡλιοστεγὴς</foreign> (Coraes) by the use of <quote xml:lang="grc">στέγω</quote> as <quote xml:lang="eng">"to keep out."</quote> The latter seems most applicable to <emph>rain:</emph> cp. <cit> <bibl n="Pind. P. 4" ref="Pind. P. 4"> Pind. <title>P.</title> 4. 81</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀμφὶ δὲ παρδαλέᾳ στέγετο φρίσσοντας ὄμβρους</quote> </cit>, <cit> <bibl> <title>Anthol. P.</title> 6. 90</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">πῖλον...ὑδασιστεγῆ</quote> </cit>: the former, to heat, cold, or wind: cp. <ref> <title>Anthol. P.</title> 6. 335</ref>, on a <quote xml:lang="grc"> καυσία</quote> (a broad-brimmed felt hat, used in Macedonia—from <quote xml:lang="grc">καῦσις</quote> ), <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ σκέπας ἐν νιφετῷ, καὶ κόρυς ἐν πολέμῳ.</quote> </gloss>

data.quote:
ἀμφὶ δὲ παρδαλέᾳ στέγετο φρίσσοντας
									ὄμβρους

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Θεσσαλὶς_κυνῆ_313">, a form of the Thessalian <foreign xml:lang="grc">πέτασος</foreign> , a felt hat (somewhat like our "wide-awake") with brim, worn esp. by travellers: cp. schol. on <ref n="Aristoph. Birds 1203"> Aristoph. <title> Av.</title> 1203</ref>(where Iris enters with a <quote xml:lang="grc"> κυνῆ</quote> ), <quote xml:lang="grc">κυνῆ δέ, ὅτι ἔχει περικεφαλαίαν τὸ πέτασον</quote> . In the <title>Inachus</title> Soph. made Iris wear an <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἀρκὰς κυνῆ</quote> <ref>(fr. 251)</ref> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πρόσωπα_314">: <cit> <bibl n="Aristoph. Lys. 542" ref="Aristoph. Lys. 542"> Aristoph. <title>Lys.</title> 542</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδὲ γόνατ’ ὰν κόπος ἕλοι με</quote> </cit> . </gloss>

data.quote: οὐδὲ γόνατ’ ὰν κόπος ἕλοι με

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πλανᾷ_316">, misleads (me): the act. never="to wander." <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Prot. 356D" ref="Plat. Prot. 356D"> Plat. <title>Prot.</title> 356D</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">αὕτη μὲν <note anchored="true" place="inline" resp="RCJ" xml:lang="eng"> ( <foreign xml:lang="lat">sc.</foreign> sc.<quote xml:lang="grc">ἡ τοῦ φαινομένου δύναμις</quote>h( tou= fainome/nou du/namis</note>ἡμᾶς ἐπλάνα</quote> </cit> . <cit> <bibl n="Hor. Carm. 3.4.5" ref="Hor. Carm. 3.4.5"> Hor. <title>Carm.</title> 3. 4. 5</bibl> <quote xml:lang="lat">an me ludit amabilis Insania?</quote> </cit> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τί_φῶ_317">, the delib. subj. in a dependent clause ( <foreign xml:lang="grc">τί</foreign> might be <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅ τι</quote> ): cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 71"> <title>O. T.</title> 71</ref>n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φαιδρὰ_319">, neut. acc. plur. as adverb: cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 1695">1695</ref> : <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 883" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg004:883"> <title>O. T.</title> 883</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑπέροπτα...πορεύεται</quote> </cit> (n.). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σαίνει_με_319">, greets me: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. Ag. 725" ref="Aesch. Ag. 725"> Aesch. <title>Agam.</title> 725</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <note anchored="true" place="inline" resp="RCJ" xml:lang="eng">(the young lion)</note>φαιδρωπὸς ποτὶ χεῖρα σαίνων <note anchored="true" place="inline" resp="RCJ" xml:lang="eng">("fawning")</note> </quote> </cit> : <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Ant. 1214" ref="Soph. Ant. 1214"> Soph. <title>Ant.</title> 1214</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">παιδός με σαίνει φθόγγος</quote> </cit> , <quote xml:lang="eng">"greets mine ear."</quote> <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Rh. 55" ref="Eur. Rh. 55"> [Eur.] <title>Rhes.</title> 55</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">σαίνει μ’ ἔννυχος φρυκτωρία</quote> </cit>, the beacon flashes on my sight. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὦ_κασιγν_324"> . <foreign xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</foreign> = <quote xml:lang="grc">ὦ πάτερ καὶ κασιγνήτη, δισσὰ ἐμοὶ ἥδιστα προσφωνήματα</quote> , two names most sweet for me to use: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Orest. 1049" ref="Eur. Orest. 1049"> <title>Or.</title> 1049</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="trimeter"> <l>ὦ στέρν’ ἀδελφῆς …</l> <l>τάδ’ ἀντὶ παίδων καὶ γαμηλίου λέχους</l> <l>προσφθέγματ’ ἀμφὶ τοῖς ταλαιπώροις πάρα</l> </lg> </quote> </cit> . <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Supp. 802" ref="Eur. Supp. 802"> <title>Suppl.</title> 802</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="trimeter"> <l>ὦ παῖδες, ὦ πικρὸν φίλων</l> <l>προσηγόρημα ματέρων</l> </lg> </quote> </cit>("sons," a name bitter for your mothers to utter). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δεύτερον_326">, when I <emph>have</emph> found you. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λύπῃ_326">, caus. dat.: <ref>Archilochus fr. 101</ref> (strong emotion) <quote xml:lang="grc">πολλὴν κατ’ ἀχλὺν ὀμμάτων ἔχευεν</quote> .</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὁρᾶν_327">, epexeg. inf.: so <cit> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄτλητον … ὁρᾶν,</quote> <bibl n="Soph. OT 792" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg004:792"> <title>O. T.</title> 792</bibl> </cit>. The form <foreign xml:lang="grc">δύσμοιρος</foreign> only here. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὦ_τροφαί_330">, wretched mode of <emph>life</emph> <ref n="Soph. OC 338">(338)</ref> , — referring to the outward signs of suffering and destitution on which Creon dwells, <ref n="Soph. OC 745">745 ff.</ref> : cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 1250">1250 ff</ref> . By his reply, <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἦ τῆσδε κἀμοῦ;</foreign> Oed. seems to hint that she separates herself from those whom she pities. Ismene with quick sensibility rejoins, <foreign xml:lang="grc">δυσμόρου τ’ ἐμοῦ τρίτης</foreign> , the life is to be mine, too, in your company (for <foreign xml:lang="grc">τρίτης</foreign> cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 8">8</ref> ). Dindorf's conjecture, <foreign xml:lang="grc">ὦ δὔ ἀθλίω τροφά</foreign> , makes the subsequent question of Oedipus appear needless. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὅμαιμον_330">expresses the sisters' relation to <emph>each other</emph> only ( <quote xml:lang="eng">"my children and my sisters"</quote> would be <quote xml:lang="grc">δύσφημον</quote> here). In Soph. <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅμαιμος, ὁμαίμων</quote> always refer to brother or sister: <ref n="Soph. OC 323">323</ref> , <ref n="Soph. OC 979">979</ref> , <ref n="Soph. OC 1275">1275</ref> , <ref n="Soph. OC 1405">1405</ref> , <ref n="Soph. OC 1772">1772</ref> : <ref n="Soph. Ant. 486"> <title>Ant.</title> 486</ref>, <ref n="Soph. OC 512">512 f.</ref> : <ref n="Soph. El. 12"> <title>El.</title> 12</ref>, <ref n="Soph. OC 325">325</ref> , <ref n="Soph. OC 531">531</ref> : <ref n="Soph. OT 639"> <title>O. T.</title> 639</ref>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σῇ_332">(caus. dat.)=an objective gen. <quote xml:lang="grc">σοῦ</quote> : <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 969" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg004:969"> <title>O. T.</title> 969</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὠμῷ πόθῳ</quote> </cit> (n.). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πότερα_333">, instead of <quote xml:lang="grc">πότερον</quote> , to avoid an anapaest: <ref n="Soph. Aj. 265"> <title>Ai.</title> 265</ref>n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πόθοισι_333">; (causal:) was it because thou wast fain to see me after so long a time? (or was there some further <emph>special</emph> cause?) Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 531" ref="Soph. Aj. 531"> <title>Ai.</title> 531</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">φόβοισί γ’ αὐτὸν ἐξελυσάμην.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λόγων_αὐτάγγ_333">. object.gen., <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐτὴλόγουσἀγγέλλουσα</quote> . <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. Ag. 646" ref="Aesch. Ag. 646"> Aesch. <title>Ag.</title> 646</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">πραγμάτων εὐάγγελον.</quote> </cit> <foreign xml:lang="grc">λόγοις</foreign>would be a dat. of circumstance ("with"), but very harsh. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ξὺν_μόνῳ_334"> = <quote xml:lang="grc">ξὺν</quote> ( <quote xml:lang="grc"> τούτῳ</quote> ) <quote xml:lang="grc">οἰκετῶν ὅνπερ εἶχον πιστὸν μόνον</quote> , the attrac tion of the relative extending to the predicative adj.: <cit> <bibl n="Dem. 18.298" ref="Dem. 18.298"> Dem. <title>De Cor.</title> § 298</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὔτε φόβος οὔτ’ ἄλλο οὐδὲν ἐπῆρεν...ὧν ἔκρινα δικαίων καὶ συμφερόντων τῇ πόλει οὐδὲν προδοῦναι</quote> </cit>. </gloss>

data.quote:
οὔτε φόβος οὔτ’ ἄλλο οὐδὲν ἐπῆρεν...ὧν
									ἔκρινα δικαίων καὶ συμφερόντων τῇ πόλει οὐδὲν
									προδοῦναι

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πονεῖν_335">, epexeg. infin. with <foreign xml:lang="grc">ποῦ</foreign> ( <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰσι</quote> ): so as to do their part. The infin. was thus used in <emph>affirmative</emph> clauses (esp. after <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅδε</quote> ), as <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Il. 9.688" ref="Hom. Il. 9.688"> <title>Il.</title> 9.688</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰσὶ καὶ οἵδε τάδ’ εἰπέμεν, οἵ μοι ἕποντο</quote> </cit>, here are these also <emph>to tell the tale</emph> , who went with me: <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Hipp. 294" ref="Eur. Hipp. 294"> Eur. <title>Hipp.</title> 294</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">γυναῖκες αἴδε συγκαθιστάναι νόσον</quote> </cit>, here are women <emph>to help</emph> in soothing thy trouble. So on the affirmative <quote xml:lang="grc">οἵδε εἰσὶ πονεῖν</quote> ( <quote xml:lang="eng"> "here they are <emph>to serve</emph> "</quote>) is modelled the interrogative <quote xml:lang="grc">ποῦ εἰσὶ πονεῖν</quote> ; <quote xml:lang="eng">"where are they, that they may serve (as they are bound to do)?"</quote> So <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Orest. 1473" ref="Eur. Orest. 1473"> Eur. <title>Or.</title> 1473</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ποῦ δῆτ’ ἀμύνειν οἱ κατὰ στέγας Φρύγες;</quote> </cit> </gloss>

data.quote:
εἰσὶ καὶ οἵδε τάδ’ εἰπέμεν, οἵ μοι
									ἕποντο

ποῦ δῆτ’ ἀμύνειν οἱ κατὰ στέγας
									Φρύγες;

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ποῦ_335">(the scholiast's reading) is right. <foreign xml:lang="grc">ποῖ</foreign> supposes a very harsh ellipse of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἥκουσιν</quote> or the like, and agrees less well with the reply. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὗπέρ_εἰσι_336">: on <ref target="cl273">273</ref> . Schaerer's <foreign xml:lang="grc"> τἀν</foreign> is better than the MS. <foreign xml:lang="grc">δ’ ἐν</foreign> because the hint is made more impressive by the abruptness. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τανῦν_336">is adv. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Αἰγύπτῳ_337">. <cit> <bibl n="Hdt. 2.35" ref="Hdt. 2.35">Her. 2.35</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰ πολλὰ πάντα ἔμπαλιν τοῖσι ἄλλοισι ἀνθρώποισι ἐστήσαντο ἤθεά τε καὶ νόμους· ἐν τοῖσι αἱ μὲν γυναῖκες ἀγοράζουσι καὶ καπηλεύουσι, οἱ δὲ ἄνδρες κατ οἴκους ἐόντες ὑφἀίνουσι</quote> </cit>. Soph. certainly seems to have had this passage of his friend's work in view: else it would be strange that v. 341 should correspond so exactly with the special tasks ascribed to the <emph>women</emph> by Her. So the reference in <ref n="Soph. El. 62"> <title>El.</title> 62</ref>to the (supposed) dead returning recalls the Thracian Salmoxis in <ref n="Hdt. 4.95">Her. 4.95</ref> , and the disputed passage <ref n="Soph. Ant. 905"> <title>Ant.</title> 905</ref>ff. recalls the wife of Intaphernes in <ref n="Hdt. 3.119">Her. 3.119</ref> . Nymphodorus of Syracuse ( <date when="-0275">275 B.C.?</date> ), in the 3rd book (read <quote xml:lang="grc">γ’</quote> for <quote xml:lang="grc">ιγ’</quote> in the schol. here, <ref> Müller <emph>fr. Hist.</emph> 2. 380</ref>) of his <quote xml:lang="grc">Νόμιμα Βαρβαρικά</quote> , repeated the statement of Her., adding (prob. of himself) that Sesostris had thus sought to tame the men of Egypt to his sway. Anaxandrides, of the Middle Comedy ( <date when="-0340"> <foreign xml:lang="lat">circ.</foreign> 340 B.C.</date>), in his <title xml:lang="grc">Πόλεις</title> , represented the Athenians as rejecting an Egyptian alliance on the ground of the opposition between the manners of Greece and Egypt: — <cit> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὔθ’ οἱ τρόποι γὰρ ὁμονοοῦς), οὔθ’ οἱ νόμοι | ἡμῶν</quote> <bibl> ( <title>Fragm. Com.</title> Bothe p. 426)</bibl> </cit> . </gloss>

data.quote:
τὰ πολλὰ πάντα ἔμπαλιν τοῖσι ἄλλοισι
									ἀνθρώποισι ἐστήσαντο ἤθεά τε καὶ νόμους· ἐν τοῖσι αἱ μὲν
									γυναῖκες ἀγοράζουσι καὶ καπηλεύουσι, οἱ δὲ ἄνδρες κατ
									οἴκους ἐόντες ὑφἀίνουσι

οὔθ’ οἱ τρόποι γὰρ ὁμονοοῦς), οὔθ’ οἱ
									νόμοι | ἡμῶν

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φύσιν_338">, <ref n="Soph. OC 270">270</ref> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τροφάς_338">, <ref n="Soph. OC 330">330</ref> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἱστουργοῦντες_340"> : <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Il. 6.490" ref="Hom. Il. 6.490"> <title>Il.</title> 6.490</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="hexameter"> <l> <note anchored="true" place="inline" resp="RCJ" xml:lang="eng">(Hector to Andromache) </note>ἀλλ’ εἰς οἶκον ἰοῦσα τὰ σ’ αὐτῆς ἔργα κόμιζε,</l> <l>ἱστόν τ’ ἠλακάτην τε, καὶ ἀμφιπόλοισι κέλευε</l> <l>ἔργον ἐποίχεσθαι· πόλεμος δ’ ἄνδρεσσι μελήσει</l> </lg> </quote> </cit> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τἄξω_τροφεῖα_341">, those means of supporting life which are sought outside of the home,—paraphrasing the <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀγοράζουσι καὶ καπηλεύουσι</quote> of <ref n="Hdt. 2.35">Her. 2.35</ref> . Elsewhere <quote xml:lang="grc">τροφεῖα</quote> always = "reward for rearing" ( <ref n="Plat. Rep. 520B"> Plat. <title>Rep.</title> 520B</ref>, etc.). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σφῷν_δ_342">, dat. of interest, "for you two" (Ant. and Ism.), in your case. Some take it as partitive gen.: then it would mean, "of you <emph>two pairs</emph> ," — the pair of brothers being one unit, and the pair of sisters another. But I know no parallel for such an use of a dual pronoun. It is different when <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄμφω</quote> is said of two "sides" or armies, considered as units ( <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Il. 2.123" ref="Hom. Il. 2.123"> <title>Il.</title> 2.123</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἴπερ γάρ κ’ ἐθέλοιμεν Ἀχαιοί τε Τρῶές τε | … ἀριθμηθήμεναι ἄμφω</quote> </cit>): or when a dual verb has a twofold dual subject, <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Il. 8.185" ref="Hom. Il. 8.185"> <title>Il.</title> 8.185</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="hexameter"> <l>Ξάνθε τε καὶ σύ, Πόδαργε, καὶ Αἴθων Λάμπε τε δῖε</l> <l>νῦν μοι τὴν κομιδὴν ἀποτίνετον</l> </lg> </quote> </cit> . </gloss>

data.quote:
εἴπερ γάρ κ’ ἐθέλοιμεν Ἀχαιοί τε Τρῶές
									τε | … ἀριθμηθήμεναι ἄμφω

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οἰκουροῦσιν_343">, not <quote xml:lang="grc">οἰκουρεῖτον</quote> , though a dual follows <ref n="Soph. OC 345">(345)</ref> : <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 1511" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg004:1511"> <title>O. T.</title> 1511f.</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰχέτην...εὔχεσθε</quote> </cit> : <cit> <bibl n="Xen. Cyrop. 6.1.47" ref="Xen. Cyrop. 6.1.47"> Xen. <title>Cyr.</title> 6.1.47</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς εἰδέτην...ἠσπάσαντο ἀλλήλους</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Prot. 330C" ref="Plat. Prot. 330C"> Plat. <title>Prot.</title> 330C</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἴπετον δή μοι...ὃ ὠνομάσατε ἄρτι</quote> </cit>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὥστε_343">= <foreign xml:lang="grc">ὡς</foreign> , an epic use freq. in Aesch. and Soph. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="παρθένοι_343"> . <cit> <bibl n="Dem. 59.86" ref="Dem. 59.86"> [Dem.] <title>In Neaer.</title> (or. 59) § 86</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἱκανὸν φόβον ταῖς γυναιξὶ παρασκευάζων τοῦ σωφρονεῖν καὶ μηδὲν ἁμαρτάνειν ἀλλὰ δικαίως οἰκουρεῖν</quote> </cit>. <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Orest. 928" ref="Eur. Orest. 928"> Eur. <title>Or.</title> 928</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="trimeter"> <l>εἰ τἄνδον οἰκουρήμαθ’ οἱ λελειμμένοι</l> <l>φθείρουσιν</l> </lg> </quote> </cit> . </gloss>

data.quote:
ἱκανὸν φόβον ταῖς γυναιξὶ παρασκευάζων
									τοῦ σωφρονεῖν καὶ μηδὲν ἁμαρτάνειν ἀλλὰ δικαίως
									οἰκουρεῖν

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τἀμὰ_δυστήνου_344"> : <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Phil. 1126" ref="Soph. Phil. 1126"> <title>Ph.</title> 1126</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰν ἐμὰν μελέου τροφάν</quote> </cit>: so <cit> <quote xml:lang="lat">nostros vidisti flentis ocellos</quote> <bibl n="Ov. Ep. 5" ref="Ov. Ep. 5"> Ov. <title>Her.</title> 5. 43.</bibl> </cit> <quote xml:lang="grc">τἀμὰ … κακὰ</quote>: cognate acc. to <foreign xml:lang="grc">ὑπερπονεῖτον</foreign> (like <quote xml:lang="grc">πονεῖν πόνους</quote> ), "ye <emph>bear</emph> the woes of me hapless <emph>for</emph> me" ( <quote xml:lang="grc">δυστήνου</quote> , placed between art. and noun, must not be taken with <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑπερππονεῖτον</quote> ). Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Laws 717C" ref="Plat. Laws 717C"> Plat. <title>Legg.</title> 717C</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <note anchored="true" place="inline" resp="RCJ" xml:lang="eng">(a son must cherish his aged parents)</note>ἀποτίνοντα δανείσματα ἐπιμελείας τε καὶ ὑπερπονουντων ὠδῖνας παλαιὰς ἐπὶ νέοις δανεισθείσας</quote> </cit>, requiting...their pangs of old, when they suffered for him.</gloss>

data.quote:
ἀποτίνοντα δανείσματα
							ἐπιμελείας τε καὶ ὑπερπονουντων ὠδῖνας παλαιὰς ἐπὶ νέοις δανεισθείσας

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="νέας_ἔληξε_345">, ceased to need the tender care which is given to children. <foreign xml:lang="grc">νέα τροφή</foreign> , here, "the nurture ( <emph>not</emph> "growth") of the young": so <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 510" ref="Soph. Aj. 510"> <title>Ai.</title> 510</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">νέας | τροφῆς στερηθείς</quote> </cit>, bereft of the tendance which childhood needs: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 1143" ref="Soph. El. 1143"> <title>El.</title> 1143</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <note anchored="true" place="inline" resp="RCJ" xml:lang="eng">(speaking of her brother's infancy) </note>τροφῆς | … τὴν … ἀμφὶ σοὶ | παρέσχον</quote> </cit>. But in <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 1" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg004:1"> <title>O. T.</title> 1</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">νέα τροφή</quote> </cit>= "last-born nurslings." </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κατίσχυσεν_345"> , <emph>became</emph> strong (ingressive aor.), <foreign xml:lang="grc">δέμας</foreign> , <quote xml:lang="eng">"in body"</quote> (acc. of respect). This compound verb, though metrically convenient, seems not to occur elsewhere before the <date from="-0200" to="-0100">2nd cent. B.C.</date> : it was usu. intrans., as <cit> <bibl n="Plb. 11.13" ref="Plb. 11.13">Polyb. 11. 13</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">κατίσχυον καὶ τῷ πλήθει καὶ ταῖς εὐχειρίαις</quote> </cit>(began to <emph> prevail</emph> in the battle). <cit> <bibl n="Matthew 16.18" ref="Matthew 16.18">Evang. Matth. xvi. 18</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">πύλαι ᾅδου οὐ κατισχύουσιν αὐτῆς</quote> </cit>. </gloss>

data.quote:
κατίσχυον καὶ τῷ πλήθει καὶ ταῖς
									εὐχειρίαις

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γερονταγωγεῖ_348">, on the analogy of <quote xml:lang="grc">παιδαγωγεῖν</quote> (so, in late Greek, <quote xml:lang="grc">ξεναγωγεῖν</quote> for <quote xml:lang="grc">ξεναγεῖν</quote> ): <cit> <bibl n="Aristoph. Kn. 1098" ref="Aristoph. Kn. 1098"> Aristoph. <title>Eq.</title> 1098</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <note anchored="true" place="inline" resp="RCJ" xml:lang="eng">("i give myself to thee," says demus)</note>γερονταγωγεῖν κἀναπαιδεύειν πάλιν</quote> </cit>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="νηλίπους_349">: schol. <quote xml:lang="grc">α·νυπόδητος</quote> . <cit> <bibl n="Apollon. 3.646" ref="Apollon. 3.646">Apoll. Rhod. 3. 646</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">νήλιπος, οἰέανος</quote> </cit>(shoeless, with only a tunic): <cit> <bibl n="Theoc. 4" ref="Theoc. 4">Theocr. 4. 56</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰς ὄρος ὅκχ’ ἕρπῃς, μὴ ἀνάλιπος ἔρχεο, Βάττε</quote> </cit>: where schol. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἦλιψ γὰρ τὸ ὑπόδημα</quote> . If the word really comes from an <quote xml:lang="grc">ἦλιψ</quote> (of which there is no other trace), then <foreign xml:lang="grc">νηλίπους</foreign> (used also by <ref>Lycophron 635</ref> , who, however, may have followed Soph.) is less correct than <quote xml:lang="grc">νήλιπος</quote> , which Blomfield ( <ref n="Aesch. PB 248"> Aesch. <title>PV</title> 248</ref>) wished to restore here. <ref>Eustathius 787. 52</ref> derives <quote xml:lang="grc">νήλιπος</quote> from <quote xml:lang="grc">λίπος</quote> (fat, unguent), explaining it by <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐχμηρὸς καὶ ἀλιπής</quote> ( <quote xml:lang="eng">"unkempt"</quote> ). </gloss>

data.quote:
εἰς ὄρος ὅκχ’ ἕρπῃς, μὴ ἀνάλιπος
									ἔρχεο, Βάττε

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἡγεῖται_351">. The sentence <quote xml:lang="grc">γερονταγωγεῖ, πολλὰ μὲν ... ἀλωμένη, πολλοῖσι δ’ ὄμβρ. μοχθοῦσα</quote> , is so far regular and complete: then we should have expected <quote xml:lang="grc">ἡγουμένη</quote> , introducing a comment on the <emph>whole</emph> sentence. Instead, we have <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἡγεῖται</foreign> , which draws <quote xml:lang="grc">μοχθοῦσα</quote> to itself, and thus breaks the symmetry of the antithesis. The substitution of a finite verb for a second participial clause is freq. in Greek; but is usu. managed as if here we had <quote xml:lang="grc">πολλὰ μὲν...ἀλωμένη, πολλοῖς δ’ ὄμβρ. μοχθεῖ, ἡγουμένη</quote> etc. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 190" ref="Soph. El. 190"> <title>El.</title> 190</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="lyric"> <l>οἰκονομῶ θαλάμους πατρός, ὦδε μὲν</l> <l>ἀεικεῖ σὺν στολᾷ,</l> <l>κεναῖς δ’ ἀμφίσταμαι τραπέζαις</l> </lg> </quote> </cit>(instead of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀμφισταμένη</quote> ): <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Phil. 213" ref="Soph. Phil. 213"> <title>Ph.</title> 213 ff.</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ μολπὰν...ἔχων,—...ἀλλὰ...βοᾷ</quote> </cit>(instead of <quote xml:lang="grc">βοῶν</quote> ): <cit> <bibl n="Lys. 12.15" ref="Lys. 12.15">Lys. or. 12 § 15</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐδόκει μοι ταύτῃ πειρᾶσθαι σωθῆ\ναι, ἐνθυμουμένῳ ὅτι, ἐὰν μὲν λάθω, σωθήσομαι·, ἐὰν δὲ ληφθῶ, ἡγούμην</quote> </cit>etc.: and <ref n="Soph. OT 1134"> <title>O. T.</title> 1134</ref>n. See also <ref n="Soph. Aj. 806"> <title>Ai.</title> 806</ref>(n. on <foreign xml:lang="grc">ζητεῖτ’</foreign> ). </gloss>

data.quote:
ἐδόκει μοι ταύτῃ πειρᾶσθαι σωθῆ\ναι,
							ἐνθυμουμένῳ ὅτι, ἐὰν μὲν λάθω, σωθήσομαι·, ἐὰν δὲ ληφθῶ,
									ἡγούμην

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὰ_τῆς_351">. There are only three other instances in Soph. of the art. so placed: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Phil. 263" ref="Soph. Phil. 263"> <title>Ph.</title> 263</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">Φιλοκτήτης ὃν οἱ | δισσοὶ στρατηγοί</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Ant. 409" ref="Soph. Ant. 409"> <title>Ant.</title> 409</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">κόνιν σήραντες ἢ κατεῖχε τὸν | νέκυν</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 879" ref="Soph. El. 879"> <title>El.</title> 879</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">κἀπὶ τοῖς | σαυτῆς κακοῖσι</quote> </cit>. Close cohesion in thought and utterance is the excuse for this, as for the elision of <quote xml:lang="grc">δ’, τ’, ταῦτ’</quote> at the end of a v. ( <ref n="Soph. OT 29"> <title>O. T.</title> 29</ref>n.). </gloss>

data.quote:
κόνιν σήραντες ἢ κατεῖχε τὸν |
									νέκυν

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἰ_ἔχοι_352">is an abstract statement of the condition: — " <title>Supposing</title> him to have tendance, she is content." For optat. in protasis, with pres. ind. in apodosis, cp. <cit> <bibl>Antiphanes fr. incert. 51 (Bothe p. 412)</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="trimeter"> <l>εἰ γὰρ ἀφέλοι τις τοῦ βίου τὰς ἡδονάς,</l> <l>καταλείπετ’ οὐδὲν ἕτερον ἢ τεθνηκέναι</l> </lg> </quote> </cit>, " <emph>supposing</emph> one takes away...then nothing is left." </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τροφὴν_352">, "tendance": see on <ref target="cl345">345</ref> : cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 1614">1614.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μαντεῖα_πάντα_354">implies several oracles, given to the Thebans about Oedipus after he had left Thebes. There is no clue to their purport, and we need not ask: they are invented merely to create a pious office for Ismene. It would not have seemed well that she should have stayed at Thebes all these years without showing any active interest in his fate: on the other hand, the poetic legend required that Antigone should be the sole guide of his wanderings. The oracle about final rest had been given to Oed. in his youth (see on <ref target="cl87">87</ref> ); the oracle about his grave has only just been received at Thebes <ref n="Soph. OC 389">(389)</ref> . Between these two, the only oracle suggested by the Sophoclean version of the story is a response to the question which Creon had proposed to ask at Delphi ( <ref n="Soph. OT 1438"> <title>O. T.</title> 1438</ref>), as to whether Oed. should remain at Thebes. But the story of the expulsion ( <ref n="Soph. OC 768">768 ff.</ref> ) implies that no such response had then been obtained. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοῦδε_σώματος_355"> (without <quote xml:lang="grc">περί</quote> ), gen. of connection; see on <ref target="cl307">307</ref> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φύλαξ_μοι_355"> <foreign xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</foreign>, a general description of her part, subjoined to the special instance just given: "and you constituted yourself a trusty watcher (at Thebes) in my interest, <emph>when I was being driven</emph> from the land," <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> from the moment when the decision to expel me had been taken, and the act was in contemplation. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μοι_355">for <foreign xml:lang="grc">μου</foreign> seems necessary: and I suspect that <quote xml:lang="grc">μου</quote> first arose from inattention to the exact sense. A gen. after <foreign xml:lang="grc">φύλαξ</foreign> always denotes the object guarded: thus <foreign xml:lang="grc">φύλαξ μου</foreign> ought to mean (not, "a watcher in my interest," but) "a guardian of my person"; this, however, was Antigone's part <ref n="Soph. OC 21">(21)</ref> : Ismene had never roamed with him. So in <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Ba. 612" ref="Eur. Ba. 612"> Eur. <title>Bacch.</title> 612</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τίς <emph>μοι</emph> φύλαξ ἦν;</quote> </cit>(say the Bacchants to Dionysus), "what overseer, master (of our rites, like <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπίσκοπος</quote> of Dionysus, <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1148"> <title>Ant.</title> 1148</ref>) had we?" — not, "what guardian of our persons was there?" Yet there L2 (cod. Laur. 32. 2) has <quote xml:lang="grc">μου</quote> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="στόλος_358">, a journey with a purpose, a "mission": <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Phil. 243" ref="Soph. Phil. 243"> <title>Ph.</title> 243 </bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τίνι | στόλῳ προσέσχες;</quote> </cit>on what mission hast thou touched here? </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μὴ_φέρουσα_360">explains the special sense of <foreign xml:lang="grc">κενή</foreign> . "You have not come empty-handed— <emph>i.e. without bringing</emph> some terror for me." <foreign xml:lang="grc">μὴ οὐ</foreign> properly stands with a partic. in a negative statement only when <quote xml:lang="grc">μή</quote> could stand with it in the corresponding affirmative statement: thus ( <emph> a</emph> ) affirmative: <quote xml:lang="grc"> βραδὺς ἔρχει <emph>μὴ</emph> φέρων</quote>, you (always) come slowly, <emph>if</emph> you are not bringing: ( <emph>b</emph> ) negative: <quote xml:lang="grc"> <emph>οὐ</emph> βραδὺς ἔρχει, <emph>μὴ οὐ</emph> φέρων</quote>, you never come slowly, <emph>unless</emph> you are bringing. Here <quote xml:lang="grc">μὴ οὐ</quote> is irregular, because the affirmative form would be <quote xml:lang="grc">ἥκεις οὐ</quote> (not <quote xml:lang="grc">μὴ</quote> ) <quote xml:lang="grc">φέρουσα</quote> , a simple statement of fact; and so the negative should be <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐχ ἥκεις οὐ φέρουσα</quote> . But <emph>bringing bad news</emph> is felt here as a <emph>condition</emph> of her coming. Hence <quote xml:lang="grc">μὴ οὐ</quote> is used as if the sentence were <emph>formally</emph> conditional: <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐκ ἂν ἦλθες μὴ οὐ φέρουσα</quote> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ζητοῦσα_τροφήν_362">, "enquiring as to your <emph>way of life</emph> " is supplemented by <foreign xml:lang="grc">ποῦ κατοικοίης</foreign> , <foreign xml:lang="lat"> i.e.</foreign> , <quote xml:lang="eng"> " <emph>where</emph> you were living."</quote>Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Thuc. 4.42" ref="Thuc. 4.42">Thuc. 4.42</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπετήρουν τοὺς Ἀθηναίους οἷ κατασχήσουσιν</quote> </cit>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀμφὶ_παίδοιν_365"> (dat.), <quote xml:lang="eng">"about"</quote> : oft. of encompassing tenderness, as <ref n="Soph. OC 1614">1614</ref> ; here, of besetting trouble: unless we take it as merely = "in the case of": cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Trach. 727" ref="Soph. Trach. 727"> <title>Tr.</title> 727</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλ’ ἀμφὶ τοῖς σφαλεῖσι μὴ )ξ ἑκουσίας | ὀργὴ πέπειρα</quote> </cit>. </gloss>

data.quote:
ἀλλ’ ἀμφὶ τοῖς σφαλεῖσι μὴ )ξ ἑκουσίας
									| ὀργὴ πέπειρα

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔρως_367">, desire <ref n="Soph. OC 436">(436)</ref> , is a necessary and a certain correction. The MS. <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἔρις</foreign> would have to mean " <emph>emulous</emph> desire," either ( <emph>a</emph> ) between the two brothers, if <quote xml:lang="grc">τε … μηδέ</quote> = " <emph> both</emph> " … "and not": or ( <emph>b</emph> ) between the brothers and ( <foreign xml:lang="grc">τε</foreign> ) Creon. Now, there is no objection to using <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐρίζω, ἔρις</quote> of <emph> noble</emph> rivalry. The fatal objection is that the idea of <emph> rivalry</emph> at all is here completely,—almost ludicrously,—out of place. The notion that Soph. was thinking of the <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀγαθὴ ἔρις</quote> , which rouses men to effort, as opp. to the <quote xml:lang="grc">κακὴ ἔρις</quote> ( <ref n="Hes. WD 11"> Hes. <title>Opp.</title> 11 ff.</ref>), is surely very frigid. It is possible, however, that it was this notion which first brought <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔρις</quote> into <ref n="Soph. OC 367">367.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Κρέοντί_τε_367">. The <foreign xml:lang="grc">τε</foreign> = "both," answering to <foreign xml:lang="grc">μηδέ</foreign> "and not." So <quote xml:lang="grc">τε</quote> is answered by <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδέ</quote> (instead of <quote xml:lang="grc">οὔτε</quote> ) <ref n="Eur. IT 697"> Eur. <title>I. T.</title> 697</ref>, or by <cit> <quote xml:lang="grc">δέ</quote> <bibl n="Soph. Phil. 1312" ref="Soph. Phil. 1312"> Soph. <title>Ph.</title> 1312</bibl> </cit>. So, too, <quote xml:lang="grc">οὔτε</quote> by <quote xml:lang="grc">δέ</quote> , <ref n="Eur. Supp. 223"> Eur. <title>Suppl.</title> 223</ref>, etc. Such irregularity is natural when the second thought is opposed to the first. The objection to reading <foreign xml:lang="grc">μήτε</foreign> in <ref n="Soph. OC 368">368</ref> is that, while <quote xml:lang="grc">οὔτε</quote> (or <quote xml:lang="grc">μήτε</quote> )... <quote xml:lang="grc">τε</quote> is common enough, there is no example of <quote xml:lang="grc">τε...οὔτε</quote> (or <quote xml:lang="grc">μήτε</quote> ).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐᾶσθαι_368">, pass., as <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Trach. 329" ref="Soph. Trach. 329"> <title>Tr.</title> 329</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἡ δ’ οὖν ἐάσθω</quote> </cit> : <cit> <bibl n="Thuc. 1.142" ref="Thuc. 1.142">Thuc. 1.142</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">( ἐασόμενοι )</quote> </cit> : <cit> <bibl n="Eur. IA 331" ref="Eur. IA 331"> Eur. <title>I. A.</title> 331</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">( ἐάσομαι ) </quote> </cit> : <cit> <bibl n="Eur. IT 1344" ref="Eur. IT 1344"> <title>I. T.</title> 1344</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">( ἐώμενος ) </quote> </cit>: etc. The midd. of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐάω</quote> is not classical. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πόλιν_368">: so in <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Ant. 776" ref="Soph. Ant. 776"> <title>Ant.</title> 776</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅπως μίασμα πᾶσ’ ὑπεκφύγῃ πόλις</quote> </cit>, it is implied that the whole State may be polluted by an act of the king. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λόγῳ_369">, in the light of reason, with calm reflection (in contrast to the blind passion for power which afterwards seized them), a dat. of manner, cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 381">381</ref> , <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 405" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg004:405"> <title>O. T.</title> 405</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὀργῇ λελέχθαι</quote> </cit> , <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Ant. 621" ref="Soph. Ant. 621"> <title>Ant.</title> 621</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">σοφίᾳ...ἔπος πέφανται.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὴν_φθοράν_369">, beginning with the curse called down on Laïus by Pelops, for robbing him of his son Chrysippus. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Ant. 596" ref="Soph. Ant. 596"> <title>Ant.</title> 596</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <note anchored="true" place="inline" resp="RCJ" xml:lang="eng">(of this labdacid house) </note>οὐδ’ ἀπαλλάσσει γενεὰν γένος, ἀλλ’ ἐρείπει | θεῶν τις</quote> </cit>etc.: one generation doth not free another, but some god brings ruin.</gloss>

data.quote:
οὐδ’ ἀπαλλάσσει γενεὰν γένος, ἀλλ’ ἐρείπει |
							θεῶν τις

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κἀλιτηρίου_371">. The MS. reading, <foreign xml:lang="grc">κἀξ ἀλĭτηροῦ</foreign> , is against metre, and gives a form of the adj. which occurs nowhere else; though, had it existed, it would have been most convenient for epic verse. <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἀλιτήριος</foreign> , and the poet. <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἀλĭτρός</foreign> , alone are found. The preceding <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἐκ</foreign> may have led the scribe into an erroneous repetition, as in <ref n="Soph. Aj. 205"> <title>Ai.</title> 205</ref>L has <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁ δεινὸς ὁ μέγας</quote> instead of <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁ δεινὸς μέγας</quote> (cp. <ref> Wecklein, <title>Ars Soph. emend.</title> XVI. pp. 69 ff.</ref>). This seems, on the whole, more likely than that the Homeric <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλείτης</quote> ("sinner," <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλοιτός</quote> in <ref>Lycophr. 579</ref> ) should have suggested a form <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἀλειτηρός</foreign> or <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἀλοιτηρός</foreign> , of which there is no other trace. <ref>Hesychius (I. 236)</ref> , <foreign xml:lang="lat"> s.v.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλιτροσύνη</quote> , says that in the <title xml:lang="grc">Αἰχμαλωτίδες</title> Soph. used the subst. <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἀλιτρία</foreign> ( <cit> <bibl n="Aristoph. Ach. 907" ref="Aristoph. Ach. 907"> Aristoph. <title>Ach.</title> 907</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὥσπερ πίθακον ἀλιτρίας πολλᾶς πλέων</quote> </cit>), whence Dindorf <quote xml:lang="grc"> <emph>κἀξ ἀλιτρίας</emph> φρενός</quote>, <quote xml:lang="eng">"from a sin of the mind."</quote> The objection to this is the unexampled lengthening of the second syllable. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τρὶς_ἀθλίοιν_372"> for <quote xml:lang="grc">τρισαθλίοιν</quote> was first given by Porson, since otherwise there would be no caesura either in the 3rd or in the 4th foot. He compares <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Od. 5.306" ref="Hom. Od. 5.306"> <title>Od.</title> 5.306</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τρὶς μάκαρες Δαναοὶ καὶ τετράκις</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Aristoph. Pl. 851" ref="Aristoph. Pl. 851"> Ar. <title>Plut.</title> 851</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ τρὶς κακοδαίμων καὶ τετράκις, κ.τ.λ.</quote> </cit>To Hermann's argument, that in any case <quote xml:lang="grc">τρὶς</quote> and <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀθλίοιν</quote> cohere, the answer is that, for the metre, the degree of coherence makes all the difference. Blaydes, keeping <quote xml:lang="grc">τρισαθλίοιν</quote> , quotes five such cases as <quote xml:lang="eng">"free from suspicion."</quote> They are the same five which Porson had discussed and proposed to amend in connection with this passage ( <emph> praef.</emph> p. xxviii): viz. <ref n="Aesch. Pers. 501"> Aesch. <title> Pers.</title> 501</ref>(transpose <quote xml:lang="grc">κρυσταλλοπῆγα</quote> ), <ref n="Eur. IA 1586"> Eur. <title>I. A.</title> 1586</ref> (transpose <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁρωμένου</quote> ), Soph. <ref n="Soph. Aj. 969"> <title>Ai.</title> 969</ref>(not strictly similar,— <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπεγγελῷεν</quote> ), <ref n="Aesch. Ag. 1261"> Aesch. <title>Ag.</title> 1261</ref>(=1252 Dind. <quote xml:lang="grc"> παρεσκόπεις</quote> , doubtful), <title>Suppl.</title> 252 (=244 D. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπεικάσαι</quote> , doubtful). Of these, <quote xml:lang="grc">παρεσκόπεις</quote> is the only exact parallel to <quote xml:lang="grc">τρισαθλίοιν</quote> , as being a single word coincident with the dipodia, and not preceded by elision. </gloss>

data.quote:
καὶ τρὶς κακοδαίμων καὶ τετράκις,
									κ.τ.λ.

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="νεάζων_374"> <emph> merely</emph> = <quote xml:lang="grc">νεώτερος ὤν</quote> , the pleonasm would be too weak: perh., then, it is tinged with the notion of <quote xml:lang="grc">νεανιευόμενος</quote> (as in <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Phoen. 713" ref="Eur. Phoen. 713"> Eur. <title>Phoen.</title> 713:</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ποῖ; μῶν νεάζων οὐχ ὁρᾷς ἃ χρῆν σ’ ὁρᾶν;</quote> </cit>— said by Creon to Eteocles). Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. Ag. 763" ref="Aesch. Ag. 763"> Aesch. <title>Ag.</title> 763</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="trimeter"> <l>φιλεῖ δὲ τίκτειν ὕβρις μὲν παλαιὰ νεά‐</l> <l>ζουσαν ὕβριν</l> </lg> </quote> </cit> . </gloss>

data.quote:
ποῖ; μῶν νεάζων οὐχ ὁρᾷς ἃ χρῆν σ’
									ὁρᾶν;

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸν_πρόσθε_375">: Polyneices alludes to his right as the firstborn, <ref n="Soph. OC 1294">1294</ref> , <ref n="Soph. OC 1422">1422</ref> : Eur. ( <ref n="Eur. Phoen. 71"> <title>Phoen.</title> 71</ref>) followed the common account in making Eteocles the elder. The change adopted by Soph. is here a twofold dramatic gain; for ( <emph>a</emph> ) Polyneices, who is to come on the scene, can be treated as the foremost ofiender; ( <emph>b</emph> ) Eteocles has now a special fault, and so the curse on <emph>both</emph> sons is further justified <ref n="Soph. OC 421">(421).</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀποστερίσκει_376">, historic pres., "deprives of" (rather than a true pres., "is excluding from"). The simple <quote xml:lang="grc">στερίσκω</quote> (Thuc., Plat., Eur., etc.) was commoner in Attic than this compound. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πληθύων_377">, lit., becoming full (of the Nile rising, <ref n="Hdt. 2.19">Her. 2.19</ref> ): <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. Ag. 869" ref="Aesch. Ag. 869"> Aesch. <title>Ag.</title> 869</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς ἐπλήθυον λόγοι</quote> </cit> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Ἄργος_378">, the territory, not only the city; called <foreign xml:lang="grc">κοῖλον</foreign> because the Argive plain is bounded on W., N. and E. by hills, as on S. by the sea. This epithet had already been given to it, acc. to the schol., in the epic called the <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἐπίγονοι</quote> , popularly ascribed to Homer ( <ref n="Hdt. 4.32">Her. 4.32</ref> , who expresses doubt), and was again used by Soph. in his <ref> <title>Thamyras</title> (fr. 222)</ref>. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Strab. 8.6.7" ref="Strab. 8.6.7">Strabo 8. 370</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> τῆς τε χώρας <note anchored="true" place="inline" resp="RCJ" xml:lang="eng">(the argive plain)</note> κοίλης οὔσης καὶ ποταμοῖς διαρρεομένης <note anchored="true" place="inline" resp="RCJ" xml:lang="eng">(the inachus and the erasînus)</note> καὶ ἕλη καὶ λίμνας παρεχομένης</quote> </cit>. So <cit> <bibl n="Hdt. 7.129" ref="Hdt. 7.129">Her. 7.129</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ μέσον δὲ τούτων τῶν λεχθέντων ὀρέων ἡ Θεσσαλίη ἐστί, ἐοῦσα κοίλη</quote> </cit> : <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Od. 4.1" ref="Hom. Od. 4.1"> <title>Od.</title> 4.1</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">Λακεδαίμονα κοίλην</quote> </cit>(the valley of the Eurotas); Polyb. 1. 3. 1 <quote xml:lang="grc">Κοίλη Συρία</quote> (as lying between Lebanon and Anti-Lebanon). The epith. <foreign xml:lang="grc">κοῖλον</foreign> has an epic tone, as suggesting a distinction from the Homeric <quote xml:lang="grc">Πελασγικὸν Ἄργος</quote> (perh. Thessaly), <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἀχαϊκὸν</quote> and <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἴασον Ἄργος</quote> (Peloponnesus). </gloss>

data.quote:
τῆς τε χώρας  
							κοίλης οὔσης καὶ ποταμοῖς διαρρεομένης   καὶ ἕλη καὶ λίμνας παρεχομένης

τὸ μέσον δὲ τούτων τῶν λεχθέντων ὀρέων
									ἡ Θεσσαλίη ἐστί, ἐοῦσα κοίλη

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κῆδος_379">, <foreign xml:lang="lat">affinitatem</foreign> , with Adrastus, by marrying his daughter Argeia ( <quote xml:lang="grc">κῆδος Ἀδράστου λαβών</quote> , <ref n="Eur. Phoen. 77"> Eur. <title>Phoen.</title> 77</ref>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καινόν_379">, in a new quarter (as opp. to his native land). Perhaps Statius, whom Schneid. quotes, was translating this: <cit> <quote xml:lang="lat"> <lg type="hexameter"> <l part="F">iamque ille novis, scit fama, superbit</l> <l>Conubiis, viresque parat, queis regna capessat</l> </lg> </quote> <bibl n="Stat. Theb. 2.108" ref="Stat. Theb. 2.108">( <title>Theb.</title> 2. 108)</bibl> </cit> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὡς_380"> <quote xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</quote>: <quote xml:lang="eng">"as purposing that Argos should either possess the Theban land in honour, or exalt Thebes to the skies"</quote> (by the glory of having defeated Argos). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὡς_βιβῶν_380">, acc. absol. in the <emph>personal</emph> constr., as <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 101" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg004:101"> <title>O. T.</title> 101</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς τόδ’ αἷμα χειμάζον πόλιν</quote> </cit> : <cit> <bibl n="Thuc. 6.24" ref="Thuc. 6.24">Thuc. 6.24</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔρως ἐνέπεσε τοῖς πᾶσιν...ἐκπλεῦσαι...ὡς ἢ καταστρεψομένοις ἐφ’ ἃ ἔπλεον, ἢ οὐδὲν ἂν σφαλεῖσαν μεγάλην δύναμιν</quote> </cit>, in the belief that they would reduce Sicily, or that at all events a great armament could suffer no disaster. <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Ion 964" ref="Eur. Ion 964"> Eur. <title>Ion</title> 964</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ΠΑΙΔ. σοὶ δ’ ἐς τί δόξ’ εἴσῆλθεν ἐκβαλεῖν τέκνον;—ΚΡΕΟΥΣΑ. ὡς τὸν θεὸν σώσοντα τόν γ’ αὑτοῦ γόνον</quote> </cit>. </gloss>

data.quote:
ἔρως ἐνέπεσε τοῖς
									πᾶσιν...ἐκπλεῦσαι...ὡς ἢ καταστρεψομένοις ἐφ’ ἃ ἔπλεον,
									ἢ οὐδὲν ἂν σφαλεῖσαν μεγάλην δύναμιν

ΠΑΙΔ. σοὶ δ’ ἐς τί δόξ’ εἴσῆλθεν
									ἐκβαλεῖν τέκνον;—ΚΡΕΟΥΣΑ. ὡς τὸν θεὸν σώσοντα τόν γ’
									αὑτοῦ γόνον

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τιμῇ_381">, dat. of manner: cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 369">369</ref> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καθέξον_381">, occupy as conquerors: <cit> <bibl n="Dem. 18.96" ref="Dem. 18.96">Dem. or. 18 § 96</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰ κύκλῳ τῆς Ἀττικῆς κατεχόντων ἁρμοσταῖς καὶ φρουραῖς</quote> </cit> . </gloss>

data.quote:
τὰ κύκλῳ τῆς Ἀττικῆς κατεχόντων
									ἁρμοσταῖς καὶ φρουραῖς

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πρὸς_βιβῶν_381">: cp. <cit> <quote xml:lang="grc">κλέος οὐρανὸν ἵκει</quote> <bibl n="Hom. Od. 9.20" ref="Hom. Od. 9.20"> ( <title>Od.</title> 9.20)</bibl> </cit> , <cit> <quote xml:lang="grc">κλέος οὐρανόμηκες</quote> <bibl n="Aristoph. Cl. 459" ref="Aristoph. Cl. 459"> (Aristoph. <title>Nub.</title> 459)</bibl> </cit> : <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Ba. 972" ref="Eur. Ba. 972"> Eur. <title>Bacch.</title> 972</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὥστ’ οὐρανῷ στηρίζον εὑρήσεις κλέος</quote> </cit>(thou wilt find thy fame towering in the sky). But the best illustration is <cit> <bibl n="Isoc. 15.134" ref="Isoc. 15.134">Isocr. or. 15 § 134</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰ μὲν ἁμαρτανόμενα παρόψονται, τὸ δὲ κατορθωθὲν οὐρανόμηκες ποιήσουσιν</quote> </cit>, they will overlook your failures, and exalt your success to the skies. So <cit> <bibl n="Lucr. 1.78" ref="Lucr. 1.78">Lucr. 1. 78</bibl> <quote xml:lang="lat"> <lg type="hexameter"> <l part="F">religio pedibus subiecta vicissim</l> <l>Opteritur, nos exaequat victoria caelo.</l> </lg> </quote> </cit>Wecklein strangely understands: — "or will make Thebes rise to the sky" (in <emph> smoke</emph> , by burning the city), comparing <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Tro. 1298" ref="Eur. Tro. 1298"> Eur. <title>Tro.</title> 1298</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="lyric"> <l>πτέρυγι δὲ καπνὸς ὥς τις οὐ‐</l> <l>ράνια πεσοῦσα δορὶ καταφθίνει γᾶ</l> </lg> </quote> </cit>, which means simply: "our land hath fallen like smoke that hath sunk down on its wing from the sky, and is perishing by the spear." </gloss>

data.quote:
τὰ μὲν ἁμαρτανόμενα παρόψονται, τὸ δὲ
							κατορθωθὲν οὐρανόμηκες ποιήσουσιν

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀριθμός_382">: <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Tro. 475" ref="Eur. Tro. 475"> Eur. <title>Tro.</title> 475</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="trimeter"> <l>κἀνταῦθ’ ἀριστεύοντ’ ἐγεινάμην τέκνα,</l> <l>οὐκ ἀριθμὸν ἄλλως, ἀλλ’ ὑπερτάτους Φρυγῶν</l> </lg> </quote> </cit> . <cit> <bibl n="Hor. Ep. 1.2.27" ref="Hor. Ep. 1.2.27"> Hor. <title>Epp.</title> 1. 2. 27</bibl> <quote xml:lang="lat">Nos numerus sumus et fruges consumere nati.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

data.quote:
Nos numerus sumus et fruges consumere
									nati.

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὡς_ἕξειν_385">. Against the tempting conjecture <foreign xml:lang="grc">θεοὶ … ἕξουσ’</foreign> , remark that in some other passages, where our MSS. give this mixed construction, the acc. and inf. could not be eliminated without strong measures: thus <cit> <bibl n="Xen. Hell. 6.5.42" ref="Xen. Hell. 6.5.42"> Xen. <title>Hellen.</title> 6.5.42</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐλπίζειν δὲ χρὴ ὡς ἄνδρας ἀγαθοὺς μᾶλλον ἢ κακοὺς αὐτοὺς γενήσεσθαι</quote> </cit> : <cit> <bibl n="Xen. Cyrop. 8.1.25" ref="Xen. Cyrop. 8.1.25"> <title>Cyr.</title> 8. 1. 25</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">πρὸς δὲ τούτοις ἐλογίζετο ὡς εἰ πάντες οἱ κοινῶνες θεοσεβεῖς εἶεν, ἧττον ἂν αὐτοὺς ἐθέλειν</quote> </cit>: where the least violent remedy would be to delete <foreign xml:lang="grc">ὡς</foreign> —a course not possible here. In some other such places, indeed, the inf. can be very easily corrected (as <cit> <bibl n="Xen. Hell. 3.4.27" ref="Xen. Hell. 3.4.27"> <title>Hellen.</title> 3. 4. 27</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἴη</quote> </cit> for <quote xml:lang="grc">εἶναι</quote> , <cit> <bibl n="Xen. Hell. 7.4.39" ref="Xen. Hell. 7.4.39">7. 4. 39</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">δεῖ</quote> </cit> for <quote xml:lang="grc">δεῖν</quote> ). </gloss>

data.quote:
ἐλπίζειν δὲ χρὴ ὡς ἄνδρας ἀγαθοὺς
									μᾶλλον ἢ κακοὺς αὐτοὺς γενήσεσθαι

πρὸς δὲ τούτοις ἐλογίζετο ὡς εἰ πάντες
									οἱ κοινῶνες θεοσεβεῖς εἶεν, ἧττον ἂν αὐτοὺς
									ἐθέλειν

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὡς_ἕξειν_385_2">may be sound. Harsh as it seems to us, usage had perhaps accustomed the ear to hearing the speaker's own view introduced by <foreign xml:lang="grc"> ὡς</foreign> , even when the corresponding construction did not follow. <foreign xml:lang="grc">ὧδ’ ἐμοῦ</foreign> would be weak. But <foreign xml:lang="grc">ὥστ’ ἐμοῦ</foreign> (against which the presence of <quote xml:lang="grc">ὥστε</quote> in 386 is not conclusive, cp. on <ref target="cl544">544</ref> ) is worth weighing: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Orest. 52" ref="Eur. Orest. 52"> Eur. <title>Or.</title> 52</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐλπίδα δὲ δή τιν’ ἔχομεν ὥστε μὴ θανεῖν</quote> </cit>. </gloss>

data.quote:
ἐλπίδα δὲ δή τιν’ ἔχομεν ὥστε μὴ
									θανεῖν

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εὐσοίας_390">, used by Soph. also in the <cit> <bibl> <title>Amphitryon</title> (fr. 119)</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="trimeter"> <l>ἐπεὶ δὲ βλάστοι, τῶν τριῶν μίαν λαβεῖν</l> <l>εὔσοιαν ἀρκεῖ</l> </lg> </quote> </cit>, quoted by the schol., who describes it as the reading <quote xml:lang="grc"> ἐν τοῖς ἀναγκαιοτέροις τῶν ἀντιγράφων <note anchored="true" place="inline" resp="RCJ" xml:lang="eng">(the better copies) </note> … ὃ καὶ οἱ ὑπομνηματισάμενοι ἀξιοῦσιν</quote>(the Alexan drian commentators). It does not occur except in Soph.: but <ref n="Theoc. 24">Theocr. 24. 8</ref> has <quote xml:lang="grc">εὔσοα τέκνα</quote> ("safe and sound"). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐν_σοὶ_392">: <ref n="Soph. OC 247">247.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γίγνεσθαι_392">is never merely <quote xml:lang="grc">εἶναι. ἐν σοὶ γίγνεται τὰ κείνων κράτη</quote> =their power <emph>comes to be</emph> in thy hand: <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> the new oracle so appoints. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φασί_392">with indef. subject, "people say," report says (we cannot supply "the <foreign xml:lang="grc">θεωροί</foreign> " from <ref n="Soph. OC 413">413</ref> ). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κράτη_392">, political predominance generally, but with esp. ref. to prevalence in war against Athens <ref n="Soph. OC 1332">(1332)</ref> : the plur. as of royal power ( <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Ant. 173" ref="Soph. Ant. 173"> <title>Ant.</title> 173</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">κράτη...καὶ θρόνους</quote> </cit> ). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀνήρ_393">, emphatic, as oft.: <cit> <bibl n="Aristoph. Cl. 823" ref="Aristoph. Cl. 823"> Aristoph. <title>Nub.</title> 823</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὃ σὺ μαθὼν ἀνὴρ ἔσει</quote> </cit> : <cit> <bibl n="Xen. Cyrop. 4.2.25" ref="Xen. Cyrop. 4.2.25"> Xen. <title>Cyr.</title> 4.2.25</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐκέτ’ ἀνήρ ἐστιν, ἀλλὰ σκευοφόρος</quote> </cit>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὤλλυσαν_394">, imperf. of intention; see on <ref target="cl274">274</ref> . This was their design up to the moment of his fall. From that moment dates the period meant by <foreign xml:lang="grc">νῦν</foreign> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δς_πέσῃ_395"> without <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄν</quote> , as oft. in poetry, seldom in prose ( <ref n="Soph. OT 1231"> <title>O. T.</title> 1231</ref>n.). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καὶ_μὴν_396">here = "Well, however that may be" (even if it <emph>is</emph> <quote xml:lang="grc">φλαῦρον</quote> ). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γε_396">throws back a light stress on <quote xml:lang="grc">Κρέοντα</quote> : " <emph> Creon</emph> thinks the matter important." For a slightly different use of <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ μήν... γε</quote> cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 345"> <title>O. T.</title> 345</ref>n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="βαιοῦ_χρόνου_397">. The gen. of the "time <emph>within which</emph> " expresses the period to which the act <emph>belongs</emph> , and might so be viewed as possessive: <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Gorg. 448A" ref="Plat. Gorg. 448A"> Plat. <title>Gorg.</title> 448A</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδείς μέ πω ἠρώτηκε καινὸν οὐδὲν πολλῶν ἐτῶν</quote> </cit>, <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> non-questioning of me has now been the attribute of many years. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐντός</quote> is sometimes added ( <cit> <bibl n="Isoc. 6.46" ref="Isoc. 6.46">Isocr. or. 6 § 46</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐντὸς τριῶν μηνῶν κατέσχεν ἅπασαν Μακεδονίαν</quote> </cit>). </gloss>

data.quote:
οὐδείς μέ πω ἠρώτηκε καινὸν οὐδὲν
									πολλῶν ἐτῶν

ἐντὸς τριῶν μηνῶν κατέσχεν ἅπασαν
									Μακεδονίαν

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κοὐχὶ_μ_397">, with warning emphasis: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 58" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg004:58"> <title>O. T.</title> 58</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">γνωτὰ κοὐκ ἄγνωτα</quote> </cit>(n.). Cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 617">617</ref> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="στήσωσι_399">, <foreign xml:lang="lat">sc.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">οἱ Θηβαῖοι</quote> : Creon himself lays stress on his mission to speak for <emph>all</emph> <ref n="Soph. OC 737">(737)</ref> . Schol. <quote xml:lang="grc"> κατοικίσωσι</quote> . The word has a certain harsh fitness for <cit> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸν πλανήτην</quote> <bibl n="Soph. OC 3" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg007:3">(3)</bibl> </cit>. Against <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς ἄγχι γῆς στήσῃ σε</quote> speaks the plural strain of the whole passage ( <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 392" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg007:392">392</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">κείνων</quote> </cit> , <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 400" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg007:400">400</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">κρατῶσι</quote> </cit> , <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 402" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg007:402">402</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">κείνοις</quote> </cit> , <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 405" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg007:405">405</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">θέλουσι</quote> </cit>, etc.). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὅρων_400">. <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἐμβαίνω</foreign> usu. takes either dat., or prep. with gen. or accus.: the simple gen. could be explained as partitive, but prob. is rather on the analogy of the gen. with <foreign xml:lang="grc"> <emph>ἐπι</emph> βαίνω</foreign>: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 825" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg004:825"> <title>O. T.</title> 825</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐμβατεύειν πατρίδος</quote> </cit>. The gen. with <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπεμβαίνω</quote> <ref n="Soph. OC 924">(924)</ref> is warranted by the first preposition. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θύρασι_401">, <foreign xml:lang="lat">foris</foreign> , as Eur. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 2074" ref="Soph. El. 2074"> <title>El.</title> 2074</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδὲν γὰρ αὐτὴν δεῖ θύρασιν εὐπρεπὲς | φαίνειν πρόσωπον</quote> </cit>(she ought not to show her beauty <emph>abroad</emph> ), where, as here, Elms. restored it from the MS. <foreign xml:lang="grc">θύραισι</foreign> . Campbell retains the latter. But, while in <quote xml:lang="grc">θύρασι, θύραζε, θύραθεν, θυραῖος</quote> the notion of "external" is uppermost, the figurative uses of the plural <quote xml:lang="grc">θύραι</quote> always speak of <emph>approaching</emph> the house: as <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπὶ τὰς θύρας φοιτᾶν, ἐπὶ ταῖς θύραις διατρίβειν, ἐπὶ ταῖς θύραις τῆς Ἑλλάδος ἐσμέν</quote> ( <ref n="Xen. Anab. 6.5.23"> Xen. <title> Anab.</title> 6.5.23</ref>). So here <quote xml:lang="grc">θύραισι</quote> would mean, not, <quote xml:lang="eng">"outside of their doors,"</quote> but <quote xml:lang="eng"> "at their <emph>very</emph> doors."</quote> </gloss>

data.quote:
οὐδὲν γὰρ αὐτὴν δεῖ θύρασιν εὐπρεπὲς |
									φαίνειν πρόσωπον

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κειμένου_401">: schol. <quote xml:lang="grc">οἰκοῦντος</quote> . </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κείνοις_402"> with </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="βαρύς_402"> only. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δυστυχῶν_402">=if it does not receive due honours: cp. <quote xml:lang="grc"> ἄμοιρος...νέκυς</quote> of a corpse denied due rites ( <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1071"> <title>Ant.</title> 1071</ref>). <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Hec. 319" ref="Eur. Hec. 319"> Eur. <title>Hec.</title> 319</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="trimeter"> <l>τύμβον δὲ βουλοίμην ἂν ἀξιούμενον</l> <l>τὸν ἐμὸν ὁρᾶσθαι</l> </lg> </quote> </cit>. Since in death <ref n="Soph. OC 390">(390)</ref> he was still to sway their destiny, they wished his grave to be where they could make the due offerings ( <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐναγίζειν</quote> ) at it: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Hdt. 2.44" ref="Hdt. 2.44">Her. 2.44</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τῷ μὲν ὡς ἀθανάτῳ...θύουσι, τῷ δὲ...ὡς ἥρωι ἐναγίζουσι</quote> </cit>. Such <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐναγισμός</quote> would be at least annual (cp. <ref n="Isaeus 2.46">Isae. or. 2 § 46</ref> ). The schol. takes <foreign xml:lang="grc">δυστυχῶν</foreign> as = "if not on Theban soil": but this is excluded by <ref n="Soph. OC 407">407</ref> . </gloss>

data.quote:
τῷ μὲν ὡς ἀθανάτῳ...θύουσι, τῷ δὲ...ὡς
									ἥρωι ἐναγίζουσι

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σε_προσθέσθαι_404"> , <quote xml:lang="eng">"to associate you with them (as a prospective ally) in the neighbourhood of their land, and not (to leave you) in a place where you will be your own master."</quote> Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Hdt. 1.69" ref="Hdt. 1.69">Her. 1.69</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">χρήσαντος τοῦ θεοῦ τὸν Ἕλληνα φίλον προσθέσθαι,...ὑμέας...προσκαλέομαι φίλος τε θέλων γενέσθαι καὶ σύμμαχος</quote> </cit>. With <foreign xml:lang="grc">μηδ’</foreign> , etc., a verbal notion such as <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐᾶσαι οἰκεῖν</quote> must be supplied from <quote xml:lang="grc">προσθέσθαι</quote> : cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 71" ref="Soph. El. 71"> <title>El.</title> 71</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="trimeter"> <l>καὶ μή μ’ ἄτιμον τῆσδ’ ἀποστείλητε γῆς,</l> <l>ἀλλ’ ἀρχέπλουτον</l> </lg> </quote> </cit> ( <foreign xml:lang="lat">sc.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc"> καταστήσατε</quote> ). </gloss>

data.quote:
χρήσαντος τοῦ θεοῦ τὸν Ἕλληνα φίλον
							προσθέσθαι,...ὑμέας...προσκαλέομαι φίλος τε θέλων
									γενέσθαι καὶ σύμμαχος

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἂν_κρατοῖς_404">, nearly = <foreign xml:lang="grc">κρατήσεις</foreign> . See on <quote xml:lang="grc">ἵν’ ἂν...εἴποιμεν</quote> , <ref target="cl189">189</ref> . With the MS. <foreign xml:lang="grc">κρατῇς</foreign> , <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἄν</foreign> belongs to <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἵνα</foreign> : "wherever you may be your own master": which is evidently less suitable here. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καὶ_406">with <foreign xml:lang="grc">κατασκιῶσι</foreign> (not with <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἧ</foreign> , which would imply that he did <emph>not</emph> expect it, <ref n="Soph. OT 368"> <title>O. T.</title> 368</ref>): "Having settled me near their land, will they <emph>further</emph> bury me within it?" For <foreign xml:lang="grc">κατασκιάζειν</foreign> cp. <ref> <title>Epigrammata Graeca</title> 493 (Kaibel, Berl. 1878)</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">θανόντα...γαῖα κατεσκίασεν</quote>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοὔμφυλον_αἷμα_407">, thy blood-guilt for the death of a kinsman: so <cit> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐμφύλιον αἷμα</quote> <bibl n="Pind. P. 2" ref="Pind. P. 2"> (Pind. <title>Pyth.</title> 2.32)</bibl> </cit> , <cit> <quote xml:lang="grc">αἷμα συγγενές</quote> <bibl n="Eur. Supp. 148" ref="Eur. Supp. 148"> (Eur. <title>Suppl.</title> 148)</bibl> </cit> , <cit> <quote xml:lang="grc">αἷμα γενέθλιον</quote> <bibl n="Eur. Orest. 89" ref="Eur. Orest. 89">(Or. 89)</bibl> </cit>: but in <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 1406" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg004:1406"> <title>O. T.</title> 1406</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">αἷμ’ ἐμφύλιον</quote> </cit> merely= <quote xml:lang="eng">"a blood-kinship."</quote> Oed. was doomed to <cit> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀειφυγία</quote> <bibl n="Soph. OC 601" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg007:601">(601)</bibl> </cit>. Even to <emph>bury</emph> him in Theban ground would seem impious towards Laïus. So, when Antigone has given the burial-rite to Polyneices, Creon asks, <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Ant. 514" ref="Soph. Ant. 514"> ( <title>Ant.</title> 514)</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">πῶς δῆτ’ ἐκείνῳ δυσσεβῆ τιμᾷς χάριν;</quote> </cit>"How, then, canst thou render a grace which is impious towards that other?" (Eteocles). </gloss>

data.quote:
πῶς δῆτ’ ἐκείνῳ δυσσεβῆ τιμᾷς
									χάριν;

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="συναλλαγῆς_410">, strictly, a bringing together (by the gods) of persons and circumstances, a "conjuncture": rarely without the defining gen. (as <quote xml:lang="grc">νόσου ξ.</quote> , <ref n="Soph. OT 960"> <title>O. T.</title> 960</ref>); for in <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Trach. 845" ref="Soph. Trach. 845"> <title>Tr.</title> 845</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὀλεθρίαισι ξυναλλαγαῖς</quote> </cit>prob.= "at the fatal meeting" (of Deianeira with Nessus).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σοῖς_τάφοις_411">, poetical locative dat. ( <ref n="Soph. OT 381"> <title>O. T.</title> 381</ref>n.), freq. in Homer, as <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Il. 21.389" ref="Hom. Il. 21.389"> <title>Il.</title> 21.389</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἥμενος Οὐλύμπῳ</quote> </cit>. Some day the Thebans will invade Attica, and will be defeated by the Athenians near the grave of Oedipus. Cp. <ref> Aristeides <title xml:lang="grc">ὑπὲρ τῶν τεττάρων</title> p. 284</ref>(the great men of the Greek past are guardian spirits), <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ ῥύεσθαί γε τὴν χώραν οὐ χεῖρον ἢ τὸν ἐν Κολωνῷ κείμενον Οἰδίπουν</quote> : where the schol. records a vague legend of his epiphany in some fight with Theban invaders. When the Persians ( <date when="-0480">480 B.C.</date> ) were repulsed from Delphi, two gigantic warriors pursued them; <cit> <quote xml:lang="grc">τούτους δὲ τοὺς δύο Δελφοὶ λέγουσι εἷναι τοὺς ἐπιχωρίους ἥρωας, Φύλακόν τε καὶ Αὐτόνοον, τῶν τὰ τεμένεά ἐστι περὶ τὸ ἱρόν</quote> <bibl n="Hdt. 8.39" ref="Hdt. 8.39">(Her. 8.39)</bibl> </cit>. So Theseus was seen at Marathon ( <ref n="Plut. Thes. 35"> Plut. <title> Thes.</title> 35</ref>); Athene appeared, and the Aeacidae helped, at Salamis ( <ref n="Hdt. 9.83">Her. 9.83 f.</ref> ). </gloss>

data.quote:
τούτους δὲ τοὺς δύο Δελφοὶ λέγουσι
									εἷναι τοὺς ἐπιχωρίους ἥρωας, Φύλακόν τε καὶ Αὐτόνοον,
									τῶν τὰ τεμένεά ἐστι περὶ τὸ ἱρόν

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θεωρῶν_413">, sent from Thebes to Delphi, to consult the oracle in solemn form ( <ref n="Soph. OT 114"> <title>O. T.</title> 114</ref>): cp. on <ref target="cl354">354</ref> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἑστίας_413">, the "hearth of the Pythian seer" ( <ref n="Soph. OT 965"> <title>O. T.</title> 965</ref>), "at earth's centre" ( <quote xml:lang="grc">μεσόμφαλος</quote> , <ref n="Eur. Ion 462"> Eur. <title> Ion</title> 462</ref>). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐφ_ἡμῖν_414">, "in my case" (n. on <ref n="Soph. OT 829"> <title>O.T.</title> 829</ref>). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οἱ_μολόντες_415">: schol. <quote xml:lang="grc">οἱ θεωροί</quote> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="παίδων_τις_416">(there being only <emph>two</emph> sons) virtually strengthens the question, as if he asked —"Had my sons any knowledge whatever of this?"</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καὶ_εἶτα_418">, "and after that," is explained by <foreign xml:lang="grc">τῶνδ’ ἀκούσαντες.</foreign> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τῶνδ_418">: see on <ref target="cl304">304</ref> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πάρος_προὔθεντο_418"> : <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Hipp. 382" ref="Eur. Hipp. 382"> Eur. <title>Hipp.</title> 382</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οἱ δ’ ἡδονὴν προθέντες ἀντὶ τοῦ καλοῦ | ἄλλην τιν’</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Isoc. L. 9.17" ref="Isoc. L. 9.17"> Isocr. <title>Ep.</title> 9 § 17</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄλλους ἀνθ’ ἡμων προκριθῆναι</quote> </cit>: and so Plat. <cit> <quote xml:lang="grc">προτιμᾶν τι ἀντί τινος</quote> <bibl n="Plat. Lysis 219d" ref="Plat. Lysis 219d"> ( <title>Lys.</title> 219 D)</bibl> </cit> , <cit> <quote xml:lang="grc">πρό τινος</quote> <bibl n="Plat. Laws 727d" ref="Plat. Laws 727d"> ( <title>Legg.</title> 727 D)</bibl> </cit> , <cit> <quote xml:lang="grc">πλέον τινός</quote> <bibl n="Plat. Laws 777d" ref="Plat. Laws 777d"> ( <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 777 D)</bibl> </cit>, <cit> <quote xml:lang="grc">μᾶλλον ἤ τι</quote> <bibl n="Plat. Laws 887b" ref="Plat. Laws 887b">(887 B)</bibl> </cit>. What is the complaint of Oed. against his sons? This: — Apollo had made him the arbiter, in life and death, of Theban welfare <ref n="Soph. OC 389">(389)</ref> . His sons might have pleaded with the Thebans: — "Apollo has now virtually condoned the <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἔμφυλον αἷμα</foreign> ( <ref n="Soph. OC 407">407</ref> ). Restore our father to the throne." But they desired the throne for themselves. Here, as in regard to his expulsion, they neglected an opportunity which natural piety should have seized <ref n="Soph. OC 441">(441)</ref> . </gloss>

data.quote:
οἱ δ’ ἡδονὴν προθέντες ἀντὶ τοῦ καλοῦ
									| ἄλλην τιν’

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοὐμοῦ_πόθου_419">: the possess. pron. =object. gen. of pers. pron.: see on <ref target="cl332">332</ref> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φέρω_ὅμως_420">is usually taken, "but such are my tidings" (cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 360">360</ref> ). This would be fitting if, with Wecklein, we might read <foreign xml:lang="grc">φέρουσα</foreign> for <foreign xml:lang="grc">κλύουσα</foreign> : but the latter is in all MSS., and naturally refers to the words just heard by Ismene from Oed., not to a report heard by her at Thebes. The indignant question of Oed. invited a defence. She replies, "I am pained to hear my brothers charged with such conduct, but I must bear it" — <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> I cannot deny the charge. The contrast between <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἀλγῶ</foreign> and <foreign xml:lang="grc">φέρω</foreign> has thus more point. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀλλ_421">. "Nay, then" — opening the imprecation, as <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Phil. 1040" ref="Soph. Phil. 1040"> <title>Ph.</title> 1040</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="trimeter"> <l>ἀλλ’, ὦ πατρῴα γῆ θεοί τ’ ἐγχώριοι,</l> <l>τείσασθε, τείσασθ’</l> </lg> </quote> </cit> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σφιν_421">, not <quote xml:lang="grc">σφι</quote> , was prob. always the form used by Attic tragedy. It is required by metre below, <ref n="Soph. OC 444"> 444</ref> , <ref n="Soph. OC 451">451</ref> , <ref n="Soph. OC 1490"> 1490</ref> : <ref n="Soph. Aj. 570"> <title>Ai.</title> 570</ref>: <ref n="Soph. El. 1070"> <title>El.</title> 1070</ref>: <ref n="Aesch. PB 252"> Aesch. <title> PV</title> 252</ref>, <ref n="Aesch. PB 457">457</ref> : <ref n="Aesch. Pers. 759"> <title>Pers.</title> 759</ref>, <ref n="Aesch. Pers. 807">807</ref> : <ref>fr. 157</ref> ( <foreign xml:lang="lat">ap.</foreign> <ref n="Plat. Rep. 391E"> Plat. <title>Rep.</title> 391E</ref>). Eur. has the dat. in two places where, as here, <quote xml:lang="grc">σφι</quote> is <emph>possible</emph> , but in both <quote xml:lang="grc">σφιν</quote> has MS. authority, and should probably be read, <ref n="Eur. Med. 398"> <title>Med.</title> 398</ref>( <foreign xml:lang="lat">v.l.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">σφι</quote> ), <ref n="Eur. Supp. 769"> <title>Suppl.</title> 769</ref>. On the other hand there is no place in trag. where metre excludes <quote xml:lang="grc">σφιν</quote> . </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὴν_πεπρωμένην_421">, by the curse in the house of Laïus <ref n="Soph. OC 369">(369)</ref> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐν_δ_422"> after <foreign xml:lang="grc">μήτε</foreign> is harsh, and Elmsley's <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἔν τ’</foreign> may be right. There is, however, a good deal of MS. evidence for <quote xml:lang="grc"> τε...δέ</quote> in trag.: see on <ref target="cl367">367</ref> . Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Ant. 1096" ref="Soph. Ant. 1096"> <title>Ant.</title> 1096</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τό τ’ εἰκαθεῖν γὰρ δεινόν, ἀντιστάντα δὲ κ.τ.λ.</quote> </cit>, n. </gloss>

data.quote:
τό τ’ εἰκαθεῖν γὰρ δεινόν, ἀντιστάντα
									δὲ κ.τ.λ.

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐν_ἐμοί_422"> (cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 247">247</ref> ), may the issue for them <emph>come to be</emph> <ref n="Soph. OC 392">(392)</ref> in my hands, <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> may the gods allow me to be the final arbiter, and to doom them <emph>both</emph> by a father's curse. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κἀπαναίρονται_424">. The words <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ ἐπαναίρονται δόρυ</quote> do not form a second relative clause,—as if, from the <foreign xml:lang="grc"> ἧς</foreign> before <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἔχονται</foreign> , we had to supply the relat. pron. in a different case ( <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐφ’ ᾗ</quote> , or <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰς ἥν</quote> ) with <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπαναίρονται</quote> . They form an independent sentence, which is co-ordinated with the relative clause, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἧς ἔχονται</quote> . This is the normal Greek construction. See note in Appendix. Cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 467">467</ref> , <ref n="Soph. OC 731">731</ref> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐπαναιροῦνται_δόρυ_424">, the MS. reading, would mean, "are taking a spear upon them," the verb being used figuratively (like <foreign xml:lang="lat">in se suscipere</foreign> ) of obligations or responsibilities ( <quote xml:lang="grc">φιλίαν, πόλεμον, τέχνην, λατρείαν</quote> etc.); but <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπαναίρεσθαι</quote> , in its literal sense of "uplifting against," is more natural and more poetical with <foreign xml:lang="grc">δόρυ</foreign> : cp. <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Her. 313" ref="Eur. Her. 313"> Eur. <title>Her.</title> 313</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ μήποτ’ ἐς γῆν ἐχθρὸν αἴρεσθαι δόρυ</quote> </cit>. </gloss>

data.quote:
καὶ μήποτ’ ἐς γῆν ἐχθρὸν αἴρεσθαι
									δόρυ

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὡς_425">, "for" (if I were to have the decision). Blomfield's conjecture <foreign xml:lang="grc">ὧδ’</foreign> is unnecessary. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οἵ_γε_427">, causal: see on <quote xml:lang="grc">οἵτινες</quote> <ref target="cl263"> 263</ref> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀτίμως_428">: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 440" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg007:440">440</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">βίᾳ</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 770" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg007:770">770</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐξεώθεις</quote> </cit>. Soph. has this adv. thrice elsewhere of ignominious or ruthless treatment, <ref n="Soph. El. 1181"> <title>El.</title> 1181</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1069"> <title>Ant.</title> 1069</ref>, <ref>fr. 593. 7.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐκ_ἔσχον_429">, did not stop me (from being expelled). We find such phrases as <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἔχω τινὰ ποιοῦντά τι</foreign> , to check one <emph>in the act</emph> of doing something ( <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 888" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg007:888"> <title>O. C.</title> 888</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">βουθυτοῦντά μ’...ἔσχετ’</quote> </cit>), but not <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἔχω τινὰ ἀδικούμενον</foreign> , to stop one <emph>from being</emph> wronged (like <foreign xml:lang="grc">παύω</foreign> ). Here, then, it is better to supply <foreign xml:lang="grc">τὸ</foreign> (or <foreign xml:lang="grc">ὥστε</foreign> ) <foreign xml:lang="grc">μὴ ἐξωθεῖσθαι</foreign> than to take <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἔσχον</foreign> with <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἐξωθούμενον</foreign> . Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Xen. Anab. 3.5.11" ref="Xen. Anab. 3.5.11"> Xen. <title>Anab.</title> 3.5.11</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">πᾶς...ἀσκὸς δύο ἄνδρας ἕξει τὸ</quote> </cit> ( <foreign xml:lang="lat">v.l.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc"> τοῦ</quote> ) <quote xml:lang="grc">μὴ καταδῦναι· ὥστε δὲ μὴ ὀλισθάνειν, ἡ ὕλη καὶ ἡ γῆ σχήσει</quote> . </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἤμυναν_429">, <foreign xml:lang="lat">sc.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐμοί.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀνάστατος_429">, made to rise up and quit one's abode, "driven from house and home," implying <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀειφυγία</quote> <ref n="Soph. OC 601"> (601)</ref> , <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Trach. 39" ref="Soph. Trach. 39"> <title>Tr.</title> 39</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="trimeter"> <l part="F">ἐν Τραχῖνι τῇδ’ ἀνάστατοι</l> <l part="I">ξένῳ παρ’ ἀνδρὶ ναίομεν</l> </lg> </quote> </cit>(driven from our home at Argos). <cit> <bibl n="Thuc. 1.8" ref="Thuc. 1.8">Thuc. 1.8</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οἱ...ἐκ τῶν νήσων κακοῦργοι ἀνέστησαν ὑπ’ αὐτοῦ</quote> </cit>(were expelled). </gloss>

data.quote:
οἱ...ἐκ τῶν νήσων κακοῦργοι ἀνέστησαν
									ὑπ’ αὐτοῦ

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αὐτοῖν_430">, not dat. of the agent (very rare except with perf., plpf., or fut. pf. pass.), but dat. of interest ("so far as they were concerned"): cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Phil. 1030" ref="Soph. Phil. 1030"> <title>Ph.</title> 1030</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τέθνηχ’ ὑμῖν πάλαι</quote> </cit> : <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. PB 12" ref="Aesch. PB 12"> Aesch. <title>PV</title> 12</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">σφῷν μὲν ἐντολὴ Διὸς | ἔχει τέλος δή</quote> </cit>. </gloss>

data.quote:
σφῷν μὲν ἐντολὴ Διὸς | ἔχει τέλος
									δή

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐξεκηρύχθην_430">, by a proclamation of Creon (as regent) to the citizens—like that which Oed. himself had made ( <ref n="Soph. OT 216"> <title>O. T.</title> 216</ref>ff.). <quote xml:lang="grc">κήρυγμα</quote> is used of the royal <emph>edict</emph> , <ref n="Soph. Ant. 8"> <title>Ant.</title> 8</ref>, 161, etc. Cp. <ref n="Lys. 12.95">Lys. or. 12 § 95</ref> (of those banished by the Thirty) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐξεκηρύχθητε...ἐκ τῆς πόλεως</quote> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἴποις_ἂν_431">: the figure called <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑποφορά</quote> (Lat. <foreign xml:lang="lat">subiectio</foreign> , <ref>Cornificius 4. 23. 33</ref> ), the "suggestion" of an objection, with the reply; <ref> Tiberius <title xml:lang="grc">περὶ σχημάτων</title> § 36</ref>( <ref> Spengel <title>Rhet.</title> III. 77</ref>) <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑποφορὰ δέ ἐστιν ὅταν μὴ ἑξῆς προβαίνῃ ὁ λόγος, ἀλλ’ ὑποθείς τι ἢ ὡς παρὰ τοῦ ἀντιδίκου ἢ ὡς ἐκ τοῦ πράγματος ἀποκρίνηται πρὸς αὑτόν, ὥσπερ δύο ἀντιλεγόμενα πρόσωπα μιμούμενος</quote> . Oed. here speaks chiefly to Ism., whose pain for her brothers <ref n="Soph. OC 420">(420)</ref> might suggest the excuse; though in <ref n="Soph. OC 445">445</ref> , <ref n="Soph.
