OC 457">457</ref> he addresses the Chorus. Wecklein conceives Oed. as speaking directly to the spectators, who might recollect the close of the <ref n="Soph. OT"> <title>O. T.</title> </ref>Need we charge the poet with this dramatic impropriety? </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θέλοντι_431">, "desiring" (not merely "consenting"): cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 767">767</ref> : <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 1356" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg004:1356"> <title>O. T.</title> 1356</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">θέλοντι κἀμοὶ τοῦτ’ ἂν ἦν</quote> </cit>. The desire of Oed. to be sent away from Thebes is passionately expressed in the <ref n="Soph. OT"> <title>O. T.</title> </ref>( <ref n="Soph. OT 1410">1410 ff.</ref> , <ref n="Soph. OT 1449">1449 ff.</ref> ). At the end of that play he repeats the request ( <ref n="Soph. OT 1518">1518</ref> ), and Creon replies that it must be referred to Delphi. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τότε_431">with <quote xml:lang="grc">κατῄνεσεν</quote> , <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> "when I was banished"; so <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 650" ref="Soph. Aj. 650"> <title>Ai.</title> 650</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τότε</quote> </cit>="in those old days." </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἡμέραν_433">: the acc. of duration (cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 1138"> <title>O. T.</title> 1138</ref>) is strictly warrantable, as in <cit> <bibl n="Xen. Cyrop. 6.3.11" ref="Xen. Cyrop. 6.3.11"> Xen. <title>Cyr.</title> 6.3.11</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ ἐχθὲς δὲ καὶ τρίτην ἡμέραν</quote> </cit>(the day before yesterday) <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ αὐτὸ τοῦτο ἔπραττον</quote> : though in a negative sentence we might have rather expected the gen. (cp. <cit> <bibl n="Xen. Anab. 3.3.11" ref="Xen. Anab. 3.3.11"> <title>An.</title> 3. 3. 11</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τῆς ἡμέρας ὅλης διῆλθον οὐ πλέον πέντε καὶ εἴκοσι σταδίων</quote> </cit>). </gloss>

data.quote: καὶ ἐχθὲς δὲ καὶ τρίτην ἡμέραν

τῆς ἡμέρας ὅλης διῆλθον οὐ πλέον πέντε
									καὶ εἴκοσι σταδίων

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὴν_αὐτίχ_433"> : <cit> <bibl n="Thuc. 2.64" ref="Thuc. 2.64">Thuc. 2.64</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔς τε τὸ μέλλον...ἔς τε τὸ αὐτίκα</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Thuc. 3.112" ref="Thuc. 3.112">3. 112</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν τῷ αὐτίκα φόβῳ</quote> </cit> . </gloss>

data.quote:
ἔς τε τὸ μέλλον...ἔς τε τὸ
									αὐτίκα

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λευσθῆναι_πέτροις_435">, the typical form of summary vengeance on one who has incurred public execration: <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Il. 3.56" ref="Hom. Il. 3.56"> <title>Il.</title> 3.56</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἠέ κεν ἤδη | λάϊνον ἕσσο χιτῶνα</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. Ag. 1616" ref="Aesch. Ag. 1616"> Aesch. <title>Ag.</title> 1616</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">δημορριφεῖς...λευσίμους ἀράς</quote> </cit> : <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 254" ref="Soph. Aj. 254"> <title>Ai.</title> 254</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">λιθόλευστον Ἄρη</quote> </cit>(on the part of the infuriated army): <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Orest. 442" ref="Eur. Orest. 442"> Eur. <title>Or.</title> 442</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">θανεῖν ὑπ’ ἀστῶν λευσίμῳ πετρώματι</quote> </cit>(the pleonasm as here). <cit> <bibl n="Hdt. 9.5" ref="Hdt. 9.5">Her. 9.5</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <note anchored="true" place="inline" resp="RCJ" xml:lang="eng"> (the "lynching" of the athenian who advised his fellowcitizens to accept the persian terms, <date when="-0479">479 b.c.</date>479 b.c.</note>περιστάντες Λυκίδην κατέλευσαν βάλλοντες</quote> </cit>. The redundant <foreign xml:lang="grc">πέτροις</foreign> adds emphasis: so <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Ant. 200" ref="Soph. Ant. 200"> <title>Ant.</title> 200</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">πυρὶ | πρῆσαι κατάκρας</quote> </cit>. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 1255" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg004:1255"> <title>O. T.</title> 1255</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">φοιτᾷ γὰρ ἡμᾶς ἔγχος ἐξαιτῶν πορεῖν</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 1411" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg004:1411">1411</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">φονεύσατ’ ἢ θαλάσσιον | ἐκρίψατ’</quote> </cit>. </gloss>

data.quote:
ἠέ κεν ἤδη | λάϊνον ἕσσο
									χιτῶνα

φοιτᾷ γὰρ ἡμᾶς ἔγχος ἐξαιτῶν
									πορεῖν

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔρωτ_τόνδ_436">, the conjecture of Papageorgius (see cr. n.), is, I think, almost certain. The MS. change supposed is of the slightest kind, and such as continually occurs in our MSS.: while <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἔρωτος τοῦδ’</foreign> cannot be defended as either ( <emph>a</emph> ) gen. of connection, "helping in regard to this desire," or ( <emph>b</emph> ) possessive gen. with <foreign xml:lang="grc">ὠφελῶν</foreign> as= <quote xml:lang="grc">εὐεργέτης</quote> , "helper of this desire." See Appendix.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πέπων_437">. The metaphor is not directly from the mellowing of fruit, but from the medical use of the word in ref. to the subsiding of inflammation (as in angry tumours, etc.). Cp. the fig. sense of <quote xml:lang="grc">ὠμός</quote> . So <cit> <quote xml:lang="grc">πεπαίνεσθαι</quote> <bibl>Hippocr. 1170 B</bibl> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Aristot. Meteo. 4.3" ref="Aristot. Meteo. 4.3"> Arist. <title>Meteor.</title> 4.3</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἡ φυμάτων <note anchored="true" place="inline" resp="RCJ" xml:lang="eng">(tumours)</note>καὶ φλέγματος...πέπανσις</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl> <title>Anthol. Pal.</title> 12. 80</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="elegiacs"> <l part="F">τί σοι τὸ πεπανθὲν Ἔρωτος</l> <l>τραῦμα διὰ σπλάγχνων αὖθις ἀναφλέγεται;</l> </lg> </quote> </cit>Hence, too, <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Trach. 728" ref="Soph. Trach. 728"> <title>Tr.</title> 728</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὀργὴ πέπειρα</quote> </cit> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐκδραμόντα_438">, had rushed out, run to excess (not, run <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔξω δρόμου</quote> , out of the course): 08: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Ant. 752" ref="Soph. Ant. 752"> <title>Ant.</title> 752</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἦ κἀπαπειλῶν ὧδ’ ἐπεξέρχει θρασύς</quote> </cit>; dost thou e'en <emph>go to the length of</emph> threatening so boldly? </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸ_ἤδη_440">, just when that time had come (the art. as in <cit> <quote xml:lang="grc"> τὸ αὐτίκα, <note anchored="true" place="inline" resp="RCJ" xml:lang="eng">"at the moment,"</note> </quote> <bibl n="Thuc. 2.41" ref="Thuc. 2.41">Thuc. 2.41</bibl> </cit>). While <foreign xml:lang="grc">τηνικάδε</foreign> ("at this time of day") was common, the simple <foreign xml:lang="grc">τηνίκα</foreign> occurs nowhere else in elass. Attic; it is found, however, in the Alexandrian poets, and in later Greek. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοῦτο_μέν_440">is answered by <foreign xml:lang="grc">δέ</foreign> <ref n="Soph. OC 441"> (441)</ref> instead of <foreign xml:lang="grc">τοῦτο δέ</foreign> , as by <cit> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔπειτα δέ</quote> <bibl n="Soph. Ant. 63" ref="Soph. Ant. 63"> ( <title>Ant.</title> 63)</bibl> </cit> , <cit> <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῦτ’ αὖθις</quote> <bibl n="Soph. Ant. 165" ref="Soph. Ant. 165"> ( <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 165)</bibl> </cit>, <cit> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἶτα</quote> <bibl n="Soph. Phil. 1345" ref="Soph. Phil. 1345"> ( <title>Ph.</title> 1345)</bibl> </cit> , <cit> <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῦτ’ ἄλλο</quote> <bibl n="Soph. OT 605" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg004:605"> ( <title>O. T.</title> 605)</bibl> </cit> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="χρόνιον_441">, <quote xml:lang="eng">"after all that time,"</quote> — repeating the thought with which he had begun ( <quote xml:lang="grc">χρόνῳ</quote> 437). <cit> <bibl n="Thuc. 1.141" ref="Thuc. 1.141">Thuc. 1.141</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">χρόνιοι...ξυνιόντες</quote> </cit>, meeting only at long intervals: <cit> <bibl n="Thuc. 3.29" ref="Thuc. 3.29">3. 29</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">σχολαῖοι κομισθέντες</quote> </cit>, having made a leisurely voyage: <cit> <bibl n="Thuc. 8.14" ref="Thuc. 8.14">8. 14</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀφικνοῦνται αἰφνίδιοι.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐπωφελεῖν_441">with dat. (like <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπαρκεῖν</quote> ) as <ref n="Eur. Andr. 677"> Eur. <title>Andr.</title> 677</ref>, elsewhere usu. with acc. ( <ref n="Soph. Phil. 905"> <title>Ph.</title> 905</ref>, etc.): cp. the poet. dat. with the simple verb, <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Ant. 560" ref="Soph. Ant. 560"> <title>Ant.</title> 560</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῖς θανοῦσιν ὠφελεῖν</quote> </cit> . <!-- note short alpha pa^ and long alpha pa_ in last sentence --> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οἱ_πατρί_442">blends two forms of antithesis,—(1) <quote xml:lang="grc">οἱ παῖδες τῷ πατρί</quote> , and (2) <quote xml:lang="grc">οἱ τοῦ Οἰδίποδος τῷ Οἰδίποδι</quote> . The gen. of <quote xml:lang="eng"> "origin,"</quote> <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῦ πατρός</quote> , really a possessive gen., comes in with peculiar force here, as suggesting that the sons <emph>belong to</emph> the sire. For <quote xml:lang="grc"> πα^τρὸς...πα—τρί</quote> cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 883">883</ref> , <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Phil. 296" ref="Soph. Phil. 296"> <title>Ph.</title> 296</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλ’ ἐν πέτροισι πέτρον</quote> </cit> , <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1310"> <title>Ant.</title> 1310</ref>n. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸ_δρᾶν_442">, <ref target="cl47">on 47</ref> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔπους_χάριν_443">, for <emph>lack of</emph> a few words in his defence ( <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀντιλογίας βραχείας</quote> , schol.). As if one said, "They incurred all this loss <emph>for the sake of</emph> a petty sum" ( <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> to <emph> save</emph> it). This is a slight deviation from the ordinary use of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἕνεκα, οὕνεκα</quote> <ref n="Soph. OC 22">(22)</ref> , <quote xml:lang="grc">ἕκατι, χάριν</quote> , in such phrases. Cp. <cit> <bibl>fr. 510. 6</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="trimeter"> <l>κἀμοὶ γὰρ ἂν πατήρ γε δακρύων χάριν</l> <l>ἀνῆκτ’ ἂν εἰς φῶς</l> </lg> </quote> </cit>, would have been brought up, <emph>if tears could bring him:</emph> <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. Pers. 337" ref="Aesch. Pers. 337"> Aesch. <title>Pers.</title> 337</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">πλήθους...ἕκατι</quote> </cit>, if numbers could give victory. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σφιν_444">, <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> they looked on and did nothing: see <ref target="cl430"> on <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐτοῖν</quote> 430</ref>. The question between <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἀεί</foreign> (L) and <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἐγώ</foreign> (A) turns on these points. With <quote xml:lang="grc"> <emph>ἀεί</emph> , ἠλώμην</quote>= "continued to wander." He can scarcely mean that, <emph>after</emph> his expulsion, they might at any time have recalled him, since he regards the new oracle as having given them an opportunity which did not exist before <ref n="Soph. OC 418"> (418)</ref> . But he may mean that their silence <emph>at the moment</emph> of his expulsion was the cause of the whole sequel. With <quote xml:lang="grc"> <emph>ἐγώ</emph> , ἠλώμην</quote> <emph>might</emph>mean, "proceeded to wander forth," referring to the moment of expulsion (cp. <cit> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐξηλαυνόμην</quote> <bibl n="Soph. OC 356" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg007:356">356</bibl> </cit>). But ( <emph>a</emph> ) the tense is somewhat awkward here, and ( <emph>b</emph> ) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐγώ</quote> is weak unless taken as= " <emph>I</emph> —their father." </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοῖνδε_445">, not <quote xml:lang="grc">ταῖνδε</quote> , is the form of the fem. du. as found in Attic inscrr. of <date from="-0450" to="-0320">c. 450—320 B.C.</date> : cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 769"> <title>Ant.</title> 769</ref>n. But as to the partic., the dual forms in <quote xml:lang="grc">‐α, ‐αιν</quote> , and those in <quote xml:lang="grc">‐ε, ‐οιν</quote> , seem to have been used concurrently (cp. <ref target="cl1676">1676 n.</ref> ): I have not, therefore, changed <quote xml:lang="grc">οὔσαιν</quote> to <quote xml:lang="grc">ὄντοιν</quote> with Nauck. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τροφάς_446">: cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 330">330</ref> , <ref n="Soph. OC 341">341</ref> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γῆς_ἄδειαν_447">, a strange phrase (perh. corrupt), must mean, security <emph>in regard to the land</emph> (where I find myself at any given time), a secure resting-place. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Thuc. 8.64" ref="Thuc. 8.64">Thuc. 8.64</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">λαβοῦσαι αἱ πόλεις...ἄδειαν τῶν πρασσομένων</quote> </cit>, security <emph>in regard to</emph> their proceedings. His daughters, so far as they can, give him in exile all that his sons should have given him at Thebes,—(1) maintenance, (2) safety in his movements, (3) generally, the support due from kinsfolk.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γένους_447">= <quote xml:lang="grc">ἣν τὸ γένος παρέχει</quote> . <cit> <bibl n="Thuc. 7.34" ref="Thuc. 7.34">Thuc. 7.34</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">διὰ τὴν τοῦ ἀνέμου</quote> </cit> (subject.) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄπωσιν τῶν ναυαγίων</quote> (object.)= <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅτι ὁ ἄνεμος ἀπωθεῖ τὰ ναυάγια</quote> ( <ref> Thompson, <title>Synt.</title> § 98</ref>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κραίνειν_448">=(1) to bring a thing to pass, (2) to exercise power, to <emph>reign</emph> , sometimes with a <emph>gen.</emph> of the persons ruled ( <ref n="Soph. OC 296">296</ref> , <ref n="Soph. OC 862">862</ref> , etc.).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σκῆπτρα_448">goes with <quote xml:lang="grc">κραίνειν</quote> as an almost adverbial cognate accus., "to rule with sceptre": as <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Phil. 140" ref="Soph. Phil. 140"> <title>Ph.</title> 140</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">σκῆπτρον ἀνάσσεται</quote> </cit>(pass.) implies a similar <quote xml:lang="grc">σκῆπτρον ἀνάσσω</quote> . Rhythm forbids to take <quote xml:lang="grc"> σκῆπτρα</quote> with <quote xml:lang="grc">εἱλέσθην</quote> , making <quote xml:lang="grc">κραίνειν</quote> epexegetic ("so as to rule"). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λάχωσι_450">: this verb with gen. is less common than with accus., but is well attested not only in poetry but in prose, as <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Laws 775E" ref="Plat. Laws 775E"> Plat. <title>Legg.</title> 775E</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τιμῆς ἐὰν τῆς προσηκούσης...λαγχάνῃ</quote> </cit> <ref>(Krüger 1. 47. 14)</ref>. It is surely needless, then, to adopt Brunck's <foreign xml:lang="grc">τύχωσι</foreign> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοῦδε_450">, very rare for <foreign xml:lang="grc">τοῦδ’ ἀνδρός</foreign> as = <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἐμοῦ</foreign> : so <quote xml:lang="grc">τῆσδε</quote> = <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐμοῦ</quote> <ref n="Soph. Trach. 305"> <title>Tr.</title> 305</ref>, <quote xml:lang="grc">τῷδε</quote> = <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐμοί</quote> <ref n="Soph. Trach. 1012"> <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 1012</ref>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="συμμάχου_450">predicate; cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 1482" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg007:1482">1482</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐναισίου δὲ σοῦ τύχοιμι</quote> </cit>, and <ref n="Soph. OC 1486">1486</ref> . </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὰ_παλαίφατα_453">=the earlier predictions which I, on my side, can produce: those which the resources of my knowledge furnish forth.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐξ_453">is appropriate, since they have been so long treasured in his inmost soul. Cp. on <ref target="cl293">293</ref> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἤνυσεν_454">, by bringing him to the grove (cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 87">87</ref> ), in earnest that the requital predicted for the authors of his exile ( <ref n="Soph. OC 93">93</ref> ) will also come to pass. If <quote xml:lang="grc">ἤνυσεν</quote> were referred to the involuntary crimes of Oed., the connection of thought would be less close. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὁμοῦ_προστάτισι_457">(predicative), along with them as your protectors or champions against Theban violence. Oed. is already under the guard of the Eumenides as their <cit> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἱκέτης</quote> <bibl n="Soph. OC 284" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg007:284">(284)</bibl> </cit>: if the Coloniates are loyal to the Eumenides, Attica and he will alike be saved.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δημούχοις_458">, holding, reigning among, your people: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 160" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg004:160"> <title>O. T.</title> 160</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">γαιάοχον … | Ἄρτεμιν</quote> </cit> : <cit> <bibl n="Aristoph. Kn. 581" ref="Aristoph. Kn. 581"> Aristoph. <title>Eq.</title> 581</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">Παλλὰς πολιοῦχος</quote> </cit> : <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. Seven 69" ref="Aesch. Seven 69"> Aesch. <title>Th.</title> 69</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">πολισσοῦχοι θεοί</quote> </cit>. But below, <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 1087" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg007:1087">1087</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">γᾶς...δαμούχοις</quote> </cit>= the Athenians, <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 1348" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg007:1348">1348</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">δημοῦχος χθονός</quote> </cit>= the king. The word is tinged here with the notion of "deme": cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 78">78</ref> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀλκὴν_ποεῖσθαι_459">(for the spelling see <ref target="cl278">278 n.</ref> ), a simple periphrasis,= <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλκαθεῖν</quote> : <cit> <bibl n="Thuc. 1.124" ref="Thuc. 1.124">Thuc. 1.124</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ποεῖσθαι τιμωρίαν</quote> </cit> = <quote xml:lang="grc">τιμωρεῖν</quote> (to succour), <cit> <bibl n="Thuc. 2.94" ref="Thuc. 2.94">2. 94</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">φυλακὴν... ἐποιοῦντο</quote> </cit> = <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐφύλασσον</quote> , etc. Distinguish <cit> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλκὴν τιθέναι τινός</quote> <bibl n="Soph. OC 1524" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg007:1524">(1524)</bibl> </cit>, to create a defence against a thing. A <emph>gen.</emph> after <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλκή</quote> as = "succour" must denote ( <emph>a</emph> ) the defender, as in <quote xml:lang="grc">Διὸς ἀλκή</quote> , or ( <emph> b</emph> ) the danger;— not the interest defended. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀρεῖσθε_460">: <ref n="Soph. Aj. 75"> <title>Ai.</title> 75</ref>n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐπάξιος_461">, <foreign xml:lang="lat">sc.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἶ</quote> . When the verb is thus omitted, the pron. is usu. added: here, the absence of <quote xml:lang="grc">σύ</quote> is excused by <foreign xml:lang="grc"> Οἰδίπους</foreign> . This form of the voc. has the best MS. authority in some 12 places of Soph., as against 3 which support <quote xml:lang="grc">Οἰδίπου</quote> (more often gen.), viz. below, <ref n="Soph. OC 557">557</ref> , <ref n="Soph. OC 1346">1346</ref> , and <ref n="Soph. OT 405"> <title>O. T.</title> 405</ref>(where see n.). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κατοικτίσαι_461"> : <cit> <bibl n="Thuc. 1.138" ref="Thuc. 1.138">Thuc. 1.138</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄξιος θαυμάσαι</quote> </cit>. The pass. inf. is rarer in this constr., as <cit> <quote xml:lang="grc">τίεσθαι δ’ ἀξιώτατος</quote> <bibl n="Aesch. Ag. 531" ref="Aesch. Ag. 531"> Aesch. <title>Ag.</title> 531</bibl> </cit>. Cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 37">37</ref> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αὐτός_θ_462">: cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 559">559</ref> , <ref n="Soph. OC 1009">1009</ref> , <ref n="Soph. OC 1125">1125</ref> , <ref n="Soph. OC 1310">1310</ref> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐπεμβάλλεις_463">, you insert yourself in this plea as a deliverer: <foreign xml:lang="lat"> i.e.</foreign> to his protest against a breach of their promise ( <ref n="Soph. OC 258">258—291</ref> ), and his appeal to pity, he adds a promise of benefit to Attica ( <ref n="Soph. OC 287">287</ref> , <ref n="Soph. OC 459">459</ref> ). Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Hdt. 2.4" ref="Hdt. 2.4">Her. 2.4</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">διὰ τρίτου ἔτεος ἐμβόλιμον ἐπεμβάλλουσι</quote> </cit>, they <emph> insert</emph> an intercalary month every other year: <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Crat. 399A" ref="Plat. Crat. 399A"> Plat. <title>Crat.</title> 399A</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">πολλάκις ἐπεμβάλλομεν γράμματα, τὰ δ’ ἐξαιροῦμεν</quote> </cit>, we <emph>insert</emph> letters (in words), or remove them.</gloss>

data.quote:
πολλάκις ἐπεμβάλλομεν γράμματα, τὰ δ’
							ἐξαιροῦμεν

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τῷδε_λόγῳ_463">is not instrum. dat., but goes with the verb. Not, "further pledge yourself to be the deliverer": in <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Phil. 813" ref="Soph. Phil. 813"> <title>Ph.</title> 813</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐμβάλλω μενεῖν</quote> </cit> follows <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔμβαλλε χειρὸς πίστιν</quote> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="προξένει_465">, grant me thy kindly offices (of advice and direction), as a man does in his own State to the foreigners who have made him their <quote xml:lang="grc">πρόξενος</quote> (see n. on <ref n="Soph. OT 1483"> <title>O. T.</title> 1483</ref>). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὡς_τελοῦντι_465">, in the assurance that I will perform anything required of me: cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 13">13.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καθαρμὸν_δαιμόνων_466">(poss. gen.), such a lustration as belongs to them, is due to them: not object. gen., since <quote xml:lang="grc">καθαίρειν</quote> could not stand for <quote xml:lang="grc">ἱλάσκεσθαι</quote> .</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κατέστειψας_467"> : <cit> <bibl n="Sapph. 95" ref="Sapph. 95">Sappho fr. 95</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="lyric"> <l>οἵαν τὰν ὑάκινθον ἐν οὔρεσι ποιμένες ἄνδρες</l> <l>ποσσὶ καταστείβοισι</l> </lg> </quote> </cit>, "trample on": here the word suggests the rash violation of the <cit> <quote xml:lang="grc">χῶρον οὐχ ἁγνὸν πατεῖν</quote> <bibl n="Soph. OC 37" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg007:37">(37)</bibl> </cit>. The <foreign xml:lang="lat">v.l.</foreign> <foreign xml:lang="grc"> κατέστεψας</foreign> was explained figuratively: "came to the ground as a suppliant," who lays his branch ( <quote xml:lang="grc">ἱκετηρία</quote> ), twined with festoons of wool ( <quote xml:lang="grc">στέφη</quote> ), on an altar: see n. on <ref n="Soph. OT 3"> <title>O. T.</title> 3</ref>. Schol.: <quote xml:lang="grc">καθικέτευσας, μετὰ ἱκετηρίων ἀφίκου</quote> : justly adding that the other reading is <quote xml:lang="grc">πιθανώτερον.</quote> <foreign xml:lang="grc"> κατάστεψον</foreign> (marg. of L) was a grammarian's attempt to improve on <quote xml:lang="grc">κατέστεψας</quote> : it would refer to the twigs <ref n="Soph. OC 483">(483)</ref> ; but a secondary detail of the rite should not be thus forestalled and emphasised. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀειρύτου_469">. The rule is that <foreign xml:lang="grc">ρ</foreign> is doubled when, by inflection or composition, a <emph>simple vowel</emph> precedes it, but remains single when a <emph>diphthong</emph> precedes it: hence <quote xml:lang="grc">νεόρρυτος</quote> , but <quote xml:lang="grc"> ἀείρυτος</quote> . Through overlooking this distinction, Blaydes follows our MSS. in writing <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἀειρρύτου</foreign> . Metre often led the poets to use <foreign xml:lang="grc">ρ</foreign> instead of <foreign xml:lang="grc">ρρ</foreign> , as <quote xml:lang="grc"> ἀμφι^ρύτου</quote> ( <ref n="Soph. Aj. 134"> <title>Ai.</title> 134</ref>), <quote xml:lang="grc">χρυσορύτους</quote> ( <ref n="Soph. Ant. 950"> <title>Ant.</title> 950</ref>), <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐτόριζος</quote> ( <ref> Babrius <emph>fab.</emph> 69</ref>); and <quote xml:lang="grc"> προρέω</quote> , not <quote xml:lang="grc">προρρέω</quote> , was the regular form, as euphony plainly required. But there is no classical instance of the opposite anomaly. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δῑ_χειρῶν_470"> , <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> after duly washing the hands before entering the sacred precinct. Blood-guilt is not thought of here: if that was in question, <cit> <quote xml:lang="grc">καθαρμοὶ χοιροκτόνοι</quote> <bibl n="Aesch. Eum. 283" ref="Aesch. Eum. 283"> (Aesch. <title>Eum.</title> 283)</bibl> </cit>would first be needed for Oed. himself: but the Chorus assume that, as he said, he is now <cit> <quote xml:lang="grc">εὐσεβής</quote> <bibl n="Soph. OC 287" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg007:287">(287)</bibl> </cit>. Washings, or sprinklings, were required before approaching shrines, and for this purpose <quote xml:lang="grc">περιρραντήρια</quote> were set at the entrances of sacred places. Cp. <cit> <bibl> Hippocr. <title>Morb. Sacr.</title> 2</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅρους τοῖσι θεοῖσι τῶν ἱερῶν καὶ τῶν τεμενέων ἀποδεικνύμενοι, ὡς ἂν μηδεὶς ὑπερβαίνοι εἰ μὴ ἁγνεύοι, εἰσιόντες δὲ περιρραινόμεθα, οὐχ ὡς μιαινόμενοι, ἀλλ’ εἴ τι καὶ πρότερον ἔχομεν μύσος, τοῦτο ἀφαγνιούμενοι</quote> </cit>. <cit> <bibl> Lucian <title>Sacrific.</title> 13</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ μὲν πρόγραμμά φησι μὴ παριέναι εἴσω τῶν περιρραντηρίων ὅστις μὴ καθαρός ἐστι τὰς χεῖρας</quote> </cit>. So <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Od. 4.750" ref="Hom. Od. 4.750"> <title>Od.</title> 4.750</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="hexameter"> <l>ἀλλ’ ὑδρηναμένη, καθαρὰ χροῒ εἵματ’ ἔχουσα, …</l> <l>εὔχε’ Ἀθηναίῃ</l> </lg> </quote> </cit> . </gloss>

data.quote:
ὅρους τοῖσι θεοῖσι τῶν ἱερῶν καὶ τῶν
									τεμενέων ἀποδεικνύμενοι, ὡς ἂν μηδεὶς ὑπερβαίνοι εἰ μὴ
									ἁγνεύοι, εἰσιόντες δὲ περιρραινόμεθα, οὐχ ὡς
									μιαινόμενοι, ἀλλ’ εἴ τι καὶ πρότερον ἔχομεν μύσος, τοῦτο
							ἀφαγνιούμενοι

τὸ μὲν πρόγραμμά φησι μὴ παριέναι εἴσω
									τῶν περιρραντηρίων ὅστις μὴ καθαρός ἐστι τὰς
									χεῖρας

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοῦτο_471">, adject., but without art. (cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 1177">1177</ref> ), an epic use sometimes allowed by the Attic poets, and not rare in Sophocles. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀκήρατον_471"> : <ref> Chrysippus <foreign xml:lang="lat">ap.</foreign> Plut. <title>Stoic. repugn.</title> 22</ref>commends Hesiod for enjoining on men that they should respect the <emph>purity</emph> of rivers and springs, since thence the gods were served ( <ref n="Hes. WD 755"> Hes. <title>Opp.</title> 755</ref>). !-- *** --> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κρατῆρές_εἰσιν_472"> : <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> the priest in charge of the shrine keeps them ready for the use of the worshippers, near the spring in the inner part of the grove (505), from which they were to be filled. The libations to the Eumenides were wineless (100), but they are associated with the mixing-bowl which was regularly used in libations (of wine) to other deities. Bekker <title>Anecd.</title> 274. 3 <quote xml:lang="grc">κρατηρίζων· ἤτοι τὸν οἶνον ἐν κρατῆρι κιρνῶν, ἢ ἀπὸ κρατήρων σπένδων</quote> . <cit> <bibl n="Dem. 19.280" ref="Dem. 19.280"> Dem. <title>De Fals. Legat.</title> § 280</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">σπονδῶν καὶ κρατήρων κοινωνούς</quote> </cit> . <cit> <bibl n="Dem. 21.53" ref="Dem. 21.53"> <title>In Mid.</title> § 53</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <note anchored="true" place="inline" resp="RCJ" xml:lang="eng">(in a spurious oracle)</note>ἱερὰ τελεῖν καὶ κρατῆρα κεράσαι</quote> </cit> .</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εὔχειρος_472">: schol. <quote xml:lang="grc">εὐπαλάμου</quote> . <cit> <bibl n="Pind. O. 9" ref="Pind. O. 9"> Pind. <title>Ol.</title> 9.111</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">εὔχειρα, δεξιόγυιον</quote> </cit> , <quote xml:lang="eng">"deft-handed, nimble-limbed,"</quote> of a wrestler. Lucian <title>Amor.</title> 11 <quote xml:lang="grc">τῆς Πραξιτέλους εὐχειρίας</quote> ( <foreign xml:lang="lat">v.l.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">εὐχερείας</quote> ). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τέχνη_472">: fr. 161 <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅπλοις ἀρρῶξιν, Ἡφαίστου τέχνῃ</quote> (the <emph>work</emph> of Hephaestus): a common use of the word in later Greek. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Verg. A. 5.359" ref="Verg. A. 5.359"> Verg. <title>Aen.</title> 5. 359</bibl> <quote xml:lang="lat">clipeum...Didymaonis artes.</quote> </cit>Mixing-bowls were made not only of earthenware, but oft. of gold, silver, or bronze: Achilles had an <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἀργύρεον κρητῆρα τετυγμένον</foreign> : <cit> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="hexameter"> <l part="F">...αὐτὰρ κάλλει ἐνίκα πᾶσαν ἐπ’ αἶαν</l> <l>πολλόν· ἐπεὶ Σιδόνες πολυδαίδαλοι εὖ ἤσκησαν</l> </lg> </quote> <bibl n="Hom. Il. 23.741" ref="Hom. Il. 23.741">( <title>Il.</title> 23.741)</bibl> </cit> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κρᾶτ_473">, acc. sing., the <quote xml:lang="eng">"top,"</quote> <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> rim, of each <quote xml:lang="grc"> κρατήρ</quote> . In <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Il. 19.93" ref="Hom. Il. 19.93"> <title>Il.</title> 19.93</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">κράατα</quote> </cit>is acc. plur., and Pindar is quoted by Eustath. ( <ref n="Hom. Od. 12.1715"> <title>Od.</title> 12.1715</ref>. 63) as having said <quote xml:lang="grc">τρία κρᾶτα</quote> (for <quote xml:lang="grc">κράατα</quote> ). But in <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Od. 8.92" ref="Hom. Od. 8.92"> <title>Od.</title> 8.92</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">κρᾶτα</quote> </cit>is sing., and so always in Attic: Soph. has ( <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ</quote> ) <quote xml:lang="grc">κρᾶτα</quote> several times as acc., and once as nom. An acc. plur. masc. <quote xml:lang="grc">κρᾶτας</quote> occurs twice in Eur. ( <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1149"> <title>Ph.</title> 1149</ref>, <title>H. F.</title> 526). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λαβὰς_ἀμφιστόμους_473">, handles on each side of the <quote xml:lang="grc">στόμα</quote> , or mouth. The festoon of wool, which was to be wreathed round the rim of the bowl, could be secured to these. Wecklein understands handles which also served as spouts. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θαλλοῖσιν_474">, of olive. <quote xml:lang="grc">κρόκη</quote> , from <quote xml:lang="grc">κρέκω</quote> , to strike the web, in weaving, with the <quote xml:lang="grc">κερκίς</quote> , or rod, is the woof, the warp being <quote xml:lang="grc">στήμων</quote> : in <cit> <bibl n="Pind. N. 10" ref="Pind. N. 10"> Pind. <title>Nem.</title> 10.43</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">μαλακαῖσι κρόκαις</quote> </cit>are cloaks of soft woollen texture. So here <foreign xml:lang="grc">κρόκαι</foreign> are woollen cloths. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λαβών_475">, <foreign xml:lang="lat">sc.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐτόν</quote> : cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Trach. 1216" ref="Soph. Trach. 1216"> <title>Tr.</title> 1216</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">( διδούς )</quote> </cit> : <cit> <bibl n="Aristoph. Birds 56" ref="Aristoph. Birds 56"> Aristoph. <title>Av.</title> 56</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">σὺ δ’ οὖν λίθῳ κόψον λαβών</quote> </cit> (in <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 607" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg004:607"> <title>O. T.</title> 607</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">λαβών</quote> </cit>is not similar): <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Il. 7.303" ref="Hom. Il. 7.303"> <title>Il.</title> 7.303</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">δῶκε ξίφος ἀργυρόηλον—σὺν κολεῷ τε φέρων καὶ ἐϋτμήτῳ τελαμῶνι</quote> </cit>. The guardian of the grove <ref n="Soph. OC 506">(506)</ref> would supply the <foreign xml:lang="grc">μαλλός</foreign> . </gloss>

data.quote: σὺ δ’ οὖν λίθῳ κόψον λαβών

δῶκε ξίφος ἀργυρόηλον—σὺν κολεῷ τε
									φέρων καὶ ἐϋτμήτῳ τελαμῶνι

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸ_ἔνθεν_476">, rare for <foreign xml:lang="grc">τὸ ἐνθένδε, τὸ ἐντεῦθεν</foreign> , but cp. <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. Ag. 247" ref="Aesch. Ag. 247"> Aesch. <title>Ag.</title> 247</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰ δ’ ἔνθεν οὔτ’ εἶδον οὔτ’ ἐννέπω</quote> </cit>. Here prob. adverbial: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Phil. 895" ref="Soph. Phil. 895"> <title>Ph.</title> 895 </bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τί δῆτ’ ἂν δρῷμ’ ἐγὼ τοὐνθένδε γε;</quote> </cit> </gloss>

data.quote:
τὰ δ’ ἔνθεν οὔτ’ εἶδον οὔτ’
									ἐννέπω

τί δῆτ’ ἂν δρῷμ’ ἐγὼ τοὐνθένδε
									γε;

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ποῖ_τελ_476">, to what conclusion am I to bring the rite? Thus far it has been all preparation. see on <ref target="cl227">227</ref> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="χοὰς_χέασθαι_477"> . <foreign xml:lang="grc">χοαί</foreign> were offered to the gods of the under-world (cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 1599">1599</ref> ), or to the dead ( <ref n="Soph. Ant. 431"> <title>Ant.</title> 431</ref>), as <quote xml:lang="grc">σπονδαί</quote> to the gods above. <quote xml:lang="grc">λοιβαί</quote> usu.= <quote xml:lang="grc">σπονδαί</quote> , but= <quote xml:lang="grc">χοαί</quote> in <ref n="Soph. El. 52"> <title>El.</title> 52</ref>(to the dead). So <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. Eum. 107" ref="Aesch. Eum. 107"> Aesch. <title>Eum.</title> 107</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">χοὰς ἀοίνους</quote> </cit>, of the Eumenides. The midd. verb as <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Od. 10.518" ref="Hom. Od. 10.518"> <title>Od.</title> 10.518</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">( χοὴν χεῖσθαι )</quote> </cit>, and <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. Pers. 219" ref="Aesch. Pers. 219"> Aesch. <title>Pers.</title> 219</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">χρὴ χοὰς | γῇ τε καὶ φθιτοῖς χέασθαι</quote> </cit>. The verb with cogn. acc. gives solemnity, as in <quote xml:lang="grc">θυσίαν θύειν, σπονδὰς σπένδειν</quote> , etc. </gloss>

data.quote:
χρὴ χοὰς | γῇ τε καὶ φθιτοῖς
									χέασθαι

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πρὸς_ἕω_477">, not meaning, of course, that the <emph>time</emph> must be dawn. On the contrary it was an ancient custom that sacrifices to the <quote xml:lang="grc">χθόνιοι</quote> and to the dead should not be offered till after midday: <title>Etym. M.</title> 468 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπὸ δὲ μεσημβρίας ἔθυον τοῖς καταχθονίοις</quote> , and in <ref n="Aesch. Eum. 109"> Aesch. <title> Eum.</title> 109</ref>Clytaemnestra speaks of sacrificing to the Eum. by night, <quote xml:lang="grc">ὥραν οὐδενὸς κοινὴν θεῶν</quote> .</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κρωσσοῖς_478"> here= <quote xml:lang="grc">κρατῆρσιν</quote> . The word is fitting, since the <quote xml:lang="grc">κρωσσός</quote> was more esp. used for <emph>water</emph> ( <ref n="Eur. Ion 1173"> Eur. <title>Ion</title> 1173</ref>, <ref n="Eur. Cycl. 89"> <title>Cycl.</title> 89</ref>), though also sometimes for wine ( <cit> <bibl>Aesch. fr. 91</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">κρωσσοὺς | μήτ’ οἰνηροὺς μήτ’ ὑδατηρούς</quote> </cit>), also for oil,—or as a cinerary urn. Guhl and Koner (p. 149) think that the krossos resembled the <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑδρία</quote> , which, like the <quote xml:lang="grc">κάλπις</quote> , was a bulky, shortnecked vessel, oft. seen in the vase-paintings as borne by maidens on their heads when fetching water.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οἷς_478">by attract. for <quote xml:lang="grc">οὕς.</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="χέω_478">delib. aor. (rather than pres.) subjunct. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πηγάς_479">: here, strictly the gushing of the water from the bowl. From each of the three bowls he is to pour a <quote xml:lang="grc">χοή</quote> . The first and second bowls are to be filled with the spring water <emph> only;</emph> and from each of these he is to make a libation without emptying the bowl. The third bowl is to contain water sweetened with honey; and, in making the libation from <emph>this</emph> , he is to empty it.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τρισσάς_479">might be distributive, <quote xml:lang="eng">"three from each bowl"</quote> (as the number nine recurs in 483); but in the <quote xml:lang="grc">χοή</quote> to the dead in <ref n="Hom. Od. 10.519"> <title>Od.</title> 10.519</ref>, at least, there are only three pourings, viz. of (1) hydromel, (2) wine, (3) water.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸν_τελευτ_479">. ( <quote xml:lang="grc">κρωσσόν</quote> ) as if <quote xml:lang="grc"> ἔκχεον</quote> , not <quote xml:lang="grc">χέον</quote> , were understood: cp. <cit> <bibl>Menander fr. 461</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸν χοᾶ | ἐκκέχυκας</quote> </cit>, you have emptied the pitcher. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θῶ_480">has raised needless doubts. The operator is to fetch water from the spring in the grove (469), fill the bowls which he will find ready, and <emph> place</emph> them in a convenient position for the rite. From the distinction just drawn between the first two bowls and the third, Oed. surmises that the contents of the latter are not to be of precisely the same nature as those of the others. He asks, then,— <quote xml:lang="eng">"With what shall I fill it, before placing it beside the other two,—preparatory to beginning the rite?"</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μελίσσης_481"> = <quote xml:lang="grc">μέλιτος</quote> : schol. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπὸ γὰρ τοῦ ποιοῦντος τὸ ποιούμενον</quote> , quoting the <title>Erastae</title> (fr. 160) <quote xml:lang="grc">γλώσσης μελίσσης τῷ κατερρυηκότι</quote> . So <quote xml:lang="grc">πορφύρα</quote> (the purple-fish)=purple, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐλέφας</quote> =ivory, <quote xml:lang="grc">χελώνη</quote> =tortoise-shell. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="προσφέρειν_481">infin. for imper., as esp. in precepts or maxims: cp. 490, <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 1466" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg004:1466"> <title>O. T.</title> 1466</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">αἷν μοι μέλεσθαι</quote> </cit>, 1529 <quote xml:lang="grc">μηδέν’ ὀλβίζειν</quote> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μελάμφυλλος_482">, overshadowed by dense foliage. <cit> <bibl n="Pind. P. 1" ref="Pind. P. 1"> Pind. <title>P.</title> 1. 27</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">Αἴτνας ἐν μελαμφύλλοις...κορυφαῖς</quote> </cit> , <cit> <bibl n="Aristoph. Thes. 997" ref="Aristoph. Thes. 997"> Ar. <title>Th.</title> 997</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">μ. τ’ ὄρη δάσκια</quote> </cit> . </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αὐτῇ_483">, <foreign xml:lang="lat">sc.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν τῇ γῇ</quote> , locative dat. (411).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐξ_χεροῖν_483">, perh. laying them with each hand alternately, beginning and ending with the right, or lucky, hand. The olive-branches symbolise the fruits of the earth and of the womb, for the increase of which the Eumenides were esp. prayed, since they could blight it: <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. Eum. 907" ref="Aesch. Eum. 907"> Aesch. <title>Eum.</title> 907</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="trimeter"> <l>καρπόν τε γαίας καὶ βοτῶν ἐπίρρυτον</l> <l>ἀστοῖσιν εὐθενοῦντα μὴ κάμνειν χρόνῳ,</l> <l>καὶ τῶν βροτείων σπερμάτων σωτηρίαν</l> </lg> </quote> </cit> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐπεύχεσθαι_484"> , <quote xml:lang="eng">"over"</quote> the rite, to complete it: the prayer was to be said while the twigs were being laid; hence <quote xml:lang="grc">τιθείς</quote> , not <quote xml:lang="grc">θείς</quote> . </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τούτων_485">(for the gen. cp. 418), <foreign xml:lang="lat">sc.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">τῶν λιτῶν</quote> :</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μέγιστα_485">, neut. pl. without subject, instead of <quote xml:lang="grc">μέγιστον</quote> (cp. 495): <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 1126" ref="Soph. Aj. 1126"> <title>Ai.</title> 1126</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">δίκαια γὰρ τόνδ’ εὐτυχεῖν</quote> </cit> ...; <cit> <bibl n="Thuc. 5.14" ref="Thuc. 5.14">Thuc. 5.14</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀδύνατα εἶναι ἐφαίνετο...πολεμεῖν</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Orest. 413" ref="Eur. Orest. 413"> Eur. <title>Or.</title> 413</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ δεινὰ πάσχειν δεινὰ τοὺς εἰγρασμένους</quote> </cit>. </gloss>

data.quote:
οὐ δεινὰ πάσχειν δεινὰ τοὺς
									εἰγρασμένους

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Εὐμενίδας_486">: see on <ref target="cl42">42</ref> .</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐξ_486">, properly with ref. to the inner spring of the feeling, but here almost= <quote xml:lang="eng"> " <emph>with</emph> "</quote> <emph>:</emph>cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 528" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg004:528"> <title>O. T.</title> 528</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐξ ὀμμάτων δ’ ὀρθῶν τε κἀξ ὀρθῆς φρενός</quote> </cit>. Slightly different is <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκ θυμοῦ</quote> ,= <quote xml:lang="eng"> " <emph>from</emph> my heart"</quote>( <ref n="Hom. Il. 9.343"> <title>Il.</title> 9.343</ref>). </gloss>

data.quote:
ἐξ ὀμμάτων δ’ ὀρθῶν τε κἀξ ὀρθῆς
									φρενός

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸν_ἱκέτην_487">: cp. 44, 284.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σωτήριον_487"> = <quote xml:lang="eng"> "with a view to <quote xml:lang="grc"> σωτηρία"</quote> ,</quote>—leaving the hearer to think of that which Oed. gives, and <emph>also</emph> of that which he receives. <quote xml:lang="grc">σωτήριος</quote> is nowhere definitely pass., as= <quote xml:lang="grc">σῶς</quote> , <quote xml:lang="eng"> " <emph>saved</emph> "</quote> <emph>;</emph>for in <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. Lib. 236" ref="Aesch. Lib. 236"> Aesch. <title>Cho.</title> 236</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">σπέρματος σωτηρίου</quote> </cit>is the seed which is to continue the race. Hence it is usu. taken here as= <quote xml:lang="eng">"fraught with good for us,"</quote> with ref. to his promise, <quote xml:lang="grc">σωτῆρ’ ἀρεῖσθε</quote> (460). That idea is present, but does not exclude the other. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σύ_τ_488">, not <quote xml:lang="grc">σύ γ’</quote> , is right. The constr. is <quote xml:lang="grc"> σύ <emph>τε</emph> αὐτὸς αἰτοῦ, <emph>καὶ</emph> </quote> ( <quote xml:lang="grc">αἰτείσθω</quote> ) <quote xml:lang="grc">εἴ τις ἄλλος ἀντὶ σοῦ</quote> ( <quote xml:lang="grc">αἰτεῖται</quote> ). This is to be the prayer, <emph>both if</emph> thou thyself prayest, <emph>and if</emph> another prays for thee. In such statements the conjunctive <quote xml:lang="grc">τε... καί</quote> is equally admissible with the disjunctive <quote xml:lang="grc">εἴτε...εἴτε</quote> . Cp. 1444: <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Hec. 751" ref="Eur. Hec. 751"> Eur. <title>Hec.</title> 751</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τολμᾶν ἀνάγκη κἂν τύχω κἂν μὴ τύχω</quote> </cit>. </gloss>

data.quote:
τολμᾶν ἀνάγκη κἂν τύχω κἂν μὴ
									τύχω

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄπυστα_489">: schol. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνήκουστα</quote> (cp. on <ref target="cl130">130</ref> ). Hence, he adds (quoting Polemon of Illium, circ. 180 B.C.), the hereditary priests of the Eumenides were called <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἡσυχίδαι</quote> . Their eponymous hero, <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἥσυχος</quote> , had an <quote xml:lang="grc">ἡρῷον</quote> between the Areiopagus and the W. foot of the acropolis, and to him, before a sacrifice, they offered a ram. Priestesses of a like name, serving the Eumenides, are mentioned by <cit> <bibl>Callimachus fr. 123</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="elegiac"> <l> νηφάλιαι καὶ τῇσιν ἀεὶ μελιηδέας ὄμπνας <note anchored="true" place="inline" resp="RCJ" xml:lang="eng">(barley cakes)</note> </l> <l>λῄτειραι καίειν ἔλλαχον Ἡσυχίδες</l> </lg> </quote> </cit> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μηκύνων_489">, <quote xml:lang="eng">"making loud"</quote> : a sense found only here (cp. 1609). In <quote xml:lang="grc">μακρὸν ἀϋτεῖν</quote> (Hom.), <quote xml:lang="grc">ἠχεῖν</quote> (Plat.), etc., the idea of <quote xml:lang="eng">"loud"</quote> comes through that of <quote xml:lang="eng">"heard afar."</quote> The schol. perh. understood here, <quote xml:lang="eng"> "loud <emph>and</emph> long,"</quote>for on <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄπυστα</quote> he says, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀντὶ τοῦ, ἠρέμα καὶ συντόμως</quote> . </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀφέρπειν_490">=imperat. (481).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄστροφος_490">: so in <ref n="Aesch. Lib. 98"> Aesch. <title>Cho.</title> 98</ref>Electra debates whether, after pouring her mother's offering at Agamemnon's grave, she shall turn away,— <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="trimeter"> <l>καθάρμαθ’ ὥς τις ἐκπέμψας, πάλιν</l> <l>δικοῦσα τεῦχος, ἀστρόφοισιν ὄμμασιν</l> </lg> </quote>. In <ref n="Theoc. 24">Theoc. 24. 92</ref> Teiresias directs that the ashes of the serpents which would have strangled the infant Heracles shall be cast beyond the borders by one of Alcmena's handmaids: <quote xml:lang="grc">ἂψ δὲ νέεσθαι | ἄστρεπτος</quote> . <cit> <bibl n="Verg. Ecl. 8" ref="Verg. Ecl. 8"> Verg. <title>Ecl.</title> 8. 101</bibl> <quote xml:lang="lat"> <lg type="hexameter"> <l>Fer cineres, Amarylli, foras, rivoque fluenti</l> <l>Transque caput iace, neu respexeris.</l> </lg> </quote> </cit> <cit> <bibl n="Ov. Fast. 6.164" ref="Ov. Fast. 6.164">Ov. <title>Fasti</title> 6. 164</bibl> <quote xml:lang="lat">Quique sacris adsunt respicere illa vetat.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

data.quote:
Quique sacris adsunt respicere illa
									vetat.

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="παρασταίην_491">, as thy friend and helper: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 1383" ref="Soph. Aj. 1383"> <title>Ai.</title> 1383</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τούτῳ γὰρ … | μόνος παρέστης χερσίν</quote> </cit>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="προσχώρων_493">, who therefore can judge best (cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 12">12</ref> ). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὁδωτά_495">, plur., as <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Ant. 677" ref="Soph. Ant. 677"> <title>Ant.</title> 677</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="trimeter"> <l>οὕτως ἀμυντέ’ ἐστι τοῖς κοσμουμένοις,</l> <l>κοὔτοι γυναικὸς οὐδαμῶς ἡσσητέα</l> </lg> </quote> </cit> : <cit> <bibl n="Thuc. 1.118" ref="Thuc. 1.118">Thuc. 1. 118</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπιχειρητέα ἐδόκει εἶναι</quote> </cit>: cp. 485, 1360.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λείπομαι_495">, pass., <quote xml:lang="eng">"I am at a disadvantage"</quote> ; usu. with gen. of thing, as <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 474" ref="Soph. El. 474"> <title>El.</title> 474</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">γνώμας λειπομένα σοφᾶς</quote> </cit>, or person, <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Trach. 266" ref="Soph. Trach. 266"> <title>Tr.</title> 266</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τῶν ὧν τέκνων λείποιτο.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐν_495">: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 1112" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg004:1112"> <title>O. T.</title> 1112</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔν τε γὰρ μακρῷ | γήρᾳ ξυνᾴδει</quote> </cit> : <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Phil. 185" ref="Soph. Phil. 185"> <title>Ph.</title> 185</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔν τ’ ὀδύναις ...λιμῷ τ’ οἰκτρός</quote> </cit>. Only here at the end of a verse: cp. on <ref target="cl265">265</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τό γε | σῶμ’</quote> . </gloss>

data.quote: ἔν τε γὰρ μακρῷ | γήρᾳ ξυνᾴδει

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δύνασθαι_496"> ( <emph>without</emph> <quote xml:lang="grc">σώματι</quote> ), of bodily strength: cp. the speech of Lysias <quote xml:lang="grc">Ὑπὲρ τοῦ ἀδυνάτου</quote> ( <quote xml:lang="eng">"For the Invalid"</quote> ), <cit> <bibl n="Lys. 24.13" ref="Lys. 24.13">or. 24 § 13</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ γὰρ δήπου τὸν αὐτὸν ὑμεῖς μὲν ὡς δυνάμενον</quote> </cit>(as being able-bodied) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀφαιρήσεσθε τὸ διδόμενον, οἱ δὲ ὡς ἀδύνατον ὄντα κληροῦσθαι κωλύσουσιν</quote> : so <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> § 12 <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς εἰμὶ τῶν δυναμένων.</quote> <foreign xml:lang="grc"> μηδ’</foreign> for <quote xml:lang="grc">μήθ’</quote> is a necessary correction here. Cp. 421. </gloss>

data.quote:
οὐ γὰρ δήπου τὸν αὐτὸν ὑμεῖς μὲν ὡς
									δυνάμενον

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀρκεῖν_παρῇ_498">. The thought is: <quote xml:lang="eng"> "I have trespassed on the grove of the Eumenides, and it might be doubted whether such deities would accept the atonement from any hand but my own. Nay, I believe that they regard the <emph>intention</emph> rather than the outward details. If my deputy approaches the shrine <emph>in a loyal spirit</emph> , the offering will be accepted—yes, would be accepted, not on behalf of one man alone, but of many."</quote> <ref n="Clem. Al. Strom. 5.258">Clemens Alex. <title>Strom.</title> 5. 258</ref>, after quoting <ref n="Isaiah 1.16">Isaiah i. 16</ref> , <quote xml:lang="eng">"Wash you, make you clean,"</quote> cites verses ascribed (though wrongly) to Menander, among which are, <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="trimeter"> <l>θεῷ δὲ θῦε διὰ τέλους δίκαιος ὤν,</l> <l>μὴ λαμπρὸς ὢν ταῖς χλαμύσιν ὡς τῇ καρδίᾳ</l> </lg> </quote>. Porphyry <title>De Abstin.</title> 2. 19 quotes an inscription from a temple at Epidaurus, <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="elegiac"> <l>ἁγνὸν χρὴ νηοῖο θυωδέος ἐντὸς ἰόντα</l> <l>ἔμμεναι· ἁγνείη δ’ ἐστὶ φρονεῖν ὅσια</l> </lg> </quote>. Cp. the frequent sentiment that the poor man's offering, if pious, is welcome ( <ref n="Eur. Fr. 940">Eur. fr. 940</ref> , <ref n="Hor. Carm. 3.23.17"> Hor. <title>Carm.</title> 3. 23. 17</ref>, etc.). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀλλ_τι_500">. Bornemann's <foreign xml:lang="grc">τοι</foreign> for the MS. <foreign xml:lang="grc">τι</foreign> has been adopted by some of the best critics; but it seems scarcely appropriate here. For <quote xml:lang="grc">τι</quote> cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Ant. 1334" ref="Soph. Ant. 1334"> <title>Ant.</title> 1334</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="trimeter"> <l>μέλλοντα ταῦτα· τῶν προκειμένων τι χρὴ | πράσσειν</l> </lg> </quote> </cit> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δίχα_502">. With <foreign xml:lang="grc">γ’ ἄνευ</foreign> the <foreign xml:lang="grc">γ’</foreign> is intolerable, and L's <foreign xml:lang="grc">δ’ ἄνευ</foreign> points to a confusion between an original <quote xml:lang="grc">δίχα</quote> and a gloss <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄνευ</quote> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τελοῦσα_503">, in its ceremonial sense: cp. <title>O.T.</title> 1448 <quote xml:lang="grc">ὀρθῶς τῶν γε σῶν τελεῖς ὕπερ</quote> (perform the funeral rites). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸν_τόπον_503"> : <quote xml:lang="grc">βούλομαι δὲ μαθεῖν τοῦτο— ἵνα χρήσει με ἐφευρεῖν τὸν τόπον</quote> . The position of the <quote xml:lang="grc">κρήνη</quote> (470) had not been indicated. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="χρῆσται_504">by crasis from <quote xml:lang="grc">χρὴ ἔσται, χρή</quote> being a subst., <quote xml:lang="eng">"need."</quote> This is the view of H. L. Ahrens. If it can hardly be considered certain, it is at least highly probable; and therefore I do not now place in the text (as in the 1st ed.) my conjecture <foreign xml:lang="grc">χρήσει</foreign> , a fut. of <quote xml:lang="grc">χρή</quote> which occurs in <cit> <bibl n="Hdt. 7.8" ref="Hdt. 7.8">Her. 7.8</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">πάντα τινὰ ὑμέων χρήσει παρεῖναι</quote> </cit>, and <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Laws 809B" ref="Plat. Laws 809B"> Plat. <title>Legg.</title> 809B</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ποῖα καὶ τίνα μεταχειρίζεσθαι χρήσοι τρόπον</quote> </cit>( <foreign xml:lang="lat">v.l.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">χρήσει</quote> ). See Appendix.—</gloss>

data.quote:
ποῖα καὶ τίνα μεταχειρίζεσθαι χρήσοι
									τρόπον

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοῦτο_504">, resuming the object ( <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸν τόπον</quote> ), with emphasis: <ref n="Soph. Trach. 457"> <title>Tr.</title> 457</ref>n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄλσους_505">, gen. after <foreign xml:lang="grc">τὸ ἐκεῖθεν</foreign> , as after <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ</quote> (or <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰ</quote> ) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπ’ ἐκεῖνα, τὰ ἐπὶ θάτερα, τὰ πρὸς βορρᾶν</quote> , etc. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔποικος_506">, here, "one who dwells close to" the grove, — hardly, <emph>on</emph> the <cit> <quote xml:lang="grc">χῶρος οὐκ οἰκητός</quote> <bibl n="Soph. OC 39" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg007:39">(39)</bibl> </cit>; though the guardians of sacred <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἄλση</foreign> sometimes dwelt within them, as Maron in Apollo's grove ( <ref n="Hom. Od. 9.200"> <title>Od.</title> 9.200</ref>), and the priest in Athena Kranaa's grove at Elatea ( <ref n="Paus. 10.34.7">Paus. 10.34.7</ref> ). Elsewhere <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔποικος</quote> usu. = "immigrant": so <ref n="Soph. El. 189"> <title>El.</title> 189</ref>(as = "alien"). In <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. PB 410" ref="Aesch. PB 410"> Aesch. <title>PV</title> 410</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔποικον … Ἀσίας ἕδος</quote> </cit>means the Greek settlers in Asia. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Ἀντιγόνη_δ_507"> : <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 150" ref="Soph. El. 150"> <title>El.</title> 150</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">Νιόβα, σὲ δ’ ἔγωγε νέμω θεόν</quote> </cit>. Cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 1459">1459</ref> . </gloss>

data.quote: Νιόβα, σὲ δ’ ἔγωγε νέμω θεόν

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐδ_509"> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ πονεῖ τις, δεῖ</quote>= <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ καὶ π. τ., οὐ δεῖ</quote> . When A and nearly all other MSS. have <foreign xml:lang="grc">πονεῖ</foreign> , L's <foreign xml:lang="grc">πονῆ</foreign> (sic) surely does not warrant <quote xml:lang="grc">πονῇ</quote> . Cp. on <ref target="cl1443">1443</ref> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κείμενον_ἐπεγείρειν_510"> . <cit> <bibl n="Eur. El. 41" ref="Eur. El. 41"> Eur. <title>El.</title> 41</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="lyric"> <l>εὕδοντ’ ἂν ἐξήγειρε τὸν</l> <l>Ἀγαμέμνονος</l> <l>φόνον</l> </lg> </quote> </cit>, he would have aroused the slumbering memory of Agamemnon's murder. <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Phileb. 15C" ref="Plat. Phileb. 15C"> Plat. <title>Phileb.</title> 15C</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">μὴ κινεῖν κακὸν εὖ κείμενον</quote> </cit> ( <quote xml:lang="eng">"Let sleeping dogs lie"</quote> ).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τί_τοῦτο_513"> ; <quote xml:lang="eng">"What means this?"</quote> Cp. 46 <quote xml:lang="grc">τί δ’ ἐστὶ τοῦτο</quote> ; He is startled and disquieted. He shrinks from all cross-questioning on the past, as from a torture (cp. 210). We lose this dramatic touch if we construe <quote xml:lang="grc">τί τοῦτο</quote> ( <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔρασαι πυθέσθαι</quote> ) as a calm query,— <quote xml:lang="eng">"What is this that thou wouldst learn?"</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τᾶς_514">: for the gen. ( <quote xml:lang="eng">"concerning"</quote> ), cp. on <ref target="cl355">355</ref> .</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀπόρου_φανείσας_514">: because the horror of the discovery consisted in relationships which could not be changed: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 1184" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg004:1184"> <title>O. T.</title> 1184 f.</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="trimeter"> <l>φύς τ’ ἀφ’ ὧν οὐ χρῆν, ξὺν οἷς τ’</l> <l>οὐ χρῆν ὁμιλῶν, οὕς τέ μ’ οὐκ ἔδει κτανών</l> </lg> </quote> </cit> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ᾇ_ξυνέστας_515">, with which you were brought into conflict,—with which you became involved: <cit> <bibl n="Hdt. 9.89" ref="Hdt. 9.89">Her. 9.89</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">λιμῷ συστάντας καὶ καμάτῳ</quote> </cit> . <cit> <bibl n="Thuc. 4.55" ref="Thuc. 4.55">Thuc. 4.55</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ξυνεστῶτες...ναυτικῷ ἀγῶνι</quote> </cit> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τᾶς_πέπονθ_516">. The objection to pointing at <foreign xml:lang="grc">σᾶς</foreign> and understanding <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐστί</quote> with <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἀναιδῆ</foreign> (as Herm. proposed) is that <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἀνοίξῃς</foreign> requires an object. We should then have to understand <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλγηδόνα</quote> . The conject. <foreign xml:lang="grc">τᾶς σᾶς, πέπον, ἔργ’ ἀναιδῆ</foreign> has found undeserved favour. The address <quote xml:lang="grc">πέπον</quote> occurs about 18 times in Homer and the hymns, and always marks familiarity: there is a touch of household intimacy in it, as when Polyphemus says to his ram, <cit> <quote xml:lang="grc">κριὲ πέπον</quote> <bibl n="Hom. Od. 9.447" ref="Hom. Od. 9.447"> ( <title>Od.</title> 9.447)</bibl> </cit>. It is absurdly out of place here (cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 521">521</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὦ ξένοι</quote> , <ref n="Soph. OC 530">530</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὦ ξεῖν’</quote> ): <quote xml:lang="grc">ὦ φίλτατε</quote> , at <ref n="Soph. OC 465">465</ref> , is different.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔργ_516">was inserted in the MSS. to explain that <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἀναιδῆ</foreign> referred to his own acts. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸ_ὀρθόν_517">(predic.):</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πολὺ_517">, on 305.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μηδαμὰ_517">(neut. plur. adv.) with causal force, being such as does not cease.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λῆγον_517">, of rumour: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 731" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg004:731"> <title>O. T.</title> 731</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ηὐδᾶτο γὰρ ταῦτ’, οὐδέ πω λήξαντ’ ἔχει.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

data.quote:
ηὐδᾶτο γὰρ ταῦτ’, οὐδέ πω λήξαντ’
									ἔχει.

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄκουσμα_517">, anything heard,—sometimes (unlike <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀκρόαμα</quote> ) in a bad sense, <cit> <bibl n="Aristot. Pol. 7.1336b" ref="Aristot. Pol. 7.1336b"> Arist. <title>Pol.</title> 7.17</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπελαύνειν ἀπὸ τῶν ἀκουσμάτων καὶ τῶν ὁραμάτων ἀνελευθερίας</quote> </cit>. </gloss>

data.quote:
ἀπελαύνειν ἀπὸ τῶν ἀκουσμάτων καὶ τῶν
									ὁραμάτων ἀνελευθερίας

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="στέρξον_519">, be patient of my request, yield to it: cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 7">7</ref> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κἀγὼ_520">(for <quote xml:lang="grc">καί</quote> cp. 53) <quote xml:lang="grc">γὰρ</quote> ( <quote xml:lang="grc">πείθο‐</quote> <quote xml:lang="grc">μαι σοί</quote> ), for I comply with thee as to all that thou cravest (by allowing him to await the coming of Theseus, and by instructing him in the rites of the grove: cp. 465). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἤνεγκ_522">emphasises his ruling thought, his great plea— that he has been a <emph>sufferer</emph> , not a <emph>doer</emph> (267).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κακότατ_522">, the misery of his two involuntary crimes. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἤνεγκον ... ἤνεγκ’</quote> might possibly stand, but would be harsh. There is nothing to offend in <quote xml:lang="grc"> ἀέκων <emph>μὲν</emph> ... τούτων <emph>δέ</emph> </quote>, meaning— <quote xml:lang="eng"> "The <emph>agent</emph> was not free—the <emph>acts</emph> were not voluntary."</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θεὸς_ἴστω_522">must clearly have been preceded by the mention of some point to which he could appeal in an <emph>extenuation</emph> of his deed,—not by an admission, such as <quote xml:lang="grc">ἑκών</quote> expresses. (2) <quote xml:lang="grc"> ἤνεγκον <emph>ἑκών</emph> </quote>, in the supposed sense, is utterly at variance with the language and the whole tone of the play. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 239" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg007:239">239</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔργων | ἀκόντων</quote> </cit> : <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 964" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg007:964">964</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἤνεγκον ἄκων</quote> </cit> : <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 977" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg007:977">977</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">πῶς ἂν τό γ’ ἆκον πρᾶγμ’ ἂν εἰκότως ψέγοις;</quote> </cit>he asks, speaking of his own deeds.</gloss>

data.quote:
πῶς ἂν τό γ’ ἆκον πρᾶγμ’ ἂν εἰκότως
									ψέγοις;

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αὐθαίρετον_523">. Heinrich Schmidt keeps this reading ( <title>Compositionslehre</title> lxxx), which is not metrically irreconcileable with 512 <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅμως δ’ ἔραμαι πυθέσθαι</quote> (see Metrical Analysis). It is possible, indeed, that <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐθαίρετον</quote> is, as Hermann thought, corrupt: but no probable correction of it has been suggested. We cannot regard as such <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐθελητόν</quote> (not found), <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐθελημόν</quote> (used in masc. by Hes.), or <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐθέλημον</quote> (used in masc. by Plut.): <quote xml:lang="grc">ἑκὸν ἔργον</quote> : or <quote xml:lang="grc">πρόδηλον</quote> . Note, on the other hand, <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 1231" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg004:1231"> <title>O. T.</title> 1231</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐθαίρετοι</quote> </cit> ( <quote xml:lang="grc">πημοναί</quote> ).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κακᾷ_εὐνᾷ_525">, instr. dat., rather than dat. in appos. with <quote xml:lang="grc"> <emph>ἄτᾳ. γάμων</emph> ἄτα</quote>, ruin coming from a marriage, like <quote xml:lang="grc">δόκησις λόγων</quote> , suspicion resting on mere assertions, <ref n="Soph. OT 681"> <title>O. T.</title> 681</ref>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἦ_ἐπλήσω_527">; Didst thou fill thy bed with a mother, <foreign xml:lang="grc">δυσώ= νυμα</foreign> (prolept.) so as to make it infamous? (I should not take <quote xml:lang="grc">ματρόθεν</quote> with <quote xml:lang="grc"> δυσών</quote> . only, <quote xml:lang="eng">"infamous from a mother."</quote> )</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ματρόθεν_527">is substituted for <quote xml:lang="grc">ματρός</quote> by a kind of euphemism: that was the quarter from which the bride was taken. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. Seven 840" ref="Aesch. Seven 840"> Aesch. <title>Theb.</title> 840</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδ’ ἀπεῖπεν | πατρόθεν εὐκταία φάτις</quote> </cit>(the curse of Oed. on his children). The aor. midd. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπλησάμην</quote> is used by Hom., Her., etc., and (in comp. with <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν</quote> ) by Attic writers: it seems needless then to write <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔπλησο</quote> (from epic aor. <quote xml:lang="grc"> ἐπλήμην</quote> ) with Bergk. The notion of <quote xml:lang="eng"> " <emph> filling</emph> "</quote>is perh. tinged with that of <quote xml:lang="eng"> " <emph>defiling</emph> "</quote>( <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀναπιμπλάναι, ἀνάπλεως</quote> ). The tone of the passage is against rendering <quote xml:lang="eng">"satisfied,"</quote> as if <quote xml:lang="grc">λέκτρα</quote> = <quote xml:lang="grc">λέκτρων ἐπιθυμίαν</quote> . Nauck's <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἐπάσω</foreign> rests on Hesychius I. 1316 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπάσω· ἐκτήσω· Αἰσχύλος Πρωτεῖ σατυρικῷ</quote> . The aor. of <quote xml:lang="grc">πάομαι</quote> <quote xml:lang="eng">"to acquire"</quote> occurs elsewhere only in part. <quote xml:lang="grc">πασάμενος</quote> (Theogn., Theocr., etc.). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀκούειν_529">: cp. 141. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐξ_ἐμοῦ_530">, sprung from me: no partic. need be supplied, since the verb <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπέβλ</quote> . follows: cp. 250 <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅ τι σοι φίλον ἐκ σέθεν</quote> ( <foreign xml:lang="lat">sc.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐστί</quote> ). The cry with which the Chorus interrupts him ( <foreign xml:lang="grc">πῶς φής</foreign> ;) marks their perception (from his first words <quote xml:lang="grc">αὗται δέ</quote> etc.) that the children of <emph> that</emph> marriage were before them. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αὗται_παῖδε_530">: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Lach. 187A" ref="Plat. Lach. 187A"> Plat. <title>Laches</title> p. 187 A</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐτοὶ εὑρεταὶ γεγονότε</quote> </cit> . </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κοινᾶς_533">= which bore me also. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σαί_ἄῤ_534">. The Chorus have known all along that Oed. had married Iocasta, and also that he was the father of the girls (cp. 170, 322); but they are supposed to learn now for the first time that Iocasta was their mother. In the earlier versions of the Oedipus-myth (as in the <title>Odyssey</title> ) Iocasta bears no issue to Oed.; his children are borne by a second wife, Euryganeia. The Attic poets seem first to have changed this (see Introd. to <ref n="Soph. OT"> <title>O. T.</title> </ref>p. xv). The Chorus would say: <quote xml:lang="eng"> "Thine, then, they are by a double tie, at once as children and...as <emph>sisters?</emph> "</quote>but Oed. takes out of their mouths the second name which they shrink from uttering, and utters it himself with terrible emphasis. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κοιναί_534">, by the same mother: cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 261"> <title>O. T.</title> 261</ref>n.: so <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Ant. 1" ref="Soph. Ant. 1"> <title>Ant.</title> 1</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">κοινὸν αὐτάδελφον … κάρα</quote> </cit> . </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πατρὸς_534"> with <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀδελφεαί</quote> only. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἰώ_δῆτα_536">: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 842" ref="Soph. El. 842"> <title>El.</title> 842</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ΗΛ. φεῦ. ΧΟ. φεῦ δῆτ’.</quote> </cit> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γε_536">after <foreign xml:lang="grc">μυρίων</foreign> marks assent.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐπιστροφαί_536">refers to the revival of the pangs in his soul by this questioning. His troubles are likened to foemen who, when they seem to have been repulsed and to be vanishing in the distance, suddenly wheel about and renew their onset. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 1044" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg007:1044">1044</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">δαΐων | ἀνδρῶν ἐπιστροφαί</quote> </cit>. Philopoemen made his cavalry <quote xml:lang="grc">ὀξεῖς πρός τε τὰς κατ’ οὐλαμὸν ἐπιστροφὰς καὶ περισπασμοὺς</quote> (wheeling sharply in troops) <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ τὰς καθ’ ἵππον ἐπιστροφὰς καὶ κλίσεις</quote> (wheeling and changing direction singly), <ref> Plut. <title>Ph.</title> 7</ref>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄλαστ_ἔχειν_537">, unforgettable (dreadful) to endure: <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔχειν</quote> epexeg.: see on <ref target="cl230">231</ref> . Trag. borrowed the word from the epic <cit> <quote xml:lang="grc">πένθος ἄλαστον</quote> <bibl n="Hom. Il. 24.105" ref="Hom. Il. 24.105"> ( <title>Il.</title> 24.105)</bibl> </cit> , <cit> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄλαστον ὀδύρομαι</quote> <bibl n="Hom. Od. 14.174" ref="Hom. Od. 14.174"> ( <title>Od.</title> 14.174)</bibl> </cit>: so <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. Pers. 990" ref="Aesch. Pers. 990"> Aesch. <title>Pers.</title> 990</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">( κακὰ ) ἄλαστα στυγνὰ πρόκακα</quote> </cit>. Cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 1482">1482</ref> . Wecklein's <foreign xml:lang="grc">ὀχεῖν</foreign> ( <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Od. 7.211" ref="Hom. Od. 7.211"> <title>Od.</title> 7.211</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὀχέοντας ὀϊζύν</quote> </cit>) is perh. right: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. PB 143" ref="Aesch. PB 143"> Aesch. <title>P.V.</title> 143</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">φρουρὰν ἄζηλον ὀχήσω</quote> </cit> . </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐκ_ἔρεξα_538">: cp. 267, 521.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τί_γάρ_538">; <quote xml:lang="eng">"Why, what else?"</quote> if not <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔρεξα</quote> . Cp. 542. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δῶρον_540">. The <quote xml:lang="grc">τυραννίς</quote> was <quote xml:lang="grc">δωρητόν, οὐκ αἰτητόν</quote> ( <ref n="Soph. OT 384"> <title>O. T.</title> 384</ref>),—the reward pressed on him by Thebes for worsting the Sphinx; and with the throne he received the hand of Iocasta.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐξελέσθαι_540">, ironical as if the bride were a <quote xml:lang="grc">γέρας ἐξαίρετον</quote> . The <emph>act.</emph> aor. is used of the army choosing a prize (out of the booty) for a chief, <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Il. 16.56" ref="Hom. Il. 16.56"> <title>Il.</title> 16.56</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">κούρην ἢν ἄρα μοι γέρας ἔξελον υἷες Ἀχαιῶν</quote> </cit>: the <emph> midd.</emph> aor., of the victor choosing his own prize, as <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Trach. 244" ref="Soph. Trach. 244"> <title>Tr.</title> 244</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ταύτας | … ἐξείλεθ’ αὑτῷ κτῆμα</quote> </cit>. Here <quote xml:lang="grc">πόλεος ἐξελέσθαι</quote> is not <quote xml:lang="eng"> "to choose for myself <emph>out</emph> of the city,"</quote> but <quote xml:lang="eng"> "to receive as a choice gift <emph> from</emph> the city."</quote> </gloss>

data.quote:
κούρην ἢν ἄρα μοι γέρας ἔξελον υἷες
									Ἀχαιῶν

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τί_γάρ_542">; <quote xml:lang="eng">"how then?"</quote> —marking the transition from the topic of the marriage to that of the parricide. (Cp. <title>Quid vero?</title> ) Others refer the words merely to <foreign xml:lang="grc">δύστανε</foreign> , <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="eng">"for what else art thou?"</quote> This seems tame. Cp. 538. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δευτέραν_544"> , <foreign xml:lang="lat">sc.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc"> πληγήν</quote> : <cit> <bibl n="Hdt. 3.64" ref="Hdt. 3.64">Her. 3.64</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">καιρίῃ...τετύφθαι</quote> </cit> : <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Ant. 1307" ref="Soph. Ant. 1307"> <title>Ant.</title> 1307</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τί μ’ οὐκ ἀνταίαν | ἔπαισέν τις;</quote> </cit> <cit> <bibl n="Xen. Anab. 5.8.12" ref="Xen. Anab. 5.8.12">Xen. <title>Anab.</title> 5.8.12</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνέκραγον...ὡς ὀλίγας παίσειεν</quote> </cit> . <foreign xml:lang="grc">νόσον</foreign> , accus. in apposition: of mental anguish, as <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 1061" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg004:1061"> <title>O. T.</title> 1061</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἅλις νοσοῦσ’ ἐγώ</quote> </cit> . </gloss>

data.quote:
τί μ’ οὐκ ἀνταίαν | ἔπαισέν
									τις;

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔχει_τι_545">: but (the deed) has for me (dat. of interest) something from the quarter of justice; <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> it has a quality which tends to place it on the side of justice,—to rank it among justifiable deeds. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 1014" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg004:1014"> <title>O. T.</title> 1014</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">πρὸς δίκης οὐδὲν τρέμων</quote> </cit>(n.). The subj. to <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἔχει</foreign> is <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ ἔργον</quote> , easily supplied from <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἔκανον</foreign> .— This is better than to take <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἔχει</foreign> as impers. with <quote xml:lang="grc">πρὸς δίκας</quote> as= <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐνδίκως, τι</quote> being then adv.: <quote xml:lang="eng">"my case is in some sort just."</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τί_γάρ_545">; <foreign xml:lang="lat">sc.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔχει</quote> : <quote xml:lang="eng">"why, what justification has it?"</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="νόμῳ_καθαρός_548">, because he had been first struck by Laïus, and was acting in self-defence. <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Laws 869C" ref="Plat. Laws 869C"> Plat. <title>Legg.</title> 869C</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀδελφὸς δ’ ἐὰν ἀδελφὸν κτείνῃ ἐν στάσησι</quote> </cit> <quote xml:lang="grc">μάχης γενομένης ἤ τινι τρόπῳ τοιούτῳ, ἀμυνόμενος ἄρχοντα χειρῶν πρότερον, καθάπερ πολέμιον ἀποκτείνας ἔστω καθαρός· καὶ ἐὰν πολίτης πολίτην ὡσαύτως, ἢ ξένος ξένον</quote>. Rhadamanthus himself was cited as the author of this rule ( <ref n="Apollod. 2.4.9">Apollod. 2. 4. 9</ref> ).</gloss>

data.quote:
ἀδελφὸς δ’ ἐὰν ἀδελφὸν κτείνῃ ἐν
									στάσησι

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐς_ἦλθον_548">, to this plight: cp. on <ref target="cl273">273</ref> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καὶ_μὴν_introduction">introducing the new person: cp. 1249: so <ref n="Soph. Ant. 526"> <title>Ant.</title> 526</ref>, 1180, 1257: <ref n="Soph. Aj. 1168"> <title>Ai.</title> 1168</ref>, 1223: <ref n="Soph. El. 78"> <title>El.</title> 78</ref>, 1422. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὀμφήν_550">, his message. Usu. of a divine or oracular voice (102), but see 1351: Pind. fr. 53 <quote xml:lang="grc">ὀμφαὶ μελέων σὺν αὐλοῖς</quote> : <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Med. 174" ref="Eur. Med. 174"> Eur. <title>Med.</title> 174</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">μύθων τ’ αὐδαθέντων | … ὀμφάν</quote> </cit>(the words of the Chorus).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐφ_ἁστάλη_550"> = <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπὶ</quote> ( <quote xml:lang="grc"> ταῦτα</quote> ) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐφ’ ἃ ἐστάλη</quote> (cp. 274); <quote xml:lang="eng">"that by a small service he might win a great gain"</quote> (72). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔν_τε_551">, answered by <foreign xml:lang="grc">τανῦν θ’</foreign> . The simplest statement would have been <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔγνωκά σε, ἀκούων ἔν τε τῷ πάρος χρόνῳ τανῦν τε</quote> . Then, by repetition of the partic., we get <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔγνωκα, ἀκούων τε ἐν τ. π. χρ., ἀκούων τε τανῦν</quote> . And then, by insertion of a new verb, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔγνωκά τέ σε, ἀκούων ἐν τ. π. χρ., ἐξεπίσταμαί τε ἀκούων τανῦν</quote> . Cp. the insertion of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἡγεῖται</quote> in 351, and n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔγνωκά_ς_553"> , <quote xml:lang="eng">"I have recognised thee"</quote> —explaining how he is able to greet him by name: not merely, <quote xml:lang="eng">"I recognise thee."</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔγνωκα_553">is used (1) with a distinctly perfect sense: <cit> <bibl n="Lys. 17.6" ref="Lys. 17.6">Lys. or. 17 § 6</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ταῦτα...πρότερον ἐγνώκατε ἡμέτερα εἶναι</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Dem. 3.10" ref="Dem. 3.10">Dem. or. 3 § 10</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅτι...δεῖ βοηθεῖν...πάντες ἐγνώκαμεν</quote> </cit>. (2) More like a present, yet always with a certain emphasis, <quote xml:lang="eng"> " <title>I have come to know</title> "</quote> <emph>:</emph> <cit> <bibl n="Aristoph. Kn. 871" ref="Aristoph. Kn. 871">Aristoph. <title>Eq.</title> 871</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔγνωκας οὖν δῆτ’ αὐτὸν οἷός ἐστιν</quote> </cit>; <quote xml:lang="eng">"have you found out what sort of man he is?"</quote> <cit> <bibl n="Hdt. 1.207" ref="Hdt. 1.207">Her. 1.207</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ δ’ ἔγνωκας ὅτι ἄνθρωπος εἶς</quote> </cit>(if you have realised that you are a mortal): <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Rep. 366C" ref="Plat. Rep. 366C"> Plat. <title>Rep.</title> 366C</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἱκανῶς...ἔγνωκεν ὅτι ἄριστον δικαιοσύνη</quote> </cit>(he has thoroughly apprehended). So <ref n="Pind. P. 4"> Pind. <title>P.</title> 4. 287</ref>, <ref n="Aesch. PB 51"> Aesch. <title>PV</title> 51</ref> , <foreign xml:lang="grc">ὁδοῖς</foreign> , the coming of Theseus from Athens (Campb. understands the coming of Oed. to Attica): the plur. of one journey, as <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Ant. 226" ref="Soph. Ant. 226"> <title>Ant.</title> 226</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁδοῖς κυκλῶν ἐμαυτὸν εἰς ἀναστροφήν</quote> </cit>, and so <ref n="Soph. El. 68"> <title>El.</title> 68</ref>: otherwise below, 1397. </gloss>

data.quote:
ἔγνωκας οὖν δῆτ’ αὐτὸν οἷός
									ἐστιν
εἰ δ’ ἔγνωκας ὅτι ἄνθρωπος εἶς

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀκούων_554">, after the same word in 551, is awkward. The <foreign xml:lang="grc">γάρ</foreign> in 555 might also suggest that the partic. here referred to the evidence of his own eyes, not to further hearsay by the way. <quote xml:lang="grc">λεύσσων</quote> is intrinsically the best substitute that has been proposed: but it has no palaeographic probability. I had thought of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἱκάνων</quote> (cp. 576). Doubtless it is possible that <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀκούων</quote> was not a corruption of a similar form, but merely an inadvertent repetition from 551.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σκευή_555">: cp. 1597 <quote xml:lang="grc">εἶτ’ ἔλυσε δυσπινεῖς στολάς</quote> . The misery of his aspect impresses Creon (747), as it had impressed the Chorus (150). His <emph>garb</emph> , then, can hardly have announced a prince, though it may have indicated a Theban. Probably the reference is simply to the tale of long and destitute wanderings which his wretched apparel told (cp. 3 ff.).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δύστηνον_555">, as showing how he had blinded himself: cp. 286. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὄνθ_εἶ_556"> , <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc"> Οἰδίπους</quote> . Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 1036" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg004:1036"> <title>O. T.</title> 1036</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὠνομάσθης...ὂς εἶ</quote> </cit> (Oedipus). <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Od. 24.159" ref="Hom. Od. 24.159"> <title>Od.</title> 24.159</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδέ τις ἡμείων δύνατο γνῶναι τὸν ἐόντα</quote> </cit>, <quote xml:lang="eng">"and not one of us could tell that he was the man"</quote> (Odysseus). </gloss>

data.quote:
οὐδέ τις ἡμείων δύνατο γνῶναι τὸν
									ἐόντα
