content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="περέσθαι_557">aor. (used by Thuc., Plat., etc.); the fut. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπερήσομαι</quote> was also Attic; but the Attic pres. was <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπερωτάω, ἐπείρομαι</quote> being only Ionic. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐπέστης_558">, hast presented thyself. <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Sym. 212D" ref="Plat. Sym. 212D"> Plat. <title>Symp.</title> 212D</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπιστῆναι ἐπὶ τὰς θύρας</quote> </cit>. Esp. of a sudden and unlooked-for appearance before a place (as in war): <cit> <bibl n="Isoc. 9.58" ref="Isoc. 9.58">Isocr. or. 9 § 58</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">μικροῦ δεῖν ἔλαθεν αὐτὸν ἐπὶ τὸ βασίλειον ἐπιστάς.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

data.quote:
μικροῦ δεῖν ἔλαθεν αὐτὸν ἐπὶ τὸ
									βασίλειον ἐπιστάς.

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πόλεως_τ_558">, obj. gen.: <quote xml:lang="grc">τί προστρέπων πόλιν ἐμέ τε</quote> (cp. on <ref target="cl49">49</ref> ). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἂν_ἐξαφισταίμην_560">: "strange would be the fortune which (=I cannot imagine <emph>what</emph> fortune) you could succeed in mentioning, from which I would hold aloof": another way of saying <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐκ ἂν τύχοις λ. τοιαύτην, ὁποίας ἐξαφισταίμην</quote> . When the optat. with <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄν</quote> stands in the antecedent clause (as <quote xml:lang="grc">ἂν τύχοις</quote> here), the optative <emph>without</emph> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄν</quote> stands in the relative clause: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Il. 13.343" ref="Hom. Il. 13.343"> <title>Il.</title> 13.343</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="hexameter"> <l>μάλα κεν θρασυκάρδιος εἴη,</l> <l>ὃς τότε γηθήσειεν</l> </lg> </quote> </cit>: he <emph>would be</emph> right bold of heart, <emph>who should</emph> then rejoice. <cit> <bibl n="Aristoph. Cl. 1250" ref="Aristoph. Cl. 1250"> Aristoph. <title>Nub.</title> 1250</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="trimeter"> <l>οὐκ ἂν ἀποδοίην οὐδ’ ἂν ὀβολὸν οὐδενί,</l> <l>ὅστις καλέσειε κάρδοπον τὴν καρδόπην</l> </lg> </quote> </cit>. Such a relative clause is equivalent to a protasis with <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ</quote> and optat.: as here to <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ αὐτῆς ἀφισταίμην</quote> . Cp. Goodwin, <title>Moods and Tenses</title> , § 62. 4. Carefully distinguish the opt. <quote xml:lang="grc">ὃν...ψέξαιμι</quote> at <ref n="Soph. OC 1172">1172</ref> , where <ref target="cl1172">see n.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πρᾶξιν_560">, "fortune," not "action." The sing. <foreign xml:lang="grc">πρᾶξις</foreign> in Soph. usu. means "fortune," <ref n="Soph. Aj. 790"> <title>Ai.</title> 790</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Trach. 152"> <title>Tr.</title> 152</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Trach. 294">294</ref> : while the sense of "action" usu. belongs to the plur. <foreign xml:lang="grc">πράξεις</foreign> , as below, <ref n="Soph. OC 958"> 958</ref> , <ref n="Soph. OT 895"> <title>O. T.</title> 895</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Ant. 435"> <title>Ant.</title> 435</ref>. There is only one Sophoclean exception each way: in <ref n="Soph. Trach. 879"> <title>Tr.</title> 879</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πρᾶξις</quote>= <quote xml:lang="eng">"mode of doing,"</quote> and in <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Ant. 1305" ref="Soph. Ant. 1305"> <title>Ant.</title> 1305</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">πράξεις</quote> </cit> = <quote xml:lang="eng">"fortunes."</quote> Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. PB 695" ref="Aesch. PB 695"> Aesch. <title>PV</title> 695</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">πέφρικ’ ἐσιδοῦσα πρᾶξιν Ἰοῦς</quote> </cit> . <cit> <bibl n="Hdt. 3.65" ref="Hdt. 3.65">Her. 3.65</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπέκλαιε τὴν ἑωυτοῦ πρῆξιν</quote> </cit> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ξένος_562">. Aethra, the mother of Theseus, was daughter of Pittheus, king of Troezen, where Theseus was brought up, in ignorance that his father was Aegeus, king of Athens. On arriving at manhood, he received from his mother the tokens of his birth ( <quote xml:lang="grc">σύμβολα, γνωρίσματα</quote> ) —the sword and sandals left at Troezen by Aegeus—and set out for Attica. There he slew the sons of his uncle Pallas, who were plotting against his father, and was acknowledged by Aegeus as his heir ( <ref n="Plut. Thes. 4"> Plut. <title>Thes</title> . 4-13</ref>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καὶ_ἀνήρ_563">. With <quote xml:lang="grc">πλεῖστος</quote> the strengthening <quote xml:lang="grc">εἷς</quote> or <quote xml:lang="grc">εἷς ἀνήρ</quote> is esp. freq.: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Trach. 460" ref="Soph. Trach. 460"> <title>Tr.</title> 460</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">πλείστας ἀνὴρ εἷς</quote> </cit> : <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Her. 8" ref="Eur. Her. 8"> Eur. <title>Her.</title> 8</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">πλείστων μετέσχον εἷς ἀνήρ</quote> </cit> : <cit> <bibl n="Xen. Cyrop. 8.2.15" ref="Xen. Cyrop. 8.2.15"> Xen. <title>Cyr.</title> 8.2.15</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">θησαυροὺς...πλείστους ἑνὶ ἀνδρί</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Thuc. 8.40" ref="Thuc. 8.40">Thuc. 8.40</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">μιᾷ γε πόλει...πλεῖστοι</quote> </cit>. Cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 1380"> <title>O. T.</title> 1380</ref>n. With the MS. <quote xml:lang="grc"> χὤς <emph>τις</emph> </quote> the <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς</quote> before <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπαιδεύθην</quote> must be repeated before <quote xml:lang="grc">ἤθλησα</quote> , the constr. being, <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ</quote> ( <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς</quote> ) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἤθλησα, ὡς ἀνήρ τις πλεῖστα ἤθλησε</quote> . This has been compared with <cit> <bibl n="Xen. Anab. 1.3.15" ref="Xen. Anab. 1.3.15"> Xen. <title>Anab.</title> 1.3.15</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄρχεσθαι ἐπίσταμαι ὥς τις καὶ ἄλλος μάλιστ’ ἀνθρώπων</quote> </cit>(where some would omit the last two words),—a passage not properly similar: and here <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἀνήρ</foreign> becomes very weak. Elmsley, adopting <foreign xml:lang="grc">χὤτι</foreign> , took <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνήρ</quote> as= <quote xml:lang="grc">εἷς ἀνήρ</quote> , quoting <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Rh. 500" ref="Eur. Rh. 500"> [Eur.] <title>Rhes.</title> 500</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ πλεῖστα χώραν τήνδ’ ἀνὴρ καθυβρίσας</quote> </cit>(where, for <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ</quote> , read <quote xml:lang="grc">εἷς</quote> with Hermann): and <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Hec. 310" ref="Eur. Hec. 310"> Eur. <title>Hec.</title> 310</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">θανὼν ὑπὲρ γῆς Ἑλλάδος κάλλιστ’ ἀνήρ</quote> </cit>, where we should perh. read <quote xml:lang="grc">κάλλιστ’ ἀνὴρ εἷς Ελλάδος θανὼν ὕπερ</quote> . </gloss>

data.quote:
ἄρχεσθαι ἐπίσταμαι ὥς τις καὶ ἄλλος
									μάλιστ’ ἀνθρώπων

καὶ πλεῖστα χώραν τήνδ’ ἀνὴρ
									καθυβρίσας

θανὼν ὑπὲρ γῆς Ἑλλάδος κάλλιστ’
									ἀνήρ

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐπὶ_ξένης_563"> : <ref n="Soph. OC 184">184</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κινδυνεύματ_564">(acc. of cognate notion), his encounters, on his way overland from Troezen to Attica, with various foes,—the robbers Periphetes, Sinis, Sciron, Procrustes,—the sow of Crommyon, —etc.; his slaying of the Minotaur in Crete;—his fighting on the side of the Lapithae against the Centaurs, etc. In all his <quote xml:lang="grc">αθλοι</quote> Theseus was depicted by the Attic legend as the champion of the oppressed,— <cit> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀδικήσων μὲν οὐδένα, τοὺς δὲ ὑπάρχοντας βίας ἀμυνούμενος</quote> <bibl n="Plut. Thes. 7" ref="Plut. Thes. 7"> (Plut. <title>Thes.</title> 7)</bibl> </cit> . </gloss>

data.quote:
ἀδικήσων μὲν οὐδένα, τοὺς δὲ
									ὑπάρχοντας βίας ἀμυνούμενος

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐν_κάρᾳ_564">, at the risk of my own life, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν</quote> denoting the stake: <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Cycl. 654" ref="Eur. Cycl. 654"> Eur. <title>Cycl.</title> 654</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν τῷ Καρὶ κινδυνεύσομεν</quote> </cit> : <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Lach. 187b" ref="Plat. Lach. 187b"> Plat. <title>Lach.</title> 187 B</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">μὴ οὐκ ἐν τῷ Καρὶ ὑμῖν ὁ κίνδυνος κινδυνεύηται, ἀλλ’ ἐν τοῖς υἱέσι</quote> </cit>. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Od. 2.237" ref="Hom. Od. 2.237"> <title>Od.</title> 2.237</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">παρθέμενοι κεφαλάς</quote> </cit>, at the risk of their lives (as <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Od. 3.74" ref="Hom. Od. 3.74">3. 74</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ψυχὰς παρθέμενοι</quote> </cit> ): <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Il. 9.322" ref="Hom. Il. 9.322"> <title>Il.</title> 9.322</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">αἰὲν ἐμὴν ψυχὴν παραβαλλόμενος πολεμίζειν</quote> </cit>. The irreg. dat. </gloss>

data.quote:
μὴ οὐκ ἐν τῷ Καρὶ ὑμῖν ὁ κίνδυνος
									κινδυνεύηται, ἀλλ’ ἐν τοῖς υἱέσι

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κάρᾳ_564"> from nom. <quote xml:lang="grc">κάρα</quote> again <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1272"> <title>Ant.</title> 1272</ref>, <ref n="Soph. El. 445"> <title>El.</title> 445</ref>( <foreign xml:lang="lat">v.l.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">κάρα</quote> ), <ref n="Soph. Fr. 141.2">fr. 141. 2</ref> : it occurs first in <ref n="Thgn. 1018">Theogn. 1018</ref> , the Homeric dat. being <quote xml:lang="grc">κάρητι</quote> or <quote xml:lang="grc">κρατί</quote> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ξένον_565"> with <foreign xml:lang="grc">ὑπεκτραποίμην</foreign> as well as <foreign xml:lang="grc">συνεκσῴζειν</foreign> : cp. <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Phaedo 108B" ref="Plat. Phaedo 108B"> Plat. <title>Phaedo</title> 108B</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὴν ἀκάθαρτον</quote> </cit> ( <quote xml:lang="grc">ψυχὴν</quote> )... <quote xml:lang="grc">ἅπας φεύγει τε καὶ ὑπεκτρέπεται</quote> . The notion is that of retiring ( <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑπο‐</quote> ) out of the path to avoid meeting a person. Soph. has the act. with gen., <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Trach. 549" ref="Soph. Trach. 549"> <title>Tr.</title> 549</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τῶν δ’ ὑπεκτρέπει πόδα.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="συνεκσῴζειν_565">, to help in extricating: <cit> <bibl n="Antiph. 5.93" ref="Antiph. 5.93">Antiph. or. 5 § 93</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ σῶμα ἀπειρηκὸς ἡ ψυχὴ συνεξέσωσεν</quote> </cit>. </gloss>

data.quote:
τὸ σῶμα ἀπειρηκὸς ἡ ψυχὴ
									συνεξέσωσεν

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀνὴρ_567">= <quote xml:lang="grc">θνητός</quote> : <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Ant. 768" ref="Soph. Ant. 768"> <title>Ant.</title> 768</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">φρονείτω μεῖζον ἢ κατ’ ἄνδρ’ ἰών</quote> </cit>. Cp. 393. </gloss>

data.quote:
φρονείτω μεῖζον ἢ κατ’ ἄνδρ’
									ἰών

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δοῦ_568">= <quote xml:lang="grc">ἢ σοί</quote> : <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Ant. 74" ref="Soph. Ant. 74"> <title>Ant.</title> 74</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">πλείων χρόνος | ὃν δεῖ μ’ ἀρέσκειν τοῖς ἐκεῖ τῶν ἐνθάδε</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Thuc. 1.85" ref="Thuc. 1.85">Thuc. 1.85</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔξεστι δ’ ἡμῖν μᾶλλον ἑτέρων</quote> </cit> . </gloss>

data.quote:
πλείων χρόνος | ὃν δεῖ μ’ ἀρέσκειν
									τοῖς ἐκεῖ τῶν ἐνθάδε

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸ_γενναῖον_569">: shown in sparing Oed. the painful task of introducing himself and telling his story. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="παρῆκεν_570">(aor. of <quote xml:lang="grc">παρίημι</quote> ) closely with <quote xml:lang="grc">ὥστε … δεῖσθαι</quote> : "has graciously <emph> permitted</emph> that there should be," etc. Cp. 591: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 1482" ref="Soph. El. 1482"> <title>El.</title> 1482</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλά μοι πάρες | κἂν σμικρὸν εἰπεῖν</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Ant. 1043" ref="Soph. Ant. 1043"> <title>Ant.</title> 1043</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">( οὐδ’ ὣς ) θάπτειν παρήσω κεῖνον</quote> </cit>. ( <emph>Not</emph> , "has so passed the matter on,""so left it.") For <foreign xml:lang="grc">ὥστε</foreign> cp. <cit> <bibl n="Hdt. 6.5" ref="Hdt. 6.5">Her. 6.5</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ γὰρ ἔπειθε τοὺς Χίους ὥστε ἑωυτῷ δοῦναι νέας</quote> </cit>: and see on <ref target="cl970">970</ref> . </gloss>

data.quote:
ἀλλά μοι πάρες | κἂν σμικρὸν
									εἰπεῖν

οὐ γὰρ ἔπειθε τοὺς Χίους ὥστε ἑωυτῷ
									δοῦναι νέας

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὥστε_δεῖσθαι_570">, so that there is need for me, <foreign xml:lang="grc">βραχέα φράσαι</foreign> , to say but little. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δεῖσθαι_570">midd., impersonal,= <quote xml:lang="grc">δεῖν</quote> . (It could not be <emph> pass.</emph> , with <quote xml:lang="grc">βραχέα</quote> for subject.) Bekker <title>Anecd.</title> p. 88. 21 <quote xml:lang="grc">δεῖται· ἀντὶ τοῦ δεῖ· ἀπελθεῖν με δεῖται</quote> . The only example (so far as I know), besides our passage, is <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Meno 79C" ref="Plat. Meno 79C"> Plat. <title>Meno.</title> 79C</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">δεῖται οὖν σοι πάλιν...τῆς αὐτῆς ἐρωτήσεως</quote> </cit>. And presently: <quote xml:lang="grc">ἢ οὐ δοκεῖ σοι πάλιν δεῖσθαι τῆς αὐτῆς ἐρωτήσεως</quote> ; In the former place, while the best MSS. have <quote xml:lang="grc">δεῖται</quote> , some have <quote xml:lang="grc">δεῖ</quote> . (I do not add <quote xml:lang="grc">δεήσεσθαι</quote> , <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> E, since that may be personal.) If, however, the text can be trusted, these are clear instances, for it would be very forced to supply <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁ λόγος</quote> . In <cit> <bibl n="Hdt. 4.11" ref="Hdt. 4.11">Her. 4.11</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">δεόμενον</quote> </cit>(as if= <quote xml:lang="grc">δέον</quote> ) is plainly corrupt: Buttmann's <quote xml:lang="grc">δέοι μένοντας</quote> may be right. If we altered <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἐμοὶ</foreign> to <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἐμοῦ</foreign> , the subject to <quote xml:lang="grc">δεῖσθαι</quote> would be <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ σὸν γενναῖον</quote> . But then <quote xml:lang="grc">δεῖσθαι</quote> would mean <quote xml:lang="eng"> "requests,"</quote> rather than <quote xml:lang="eng">"requires,"</quote> of me. </gloss>

data.quote:
δεῖται οὖν σοι πάλιν...τῆς αὐτῆς
									ἐρωτήσεως

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="χὡ_διοίχεται_574">, and the statement is at an end. <foreign xml:lang="grc">ὁ λόγος</foreign> is the explanation due from Oedipus after sending for Theseus. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Supp. 528" ref="Eur. Supp. 528"> Eur. <title>Suppl.</title> 528</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="trimeter"> <l> <note anchored="true" place="inline" resp="RCJ" xml:lang="eng">(theseus to the thebans)</note>εἰ γάρ τι καὶ πεπόνθατ’ Ἀργείων ὕπο,</l> <l>τεθνᾶσιν, ἠμύνασθε πολεμίους καλῶς,</l> <l>αἰσχρῶς δ’ ἐκείνοις, χἡ δίκη διοίχεται</l> </lg> </quote> </cit> : <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> , if you have been wronged, you have had satisfaction, <quote xml:lang="eng"> "and <emph>the cause is closed.</emph> "</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="διέρχεται_574">(L) is certainly corrupt. It ought to mean, <quote xml:lang="eng">"the discussion is being carried through,"</quote> rather than, <quote xml:lang="eng">"our conference draws to an end"</quote> (as Campbell, comparing <quote xml:lang="grc">διεξελήλυθα πάντα, διεξῆλθον διὰ μακροῦ λόγου</quote> , which are not similar). And if <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁ λόγος</quote> means <quote xml:lang="eng">"our comerence,"</quote> then Oedipus is assuming that his petition has only to be stated in order to be granted. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοῦτ_αὐτὸ_575">marks eagerness: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 545" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg004:545"> <title>O. T.</title> 545</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ΟΙ. ...βαρύν σ’ εὕρηκ’ ἐμοί. ΚΡ. τοῦτ αὐτὸ νῦν μου πρῶτ’ ἄκουσον ὡς ἐρῶ</quote> </cit> . </gloss>

data.quote:
ΟΙ. ...βαρύν σ’ εὕρηκ’ ἐμοί. ΚΡ. τοῦτ
									αὐτὸ νῦν μου πρῶτ’ ἄκουσον ὡς ἐρῶ

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὰ_κέρδη_577">: cp. 265. Doederlein understands, <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰ δὲ κέρδη μᾶλλον ἀγαθά ἐστιν ἢ καλή ἐστιν ἡ μορφή</quote> . Schneidewin and Wecklein adopt this forced explanation, which is condemned both by <quote xml:lang="grc">παρ’ αὐτοῦ</quote> and by the absence of the art. with <quote xml:lang="grc">μορφή</quote> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="που_580">, <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> so far as Oed. can conjecture the purpose of Apollo. He could not be sure that the close of his life would <emph> immediately</emph> follow on his arrival at the grove. The promised sign of the end had not yet been given (94). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ποίῳ_581">, <foreign xml:lang="lat">sc.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">χρόνῳ</quote> , asks with surprise for some further definition of the vague <quote xml:lang="grc">χρόνῳ μάθοις ἄν</quote> . Theseus naturally assumes that the blessings are to come in the lifetime of Oedipus. And if not now, he asks, then in what contingency? The answer startles him.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="προσφορὰ_581">, offering, present. <ref n="Thphr. Char. 30"> Theophrast. <title>Char.</title> XXX</ref>(=XXVI in my 1st ed.) it is like the <quote xml:lang="grc">αἰσχροκερδής, γαμοῦντός τινος τῶν φίλων καὶ ἐκδιδομένου θυγατέρα πρὸ χρόνου τινὸς ἀποδημῆσαι, ἵνα μὴ πέμψῃ προσφοράν</quote> (a wedding-present). Cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 1270">1270.</ref> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δηλώσεται_581">, pass.: see <ref n="Soph. OT 672"> <title>O. T.</title> 672</ref>n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὰ_μέσῳ_583">is governed by <foreign xml:lang="grc">λῆστιν ἴσχεις</foreign> as= <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπιλανθάνει</quote> (see on <ref target="cl223">223</ref> ), no less than by <quote xml:lang="grc">ποεῖ</quote> . To make <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰ δ’ ἐν μ</quote> . an accus. of respect would suit the first verb, but not the second. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δῑ_ποεῖ_583">, a solitary instance of this phrase (instead of <quote xml:lang="grc"> οὐδενὸς</quote> or <quote xml:lang="grc">παρ’ οὐδὲν ποεῖσθαι</quote> ), perh. suggested by the use of the prep. in such phrases as <quote xml:lang="grc">διὰ φυλακῆς ἔχω τι</quote> , etc. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐνταῦθα_γάρ_585"> , <quote xml:lang="eng">"yes"</quote> for <emph>there</emph> ,— in <emph>that</emph> boon ( <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν τῷ θάπτεσθαι</quote> ),—those other things ( <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰ ἐν μέσῳ</quote> ) are brought together for me: <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> if you promise that I shall eventually be buried in Attica, you cannot meanwhile allow me to be forcibly removed to the Theban frontier. He is thinking of protection against Creon's imminent attempt (399). <quote xml:lang="grc">συγκομίζομαι</quote> , to collect or store up for oneself, was, like <quote xml:lang="grc">συγκομιδή</quote> , esp. said of harvesting ( <ref n="Xen. Anab. 6.6.37"> Xen. <title>Anab.</title> 6.6.37</ref>), and that notion perhaps tinges the word here. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐν_βραχεῖ_586">in sense= <quote xml:lang="grc">βραχεῖαν</quote> : <quote xml:lang="eng">"this grace which you ask of me lies in a small compass"</quote> ( <emph>not</emph> , <quote xml:lang="eng">"you ask me this favour in brief speech"</quote> ). The adverbial <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν βραχεῖ</quote> does not go with the verb, but is equiv. to a predicative adj. agreeing with <quote xml:lang="grc">χάριν</quote> . Such phrases imply the omission of the partic. <quote xml:lang="grc"> ὤν</quote> : so 29 <quote xml:lang="grc">πέλας γὰρ ἄνδρα τόνδ’ ὁρῶ</quote> : <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Phil. 26" ref="Soph. Phil. 26"> <title>Ph.</title> 26</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τοὔργον οὐ μακρὰν λέγεις</quote> </cit>(the task which you set is not distant): <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 899" ref="Soph. El. 899"> <title>El.</title> 899</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς δ’ ἐν γαλήνῃ πάντ’ ἐδερκόμην τόπον</quote> </cit>. For <quote xml:lang="grc">βραχεῖ</quote> cp. 293, <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Laws 641B" ref="Plat. Laws 641B"> Plat. <title>Legg.</title> 641B</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">βραχύ τι...ὄφελος</quote> </cit> . </gloss>

data.quote:
ὡς δ’ ἐν γαλήνῃ πάντ’ ἐδερκόμην
									τόπον

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γε_μήν_587">, however: <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. Ag. 1378" ref="Aesch. Ag. 1378"> Aesch. <title>Ag.</title> 1378</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἦλθε, σὺν χρόνῳ γε μήν</quote> </cit>. The only ground for <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἁγὼν</foreign> rather than <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἀγὼν</foreign> is the trace of <foreign xml:lang="grc">χ</foreign> from the first hand in L: neither reading is intrinsically better than the other. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 1491" ref="Soph. El. 1491"> <title>El.</title> 1491</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">λόγων γὰρ οὐ | νῦν ἐστιν ἁγών, ἀλλὰ σῆς ψυχῆς πέρι</quote> </cit>. The word <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἀγών</foreign> is so far ambiguous that it does not necessarily mean a physical <emph>contest</emph> , but can mean an <emph>issue</emph> or <emph>crisis</emph> (Lat. <foreign xml:lang="lat">discrimen, momentum</foreign> ). <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Rep. 608B" ref="Plat. Rep. 608B"> Plat. <title>Rep.</title> 608B</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">μέγας...ὁ ἀγὼν...τὸ χρηστὸν ἢ κακὸν γενέσθαι</quote> </cit>. </gloss>

data.quote:
λόγων γὰρ οὐ | νῦν ἐστιν ἁγών, ἀλλὰ
									σῆς ψυχῆς πέρι

μέγας...ὁ ἀγὼν...τὸ χρηστὸν ἢ κακὸν
									γενέσθαι

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πότερα_588">. Oed. has said, <quote xml:lang="eng">"If you pledge yourself to keep me in Attica, a serious issue will be raised."</quote> Theseus:— <quote xml:lang="eng">"Do you speak of the relations between your sons and me?"</quote> You mean that they will contest my right to retain you? </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐγγενῶν_588">(Hartung) may be right; but the MS. <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἐκγόνων</foreign> is not condemned by the evident fact that Theseus does not yet know of the <emph> quarrel</emph> between the father and the sons (599). It is enough if he knows the sons to exist: they would represent the claim of the <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐγγενεῖς.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κἀμοῦ_588">: the MS. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἢ )μοῦ</quote> is certainly wrong. Theseus does not ask— <quote xml:lang="eng">"Will the issue be serious for your kinsmen, or for me?"</quote> but, <quote xml:lang="eng">"In what quarter will the issue arise?"</quote> Cp. 606 <quote xml:lang="grc">τἀμὰ κἀκείνων</quote> . The conject. <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἢ τοῦ</foreign> (= <quote xml:lang="grc">τίνος</quote> ) is tame: and <foreign xml:lang="grc">κἀμοῦ</foreign> has the advantage in clearness, by indicating the second party to the <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀγών</quote> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐδὲ_σοὶ_590">: while <emph>they</emph> , on their part, call you home, for <emph>you</emph> , on yours, exile is not desirable,—if, indeed, their offer is agreeable to you ( <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> if you have no repugnance to Thebes).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐδέ_590">is here the negative counterpart of <foreign xml:lang="grc">δέ</foreign> in apodosis: <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> as we can say, <quote xml:lang="grc"> <emph>εἰ</emph> θέλοντά σε ἐκεῖνοι κατάγουσι, σοὶ <emph>δὲ</emph> κατελθεῖν καλόν</quote>, so also <quote xml:lang="grc"> <emph>οὐδὲ</emph> σοὶ φεύγειν καλόν</quote>. The same resoluble quality of <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδέ</quote> is seen in its use for <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλ’ οὐ</quote> ( <ref n="Hom. Il. 24.25"> <title>Il.</title> 24.25</ref>). Cp. on <ref target="cl591">591</ref> .</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φεύγειν_590">= <quote xml:lang="grc">φυγάδι εἶναι</quote> , rather than <quote xml:lang="eng">"to shun them."</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀλλ_οὐδ_591">presupposes his refusal, and justifies it: <quote xml:lang="eng"> "Nay, <emph> neither</emph> did they consider my wishes."</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="παρίεσαν_591"> , <quote xml:lang="eng">"concede,"</quote> <foreign xml:lang="lat">sc.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐμοὶ κατελθεῖν</quote> , cp. 570: not <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐμὲ εἰς τὴν πόλιν</quote> , <quote xml:lang="eng">"admit"</quote> (in which sense usu. of allowing armies to enter territory, or the like: <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Supp. 468" ref="Eur. Supp. 468"> Eur. <title>Suppl.</title> 468</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἄδραστον ἐς γῆν τήνδε μὴ παριέναι</quote> </cit>). </gloss>

data.quote:
Ἄδραστον ἐς γῆν τήνδε μὴ
									παριέναι

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θυμὸς_δ_592"> . <quote xml:lang="grc">δέ</quote> sometimes corrects or objects: <ref n="Soph. OT 379"> <title>O. T.</title> 379</ref>(n.) <quote xml:lang="grc">Κρέων δέ σοι πῆμ’ οὐδέν</quote> ( <quote xml:lang="eng"> " <title>Nay</title> "</quote> ).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ξύμφορον_592">: the neut. as often in maxims, when the masc. or fem. subj. is viewed in its most general aspect: <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Orest. 232" ref="Eur. Orest. 232"> Eur. <title>Or.</title> 232</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">δυσάρεστον οἱ νοσοῦντες</quote> </cit>: cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 542"> <title>O. T.</title> 542</ref>n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μάθῃς_μου_593">, heard (the matter) from me. Distinguish the gen. with <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκμάθω</quote> in 114, where see n. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 889" ref="Soph. El. 889"> <title>El.</title> 889</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="trimeter"> <l>ἄκουσον ὡς μαθοῦσά μου</l> <l>τὸ λοιπὸν ἢ φρονοῦσαν ἢ μώραν λέγῃς</l> </lg> </quote> </cit> . <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 545" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg004:545"> <title>O. T.</title> 545</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">μανθάνειν δ’ ἐγὼ κακὸς | σοῦ</quote> </cit> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ξυμφορὰν_596">euphemistic ( <ref n="Soph. OT 99"> <title>O. T.</title> 99</ref>n.): cp. 369 <quote xml:lang="grc">τὴν πάλαι γένους φθοράν</quote> . Here, as there, <quote xml:lang="grc"> γένους</quote> = <quote xml:lang="eng">"race,"</quote> not <quote xml:lang="eng">"birth."</quote> Theseus supposes Oed. to mean that the hereditary curse has fallen on him with especial weight.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐρεῖς_596">, <quote xml:lang="eng"> " <emph>will you</emph> mention,"</quote> <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="eng">"do you allude to"</quote>: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Phil. 439" ref="Soph. Phil. 439"> <title>Ph.</title> 439 ff. </bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ΦΙ. ἀναξίου μὲν φωτὸς ἐξερήσομαι... ΝΕ. ποίου γε τούτου πλὴν Ὀδυσσέως ἐρεῖς; ΦΙ. οὐ τοῦτον εἷπον</quote> </cit>. </gloss>

data.quote:
ΦΙ. ἀναξίου μὲν φωτὸς ἐξερήσομαι...
									ΝΕ. ποίου γε τούτου πλὴν Ὀδυσσέως ἐρεῖς; ΦΙ. οὐ τοῦτον
									εἷπον

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μεῖζον_ἄνθρ_598"> , <foreign xml:lang="lat">gravius quam pro mortali</foreign> : <cit> <bibl n="Xen. Mem. 4.4.24" ref="Xen. Mem. 4.4.24"> Xen. <title>Mem.</title> 4.4.24</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">βελτίονος ἢ κατ’ ἄνθρωπον νομοθέτου</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Thuc. 7.75" ref="Thuc. 7.75">Thuc. 7.75</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">μείζω ἢ κατὰ δάκρυα ἐπεπόνθεσαν</quote> </cit>. If the woe to which he alludes is something greater than the calamity of his house, then it must be superhuman. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="νοσεῖς_598">: see on <ref target="cl544">544</ref> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πεμψαίαθ_602">(cp. on <ref target="cl44">44</ref> ), summon to themselves: <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Hec. 977" ref="Eur. Hec. 977"> Eur. <title>Hec.</title> 977</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τί χρῆμ’ ἐπέμψω τὸν ἐμὸν ἐκ δόμων πόδα;</quote> </cit> </gloss>

data.quote:
τί χρῆμ’ ἐπέμψω τὸν ἐμὸν ἐκ δόμων
									πόδα;

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὥστ_δίχα_602"> , <emph>if it is understood</emph> that you cannot live with them in Thebes.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὥστε_602">introduces the condition: <cit> <bibl n="Thuc. 1.28" ref="Thuc. 1.28">Thuc. 1.28</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἑτοῖμοι δὲ εἷναι καὶ ὥστε ἀμφοτέρους μένειν κατὰ χώραν</quote> </cit>, the Corcyreans said that they were also ready (to make an armistice) <emph>under the condition</emph> that each party should remain where they were. </gloss>

data.quote:
ἑτοῖμοι δὲ εἷναι καὶ ὥστε ἀμφοτέρους
									μένειν κατὰ χώραν

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐξαναγκάσει_603">. There is no reason for changing fut. to pres. here. The oracle had been given (388), but its effect was to come. Cp. 1179. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὅτι_605">, as if <quote xml:lang="grc">ποῖον χρησμὸν ἀκούσαντας</quote> had preceded. Not with <quote xml:lang="grc">δείσαντας</quote> : verbs of fearing are sometimes followed by <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς</quote> or <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅπως</quote> with indic. (instead of <quote xml:lang="grc">μή</quote> with subj.), as in <ref n="Soph. El. 1309"> <title>El.</title> 1309</ref>; but by <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅτι</quote> only as= <quote xml:lang="eng">"because."</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τῇδε_χθονί_605">, locative dat., not instrum. (as schol. <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑπὸ ταύτης τῆς χθονός</quote> ). Oed. interprets Ismene's less explicit statement (411). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τἀμὰ_κἀκείνων_606"> = <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰ ἐμὰ καὶ</quote> ( <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰ</quote> ) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκείνων</quote> : cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 588">588</ref> : <ref n="Soph. Trach. 1068"> <title>Tr.</title> 1068</ref>: <cit> <bibl n="Eur. El. 301" ref="Eur. El. 301"> <title>Eur. El.</title> 301</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τύχας βαρείας τὰς ἐμὰς κἀμοῦ πατρός</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Phoen. 474" ref="Eur. Phoen. 474"> <title>Phoen.</title> 474</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τοὐμόν τε καὶ τοῦδ’</quote> </cit>. Poetry tolerated such omission of the second art. even when the subjects were sharply opposed: <ref n="Soph. El. 991"> <title>El.</title> 991</ref>: <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. Ag. 324" ref="Aesch. Ag. 324"> Aesch. <title>Ag.</title> 324</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ τῶν ἁλόντων καὶ κρατησάντων</quote> </cit>. Theseus cannot foresee any cause which should trouble the ancient amity between Athens and Thebes ( <ref n="Soph. OC 619">619</ref> , <ref n="Soph. OC 632">632</ref> ). </gloss>

data.quote:
τύχας βαρείας τὰς ἐμὰς κἀμοῦ
									πατρός

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γῆρας_κατθανεῖν_608">: for the inf. <emph>without art.</emph> co-ordinated with another noun cp. <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Il. 10.173" ref="Hom. Il. 10.173"> <title>Il.</title> 10.173</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="hexameter"> <l>ἐπὶ ξυροῦ ἵσταται ἀκμῆς</l> <l>ἢ μάλα λυγρὸς ὄλεθρος Ἀχαιοῖς ἠὲ βιῶναι</l> </lg> </quote> </cit> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="συγχεῖ_609">, confounds, ruins, effaces: <ref n="Soph. Trach. 1229"> <title>Tr.</title> 1229</ref>: <cit> <bibl n="Hdt. 7.136" ref="Hdt. 7.136">Her. 7.136</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">συγχέαι τὰ πάντων ἀνθρώπων νόμιμα</quote> </cit>: esp. fitting here, since applicable to breach of treaties, <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Il. 4.269" ref="Hom. Il. 4.269"> <title>Il.</title> 4.269</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">σύν γ’ ὅρκῑ ἔχευαν | Τρῶες</quote> </cit> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="παγκρατὴς_609">, epithet of sleep in <ref n="Soph. Aj. 675"> <title>Ai.</title> 675</ref>, and of fire in <ref n="Soph. Phil. 986"> <title>Ph.</title> 986</ref>. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Shak. son lxiii" ref="Shak. son lxiii"> Shaksp. <title>Sonnets</title> 63, 64</bibl> <quote xml:lang="eng">"With Time's injurious hand crush'd and o'erworn"</quote> </cit>:... <quote xml:lang="eng">"by Time's fell hand defaced."</quote> </gloss>

data.quote:
"With Time's injurious hand crush'd
									and o'erworn"

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φθίνει_δὲ_610">, epanaphora, as 5, <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 25" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg004:25"> <title>O. T.</title> 25</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">φθίνουσα μὲν … | φθίνουσα δ’</quote> </cit> , <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 259" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg004:259">259</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔχων μὲν...ἔχων δέ.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γῆς_610">has been needlessly suspected: here, as in the great speech of Ajax ( <ref n="Soph. Aj. 669"> <title>Ai.</title> 669</ref>—677), human destiny is viewed in relation to the whole order of nature. Cp. Tennyson, <title>Tithonus</title> 1 <quote xml:lang="eng">"The woods decay, the woods decay and fall, The vapours weep their burthen to the ground, Man comes and tills the field and lies beneath, And after many a summer dies the swan."</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="βλαστάνει_611">, comes into existence, —like the other natural growths which wax and wane: fig. of customs and institutions in <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Ant. 296" ref="Soph. Ant. 296"> <title>Ant.</title> 296</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">νόμισμ’ ἔβλαστε</quote> </cit> , <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 1095" ref="Soph. El. 1095"> <title>El.</title> 1095</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔβλαστε νόμιμα</quote> </cit> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πνεῦμα_612">is not here the wind of fortune (as <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Her. 216" ref="Eur. Her. 216"> Eur. <title>H. F.</title> 216</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅταν θεός σοι πνεῦμα μεταβαλὼν τύχῃ</quote> </cit>), but the spirit which man breathes towards man, and city towards city; the spirit of friendship or enmity. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. Seven 705" ref="Aesch. Seven 705"> Aesch. <title>Theb.</title> 705</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="lyric"> <l> <note anchored="true" place="inline" resp="RCJ" xml:lang="eng">(where, though fortune is meant, the <foreign xml:lang="grc">δαίμων</foreign>dai/mwn</note> δαίμων</l> <l>λήματος ἐν τροπαίᾳ χρονίᾳ μεταλ‐</l> <l>λακτὸς ἴσως ἂν ἔλθοι</l> <l>θαλερωτέρῳ</l> <l>πνεύματι</l> </lg> </quote> </cit> . <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Ant. 136" ref="Soph. Ant. 136"> <title>Ant.</title> 136</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <note anchored="true" place="inline" resp="RCJ" xml:lang="eng">(capaneus menacing thebes)</note>βακχεύων ἐπέπνει | ῥιπαῖς ἐχθίστων ἀνέμων</quote> </cit> . <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Supp. 1029" ref="Eur. Supp. 1029"> Eur. <title>Suppl.</title> 1029</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">αὔραις ἀδόλοις | γενναίας … ψυχᾶς</quote> </cit>. So <quote xml:lang="grc">πνεῖν μένος, κότον, ἔρωτα</quote> etc. </gloss>

data.quote:
ὅταν θεός σοι πνεῦμα μεταβαλὼν
									τύχῃ

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="βέβηκεν_613">, <emph>is set</emph> (cp. 1052). Though ( <foreign xml:lang="lat">e.g.</foreign> ) <quote xml:lang="grc">πνεῦμα φίλιον βέβηκεν ἐν ἀνδράσιν</quote> could not mean, <quote xml:lang="eng"> "a friendly spirit <emph>is steady</emph> among men,"</quote>yet <quote xml:lang="grc">πνεῦμα ταὐτὸν βέβηκεν</quote> can mean, <quote xml:lang="eng"> "the <emph>same</emph> spirit <emph>is set</emph> ,"</quote> <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>blows steadily. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Aristoph. Frogs 1003" ref="Aristoph. Frogs 1003"> Aristoph. <title>Ran.</title> 1003</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="lyric"> <l>ἡνίκ’ ἂν τὸ πνεῦμα λεῖον</l> <l>καὶ καθεστηκὸς λάβῃς</l> </lg> </quote> </cit> . </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πόλει_613">ethic dat., on the part of.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοῖς_ἤδη_614">, for some men <emph>at once</emph> ( <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> , after but a brief friendship), for others, later.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἤδη_614">is here used as <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐτίκα</quote> more often is: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. Lib. 1020" ref="Aesch. Lib. 1020"> Aesch. <title>Cho.</title> 1020</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">μόχθος δ’ ὁ μὲν αὐτίχ’, ὁ δ’ ἥξει</quote> </cit>. No relationship between men or states is permanent, <emph>for</emph> the feelings with which they regard each other are liable to change,—from liking to dislike, yes, and back again to liking.</gloss>

data.quote:
μόχθος δ’ ὁ μὲν αὐτίχ’, ὁ δ’
									ἥξει

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καὖθις_φίλα_614">, by completing the circle, completes the picture of inconstancy. Nauck has quite needlessly suspected these two vv. The maxim ascribed to Bias of Priene (c. 550 B.C.), <quote xml:lang="grc">φιλεῖν ὡς μισήσοντας καὶ μισεῖν ὡς φιλήσοντας</quote> ( <ref n="Aristot. Rh. 2.13"> Arist. <title>Rhet.</title> 2.13</ref>, <cit> <bibl n="Cic. Amic. 16.59" ref="Cic. Amic. 16.59"> Cic. <title>De Amic.</title> 16. 59</bibl> <quote xml:lang="lat">ita amare oportere ut si aliquando esset osurus</quote> </cit>), is paraphrased in <ref n="Soph. Aj. 679"> <title>Ai.</title> 679</ref>ff., with the comment, <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῖς πολλοῖσι γὰρ | βροτῶν ἄπιστός ἐσθ’ ἑταιρείας λιμήν</quote> : cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 1359" ref="Soph. Aj. 1359"> <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 1359</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἦ κάρτα πολλοὶ νῦν φίλοι καὖθις πικροί</quote> </cit>. </gloss>

data.quote:
ita amare oportere ut si aliquando
									esset osurus

ἦ κάρτα πολλοὶ νῦν φίλοι καὖθις
									πικροί

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Θήβαις_616">dat. of interest, if she has her relations with you in a peaceful state. <quote xml:lang="grc"> <emph>εὐημερεῖ</emph> . εὐημερία</quote>=either (1) <quote xml:lang="eng">"fine weather,"</quote> <quote xml:lang="grc">εὐδία</quote> , as Arist. <title>Hist. An.</title> 6. 15 <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅταν εὐημερίας γενομένης ἀναθερμαίνηται ἡ γῆ</quote> , or (2) <quote xml:lang="eng"> "prosperity."</quote> The verb is always figurative. <ref n="Aristot. Pol. 6.1322b"> Arist. <title>Pol.</title> 6.8</ref>. 22 <quote xml:lang="grc">ταῖς σχολαστικωτέραις καὶ μᾶλλον εὐημερούσαις πόλεσιν</quote> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καλῶς_617">has been censured as faulty after the <quote xml:lang="grc">εὖ</quote> in <quote xml:lang="grc">εὐημερεῖ</quote> : its defenders might have quoted Eur. fr. 886 <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸν καλῶς εὐδαίμονα</quote> . It means, <quote xml:lang="eng"> "satisfactorily,"</quote> <quote xml:lang="eng">"as we could wish,"</quote> and is represented by the word <quote xml:lang="eng">"all"</quote> in the version above.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὰ_617">is better than <foreign xml:lang="grc">τὸ</foreign> for the MS. <quote xml:lang="grc">τε</quote> (or <quote xml:lang="grc">τὲ</quote> ).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸ_σέ_617">would be rather, <quote xml:lang="eng">"so far as her relation to you is concerned"</quote> (acc. of respect),—when <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰ νῦν</quote> should be read in 616. This would make the welfare of Thebes more prominent than the mutual amity.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὁ_μυρίος_617">: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 646" ref="Soph. Aj. 646"> <title>Ai.</title> 646</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁ μακρὸς κἀναρίθμητος χρόνος</quote> </cit> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τεκνοῦται_618">. The midd. was more commonly used of the mother, the act. of the father (though converse instances occur); the midd. is used figuratively, as here, in <ref n="Aesch. Ag. 754"> Aesch. <title>Ag.</title> 754</ref>( <quote xml:lang="grc">ὄλβος</quote> ), <ref n="Eur. IT 1262"> Eur. <title>I. T.</title> 1262</ref>( <quote xml:lang="grc">χθών</quote> ).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἰών_618">, as it proceeds. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 1365" ref="Soph. El. 1365"> <title>El.</title> 1365</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="trimeter"> <l>πολλαὶ κυκλοῦνται νύκτες ἡμέραι τ’ ἴσαι</l> <l>αἳ ταῦτά σοι δείξουσιν</l> </lg> </quote> </cit> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐν_αἷς_619">, in the course of which: <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> at some moment in them. So <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Ant. 1064" ref="Soph. Ant. 1064"> <title>Ant.</title> 1064</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="trimeter"> <l>κάτισθι μὴ πολλοὺς ἔτι</l> <l>τρόχους ἁμιλλητῆρας ἡλίου τελῶν</l> <l>ἐν οἶσι … ἀμοιβὸν ἀντιδοὺς ἔσει</l> </lg> </quote> </cit> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δεξιώματα_619">, pledges given by placing one's right hand in another's: the word occurs only here, and in Athen. 159 B (poet. anonym.) <quote xml:lang="grc">ὦ χρυσέ, δεξίωμα κάλλιστον βροτοῖς</quote> , gift most welcome to men. <quote xml:lang="grc">δεξιοῦσθαι</quote> is only <quote xml:lang="eng">"to greet"</quote> or <quote xml:lang="eng">"welcome"</quote> : but <quote xml:lang="grc">δεξιὰς διδόναι καὶ λαμβάνειν</quote> , etc., suggested the phrase here. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Il. 2.341" ref="Hom. Il. 2.341"> <title>Il.</title> 2.341</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">σπονδαί τ’ ἄκρητοι καὶ δεξιαί, ᾗς ἐπέπιθμεν</quote> </cit>. In <ref n="Eur. Supp. 930"> Eur. <title>Suppl.</title> 930</ref>Theseus says of Polyneices, <quote xml:lang="grc">ξένος γὰρ ἦν μοι</quote> , as if alluding to hereditary <quote xml:lang="grc">ξενία</quote> between the royal houses. Cp. 632. </gloss>

data.quote:
σπονδαί τ’ ἄκρητοι καὶ δεξιαί, ᾗς
									ἐπέπιθμεν

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δόρει_διασκεδῶσιν_620">, they will <quote xml:lang="eng">"throw their pledges to the winds"</quote> by an armed invasion of Attica. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Ant. 287" ref="Soph. Ant. 287"> <title>Ant.</title> 287</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">νόμους διασκεδῶν</quote> </cit>, to make havoc of laws.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δόρει_620">(instead of the more freq. <quote xml:lang="grc">δορί</quote> ) is required by metre also in <ref n="Soph. OC 1314">1314</ref> , <ref n="Soph. OC 1386">1386</ref> , <cit> <bibl n="Aristoph. Peace 357" ref="Aristoph. Peace 357"> Aristoph. <title>Pax</title> 357</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">σὺν δόρει σὺν ἀσπίδι</quote> </cit> , <ref n="Aristoph. Wasps 1081"> <title>Vesp.</title> 1081</ref>(where MSS. <quote xml:lang="grc">ξὺν δορὶ ξὺν ἀσπίδι</quote> ),—all iambic or trochaic. The phrase in Ar. came from Soph. <quote xml:lang="grc">Μῶμος</quote> , acc. to Choeroboscus 376. 19. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Rh. 274" ref="Eur. Rh. 274"> [Eur.] <title>Rhes.</title> 274</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">μάχας πρὸ χειρῶν καὶ δόρη βαστάζομεν</quote> </cit>,—a plur. on the analogy of this dat. sing. </gloss>

data.quote:
μάχας πρὸ χειρῶν καὶ δόρη
									βαστάζομεν

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἵν_621">could mean, <quote xml:lang="eng">"at a place where,"</quote> at the grave (see on <ref target="cl411">411</ref> ), but is better taken as= <quote xml:lang="eng">"in which case,"</quote> <quote xml:lang="eng">"when,"</quote> since the moment of rupture ( <quote xml:lang="grc">διασκεδῶσιν</quote> ) would not be the battle at Colonus, but the preceding declaration of war.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εὕδων_621">(cp. on <ref target="cl307">307</ref> ), in contrast with the fierce combatants on the ground above him. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ψυχρὸς_θερμὸν_622">, here of the physical contrast between death and life; but in <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Ant. 88" ref="Soph. Ant. 88"> <title>Ant.</title> 88</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">θερμὴν ἐπὶ ψυχροῖσι καρδίαν ἔχεις</quote> </cit>, <quote xml:lang="eng">"thy heart is hot on chilling deeds"</quote> ( <quote xml:lang="grc">κρυεροῖς</quote> ). Simonides 120. 5 <quote xml:lang="grc">νῦν δ’ ὁ μὲν ἐν πόντῳ κρυερὸς νέκυς</quote> . For the idea of the buried dead draining the lifeblood of their foes cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 1420" ref="Soph. El. 1420"> <title>El.</title> 1420</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">παλίρρυτον γὰρ αἷμ’ ὑπεξαιροῦσι τῶν | κτανόντων οἱ πάλαι θανόντες</quote> </cit> . </gloss>

data.quote:
παλίρρυτον γὰρ αἷμ’ ὑπεξαιροῦσι τῶν |
									κτανόντων οἱ πάλαι θανόντες

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σαφής_623">, true (as a prophet): 792: <title>O.T.</title> 1011 <quote xml:lang="grc">ταρβῶ γε μή μοι Φοῖβος ἐξέλθῃ</quote> <quote xml:lang="grc">σαφής</quote> . So <quote xml:lang="grc">φίλος σαφής</quote> , a proved friend ( <ref n="Eur. Orest. 1155"> Eur. <title>Or.</title> 1155</ref>), <quote xml:lang="grc">γραμματεὺς σαφής</quote> an accurate scribe (Aesch. fr. 348). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τἀκίνητα_624"> ,= <quote xml:lang="grc">ἃ μὴ δεῖ λόγῳ κινεῖσθαι</quote> (see 1526), secrets which should be allowed to <emph>rest</emph> beyond the veil: so <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Ant. 1060" ref="Soph. Ant. 1060"> <title>Ant.</title> 1060</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὄρσεις με τἀκίνητα διὰ φρενῶν φράσαι</quote> </cit>, the secrets locked in my soul. (Cp. Gray: <quote xml:lang="eng"> "No farther seek his merits to disclose, <emph>Or draw his frailties from their dread abode.</emph> "</quote>) </gloss>

data.quote:
ὄρσεις με τἀκίνητα διὰ φρενῶν
									φράσαι

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔα_με_625">, leave me (permit me to cease) at the point where I began (the prayer for an Attic home). Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Il. 9.97" ref="Hom. Il. 9.97"> <title>Il.</title> 9.97</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν σοὶ μὲν λήξω, σέο δ’ ἄρξομαι</quote> </cit>. Here we cannot well evolve <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀφ’</quote> or <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐξ ὧν</quote> from <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἐν οἷσιν</foreign> : nor, again, would <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν οἷς ἠρξάμην</quote> be idiomatic. </gloss>

data.quote:
ἐν σοὶ μὲν λήξω, σέο δ’
									ἄρξομαι

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸ_φυλάσσων_625">, taking care that thy part is loyally done: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 320" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg004:320"> <title>O. T.</title> 320</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ σόν τε σὺ | κἀγὼ διοίσω τοὐμόν</quote> </cit>(thy part): <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 1313" ref="Soph. Aj. 1313"> <title>Ai.</title> 1313</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅρα μὴ τοὐμὸν ἀλλὰ καὶ τὸ σόν</quote> </cit>(thine interest): <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 99 <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς τὸ σὸν ξυνῆκ’ ἐγώ</quote> (thy saying). Both idiom and rhythm are against joining <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ σὸν πιστόν</quote> as <quote xml:lang="eng">"thy good faith."</quote> </gloss>

data.quote:
τὸ σόν τε σὺ | κἀγὼ διοίσω
									τοὐμόν
ὅρα μὴ τοὐμὸν ἀλλὰ καὶ τὸ σόν

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἴπερ_ψεύσουσι_628">, you will find me helpful,—that is to say, if the gods do not disappoint me.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἴπερ_628">marks the point which must be taken for granted, in order that <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐρεῖς</quote> (626) should hold good: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Her. 1345" ref="Eur. Her. 1345"> Eur. <title>H. F.</title> 1345</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="trimeter"> <l>δεῖται γὰρ ὁ θεός, εἴπερ ἔστ’ ὄντως θεός <note anchored="true" place="inline" resp="RCJ" xml:lang="eng"> ( <emph>assuming</emph>assuming</note> </l> <l>οὐδενός</l> </lg> </quote> </cit> . <cit> <bibl n="Lys. 12.48" ref="Lys. 12.48">Lys. or. 12 § 48</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἴπερ ἦν ἀνὴρ ἀγαθός, ἐχρῆν ἄν</quote> </cit>, etc. (Cp. Thompson <title>Syntax</title> § 225. 4.) </gloss>

data.quote: εἴπερ ἦν ἀνὴρ ἀγαθός, ἐχρῆν ἄν

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πάλαι_629">: 287, 459. The Chorus, tempering caution with good-nature, testify that the promise of Oedipus is, at least, not merely a device inspired by the arrival of the King. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐφαίνετο_τελῶν_630"> (without <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς</quote> )= <quote xml:lang="eng">"was manifestly intending to perform"</quote> : <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐφαίνετο ὡς τελῶν</quote> = <quote xml:lang="eng"> "appeared <emph>as</emph> one intending to perform,"</quote> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς</quote>marking the aspect in which he presented himself to their minds. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 326" ref="Soph. Aj. 326"> <title>Ai.</title> 326</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ δῆλός ἐστιν ὥς τι δρασείων κακόν</quote> </cit>. For the imperf., cp. <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. Ag. 593" ref="Aesch. Ag. 593"> Aesch. <title>Ag.</title> 593</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">λόγοις τοιούτοις πλαγκτὸς οὖσ’ ἐφαινόμην</quote> </cit>, by such reasonings I appeared (was made out to be) in error. </gloss>

data.quote:
καὶ δῆλός ἐστιν ὥς τι δρασείων
									κακόν

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δῆτ_631">, <quote xml:lang="eng">"then,"</quote> a comment on the speech of Oed. rather than on the words of the Chorus, as oft in questions (cp. 602).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐκβάλοι_631">: properly, <quote xml:lang="eng">"cast out of doors,"</quote> as a worthless thing: hence, <quote xml:lang="eng">"reject,"</quote> <quote xml:lang="eng">"repudiate"</quote> : Eur. fr. 362. 45 <quote xml:lang="grc">προγόνων παλαιὰ θέσμῑ ὅστις ἐκβαλεῖ</quote> : <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Crito 46B" ref="Plat. Crito 46B"> Plat. <title>Crito</title> 46B</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τοὺς δὲ λόγους, οὓς ἐν τῷ ἔμπροσθεν ἔλεγον, οὐ δύναμαι νῦν ἐκβαλεῖν</quote> </cit>. Others take it literally, <quote xml:lang="eng">"cast out of the land,"</quote> so that <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνδρὸς εὐμένειαν τοιοῦδε</quote> = <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄνδρα εὐμενῆ τοιόνδε</quote> . But the notion of rashly scorning what is really precious gives more point both here and in 636. </gloss>

data.quote:
τοὺς δὲ λόγους, οὓς ἐν τῷ ἔμπροσθεν
									ἔλεγον, οὐ δύναμαι νῦν ἐκβαλεῖν

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὅτῳ_632">, not <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅτου</quote> , is right. Con strue: <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅτῳ ἡ δορύξενος ἑστία αἰὲν κοινή ἐστι παρ’ ἡμῖν</quote> , lit., <quote xml:lang="eng">"to whom the hearth of an ally is always common among us"</quote> :</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κοινή_632">, <quote xml:lang="eng">"common,"</quote> = <quote xml:lang="eng"> "giving <emph> reciprocal</emph> hospitality,"</quote>which Theseus could claim at Thebes, as Oedipus at Athens.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αἰέν_632">, <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="eng">"even if he had not this special claim."</quote> This seems better than to take <quote xml:lang="grc"> κοινή</quote> as (1) <quote xml:lang="eng">"common to him with other Thebans,"</quote> (2) <quote xml:lang="eng">"provided by our State,"</quote> (3) <quote xml:lang="eng"> "common to him with <emph>us</emph> ,"</quote>or (4) <quote xml:lang="eng">"accessible,"</quote> as <cit> <bibl n="Andoc. 1.147" ref="Andoc. 1.147">Andoc. or. 2 § 147</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οἰκία κοινοτάτη τῷ δεομένῳ</quote> </cit>. With <foreign xml:lang="grc">ὅτου</foreign> the above version could not stand (since <quote xml:lang="eng"> " <emph>belongs to him</emph> "</quote>could not replace <quote xml:lang="eng"> " <emph>exists for him</emph> "</quote>), and so we should have to understand, <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅτου ἡ δορύξενος ἑστία αἰὲν κοινή ἐστι παρ’ ἡμῖν</quote> , whose allied hearth (at Thebes) is always regarded among us as open to us ( <quote xml:lang="eng">"as a common possession,"</quote> Campb.): but this seems very forced. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δορύξενος_632"> , <quote xml:lang="eng">"spear-friend,"</quote> is one with whom one has the tie of <quote xml:lang="grc">ξενία</quote> in respect of war: <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> , who will make common cause with one in war. It is applied by Aesch., Soph., and Eur. only to princes or chiefs, with an armed force at their command. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. Lib. 562" ref="Aesch. Lib. 562"> Aesch. <title>Cho.</title> 562</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ξένος τε καὶ δορύξενος δόμων</quote> </cit>, said by Orestes when he presents himself <quote xml:lang="grc">παντελῆ σαγὴν ἔχων</quote> : <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> he comes not merely as the personal <quote xml:lang="grc">ξένος</quote> of the royal house, but as a chief in armed alliance with it. Plut. ( <ref n="Plut. Quaes. Graec. 295b"> <title>Mor.</title> 295 B</ref>, <ref> <title>Quaest. Gr.</title> 17</ref>) asks, <quote xml:lang="grc">τίς ὁ δορύξενος</quote> ; He conjectures that it meant, <emph>a ransomed prisoner of war</emph> , in his subsequent friendly relation to the ransomer ( <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκ δοριαλώτου δορύξενος προσαγορευόμενος</quote> ). This is against the usage of the poets, our only witnesses. And the source of the guess is clear. Plutarch was thinking of the verbal compounds, <quote xml:lang="grc">δοριάλωτος, δορίκτητος, δορίληπτος</quote> , etc. From these he inferred that <quote xml:lang="grc">δορύξενος</quote> would mean primarily, <quote xml:lang="eng">"a friend gained through the spear."</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀφιγμένος_634">, not, <quote xml:lang="eng">"because,"</quote> but, <quote xml:lang="eng"> "while,"</quote> he has come. Besides his public claim (632), Oed. has two personal claims, (1) as the suppliant of the Eumenides, (2) as a visitor who can make a valuable return to Athens for protecting him.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δασμός_634">, usu. <quote xml:lang="eng">"tribute"</quote> ( <ref n="Soph. OT 36"> <title>O. T.</title> 36</ref>, and so in Xen.); here fig., <quote xml:lang="eng">"recompense."</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐκβαλῶ_636">: cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 631">631.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔμπολιν_637">is Musgrave's certain correction of the MS. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔμπαλιν</quote> : cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 1156" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg007:1156">1156</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">σοὶ μὲν ἔμπολιν | οὐκ ὄντα, συγγενῆ δέ</quote> </cit>, not thy fellow-citizen, indeed, but thy kinsman. The word does not occur elsewhere. <quote xml:lang="eng">"I will establish him in the land,"</quote> says Theseus, <quote xml:lang="eng">"as a member of our State"</quote> : he who now is <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄπολις</quote> (cp. 208) shall in Attica have the full protection of our laws.</gloss>

data.quote:
σοὶ μὲν ἔμπολιν | οὐκ ὄντα, συγγενῆ
									δέ

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔμπαλιν_637">has been rendered (1) <quote xml:lang="eng">"on the contrary,"</quote> <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="eng">"so far from rejecting him"</quote> : so the schol., and this version is alone correct: (2) <quote xml:lang="eng">"once more,"</quote> <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> renewing the alliance between the states,— Paley: (3) <quote xml:lang="eng">"in return"</quote> for his benefits,— Ellendt. Campbell objects that with <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔμπολιν</quote> <quote xml:lang="eng"> "the opposition of the clauses would not be sufficiently marked by <quote xml:lang="grc">δέ"</quote> </quote>: but for</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δέ_637">= <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλά</quote> cp. <cit> <bibl n="Antiph. 5.4" ref="Antiph. 5.4">Antiph. or. 5 §§ 4, 5</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">αἰτήσομαι ὑμᾶς οὐχ ἅπερ οἱ πολλοί..., τάδε δὲ δέομαι ὑμῶν</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Thuc. 4.86" ref="Thuc. 4.86">Thuc. 4.86</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐκ ἐπὶ κακῷ, ἐπ’ ἐλευθερώσει δὲ τῶν Ἑλλήνων παρελήλυθα</quote> </cit>. </gloss>

data.quote:
αἰτήσομαι ὑμᾶς οὐχ ἅπερ οἱ πολλοί...,
									τάδε δὲ δέομαι ὑμῶν

οὐκ ἐπὶ κακῷ, ἐπ’ ἐλευθερώσει δὲ τῶν
									Ἑλλήνων παρελήλυθα

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σέ_638">, the Coryphaeus. Cp. <ref n="Aesch. Supp. 955"> Aesch. <title>Suppl.</title> 955ff.,</ref>where the king gives the Danaïdes their choice between Argos and a private home apart; <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. Supp. 955" ref="Aesch. Supp. 955"/> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="trimeter"> <l>στείχετ’ εὐερκῆ πόλιν</l> <l>… εἰ δέ τις μείζων χάρις,</l> <l>πάρεστιν οἰκεῖν καὶ μονορρύθμους δόμους.</l> <l>τούτων τὰ λῷστα καὶ τὰ θυμηδέστατα</l> <l>πάρεστι, λωτίσασθε</l> </lg> </quote> </cit> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἰ_αὐτᾦ_639">. For <foreign xml:lang="grc">τόδ’</foreign> in appos. with <foreign xml:lang="grc"> στείχειν</foreign> cp. <cit> <bibl n="Xen. Cyrop. 8.4.4" ref="Xen. Cyrop. 8.4.4"> Xen. <title>Cyr.</title> 8.4.4</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">σαφηνίζεσθαι δέ, ὡς ἕκαστον ἐτίμα, τοῦτο ἐδόκει αὐτῷ ἀγαθὸν εἶναι</quote> </cit> : <cit> <bibl n="Aeschin. 2.106" ref="Aeschin. 2.106">Aeschin. or. 2 § 106</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ μὴ πολυπραγμονεῖν ἡμᾶς τοὺς πρέσβεις μηδέν, τοῦτ’ ἀγαθὸν ὑπολαμβάνων εἶναι</quote> </cit>. Here <foreign xml:lang="grc">τόδε</foreign> similarly follows the word with which it is in appos., though it should properly precede it, as <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Phoen. 550" ref="Eur. Phoen. 550"> Eur. <title>Phoen.</title> 550</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">μέγ’ ἥγησαι τόδε, | περιβλέπεσθαι τίμιον;</quote> </cit> </gloss>

data.quote:
σαφηνίζεσθαι δέ, ὡς ἕκαστον ἐτίμα,
									τοῦτο ἐδόκει αὐτῷ ἀγαθὸν εἶναι

τὸ μὴ πολυπραγμονεῖν ἡμᾶς τοὺς
									πρέσβεις μηδέν, τοῦτ’ ἀγαθὸν ὑπολαμβάνων εἶναι

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τούτων_639">partitive gen. with <foreign xml:lang="grc">κρίναντι</foreign> , <quote xml:lang="eng">"having chosen (one) of these things"</quote> ; cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 640" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg004:640"> <title>O. T.</title> 640</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="trimeter"> <l>δυοῖν δικαιοῖ δρᾶν ἀποκρίνας κακοῖν,</l> <l>ἢ γῆς ἀπῶσαι...ἢ κτεῖναι.</l> </lg> </quote> </cit> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δίδωμι_χρῆσθαι_639">: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Xen. Anab. 3.4.41" ref="Xen. Anab. 3.4.41"> Xen. <title>Anab.</title> 3. 4 §§ 41 f.</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ βούλει, μένε..., εἰ δὲ χρῄζεις, πορεύου...Ἀλλὰ δίδωμί σοι, ἔφη ὁ Χειρίσοφος, ὁπότερον βούλει ἑλέσθαι</quote> </cit>.</gloss>

data.quote:
εἰ βούλει, μένε..., εἰ δὲ χρῄζεις,
							πορεύου...Ἀλλὰ δίδωμί σοι, ἔφη ὁ Χειρίσοφος, ὁπότερον
									βούλει ἑλέσθαι

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τῇδε_639">, <quote xml:lang="eng">"in that sense,"</quote> <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> in whichever course you may prefer, <quote xml:lang="grc">ᾗ ἂν σὺ βούλῃ</quote> : cp. 1444: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Ant. 1111" ref="Soph. Ant. 1111"> <title>Ant.</title> 1111</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">δόξα τῇδ’ ἐπεστράφη</quote> </cit> : <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 1301" ref="Soph. El. 1301"> <title>El.</title> 1301</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅπως καὶ σοὶ φίλον | καὶ τοὐμὸν ἔσται τῇδ’.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

data.quote:
ὅπως καὶ σοὶ φίλον | καὶ τοὐμὸν ἔσται
									τῇδ’.

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ξυνοίσομαι_639">, agree: <cit> <bibl n="Antiph. 5.42" ref="Antiph. 5.42">Antiph. or. 5 § 42</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῖς μὲν πρώτοις ( λόγοις ) συνεφέρετο,... τούτοις δὲ διεφέρετο</quote> </cit> . </gloss>

data.quote:
τοῖς μὲν πρώτοις ( λόγοις )
									συνεφέρετο,... τούτοις δὲ διεφέρετο

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="διδοίης_εὖ_642">: 1435: <ref n="Soph. OT 1081"> <title>O. T.</title> 1081</ref>( <quote xml:lang="grc">τύχης</quote> ) <quote xml:lang="grc">τῆς εὖ διδούσης</quote> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δόμους_στείχειν_643"> : <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 1769" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg007:1769">1769</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">Θήβας … | πέμψον</quote> </cit> : <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 1178" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg004:1178"> <title>O. T.</title> 1178</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄλλην χθόνα | δοκῶν ἀποίσειν</quote> </cit> . </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἰ_ἦν_644">, <foreign xml:lang="lat">sc.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔχρῃζον ἂν δόμους στείχειν</quote> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐν_πράξεις_645">; Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 558" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg004:558"> <title>O. T.</title> 558</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="trimeter"> <l>ΟΙ. πόσον τιν’ ἤδη δῆθ’ ὁ Λάϊος χρόνον</l> <l>ΚΠ. δέδρακε ποῖον ἔργον; οὐ γὰρ ἐννοῶ.</l> <l>ΟΙ. ἄφαντος ἔρρει …</l> </lg> </quote> </cit> etc.; <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Phil. 210" ref="Soph. Phil. 210"> <title>Ph.</title> 210 </bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ΧΟ. ἀλλ’ ἔχε, τέκνον, ΝΕ. λέγ’ ὅτι ΧΟ. φροντίδας νέας</quote> </cit>: <ref n="Soph. El. 854"> <title>El.</title> 854</ref>ff. An interruption of this kind serves to bespeak the attention of the audience for a point which the dramatist desires to emphasize. </gloss>

data.quote:
ΧΟ. ἀλλ’ ἔχε, τέκνον, ΝΕ. λέγ’ ὅτι ΧΟ.
							φροντίδας νέας

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κρατήσω_646">: near the shrine he was to close his life (91), and at his grave the Thebans were to be defeated (411, 621). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μέγ_δώρημα_647"> ,= <quote xml:lang="grc">μέγα ἂν εἴη δώρημα ὃ λέγεις</quote> , it would be a great benefit of which you speak ( <foreign xml:lang="lat"> sc.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ κρατοῖς</quote> ). Cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 218"> <title>Ant.</title> 218</ref>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τῆς_συνουσίας_647"> , <quote xml:lang="eng">"from your abiding with the people here (at Colonus)"</quote> : <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="eng"> "You have suggested a strong reason for your staying <emph> here</emph> , rather than for going with me to <title>Athens.</title> "</quote> Cp. <quote xml:lang="grc">τῇ ξυνουσίᾳ</quote> in 63. It would be tame, at this stage, to take <quote xml:lang="grc">ξυνουσία</quote> merely of his presence in Attica. The belief of Theseus in Theban amity (606) has now been shaken by his visitor (620).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τῆς_ς_647">, gen. of source (ultimately possessive): <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 170" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg004:170"> <title>O. T.</title> 170</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">φροντίδος ἔγχος</quote> </cit>, a weapon furnished by thought. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἰ_ἐμμενεῖ_648"> , <quote xml:lang="eng"> "yes, if on <emph>your</emph> part (ethic dat.) the promise (of protection and burial) shall be observed, <quote xml:lang="grc">τελοῦντι"</quote> by your performing it <quote xml:lang="grc">μοι</quote> for me</quote>(dat. of interest). <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἐμμενεῖ</foreign> <emph>alone</emph> might have meant merely, <quote xml:lang="eng">"if you abstain from withdrawing your promise"</quote> : <quote xml:lang="grc">τελοῦντι</quote> supplements it, marking that good faith must be shown by deeds. We can say either <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐμμένεις οἷς λέγεις</quote> or <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐμμένει σοι ἃ λέγεις</quote> : cp. <cit> <bibl n="Thuc. 2.2" ref="Thuc. 2.2">Thuc. 2.2</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τέσσαρα μὲν γὰρ καὶ δέκα ἔτη ἐνέμειναν αἱ τριακοντούτεις σπονδαί</quote> </cit> : <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Phaedrus 258b" ref="Plat. Phaedrus 258b"> Plat. <title>Phaedr.</title> 258 B</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐὰν...ἐμμένῃ</quote> </cit>, if (his proposal) stand good. </gloss>

data.quote:
τέσσαρα μὲν γὰρ καὶ δέκα ἔτη ἐνέμειναν
									αἱ τριακοντούτεις σπονδαί

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸ_ἀνδρ_649">. might be acc. of respect ( <quote xml:lang="eng">"as to"</quote> ), but is more simply taken with <foreign xml:lang="grc">θάρσει</foreign> : cp. <cit> <bibl n="Dem. 3.7" ref="Dem. 3.7">Dem. or. 3 § 7</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὔτε Φίλιππος ἐθάρρει τούτους οὔθ’ οὗτοι Φίλιππον</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Xen. Cyrop. 5.5.42" ref="Xen. Cyrop. 5.5.42"> Xen. <title>Cyr.</title> 5.5.42</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">εὐώχει αὐτούς, ἵνα σε καὶ θαρρήσωσιν</quote> </cit>. (Distinguish this acc. with <quote xml:lang="grc">θαρσεῖν</quote> , of confidence <emph>in</emph> , from the more freq. acc. of confidence <emph> against</emph> , as <quote xml:lang="grc">θαρσεῖν μάχας</quote> .) Cp. <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ σόν</quote> , 625 n. </gloss>

data.quote:
οὔτε Φίλιππος ἐθάρρει τούτους οὔθ’
									οὗτοι Φίλιππον

εὐώχει αὐτούς, ἵνα σε καὶ
									θαρρήσωσιν

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὡς_κακὸν_650">: cp. <ref n="Eur. Med. 731"> Eur. <title>Med.</title> 731</ref>ff., where Medea asks Aegeus to clinch his promise with an oath, and he asks, <quote xml:lang="grc">μῶν οὐ πέποιθας</quote> ; <ref n="Shak. JC 2.1.129"> Shaks. <title>Jul. Caes.</title> 2. 1. 129 ff.</ref> <quote xml:lang="eng"> <lg type="blank"> <l>Swear priests and cowards and men cautelous,—</l> <l part="F">...unto bad causes swear</l> <l part="I">Such creatures as men doubt.</l> </lg> </quote> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <emph>πιστώσομαι</emph>. πιστόω</quote>is <quote xml:lang="eng"> "to make <quote xml:lang="grc">πιστός"</quote> </quote> : <cit> <bibl n="Thuc. 4.88" ref="Thuc. 4.88">Thuc. 4.88</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">πιστώσαντες αὐτὸν τοῖς ὅρκοις</quote> </cit>, when they had bound him by the oaths ( <emph>iureiurando obstrinxerant</emph> ): so the pass., <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Od. 15.435" ref="Hom. Od. 15.435"> <title>Od.</title> 15.435</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἴ μοι ἐθέλοιτέ γε, ναῦται, | ὅρκῳ πιστωθῆναι</quote> </cit>. The midd. expresses <quote xml:lang="eng">"in one's own interest,"</quote> as here; or reciprocity, as <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Il. 21.286" ref="Hom. Il. 21.286"> <title>Il.</title> 21.286</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">χειρὶ δὲ χεῖρα λαβόντες ἐπιστώσαντ’ ἐπέεσσιν</quote> </cit>. </gloss>

data.quote:
εἴ μοι ἐθέλοιτέ γε, ναῦται, | ὅρκῳ
									πιστωθῆναι

χειρὶ δὲ χεῖρα λαβόντες ἐπιστώσαντ’
									ἐπέεσσιν

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἢ_λόγῳ_651">, than by word (without my oath). <cit> <bibl n="Dem. 27.54" ref="Dem. 27.54">Dem. or. 27 § 54</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> καὶ μαρτυρίαν μὲν οὐδεμίαν ἐνεβάλετο τούτων ὁ ταῦτ’ εἰπεῖν ἀξιώσας, ψιλῷ δὲ λόγῳ χρησάμενος <note anchored="true" place="inline" resp="RCJ" xml:lang="eng"> [rather <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἐχρήσατο</foreign>e)xrh/s1ato</note>ὡς πιστευθησόμενος δῑ ἐκείνων</quote> </cit>. Cp. <ref n="Antiph. 5.8">Antiphon or. 5 § 8</ref> quoted <ref target="cl22">on 22</ref> . <cit> <bibl n="Shak. TGV 2.7" ref="Shak. TGV 2.7"> Shaksp., <title>G. of Verona</title> 2. 7. 75</bibl> <quote xml:lang="eng">"His words are bonds."</quote> </cit> —Not, <quote xml:lang="eng">"than in name,"</quote> <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="eng">"form,"</quote> as opp. to <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔργῳ</quote> . </gloss>

data.quote:
καὶ μαρτυρίαν μὲν οὐδεμίαν ἐνεβάλετο τούτων ὁ
							ταῦτ’ εἰπεῖν ἀξιώσας, ψιλῷ δὲ λόγῳ χρησάμενος  ὡς
							πιστευθησόμενος δῑ ἐκείνων

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοῦ_ἔχει_652">; not, <quote xml:lang="eng"> "what do you fear <emph>most?</emph> "</quote> but, <quote xml:lang="eng">"What, exactly, do you fear?"</quote> —a polite way of asking the question. <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Gorg. 448D" ref="Plat. Gorg. 448D"> Plat. <title>Gorg.</title> 448D</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ΣΩ. ἀλλὰ γὰρ ὃ ὑπέσχετο Χαιρεφῶντι οὐ ποιεῖ. ΓΟΡ. τί μάλιστα, ὦ Σώκρατες</quote> </cit> ; </gloss>

data.quote:
ΣΩ. ἀλλὰ γὰρ ὃ ὑπέσχετο Χαιρεφῶντι οὐ
									ποιεῖ. ΓΟΡ. τί μάλιστα, ὦ Σώκρατες

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὅρα_λείπων_654">, like his utterances in 653 and 656, is left unfinished, —Theseus striking in: <foreign xml:lang="lat">sc.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">μὴ ἐκείνοις προδῷς</quote> . Taken as a sentence, the words could mean only <quote xml:lang="eng">"see that"</quote> ( <emph>not</emph> <quote xml:lang="eng">"how"</quote> ) <quote xml:lang="eng">"you are leaving me."</quote> The conj. <foreign xml:lang="grc">ὁρᾶν</foreign> (for <foreign xml:lang="grc">δρᾶν</foreign> ), adopted by Nauck and Wecklein, would be an echo of <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅρα</quote> : it is not only quite needless, but bad, because here it would give an angry tone, as such echoes usually do in trag.; see on <ref n="Soph. OT 548"> <title>O. T.</title> 548</ref>f. The case of <quote xml:lang="grc"> οἶσθα...οἶδα</quote> 656 is different. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὀκνοῦντ_ἀνάγκη_655"> : <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> , <quote xml:lang="grc">ὀκνοῦντά με ἀνάγκη διδάσκειν σε</quote> ,—feeling such fear as I do, I am constrained to be thus urgent with you. (Not, <quote xml:lang="eng">"I must thus urge you, since you are slack."</quote> ) Wecklein's change to <quote xml:lang="grc">ὀκνεῖν γ’</quote> is unnecessary. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐκ_οἶσθ_656">: Oed. had said nothing of Creon's threatened visit (396).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μή_656">, not <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ</quote> , in strong assurance, as with inf. after <quote xml:lang="grc">ὄμνυμι</quote> , etc.: cp. 281, 797: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Ant. 1092" ref="Soph. Ant. 1092"> <title>Ant.</title> 1092</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπιστάμεσθα … | μήπω ποτ’ αὐτὸν ψεῦδος … λακεῖν</quote> </cit> </gloss>

data.quote:
ἐπιστάμεσθα … | μήπω ποτ’ αὐτὸν ψεῦδος
									… λακεῖν

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θυμῷ_658">modal dat., <quote xml:lang="eng">"in wrath-"</quote> (not locative, <quote xml:lang="eng">"in the soul"</quote> ): cp. <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Laws 866D" ref="Plat. Laws 866D"> Plat. <title>Legg.</title> 866D</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">( ἐὰν ) θυμῷ … ᾖ τὸ πεπραγμένον ἐκπραχθέν</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 405" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg004:405"> <title>O. T.</title> 405</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὀργῇ λελέχθαι</quote> </cit> . </gloss>

data.quote:
( ἐὰν ) θυμῷ … ᾖ τὸ πεπραγμένον
									ἐκπραχθέν

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αὑτοῦ_660">, become its own master, regain its control over passion: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Dem. 4.7" ref="Dem. 4.7">Dem. or, 4 § 7</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἣν ὑμῶν αὐτῶν ἐθελήσητε γενέσθαι</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Phaedrus 250a" ref="Plat. Phaedrus 250a"> Plat. <title>Phaedr.</title> 250 A</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκπλήττονται καὶ οὐκέθ’ αὑτῶν γίγνονται</quote> </cit>. So <cit> <bibl n="Hdt. 1.119" ref="Hdt. 1.119">Her. 1.119</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὔτε ἐξεπλάγη ἐντός τε ἑωυτοῦ γίνεται</quote> </cit>, <quote xml:lang="eng">"was not dismayed, but mastered his feelings"</quote> : <cit> <bibl n="Dem. 34.35" ref="Dem. 34.35">Dem. or. 34 § 35</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐκ ἐντὸς ὢν αὑτοῦ</quote> </cit> : <cit> <bibl n="Dem. 19.198" ref="Dem. 19.198">or. 19 § 198</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔξω δ’ αὑτῆς οὖσα ὑπὸ τοῦ κακοῦ</quote> </cit>. Elmsley strangely preferred <foreign xml:lang="grc">αὐτοῦ</foreign> , taking it as adv., <quote xml:lang="eng">"there."</quote> </gloss>

data.quote:
οὔτε ἐξεπλάγη ἐντός τε ἑωυτοῦ
									γίνεται

ἔξω δ’ αὑτῆς οὖσα ὑπὸ τοῦ
									κακοῦ

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φροῦδα_660">, there is an end of them: <ref n="Eur. Tro. 1071"> Eur. <title>Tro.</title> 1071</ref>(to Zeus) <quote xml:lang="grc">φροῦδαί σοι θυσίαι</quote> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κείνοις_661">(referring to <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄνδρες</quote> in 653) goes both with <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπερρ</quote> . and with <quote xml:lang="grc"> φανήσεται.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καὶ_ἐπερρώσθη_661">(impersonal) even if courage has come to them <foreign xml:lang="grc">δεινὰ λέγειν</foreign> to say dread things <foreign xml:lang="grc">τῆς σῆς ἀγωγῆς</foreign> about your removal (for the gen. see on <ref target="cl355">355</ref> : for <quote xml:lang="grc">τῆς σῆς</quote> as=an objective <quote xml:lang="grc">σου</quote> , on 332). The normal phrase would be <quote xml:lang="grc">κεῖνοι ἐπερρώσθησαν</quote> , and the use of the impersonal form here is bolder than in the ordinary passive examples (usu. with perf.) such as <quote xml:lang="grc">ἱκανὰ τοῖς...πολεμίοις εὐτύχηται</quote> ( <ref n="Thuc. 7.77">Thuc. 7.77</ref> ). Possibly the common impers. use <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπῆλθέ μοι λέγειν</quote> ( <quote xml:lang="eng">"it occurred to me to say"</quote> ) may have helped to suggest the impers. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπερρώσθη</quote> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λέγειν_661">. An inf., which here depends on the notion <quote xml:lang="grc"> ἐτόλμησαν</quote> , does not elsewhere occur with <quote xml:lang="grc"> ἐπιρρώννυσθαι</quote> , but stands with the simple pf. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔρρωμαι</quote> as= <quote xml:lang="eng"> " <emph>to be bent</emph> on doing"</quote>( <cit> <bibl n="Lys. 13.31" ref="Lys. 13.31">Lys. or. 13. 31</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔρρωτο...κακόν τι</quote> </cit> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐργάζεσθαι</quote>). Whitelaw: <quote xml:lang="eng">"though terrible things were emboldened to the utterance,"</quote> —comparing 658 <quote xml:lang="grc"> ἀπειλαὶ...κατηπείλησαν</quote> . But, if the <quote xml:lang="grc">δεινά</quote> are personified, do we not then want a stronger word than <quote xml:lang="grc">λέγειν</quote> ?</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἴσως_661">is fitting: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 962" ref="Soph. Aj. 962"> <title>Ai.</title> 962</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἴσως τοι, κεἰ βλέποντα μὴ )πόθουν | θανόντ’ ἂν οἰμώξειαν.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

data.quote:
ἴσως τοι, κεἰ βλέποντα μὴ )πόθουν |
									θανόντ’ ἂν οἰμώξειαν.

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κεἰ_661">here where <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ καὶ</quote> would be natural (as granting the <emph>fact</emph> ); whereas in 306 the <quote xml:lang="grc">κεἰ</quote> is normal: see <ref n="Soph. OT"> <title>O. T.</title> </ref>Append. Note 8, p. 296. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸ_δεῦρο_663">, instead of <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ μεταξύ</quote> , since <quote xml:lang="grc">πέλαγος</quote> suggests <quote xml:lang="grc">πλοῦς</quote> : cp. 1165. If the Thebans attempt an armed invasion, they will find <quote xml:lang="eng">"a sea of troubles"</quote> interposed. <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Hipp. 822" ref="Eur. Hipp. 822"> Eur. <title>Hipp.</title> 822</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="lyric"> <l>κακῶν δ’, ὦ τάλας, πέλαγος εἰσορῶ</l> <l>τοσοῦτον ὥστε μήποτ’ ἐκνεῦσαι πάλιν,</l> <l>μήτ’ ἐκπερᾶσαι κῦμα τῆσδε συμφορᾶς</l> </lg> </quote> </cit>. So of prosperity, <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 423" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg004:423"> <title>O. T.</title> 423</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">εὐπλοίας τυχών</quote> </cit>. The form <foreign xml:lang="grc">πλώσιμον</foreign> only here: Attic writers elsewhere use <quote xml:lang="grc">πλώϊμος</quote> (oft. <quote xml:lang="grc">πλόϊμος</quote> in our MSS.), Her. <quote xml:lang="grc">πλωτός</quote> : <quote xml:lang="grc">πλεύσιμος</quote> is not found. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θαρσεῖν_οὖν_664"> . <quote xml:lang="eng"> "Now ( <quote xml:lang="grc">οὖν"</quote> ) you are safe indeed ( <quote xml:lang="grc">μὲν</quote> ), even <emph> without</emph> my protection,—Phoebus being with you; but ( <quote xml:lang="grc">δὲ</quote> 666) that protection,—superfluous though it be,—will be afforded by my name just as well as by my presence.</quote>For <quote xml:lang="grc">μὲν οὖν</quote> with this distributed force cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 483"> <title>O. T.</title> 483</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Ant. 65"> <title>Ant.</title> 65</ref>; for its composite force, <ref n="Soph. OT 705"> <title>O. T.</title> 705</ref>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κἄνευ_γνώμης_664">, even apart from my resolve (636) to protect you. Though <quote xml:lang="grc">τῆς ἐμῆς</quote> form a cretic, the spondee <quote xml:lang="grc">κἄνευ</quote> can stand because the prep. coheres closely with its case. Cp. 115. In 1022 <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδὲν δεῖ πονεῖν</quote> , and 1543 <quote xml:lang="grc">ὥσπερ σφὼ πατρί</quote> , the monosyllable excuses the spondee.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐπαινῶ_664">with inf., advise: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 1322" ref="Soph. El. 1322"> <title>El.</title> 1322</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">σιγᾶν ἐπῄνες).</quote> </cit> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Φοῖβος_664">: Theseus infers this from 623. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὅμως_666">with <foreign xml:lang="grc">μὴ παρόντος</foreign> : it usu. follows the partic. (as <ref n="Soph. OC 851">851</ref> , <ref n="Soph. OC 1529">1529</ref> ), but sometimes precedes it, as <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Ion 734" ref="Eur. Ion 734"> Eur. <title>Ion</title> 734</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">δέσποιν’ ὅμως οὖσ’</quote> </cit>. It would be possible, however, to take <foreign xml:lang="grc">ὅμως</foreign> with <foreign xml:lang="grc">οἶδα</foreign> : "but nevertheless (though my protection is needless)." Possibly it should be <foreign xml:lang="grc">ὁμῶς</foreign> , "equally" ( <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 1372" ref="Soph. Aj. 1372"> <title>Ai.</title> 1372</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">κἀκεῖ κἀνθάδ’ ὢν... ὁμῶς</quote> </cit> ). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γᾶς_669">is partitive gen. with the superl., as <cit> <bibl n="Lys. 21.6" ref="Lys. 21.6">Lys. or. 21 § 6</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἡ ναῦς ἄριστα... ἔπλει παντὸς τοῦ στρατοπέδου</quote> </cit>. When <quote xml:lang="grc">γῆ</quote> stands alone it usu.= <quote xml:lang="eng">"the earth,"</quote> as <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 480" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg004:480"> <title>O. T.</title> 480</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰ μεσόμφαλα γᾶς...μαντεῖα</quote> </cit>. Some understand, less well, <quote xml:lang="eng"> "the best abodes in Attica ( <quote xml:lang="grc">γᾶς"</quote> ), belonging to (or consisting in) Colonus ( <quote xml:lang="grc">χώρας</quote> ).</quote> </gloss>

data.quote:
ἡ ναῦς ἄριστα... ἔπλει παντὸς τοῦ
									στρατοπέδου

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔπαυλα_669">, prop. a fold for cattle, as in <ref n="Soph. OT 1138"> <title>O. T.</title> 1138</ref>, where <quote xml:lang="grc">σταθμά</quote> is its synonym. So <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔπαυλοι</quote> in <ref n="Hom. Od. 23.358"> <title>Od.</title> 23.358</ref>, and <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔπαυλις</quote> in <ref n="Hdt. 1.111">Her. 1.111</ref> . Then, just like <quote xml:lang="grc">σταθμά</quote> in poetry, "homesteads," "dwellings": <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. Pers. 869" ref="Aesch.
