Pers. 869"> Aesch. <title>Pers.</title> 869</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">πάροικοι | Θρῃκίων ἐπαύλων</quote> </cit>. The form <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔπαυλις</quote> was similarly used in late prose. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸν_670">: the antistrophic syll. ( <quote xml:lang="grc">νάρκ‐</quote> 683), is long, but it is needless to write <quote xml:lang="grc">τόνδ’</quote> , since the anacrusis is common. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀργῆτα_670">, <quote xml:lang="eng">"white,"</quote> contrasting with <quote xml:lang="grc">χλωραῖς</quote> (673). See Tozer, <title>Geography of Greece</title> p. 242: <quote xml:lang="eng"> "The site of Colonus is distinguished by two bare knolls of lightcoloured earth, the <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀργῆτα Κολωνόν"</quote> of the poet,—not chalky, as the expositors of that passage often describe it to be.</quote>Schol. <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸν λευκόγεων</quote> . From √ARG, denoting <quote xml:lang="eng">"brightness,"</quote> come ( <emph>a</emph> ) the group of words for <quote xml:lang="eng">"bright"</quote> or white, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀργός, ἀργής, ἀργινόεις, ἀργεννός, ἄργυφος</quote> : ( <emph>b</emph> ) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄργυρος</quote> : ( <emph>c</emph> ) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄργιλος</quote> , <foreign xml:lang="lat">argilla</foreign> , white clay. Thus the notion of a light-coloured soil was specially associated with this root. And this was certainly one reason why places were called <quote xml:lang="eng"> "white,"</quote> —whether the soil was <emph>merely</emph> light-coloured, as at Colonus, or chalky. Pindar puts Cyrene <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν ἀργινόεντι μαστῷ</quote> ( <title>P.</title> 4. 8), and it is known to have stood on a chalk cliff (F. B. Goddard in <title>Amer. Journ. Philol.</title> v. 31 <foreign xml:lang="lat">ap.</foreign> Gildersleeve <foreign xml:lang="lat">ad loc.</foreign> ). Soil is suggested by <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀργείλοφον πὰρ Ζεφυρίων κολώναν</quote> (the town <quote xml:lang="grc">Λοκροὶ Ἐπιζεφύριοι</quote> on the S.E. coast of Italy, Pind. fr. 200); and soil or light-coloured rocks by <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἀργινοῦσαι</quote> , the three islets off the coast of Aeolis (Strabo 617). Cp. <quote xml:lang="eng">"Albion."</quote> But a town on a hill might also owe the epithet to its buildings. We cannot now decide between soil and buildings in the cases of <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸν ἀργινόεντα Λύκαστον</quote> and <quote xml:lang="grc">Κάμειρον</quote> ( <ref n="Hom. Il. 2.647"> <title>Il.</title> 2.647</ref>, 656) in central Crete (?), nor always in the case of the name <quote xml:lang="eng">"Alba."</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μινύρεται_θαμίζουσα_671">inverts the usual constr.; cp. <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Od. 8.450" ref="Hom. Od. 8.450"> <title>Od.</title> 8.450</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="hexameter"> <l>ὁ δ’ ἄρ’ ἀσπασίως ἴδε θυμῷ</l> <l>θερμὰ λοέτῤ, ἐπεὶ οὔτι κομιζόμενός γε θάμιζεν</l> </lg> </quote> </cit>, "since he was not often so cared for"; <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Rep. 328C" ref="Plat. Rep. 328C"> Plat. <title>Rep.</title> 328C</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὦ Σώκρατες, οὐδὲ θαμίζεις ἡμῖν καταβαίνων εἰς τὸν Πειραιᾶ</quote> </cit>. Here, however, <quote xml:lang="grc">θαμίζουσα</quote> may be taken separately, <quote xml:lang="eng">"frequenting"</quote> (the place): <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Il. 18.386" ref="Hom. Il. 18.386"> <title>Il.</title> 18.386</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">πάρος γε μὲν οὔτι θαμίζεις</quote> </cit> , <quote xml:lang="eng">"hitherto thou comest not oft."</quote> The midd. in fr. 460 <quote xml:lang="grc">τῷδε</quote> ( <foreign xml:lang="lat">v.l.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">τῇδε</quote> ) <quote xml:lang="grc">θαμίζεται</quote> , (the fish) haunts those waters. </gloss>

data.quote:
ὦ Σώκρατες, οὐδὲ θαμίζεις ἡμῖν
									καταβαίνων εἰς τὸν Πειραιᾶ

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="χλω_βάσσαις_673"> , <quote xml:lang="eng">"under"</quote> (screened by) green glades,—in the sacred grove (cp. 17) and in the neighbouring Academy. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 198" ref="Soph. Aj. 198"> <title>Ai.</title> 198</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν εὐανέμοις βάσσαις</quote> </cit>(Ida's glens). If the word could be referred, like <quote xml:lang="grc">βυσσός</quote> , to the rt. of <quote xml:lang="grc">βαθύς</quote> , it would be peculiarly appropriate here to the haunts of the bird that <quote xml:lang="eng">"sings darkling."</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μυριόκαρπον_675">refers to the berries of the laurel ( <cit> <quote xml:lang="grc">παγκάρπου δάφνης</quote> <bibl n="Soph. OT 83" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg004:83"> <title>O. T.</title> 83</bibl> </cit>), the fruit of the olive and of the vine. Cp. on <ref target="cl17">17</ref> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀνήνεμον_χειμώνων_677">, cp. 786, 1519: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 36" ref="Soph. El. 36"> <title>El.</title> 36</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄσκευον ἀσπίδων</quote> </cit> : <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 1002 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄλυπος ἄτης</quote> : <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Trach. 691" ref="Soph. Trach. 691"> <title>Tr.</title> 691</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλαμπὲς ἡλίου</quote> </cit> : <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Od. 6.250" ref="Hom. Od. 6.250"> <title>Od.</title> 6.250</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐδητύος...ἄπαστος</quote> </cit> : <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Phoen. 324" ref="Eur. Phoen. 324"> Eur. <title>Phoen.</title> 324</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄπεπλος φαρέων</quote> </cit>. In these poet. phrases, the gen. might be viewed either as (1) simply a gen. of want, as after <quote xml:lang="grc">καθαρός</quote> , etc.: (2) an attrib. gen. depending on the implied noun (here, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄνεμοι</quote> ). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="βακχιώτας_678">(only here)= <quote xml:lang="grc">βακχευτής, βάκχος</quote> , reveller. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 1105" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg004:1105"> <title>O. T.</title> 1105</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁ Βακχεῖος θεός</quote> </cit> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐμβατεύει_679">, haunts the ground, <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. Pers. 449" ref="Aesch. Pers. 449"> Aesch. <title>Pers.</title> 449</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">Πὰν ἐμβατεύει ποντίας ἀκτῆς ἔπι.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀμφιπολῶν_679">, properly, <quote xml:lang="eng">"moving around,"</quote> so, <quote xml:lang="eng">"attending on,"</quote> <quote xml:lang="eng">"roaming in company with."</quote> The bold use seems to have been suggested by the noun <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀμφίπολος</quote> as= <quote xml:lang="eng">"follower"</quote> (Pind., etc.), <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀμφιπολεῖν</quote> being here to that noun as <quote xml:lang="grc">ὀπαδεῖν</quote> to <quote xml:lang="grc">ὀπαδός.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="tiqhnais_679">, the nymphs of the mythical Nysa, who nurtured the infant god, and were afterwards the companions of his wanderings: <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Il. 6.132" ref="Hom. Il. 6.132"> <title>Il.</title> 6.132</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <note anchored="true" place="inline" resp="RCJ" xml:lang="eng">(lycurgus, king of thrace) </note>Διωνύσοιο τιθήνας | σεῦε κατ’ ἠγάθεον Νυσήϊον</quote> </cit> . </gloss>

data.quote: Διωνύσοιο τιθήνας | σεῦε κατ’ ἠγάθεον Νυσήϊον

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θάλλει_δ_681">. After the mention of Dionysus, the narcissus now serves to introduce a mention of Demeter and Persephone (Cora). Under the name of <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἴακχος</quote> , represented as the son of Cora (or sometimes of Demeter), Dionysus was associated in the Eleusinian mysteries with the <quote xml:lang="eng">"two goddesses"</quote> ( <quote xml:lang="grc">τὼ θεώ</quote> ): thus <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1119"> <title>Ant.</title> 1119</ref>he reigns <quote xml:lang="grc">παγκοίνοις Ἐλευσινίας | Δηοῦς ἐν κόλποις</quote> . A relief found at Eleusis in 1859, and referable to the period between Pheidias and Praxiteles, shows Persephone with her right hand on the head of the young Iacchos (a boy of some fifteen years), who is facing Demeter. It is reproduced in Baumeister's <title>Denkmäler des klass. Alterth.</title> , <foreign xml:lang="lat">s.v.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="eng">"Eleusinia,"</quote> p. 471. There was a shrine of Demeter near Colonus, 1600. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="νάρκισσος_683">. As the epithet shows, some thickly-flowering variety is meant: cp. Vergil's <cit> <quote xml:lang="lat">"comantem Narcissum,"</quote> <bibl n="Verg. G. 4.122" ref="Verg. G. 4.122"> <title>Geo.</title> 4. 122</bibl> </cit> . <ref> Wieseler ( <title>Narkissos</title> , pp. 114 ff., Gött. 1856)</ref>thinks that a lily is meant here. <ref> Bentham ( <title>British Flora</title> , 4th ed., p. 473)</ref>says that the <foreign xml:lang="lat">narcissus poeticus</foreign> of the Mediterranean region <quote xml:lang="eng"> "has usually <emph>a solitary flower</emph> of a pure white, except the crown, which is yellow, often edged with orange or crimson."</quote>This does not suit <quote xml:lang="grc">καλλίβοτρυς</quote> . There is a like doubt about the classical <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑάκινθος</quote> , variously taken as iris, gladiolus, or larkspur—at any rate, not our hyacinth. But, whatever the true identification here may be, the <emph> symbolism</emph> of <foreign xml:lang="grc">νάρκισσος</foreign> in Greek mythology is clear. It is <emph>the flower of imminent death</emph> , being associated, through its narcotic fragrance, with <quote xml:lang="grc">νάρκη</quote> ,—the pale beauty of the flower helping the thought. It is the <emph>last</emph> flower for which Persephone is stretching forth her hand when Pluto seizes her,—Earth having put forth a wondrous narcissus, with a hundred flowers, on purpose to tempt her: <cit> <bibl n="HH 5.15" ref="HH 5.15"> <title>Hom. Hymn.</title> 5.15</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="hexameter"> <l>ἡ δ’ ἄρα θαμβήσασ’ ὠρέξατο χερσὶν ἅμ’ ἄμφω</l> <l>καλὸν ἄθυρμα λαβεῖν· χάνε δὲ χθὼν εὐρυάγυια</l> </lg> </quote> </cit> . <ref n="Paus. 9.31.9">Paus. 9.31.9</ref> (quoting an ancient hymn by the legendary poet Pamphos) says that Cora was seized <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐκ ἴοις ἀπατηθεῖσαν ἀλλὰ ναρκίσσοις</quote> . So Euphorion ( <date when="-0220">220 B.C.</date> ) fr. 52 <quote xml:lang="grc">Εὐμενίδες ναρκίσσου ἐπιστεφέες πλοκαμῖδας</quote> . Artemidorus ( <date when="0160">160 A.D.</date> ), interpreting dreams of <emph>crowning the head</emph> , says, <quote xml:lang="grc">στέφανοι ναρκίσσων πεποιημένοι πᾶσι κακοί</quote> ( <ref> <title>Oneirocr.</title> I. 77</ref>). Narcissus is the fair youth cold to love, whose face seen by himself in the water was the prelude of death (cp. <ref>Artemid. 2. 7</ref> ). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μεγάλαιν_θεαῖν_683"> : <ref n="Paus. 8.31.1">Paus. 8.31.1</ref> (at Megalopolis) <quote xml:lang="grc">θεῶν ἱερὸν τῶν μεγάλων· αἱ δέ εἰσιν αἱ μεγάλαι θεαὶ Δημήτηρ καὶ Κόρη</quote> . In Attic usu. <quote xml:lang="grc">τὼ θεώ</quote> , and so <ref n="Andoc. 1.32">Andoc. or. 1 § 32</ref> (of these goddesses) <quote xml:lang="grc">πρὸς τοῖν θεοῖν</quote> is now read ( <foreign xml:lang="lat">v.l.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">ταῖν θεαῖν</quote> ). Indeed <quote xml:lang="grc"> θεά</quote> is rare in Attic prose except in such phrases as <quote xml:lang="grc">θεοὺς καὶ θεάς</quote> . But here, in a lyric passage, and with an epithet added, the poet may have preferred the less familiar <quote xml:lang="grc">θεαῖν</quote> . The schol. was wrong in desiring <quote xml:lang="grc">τᾶν μεγαλᾶν θεᾶν</quote> (meaning the Eumenides). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀρχαῖον_στεφάνωμ_684">. The narcissus does not figure <emph>specially</emph> as an attribute of the goddesses—as the corn-ears and poppy of Demeter, the pomegranate of Cora, and the myrtle of Iacchos. But, as the flower which Cora was plucking when seized, it was associated with their cult from the first ( <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀρχαῖον</quote> ), and was <emph>one</emph> of the flowers which would be most fitly woven into those floral wreaths which, on the wall-paintings, sometimes replace Demeter's more usual crown of corn-ears (see Baumeister, <title>Denkm.</title> p. 417). Hesych. says that in Crete the narcissus was called <quote xml:lang="grc">δαμάτριον</quote> . In Rhodes Cora was crowned with asphodel (Bekker <title>Anecd.</title> I. 457. 9). At Hermione a flower like the <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑάκινθος</quote> , locally called <quote xml:lang="grc">κοσμοσάνδαλον</quote> , was worn by the worshippers of Demeter Chthonia ( <ref n="Paus. 2.35.5">Paus. 2.35.5</ref> ). Schneidewin's explanation, <quote xml:lang="eng"> " <emph> original</emph> crown,"</quote>— before they changed it for others,—is against the myth itself, which makes the narcissus a <emph>new</emph> joy to Cora's eyes ( <ref n="HH 5.15"> <title>Hom. Hymn.</title> 5.15</ref>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="χρυσαυγὴς_κρόκος_685">. Tozer, <title>Geogr. of Greece</title> p. 162: <quote xml:lang="eng"> "when Sophocles... speaks of the <quote xml:lang="eng">crocus with its golden sheen,"</quote> we would fain regard this as the same with the splendid flower that displays its golden blossoms close to the snow on Parnassus and the mountains of Arcadia. But, in reality, there can be little doubt that it was the cultivated crocus, from which the saffron was obtained, and which was introduced into Greece from the East, where it was prized as a dye for robes and slippers,— the <quote xml:lang="grc">κροκόβαπτον ποδὸς εὔμαριν</quote> of the <title> Persae</title> [660]—the sign of royalty and majesty.</quote>Cp. <cit> <bibl n="HH 4.177" ref="HH 4.177"> <title>Hom. Hymn.</title> 4.177</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="hexameter"> <l> <note anchored="true" place="inline" resp="RCJ" xml:lang="eng">(of fair maidens) </note>ἀμφὶ δὲ χαῖται</l> <l>ὤμοις ἀΐσσοντο κροκηΐῳ ἄνθει ὁμοῖαι</l> </lg> </quote> </cit>. Along with roses, violets, "hyacinth," "narcissus," and "agallis" (iris?), the "crocus" is gathered by Cora ( <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 6 ff.). Schol. <quote xml:lang="grc">κἀν τῇ Νιοβῃ ὁ Σοφοκλῆς τὸν κρόκον ἄντικρυς τῇ Δήμητρι ἀνατίθεται</quote> . At the Thesmophoria (the festival of Demeter <quote xml:lang="grc">θεσμοφόρος</quote> ), when wreaths of flowers were not worn (schol.), the women appeared in <quote xml:lang="grc">κροκωτοί</quote> , saffroncoloured robes ( <ref n="Aristoph. Thes. 138"> Ar. <title>Thesm.</title> 138</ref>). The crocus was planted on graves ( <ref n="Juv. 7.208"> Juv. <title>Sat.</title> 7. 208</ref>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κρῆναι_686">, the <quote xml:lang="eng">"founts."</quote> <quote xml:lang="eng">"The most distant sources of the river are on the w. side of Mt. Pentelicus and the s. side of Mt. Parnes, and in the intermediate ridge which unites them"</quote> (Leake): in particular, a broad stream descends from the steepest part of Parnes. The Cephisus has a course of about 20 miles to the bay of Phalerum. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μινύθουσιν_686">. Soph. has seized a distinctive point. Even at this day, when the plain has much less shade than of old, the <title>Cephisus</title> <quote xml:lang="eng">"never fails,"</quote> while in the long droughts of summer the bed of the <title>Ilissus</title> is absolutely dry. Cp. <title>Modern Greece</title> by H. M. Baird (1856) p. 294: <quote xml:lang="eng">"The little river Cephisus...scatters fertility and verdure around. Great was the contrast between its banks and the rest of the plain, which in the month of October is dry, parched, and dusty. The whole valley, in its width of six miles, had been stripped of nearly every vestige of vegetation; for not a drop of water had fallen during the previous four or five months."</quote> — <quote xml:lang="grc">μινύθω</quote> is both trans. and intrans. in Homer; intrans. in the Ionic of Hippocr. (who has it of flesh <quote xml:lang="eng">"wasting"</quote> ). Aesch. has it twice in lyrics (intrans.); Soph. only here. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Κηφισοῦ_687">. Chr. Wordsworth ( <title>Athens and Attica</title> p. 137) observes that the Athenian poets never praise the <title>Ilissus</title> (perhaps because it was too much associated with the prose of daily life), though Plato, in the <title>Phaedrus</title> , makes some amends; they keep their praises for the Cephisus (so <ref n="Eur. Med. 835"> Eur. <title> Med.</title> 835</ref>). On the other hand the Ilissus, not the Cephisus, is the representative river of Attica for more distant singers, from Apollonius Rhodius ( <ref n="Apollon. 1.215">I. 215</ref> ) to Milton ( <ref> <title>Par. Reg.</title> 4. 249</ref>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="νομάδες_687">, wandering. The word <emph>alludes</emph> to irrigation by ducts or canals (a system still in use), but does so far more poetically than would be the case if (with E. Curtius) we made it active, with <foreign xml:lang="grc">ῥεέθρων</foreign> for object. gen., <quote xml:lang="eng">"distributing the streams."</quote> There is no example of an adj. of this form (as <quote xml:lang="grc">σποράς, στροφάς, φορβάς</quote> ) having an active sense. Cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 1350"> <title>O. T.</title> 1350</ref>n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐπ_ἤματι_688">, a very rare use in Attic, meaning here that <emph>on</emph> (or <emph> for</emph> ) each day the river gives what that day requires. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Il. 10.48" ref="Hom. Il. 10.48"> <title>Il.</title> 10.48</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <note anchored="true" place="inline" resp="RCJ" xml:lang="eng">(never did i hear)</note>ἄνδρ’ ἕνα τοσσάδε μέρμερ’ ἐπ’ ἤματι μητίσασθαι <note anchored="true" place="inline" resp="RCJ" xml:lang="eng">(as one day's work)</note> </quote> </cit>: more oft. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπ’ ἤματι τῷδε</quote> , "on this day," <ref n="Hom. Il. 13.234"> <title>Il.</title> 13.234</ref>, <ref n="Hom. Il. 19.110">19. 110</ref> . Herodotus has the gen. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπ’ ἡμέρης ἑκάστης</quote> in a similar sense <ref n="Hdt. 5.117">(5. 117)</ref> ; this phrase, too, is un-Attic. </gloss>

data.quote: ἄνδρ’ ἕνα τοσσάδε μέρμερ’ ἐπ’ ἤματι μητίσασθαι

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὠκυτόκος_689">, giving an early reward to the cultivator's labour. Cp. <quote xml:lang="grc">ὠκυτόκοιο Σελάνας</quote> (because thought <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπὶ ταῖς λοχείαις καὶ ὠδῖσι βοηθεῖν</quote> ), <cit> <bibl> poet. <foreign xml:lang="lat">ap.</foreign> Plut. <title>Mor.</title> 282 C.</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὠκυτόκιον</quote> </cit>, a medicine used in childbed, <ref n="Aristoph. Thes. 504"> Ar. <title> Th.</title> 504</ref>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πεδίων_ἐπινίσσεται_689">, a partitive gen. (helped by <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπι‐</quote> ), cp. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔρχονται πεδίοιο</quote> , <ref n="Hom. Il. 2.801"> <title>Il.</title> 2.801</ref>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὄμβρῳ_690">, water: see on <ref n="Soph. OT 1427"> <title>O. T.</title> 1427</ref>. ( <title>Not</title> , <quote xml:lang="eng">"with the help of rain."</quote> ) </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="στ_χθονός_691">, possessive gen. with <quote xml:lang="grc">πεδίων.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="στερνούχου_691">, having <quote xml:lang="grc">στέρνα</quote> : an expressive word for the expanse of the Attic <quote xml:lang="grc">πεδίον</quote> , varied by gentle undulations, or by rocky knolls like Colonus itself. Suidas quotes a poet. phrase <quote xml:lang="grc">στέρνα γῆς</quote> : cp. the common use of <quote xml:lang="grc">μαστοί</quote> for round hills or knolls. <cit> <bibl n="Hes. Th. 117" ref="Hes. Th. 117"> Hes. <title>Theog.</title> 117</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">Γαἶ εὐρύστερνος</quote> </cit> : <cit> <bibl n="Pind. N. 7" ref="Pind. N. 7"> Pind. <title>Nem.</title> 7.33</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="lyric"> <l>εὐρυκόλπου</l> <l>...χθονός</l> </lg> </quote> </cit>. Both <quote xml:lang="grc">στέρνα</quote> and <quote xml:lang="grc">νῶτα</quote> were applied, says the schol., to <quote xml:lang="grc">τῆς γῆς τὰ πεδιώδη καὶ εὐρέα</quote> . The epithet helps, with <quote xml:lang="grc">ὠκυτόκος</quote> , to suggest the image of a mighty living frame, quickened by the veins of irrigation. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Μουσᾶν_691">. <ref n="Paus. 1.30.2">Paus. 1.30.2</ref> (in the Academy, cp. on <ref target="cl55">55</ref> ) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔστι δε καὶ Μουσῶν τε βωμὸς καὶ ἕτερος Ἑρμοῦ καὶ ἔνδον Ἀθηνᾶς</quote> .</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="νιν_692">refers to <quote xml:lang="grc">χθονός</quote> in 691: this region generally. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐδ_ἁ_692">. The <foreign xml:lang="grc">οὐδ’ αὖ</foreign> of L is somewhat prosaic, and implies a contrast between the deities which is unfitting here.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Ἀφροδίτη_692">is not among the divinities of the Academy or Colonus in <ref n="Paus. 1.30">Paus. 1. 30</ref> , though there was an altar of <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἔρως</quote> in front of the entrance to the Academy. But she was often associated with Demeter and Cora (cp. <ref n="Paus. 3.19.4">Paus. 3.19.4</ref> , and Baumeister <title> Denkmäler</title> p. 419); and she was also specially connected by an Attic legend with the Cephisus ( <ref n="Eur. Med. 835"> Eur. <title> Med.</title> 835</ref>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="χρυσάνιος_692">, when she drives her chariot drawn by sparrows (Sappho fr. 1. 10), doves, or swans. The word occurs only once in <title>Il.</title> (6. 205), as epith. of Artemis, and once in <title>Od.</title> (8. 285), as epith. of Ares. <ref n="Paus. 9.23.4">Paus. 9.23.4</ref> (speaking of a lost hymn by Pindar to Persephone) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄλλαι τε ἐς τὸν ᾍδην εἰσὶν ἐπικλήσεις καὶ ὁ χρυσήνιος, δῆλα ὡς ἐπὶ τῆς Κόρης τῇ ἁρπαγῇ</quote> . So, here, the epith. suggests a visit of the goddess from above.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γᾶς_Ἀσίας_694"> , <foreign xml:lang="lat">sc.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὄν</quote> , possessive gen., with <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπακούω</quote> , hear of as belonging to. The poet does not mean, of course, that he has never heard of the olive as <emph>growing</emph> in the Peloponnesus or in Asia Minor. It is enough to recall the <quote xml:lang="grc">στρεπτῆς καλουμένης ἐλαίας φυτόν</quote> of Epidaurus (said to have been twisted by Heracles, <ref n="Paus. 2.28.2">Paus. 2.28.2</ref> ), and the speculation of Thales in the olive-oil presses of Miletus and Chios ( <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐλαιουργεῖα</quote> , <ref n="Aristot. Pol. 1.1259a"> Arist. <title>Pol.</title> 1.11</ref>). He means that nowhere else has he heard of an olive-tree springing from the earth at a divine command, or flourishing so greatly and so securely under divine protection. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Δωρίδι_695">, as Schneidewin remarked, is an anachronism (cp. 1301), since legend placed Oedipus before the Trojan war, and the Dorian conquest of the Peloponnesus after it; but Attic tragedy was not fastidious on such points. In <ref n="Eur. Hec. 450"> Eur. <title>Hec.</title> 450</ref>the Peloponnesus is <quote xml:lang="grc">Δωρὶς αἶα</quote> . Cp. on <ref target="cl66">66</ref> .</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="νάσῳ_695">: cp. Eust. <foreign xml:lang="lat">ad</foreign> Dion. Perieg. 403 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἡ τοῦ Πέλοπος νῆσος ἔστι μὲν κυρίως Χερρόνησος, ὅμως δὲ νῆσος μὲν λέγεται, ὡς παρὰ βραχὺ τοιαύτη οὖσα</quote> . In the 10th century we find the Peloponnesus called simply <quote xml:lang="grc">ἡ νῆσος</quote> by Constantinus Porphyrogenitus, <quote xml:lang="grc">περὶ τῶν Θεμάτων</quote> ( <quote xml:lang="eng">"the provinces"</quote> ) p. 52 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔστι δὲ πᾶσα ἡ νῆσος ὑπὸ ἑνὶ στρατηγῷ τεταγμένη</quote> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Πέλοπος_695">has been regarded by some as a gloss: see on <ref target="cl709">709</ref> f. But, apart from the fact that 709 f. are shorter by <foreign xml:lang="meter">uu</foreign> , it need move no suspicion; for, if not necessary here, it is at least fitting, and is often joined with <quote xml:lang="grc">νῆσος</quote> . Tyrtaeus fr. 2 <quote xml:lang="grc">εὐρεῖαν Πέλοπος νᾶσον ἀφικόμεθα</quote> . <cit> <bibl> <title>Cypria</title> fr. 8</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">διεδέρκετο νῆσον ἅπασαν | Τανταλίδεω Πέλοπος</quote> </cit>. Ion <title> Omphale</title> fr. 24 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄμεινον ἢ τὸν Πέλοπος ἐν νήσῳ τρόπον</quote> . — Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. Eum. 702" ref="Aesch. Eum. 702"> Aesch. <title>Eum.</title> 702</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="trimeter"> <l> <note anchored="true" place="inline" resp="RCJ" xml:lang="eng">(the areiopagus is a safeguard) </note>οἷον οὔτις ἀνθρώπων ἔχει</l> <l>οὔτ’ ἐν Σκύθαισιν οὔτε Πέλοπος ἐν τόποις</l> </lg> </quote> </cit> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φύτευμ_698">. <quote xml:lang="grc">φίτευμ’</quote> , which Blaydes prefers, occurs only once in trag. ( <ref n="Aesch. Ag. 1281"> Aesch. <title>Ag.</title> 1281</ref>, of Orestes); it seems more appropriate to a <quote xml:lang="eng">"scion"</quote> (child) than to a plant. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀχείρωτον_698">was read here by Pollux (2. 154), and is thus carried back to about <date when="0160">160 A.D.</date> ; it is also in A and a majority of our other MSS.; while L's <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀχείρητον</quote> is clearly a corruption. The question is whether <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἀχείρωτον</foreign> means (1) <quote xml:lang="eng"> " <emph>unvanquished</emph> ,"</quote>the only sense in which it occurs elsewhere, as <cit> <bibl n="Thuc. 6.10" ref="Thuc. 6.10">Thuc. 6.10</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οἱ Χαλκιδῆς...ἀχείρωτοί εἰσι</quote> </cit>: or (2) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀχειρούργητον</quote> , as Pollux takes it, <quote xml:lang="eng"> " <emph>not cultivated by human hands.</emph> "</quote> <quote xml:lang="grc">χείρωμα</quote>usu. meant <quote xml:lang="eng">"a conquest,"</quote> or <quote xml:lang="eng">"a violent deed"</quote> ; yet Aesch. could say <quote xml:lang="grc">τυμβοχόα χειρώματα</quote> (work of the hand in mound-making) <ref n="Aesch. Seven 1022"> <title>Theb.</title> 1022</ref>. A bold artist in language might similarly, perhaps, have ventured on <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀχείρωτος</quote> as = <quote xml:lang="eng">"not hand-wrought."</quote> My reason for preferring <quote xml:lang="eng"> " <emph>unvanquished</emph> "</quote>is the context. While <foreign xml:lang="grc">βλαστόν</foreign> (697) refers to the miraculous <emph>creation</emph> of the olive by Athena, <foreign xml:lang="grc">αὐτοποιόν</foreign> refers (I think) to its miraculous <emph>self-renewal</emph> after the Persians had burnt it. <cit> <bibl n="Hdt. 8.55" ref="Hdt. 8.55">Her. 8.55</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">δευτέρῃ τε ἡμέρῃ ἀπὸ τῆς ἐμπρήσιος Ἀθηναίων οἱ θύειν ὑπὸ βασιλέος κελευόμενοι ὡς ἀνέβησαν ἐς τὸ ἱρόν, ὥρων βλαστὸν ἐκ τοῦ στελέχεος ὅσον τε πηχυαῖον ἀναδεδραμηκότα</quote> </cit>. This connection of ideas is further indicated by the next phrase, <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἐγχέων</foreign> etc. For <foreign xml:lang="grc"> αὐτοποιός</foreign> as <quote xml:lang="eng">"self-produced"</quote> ( <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> producing itself from itself) cp. <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐτοτόκος, αὐτοφάγος, αὐτοφόνος</quote> . Chandler ( <title>Accent.</title> § 457 2nd ed.) remarks that all compounds of <quote xml:lang="grc">‐ποιος</quote> are oxytone (quoting Arcadius 88. 2): <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐτόποιος</quote> (as our MSS. give it) in this passage <quote xml:lang="eng">"is the one solitary exception, and therefore probably a false accent."</quote> </gloss>

data.quote:
δευτέρῃ τε ἡμέρῃ ἀπὸ τῆς ἐμπρήσιος
									Ἀθηναίων οἱ θύειν ὑπὸ βασιλέος κελευόμενοι ὡς ἀνέβησαν
									ἐς τὸ ἱρόν, ὥρων βλαστὸν ἐκ τοῦ στελέχεος ὅσον τε
									πηχυαῖον ἀναδεδραμηκότα

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φόβημα_699">. Androtion (circ. 280 B.C.), in his <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἀτθίς</quote> , stated that the sacred olives ( <quote xml:lang="grc">μορίαι</quote> ) in Attica had been spared by the Peloponnesian invaders under Archidamus, who sacrificed to Athena. The <title>Atthis</title> of Philochorus, a contemporary of Androtion, made the same statement (schol. <foreign xml:lang="lat">ad loc.</foreign> ).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τᾷδε_χώρᾳ_700">, locative dat.: in Attica.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μέγιστα_700">: cp. 219 <quote xml:lang="grc">μακρά</quote> , 319 <quote xml:lang="grc"> φαιδρά</quote> , <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 883" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg004:883"> <title>O. T.</title> 883</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑπέροπτα</quote> </cit>n. The light soil of Attica ( <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ λεπτόγεων</quote> ), and the climate, esp. favoured the olive: cp. Theophr. <title>Causs. Plantt.</title> 2. 4. 4 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἡ σπιλὰς</quote> (stony ground) <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ ἔτι μᾶλλον ἡ λευκόγειος</quote> (670 n.) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐλαιοφόρος</quote> . For Greece, the olive-zone begins s. of the plains of Thessaly, as for Italy it begins s. of the plains of Lombardy. The olive is found in Phthiotis and Magnesia: in Epeirus, only on the sea-coast. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="παιδοτρόφου_701">, nourishing the young lives in the land. The epithet is especially fitting here, after the recent allusion to Demeter and Cora, because at the Thesmophoria the prayer to those goddesses associated Earth with them as <quote xml:lang="grc">ἡ κουροτρόφος</quote> : see <ref n="Aristoph. Thes. 295"> Ar. <title>Thesm.</title> 295</ref>. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Juv. 3.84" ref="Juv. 3.84"> Juv. <title>Sat.</title> 3. 84</bibl> <quote xml:lang="lat">quod nostra infantia caelum Hausit Aventini, baca nutrita Sabina</quote> </cit>(the olive). Hesych. ( <foreign xml:lang="lat">s.v.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">στέφανον ἐκφέρειν</quote> ) says that it was the Attic custom <quote xml:lang="grc">στέφανον ἐλαίας τιθέναι πρὸ τῶν θυρῶν</quote> , when a male child was born; as wool, when a female (cp. <foreign xml:lang="lat">foribus suspende coronas: Iam pater es</foreign> ). But there is no such allusion here. Nor could <quote xml:lang="grc">παιδοτρ</quote> . mean <quote xml:lang="eng">"propagated from the parent olive"</quote> on the acropolis, as Schneidewin thought. </gloss>

data.quote:
quod nostra infantia caelum Hausit
									Aventini, baca nutrita Sabina

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸ_τις_702"> <quote xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</quote>Two points first claim notice. (1) <foreign xml:lang="grc">οὔτε</foreign> and <foreign xml:lang="grc">νεαρός</foreign> are both in the MSS., but both cannot be right. Cp. v. 715. If with Porson the first <quote xml:lang="grc">οὔτε</quote> is changed to <foreign xml:lang="grc">οὐ</foreign> , the second <quote xml:lang="grc">οὔτε</quote> must certainly be changed to <foreign xml:lang="grc">οὐδέ</foreign> . A single <quote xml:lang="grc">οὔτε</quote> cannot follow <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ</quote> , though a repeated <quote xml:lang="grc"> οὔτε</quote> can ( <ref n="Soph. Trach. 1058"> <title>Tr.</title> 1058</ref>). Elmsley's <quote xml:lang="grc">οὔτε νέος</quote> is hardly probable. (2) <foreign xml:lang="grc">γήρᾳ σημαίνων</foreign> seems to me impossible. It surely could not mean either ( <emph>a</emph> ) <quote xml:lang="eng">"commanding in old age"</quote> —the elderly Archidamus in contrast with the young Xerxes— or ( <emph>b</emph> ) <quote xml:lang="eng">"commanding the elderly men."</quote> The difficulty is not in the sense of</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σημαίνων_702">itself, for which cp. <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Il. 1.288" ref="Hom. Il. 1.288"> <title>Il.</title> 1.288</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="hexameter"> <l>πάντων μὲν κρατέειν ἐθέλει, πάντεσσι δ’ ἀνάσσειν,</l> <l>πᾶσι δὲ σημαίνειν</l> </lg> </quote> </cit>,—he would be master, king, captain ( <quote xml:lang="grc">σημάντωρ</quote> ): it is in the combination with <foreign xml:lang="grc">γήρᾳ</foreign> .</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κύκλος_704">, the eye of Zeus (so <quote xml:lang="grc">κύκλοι</quote> , <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1354"> <title>Ph.</title> 1354</ref>), not the <quote xml:lang="eng">"orb"</quote> of the sun. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Μορίου_Διός_705"> . <title>Attic Orators</title> , vol. I. p. 289: <quote xml:lang="eng"> "Throughout Attica, besides the olives which were private property ( <quote xml:lang="grc">ἴδιαι ἐλαῖαι"</quote> , <ref n="Lys. 7.10">Lys. or. 7 § 10</ref> ) there were others which, whether on public or on private lands, were considered as the property of the state. They were called <foreign xml:lang="lat">moriae</foreign> ( <quote xml:lang="grc"> μορίαι</quote> )—the legend being that they had been propagated ( <quote xml:lang="grc">μεμορημέναι</quote> ) from the original olive which Athena herself had caused to spring up on the Acropolis. This theory was convenient for their conservation as State property, since, by giving them a sacred character, it placed them directly under the care of the Areiopagus, which caused them to be visited once a month by Inspectors ( <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπιμεληταί</quote> , <ref n="Lys. 7.29">Lys. or. 7 § 29</ref> ), and once a year by special Commissioners ( <quote xml:lang="grc">γνώμονες</quote> , <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> § 25). To uproot a <foreign xml:lang="lat">moria</foreign> was an offence punishable by banishment and confiscation of goods ( <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> § 41).</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Μορίου_705">, from the objects protected; so <quote xml:lang="grc">Ζεὺς ἱκέσιος, κτήσιος</quote> , etc. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γλαυκῶπις_706">, with grayish-blue eyes: the Homeric epithet has been suggested by <quote xml:lang="grc">γλαυκᾶς</quote> in 701. The altar of <quote xml:lang="grc">Ζεὺς Μόριος</quote> , otherwise called <quote xml:lang="grc">Καταιβάτης</quote> , was in the Academy, where there was also a shrine of Athena close to the <quote xml:lang="grc">μορίαι</quote> (Apollodorus <foreign xml:lang="lat">ap.</foreign> schol.); hence there was a special reason for the conjunction of the deities here. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ματρόπολει_707"> , <quote xml:lang="eng">"mother-city"</quote> (Athens), since the men of Colonus, like all other dwellers in Attica, may deem themselves her children. So <cit> <bibl n="Pind. N. 5" ref="Pind. N. 5"> Pind. <title>Nem.</title> 5.8</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">Αἰακίδας ...ματρόπολίν τε</quote> </cit>, their native state (Aegina): <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Ant. 1122" ref="Soph. Ant. 1122"> <title>Ant.</title> 1122</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">Βακχᾶν ματρόπολιν Θήβαν</quote> </cit>(with allusion to Semelè). <title>Not</title> , <quote xml:lang="eng">"capital city,"</quote> which would be prosaic: this sense occurs as early, however, as <cit> <bibl n="Xen. Anab. 5.2.3" ref="Xen. Anab. 5.2.3"> Xen., <title>Anab.</title> 5. 2. 3</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἒν δὲ ἦν χωρίον μητρόπολις αὐτῶν</quote> </cit>. </gloss>

data.quote:
ἒν δὲ ἦν χωρίον μητρόπολις
									αὐτῶν

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εὔιππον_εὔπωλον_711">harmonizes with a strain of feeling which pervades the ode,—that the bounty of the gods to Attica is continued from day to day and from age to age. The supply of good <quote xml:lang="grc">ἵπποι</quote> is perpetually replenished by good <quote xml:lang="grc">πῶλοι</quote> : <quote xml:lang="lat">"est in equis patrum Virtus."</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εὔιππον_711"> further <emph>suggests</emph> <quote xml:lang="grc"> ἱππεῖς</quote> , since (as = <quote xml:lang="eng">"well-horsed"</quote> ) it is often said of heroes ( <cit> <bibl n="Pind. O. 3" ref="Pind. O. 3"> Pind. <title>Ol.</title> 3.39</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">εὐ. Τυνδαριδᾶν</quote> </cit>). The Boeotian Orchomenus is <quote xml:lang="grc">καλλίπωλος</quote> , <ref n="Pind. O. 14"> Pind. <title>Ol.</title> 14.2</ref>. For <foreign xml:lang="grc">αὔχημα εὔιππον</foreign> , a glory consisting in good horses, cp. 1062, <cit> <bibl n="Pind. O. 3" ref="Pind. O. 3"> Pind. <title>Ol.</title> 3.37</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ῥιμφαρμάτου | διφρηλασίας</quote> </cit> : <cit> <bibl n="Pind. P. 8" ref="Pind. P. 8"> <title>P.</title> 8. 37</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">νίκαν...θρασύγυιον</quote> </cit> : <cit> <bibl n="Pind. I. 1" ref="Pind. I. 1"> <title>Isth.</title> 1. 12</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">καλλίνικον...κῦδος</quote> </cit> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εὐθάλασσον_711">. The well of salt water shown in the Erechtheum ( <cit> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὕδωρ θαλάσσιον ἐν φρέατι</quote> <bibl n="Paus. 1.26.5" ref="Paus. 1.26.5">Paus. I. 26. 5</bibl> </cit>) was called <quote xml:lang="grc">θάλασσα</quote> . It was said to have been created by a blow from Poseidon's trident; the three holes which were shown are still visible (see Penrose's drawing and description in Smith's <title>Dict. Geog.</title> 1. 279 b). <cit> <bibl n="Hdt. 8.55" ref="Hdt. 8.55">Her. 8.55</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἐρεχθέος...νηός, ἐν τῷ ἐλαίη τε καὶ θάλασσα ἔνι</quote> </cit>. <ref n="Apollod. 3.14.1">Apollod. 3. 14. 1</ref> (Poseidon) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνέφηνε θάλασσαν ἣν νῦν Ἐρεχηΐδα καλοῦσι.</quote> </gloss>

data.quote:
Ἐρεχθέος...νηός, ἐν τῷ ἐλαίη τε καὶ
									θάλασσα ἔνι

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εὔιππον_εὐθάλασσον_711">are brought close together as expressing the two great attributes of Poseidon, <cit> <bibl n="HH 22" ref="HH 22"> <title>Hom. Hymn.</title> 22.4</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="hexameter"> <l>διχθά τοι, Ἐννοσίγαιε, θεοὶ τιμὴν ἐδάσαντο,</l> <l>ἵππων τε δμητῆρ’ ἔμεναι σωτῆρά τε νηῶν</l> </lg> </quote> </cit> : <cit> <bibl n="Aristoph. Kn. 551" ref="Aristoph. Kn. 551"> Aristoph. <title>Eq.</title> 551</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="lyric"> <l>ἵππῑ ἄναξ Πόσειδον, ᾧ</l> <l>χαλκοκρότων ἵππων κτύπος</l> <l>… ἁνδάνει,</l> <l>καὶ κυανέμβολοι θοαὶ</l> <l>μισθοφόροι τριήρεις</l> </lg> </quote> </cit> . </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σὺ_γάρ_712">, after the voc.: cp. <quote xml:lang="grc">σὺ δέ</quote> (507). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἷσας_713">( <quote xml:lang="grc">ἵζω</quote> ) <quote xml:lang="grc">νιν εἰς τόδ’ αὔχημα</quote> , didst establish her in this glory, as in a royal throne: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Hdt. 3.61" ref="Hdt. 3.61">Her. 3.61</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῦτον... εἷσε ἄγων ἐς τὸν βασιλήιον θρόνον</quote> </cit>. The phrase is Homeric, <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Od. 1.130" ref="Hom. Od. 1.130"> <title>Od.</title> 1.130</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐτὴν δ’ ἐς θρόνον εἷσεν ἄγων</quote> </cit> . </gloss>

data.quote:
τοῦτον... εἷσε ἄγων ἐς τὸν βασιλήιον
									θρόνον
αὐτὴν δ’ ἐς θρόνον εἷσεν ἄγων

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἵπποισιν_714"> with <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸν ἀκεστῆρα</quote> : cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 1166" ref="Soph. Aj. 1166"> <title>Ai.</title> 1166</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">βροτοῖς τὸν ἀείμνηστον | τάφον.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀκεστῆρα_714"> = <quote xml:lang="grc">σωφρονιστήν</quote> , healing their <quote xml:lang="grc">μανιάδες νόσοι</quote> , and bringing them to a calm temper ( <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Il. 13.115" ref="Hom. Il. 13.115"> <title>Il.</title> 13.115</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλ’ ἀκεώμεθα θᾶσσον· ἀκεσταί τοι φρένες ἐσθλῶν</quote> </cit>): cp. Athen. 627 E (music is introduced at banquets) <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅπως ἕκαστος τῶν εἰς μέθην καὶ πλήρωσιν ὡρμημένων ἰατρὸν λαμβάνῃ τῆς ὕβρεως καὶ τῆς ἀκοσμίας τὴν μουσικήν</quote> . <cit> <bibl n="Pind. O. 13" ref="Pind. O. 13"> Pind. <title>Ol.</title> 13.68</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">φίλτρον τόδ’ ἵππειον</quote> </cit>, 85 <quote xml:lang="grc">φάρμακον πραΰ</quote> , said of the bit ( <quote xml:lang="grc">χαλινός</quote> ) given by Athena to Bellerophon for Pegasus. </gloss>

data.quote:
ἀλλ’ ἀκεώμεθα θᾶσσον· ἀκεσταί τοι
									φρένες ἐσθλῶν

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πρώταισι_ἀγυιαῖς_715">, first in these roads (about Colonus); locative dat.:</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κτίσας_715">, <quote xml:lang="eng">"having instituted,"</quote> brought into use among men, as one could say <quote xml:lang="grc">κτίζειν νὁμιμα</quote> on the analogy of <quote xml:lang="grc">κτίζειν ἑορτήν</quote> etc. Greek mythology places Poseidon in two distinct relations to the horse. ( <emph> a</emph> ) As <emph>creator.</emph> <cit> <bibl n="Serv. G. 1.12" ref="Serv. G. 1.12"> Servius <foreign xml:lang="lat">ad</foreign> Verg. <title>Geo.</title> 1. 12</bibl> <quote xml:lang="lat">ideo dicitur ecum invenisse quia velox est eius numen et mobile sicut mare.</quote> </cit>(So waves on a rough sea are <quote xml:lang="eng">"white horses,"</quote> Ital. <foreign xml:lang="ita">cavalloni.</foreign> ) The Thessalians connected this myth with the cult of Poseidon <quote xml:lang="grc">Πετραῖος</quote> , who had caused the first horse ( <quote xml:lang="grc">Σκύφιος</quote> ) to spring from a rock in Thessaly,—the name being taken from <quote xml:lang="grc">σκύφος</quote> , a rocky cup, where perh. marks in the rock were shown. From Tzetzes on Lycophron 767 it seems that this legend was in later times localised at Colonus also. Arcadia and Boeotia, too, had their legends, in which the first horse was called <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἀρίων</quote> (the wondrous steed of Adrastus in <ref n="Hom. Il. 23.346"> <title>Il.</title> 23.346</ref>). ( <emph>b</emph> ) As <emph> tamer.</emph> This was the prominent trait of the Corinthian and Attic legends. At Corinth Poseidon was worshipped as <quote xml:lang="grc"> δαμαῖος</quote> , and Athena as <quote xml:lang="grc">χαλινῖτις</quote> (cp. <ref n="Pind. O. 13"> Pind. <title>Ol.</title> 13.65</ref>ff.). In Thessaly the horse- <emph>yoking</emph> Poseidon was called <quote xml:lang="grc">ἴμψιος</quote> : Hesych. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἴμψας· ζεύξας Θετταλοί, ἴμψιος Ποσειδῶν ὁ ζύγιος</quote> . In <ref n="Aesch. PB 462"> Aesch. <title>PV</title> 462</ref>ff. Prometheus is the first who taught men to drive animals,— <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑφ’ ἅρμα τ’ ἤγαγον φιληνίους | ἵππους</quote> . </gloss>

data.quote:
ideo dicitur ecum invenisse quia velox
									est eius numen et mobile sicut mare.

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εὐήρετμος_716">, adj. compounded with a noun cognate in sense to the subst. ( <foreign xml:lang="grc">πλάτα</foreign> ): cp. <quote xml:lang="grc">βίος μακραίων</quote> ( <ref n="Soph. OT 518"> <title>O. T.</title> 518</ref>n.), <quote xml:lang="grc">λογος κακόθρους</quote> ( <ref n="Soph. Aj. 138"> <title>Ai.</title> 138</ref>), <quote xml:lang="grc">εὔπαις γόνος</quote> ( <ref n="Eur. IT 1234"> Eur. <title>I. T.</title> 1234</ref>), <quote xml:lang="grc">εὐπήχεις χεῖρες</quote> ( <ref n="Eur. Hipp. 200"> <title>Hipp.</title> 200</ref>).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔκπαγλα_716">, neut. plur. as adv., cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 319">319.</ref> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἁλία_716">with <foreign xml:lang="grc">θρῴσκει</foreign> : cp. on <ref target="cl119">119</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκτόπιος</quote> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τῶν_Νηρῄδων_718">, the Nereids with their hundred feet, the fifty Nereids whose dance and song lead the ship on her way. (But in <cit> <bibl n="Pind. Fr. 122" ref="Pind. Fr. 122">Pind. fr. 122</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">κορᾶν ἀγέλαν ἑκατόγγυιον</quote> </cit>prob. denotes 100, not 50, persons, as though <quote xml:lang="grc">γυῖον</quote> were <quote xml:lang="grc">σῶμα</quote> .) The choice of the number (though here meant merely to suggest a <emph>numerous</emph> sisterhood) is not accidental: <emph>fifty</emph> was the number regularly assigned to the Nereids by the earlier Greek poets, as <ref n="Hes. Th. 264"> Hesiod <title>Th.</title> 264</ref>, <ref n="Pind. I. 5"> Pindar <title> Isthm.</title> 5. 6</ref>, <ref n="Aesch. Fr. 168">Aesch. Fr. 168</ref> , <ref n="Eur. Ion 1081"> Eur. <title>Ion</title> 1081</ref>. Later it becomes a hundred; so <ref n="Plat. Criti. 116E"> Plat. <title>Critias</title> 116E</ref>(describing Poseidon's temple in the island of Atlantis) <quote xml:lang="grc">Νηρῇδας δὲ ἐπὶ δελφίνων ἑκατὸν κύκλῳ· τοσαύτας γὰρ ἐνόμιζον αὐτὰς οἱ τότε εἶναι</quote> ; and so <ref n="Ov. Fast. 6.499"> Ovid <title>Fasti</title> 6. 499</ref>. <quote xml:lang="grc">Νηρεύς</quote> ( <quote xml:lang="grc">√νυ, νέω, νᾶμα</quote> , etc.) and his daughters represent the sea's kindly moods: the Nereids who dance and sing around and before the ship are the waves. In <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἑκατομπόδων</foreign> the second part of the compound suggests <quote xml:lang="eng">"dancing,"</quote> cp. on <quote xml:lang="grc">πυκνόπτεροι</quote> (17). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σὸν_δὴ_721">is more poetical and more impressive than <foreign xml:lang="grc">σοὶ … δεῖ</foreign> : cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 197">197</ref> , <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 1470" ref="Soph. El. 1470"> <title>El.</title> 1470</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="trimeter"> <l>οὐκ ἐμὸν τόδ’, ἀλλὰ σόν,</l> <l>τὸ ταῦθ’ ὁρᾶν</l> </lg> </quote> </cit> : <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Phil. 15" ref="Soph. Phil. 15"> <title>Ph.</title> 15</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλ’ ἔργον ἤδη σὸν τὰ λοίφ’ ὑπηρετεῖν</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. Seven 232" ref="Aesch. Seven 232"> Aesch. <title>Theb.</title> 232</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">σὸν δ’ αὖ τὸ σιγᾶν</quote> </cit>. But <quote xml:lang="grc">σοὶ...δεῖ</quote> , though a rare, is an admissible construction; besides <ref n="Eur. Hipp. 940"> Eur. <title> Hipp.</title> 940</ref>(quoted <ref target="cl570">on 570</ref> ) cp. <cit> <bibl n="Xen. Anab. 3.4.35" ref="Xen. Anab. 3.4.35"> Xen. <title>Anab.</title> 3.4.35</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">δεῖ ἐπισάξαι τὸν ἵππον Πέρσῃ ἀνδρὶ καὶ χαλινῶσαι δεῖ</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Xen. Mem. 3.3.10" ref="Xen. Mem. 3.3.10"> <title>Mem.</title> 3. 3. 10</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἴ σοι δέοι διδάσκειν</quote> </cit> : <cit> <bibl n="Xen. Ec. 7.20" ref="Xen. Ec. 7.20"> <title>Oecon.</title> 7. 20</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">δεῖ μέντοι τοῖς μέλλουσιν ἀνθρώποις ἕξειν ὅ τι εἰσφέρωσιν</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Xen. Ec. 8.9" ref="Xen. Ec. 8.9"> <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 8. 9</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ...διαλέγειν δέοι αὐτῷ</quote> </cit>. We cannot read <foreign xml:lang="grc">σοὶ … δὴ</foreign> with L, and understand <quote xml:lang="grc">πάρεστι</quote> , as Campbell proposes. </gloss>

data.quote:
ἀλλ’ ἔργον ἤδη σὸν τὰ λοίφ’
									ὑπηρετεῖν

δεῖ ἐπισάξαι τὸν ἵππον Πέρσῃ ἀνδρὶ καὶ
							χαλινῶσαι δεῖ

δεῖ μέντοι τοῖς μέλλουσιν ἀνθρώποις
									ἕξειν ὅ τι εἰσφέρωσιν

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φαίνειν_ἔπη_721"> = <foreign xml:lang="grc">φαίνειν τὰς ἀρετὰς δῑ ἃς ἐπαινεῖσθε</foreign> , to <emph>illustrate</emph> the praises by deeds: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Od. 8.237" ref="Hom. Od. 8.237"> <title>Od.</title> 8.237</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλ’ ἐθέλεις ἀρετὴν σὴν φαινέμεν ἥ τοι ὀπηδεῖ</quote> </cit>. <foreign xml:lang="grc">φαίνειν ἔπη</foreign> could not mean strictly <quote xml:lang="grc">βεβαιοῦν ἔπη</quote> , to "make" the words "good." </gloss>

data.quote:
ἀλλ’ ἐθέλεις ἀρετὴν σὴν φαινέμεν ἥ τοι
									ὀπηδεῖ

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἡμῖν_723">, ethic dat.: cp. 81. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φαίνοιτ_ἄν_725">, a courteous entreaty. <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. Seven 261" ref="Aesch. Seven 261"> Aesch. <title>Theb.</title> 261</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">λέγοις ἂν ὡς τάχιστα.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τέρμα_σωτηρίας_725">(defining gen.), the end which consists in safety, cp. <quote xml:lang="grc">τέλος θανάτοιο</quote> . When the attack has been made and repulsed, he will feel finally assured. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="παρέσται_726"> , <foreign xml:lang="lat">sc.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ τέρμα τῆς ς.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐγώ_726">. It is unsafe to argue that <foreign xml:lang="grc">κυρῶ</foreign> could not be a gloss, because it is a poetical word. It was just such a conjecture as correctors of the later age readily made, to smooth a supposed difficulty, or in mere wantonness. With <foreign xml:lang="grc">γέρων</foreign> opposed to <foreign xml:lang="grc">οὐ γεγήρακε</foreign> we requlre <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἐγώ</foreign> opposed to <foreign xml:lang="grc">χώρας</foreign> . It is different when the pers. pron. is omitted because the <emph>main</emph> antithesis is between two verbal notions: as in <ref n="Aesch. Eum. 84"> Aesch. <title> Eum.</title> 84</ref>(I will not betray thee) <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ γὰρ κτανεῖν σ’ ἔπεισα</quote> , for I <emph>persuaded</emph> (not <title> I</title> persuaded) thee to slay. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὀμμάτων_729">possessive gen., <foreign xml:lang="grc">τῆς ἐμῆς ἐπεισόδου</foreign> objective gen., both with <foreign xml:lang="grc">φόβον</foreign> : a fear belonging to the eyes (showing itself in them), about my advent.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἰληφότας_729"> : <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 345" ref="Soph. Aj. 345"> <title>Ai.</title> 345</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τάχ’ ἄν τιν’ αἰδῶ... λάβοι</quote> </cit> (conceive): <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Supp. 1050" ref="Eur. Supp. 1050"> Eur. <title>Suppl.</title> 1050</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὀργὴν λάβοις ἄν.</quote> </cit> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="νεώρη_729">: cp. on <ref target="cl475">475</ref> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὃν_731">, relat. to <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐμέ</quote> implied in <foreign xml:lang="grc">τῆς ἐμῆς</foreign> (cp. on <ref target="cl263">263</ref> ). <quote xml:lang="grc"> <emph>μήτ’ ἀφῆτ’</emph> κ.τ.λ.</quote>is an independent sentence, co-ordinated with the relat. clause <quote xml:lang="grc">ὃν μήτ’ ὀκνεῖτε</quote> : see on <ref target="cl424">424</ref> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὡς_732">with <foreign xml:lang="grc">βουληθείς</foreign> , marking more strongly the agent's own point of view, cp. on <ref target="cl71">71</ref> .</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δρᾶν_τι_732">, euphemistic, to take any forcible measures: so, in a good sense, <cit> <bibl n="Thuc. 1.20" ref="Thuc. 1.20">Thuc. 1.20</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">βουλόμενοι...δράσαντές τι καὶ κινδυνεῦσαι</quote> </cit>, to do something notable if they must incur the risk.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἴ_τιν_734">, instead of <quote xml:lang="grc">εἴτις</quote> ( <quote xml:lang="grc"> σθένει</quote> ), by assimilation, <quote xml:lang="grc">εἴτις</quote> being treated as forming a single adj.: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 488" ref="Soph. Aj. 488"> <title>Ai.</title> 488</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἴπερ τινός, σθένοντος ἐν πλούτῳ, Φρυγῶν</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Thuc. 7.21" ref="Thuc. 7.21">Thuc. 7.21</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῦ τε Γυλίππου καὶ Ἑρμοκράτους καὶ εἴ του ἄλλου πειθόντων</quote> </cit>: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Xen. Anab. 5.2.24" ref="Xen. Anab. 5.2.24"> Xen. <title>Anab.</title> 5.2.24</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνέλαμψεν οἰκία...ὅτου δὴ ἐνάψαντος</quote> </cit>(some one or other). </gloss>

data.quote:
εἴπερ τινός, σθένοντος ἐν πλούτῳ,
									Φρυγῶν

τοῦ τε Γυλίππου καὶ Ἑρμοκράτους καὶ εἴ
									του ἄλλου πειθόντων

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τηλικόσδ_735">is clearly right. It confirms the previous assurance that his errand is peaceful, and it harmonises with <foreign xml:lang="grc">πείσων</foreign> . <quote xml:lang="eng"> "I have not come to use <emph>force.</emph> No, I was sent, an aged envoy, to <emph>persuade</emph> him,"</quote>etc. If we read <foreign xml:lang="grc">τηλικόνδ’</foreign> Creon's diplomacy is at fault. He should not begin by reminding them that Thebes had suffered Oedipus to wander in misery for so many years. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐκ_στείλαντος_737">, not in consequence of one man's sending ( <quote xml:lang="grc"> στείλαντος</quote> predicate): <foreign xml:lang="grc">κελευσθεὶς</foreign> goes only with <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀστῶν ὑπὸ πάντων</quote> . The combination of particles in different cases is esp. freq. when one is a gen. absol. (as if <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐξ</quote> were absent here): <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Phil. 170" ref="Soph. Phil. 170"> <title>Ph.</title> 170 f.</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="lyric"> <l>μή του κηδομένου βροτῶν</l> <l>μηδὲ ξύντροφον ὄμμ’ ἔχων</l> </lg> </quote> </cit> : <ref n="Soph. Trach. 292"> <title>Tr.</title> 292</ref>: <cit> <bibl n="Dem. 23.156" ref="Dem. 23.156">Dem. or. 23 § 156</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἶδεν, εἴτε δή τινος εἰπόντος εἴτ’ αὐτὸς συνείς</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Thuc. 1.67" ref="Thuc. 1.67">Thuc. 1.67</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐχ ἡσύχαζον ἀνδρῶν τε σφίσιν ἐνόντων καὶ ἅμα περὶ τῷ χωρίῳ δεδιότες</quote> </cit>. But it occurs also without gen. abs., as <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Ant. 381" ref="Soph. Ant. 381"> <title>Ant.</title> 381</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπιστοῦσαν | … ἄγουσι … καὶ ἐν ἀφροσύνῃ καθελόντες</quote> </cit>. </gloss>

data.quote:
εἶδεν, εἴτε δή τινος εἰπόντος εἴτ’
									αὐτὸς συνείς

οὐχ ἡσύχαζον ἀνδρῶν τε σφίσιν ἐνόντων
									καὶ ἅμα περὶ τῷ χωρίῳ δεδιότες

ἀπιστοῦσαν | … ἄγουσι … καὶ ἐν
									ἀφροσύνῃ καθελόντες

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀστῶν_737">marks the public character of his mission from Thebes, while <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἀνδρῶν</foreign> would be intolerably weak. It cannot be justified by Herm.'s argument, that Soph. added it in the second clause because he had omitted it in the first, since <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἑνὸς</foreign> needed no addition. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄνδρα</quote> in <ref n="Soph. OC 735">735</ref> probably caused the slip. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἧκέ_γένει_738">, it <emph>devolved on me</emph> by kinship. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Alc. 291" ref="Eur. Alc. 291"> Eur. <title>Alc.</title> 291</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">καλῶς μὲν αὐτοῖς κατθανεῖν ἧκον βίου</quote> </cit>(acc. absol.), when they had reached a time of life mature for dying. The personal constr. occurs in <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Her. 213" ref="Eur. Her. 213"> Eur. <title>Her.</title> 213</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">γένους μὲν ἥκεις ὧδε τοῖσδε</quote> </cit>, thou art related to them <emph>in this degree.</emph> In such examples <quote xml:lang="grc">ἥκει, ἥκω</quote> cannot properly be regarded as mere substitutes for <quote xml:lang="grc">προσήκει, προσήκω.</quote> </gloss>

data.quote:
καλῶς μὲν αὐτοῖς κατθανεῖν ἧκον
									βίου

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γένει_738">(caus. dat.): cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 1016" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg004:1016"> <title>O. T.</title> 1016</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἦν σοι Πόλυβος οὐδὲν ἐν γένει</quote> </cit>. Bergk's <quote xml:lang="grc">ἦκ’ ἔμοιγ’ ἑνί</quote> is unnecessary. </gloss>

data.quote: ἦν σοι Πόλυβος οὐδὲν ἐν γένει

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἰς_πόλεως_739">, to the greatest extent of all the citizens, <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> more than any other Theban.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἰς_739">as in <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰς ὑπερβολήν, ἐς τὰ μάλιστα</quote> , etc. (cp. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπὶ πλέον</quote> ): the gen. after the superl. adv., as <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 502" ref="Soph. Aj. 502"> <title>Ai.</title> 502</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">μέγιστον ἴσχυσε στρατοῦ</quote> </cit> . </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀλλ_740">opens his direct appeal: cp. 101. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δικαίως_742">, with right, since Thebes, which had been his <quote xml:lang="grc"> τρόφος</quote> so long (760), has a better claim to him than Athens, however hospitable. And Creon has an especial right to urge the claim as being now the guardian of the family honour (755). <title>Not</title> : <quote xml:lang="eng">"as they owed it to thee to do"</quote> : nor, <quote xml:lang="eng">"in due form,"</quote> as opp. to private overtures. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐκ_τῶν_742">. When the art. stands as demonstr. pron., it is usu. the first word in the clause: but cp. 1699 ( <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸν</quote> ): <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. Eum. 2" ref="Aesch. Eum. 2"> Aesch. <title>Eum.</title> 2</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκ δὲ τῆς Θέμιν</quote> </cit> : <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Euthyd. 303c" ref="Plat. Euthyd. 303c"> Plat. <title>Euthyd.</title> 303 C</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">πολλὰ μὲν οὖν καὶ ἄλλα...ἐν δὲ τοῖς καὶ τοῦτο</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Alc. 264" ref="Eur. Alc. 264"> Eur. <title>Alc.</title> 264</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οἰκτρὰν φίλοισιν, ἐκ δὲ τῶν μάλιστ’ ἐμοί</quote> </cit>. (In Soph. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Phil. 1243" ref="Soph. Phil. 1243"> <title>Ph.</title> 1243 </bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν δὲ τοῖς ἑγώ</quote> </cit>is doubtful; L has <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῖσδ’</quote> .)</gloss>

data.quote:
πολλὰ μὲν οὖν καὶ ἄλλα...ἐν δὲ τοῖς
									καὶ τοῦτο

οἰκτρὰν φίλοισιν, ἐκ δὲ τῶν μάλιστ’
									ἐμοί

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὅσῳπερ_743">, <foreign xml:lang="lat">sc.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">μάλιστα</quote> : cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Trach. 312" ref="Soph. Trach. 312"> <title>Tr.</title> 312</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="trimeter"> <l>ἐπείνιν τῶνδε πλεῖστον ὤκτισα</l> <l>βλέπουσ’, ὅσῳπερ καὶ φρονεῖν ὀ=δεν μόνη</l> </lg> </quote> </cit>, where <quote xml:lang="grc">πλεῖστον</quote> is grammatically needed with <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅσῳπερ</quote> , though <quote xml:lang="grc"> μόνη</quote> is added as if <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπειδή</quote> , and not <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅσῳπερ</quote> , had preceded. Schol. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐγὼ μάλιστά σε καλῶ, ὅσῳπερ πλεῖστον ἀλγῶ τοῖς παθήμασιν</quote> ,—where the absence of any ref. to the words <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ μὴ...κάκιστος</quote> has caused suspicion: but the schol.'s aim was simply to explain the syntax. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πλειστον_κάκιστος_743"> : <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Phil. 631" ref="Soph. Phil. 631"> <title>Ph.</title> 631 </bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τῆς πλεῖστον ἐχθίστης</quote> </cit> : <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Med. 1323" ref="Eur. Med. 1323"> Eur. <title>Med.</title> 1323</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὦ μέγιστον ἐχθίστη γύναι</quote> </cit> : <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Alc. 790" ref="Eur. Alc. 790"> <title>Alc.</title> 790</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὴν πλεῖστον ἡδίστην</quote> </cit> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ξένον_745">would apply to any one living in a country not his own: cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 562">562</ref> . Oed. is not merely an exile, but a wandering beggar. The rhythm makes it better to take <foreign xml:lang="grc">ὄντα</foreign> with <quote xml:lang="grc">ξένον</quote> only, and to connect <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἀλήτην</foreign> with <foreign xml:lang="grc">χωροῦντα.</foreign> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐπὶ_πρ_745">., in dependence on (cp. on <ref target="cl148">148</ref> ), but without conscious reference to the metaphor of an anchor: cp. <ref n="Lys. 31.9">Lys. or. 31 § 9</ref> (of a <quote xml:lang="grc"> μέτοικος</quote> ) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπὶ προστάτου ᾠκει</quote> , he lived under the protection of a citizen as his patron (so Lycurg. <title>Leocr.</title> § 145 <quote xml:lang="grc">οἰκήσας...ἐπὶ προστάτου</quote> ). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὴν_747">: Soph. freely uses the art. for the relat. pron., in dialogue no less than in lyrics; but (except in <ref n="Soph. Trach. 47"> <title>Tr.</title> 47</ref>) only where metre requires: cp. crit. n. <ref target="cl35">on 35</ref> : so in dialogue 1258 ( <quote xml:lang="grc">τῆς</quote> ), <ref n="Soph. OT 1379"> <title>O. T.</title> 1379</ref>( <quote xml:lang="grc">τῶν</quote> ), 1427 ( <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ</quote> ), <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1086"> <title>Ant.</title> 1086</ref>( <quote xml:lang="grc">τῶν</quote> ), <ref n="Soph. El. 1144"> <title>El.</title> 1144</ref>( <quote xml:lang="grc">τὴν</quote> ), <ref n="Soph. Trach. 47"> <title>Tr.</title> 47</ref>( <quote xml:lang="grc">τὴν</quote> ), 381, 728 ( <quote xml:lang="grc">τῆς</quote> ), <ref n="Soph. Phil. 14"> <title>Ph.</title> 14 </ref>( <quote xml:lang="grc">τῷ</quote> ), etc.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τάλας_747">has nearly the force of an interjection, <quote xml:lang="eng">"ah me!"</quote> : cp. 318.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐκ_ἄν_748"> = <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅτι πέσοι ἄν.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐς_αἰκίας_748">: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 771" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg004:771"> <title>O. T.</title> 771</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐς τοσοῦτον ἐλπίδων | ἐμοῦ βεβῶτος</quote> </cit>, n. So <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 191" ref="Soph. El. 191"> <title>El.</title> 191</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀεικεῖ σὺν στολᾷ</quote> </cit>(of Electra). The penult. of <quote xml:lang="grc">αἰκία</quote> , as of the epic <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀεικία</quote> , is always long; hence the later spelling <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀείκεια, αἴκεια</quote> (Eustath. 1336. 58), often found in our MSS.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὅσον_748">, <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰς ὅσον</quote> : cp. <cit> <bibl n="Dem. 19.342" ref="Dem. 19.342">Dem. or. 19 § 342</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπὶ τῆς αὐτῆς ἧσπερ νῦν ἐξουσίας...μενεῖ</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Rep. 533E" ref="Plat. Rep. 533E"> Plat. <title>Rep.</title> 533E</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ περὶ ὀνόματος ἡ ἀμφισβήτησις, οἷς τοσούτων πέρι σκέψις ὅσων ἡμῖν πρόκειται.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

data.quote:
ἐπὶ τῆς αὐτῆς ἧσπερ νῦν
									ἐξουσίας...μενεῖ

οὐ περὶ ὀνόματος ἡ ἀμφισβήτησις, οἷς
									τοσούτων πέρι σκέψις ὅσων ἡμῖν πρόκειται.

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἥδε_δύσμορος_748">is added as if the preceding statement had been general ( <quote xml:lang="eng"> "I had not thought that <emph>any</emph> royal maiden,"</quote>etc.) </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸ_κάρα_750">, a way of alluding to his blindness without mentioning it: cp. 285. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πτωχῷ_751">. The poet. tendency was often to treat adjectives with three terminations as if they had only two. Cp. the Homeric <cit> <quote xml:lang="grc">πουλὺν ἐφ’ ὑγρήν</quote> <bibl n="Hom. Il. 10.27" ref="Hom. Il. 10.27"> ( <title>Il.</title> 10.27)</bibl> </cit> : <cit> <quote xml:lang="grc">θῆλυς ἐέρση</quote> <bibl n="Hom. Od. 5.467" ref="Hom. Od. 5.467"> ( <title>Od.</title> 5.467)</bibl> </cit> , <cit> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἡδὺς ἀϋτμή</quote> <bibl n="Hom. Od. 12.369" ref="Hom. Od. 12.369"> ( <title>Od.</title> 12.369)</bibl> </cit> , <cit> <quote xml:lang="grc">πικρὸν...ὀδμήν</quote> <bibl n="Hom. Od. 4.406" ref="Hom. Od. 4.406"> ( <title>Od.</title> 4.406)</bibl> </cit>: below, <ref n="Soph. OC 1460">1460</ref> (cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 384"> <title>O. T.</title> 384</ref>n.): <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Trach. 207" ref="Soph. Trach. 207"> <title>Tr.</title> 207</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">κοινὸς...κλαγγά</quote> </cit>: so <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Trach. 478" ref="Soph. Trach. 478"> <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 478</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">πατρῷος</quote> </cit>, and <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Trach. 533" ref="Soph. Trach. 533">533</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">θυραῖος</quote> </cit> : <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Ba. 598" ref="Eur. Ba. 598"> Eur. <title>Bacch.</title> 598</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">δίου βροντᾶς</quote> </cit> , <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Ba. 992" ref="Eur. Ba. 992">992</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἴτω δίκα φανερός, ἴτω</quote> </cit> : <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Hel. 623" ref="Eur. Hel. 623"> <title>Helen</title> 623</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὦ ποθεινὸς ἡμέρα</quote> </cit> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τηλικοῦτος_751">is fem. only here and <ref n="Soph. El. 614"> <title>El.</title> 614</ref>. The point of <quote xml:lang="grc"> τηλικοῦτος</quote> is that her marriageable age is passing by in these perilous wanderings. There is a similar thought in Electra's complaint ( <ref n="Soph. El. 962"> <title>El.</title> 962</ref>). Cp. 1116, 1181. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοὐπιόντος_752"> possessive, <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἁρπάσαι</foreign> epexegetic: belonging to the first comer, for him to seize. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 393" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg004:393"> <title>O. T.</title> 393</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="trimeter"> <l>τό γ’ αἴνιγμ’ οὐχὶ τοὐπιόντος ἦν</l> <l>ἀνδρὸς διειπεῖν</l> </lg> </quote> </cit> (n.). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἆῤ_753">; equiv. in <emph>sense</emph> to <quote xml:lang="grc">ἆρ’ οὐ</quote> ; <quote xml:lang="eng">"are you satisfied that it is so?"</quote> <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="eng">"is it not so?"</quote> <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 822" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg004:822"> <title>O. T.</title> 822</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἆρ’ ἔφυν κακός; | ἆρ’ οὐχὶ πᾶς ἄναγνος;</quote> </cit> </gloss>

data.quote:
ἆρ’ ἔφυν κακός; | ἆρ’ οὐχὶ πᾶς
									ἄναγνος;

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὦ_τάλας_753">, nom. instead of voc., cp. 185; so <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 744" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg004:744"> <title>O. T.</title> 744</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οἴμοι τάλας</quote> </cit>, n.; below, 847. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πρὸς_πατρῴων_756">, the gods of thy fathers, <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> of the Labdacid house, which traced its descent from Agenor, son of Poseidon and father of Cadmus. This peculiarly strong adjuration occurs also <ref n="Soph. Ant. 839"> <title>Ant.</title> 839</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Phil. 933"> <title>Ph.</title> 933</ref>: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 411" ref="Soph. El. 411"> <title>El.</title> 411</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὦ θεοὶ πατρῷοι, συγγένεσθέ γ’ ἀλλὰ νῦν</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl>fr. 521. 8</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <note anchored="true" place="inline" resp="RCJ" xml:lang="eng">(women are parted by marriage)</note> θεῶν πατρῴων τῶν τε φυσάντων ἄπο</quote> </cit> . </gloss>

data.quote:
ὦ θεοὶ πατρῷοι, συγγένεσθέ γ’ ἀλλὰ
									νῦν
θεῶν πατρῴων τῶν τε φυσάντων ἄπο

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θελήσας_757">: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 649" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg004:649"> <title>O. T.</title> 649</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">πιθοῦ θελήσας φρονήσας τ’</quote> </cit> (n.).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄστυ_757">, no less than <foreign xml:lang="grc">δόμους</foreign> , is qualified by <foreign xml:lang="grc">τοὺς πατρῴους</foreign> (cp. 297). Creon's real purpose was to establish Oedipus just beyond the Theban border (399). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἰπών_759"> here= <quote xml:lang="grc">προσειπών</quote> : so <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Il. 12.210" ref="Hom. Il. 12.210"> <title>Il.</title> 12.210</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">δὴ τότε Πουλυδάμας θρασὺν Ἕκτορα εἶπε παραστάς</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 764" ref="Soph. Aj. 764"> <title>Ai.</title> 764</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁ μὲν γὰρ αὐτὸν ἐννέπει· τέκνον</quote> </cit>, etc. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 862" ref="Soph. Aj. 862"> <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 862</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="trimeter"> <l>τὰ Τρωϊκὰ</l> <l>πεδία προσαυδῶ· χαίρετ’, ὦ τροφῆς ἐμοί</l> </lg> </quote> </cit> : <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 1221" ref="Soph. Aj. 1221"> <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 1221</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰς ἱερὰς ὅπως | προσείποιμεν Ἀθάνας</quote> </cit>. Usu. <quote xml:lang="grc">εὖ</quote> or <quote xml:lang="grc">κακῶς λέγειν τινά</quote> is to speak well or ill <emph>of</emph> him: <cit> <bibl n="Xen. Mem. 2.3.8" ref="Xen. Mem. 2.3.8"> Xen. <title>Mem.</title> 2.3.8</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">εὖ λέγειν τὸν εὖ λέγοντα.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

data.quote:
δὴ τότε Πουλυδάμας θρασὺν Ἕκτορα εἶπε
									παραστάς

ὁ μὲν γὰρ αὐτὸν ἐννέπει·
									τέκνον

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἡ_οἴκοι_759"> ( <quote xml:lang="grc">πόλις</quote> ) is somewhat bold, but scarcely warrants Wecklein's change to <quote xml:lang="grc">ἡ δ’ ἐκεἰ</quote> . Cp. 351, <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. Supp. 390" ref="Aesch. Supp. 390"> Aesch. <title>Suppl.</title> 390</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">κατὰ νόμους τοὺς οἴκοθεν</quote> </cit>(the laws of your country). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="παντὸς_761"> with <foreign xml:lang="grc">λόγου δικαίου</foreign> : <quote xml:lang="eng">"thou who wouldst borrow a crafty device from any plea of right"</quote> —as he here uses the <quote xml:lang="grc">λόγος δίκαιος</quote> about duty to friends and fatherland for the purpose of enticing Oedipus back. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Phil. 407" ref="Soph. Phil. 407"> <title>Ph.</title> 407</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="trimeter"> <l>ἔξοιδα γάρ νιν παντὸς ἂν λόγου κακοῦ</l> <l>γλώσσῃ θιγόντα</l> </lg> </quote> </cit> : <cit> <bibl n="Eur. IA 97" ref="Eur. IA 97"> Eur. <title>I. A.</title> 97</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">πάντα προσφέρων λόγον</quote> </cit>. This is better than to make <foreign xml:lang="grc">παντὸς</foreign> neut., taking <foreign xml:lang="grc">λόγου δ.</foreign> as defining gen. with <foreign xml:lang="grc">μηχάνημα</foreign> : <quote xml:lang="eng">"thou who from anything wouldst borrow a crafty device consisting in a fair plea"</quote> : for which, however, we might cp. <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Hec. 248" ref="Eur. Hec. 248"> Eur. <title>Hec.</title> 248</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">πολλῶν λόγων εὑρήμαθ’ ὥστε μὴ θανεῖν</quote> </cit>, <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Ant. 312" ref="Soph. Ant. 312"> <title>Ant.</title> 312</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐξ ἄπαντος...κερδαίνειν</quote> </cit>, and below, 807.</gloss>

data.quote:
πολλῶν λόγων εὑρήμαθ’ ὥστε μὴ
									θανεῖν

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἂν_φέρων_761"> = <quote xml:lang="grc">ὃς φέροις ἄν</quote> : as in <ref n="Soph. Phil. 407"> <title>Ph.</title> 407 f. </ref>(quoted above) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἂν...θιγόντα</quote> = <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅτι θίγοι ἄν</quote> . <cit> <bibl n="Dem. 18.258" ref="Dem. 18.258">Dem. or. 18 § 258</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">πόλλ’ ἂν ἔχων ἕτερ’ εἰπεῖν περὶ αὐτῆς παραλείπω</quote> </cit>,= <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅτε ἔχοιμι ἄν</quote> . Cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 11"> <title>O. T.</title> 11</ref>n. </gloss>

data.quote:
πόλλ’ ἂν ἔχων ἕτερ’ εἰπεῖν περὶ αὐτῆς
									παραλείπω

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πειρᾷ_763">is manifestly right: <foreign xml:lang="grc">ταῦτα</foreign> is cogn. accus., <quote xml:lang="grc">μου</quote> being understood.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δεύτερον_ἑλεῖν_763">, to get me <emph>a second time</emph> into thy power. This is explained by vv. 765—771, which set forth how they had abused their <emph>former</emph> control over the blind man.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐν_οἷς_763">= <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν τούτοις, ἐν οἷς</quote> , in things (snares), having been caught in which, etc.: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 1476" ref="Soph. El. 1476"> <title>El.</title> 1476</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="trimeter"> <l>τίνων ποτ’ ἀνδρῶν ἐν μέσοις ἀρκυστάτοις</l> <l>πέπτωχ’ ὁ τλήμων;</l> </lg> </quote> </cit> <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Phoen. 263" ref="Eur. Phoen. 263">Eur. <title>Phoen.</title> 263</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="trimeter"> <l>δέδοικα μή με δικτύων ἔσω</l> <l>λαβόντες οὐκ ἐκφρῶς).</l> </lg> </quote> </cit> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μάλιστ_ἀλγοίην_763">: because his dearest wish now is that his grave should bless his friends and harm his foes (92). If the Thebans could entice him back, and become masters of his grave, they might baffle that wish; and yet he would not even have burial in Theban soil (406). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πρόσθεν_τε_765">, answered by <foreign xml:lang="grc">νῦν τε</foreign> in 772. The interval is somewhat long, but the first <foreign xml:lang="grc">τε</foreign> merely prepares the ear for a statement in two parts.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οἰκείοις_765">, due to my own acts: it was horror at his own involuntary crimes that made him eager to quit Thebes: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 819" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg004:819"> <title>O. T.</title> 819</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="trimeter"> <l>καὶ τάδ’ οὔτις ἄλλος ἦν</l> <l>ἢ ’γὼ ’π’ ἐμαυτῷ τάσδ’ ἀρὰς ὁ προστιθείς</l> </lg> </quote> </cit>. So <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 260" ref="Soph. Aj. 260"> <title>Ai.</title> 260</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οἰκεῖα πάθη, | μηδενὸς ἄλλου παραπράξαντος</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 215" ref="Soph. El. 215"> <title>El.</title> 215</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οἰκείας εἰς ἄτας | ἐμπίπτεις</quote> </cit> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="νοσοῦνθ_766">, as if <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐκ ἤθελες ἐκπέμπειν</quote> was to follow; but the changed form of phrase requires the dat. <foreign xml:lang="grc"> θέλοντι</foreign> . Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 350" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg004:350"> <title>O. T.</title> 350</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐννέπω σὲ...ἐμμένειν,...ὡς ὄντι</quote> </cit>(n.). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐκ_θέλοντι_767"> <quote xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</quote>, the will on my side was not met by will on yours: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Trach. 198" ref="Soph. Trach. 198"> <title>Tr.</title> 198</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐχ ἑκών, ἑκοῦσι δὲ | ξύνεστιν</quote> </cit> : <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Ant. 276" ref="Soph. Ant. 276"> <title>Ant.</title> 276</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">πάρειμι δ’ ἄκων οὐχ ἑκοῦσιν.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="προσθέσθαι_767"> , <quote xml:lang="eng">"bestow,"</quote> a sense freq. in the active, but somewhat rare in the midd.: cp., however, <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Ant. 40" ref="Soph. Ant. 40"> <title>Ant.</title> 40</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">προσθείμην</quote> </cit> ( <quote xml:lang="grc">πλέον τι</quote> , <quote xml:lang="eng"> "contribute"</quote> ), <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. Eum. 735" ref="Aesch. Eum. 735"> Aesch. <title>Eum.</title> 735</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ψῆφον δ’ Ὀρέστῃ τήνδ’ ἐγὼ προσθήσομαι</quote> </cit>. The <emph> midd.</emph> usu.= <quote xml:lang="eng">"to annex"</quote> (404), or <quote xml:lang="eng">"to take on oneself"</quote> ( <ref n="Soph. OT 1460"> <title>O. T.</title> 1460</ref>n.). Cp. on <quote xml:lang="grc"> προσθήσει</quote> , 153. </gloss>

data.quote:
ψῆφον δ’ Ὀρέστῃ τήνδ’ ἐγὼ
									προσθήσομαι

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἦ_768">, the old Attic form, given by L in 973, 1366 (though not elsewhere), and attested by ancient scholia for fr. 406 and <ref n="Soph. OT 1123"> <title>O. T.</title> 1123</ref>, where see n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μεστὸς_768">with partic.: <cit> <bibl n="Dem. 48.28" ref="Dem. 48.28">[Dem.] or. 48 § 28</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <note anchored="true" place="inline" resp="RCJ" xml:lang="eng">(prob. by a contemporary of dem.)</note> ἐπειδὴ δὲ μεστὸς ἐγένετο ἀγανακτῶν</quote> </cit> : <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Hipp. 664" ref="Eur. Hipp. 664"> Eur. <title>Hipp.</title> 664</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">μισῶν δ’ οὔποτ’ ἐμπλησθήσομαι | γυναῖκας</quote> </cit>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐξεώθεις_κἀξέβ_770">.: for the impf. cp. 356, 441. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοῦτ_771">: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Ant. 96" ref="Soph. Ant. 96"> <title>Ant.</title> 96</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ δεινὸν τοῦτο</quote> </cit>, this danger of which thou speakest. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πόλιν_772">, the State in the person of its head, Theseus: </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γένος_772"> , the people of Attica, as represented by the elders of Colonus. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 861" ref="Soph. Aj. 861"> <title>Ai.</title> 861</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">κλειναί τ’ Ἀθῆναι καὶ τὸ σύντροφον γένος</quote> </cit>. So <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 706" ref="Soph. El. 706"> <title>El.</title> 706</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">Αἰνιὰν γένος</quote> </cit>: fr. 61 <quote xml:lang="grc">κόρῃ τε κἀργείᾳ γένος</quote> . </gloss>

data.quote:
κλειναί τ’ Ἀθῆναι καὶ τὸ σύντροφον
									γένος

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μετασπᾶν_774">, to snatch to the other side (cp. <quote xml:lang="grc">μετακινεῖν, μεταπείθειν</quote> etc.), found only here, but not open to just suspicion, though Blaydes changes it to <quote xml:lang="grc">μ’ ἀποσπᾶν</quote> . So <quote xml:lang="grc">μεθέλκειν</quote> in <ref> <title>Anth. Plan.</title> 5. 384.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σκληρὰ_λέγων_774">, putting hard purposes into soft words: disguising the ungenerous treatment which was really contemplated <ref n="Soph. OC 399">(399)</ref> under the name of a recall to home and friends <ref n="Soph. OC 757"> (757)</ref> . For the verbal contrast cp. <cit> <bibl>Epicharmus fr. 121</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὦ πονηρέ, μὴ τὰ μαλακὰ μώεο μὴ τὰ σκλήρ’ ἔχῃς</quote> </cit>, "woo not softness, lest thou wed hardness." <cit> <bibl n="Aristot. Rh. 3.7.10" ref="Aristot.
