Rh. 3.7.10"> Arist. <title>Rhet.</title> 3.7.10</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <note anchored="true" place="inline" resp="RCJ" xml:lang="eng">(speaking of the relation to be observed between the <emph>sounds</emph>sounds<emph> tones</emph>tones</note>ἐὰν οὖν τὰ μαλακὰ σκληρῶς καὶ τὰ σκληρὰ μαλακῶς λέγηται, ἀπίθανον γίγνεται</quote> </cit>. Cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 1406">1406</ref> .</gloss>

data.quote:
ὦ πονηρέ, μὴ τὰ μαλακὰ μώεο μὴ τὰ
									σκλήρ’ ἔχῃς

ἐὰν οὖν τὰ μαλακὰ σκληρῶς καὶ τὰ σκληρὰ μαλακῶς
							λέγηται, ἀπίθανον γίγνεται

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αὕτη_775">, subject (instead of <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῦτο</quote> , see on <ref target="cl88">88</ref> ),</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τίς_τέρψις_775"> predicate: <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἄκοντας</foreign> object to <foreign xml:lang="grc">φιλεῖν</foreign> : What pleasure is this, —that people should be hospitable to one against one's inclination? <cit> <bibl n="Thuc. 3.12" ref="Thuc. 3.12">Thuc. 3.12</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τίς οὖν αὕτη ἢ φιλία ἐγίγνετο ἢ ἐλευθερία πιστή;</quote> </cit> </gloss>

data.quote:
τίς οὖν αὕτη ἢ φιλία ἐγίγνετο ἢ
									ἐλευθερία πιστή;

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φιλεῖν_775">, <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Il. 6.15" ref="Hom. Il. 6.15"> <title>Il.</title> 6.15</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">πάντας γὰρ φιλέεσκεν ὁδῷ ἔπι οἰκία ναίων</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Od. 8.42" ref="Hom. Od. 8.42"> <title>Od.</title> 8.42</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὄφρα ξεῖνον ἐνὶ μεγάροισι φιλέωμεν</quote> </cit>. So often <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀγαπάω</quote> . Better thus than: <quote xml:lang="eng"> "what joy is it ( <emph>for thee</emph> ) to caress me against my will?"</quote>The illustration (776 ff.) shows that <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἄκοντας</foreign> refers to the reluctance of Oed., not to the constraint put by the oracle on the Thebans.</gloss>

data.quote:
πάντας γὰρ φιλέεσκεν ὁδῷ ἔπι οἰκία
									ναίων

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοσαύτη_775">was a mere blunder. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὥσπερ_776">merely introduces the illustration, like <quote xml:lang="eng">"For instance."</quote> <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Gorg. 451A" ref="Plat. Gorg. 451A"> Plat. <title>Gorg.</title> 451A</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὥσπερ ἄν, εἴ τίς με ἔροιτο... εἴποιμ’ ἄν</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Rep. 420c" ref="Plat. Rep. 420c"> <title>Rep.</title> 420 C</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὥσπερ οὖν ἂν εἰ ἡμᾶς ἀνδριάντας γράφοντας προσελθών τις ἔψεγε λέγων,...μετρίως ἂν ἐδοκοῦμεν πρὸς αὐτὸν ἀπολογεῖσθαι λέγοντες.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

data.quote:
ὥσπερ ἄν, εἴ τίς με ἔροιτο... εἴποιμ’
									ἄν

ὥσπερ οὖν ἂν εἰ ἡμᾶς ἀνδριάντας
									γράφοντας προσελθών τις ἔψεγε λέγων,...μετρίως ἂν
									ἐδοκοῦμεν πρὸς αὐτὸν ἀπολογεῖσθαι λέγοντες.

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τις_776"> before <foreign xml:lang="grc">εἰ</foreign> is here a case of <quote xml:lang="eng">"hyperbaton,"</quote> in which Soph. is sometimes bold: cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 1251"> <title>O. T.</title> 1251</ref>n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τυχεῖν_776">: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 1435" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg004:1435"> <title>O. T.</title> 1435</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ τοῦ με χρείας ὧδε λιπαρεῖς τυχεῖν</quote> </cit>; </gloss>

data.quote:
καὶ τοῦ με χρείας ὧδε λιπαρεῖς
									τυχεῖν

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὧν_χρῄζοις_778">. The verb in the relative clause takes the optative mood of the verb in the principal clause ( <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔχοντι</quote> = <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅτε ἔχοις</quote> ): cp. <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Hel. 435" ref="Eur. Hel. 435"> Eur. <title>Hel.</title> 435</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τίς ἂν μόλοι | ὅστις διαγγείλειε</quote> </cit>, and n. to <ref n="Soph. OT 506"> <title>O. T.</title> 506</ref>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἡ_χάρις_779">: when the <emph>benefit</emph> (the thing done) should bring with it no <emph> sense</emph> of a <emph>favour</emph> conferred: <foreign xml:lang="grc"> χάρις</foreign> and <foreign xml:lang="grc">χάριν</foreign> being used in two different senses: cp. <quote xml:lang="grc">χάριν ἄχαριν...ἐπικρᾶναι</quote> ( <ref n="Aesch. Ag. 1545"> Aesch. <title> Ag.</title> 1545</ref>) to grant a boon which gives no pleasure. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἆῤ_780">: see on <ref target="cl753">753</ref> . The second <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἄν</foreign> is warranted by the stress on <foreign xml:lang="grc">τῆσδ’</foreign> , and is more likely than <foreign xml:lang="grc">τῆσδέ γ’</foreign> : cp. on <ref n="Soph. OT 339"> <title>O. T.</title> 339</ref>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καὶ_σὺ_781">, thou on thy part: cp. on <ref target="cl53">53</ref> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λόγῳ_ἔργοισιν_782">: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 60" ref="Soph. El. 60"> <title>El.</title> 60</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅταν λόγῳ θανὼν | ἔργοισι σωθῶ</quote> </cit>: Eur. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Trach. 1233" ref="Soph. Trach. 1233"> <title>Tr.</title> 1233</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὄνομ’ ἔχουσα, τἄργα δ’ οὔ</quote> </cit> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καὶ_τοῖσδ_783">. The Chorus had been present when Ismene told Oed. of the Theban designs, and when he uttered an imprecation on his sons (399—460): and Theseus left the stage at 667. But <foreign xml:lang="grc">φράσω</foreign> refers to the explicit and public statement of Creon's baseness, now addressed, before his face, to the Chorus. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πάραυλον_785">, having my abode ( <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐλή</quote> ) beside you, <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄγχι γῆς Καδμείας</quote> (399), but outside of it. So <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 892" ref="Soph. Aj. 892"> <title>Ai.</title> 892</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τίνος βοὴ πάραυλος ἐξέβη νάπους</quote> </cit>; <quote xml:lang="eng">"whose cry burst from the covert of the wood at our side?"</quote> : fr. 460 <quote xml:lang="grc">πάραυλος Ἑλλησποντίς</quote> , a neighbour at the Hellespont. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κακῶν_ἄνατος_786">: see on <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνήνεμον χειμώνων</quote> 677.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τῆσδ_786">is a certain correction of the MS. <foreign xml:lang="grc">τῶνδ’</foreign> , which would be awkward if masc. (as = the Athenians), and pointless if neut., since nothing has yet been said between Creon and Oed. about such <quote xml:lang="grc">κακά</quote> . The schol., <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ ἵνα ἡ Θήβη ἀβλαβὴς ἔσται ἐκ ταύτης τῆς γῆς</quote> , confirms <foreign xml:lang="grc">τῆσδ’</foreign> . Join <foreign xml:lang="grc">τῆσδε χθονός</foreign> with <foreign xml:lang="grc">κακῶν</foreign> , <quote xml:lang="eng">"evils coming from this land"</quote> (gen. of source). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀπαλλαχθῇ_786">is absol., <quote xml:lang="eng">"get off,"</quote> as <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 1002" ref="Soph. El. 1002"> <title>El.</title> 1002</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄλυπος ἄτης ἐξαπαλλαχθήσομαι</quote> </cit> : <cit> <bibl n="Aristoph. Pl. 271" ref="Aristoph. Pl. 271"> Ar. <title>Plut.</title> 271</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπαλλαγῆναι | ἀζήμιος</quote> </cit>. If it were joined with <quote xml:lang="grc">τῆσδε χθονός</quote> , <quote xml:lang="eng">"get free of this land"</quote> (as Wecklein takes it), the phrase would imply that Thebes was already involved in a feud with Athens. Besides, the words would naturally mean, <quote xml:lang="eng">"get safely out of this land."</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ταῦτα_τάδ_787">, a good instance of the normal distinction. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Hdt. 6.53" ref="Hdt. 6.53">Her. 6.53</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ταῦτα μὲν Λακεδαιμόνιοι λέγουσι,...τάδε δὲ...ἐγὼ γράφω</quote> </cit> : <cit> <bibl n="Xen. Anab. 2.1.20" ref="Xen. Anab. 2.1.20"> Xen. <title>Anab.</title> 2.1.20</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ταῦτα μὲν δὴ σὺ λέγεις· παρ’ ἡμῶν δὲ ἀπάγγελλε τάδε</quote> </cit>. In poetry, however, <quote xml:lang="grc">οὗτος</quote> often refers to what follows (as <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Od. 2.306" ref="Hom. Od. 2.306"> <title>Od.</title> 2.306</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="hexameter"> <l>ταῦτα δέ τοι μάλα πάντα τελευτήσουσιν Ἀχαιοί,</l> <l>νῆα καὶ ἐξαίτους ἐρέτας</l> </lg> </quote> </cit>), and <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅδε</quote> to what has just preceded: cp. on <ref target="cl1007">1007</ref> . </gloss>

data.quote:
ταῦτα μὲν Λακεδαιμόνιοι
									λέγουσι,...τάδε δὲ...ἐγὼ γράφω

ταῦτα μὲν δὴ σὺ λέγεις· παρ’ ἡμῶν δὲ
									ἀπάγγελλε τάδε

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="χώρας_787"> with <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἀλάστωρ</foreign> , my scourge of the land, the avenging spirit which, through my curse, will ever haunt the land: for the gen., cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Trach. 1092" ref="Soph. Trach. 1092"> <title>Tr.</title> 1092</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">Νεμέας ἔνοικον</quote> </cit>(the lion), <quote xml:lang="grc">βουκόλων ἀλάστορα</quote> , scourge of herdsmen: Xenarchus (Midd. Comedy, c. 350 B.C.) <quote xml:lang="grc">Βουταλίων</quote> fr. 1. 3 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλάστωρ εἰσπέπαικε Πελοπιδῶν</quote> , a very fiend of the Pelopidae has burst in. If we joined <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἐκεῖ χώρας</foreign> , the phrase could mean nothing but <quote xml:lang="eng"> " <emph>in that part of</emph> the country,"</quote>which is pointless here. For <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἐνναίων</foreign> cp. <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. Supp. 415" ref="Aesch. Supp. 415"> Aesch. <title>Suppl.</title> 415</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">βαρὺν ξύνοικον...ἀλάστορα</quote> </cit>. The erasure after <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἔστ’</foreign> in L suggests to me the possibility that <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἔσω | χώρας</foreign> and <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἐκεῖ | χώραν</foreign> (depending on <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐνναίων</quote> ) may have been alternative readings, from which ours has been composed; but there is no evidence. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοσοῦτον_μόνον_790">is bold. The infin. must be explained as in appos. with <quote xml:lang="grc">τοσοῦτον</quote> ,— <quote xml:lang="eng">"just thus much right in the land—the right to die in it."</quote> For the regular construction, see <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 1191" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg004:1191"> <title>O. T.</title> 1191</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τοσοῦτον ὅσον δοκεῖν</quote> </cit> : <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. Seven 730" ref="Aesch. Seven 730"> Aesch. <title>Theb.</title> 730</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="lyric"> <l> <note anchored="true" place="inline" resp="RCJ" xml:lang="eng">(in ref. to these same brothers) </note> σίδαρος</l> <l>χθόνα ναίειν διαπήλας, ὁπόσαν καὶ φθιμένοισιν κατέχειν,</l> <l>τῶν μεγάλων πεδίων ἀμοίρους</l> </lg> </quote> </cit> : <cit> <bibl n="Xen. Anab. 4.8.12" ref="Xen. Anab. 4.8.12"> Xen. <title>Anab.</title> 4.8.12</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τοσοῦτον χωρίον κατασχεῖν...ὅσον ἔξω τοὺς ἐσχάτους λόχους γενέσθαι τῶν πολεμίων κεράτων</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Thuc. 1.2" ref="Thuc. 1.2">Thuc. 1.2</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">νεμόμενοι...τὰ αὑτῶν ἕκαστοι ὅσον ἀποζῆν</quote> </cit>. The conjecture of Blaydes, <foreign xml:lang="grc">ὅσονπερ</foreign> instead of <foreign xml:lang="grc">τοσοῦτον</foreign> , is hardly probable. </gloss>

data.quote:
τοσοῦτον χωρίον κατασχεῖν...ὅσον ἔξω
									τοὺς ἐσχάτους λόχους γενέσθαι τῶν πολεμίων
									κεράτων

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐνθανεῖν_790">: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Rh. 869" ref="Eur. Rh. 869"> [Eur.] <title>Rhes.</title> 869</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὦ γαῖα πατρίς, πῶς ἂν ἐνθάνοιμί σοι</quote> </cit>; a poet. word: in <ref n="Lys. 16.15">Lys. or. 16 § 15</ref> the prose <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐναποθανόντων</quote> should prob. be restored. Remark that <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἐνθανεῖν</foreign> can mean only "to <emph>die</emph> in," not "to <emph>lie dead</emph> in": but the sense is, "just enough ground, with a view to <emph>dying</emph> (instead of <emph>reigning</emph> ) on Theban soil"; <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> , as much as a dead man will need. The phrase is half-proverbial: <cit> <bibl n="Aristoph. Eccl. 592" ref="Aristoph. Eccl. 592"> Aristoph. <title>Eccl.</title> 592</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">μηδὲ γεωργεῖν τὸν μὲν πολλήν, τῷ δ’ εἶναι μηδὲ ταφῆναι</quote> </cit>. <cit> <bibl> Freeman, <title>Old English History</title> p. 313,/bibl> </bibl> <quote xml:lang="eng">"...What will my brother King Harold of England give to King Harold of Norway?" … "Seven foot of the ground of England, or more perchance, seeing he is taller than other men."</quote> </cit> <cit> <bibl n="Shak. 1h4 5.4" ref="Shak. 1h4 5.4">Shaksp. <title>H. IV.</title> Pt. i. 5. 4. 89</bibl> <quote xml:lang="eng"> <lg type="verse" subtype="iambic-pentameter"> <l>When that this body did contain a spirit,</l> <l>A kingdom for it was too small a bound;</l> <l>But now two paces of the vilest earth</l> <l>Is room enough.</l> </lg> </quote> </cit> </gloss>

data.quote:
ὦ γαῖα πατρίς, πῶς ἂν ἐνθάνοιμί
									σοι

μηδὲ γεωργεῖν τὸν μὲν πολλήν, τῷ δ’
									εἶναι μηδὲ ταφῆναι

"...What will my brother King Harold
									of England give to King Harold of Norway?" … "Seven foot
									of the ground of England, or more perchance, seeing he
									is taller than other men."

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σαφεστέρων_792">: see on <ref target="cl623">623</ref> . The <foreign xml:lang="grc">καὶ</foreign> of two MSS. (A, R) is strongly recommended by Greek usage, and is probably to be combined with <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἐκ</foreign> , which, though not necessary with <quote xml:lang="grc">κλύω</quote> , has L's support.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κλύω_792">, pres., know by hearing, as <ref n="Soph. Phil. 261"> <title>Ph.</title> 261</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Trach. 68"> <title>Tr.</title> 68</ref>, etc.: cp. 240 n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸ_στόμα_794">, thy mouth has come hither suborned: thou hast come as a mere mouthpiece of the Thebans, secretly pledged to aid their designs on me. Cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 426"> <title>O. T.</title> 426</ref>(Teiresias says) <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ Κρέοντα καὶ τοὐμὸν στόμα | προπηλάκιζε</quote> , my message from Apollo.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὑπόβλητον_794">: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 481" ref="Soph. Aj. 481"> <title>Ai.</title> 481</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="trimeter"> <l>οὐδεὶς ἐρεῖ ποθ’ ὡς ὑπόβλητον λόγον,</l> <l>Αἴας, ἔλεξας, ἀλλὰ τῆς σαυτοῦ φρενός</l> </lg> </quote> </cit>, a word not true to thy nature. So <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑπόπεμπτος</quote> of an insidious emissary, <ref n="Xen. Anab. 3.3.4"> Xen. <title>Anab.</title> 3.3.4</ref>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πολλὴν_στόμωσιν_795">, with a hard and keen edge,—thoroughly attempered to a shameless and cruel task. <quote xml:lang="grc">στόμωσις</quote> was the process of <emph> tempering</emph> iron to receive an edge or point ( <quote xml:lang="grc">στόμα</quote> ); cp. <cit> <bibl n="Aristot. Meteo. 4.6" ref="Aristot. Meteo. 4.6"> Arist. <title>Meteor.</title> 4.6</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τήκεται δὲ καὶ ὁ εἰργασμένος σίδηρος, ὥστε ὑγρὸς γίγνεσθαι καὶ πάλιν πήγνυσθαι. καὶ τὰ στομώματα ποιοῦσιν οὔτως· ὑφίσταται γὰρ καὶ ἀποκαθαίρεται κάτω ἡ σκωρία</quote> </cit> (dross). <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅταν δὲ πολλάκις πάθῃ καὶ καθαρὸς γένηται, τοῦτο στόμωμα γίγνεται</quote> (this makes <emph>tempered iron</emph> ). Hence, fig., <cit> <bibl n="Plut. Bruta 988d" ref="Plut. Bruta 988d"> Plut. <title>Mor.</title> 988 D</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τῆς ἀνδρείας οἷον βαφή τις ὁ θυμός ἐστι καὶ στόμωμα</quote> </cit>: <title> Lycurg.</title> 16 <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰ δ’</quote> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑγιεινὰ</quote> ( <quote xml:lang="grc">βρέφη</quote> ) <quote xml:lang="grc">μᾶλλον στομοῦσθαι καὶ κρατύνεσθαι τὴν ἕξιν</quote> , have their constitutions <emph> tempered</emph> and strengthened. <cit> <bibl n="Aristoph. Cl. 1107" ref="Aristoph. Cl. 1107"> Aristoph. <title>Nub.</title> 1107</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="trimeter"> <l>μέμνησ’ ὅπως</l> <l>εὖ μοι στομώσεις αὐτόν, ἐπὶ μὲν θάτερα</l> <l>οἷον δικιδίοις, τὴν δ’ ἑτέραν αὐτοῦ γνάθον</l> <l>στόμωσον οἵαν ἐς τὰ μείζω πράγματα</l> </lg> </quote> </cit>, alluding to a two-edged blade; schol. <quote xml:lang="grc">ὀξυνεῖς... ἀκονήσεις</quote> . The double sense of <foreign xml:lang="grc">στόμα</foreign> has suggested the <quote xml:lang="grc">παρήχησις</quote> with <foreign xml:lang="grc">στόμωσιν</foreign> : cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Trach. 1176" ref="Soph. Trach. 1176"> <title>Tr.</title> 1176</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τοὐμὸν ὀξῦναι στόμα</quote> </cit> . <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 650" ref="Soph. Aj. 650"> <title>Ai.</title> 650</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="trimeter"> <l>ὃς τὰ δείν’ ἐκαρτέρουν τότε,</l> <l>βαφῇ σίδηρος ὥς, ἐφηλύνφην στόμα</l> </lg> </quote> </cit>: "I, erst so wondrous firm,—yea, as iron hardened in the dipping,—felt the keen edge of my temper softened." Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 584" ref="Soph. Aj. 584"> <title>Ai.</title> 584</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">γλῶσσα... τεθηγμένη</quote> </cit> . </gloss>

data.quote:
τήκεται δὲ καὶ ὁ εἰργασμένος σίδηρος,
									ὥστε ὑγρὸς γίγνεσθαι καὶ πάλιν πήγνυσθαι. καὶ τὰ
									στομώματα ποιοῦσιν οὔτως· ὑφίσταται γὰρ καὶ
									ἀποκαθαίρεται κάτω ἡ σκωρία

τῆς ἀνδρείας οἷον βαφή τις ὁ θυμός
									ἐστι καὶ στόμωμα

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κακά_796">and </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σωτήρια_796"> are predicates: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Hipp. 471" ref="Eur. Hipp. 471"> Eur. <title>Hipp.</title> 471</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλ’ εἰ τὰ πλείω χρηστὰ τῶν κακῶν ἔχεις</quote> </cit>. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Ant. 313" ref="Soph. Ant. 313"> <title>Ant.</title> 313</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="trimeter"> <l>ἐκ τῶν γὰρ αἰσχρῶν λημμάτων τοὺς πλείονας</l> <l>ἀτωμένους ἴδοις ἂν ἢ σεσωσμένους</l> </lg> </quote> </cit>. Oed. means: "By pleading with me to return, you will only illustrate your own heartlessness: you will never win me as a safeguard for Thebes." </gloss>

data.quote:
ἀλλ’ εἰ τὰ πλείω χρηστὰ τῶν κακῶν
									ἔχεις

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἰ_τερποίμεθα_799">, if we should have content therewith: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Ant. 1168" ref="Soph. Ant. 1168"> <title>Ant.</title> 1168</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="trimeter"> <l>πλούτει τε γὰρ κατ’ οἶκον, εἰ βούλει, μέγα,</l> <l>καὶ ζῆ τύραννον σχῆμ’ ἔχων· ἐὰν δ’ ἀπῇ</l> <l>τούτων τὸ χαίρειν, τἄλλ’ ἐγὼ καπνοῦ σκιᾶς</l> <l>οὐκ ἂν πριαίμην ἀνδρὶ πρὸς τὴν ἡδονήν</l> </lg> </quote> </cit> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δυστυχεῖν_800">has been explained as <quote xml:lang="eng">"to be in error,"</quote> referring to Creon's ignorance of the lot in store for Thebes (787); but it is simpler to take it of Creon's failure to win Oedipus. However great that loss may be, Creon means, the loss to Oed. himself will be greater still.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐς_σά_800"> , <quote xml:lang="eng">"with regard to your doings"</quote> ; cp. 1121: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 980" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg004:980"> <title>O. T.</title> 980</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">σὺ δ’ εἰς τὰ μητρὸς μὴ φοβοῦ νυμφεύματα</quote> </cit>(n.). <quote xml:lang="grc"> <emph>ἢ σ’ εἰς τὰ σαυτοῦ</emph> , σὲ</quote>being elided, though emphatic: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 64" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg004:64"> <title>O. T.</title> 64</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">πόλιν τε κἀμὲ καὶ σ’ ὁμοῦ στένει.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

data.quote:
σὺ δ’ εἰς τὰ μητρὸς μὴ φοβοῦ
									νυμφεύματα

πόλιν τε κἀμὲ καὶ σ’ ὁμοῦ
									στένει.

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐν_λόγῳ_800">, in our present discussion (from 728). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φύσας_804">, cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 150">150</ref> , <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 1463" ref="Soph. El. 1463"> <title>El.</title> 1463</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">( ὡς ) κολαστοῦ προστυχὼν φύσῃ φρένας</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Hdt. 5.91" ref="Hdt. 5.91">Her. 5.91</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">δόξαν...φύσας αὐξάνεται</quote> </cit> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λῦμα_805">, a <quote xml:lang="eng">"stain,"</quote> or <quote xml:lang="eng">"reproach."</quote> In the only other place where Soph. has the word ( <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 655" ref="Soph. Aj. 655"> <title>Ai.</title> 655</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">λύμαθ’ ἁγνίσας ἐμά</quote> </cit>) it has its primary sense of <quote xml:lang="eng">"something washed off"</quote> (from <quote xml:lang="grc">√ΛΓ</quote> , another form of <quote xml:lang="grc">√ΛΟv</quote> , whence <quote xml:lang="grc">λούω</quote> ). <quote xml:lang="grc">λύμη</quote> is only another form, and Eur. uses <quote xml:lang="grc">λῦμα</quote> in the sense proper to <quote xml:lang="grc">λύμη</quote> , <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Tro. 588" ref="Eur. Tro. 588"> Eur. <title>Tro.</title> 588</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">λῦμ’ Ἀχαιῶν</quote> </cit>, their <quote xml:lang="eng">"bane"</quote> (Hector).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τρέφει_805">, pass. (as <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 374" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg004:374"> <title>O. T.</title> 374</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">μιᾶς τρέφει πρὸς νυκτός</quote> </cit>), thou <emph>livest on</emph> to disgrace thy years by thy folly. Not midd., <quote xml:lang="eng">"dost nourish a reproach."</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐξ_ἅπαντος_807"> , <emph>starting from</emph> anything as the <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀφορμή</quote> or <quote xml:lang="grc">ὕλη</quote> of discourse; <quote xml:lang="eng">"on any theme."</quote> So <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκ</quote> marks the conditions from which action sets out ( <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς ἐκ τῶνδ’</quote> , <ref n="Soph. Aj. 537"> <title>Ai.</title> 537</ref>).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εὖ_λέγει_807">, pleads <emph>speciously:</emph> <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Hec. 1191" ref="Eur. Hec. 1191"> Eur. <title>Hec.</title> 1191</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">δύνασθαι τἄδικ’ εὖ λέγειν</quote> </cit> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸ_καίρια_808">, the reading of Suidas, is supported by such passages as <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. PB 927" ref="Aesch. PB 927"> Aesch. <title>PV</title> 927</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅσον τό τ’ ἄρχειν καὶ τὸ δουλεύειν δίχα</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Alc. 528" ref="Eur. Alc. 528"> Eur. <title>Alc.</title> 528</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">χωρὶς τό τ’ εἶναι καὶ τὸ μὴ νομίζεται</quote> </cit>. In <cit> <bibl> Philemon <title xml:lang="grc">Σικελικός</title> fr. 1. 7</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἕτερον τό τ’ ἀλγεῖν καὶ τὸ θεωρεῖν ἔστ’ ἴσως</quote> </cit>, the second <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ</quote> is doubtful. But for <quote xml:lang="grc"> <emph>τὰ</emph> καίρια</quote>, the reading of the MSS., it may be urged that the phrase is <quote xml:lang="grc"> <emph>τὰ</emph> καίρια</quote>( <quote xml:lang="grc">λέγειν, δρᾶν</quote> etc.) in <ref n="Aesch. Seven 1"> Aesch. <title>Th.</title> 1</ref>, <ref n="Aesch. Seven 619">619</ref> , <ref n="Aesch. Supp. 446"> <title>Suppl.</title> 446</ref>, <ref n="Aesch. Lib. 582"> <title>Ch.</title> 582</ref>, <ref n="Eur. IA 829"> Eur. <title>I. A.</title> 829</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Aj. 120"> Soph. <title>Ai.</title> 120</ref>, while <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 228" ref="Soph. El. 228"> <title>El.</title> 228</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">φρονοῦντι καίρια</quote> </cit>(without art.) is isolated. If <foreign xml:lang="grc">τὰ</foreign> is retained, the ellipse of <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ</quote> is illustrated by <ref n="Soph. OC 606">606</ref> , where <ref target="cl606">see n.</ref> : and add <cit> <bibl>trag. incert. fr. 469</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">χωρὶς τὰ Μυσῶν καὶ Φρυγῶν ὁρίσματα</quote> </cit>. </gloss>

data.quote:
ὅσον τό τ’ ἄρχειν καὶ τὸ δουλεύειν
									δίχα

χωρὶς τό τ’ εἶναι καὶ τὸ μὴ
									νομίζεται

ἕτερον τό τ’ ἀλγεῖν καὶ τὸ θεωρεῖν
									ἔστ’ ἴσως

χωρὶς τὰ Μυσῶν καὶ Φρυγῶν
									ὁρίσματα

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὡς_δὴ_809">, <foreign xml:lang="lat">quasi vero</foreign> , strictly an elliptical phrase, <quote xml:lang="eng">"(do you mean) forsooth that you speak,"</quote> etc. <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. Ag. 1633" ref="Aesch. Ag. 1633"> Aesch. <title>Ag.</title> 1633</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς δὴ σύ μοι τύραννος Ἀργείων ἔσει</quote> </cit>. <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Andr. 234" ref="Eur. Andr. 234"> Eur. <title>Andr.</title> 234</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="trimeter"> <l>τί σεμνομυθεῖς κεἰς ἀγῶν’ ἔρχει λόγων,</l> <l>ὡς δὴ σὺ σώφρων τἀμὰ δ’ οὐχὶ σώφρονα;</l> </lg> </quote> </cit> </gloss>

data.quote:
ὡς δὴ σύ μοι τύραννος Ἀργείων
									ἔσει

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὅτῳ_810">= <quote xml:lang="grc">τούτῳ ὅτῳ</quote> , in the opinion of one who possesses only such sense as yours: for the ethic dat. cp. 1446, <cit> <bibl n="Aristoph. Birds 445" ref="Aristoph. Birds 445"> Aristoph. <title>Av.</title> 445</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">πᾶσι νικᾶν τοῖς κριταῖς</quote> </cit> : <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Ant. 904" ref="Soph. Ant. 904"> <title>Ant.</title> 904</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">καίτοι σ’ ἐγὼ )τίμησα τοῖς φρονοῦσιν εὖ</quote> </cit>. For <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἴσος</foreign> , <emph>only</emph> so much, cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 810" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg004:810"> <title>O. T.</title> 810</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ μὴν ἴσην γ’ ἔτισεν</quote> </cit> : <cit> <bibl n="Hdt. 2.3" ref="Hdt. 2.3">Her. 2.3</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">νομίζων πάντας ἀνθρώπους ἴσον περὶ αὐτῶν ἐπίστασθαι</quote> </cit>, equally little: for <quote xml:lang="grc"> ἴσος <emph>καὶ</emph> </quote>instead of <quote xml:lang="grc">ὥσπερ</quote> , <ref n="Soph. OT 1187"> <title>O. T.</title> 1187</ref>. </gloss>

data.quote:
καίτοι σ’ ἐγὼ )τίμησα τοῖς φρονοῦσιν
									εὖ

νομίζων πάντας ἀνθρώπους ἴσον περὶ
									αὐτῶν ἐπίστασθαι

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πρὸ_τῶνδε_811">, as <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 10" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg004:10"> <title>O. T.</title> 10</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">πρὸ τῶνδε φωνεῖν</quote> </cit> (n.). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐφορμῶν_812"> with <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἔνθα χρή</foreign> , keeping jealous watch at the place where I am destined to dwell: fig. from a hostile fleet watching a position; cp. <cit> <bibl n="Dem. 3.7" ref="Dem. 3.7">Dem. or. 3 § 7</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἦν τοῦτο ὥσπερ ἐμπόδισμά τι τῷ Φιλίππῳ καὶ δυσχερές, πόλιν μεγάλην ἐφορμεῖν τοῖς ἑαυτοῦ καιροῖς</quote> </cit>. <foreign xml:lang="grc">με</foreign> with <foreign xml:lang="grc">φύλασσ’</foreign> only: in class. Gk. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐφορμεῖν</quote> does not take acc. </gloss>

data.quote:
ἦν τοῦτο ὥσπερ ἐμπόδισμά τι τῷ Φιλίππῳ
									καὶ δυσχερές, πόλιν μεγάλην ἐφορμεῖν τοῖς ἑαυτοῦ
									καιροῖς

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μαρτύρομαι_813"> , <foreign xml:lang="lat">antestor</foreign> : cp. <cit> <bibl n="Aristoph. Peace 1119" ref="Aristoph. Peace 1119"> Aristoph. <title>Pax</title> 1119</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ΤΠ. ὦ παῖε παῖε τὸν Βάκιν. ΙΕ. μαρτύρομαι</quote> </cit>. </gloss>

data.quote:
ΤΠ. ὦ παῖε παῖε τὸν Βάκιν. ΙΕ.
									μαρτύρομαι

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀνταμείβει_814"> : <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀμείβομαι</quote> usu. takes a simple acc. of the person to whom a reply is made (991); but cp. <cit> <bibl n="Hdt. 8.60" ref="Hdt. 8.60">Her. 8.60</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τότε μὲν ἠπίως πρὸς τὸν Κορίνθιον ἀμείψατο</quote> </cit>: and, since <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀποκρίνομαι πρός τινα</quote> was common, it would have been strange if the same construction had been rigidly denied to <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀμείβομαι</quote> . Even if</gloss>

data.quote:
τότε μὲν ἠπίως πρὸς τὸν Κορίνθιον
									ἀμείψατο

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πρὸς_814">were not taken with <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνταμείβει</quote> here, it could still mean <quote xml:lang="eng">"in relation to"</quote> : cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Trach. 468" ref="Soph. Trach. 468"> <title>Tr.</title> 468</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">κακὸν | πρὸς ἄλλον εἶναι, πρὸς δ’ ἔμ’ ἀψευδεῖν ἀεί</quote> </cit>. So <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 680" ref="Soph. Aj. 680"> <title>Ai.</title> 680</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="trimeter"> <l>ἔς τε τὸν φίλον</l> <l>τοσαῦθ’ ὑπουργῶν ὠφελεῖν βουλήσομαι.</l> </lg> </quote> </cit> </gloss>

data.quote:
κακὸν | πρὸς ἄλλον εἶναι, πρὸς δ’ ἔμ’
									ἀψευδεῖν ἀεί

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οἷα_814"> causal= <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπεὶ τοιαῦτα</quote> : cp. on <ref target="cl263">263</ref> .</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τῶνδε_συμμ_815">. with <foreign xml:lang="grc">βίᾳ</foreign> : cp. 657. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἦ_μὴν_816">in a threat, as <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. PB 907" ref="Aesch. PB 907"> Aesch. <title>PV</title> 907</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="trimeter"> <l>ἦ μὴν ἔτι Ζεύς, καίπερ αὐθάδη φρονῶν,</l> <l>ἔσται ταπεινός.</l> </lg> </quote> </cit> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κἄνευ_τοῦδε_816"> , <foreign xml:lang="lat">sc.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῦ ἑλεῖν σε</quote> . Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 1158" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg004:1158"> <title>O. T.</title> 1158</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλ’ εἰς τόδ’ ἥξεις</quote> </cit> , <foreign xml:lang="lat">sc.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰς τὸ ὀλέσθαι</quote> . The MS. <foreign xml:lang="grc">κἄνευ τῶνδε</foreign> could here mean nothing but <quote xml:lang="eng">"e'en apart from these men."</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λυπηθεὶς_ἔσει_816">,=a fut. perf., here implying, <quote xml:lang="eng"> "wilt <emph>soon</emph> be grieved"</quote>(though it could also mean, <quote xml:lang="eng"> "wilt suffer a <emph>lasting</emph> grief"</quote>): so <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 1146" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg004:1146"> <title>O. T.</title> 1146</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ σιωπήσας ἔσει</quote> </cit> ; <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Ant. 1067" ref="Soph. Ant. 1067"> <title>Ant.</title> 1067</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀντιδοὺς ἔσει</quote> </cit>. In prose the part. thus used with <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔσομαι</quote> is the perf., not the aor. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ποίῳ_ἔργῳ_817">, on the warrant of what deed,—since <quote xml:lang="grc">λυπηθεὶς ἔσει</quote> implies that something has already been done to cause the pain which will soon be felt.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σὺν_817">has the same force as in <quote xml:lang="grc">σὺν θεῷ</quote> :— <quote xml:lang="eng"> "with what deed to <emph>support</emph> the threat."</quote> Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 656" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg004:656"> <title>O. T.</title> 656</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν αἰτίᾳ | σὺν ἀφανεῖ λόγῳ … βαλεῖν</quote> </cit>, to accuse one <emph>with the help of</emph> an unproved story. Xen. sometimes has <quote xml:lang="grc">σύν</quote> thus where a simple instrum. dat. would suffice: <cit> <bibl n="Xen. Anab. 3.1.22" ref="Xen. Anab. 3.1.22"> <title>An.</title> 3. 1. 22</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἰέναι ἐπὶ τὸν ἀγῶνα πολὺ σὺν φρονήματι μείζονι</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Xen. Anab. 3.2.8" ref="Xen. Anab. 3.2.8">3. 2. 8</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ...διανοούμεθα σὺν τοῖς ὅπλοις...δίκην ἐπιθεῖναι αὐτοῖς.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

data.quote:
ἐν αἰτίᾳ | σὺν ἀφανεῖ λόγῳ …
									βαλεῖν

ἰέναι ἐπὶ τὸν ἀγῶνα πολὺ σὺν φρονήματι
									μείζονι

εἰ...διανοούμεθα σὺν τοῖς
									ὅπλοις...δίκην ἐπιθεῖναι αὐτοῖς.

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀπειλήσας_ἔχεις_817">=a perf.: cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 577"> <title>O. T.</title> 577</ref>n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὴν_μὲν_818">, Ismene, who left the scene at 509 to make the offerings in the grove. Creon may have seized her, as a hostage, before his entrance at 728; or may have signed to one of his guards to go and do so, when he found that Oedipus was stubborn. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τάδε_820">might be cognate acc.,= <quote xml:lang="grc">τάδε τὰ οἰμώγματα</quote> (cp. <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. Ag. 1307" ref="Aesch. Ag. 1307"> Aesch. <title>Ag.</title> 1307</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ΚΑ. φεῦ, φεῦ. ΧΟ. τί τοῦτ’ ἔφευξας</quote> </cit>;), but it rather means, <quote xml:lang="eng">"this capture."</quote> </gloss>

data.quote:
ΚΑ. φεῦ, φεῦ. ΧΟ. τί τοῦτ’
									ἔφευξας

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐ_χρόνου_821">: see on <ref target="cl397">397</ref> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸν_ἀσεβῆ_823">, because Oedipus is under the protection of the deities (287), and especially because, as he may well suppose, Ismene has been snatched from the sacred grove (cp. on <ref target="cl818">818</ref> ). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θᾶσσον_824">, oft. in impatient command, as 839, <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 581" ref="Soph. Aj. 581"> <title>Ai.</title> 581</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">πύκαζε θᾶσσον</quote> </cit> : <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 430" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg004:430"> <title>O. T.</title> 430</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐκ εἰς ὄλεθρον; οὐχὶ θᾶσσον</quote> </cit>; Write <foreign xml:lang="grc">τὰ νῦν</foreign> rather than <quote xml:lang="grc">τανῦν</quote> , since it is opp. to <quote xml:lang="grc">ἃ πρόσθεν</quote> : <foreign xml:lang="grc">δίκαια</foreign> , predicate.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἴργασαι_824">(his capture of Ismene) need not be changed to <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰργάσω</quote> , since <foreign xml:lang="grc">πρόσθεν</foreign> can mean "already." </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὑμῖν_826">, addressing his guards (723). Cp. the order given by Oed. to pinion the herdsman ( <ref n="Soph. OT 1154"> <title>O. T.</title> 1154</ref>), and by Creon (in <ref n="Soph. Ant. 578"> <title>Ant.</title> 578</ref>) to lead off the sisters.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἂν_εἴη_826">: here in giving a command with cold sternness. Cp. 725 (in request), <ref n="Soph. OT 343"> <title>O. T.</title> 343</ref>(in fixed resolve). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ποῖ_φύγω_828">; cp. on <ref target="cl310">310</ref> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θεῶν_βροτῶν_828"> <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 399" ref="Soph. Aj. 399"> <title>Ai.</title>399</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="trimeter"> <l>οὔτε γὰρ θεῶν γένος οὔθ’ ἁμερίων</l> <l>ἔτ’ ἄξιος βλέπειν τιν’ εἰς ὄνασιν ἀνθρώπων</l> </lg> </quote> </cit> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐχ_ἅψομαι_830">. With these words, Creon steps towards Antigone. His actual seizure of her is marked by the words <foreign xml:lang="grc">τοὺς ἐμοὺς ἄγω</foreign> . The fut., therefore, is more dramatic than <quote xml:lang="grc">ἅπτομαι</quote> would be. And Wecklein's rejection of 830 f. would enfeeble the scene.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τῆς_ἐμῆς_830">, since he considers himself as now the guardian of his nieces,—their father having forfeited all rights at Thebes (cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 1506"> <title>O. T.</title> 1506</ref>n.): <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 536" ref="Soph. El. 536"> <title>El.</title> 536</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλ’ οὐ μετῆν αὐτοῖσι τήν γ’ ἐμὴν κτανεῖν</quote> </cit>. </gloss>

data.quote:
ἀλλ’ οὐ μετῆν αὐτοῖσι τήν γ’ ἐμὴν
									κτανεῖν

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοὺς_ἐμοὺς_832">: cp. 148 <quote xml:lang="grc">σμικροῖς</quote> (=Antigone); <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Ant. 48" ref="Soph. Ant. 48"> <title>Ant.</title> 48</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλ’ οὐδὲν αὐτῷ τῶν ἐμῶν μ’ εἴργειν μέτα</quote> </cit>( <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> from my brother): <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 1448" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg004:1448"> <title>O. T.</title> 1448</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὀρθῶς τῶν γε σῶν τελεῖς ὕπερ</quote> </cit>(for thy sister). </gloss>

data.quote:
ἀλλ’ οὐδὲν αὐτῷ τῶν ἐμῶν μ’ εἴργειν
									μέτα
ὀρθῶς τῶν γε σῶν τελεῖς ὕπερ

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀφήσεις_834">: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 838" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg007:838">838</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">μέθες</quote> </cit>. The former is properly, <quote xml:lang="eng">"allow to depart,"</quote> —the latter, <quote xml:lang="eng">"release from one's grasp"</quote> ; but they differ here only as <quote xml:lang="eng">"let her alone"</quote> from the more specific <quote xml:lang="eng">"unhand her."</quote> Cp. 857 <quote xml:lang="grc">οὔτοι σ’ ἀφήσω</quote> , I will not allow thee to leave Colonus. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἰς_χερῶν_835">, to the test of (afforded by) blows: cp. <quote xml:lang="grc">χειρῶν νόμος</quote> , the arbitrament of blows (as opp. to <quote xml:lang="grc">δίκης νόμος</quote> ), <cit> <bibl n="Hdt. 9.48" ref="Hdt. 9.48">Her. 9.48</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">πρὶν...ἢ συμμίξαι ἡμέας ἐς χειρῶν τε νόμον ἀπικέσθαι</quote> </cit>. <cit> <bibl n="Xen. Cyrop. 2.1.11" ref="Xen. Cyrop. 2.1.11"> Xen. <title>Cyr.</title> 2.1.11</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς χεῖρας συμμίξοντας τοῖς πολεμίοις.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

data.quote:
πρὶν...ἢ συμμίξαι ἡμέας ἐς χειρῶν τε
									νόμον ἀπικέσθαι

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἶ_835">as in the common phrase <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰς χεῖρας ἰέναι τινί</quote> , or <quote xml:lang="grc">συνιέναι</quote> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἴργου_836">, said as the Chorus approach him threateningly: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 890" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg004:890"> <title>O. T.</title> 890</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τῶν ἀσέπτων ἔρξεται</quote> </cit> (n.).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μωμένου_836">, meditating, designing: a part. used once in dialogue by Soph. ( <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Trach. 1136" ref="Soph. Trach. 1136"> <title>Tr.</title> 1136</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἥμαρτε χρηστὰ μωμένη</quote> </cit>), and twice in lyrics by Aesch. ( <ref n="Aesch. Lib. 45"> <title>Ch.</title> 45</ref>, <ref n="Aesch. Lib. 441">441</ref> ). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πόλει_837">: <quote xml:lang="grc">ταῖς Θήβαις</quote> schol. The accent of <quote xml:lang="grc">μάχηι</quote> in the MSS. cannot weigh in deciding between <quote xml:lang="grc">μάχει</quote> and <foreign xml:lang="grc">μαχεῖ</foreign> , since such errors of accent are countless; and the fut. is distinctly better here. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐκ_ἠγόρευον_838">...; a familiar phrase; <cit> <bibl n="Aristoph. Ach. 41" ref="Aristoph. Ach. 41"> Aristoph. <title>Ach.</title> 41</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐκ ἠγόρευον; τοῦτ’ ἐκεῖν’ οὑγὼ )λεγον</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Aristoph. Pl. 102" ref="Aristoph. Pl. 102"> <title>Plut.</title> 102</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐκ ἠγόρευον ὅτι παρέξειν πράγματα | ἠμελλέτην μοι;</quote> </cit> <cit> <bibl n="Aristoph. Cl. 1456" ref="Aristoph. Cl. 1456"> <title>Nub.</title>1456</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τί δῆτα ταῦτ’ οὔ μοι τότ’ ἠγορεύετε</quote> </cit>; So <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 973" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg004:973"> <title>O. T.</title> 973</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὔκουν ἐγώ σοι ταῦτα προὔλεγον πάλαι</quote> </cit>;—Oed. alludes to 587, 653. </gloss>

data.quote:
οὐκ ἠγόρευον ὅτι παρέξειν πράγματα |
									ἠμελλέτην μοι;

τί δῆτα ταῦτ’ οὔ μοι τότ’
									ἠγορεύετε

οὔκουν ἐγώ σοι ταῦτα προὔλεγον
									πάλαι

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μὴ_κρατεῖς_839">, do not give orders in matters where you are not master. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἃ</quote> is not for <quote xml:lang="grc">ὧν</quote> , but is cogn. accus. (or acc. of respect), as <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 1522" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg004:1522"> <title>O. T.</title> 1522</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">πάντα μὴ βούλου κρατεῖν· | καὶ γὰρ ἁκράτησας</quote> </cit>. For the gen. in a like sense cp. <cit> <bibl n="Hdt. 9.16" ref="Hdt. 9.16">Her. 9.16</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐχθίστη...ὀδύνη..., πολλὰ φρονέοντα μηδενὸς κρατέειν</quote> </cit>, to have many presentiments, and power over nothing. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Ant. 664" ref="Soph. Ant. 664"> <title>Ant.</title> 664</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τοὐπιτάσσειν τοῖς κρατύνουσιν</quote> </cit>, to dictate to one's masters. <cit> <bibl n="Theoc. 15" ref="Theoc. 15">Theocr. 15. 90</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">πασάμενος ἐπίτασσε</quote> </cit>(wait till you are our master before you give us orders).</gloss>

data.quote:
πάντα μὴ βούλου κρατεῖν· | καὶ γὰρ
									ἁκράτησας

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="χαλᾶν_σοι_840">, like <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐκ ἀφήσεις</quote> and <quote xml:lang="grc"> μέθες</quote> , is said to <title>Creon.</title> Creon's <foreign xml:lang="grc">σοί</foreign> , a mocking echo of theirs, is said to <emph>the guard:</emph> <quote xml:lang="eng"> "and <title>I</title> tell <emph> thee</emph> to start on thy journey."</quote>If it were said to <emph>the Chorus</emph> , the sense would be either, (1) <quote xml:lang="eng">"and I tell thee to begone,"</quote> or (2) <quote xml:lang="eng">"and I tell thee that [she] is to go"</quote> : but (1) is not idiomatic, and (2) is impossible. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πρόβαθ_βᾶτε_841">, as oft. esp. in Eur., <foreign xml:lang="lat">e.g.</foreign> <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Orest. 181" ref="Eur. Orest. 181"> <title>Or.</title> 181</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">διοιχόμεθ’, οἰχόμεθ’.</quote> </cit> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὧδε_841">= <quote xml:lang="grc">δεῦρο</quote> ( <ref n="Soph. OT 7"> <title>O. T.</title> 7</ref>n.): cp. 182. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔντοποι_841">, the other dwellers at Colonus. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πόλις_σθένει_842">: our city—yea, our city—is being brought low by sheer strength: <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἐναίρεται</foreign> , because the majesty of the State is destroyed when its asylum is violated. In <foreign xml:lang="grc">πόλις ἐμά</foreign> , the stress is on the first word, not on the second.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σθένει_842"> with <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐναίρεται</quote> seems to be sufficiently defended by <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Ba. 953" ref="Eur. Ba. 953"> Eur. <title>Bacch.</title> 953</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ σθένει νικητέον | γυναῖκας</quote> </cit>, where it differs from <quote xml:lang="grc">βίᾳ</quote> only as it differs here,— <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> as meaning strictly, <quote xml:lang="eng">"by an exertion of strength,"</quote> not, <quote xml:lang="eng">"by violence"</quote> : cp. <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 1127 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπεσπάραξεν ὦμον, οὐχ ὑπὸ σθένους</quote> , not by her own strength (since the god made it easy for her). Some place a point at <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἐμά</foreign> , taking <foreign xml:lang="grc">σθένει</foreign> with <foreign xml:lang="grc">πρόβαθ’</foreign> , come forth <emph>in</emph> strength: but such a use of <quote xml:lang="grc">σθένει</quote> <emph> alone</emph> is harsher than those in which <quote xml:lang="grc">σθένει</quote> takes an adj., as <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπελθὼν οὐκ ἐλάσσονι σθένει</quote> ( <ref n="Soph. Aj. 438"> <title>Ai.</title> 438</ref>), or <quote xml:lang="grc">παντὶ σθένει</quote> , <quote xml:lang="eng">"with all one's might."</quote> Rather than Wecklein's <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἔτ’ οὐ σθένει</foreign> , I would propose—if any change were needed— <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="dochmiacs"> <l> πόλις ἐναίρεται, πόλις· <emph>ἴτ’, ἀσθενεῖ</emph> </l> <l>πρόβαθ’ ὧδέ μοι</l> </lg> </quote>. But no change seems needful. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μοι_845">: ethic dat.; cp. 81. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὦ_τάλας_847">: cp. 753. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐκ_σκήπτροιν_848">, by means of these two supports,—the art. being omitted, as 471 <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῦτο χεῦμ’</quote> . This is simpler than to construe, <quote xml:lang="eng"> "with the help of these (girls) <emph> as</emph> supports."</quote>(For <quote xml:lang="grc">τούτοιν</quote> , as fem. dual, cp. on <ref target="cl445">445</ref> .) We should then have to take it as a case of the pron. assimilated to the gender of the predicate (see on <quote xml:lang="grc">ταύτην...παῦλαν</quote> 88).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐκ_848">refers to the <quote xml:lang="grc">σκῆπτρα</quote> as an antecedent condition of his walking. Essentially the same use, though under slightly different phases, appears in <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 807" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg007:807">807</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐξ ἅπαντος</quote> </cit> : <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Trach. 875" ref="Soph. Trach. 875"> <title>Tr.</title> 875</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">( βέβηκεν ) ἐξ ἀκινήτου ποδός</quote> </cit> : <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Phil. 91" ref="Soph. Phil. 91"> <title>Ph.</title> 91</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐξ ἑνὸς ποδός</quote> </cit> : <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 742" ref="Soph. El. 742"> <title>El.</title> 742</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὠρθοῦθ’ ὁ τλήμων ὀρθὸς ἐξ ὀρθῶν δίφρων</quote> </cit>. </gloss>

data.quote:
ὠρθοῦθ’ ὁ τλήμων ὀρθὸς ἐξ ὀρθῶν
									δίφρων

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σκήπτροιν_848">: cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 1109">1109</ref> : <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Hec. 280" ref="Eur. Hec. 280"> Eur. <title>Hec.</title> 280</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="trimeter"> <l>ἥδ’ ἀντὶ τολλῶν ἐστί μοι παραψυχή,</l> <l>πόλις, τιθήνη, βάκτρον, ἡγεμὼν ὁδοῦ</l> </lg> </quote> </cit> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οδοιπορήσῃς_849">. As between <quote xml:lang="grc">‐εις</quote> and <quote xml:lang="grc"> ‐ηις</quote> in verbal endings, neither L nor any of our MSS. has authority. The reason for preferring the aor. subj. here is one of usage. <foreign xml:lang="grc">οὐ μὴ ὁδοιπορήσῃς</foreign> is a denial: <foreign xml:lang="grc">οὐ μὴ ὁδοιπορήσεις</foreign> , a prohibition. The latter is grammatically as right as the other, but does not suit this context. (The remarks on 177 refer to the 1st and 3rd pers. fut. ind., not to the 2nd.) </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="νικᾶν_849">, to worst,—by carrying your point against them ( <emph>not</emph> with ref. to future defeats of Thebans by Athenians, 621). Cp. 1204: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 1353" ref="Soph. Aj. 1353"> <title>Ai.</title> 1353</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">παῦσαι· κρατεῖς τοι τῶν φίλων νικώμενος</quote> </cit>, thou conquerest, when thy friends conquer thee. </gloss>

data.quote:
παῦσαι· κρατεῖς τοι τῶν φίλων
									νικώμενος

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τύραννος_851">, one of the royal house: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Trach. 316" ref="Soph. Trach. 316"> <title>Tr.</title> 316</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">μὴ τῶν τυράννων</quote> </cit> ; <quote xml:lang="eng">"is she of the royal stock?"</quote> The Creon of <ref n="Soph. OT 588"> <title>O. T.</title> 588</ref>does not wish <quote xml:lang="grc">τύραννος εἶναι</quote> (to be <emph>king</emph> ) <quote xml:lang="grc">μᾶλλον ἢ τύραννα δρᾶν</quote> : but the captor of the blind man's daughters must seek a touch of dignity from any source. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γνώσει_τάδε_852"> , <quote xml:lang="eng"> "thou wilt understand <emph>these things</emph> "</quote>(=thy present acts in their true bearings),—explained by <foreign xml:lang="grc">ὁθούνεκ’</foreign> , etc., <quote xml:lang="eng">"viz., that"</quote> etc.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αὑτὸν_852">= <quote xml:lang="grc">σεαυτόν</quote> : so 930, 1356: but <quote xml:lang="grc">αὑτὸν</quote> = <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐμαυτόν</quote> 966, <ref n="Soph. OT 138"> <title>O. T.</title> 138</ref>(n.). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="βίᾳ_φίλων_854">applies to his <emph>former</emph> conduct, since, in searching out his origin, he acted against the passionate entreaties of Iocasta ( <ref n="Soph. OT 1060"> <title>O. T.</title> 1060ff. </ref>). Greek idiom uses a parataxis, <quote xml:lang="grc">οὔτε νῦν ... οὔτε πρόσθεν</quote> , where ours would subordinate the second clause to the first, "now, <emph>as</emph> before": cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 308">308</ref> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὀργῇ_δούς_855">: cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 1182">1182</ref> : <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 331" ref="Soph. El. 331"> <title>El.</title> 331</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">θυμῷ ματαίῳ μὴ χαρίζεσθαι κενά</quote> </cit> : <cit> <bibl>Cratinus fr. inc. 146</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔσθιε καὶ σῇ γαστρὶ δίδου χάριν</quote> </cit>. We remember his blow at Laïus ( <cit> <quote xml:lang="grc">παίω δῑ ὀργῆς</quote> <bibl n="Soph. OT 807" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg004:807"> <title>O. T.</title> 807</bibl> </cit>) — his anger with Teiresias ( <cit> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς ὀργῆς ἔχω,</quote> <bibl n="Soph. OT 345" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg004:345"> <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 345</bibl> </cit>) — his anger with Iocasta ( <ref n="Soph. OT 1067"> <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 1067</ref>) — his frantic self-blinding ( <ref n="Soph. OT 1268"> <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 1268</ref>). </gloss>

data.quote:
ἔσθιε καὶ σῇ γαστρὶ δίδου
									χάριν

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τῶνδε_857">. So the <emph>plur.</emph> <quote xml:lang="grc">αἵδε</quote> of the two sisters below, <ref n="Soph. OC 1107">1107</ref> , <ref n="Soph. OC 1367">1367</ref> , <ref n="Soph. OC 1379">1379</ref> (immediately after the <emph>masc.</emph> dual <quote xml:lang="grc"> τοιώδ’</quote> , referring to the brothers), <ref n="Soph. OC 1668"> 1668</ref> ; <quote xml:lang="grc">τάσδ’</quote> <ref n="Soph. OC 1121"> 1121</ref> , <ref n="Soph. OC 1146">1146</ref> , <ref n="Soph. OC 1634">1634</ref> , <ref n="Soph. OT 1507"> <title>O. T.</title> 1507</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Ant. 579"> <title>Ant.</title> 579</ref>. On the other hand the <emph>dual</emph> of <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅδε</quote> occurs only thrice in Soph.; above, <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 445" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg007:445">445</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῖνδε</quote> </cit> : <cit> <quote xml:lang="grc">τώδε</quote> <bibl n="Soph. El. 981" ref="Soph. El. 981"> <title>El.</title> 981f.</bibl> </cit> <foreign xml:lang="lat">bis.</foreign>(Below, <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 1121" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg007:1121">1121,</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τάδε</quote> </cit>is a corrupt <foreign xml:lang="lat">v.l.</foreign> for <quote xml:lang="grc">τάσδε</quote> .) It is surely needless, then, to write <foreign xml:lang="grc">τοῖνδε</foreign> here. But Reisig's plea for the plur. is over-subtle,— that it contrasts with the <emph> extenuating</emph> tone of <quote xml:lang="grc">τούτοιν</quote> in <ref n="Soph. OC 859">859</ref> (merely two). Rather Creon uses the dual because he is thinking of the two sisters <emph>together</emph> as the " <emph>two supports</emph> " of Oed. ( <ref n="Soph. OC 848">848</ref> , <ref n="Soph. OC 445">445</ref> ). The plur. differs from the dual simply by the <emph>absence</emph> of any stress on the notion of "a pair." The Chorus are thinking how he had first seized Ismene <ref n="Soph. OC 818">(818)</ref> and then Antigone. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ῥύσιον_858">: Then thou shalt soon deposit even a greater security for my city.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πόλει_858">=Thebes, as in Creon's former words, 837 <quote xml:lang="grc">πόλει μαχεῖ. ῥύσιος</quote> denotes what one draws to oneself, carries off, (1) as booty, (2) as a security, (3) in reprisal. Here <foreign xml:lang="grc"> θήσεις</foreign> points to (2), since <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐνέχυρον τιθέναι</quote> , to deposit a pledge, was a regular phrase: <cit> <bibl n="Aristoph. Eccl. 754" ref="Aristoph. Eccl. 754"> Aristoph. <title>Eccl.</title> 754</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="trimeter"> <l>πότερον μετοικιζόμενος ἐξενήνοχας</l> <l>αὔτ’, ἢ φέρεις ἐνέχυρα θήσων;</l> </lg> </quote> </cit>"or are you taking them to be deposited as securities?" <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Laws 820e" ref="Plat. Laws 820e"> Plat. <title>Legg.</title> 820 E</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐνέχυρα...τοὺς θέντας <note anchored="true" place="inline" resp="RCJ" xml:lang="eng">(those who have given the pledges)... </note> τοὺς θεμένους <note anchored="true" place="inline" resp="RCJ" xml:lang="eng">(those to whom they have been given).</note> </quote> </cit> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πόλει_858_2">dat. of interest, as <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑποτιθέναι</quote> <quote xml:lang="eng">"to mortgage"</quote> takes a dat. of the mortgagee: <cit> <bibl n="Dem. 27.25" ref="Dem. 27.25">Dem. or. 27 § 25</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁ ὑποθεὶς τῷ πατρὶ τἀνδράποδα</quote> </cit>.—The version, <quote xml:lang="eng">"you will cause a greater prize to be taken from Athens,"</quote> is inadmissible.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θήσεις_πόλει_858">could not mean, <quote xml:lang="eng"> " <emph>cause for</emph> Athens,"</quote>in the sense, <quote xml:lang="eng"> " <emph>cause to be taken from</emph> Athens."</quote>If <quote xml:lang="grc">θήσεις</quote> meant <quote xml:lang="eng">"cause"</quote> (instead of <quote xml:lang="eng"> "pay"</quote> ), <quote xml:lang="grc">πόλει</quote> would still be the city which <emph>received</emph> the <quote xml:lang="grc">ῥύσιον</quote> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐφάψομαι_858"> : <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. Supp. 412" ref="Aesch. Supp. 412"> Aesch. <title>Suppl.</title> 412</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ μήτε δῆρις ῥυσίων ἐφάψεται</quote> </cit>, (and so) <quote xml:lang="eng">"that the foeman shall not lay hands on you as prizes"</quote> (where the king of Argos is speaking to the Danaïdes whom he protects). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πεπράξεται_861"> , <quote xml:lang="eng">"will have been done"</quote> : <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> will be done forthwith: <cit> <bibl n="Dem. 19.74" ref="Dem. 19.74">Dem. or. 19 § 74</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔφη...ταῦτα πεπράξεσθαι δυοῖν ἢ τριῶν ἡμερῶν</quote> </cit>. Cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 1146"> <title>O. T.</title> 1146</ref>n. </gloss>

data.quote:
ἔφη...ταῦτα πεπράξεσθαι δυοῖν ἢ τριῶν
									ἡμερῶν

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἢν_γ_862">. Piderit is clearly right (I think) in giving this verse to the Chorus, not to Creon. Creon, who has long since dropped the semblance of courtesy with which he began (759), cannot, of course, mean to express serious deference for the wishes of Theseus; while, as an ironical defiance, the words would be extremely tame. In the mouth of the Chorus, however, the threat has point, since they know their king's public resolve (656); it has also dramatic force, since he is soon to appear (887). The words of Oed. (863) refer to 861.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀπειργάθῃ_862">: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 1271" ref="Soph. El. 1271"> <title>El.</title> 1271</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰργαθεῖν</quote> </cit>(and so Eur.): <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. Eum. 566" ref="Aesch. Eum. 566"> Aesch. <title>Eum.</title> 566</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">κατειργαθοῦ</quote> </cit>(aor. imper. midd.). The forms <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐέργαθεν, ἀποέργαθε</quote> (aor., or, as some would call them, impf.) are Homeric. See n. on <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 651" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg004:651"> <title>O. T.</title> 651</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰκάθω</quote> </cit> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φθέγμ_863">, <quote xml:lang="eng">"voice,"</quote> rather than <quote xml:lang="eng"> "word"</quote> : the conj. <foreign xml:lang="grc">θρέμμ’</foreign> (Blaydes) would efface an expressive touch. <foreign xml:lang="grc"> ψαύεις</foreign> might be defended as present of intention or attempt (cp. on <ref target="cl992">993</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">κτείνοι</quote> ): but <foreign xml:lang="grc">ψαύσεις</foreign> is more natural, and expresses indignation with greater force. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αὐδῶ_σιωπᾶν_864">. Creon forbids the utterance of the curse which he forebodes; and the injunction reminds Oedipus that he is near the Awful Goddesses who impose abstinence from all ill-omened words. <quote xml:lang="eng">"Nay"</quote> ( <quote xml:lang="grc">γάρ</quote> ), he cries, <quote xml:lang="eng"> "may they suffer me to utter one imprecation more ( <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔτι"</quote> ).</quote> <foreign xml:lang="grc"> γάρ</foreign>implies, <quote xml:lang="eng">"I will not yet be mute"</quote> ; cp. also its use in wishes, <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ γάρ, εἴθε γάρ</quote> , etc. <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἔτι</foreign> recalls the former imprecation on his sons (421 ff.). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄφωνον_ἀρᾶς_864">: cp. on <ref target="cl677">677</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc"> ἀνήνεμον...χειμώνων.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τῆς_864">is a certain correction of the MS. <foreign xml:lang="grc">γῆς</foreign> ( <quote xml:lang="grc">Τ</quote> for <quote xml:lang="grc">Γ</quote> ). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὅς_866">, with caus. force, <quote xml:lang="eng">"since thou hast..."</quote> : see on <quote xml:lang="grc">οἵτινες</quote> , 263.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ψιλὸν_ὄμμ_866">can mean only <quote xml:lang="eng">"a defenceless eye,"</quote> <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> a defenceless maiden (Antigone) who was to him as eyesight. The phrase has bitter point, since Creon himself, in his smooth speech, had pathetically described Antigone as <quote xml:lang="grc">τοὐπιόντος ἁρπάσαι</quote> (752). It is also less bold in Greek than in English, owing to the common figurative use of <foreign xml:lang="grc">ὄμμα</foreign> , as if he had said, <quote xml:lang="eng"> "my defenceless <emph>darling</emph> "</quote>(cp. on <ref n="Soph. OT 987"> <title>O. T.</title> 987</ref>).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ψιλὸν_866">should not be taken as acc. <emph>masc.</emph> with <foreign xml:lang="grc">με</foreign> : this would be tame and forced. Cp. below 1029 <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ ψιλὸν οὐδ’ ἄσκευον</quote> , not <emph>without allies</emph> or instruments: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Phil. 953" ref="Soph. Phil. 953"> <title>Ph.</title> 953 </bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ψιλός, οὐκ ἔχων τροφήν</quote> </cit> (when <emph>stripped of his bow</emph> ).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀποσπάσας_866">takes a double acc. (like <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀφαιρεῖν</quote> , etc.): this is so natural that we need not desire <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅς γ’</quote> or <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅς μου</quote> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐξοίχει_867">, as 894 <quote xml:lang="grc">οἴχεται...ἀποσπάσας</quote> , though he is still present: so 1009 <quote xml:lang="grc">οἴχει λαβών</quote> . As <quote xml:lang="grc">οἴχομαι</quote> cannot have a <emph>pres.</emph> sense, the departure meant can be only that of his guards (847): so that <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐξοίχει</quote> merely adds the notion of <quote xml:lang="eng"> " <emph>away</emph> "</quote>to <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἀποσπάσας</foreign> .—Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 809" ref="Soph. El. 809"> <title>El.</title> 809</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀποσπάσας γὰρ τῆς ἐμῆς οἴχει φρενός</quote> </cit>, etc. </gloss>

data.quote:
ἀποσπάσας γὰρ τῆς ἐμῆς οἴχει
									φρενός

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σέ_αὐτὸν_868">seems preferable to <foreign xml:lang="grc">σὲ καὐτὸν</foreign> , since <quote xml:lang="grc">τε … καὶ</quote> was usual in such formulas with <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐτός</quote> , cp. 462, 559, 952, 1009, 1125: though <foreign xml:lang="grc">τε</foreign> was sometimes omitted when a third clause followed, as <cit> <bibl n="Antiph. 5.11" ref="Antiph. 5.11">Antiph. or. 5 § 11</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐξώλειαν αὑτῷ καὶ γένει καὶ οἰκίᾳ τῇ σῇ ἐπαρώμενον</quote> </cit>. I hardly think that <foreign xml:lang="grc">θεῶν</foreign> can be right. It would be partitive, <quote xml:lang="eng">"of the gods, the allseeing Sun."</quote> When a partitive gen. stands thus, it ought to be emphatic, as in <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 1485" ref="Soph. El. 1485"> <title>El.</title> 1485</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τί γὰρ βροτῶν ἂν σὺν κακοῖς μεμιγμένων | θνήσκειν ὁ μέλλων</quote> </cit>etc. But here there is no stress on <quote xml:lang="eng"> " <emph>gods</emph> "</quote>as opp. to other beings. I should prefer <foreign xml:lang="grc">θεὸς</foreign> , from which <foreign xml:lang="grc"> θεῶν</foreign> may have arisen by the carelessness of a copyist who connected it with <foreign xml:lang="grc">γένος</foreign> . </gloss>

data.quote:
ἐξώλειαν αὑτῷ καὶ γένει καὶ οἰκίᾳ τῇ
									σῇ ἐπαρώμενον

τί γὰρ βροτῶν ἂν σὺν κακοῖς μεμιγμένων
									| θνήσκειν ὁ μέλλων

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Ἥλιος_869">: invoked <ref n="Soph. OT 660"> <title>O. T.</title> 660</ref>(n.) <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ τὸν πάντων θεῶν θεὸν πρόμον Ἅλιον</quote> , as the all-seeing god whom no deceit can escape.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="βίον_869">cogn. acc., instead of <quote xml:lang="grc">γῆρας.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κἀμὲ_869">: see on <ref target="cl53">53</ref> . In the <title>Antigone</title> Creon's wife Eurydicè and his son Haemon com mit suicide,—another son, Megareus, having already devoted his life for Thebes. But in Creon's own person, at least, the curse was fulfilled by his surviving all that he loved best. (Cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1317"> <title>Ant.</title> 1317</ref>ff.) </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὁρᾶτε_871">: he calls on them to witness the unnatural imprecation: cp. 813 <quote xml:lang="grc">μαρτύρομαι</quote> . </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔργοις_873">: cp. on <ref target="cl782">782</ref> .</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ῥήμασιν_873">is said with a bitter consciousness of impotence at this critical moment. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μοῦνος_875">, as 991, 1250: cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 1418"> <title>O. T.</title> 1418</ref>n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="βραδύς_875">(cp. 306) seems more fitting here than <foreign xml:lang="grc">βαρύς</foreign> , which has no MS. warrant except L's superscript variant <quote xml:lang="grc">γηρῶν</quote> (sic) <quote xml:lang="grc">βαρύς</quote> ,—perh. a corruption of <quote xml:lang="grc">γήρᾳ βαρύς</quote> . In <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 17" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg004:17"> <title>O. T.</title> 17</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">σὺν γήρᾳ βαρεῖς</quote> </cit>=weighed down with age, while in <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 1017" ref="Soph. Aj. 1017"> <title>Ai.</title> 1017</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν γήρᾳ βαρύς</quote> </cit>=peevish in old age. The conjecture <foreign xml:lang="grc">βραχύς</foreign> was intended to mean "weak" <ref n="Soph. OC 880">(880)</ref> . </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἰὼ_τάλας_876">: see on <ref target="cl833">833</ref> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τάνδε_νεμῶ_879">, I will no longer reckon Athens a city. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 1080" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg004:1080"> <title>O. T.</title> 1080</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐμαυτὸν παῖδα τῆς Τύχης νέμων</quote> </cit> : <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 597" ref="Soph. El. 597"> <title>El.</title> 597</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="trimeter"> <l>καί σ’ ἔγωγε δεσπότιν</l> <l>ἢ μητέρ’ οὐκ ἔλασσον εἰς ἡμᾶς νέμω</l> </lg> </quote> </cit>. The <emph>fut.</emph> is better than the <emph>pres.</emph> here, since the latter would assume Creon's triumph. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοῖς_δικαίοις_880">, instrumental dat., by means of <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰ δίκαια</quote> , <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> , by having justice on one's side. <quote xml:lang="eng">"In a just cause, one feeble man is stronger than a city."</quote> Cp. fr. 76 <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῖς γὰρ δικαίοις ἀντέχειν οὐ ῥᾴδιον</quote> : fr. 78 <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ γὰρ δικαία γλῶσσ’ ἔχει κράτος μέγα</quote> . Here he speaks of the moral force with which <quote xml:lang="grc">Δίκη</quote> inspires her champion, while in 957 he admits himself to be physically helpless— <quote xml:lang="grc">κεἰ δίκαῑ ὅμως λέγω.</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="βραχὺς_880">, of slight physical strength: cp. 586: and for <foreign xml:lang="grc"> μέγαν</foreign> cp. on <ref target="cl148">148</ref> . </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τά_881">= <quote xml:lang="grc">ἅ</quote> : cp. on <ref target="cl747">747</ref> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Ζεύς_οὔ_882">. The lacuna certainly <emph>preceded</emph> these words. The words in the strophe answering to <quote xml:lang="grc">τά γ’ οὐ τελεῖ</quote> and to the lacuna are <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 838" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg007:838">838 f.</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ΧΟ. μέθες χεροῖν | τὴν παῖδα θᾶσσον</quote> </cit>. It is probable, then, that the lost words here belonged to the Chorus, being such as <quote xml:lang="grc">Ζεύς μοι ξυνίστω</quote> . </gloss>

data.quote:
ΧΟ. μέθες χεροῖν | τὴν παῖδα
									θᾶσσον

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὕβρις_883">: for the quantity, cp. 442 n.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀνεκτέα_883">, nom. neut. plur.: cp. on <ref target="cl495">495</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁδωτά</quote> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πρόμοι_884">, invoking a higher power than the <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔντοποι</quote> of Colonus (841), prepares the entrance of the king. For the plur., meaning Theseus, cp. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄνακτας</quote> 295 n., 1667.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πέραν_δή_885"> , <quote xml:lang="eng">"yonder men"</quote> (with a gesture in the direction taken by Creon's guards) <quote xml:lang="eng"> "are already passing <emph>towards the other side.</emph> "</quote>Elmsley wrote <foreign xml:lang="grc">πέρα</foreign> , which as adv. would mean "further." But <foreign xml:lang="grc">πέραν</foreign> is right, since the Chorus is thinking of a passage from the Attic to the Boeotian side of the frontier, as of a passage across a river. <foreign xml:lang="grc">πέρα</foreign> is <foreign xml:lang="lat">ultra</foreign> , <quote xml:lang="eng"> " <emph>to some point beyond</emph> "</quote>a line which is either left to be understood, or expressed in the gen.: <foreign xml:lang="grc">πέραν</foreign> is <foreign xml:lang="lat">trans</foreign> , <quote xml:lang="eng"> " <emph> on</emph> , or <emph>to</emph> , the <emph>further side</emph> "</quote>of a river, sea, or intervening space. <foreign xml:lang="grc">περῶσι</foreign> implies only that the fugitives are <emph>on their way to</emph> the border,—not that they are now actually crossing it. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δή_885"> nearly= <quote xml:lang="grc">ἤδη</quote> : <ref n="Soph. OT 968"> <title>O. T.</title> 968</ref>n. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="βωμὸν_888">, Poseidon's altar at Colonus: see on <ref target="cl55">55</ref> .</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔσχετ_888">: see on <ref target="cl429">429</ref> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔγνων_891">: so <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 1325" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg004:1325"> <title>O. T.</title> 1325</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">γιγνώσκω σαφῶς | καίπερ σκοτεινός, τήν γε σὴν αὐδὴν ὅμως</quote> </cit>. </gloss>

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὰ_ταῦτα_893">; The art. is prefixed to <quote xml:lang="grc">ποῖος</quote> when it asks for further definition: <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Crat. 395D" ref="Plat. Crat. 395D"> Plat. <title>Crat.</title> 395D</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ΣΩ. εἰ ἀληθῆ</quote> </cit> ( <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐστὶ</quote> ) <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰ περὶ αὐτὸν λεγόμενα. ΕΠΜ. τὰ ποῖα ταῦτα</quote> ; </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οἴχεται_894">: cp. on <ref target="cl867">867</ref> .</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὴν_μόνην_894">: his sons are as dead to him (cp. 445). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="περ_896">in the thesis of the 3rd foot is remarkable, and very unpleasing. Rhythm and sense would both gain if we could read <quote xml:lang="grc">οἶα καὶ πέπονθ’</quote> ( <quote xml:lang="eng"> " <emph>indeed</emph> suffered"</quote> ). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὔκουν_ἀναγκάσει_897"> , <quote xml:lang="eng">"will not some one, then, compel?"</quote> = <quote xml:lang="eng">"then let some one compel"</quote> : cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 430"> <title>O. T.</title> 430</ref>n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τούσδε_βωμοὺς_897">: the plur. might be merely poetical for the sing. (888, cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1006"> <title>Ant.</title> 1006</ref>), but here perh. refers to the association of Poseidon <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἵππιος</quote> with Athena <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἱππία</quote> (1069). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σπεύδειν_ῥυτῆρος_899"> : to hasten from the sacrifice, some on foot, others on horseback, with slack rein. The worshippers of the <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἵππιος</quote> and <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἱππία</quote> are in part <quote xml:lang="grc">ἱππεῖς</quote> (cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 1070">1070</ref> ), and have their horses with them. The place of <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἀπὸ ρ’</foreign> . is due to the fact that these horsemen are the important pursuers, <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἄνιππον</foreign> being added merely to give the notion of a pursuit <foreign xml:lang="fra">en masse.</foreign> Thus there is some formal resemblance to <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Ant. 1108" ref="Soph. Ant. 1108"> <title>Ant.</title> 1108</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="lyric"> <l>ἴτ’, ἴτ’, ὀπάονες</l> <l>οἵ τ’ ὄντες οἵ τ’ ἀπόντες</l> </lg> </quote> </cit>, though there "present and absent" is merely a colloquial phrase for <quote xml:lang="eng">"every one."</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀπὸ_ῥυτῆρος_899"> , <quote xml:lang="eng">"away from the rein,"</quote> <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="eng">"unchecked by the rein,"</quote> <foreign xml:lang="lat">immissis habenis</foreign> : Phrynichus <foreign xml:lang="lat">ap.</foreign> Bekker <title>Anecd.</title> p. 24 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπὸ</quote> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁυτῆρος τρέχειν ἵππον· οἷον ἀπὸ χαλινοῦ ἣ ἄνευ χαλινοῦ</quote> . Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 1127" ref="Soph. El. 1127"> <title>El.</title> 1127</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπ’ ἐλπίδων</quote> </cit>, contrary to my hopes: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Trach. 389" ref="Soph. Trach. 389"> <title>Tr.</title> 389</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐκ ἀπὸ γνώμης</quote> </cit>, not against my judgment: and so <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐκ ἀπὸ τρόπου</quote> (not unreasonably), <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐκ ἀπὸ καιροῦ</quote> , etc. <cit> <bibl n="Plut. Dio 42" ref="Plut. Dio 42"> Plut. <title>Dion</title> 42</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὗτοι διελάσαντες τὴν ὁδὸν ἵπποις ἀπὸ ῥυτῆρος ἧκον εἰς Λεοντίνους τῆς ἡμέρας ἤδη καταφερομένης</quote> </cit>, <quote xml:lang="eng"> "having ridden the whole distance <emph>at full speed.</emph> "</quote>For the <quote xml:lang="grc">ο—</quote> in <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπὸ</quote> before <quote xml:lang="grc">ῥ</quote> , cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 712"> <title>Ant.</title> 712</ref>n. </gloss>

οὗτοι διελάσαντες τὴν ὁδὸν ἵπποις ἀπὸ
									ῥυτῆρος ἧκον εἰς Λεοντίνους τῆς ἡμέρας ἤδη
									καταφερομένης

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δίστομοι_ὁδοί_899">. See map in Appendix on 1059. The two roads meant are probably:—(1) A road leading from Colonus, north of the Sacred Way, to the pass now called Daphnè, a depression in the range of Mount Aegaleos through which the Sacred Way issued from the plain of Athens, after which it skirted the shores of the bay of Eleusis. The beginning of this road is shown by the map in the Introduction. (2) A road diverging from the former in a N.W. direction, and going round the N. end of the same range of Aegaleos, at a point some miles N. of the Daphnè pass, into the Thriasian plain. By either route the captors could gain the pass of Dryoscephalae, over Mount Cithaeron, leading from Attica into Boeotia. The hope of Theseus is that the pursuers may reach the point of bifurcation before the captors, since it is conceivable that the latter should wait to be joined by their master, Creon. see on <ref target="cl1054">1054</ref> ff. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μάλιστα_899"> with </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔνθα_899"> , lit., <quote xml:lang="eng"> "to <emph>about</emph> the place where"</quote>: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Hdt. 1.191" ref="Hdt. 1.191">Her. 1.191</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνδρὶ ὡς ἐς μέσον μηρὸν μάλιστά κῃ</quote> </cit>, <quote xml:lang="eng">"just about to the height of a man's thigh."</quote> </gloss>

data.quote:
ἀνδρὶ ὡς ἐς μέσον μηρὸν μάλιστά
									κῃ

Orest. 757" ref="Eur. Orest. 757"> Eur. <title>Or.</title> 757</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">λέξον· διὰ φόβου γὰρ ἔρχομαι</quote> </cit> , <quote xml:lang="eng">"for I begin to fear."</quote> <cit> <bibl n="Hdt. 1.169" ref="Hdt. 1.169">Her. 1.169</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">διὰ μάχης...ἀπίκοντο Ἁρπάγῳ</quote> </cit>, gave him battle. Cp. on <ref n="Soph. OT 773"> <title>O. T.</title> 773</ref>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μεθῆκ_906">, suggesting a relaxed grasp, is better than the more general <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀφῆκ’</quote> here: cp. 834. </gloss>

Ant. 404"> <title>Ant.</title> 404</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">θάπτουσαν ὃν σὺ τὸν νεκρὸν | ἀπεῖπας</quote> </cit>, where the schol. quotes Cratinus (fr. 159), <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅνπερ Φιλοκλέης τὸν λόγον διέφθορεν</quote> .</gloss>

θάπτουσαν ὃν σὺ τὸν νεκρὸν |

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἁρμοσθήσεται_908">, he shall be brought to order, regulated: <cit> <bibl n="Aristoph. Kn. 1235" ref="Aristoph. Kn. 1235"> Aristoph. <title>Eq.</title> 1235</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="trimeter"> <l>ΚΛ. παῖς ὢν ἐφοίτας ἐς τίνος διδασκάλου;</l> <l>ΑΛ. ἐν ταῖσιν εὔστραις κονδύλοις ἡρμοττόμην</l> </lg> </quote> </cit> , <quote xml:lang="eng"> "was <emph>kept in order</emph> "</quote>by blows: Lucian <title>Toxaris</title> 17 <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸν ἁρμοστὴν ὃς ἥρμοζε τὴν Ἀσίαν τότε</quote> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐναργεῖς_910">, before my eyes: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Trach. 223" ref="Soph. Trach. 223"> <title>Tr.</title> 223</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τάδ’ ἀντίπρῳρα δή σοι | βλέπειν πάρεστ’ ἐναργῆ.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄγων_910">, as <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Il. 2.558" ref="Hom. Il. 2.558"> <title>Il.</title> 2.558</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">στῆσε δ’ ἄγων</quote> </cit>: below, 1342. Cp. 475 <quote xml:lang="grc">λαβών</quote> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὧν_912">= <quote xml:lang="grc">τούτων ὧν</quote> , possessive gen., here denoting origin: cp. on <ref target="cl214">214</ref> . </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἶτ_914">, <quote xml:lang="eng">"after that,"</quote> <quote xml:lang="eng">"nevertheless"</quote> : cp. 418, 1005.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀφεὶς_914">: cp. 1537. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὰ_κύρια_915">, the constituted authorities, like <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰ τέλη</quote> , a phrase suggestive of <emph>constitutional</emph> monarchy, in which the citizens have some voice: as Theseus himself says in <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Supp. 350" ref="Eur. Supp. 350"> Eur. <title>Suppl.</title> 350</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλὰ τοῦ λόγου | προσδοὺς ἔχοιμ’ ἂν δῆμον εὐμενέστερον</quote> </cit>(proposing to refer a question to the people), and describes himself ( <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 353) as <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐλευθερώσας τήνδ’ ἰσόψηφον πόλιν</quote> . </gloss>

									δῆμον εὐμενέστερον

Cyrop. 7.5.27"> Xen. <title>Cyr.</title> 7.5.27</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οἱ δ’ ἐπὶ τοὺς φύλακας ταχθέντες ἐπεισπίπτουσιν αὐτοῖς πίνουσι</quote> </cit> . </gloss>

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄγεις_916">, of taking captive, as in <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄγειν καὶ φέρειν</quote> :</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="παρίστασαι_916">, bring to your own side, subjugate; <cit> <bibl n="Thuc. 1.98" ref="Thuc. 1.98">Thuc. 1.98</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">Ναξίοις...ἐπολέμησαν καὶ πολιορκίᾳ παρεστήσαντο</quote> </cit>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κένανδρον_τινὰ_917">, some State destitute of inhabitants, or else only peopled by spiritless slaves. Cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 56"> <title>O. T.</title> 56</ref>, and <cit> <bibl n="Thuc. 7.77" ref="Thuc. 7.77">Thuc. 7.77</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄνδρες γὰρ πόλις, καὶ οὐ τείχη οὐδὲ νῆες ἀνδρῶν κεναί</quote> </cit>. So in <ref n="Aesch. Supp. 913"> Aesch. <title>Suppl.</title> 913</ref>the king of Argos asks the insolent herald, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλ’ ἦ γυναικῶν ἐς πόλιν δοκεῖς μολεῖν;</quote> The desire to find Creon's <cit> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄβουλον</quote> <bibl n="Soph. OC 940" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg007:940">(940)</bibl> </cit>here has prompted the conjecture <quote xml:lang="grc">ἢ βουλῆς δίχα</quote> : but see on <ref target="cl939">940</ref> . </gloss>
