									νῆες ἀνδρῶν κεναί

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τῷ_μηδενί_918">, dat. of <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ μηδέν</quote> : cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Trach. 1107" ref="Soph. Trach. 1107"> <title>Tr.</title> 1107</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">κἂν τὸ μηδὲν ὦ</quote> </cit> . <cit> <bibl n="Hdt. 8.106" ref="Hdt. 8.106">Her. 8.106</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅτι με ἀντ’ ἀνδρὸς ἐποίησας τὸ μηδὲν εἶναι</quote> </cit>( <foreign xml:lang="lat">sc.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">εὐνοῦχον</quote> ). Cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 638"> <title>O. T.</title> 638</ref>, 1019. </gloss>

data.quote:
ὅτι με ἀντ’ ἀνδρὸς ἐποίησας τὸ μηδὲν
									εἶναι

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Θῆβαι_919">. A courteous exoneration of Thebes accords with the hereditary <quote xml:lang="grc">ξενία</quote> which this play supposes: see on <ref target="cl632">632</ref> , and cp. the compliments to Thebes in 929, 937. It has been seriously suggested that all these touches must have been inserted by Sophocles the grandson, because in the poet's time Athens and Thebes were not usually on the best terms.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐπαίδευσαν_919">, more than <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔθρεψαν</quote> , implying a moral and mental training: cp. <cit> <bibl>Pind. fr. 180</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="lyric"> <l>οὔτοι με ξένον</l> <l>οὐδ’ ἀδαήμονα Μοισᾶν ἐπαίδευσαν κλυταὶ</l> <l>Θῆβαι</l> </lg> </quote> </cit>: so of the Spartan public training, <cit> <bibl n="Thuc. 1.84" ref="Thuc. 1.84">Thuc. 1.84</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀμαθέστερον τῶν νόμων τῆς ὑπεροψίας παιδευόμενοι</quote> </cit>. Athens is <quote xml:lang="grc">τῆς Ἑλλάδος παίδευσις</quote> (id. 2. 41). </gloss>

data.quote:
ἀμαθέστερον τῶν νόμων τῆς ὑπεροψίας
							παιδευόμενοι

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πυθοίατο_921">, cp. 945, and n. on 44.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="συλῶντα_922"> <quote xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</quote>, forcibly carrying off what belongs to me,—yes, and what belongs to the <emph>gods</emph> , when you seek to lead captive unhappy men who are suppliants. It is best to put a comma after <foreign xml:lang="grc">τὰ τῶν θεῶν</foreign> , which is explained by <foreign xml:lang="grc">βίᾳ ἄγοντα</foreign> , etc. He robs the <emph>gods</emph> when he seeks to seize the sacred suppliant of the Eumenides (44, 287). He robs Theseus ( <foreign xml:lang="grc">τἀμὰ</foreign> ) when he seizes persons who are under the protection of Attic law (915). If <foreign xml:lang="grc">τὰ τῶν θεῶν φωτῶν ἀθλ. ἱκτήρια</foreign> were joined (as Blaydes prefers), the double gen. would be very awkward. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φωτῶν_ἱκτήρια_922"> =literally <quote xml:lang="eng">"suppliant objects consisting in hapless per sons,"</quote> = <quote xml:lang="grc">φῶτας ἀθλίους ἱκτηρίους</quote> . The gen. defines the <quote xml:lang="eng">"material,"</quote> or nature, of the <quote xml:lang="grc">ἱκτήρια</quote> , as in <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 758" ref="Soph. El. 758"> <title>El.</title> 758</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">σῶμα δειλαίας σποδοῦ</quote> </cit>is a body consisting in (reduced to) ashes. We could not render, <quote xml:lang="eng">"the emblems of supplication brought by hapless persons."</quote> Nor, again, <quote xml:lang="eng">"the suppliants belonging to a wretched man"</quote> (the two maidens). In the following periphrases we see an analogous poet. use of the neut. plur., though the relation to the gen. is not precisely the same: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Ant. 1209" ref="Soph. Ant. 1209"> <title>Ant.</title> 1209</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀθλίας ἄσημα...βοῆς</quote> </cit> , <quote xml:lang="eng">"confused accents of a mournful cry,"</quote> where the gen. might be either of material, as here, or possessive: <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 1265 <quote xml:lang="grc">ὤμοι ἐμῶν ἄνολβα βουλευμάτων</quote> (partitive gen.): <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Phoen. 1485" ref="Eur. Phoen. 1485"> Eur. <title>Phoen.</title> 1485</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ προκαλυπτομένα βοτρυχώδεος | ἁβρὰ παρήιδος</quote> </cit>, <quote xml:lang="eng">"not veiling the delicate cheek,"</quote> —for this is clearly the sense, rather than <quote xml:lang="eng">"spreading a delicate veil"</quote> ( <foreign xml:lang="lat">sc.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">καλύμματα</quote> ) over it. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐπεμβαίνων_924">: cp. on <ref target="cl400">400</ref> . Theseus points his reproof, as Oed. did in 776 ff., by asking Creon to imagine their respective situations reversed. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἶχον_925">, since <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἐνδικώτατα</foreign> = <quote xml:lang="grc">μέγιστα δικαιώματα</quote> : <cit> <bibl n="Thuc. 1.41" ref="Thuc. 1.41">Thuc. 1.41</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">δικαιώματα τάδε πρὸς ὑμᾶς ἔχομεν</quote> </cit>: and so id. 3. 54 <quote xml:lang="grc">παρεχόμενοι...ἃ ἔχομεν δίκαια</quote> , advancing the just pleas which are ours. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄνευ_κραίνοντος_926"> , <foreign xml:lang="lat">iniussu dominatoris</foreign> , cp. <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Il. 15.213" ref="Hom. Il. 15.213"> <title>Il.</title> 15.213</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄνευ ἐμέθεν καὶ Ἀθηναίης ἀγελείης</quote> </cit>, <emph> without</emph> my <emph>consent</emph> and hers.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="χθονὸς_926">, gen. with <foreign xml:lang="grc">κρ.</foreign> , as <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 1050" ref="Soph. Aj. 1050"> <title>Ai.</title> 1050</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὃς κραίνει στρατοῦ.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὅστις_ἦν_926">: the verb in the relative clause is assimilated to the form of the conditional sentence: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Meno 89b" ref="Plat. Meno 89b"> Plat. <title>Men.</title> 89 B</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ φύσει οἱ ἀγαθοὶ ἐγίγνοντο, ἦσάν που ἂν ἡμῖν οἳ ἐγίγνωσκον τῶν νέων τοὺς ἀγαθοὺς τὰς φύσεις</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Xen. Mem. 1.7.3" ref="Xen. Mem. 1.7.3"> Xen. <title>Mem.</title> 1.7.3</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">κυβερνᾶν κατασταθεὶς</quote> </cit> (= <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ κατασταθείη</quote> ) <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁ μὴ ἐπιστάμενος ἀπολέσειεν ἂν οὒς ἥκιστα βούλοιτο</quote> . </gloss>

data.quote:
εἰ φύσει οἱ ἀγαθοὶ ἐγίγνοντο, ἦσάν που
									ἂν ἡμῖν οἳ ἐγίγνωσκον τῶν νέων τοὺς ἀγαθοὺς τὰς
									φύσεις

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὔθ_ἦγον_927">. The chief protasis is contained in the partic. <foreign xml:lang="grc"> ἐπεμβαίνων</foreign> ( <ref n="Soph. OC 924">924</ref> ),= <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ ἐπενέβαινον</quote> , while <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ … εἶχον</quote> merely subjoins a special case in which the apodosis would still hold good:— <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ ἐπενέβαινον, οὐκ ἂν εἷλκον, οὐδὲ</quote> ( <quote xml:lang="grc">εἷλκον ἂν</quote> ) <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ εἶχον</quote> . Remark that the form of the apodosis, <quote xml:lang="grc">οὔθ’ εἷλκον...ἂν</quote> etc., does not logically imply, <quote xml:lang="eng"> "I <emph>am</emph> now dragging,"</quote>but merely, <quote xml:lang="eng"> "I am <emph>not</emph> now <emph>forbearing to</emph> drag"</quote>: there is no opportunity for such abstention, since the fact supposed by <foreign xml:lang="grc"> ἐπεμβαίνων</foreign> ( <quote xml:lang="eng">"If I were on Theban soil"</quote> ) is non-existent. The conditional form with the imperf. indic. has been preferred to that with the optative (used in the similar illustration at 776), because Theseus is thinking of what Creon is actually doing. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ξένον_928">, for whom the first rule should be, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀστοῖς ἴσα μελετᾶν</quote> (171, cp. 13). Cp. <ref n="Aesch. Supp. 917"> Aesch. <title> Suppl.</title> 917</ref>(the Argive king to the Egyptian herald who threatens to drag off the Danaïdes by force), <quote xml:lang="grc">ξένος μὲν εἶναι πρῶτον οὐκ ἐπίστασαι</quote> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀξίαν_οὖσαν_929"> , <foreign xml:lang="lat">immeritam;</foreign> <cit> <bibl n="Dem. 21.217" ref="Dem. 21.217">Dem. or. 21 § 217</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰμὶ δ’ οὐ τούτων ὑμῖν ἄξιος</quote> </cit> , <quote xml:lang="eng">"I do not deserve such (harsh) treatment at your hands"</quote> : cp. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀξιοῦν τινά τινος</quote> , <emph>to condemn</emph> one to a punishment, <ref n="Soph. OT 1449"> <title>O. T.</title> 1449</ref>(n.). </gloss>

data.quote: εἰμὶ δ’ οὐ τούτων ὑμῖν ἄξιος

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὴν_αὑτοῦ_930">: cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 1356">1356</ref> , <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 1132" ref="Soph. Aj. 1132"> <title>Ai.</title> 1132</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τούς γ’ αὐτὸς αὑτοῦ πολεμίους</quote> </cit> : <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. PB 921" ref="Aesch. PB 921"> Aesch. <title>PV</title> 921</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπ’ αὐτὸς αὑτῷ</quote> </cit> : <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. PB 762" ref="Aesch. PB 762"> <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 762</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">πρὸς αὐτὸς αὑτοῦ κενοφρόνων βουλευμάτων</quote> </cit>. In this hyperbaton <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐτός</quote> merely adds emphasis to the reflexive. If <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐτός</quote> is meant to stand out with its full separate force, it precedes the prep., as <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐτὸς πρὸς αὑτοῦ</quote> twice in Soph. ( <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1177"> <title>Ant.</title> 1177</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Aj. 906"> <title>Ai.</title> 906</ref>). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὁ_χρόνος_930">, the growing number of thy years; cp. on <ref target="cl377">377</ref> and <ref target="cl7">7</ref> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοῦ_νοῦ_930">, which is just what old age ought to bring: <cit> <bibl>fr. 240</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="trimeter"> <l>καίπερ γέρων ὤν· ἀλλὰ τῷ γήρᾳ φιλεῖ</l> <l>χὡ νοῦς ὁμαρτεῖν καὶ τὸ βουλεύειν ἃ δεῖ</l> </lg> </quote> </cit> : <cit> <bibl>Aesch. fr. 391</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">γῆρας γὰρ ἥβης ἐστὶν ἐνδικώτερον</quote> </cit>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τινα_933">, simply <quote xml:lang="eng">"some one"</quote> : not here a threatening substitute for <quote xml:lang="grc">σέ</quote> (as in <ref n="Soph. Aj. 1138"> <title>Ai.</title> 1138</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Ant. 751"> <title>Ant.</title> 751</ref>). Indifference as to the <emph>agent</emph> strengthens insistence on the <emph>act.</emph> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μέτοικος_934"> , in ordinary Attic usage, was a voluntary sojourn, terminable at the will of the sojourner. Hence the irony here. With a similar force the Attic poets apply it to one who has found his <quote xml:lang="eng">"last, long home"</quote> in foreign earth. <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. Lib. 683" ref="Aesch. Lib. 683"> Aesch. <title>Cho.</title> 683</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="trimeter"> <l>εἴτ’ οὖν κομίζειν δόξα νικήσει φίλων,</l> <l>εἴτ’ οὖν μέτοικον, εἰς τὸ πᾶν ἀεὶ ξένον,</l> <l>θάπτειν</l> </lg> </quote> </cit> : <quote xml:lang="eng">"whether his friends decide to bring his ashes home, or to bury him among strangers, an alien utterly for ever"</quote> : so a Persian whose corpse was left at Salamis is <quote xml:lang="grc">σκληρᾶς μέτοικος γῆς ἐκεῖ</quote> ( <ref n="Aesch. Pers. 319"> <title>Pers.</title> 319</ref>): <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Her. 1033" ref="Eur. Her. 1033"> Eur. <title>Her.</title> 1033</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">μέτοικος ἀεὶ κείσομαι κατὰ χθονὸς</quote> </cit>(the Argive Eurystheus buried in Attica). Cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 452"> <title>O. T.</title> 452</ref>n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="βίᾳ_ἑκ_935">. as <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 1275" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg004:1275"> <title>O. T.</title> 1275</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">πολλάκις τε κοὐχ ἅπαξ.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κοὐχ_ἑκών_935">, not <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ μὴ ἑκών</quote> , though dependent on <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ</quote> , since <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐχ ἑκών</quote> = <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄκων</quote> : cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 1131" ref="Soph. Aj. 1131"> <title>Ai.</title> 1131</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ τοὺς θανόντας οὐκ — ἐᾷς θάπτειν</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Lys. 13.62" ref="Lys. 13.62">Lys. or. 13 § 62</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ μὲν οὖν οὐ‐πολλοὶ ἦσαν</quote> </cit> . </gloss>

data.quote:
εἰ τοὺς θανόντας οὐκ — ἐᾷς
									θάπτειν

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀπὸ_γλώσσης_936"> usu.= <quote xml:lang="eng">"by word of mouth"</quote> (as opp. to <quote xml:lang="eng">"by letter"</quote> ), as in <ref n="Thuc. 7.10">Thuc. 7.10</ref> .— For the antithesis cp. <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Sym. 199A" ref="Plat. Sym. 199A"> Plat. <title>Symp.</title> 199A</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἡ γλῶττα οὖν ὑπέσχετο, ἡ δὲ φρὴν οὔ</quote> </cit>(alluding to <ref n="Eur. Hipp. 612"> Eur. <title>Hipp.</title> 612</ref>). </gloss>

data.quote:
ἡ γλῶττα οὖν ὑπέσχετο, ἡ δὲ φρὴν
									οὔ

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὁρᾷς_ἥκεις_937">; an indignant reproach, as <ref n="Soph. OT 687"> <title>O. T.</title> 687</ref>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀφ_ὧν_937">= <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπὸ τούτων, ἀφ’ ὧν</quote> (cp. on <ref target="cl274">274</ref> ): <quote xml:lang="eng"> " <title>Judging by</title> the folk <emph>from whom</emph> thou art sprung (the Thebans, cp. 919), thou seemest just"</quote>— <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> , a member of a just race. For <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἀπό</foreign> of judging <emph>by</emph> a thing, cp. on <ref target="cl15">15</ref> . The Greek sense of the prep. with the relative here is really the same as with the supplied antecedent. It is our idiom which makes them seem different. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐγὼ_οὔτ_939">: so 998: <title>O.T.</title> 332 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐγὼ οὔτ’ ἐμαυτόν</quote> : <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Ant. 458" ref="Soph. Ant. 458"> <title>Ant.</title> 458</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐγὼ οὐκ ἔμελλον</quote> </cit> : <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Phil. 585" ref="Soph. Phil. 585"> <title>Ph.</title> 585 </bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐγώ εἰμ’ Ἀτρείσαις</quote> </cit> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αὐτοὺς_942">, the people implied in <quote xml:lang="grc">τὴν πόλιν</quote> (939). Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Ba. 961" ref="Eur. Ba. 961"> Eur. <title>Bacch.</title> 961</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="trimeter"> <l>κόμιζε διὰ μέσης με Θηβαίας χθονός,</l> <l>μόνος γὰρ αὐτῶν εἰμ’ ἀνὴρ τολμῶν τάδε</l> </lg> </quote> </cit>. So in <cit> <bibl n="Hdt. 5.63" ref="Hdt. 5.63">Her. 5.63</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐτοὺς</quote> </cit> after <quote xml:lang="grc">Θεσσαλίης</quote> , and 8. 121 <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐτῶν</quote> after <quote xml:lang="grc">Κάρυστον.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐμπέσοι_942">has here the constr. of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἕλοι</quote> : cp. <cit> <bibl n="Eur. IA 808" ref="Eur. IA 808"> Eur. <title>I. A.</title> 808</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="trimeter"> <l>δεινὸς ἐμπέπτωκ’ ἔρως</l> <l>τῆσδε στρατείας Ἑλλάδ’, οὐκ ἄνευ θεῶν</l> </lg> </quote> </cit>. This is decisive against here reading <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐτοῖς</quote> , the commoner constr. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ξυναίμων_943">, Oed., Ant., and Ism.: Creon refers first to the <emph>general</emph> claim of kindred; then to the <emph>special</emph> reasons against detaining Oed.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κἄναγνον_945">. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 821" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg004:821"> <title>O. T.</title> 821</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="trimeter"> <l>λέχη δὲ τοῦ θανόντος ἐν χεροῖν ἐμαῖν</l> <l>χραίνω, δῑ ὧνπερ ὤλετ)· ἆρ’ ἔφυν κακός;</l> <l>ἆρ’ οὐχὶ πᾶς ἄναγνος;</l> </lg> </quote> </cit>So here, too, </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄναγνον_945"> refers to the taint of murder, aggravated by union with the wife of the slain. <quote xml:lang="eng"> " <title>Both</title> a parricide, <emph> and</emph> , in a complex sense, impure,—yea, guilty of incest."</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δεξοίατ_945">: cp. on <ref target="cl44">44</ref> . The fut. optat. after a secondary tense, as <ref n="Soph. OT 538"> <title>O. T.</title> 538</ref>f., 792, 796, 1271 ff. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ηὑρέθησαν_946">. Attic inscriptions nearly as old as the poet's time confirm <quote xml:lang="grc">ηὑ‐</quote> against <quote xml:lang="grc">εὑ‐</quote> : cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 546"> <title>O. T.</title> 546</ref>n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τέκνων_946">has been suspected. The literal meaning of <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἀνόσιοι γάμοι τέκνων</foreign> can be nothing but <quote xml:lang="eng"> "unholy nuptials <emph>with children</emph> "</quote>(such as Iocasta's with Oed.). But here the sense should be, <quote xml:lang="eng"> "unholy nuptials <emph>with parents</emph> "</quote>: cp. 978 <quote xml:lang="grc"> μητρὸς...γάμους</quote> . Can <foreign xml:lang="grc">τέκνων</foreign> , then, be defended? Thus, I think.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ξυνόντες_946">suggests the <emph>consort.</emph> Hence <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνόσιοι γάμοι τέκνων</quote> is said, with poetical boldness and also with a certain designed obscurity, in this sense:— <quote xml:lang="eng"> " <emph>a woman who has made an unholy marriage with her son.</emph> "</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοιοῦτον_947">, introducing a reason for a preceding statement, as <ref n="Soph. Aj. 164"> <title>Ai.</title> 164</ref>( <quote xml:lang="grc">τοιούτων</quote> ), 218 ( <quote xml:lang="grc">τοιαῦτ’</quote> ), 251 ( <quote xml:lang="grc">τοίας</quote> ), 562 ( <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῖον</quote> ).—</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εὔβουλον_947">suggests the title of the Court, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἡ ἐξ Ἀρείου πάγου βουλή</quote> . If the Council of the Areiopagus (Creon assumes) became aware that a polluted person, such as Oedipus, was in Attica, it would take steps for his expulsion. Such a proceeding would doubtless have come within the limits of the general moral censorship actually possessed by the Areiopagus, at least in the earlier days of the Athenian democracy. Indeed that court is found exercising authority of a like kind (though only by special warrant) even after the reforms of Pericles and Ephialtes. Cp. Deinarchus or. 1 § 58, where the Ecclesia commissions the Areiopagus to inquire into the conduct of a merely suspected person, and the Areiopagus, having done so, reports to the Ecclesia ( <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῦ δήμου προστάξαντος ζητῆσαι τὴν βουλήν,...καὶ ζητήσασαν ἀποφῆναι πρὸς ὑμᾶς, ἀπέφηνεν ἡ βουλή</quote> , etc.). See also <ref n="Plut. Sol. 22"> Plut. <title> Sol.</title> 22</ref>, <ref n="Isoc. 7.36">Isocr. or. 7 §§ 36-55</ref> , and <ref> my <title>Attic Orators</title> vol. II. p. 211</ref>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="χθόνιον_948">= <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐγχώριον</quote> , a use found in <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 202" ref="Soph. Aj. 202"> <title>Ai.</title> 202</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">χθονίων ἀπ’ Ἐρεχθειδᾶν</quote> </cit> (= <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐτοχθόνων</quote> ), and fr. adesp. (Nauck^{2}) <quote xml:lang="grc">χθονίους Ἰναχίδας</quote> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θυμοῦ_954">, the anger which moved Creon to make the seizure: cp. 874 <quote xml:lang="grc">οὔτοι καθέξω θυμόν</quote> . Theseus had said that Creon's violence disgraced his years (931). Creon replies, <quote xml:lang="eng">"There is no old age for anger, except death"</quote> ; <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> , <quote xml:lang="eng">"anger, under gross insult, ceases to be felt only when a man is dead, and can feel nothing."</quote> Schol.: <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῦτο δὲ καὶ παροιμιακῶς λέγεται, ὅτι ὁ θυμὸς ἔσχατον γηράσκει</quote> . Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. Seven 682" ref="Aesch. Seven 682"> Aesch. <title>Theb.</title> 682</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐκ ἔστι γῆρας τοῦδε τοῦ μιάσματος</quote> </cit>. Here, too, <foreign xml:lang="grc">γῆρας</foreign> is figurative,— <quote xml:lang="eng"> "decay,"</quote> <quote xml:lang="eng">"abatement,"</quote> of anger; while <foreign xml:lang="grc">θανεῖν</foreign> has its literal sense, the subject being <quote xml:lang="grc">τινά</quote> understood. </gloss>

data.quote:
οὐκ ἔστι γῆρας τοῦδε τοῦ
									μιάσματος

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θανόντων_954"> : <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 1170" ref="Soph. El. 1170"> <title>El.</title> 1170</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τοὺς γὰρ θανόντας οὐχ ὁρῶ λυπουμένους</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Trach. 1173" ref="Soph. Trach. 1173"> <title>Tr.</title> 1173</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῖς γὰρ θανοῦσι μόχθος οὐ προσγίγνεται</quote> </cit>. </gloss>

data.quote:
τοὺς γὰρ θανόντας οὐχ ὁρῶ
									λυπουμένους

τοῖς γὰρ θανοῦσι μόχθος οὐ
									προσγίγνεται

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κεἰ_957">here= <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ καὶ</quote> : cp. 661. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σμικρὸν_957">: cp. 148 ( <quote xml:lang="grc">σμικροῖς</quote> ), 880 ( <quote xml:lang="grc">βραχύς</quote> ), where see n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πρὸς_πράξεις_957"> , <quote xml:lang="eng">"against your deeds,"</quote> <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> , any measures that you may take to deprive me of my captives. Cp. <ref n="Aristot. Pol. 6.1320a"> Arist. <title> Pol.</title> 6.5</ref>. 3 <quote xml:lang="grc">πρὸς ταῦτα ἀντιπράττειν</quote> . He hints that, though he cannot resist now, he will take steps, when he returns to Thebes, for obtaining redress by force of arms: cp. 1036 <quote xml:lang="grc">οἴκοι δὲ χἡμεῖς εἰσόμεσθ’ ἂ χρὴ ποεῖν</quote> .— Note the repeated <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀντιδρᾶν</quote> (953, 959) and <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅμως</quote> (957 f.): cp. 554 n. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοῦ_960">. Which is more disgraced,— the involuntary sufferer, or the author of deliberate insults to an unhappy kinsman? </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μοι_962">, dat. of interest, <quote xml:lang="eng">"for my reproach,"</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="διῆκας_962">, <emph>sent through</emph> thy mouth, —poured forth: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 596" ref="Soph. El. 596"> <title>El.</title> 596</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἣ πᾶσαν ἵης γλῶσσαν</quote> </cit>: fr. 844. 3 <quote xml:lang="grc">πολλὴν γλῶσσαν ἐκχέας μάτην</quote> . In <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Trach. 323" ref="Soph. Trach. 323"> <title>Tr.</title> 323</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">διήσει γλῶσσαν</quote> </cit>is Wakefield's correction of <quote xml:lang="grc">διοίσει</quote> . </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄκων_964">: cp. on <ref target="cl522">522</ref> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θεοῖς_964">: the synizesis as in <ref n="Soph. OT 1519"> <title>O. T.</title> 1519</ref>, and about 26 other places of dialogue in Soph.: he admits it also in lyrics, as <ref n="Soph. OT 215"> <title>O. T.</title> 215</ref>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄν_965">cannot go with <foreign xml:lang="grc">μηνίουσιν</foreign> . since the partic. does not represent an apodosis, as <quote xml:lang="grc">ἂν φέρων</quote> does in 761 (n.). On the other hand, <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἄν</foreign> does not here give any conditional force to <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἦν</foreign> , which is a simple statement of fact. Rather <foreign xml:lang="grc">τάχ’ ἄν</foreign> is here felt as one word,= <quote xml:lang="eng">"perhaps."</quote> <quote xml:lang="eng">"It was dear to the gods,—perhaps because they were wroth."</quote> The origin of this usage was an ellipse: <quote xml:lang="grc">θεοῖς ἦν φίλον, τάχα</quote> ( <quote xml:lang="grc"> δ’</quote> ) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἂν</quote> ( <quote xml:lang="grc">φίλον εἴη</quote> ) <quote xml:lang="grc">μηνίουσιν</quote> , <quote xml:lang="eng">"and perhaps (it would be dear) because they were wroth"</quote> : where the supplied <quote xml:lang="grc">εἴη</quote> expresses a conjecture about a past fact, as in <cit> <bibl n="Hdt. 1.2" ref="Hdt. 1.2">Her. 1.2</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἴησαν δ’ ἂν οὗτοι Κρῆτες</quote> </cit>. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 523" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg004:523"> <title>O. T.</title> 523</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλ’ ἦλθε μὲν δὴ τοῦτο τοὔνειδος τάχ’ ἂν | ὀργῇ βιασθέν</quote> </cit>, <quote xml:lang="eng">"this reproach came under stress, perchance, of anger."</quote> See Appendix. </gloss>

data.quote:
ἀλλ’ ἦλθε μὲν δὴ τοῦτο τοὔνειδος τάχ’
									ἂν | ὀργῇ βιασθέν

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐπεὶ_γ_966"> . <quote xml:lang="eng"> "My fate must have been a divine judgment upon me for the sins of ancestors. For you could not discover against me ( <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἐμοί</foreign> , dat. of interest, cp. 962),—taken <emph>by myself</emph> ( <foreign xml:lang="grc">καθ’ αὑτόν</foreign> , apart from those ancestors),—any charge of sin, <emph>in retribution for which</emph> ( <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἀνθ’ ὅτου</foreign> ) I proceeded to sin (impf. <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἡμάρτανον</foreign> ) against myself and my kindred."</quote>If any <emph>voluntary</emph> crime on his part had preceded his <emph>involuntary</emph> crimes, the latter might have been ascribed to an <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄτη</quote> sent on him by angry gods. But he had committed <emph>no</emph> such voluntary crime. For <quote xml:lang="grc">αὑτόν</quote> = <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐμαυτόν</quote> see on <ref target="cl852">852</ref> f. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐπεὶ_δίδαξον_969"> : <quote xml:lang="eng"> "for <emph>else</emph> —if this is <emph> not</emph> so—tell me"</quote>: the controversial <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἐπεί</foreign> , on which see <ref n="Soph. OT 390"> <title>O. T.</title> 390</ref>n. Note the early repetition (after 966): see on <ref target="cl554">554</ref> : cp. <quote xml:lang="grc"> ἀλλ’</quote> 985, 988. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἴ_θέσφατον_969"> : <quote xml:lang="eng"> "if, by oracles ( <foreign xml:lang="grc">χρησμ</foreign> ., instrum. dat.), some divine doom was coming on my sire, that he should die,"</quote>etc.: <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἱκνεῖτο</foreign> , <emph>impf.</emph> , because the doom was impending from the moment at which the Delphic oracle spoke: that moment itself, on the other hand, is marked by the <emph>aor.</emph> in <ref n="Soph. OT 711"> <title>O. T.</title> 711</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="trimeter"> <l>χρησμὸς γὰρ ἦλθε Λαΐῳ ποτ’...</l> <l>ὡς αὐτὸν ἥξοι μοῖρα πρὸς παιδὸς θανεῖν</l> </lg> </quote>. See Introd. to the <ref n="Soph. OT"> <title>O. T.</title> </ref>p. xix. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="παίδων_970">, allusive plur. for sing., cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 295" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg007:295">295</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄνακτας</quote> </cit> <ref target="cl295">(n.)</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὔτεοὐ_972">: cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 249"> <title>Ant.</title> 249</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="trimeter"> <l>οὔτε του γενῇδος ἦν</l> <l>πλῆγμ’, οὐ δικέλλης ἐκβολή</l> </lg> </quote> : <ref n="Eur. Orest. 41"> Eur. <title>Or.</title> 41</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="trimeter"> <l>ὧν οὔτε δῖτα διὰ δέρης ἐδέξατο,</l> <l>οὐ λούτρ’ ἔδωκε χρωτί</l> </lg> </quote> : <ref n="Eur. Orest. 1086"> <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 1086</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="trimeter"> <l>μήθ’ αἷμά μου δέξαιτο κάρπιμον πέδον,</l> <l>μὴ λαμπρὸς αἰθήρ</l> </lg> </quote> : <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Her. 8.98" ref="Eur. Her. 8.98">Her. 8.98</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὔτε νιφετός, οὐκ ὄμβρος, οὐ καῦμα, οὐ νύξ</quote> </cit>. But of the converse, <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ...οὔτε</quote> , there is no certain example.— </gloss>

data.quote:
οὔτε νιφετός, οὐκ ὄμβρος, οὐ καῦμα, οὐ
									νύξ

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="βλάστας_972">, plur., <ref n="Soph. OT 717"> <title>O. T.</title> 717</ref>: <quote xml:lang="grc">πατρὸς</quote> and <quote xml:lang="grc">μητρὸς</quote> , gen. of origin with <quote xml:lang="grc">βλ. γεν. εἶχον</quote> as= <quote xml:lang="grc"> ἔβλαστον</quote> : he was not yet begotten or conceived. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φανεὶς_δύστηνος_974">, having been born to misery (as being fated to slay his sire): so <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 1225" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg007:1225">1225</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπεὶ φανῇ</quote> </cit>, when one has come into the world. This is better here than, "having proved unfortunate." </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐγὼ_φάνην_974">: for the prodelision of the temporal augment in the 6th place, cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 457"> <title>Ant.</title> 457</ref>: <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Hel. 263" ref="Eur. Hel. 263"> Eur. <title>Helen</title> 263</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">( εἴθε ) αἴσχιον εἶδος ἀντὶ τοῦ καλοῦ )λαβον</quote> </cit>(Porson's correction of <quote xml:lang="grc">λαβεῖν</quote> ): <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 557" ref="Soph. Aj. 557"> <title>Ai.</title> 557</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐξ οἵου )τράφης</quote> </cit> . </gloss>

data.quote:
( εἴθε ) αἴσχιον εἶδος ἀντὶ τοῦ καλοῦ
									)λαβον

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐς_χεῖρας_975">: cp. on <ref target="cl835">835</ref> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μηδὲν_976">is adjective with <foreign xml:lang="grc">ὧν</foreign> (= <quote xml:lang="grc">τούτων ἃ</quote> ), and adverb with ( <quote xml:lang="grc">τούτους</quote> ) <foreign xml:lang="grc">εἰς οὕς</foreign> .</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἆκον_977">= <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀκούσιον</quote> : see on <ref target="cl240">240</ref> .</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὖν_980">here= <quote xml:lang="eng">"indeed"</quote> ; in 985 <quote xml:lang="eng">"at all events."</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἰς_στόμα_981">, having gone to such lengths of impious speech, <foreign xml:lang="lat"> i.e.</foreign> having outraged the most sacred ties of kinship by these public taunts. Cp. 438 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκδραμόντα</quote> (n.).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀνόσιον_στόμα_981">agrees with <foreign xml:lang="grc">τόδ’</foreign> , depending on <foreign xml:lang="grc">εἰς</foreign> . Since <foreign xml:lang="grc">στόμα</foreign> was familiar to poetry in the sense of <quote xml:lang="grc">λόγος</quote> (cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 426"> <title>O. T.</title> 426</ref>), this version is clearly preferable to taking <foreign xml:lang="grc">εἰς τόδ’</foreign> separately and <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἀνός. στ</foreign> . as accus. of respect. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔτικτεν_982">= <quote xml:lang="eng">"she was my mother"</quote> —she, who was becoming my bride—though neither of us knew it at the time of the marriage. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Ion 1560" ref="Eur. Ion 1560"> Eur. <title>Ion</title> 1560</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἥδε τίκτει σ’</quote> </cit> , <quote xml:lang="eng">"she is thy mother"</quote> ; and <ref n="Soph. OT 437"> <title>O. T.</title> 437</ref>, 870.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αὑτῆς_ὄνειδος_982">, because, although she was morally guiltless in the marriage, yet such a union was, in fact, shameful: cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 1494"> <title>O. T.</title> 1494</ref>, 1500. Yet Nauck condemns these vv. because (1) they do <emph>not</emph> explain the <quote xml:lang="grc">μητρὸς γάμους</quote> of 978, and (2) <quote xml:lang="grc">ὄνειδος</quote> is illogical after <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐκ εἰδυῖα</quote> . Kaibel, who also condemns them, compares ( <title>Deutsche Litteraturz.</title> , 1886, p. 733) <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Her. 224" ref="Eur. Her. 224"> Eur. <title>Her.</title> 224 f. </bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="trimeter"> <l>ἱκέτας ἀλήτας συγγενεῖς, οἴμοι κακῶν,</l> <l>βλέψον πρὸς αὐτοὺς βλέψον, ἕλκεσθαι βίᾳ</l> </lg> </quote> </cit>: where the only points of likeness are <quote xml:lang="grc">οἴμοι κακῶν</quote> and the iteration. Rhetoric of a similar cast, and prompted by the same thought, occurs in <ref n="Soph. OT 1403"> <title>O. T.</title> 1403</ref>ff., 1496 ff. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δυσστομεῖν_986">(only here) with acc., as <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 596" ref="Soph. El. 596"> <title>El.</title> 596</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὴν μητέρα | κακοστομοῦμεν</quote> </cit>. Those who still write <foreign xml:lang="grc">δυστομεῖν</foreign> have some eminent modern critics with them; but on the other side it should be borne in mind that the MSS. (rarely older than the 11th or 10th cent.) which vouch for such forms as <quote xml:lang="grc">δυστατεῖν</quote> or <quote xml:lang="grc">δυστίβευτος</quote> vouch also for such spellings of compounds with <quote xml:lang="grc">πρός</quote> as <quote xml:lang="grc">προστείχω, προσταυρόω, προστέλλω</quote> , where <quote xml:lang="grc">προσς</quote> is unquestionably right. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄκων_987">. A single <foreign xml:lang="grc">τε</foreign> linking whole sentences is not rare in Soph. ( <foreign xml:lang="lat">e.g.</foreign> 1437, <ref n="Soph. OT 995"> <title>O. T.</title> 995</ref>); but <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄκων τ’</quote> (Vat.) may be right here. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀλλ_γὰρ_988">. Distinguish two uses of this formula. (1) With an ellipse, as here,— <quote xml:lang="eng"> "but (your charges are untrue), <emph>for.</emph> "</quote>In this, <foreign xml:lang="grc">γάρ</foreign> may be represented by <quote xml:lang="eng"> " <emph>in fact</emph> ,"</quote>or <quote xml:lang="eng"> " <emph>indeed.</emph> "</quote>Cp. on <ref target="cl754">755</ref> . (2) When there is no ellipse, as <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 1409" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg004:1409"> <title>O. T.</title> 1409</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλ’ οὐ γὰρ αὐδᾶν ἔσθ’ ἃ μηδὲ δρᾶν καλόν,...καλύψατ’</quote> </cit>. Then <foreign xml:lang="grc">γάρ</foreign> = <quote xml:lang="eng"> " <emph>since.</emph> "</quote> </gloss>

data.quote:
ἀλλ’ οὐ γὰρ αὐδᾶν ἔσθ’ ἃ μηδὲ δρᾶν
							καλόν,...καλύψατ’

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐμφορεῖς_989"> ( <foreign xml:lang="lat">ingeris</foreign> ), <quote xml:lang="eng">"heapest on me,"</quote> <quote xml:lang="eng">"urgest against me,"</quote> is supported, as against <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἐμφέρεις</foreign> , by the common use of the word in later Greek, as <cit> <bibl n="Plut. Pomp. 3" ref="Plut. Pomp. 3"> Plut. <title>Pomp.</title> 3</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">πολλὰς ἐνεφόρει πληγὰς τοῖς στρώμασιν</quote> </cit>: Alciphro 1. 9 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπὶ τῷ σφετέρῳ κέρδει εἰς τοὺς ἀπράγμονας ἐμφοροῦσιν ὕβρεις</quote> , <quote xml:lang="eng">"for their own gain they heap insults on quiet people."</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φόνους_989">: the rhetorical pl., as 962. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄμειψαι_991">: cp. on <ref target="cl814">814</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνταμείβει</quote> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἴ_κτείνοι_992">, should <emph>attempt</emph> to slay; cp. <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Od. 16.432" ref="Hom. Od. 16.432"> <title>Od.</title> 16.432</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">παῖδά τ’ ἀποκτείνεις</quote> </cit> , <quote xml:lang="eng"> "and <emph>art seeking to</emph> slay his son"</quote>: <cit> <bibl n="Antiph. 5.7" ref="Antiph. 5.7">Antiph. or. 5 § 7</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅταν δ’ ἄνευ κινδύνων τι διαπράσσωνται</quote> </cit>, are <emph>seeking to</emph> effect. (For the parallel use of the imperf., see 274.) The optat. in putting the imaginary case, as 776: cp. on <ref target="cl927"> 927</ref> .</gloss>

data.quote:
ὅταν δ’ ἄνευ κινδύνων τι
									διαπράσσωνται

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αὐτίκα_992"> (not, <quote xml:lang="eng">"for instance,"</quote> but) with <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἐνθάδε</foreign> , at this moment and on this spot, cp. <cit> <quote xml:lang="lat">nunc iam ilico</quote> <bibl n="Ter. Ad. 2.1" ref="Ter. Ad. 2.1"> (Ter. <title>Ad.</title> 2. 1. 2).</bibl> </cit> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸν_δίκ_992">.: for the ironical article cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Ant. 31" ref="Soph. Ant. 31"> <title>Ant.</title> 31</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸν ἀγαθὸν Κρέοντα</quote> </cit> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δοκῶ_μέν_995"> , <quote xml:lang="eng">"I should think so,"</quote> with the emphasis on the verb, not on the 1st pers.: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 61" ref="Soph. El. 61"> <title>El.</title> 61</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">δοκῶ μέν, οὐδὲν ῥῆμα σὺν κέρδει κακόν</quote> </cit>: fr. 83 <quote xml:lang="grc">δοκῶ μέν, οὐδείς</quote> . Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Phaedo 68B" ref="Plat. Phaedo 68B"> Plat. <title>Phaedo</title> 68B</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐκ ἄσμενος εἶσιν αὐτόσε; οἴεσθαί γε χρή</quote> </cit>. So <ref n="Plat. Crito 53D"> <title>Crito</title> 53 D</ref>, <ref n="Plat. Crito 54b">54 B</ref> : also <cit> <quote xml:lang="grc">οἶμαι ἔγωγε</quote> <bibl n="Plat. Crito 47d" ref="Plat. Crito 47d"> <title>Crito</title> 47 D.</bibl> </cit> </gloss>

data.quote:
δοκῶ μέν, οὐδὲν ῥῆμα σὺν κέρδει
									κακόν

οὐκ ἄσμενος εἶσιν αὐτόσε; οἴεσθαί γε
									χρή

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="περιβλέποις_996">. This compound occurs nowhere else in Soph., nor does he use <quote xml:lang="grc">περίβλεπτος</quote> . But Eur. uses them five times ( <ref n="Eur. Andr. 89"> <title>Andr.</title> 89</ref>, <ref n="Eur. Her. 508"> <title>H. F.</title> 508</ref>, <ref n="Eur. Ion 624"> <title>Ion</title> 624</ref>, <ref n="Eur. IA 429"> <title>I. A.</title> 429</ref>, <ref n="Eur. Phoen. 551"> <title>Ph.</title> 551</ref>), and Ar. has the verb once ( <ref n="Aristoph. Eccl. 403"> <title>Eccl.</title> 403</ref>). In all six places, as here, the <foreign xml:lang="grc">ι</foreign> is made long. On the other hand, the <foreign xml:lang="grc">ι</foreign> of <quote xml:lang="grc">περιδρομή</quote> and its cognates is usually, if not always, short ( <ref n="Eur. El. 458"> Eur. <title>El.</title> 458</ref>, <ref n="Eur. Hel. 776"> <title>Helen.</title> 776</ref>, <ref n="Eur. Tro. 1197"> <title>Tro.</title> 1197</ref>, <ref>fr. 1068. 2</ref> : <ref n="Aesch. Supp. 349"> Aesch. <title>Suppl.</title> 349</ref>: <ref n="Aristoph. Wasps 138"> Aristoph. <title>Vesp.</title> 138</ref>, <ref n="Aristoph. Kn. 56"> <title>Eq.</title> 56</ref>, etc.): and <ref n="Aesch. Lib. 207"> Aesch. <title>Ch.</title> 207</ref>has <quote xml:lang="grc">περι^γραφά</quote> . In <ref n="Aristoph. Peace 879"> Aristoph. <title>Pax</title> 879</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">περιγράφεις</quote>is ambiguous in the comic trimeter. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἰσέβην_997">suits the imagery of <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἀγόντων</foreign> (see on <ref target="cl253">253</ref> ): cp. <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. Supp. 470" ref="Aesch. Supp. 470"> Aesch. <title>Suppl.</title> 470</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄτης δ’ ἄβυσσον πέλαγος οὐ μάλ’ εὔπορον | τόδ’ ἐσβέβηκα</quote> </cit> . </gloss>

data.quote:
ἄτης δ’ ἄβυσσον πέλαγος οὐ μάλ’
									εὔπορον | τόδ’ ἐσβέβηκα

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οἶς_997">(instead of <quote xml:lang="grc">πρὸς ἃ</quote> or <quote xml:lang="grc">περὶ ὧν</quote> ), which implies a personification of the <quote xml:lang="grc">λόγος</quote> . Then, at the end of the sentence,</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐμοί_997">is pleonastically added, by a sort of afterthought. This double dative, though irregular, does not seem to warrant the change of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐμοί</quote> into <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔχειν.</quote> <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἐμοί</foreign> gives greater vividness to the thought of the dead brought face to face with the living. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἂν_997">with </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀντειπεῖν_997"> . </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐγὼ_οὐδὲ_997">: cp. 939. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πατρὸς_ζῶσαν_998"> = <quote xml:lang="eng">"my father's life, if it could live again,"</quote> =simply <quote xml:lang="grc">πατέρα ζῶντα</quote> : not, <quote xml:lang="eng">"his departed spirit, if it could visit this world."</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ψυχή_998">in the trag. never means <quote xml:lang="eng">"a departed spirit"</quote> ( <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Il. 23.104" ref="Hom. Il. 23.104"> <title>Il.</title> 23.104</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ψυχὴ καὶ εἴδωλον</quote> </cit>), but always the <foreign xml:lang="lat">anima</foreign> of the living: cp. <ref n="Aesch. Ag. 1456"> Aesch. <title>Ag.</title> 1456</ref>(of Helen) <quote xml:lang="grc">μία τὰς πολλὰς … | ψυχὰς ὀλέσασ’ ὑπὸ Τροίᾳ</quote> . For the periphrasis here cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 1126" ref="Soph. El. 1126"> <title>El.</title> 1126</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="trimeter"> <l>ὦ φιλτάτου μνημεῖον ἀνθρώπων ἐμοὶ</l> <l>ψυχῆς Ὀρέστου λοιπόν</l> </lg> </quote> </cit> : <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Ant. 559" ref="Soph. Ant. 559"> <title>Ant.</title> 559</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἡ δ’ ἐμὴ ψυχὴ πάλαι | τέθνηκεν</quote> </cit> . </gloss>

data.quote: ἡ δ’ ἐμὴ ψυχὴ πάλαι | τέθνηκεν

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἅπαν_1000">, <quote xml:lang="eng">"anything,"</quote> cp. on <ref target="cl761">761</ref> :</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καλὸν_1000"> with <foreign xml:lang="grc">λέγειν</foreign> , <foreign xml:lang="lat">dictu honestum</foreign> , cp. on <ref target="cl37">37</ref> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ῥητὸν_ἄρρ_1000"> .: <cit> <bibl n="Dem. 18.122" ref="Dem. 18.122">Dem. or. 18 § 122</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">βοᾷς ῥητὰ καὶ ἄρρητα ὀνομάζων</quote> </cit> : <cit> <bibl n="Dem. 21.79" ref="Dem. 21.79">or. 21 § 79</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">πάντας ἡμᾶς ῥητὰ καὶ ἄρρητα κακὰ ἐξεῖπον</quote> </cit>. Remark that in neither place does Dem. place a <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ</quote> <emph> before</emph> <quote xml:lang="grc">ῥητά</quote> , or a <quote xml:lang="grc">τε</quote> after it. The form which he gives was doubtless the familiar one. On the other hand, in a phrase of different meaning, <cit> <bibl n="Dem. 1.4" ref="Dem. 1.4">Dem. or. 1 § 4</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ γὰρ εἶναι πάντων ἐκεῖνον ἕνα ὄντα κύριον καὶ ῥητῶν καὶ ἀπορρήτων</quote> </cit> , <quote xml:lang="eng">"of what is to be published or to be kept secret."</quote> <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 300" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg004:300"> <title>O. T.</title> 300</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">διδακτά τε | ἄρρητά τ’</quote> </cit> (n.). <cit> <bibl n="Verg. A. 1.543" ref="Verg. A. 1.543"> Verg. <title>Aen.</title> 1. 543</bibl> <quote xml:lang="lat">deos memores fandi atque nefandi</quote> </cit>: Hor. <title> Epp.</title> 1. 7. 72 <quote>dicenda tacenda locutus.</quote> </gloss>

data.quote:
πάντας ἡμᾶς ῥητὰ καὶ ἄρρητα κακὰ
									ἐξεῖπον

τὸ γὰρ εἶναι πάντων ἐκεῖνον ἕνα ὄντα
									κύριον καὶ ῥητῶν καὶ ἀπορρήτων

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸ_θωπ_1003"> ., <quote xml:lang="eng">"to pay court to the great name of Th. (to the renowned Th.)."</quote> Creon had been courteous to Theseus, as Theseus towards Thebes, and nothing more: there is no <quote xml:lang="grc">θωπεία</quote> in 940. But Oed. is incensed by the contrast between the rough words spoken of himself (944 ff.) by Creon, and the fair words to Theseus.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θωπεῦσαι_1003">: cp. 1336. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καλόν_1003">, not as in 1000, but= <quote xml:lang="eng"> " <emph>seasonable</emph> "</quote>: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 78" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg004:78"> <title>O. T.</title> 78</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰς καλὸν...εἶπας</quote> </cit> (n.): <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 384" ref="Soph. El. 384"> <title>El.</title> 384</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">νῦν γὰρ ἐν καλῷ φρονεῖν</quote> </cit> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὡς_καλῶς_1004">, lit., <quote xml:lang="eng">"that it has been administered well,"</quote> the perf. here denoting that a good administration is thoroughly <emph> established</emph> in it (cp. on <quote xml:lang="grc">τέτροφεν</quote> , 186). The political senses of <quote xml:lang="grc">κατοικέω</quote> and <quote xml:lang="grc">κατοικίζω</quote> should be carefully distinguished. (1) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἡ πόλις καλῶς κατοικεῖται</quote> =the city <emph>is dwelt-in</emph> on good principles, <quote xml:lang="eng">"is well administered"</quote> : see <ref n="Plat. Laws 683A"> Plat. <title>Legg.</title> 683A</ref>. (2) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἡ πόλις καλῶς κατῴκισται</quote> =the city <emph>has been established</emph> on good principles, <quote xml:lang="eng">"has a good constitution"</quote> : see <title>Legg.</title> 752 B. In this verse the poetical peculiarity is the use of the perf. where a prose-writer would have said either <quote xml:lang="grc">κατοικοῦνται</quote> or else <quote xml:lang="grc">κατῳκισμέναι εἰσί</quote> . Oed. refers to Creon's implied praise of Athenian loyalty (941 ff.), and esp. to his mention of the Areiopagus (947 ff.). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κᾆθ_1005">: cp. on <ref target="cl914">914</ref> . </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πολλὰ_1005"> with <foreign xml:lang="grc">ὧδ’</foreign> . </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἴ_θεοὺς_1006">: see on <ref target="cl260">260</ref> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τῷδ_1007">, referring to what has just <emph>preceded</emph> (cp. on <ref target="cl787">787</ref> ), as <ref n="Soph. Ant. 464"> <title>Ant.</title> 464</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Ant. 566">566</ref> , <ref n="Soph. Aj. 1080"> <title>Ai.</title> 1080</ref>. The dat., marking <emph>the point in which</emph> the excellence is shown, is the usu. constr.: so Thuc. has <cit> <quote xml:lang="grc">προέχειν δυνάμει</quote> <bibl n="Thuc. 1.9" ref="Thuc. 1.9">(1. 9)</bibl> </cit>, <cit> <quote xml:lang="grc">ναυτικῷ</quote> <bibl n="Thuc. 1.25" ref="Thuc. 1.25">(1. 25)</bibl> </cit> , <cit> <quote xml:lang="grc">πλήθει … καὶ ἐμπειρίᾳ</quote> <bibl n="Thuc. 1.121" ref="Thuc. 1.121">(1. 121)</bibl> </cit> , <cit> <quote xml:lang="grc">γνώμῃ</quote> <bibl n="Thuc. 2.62" ref="Thuc. 2.62">(2. 62)</bibl> </cit>, etc.: <cit> <bibl n="Xen. Anab. 3.2.19" ref="Xen. Anab. 3.2.19"> Xen. <title>Anab.</title> 3.2.19</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἑνὶ δὲ μόνῳ προέχουσιν ἡμᾶς</quote> </cit> : <cit> <bibl n="Xen. Const. Lac. 15.3" ref="Xen. Const. Lac. 15.3"> <title>Lac.</title> 15. 3</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">πλούτῳ ὑπερφέρειν</quote> </cit> : <cit> <bibl n="Hdt. 8.138" ref="Hdt. 8.138">Her. 8.138</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ῥόδα ὀδμῇ ὑπερφέροντα</quote> </cit> : <cit> <bibl n="Hdt. 8.144" ref="Hdt. 8.144">8. 144</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">χώρη κάλλεϊ καὶ ἀρετῇ μέγα ὑπερφέρουσα</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Hdt. 9.96" ref="Hdt. 9.96">9. 96</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">κάλλεϊ καὶ μεγάθεϊ ὑπερφέρων</quote> </cit>. Surely, then, usage is strongly for <foreign xml:lang="grc">τῷδ’</foreign> as against <foreign xml:lang="grc">τοῦθ’</foreign> . </gloss>

data.quote:
χώρη κάλλεϊ καὶ ἀρετῇ μέγα
									ὑπερφέρουσα

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κλέψας_1008">, in purpose (so far as Oed. himself is concerned), though not in fact: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 1126" ref="Soph. Aj. 1126"> <title>Ai.</title> 1126</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">δίκαια γὰρ τόνδ’ εὐτυχεῖν, κτείναντά με</quote> </cit>; <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Ion 1500" ref="Eur. Ion 1500"> Eur. <title>Ion</title> 1500</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔκτεινά σ’ ἄκουσ’</quote> </cit>(Creusa to her living son), "doomed thee to perish." We could hardly detach <foreign xml:lang="grc">κλέψας</foreign> from <foreign xml:lang="grc">τὸν ἱκέτην</foreign> , and render: "from which having stolen (the maidens), thou didst seek to seize <emph>me</emph> , after carrying off my daughters." </gloss>

data.quote:
δίκαια γὰρ τόνδ’ εὐτυχεῖν, κτείναντά
									με

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐχειροῦ_1009">, impf. of endeavour: see 274: cp. 950.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οἴχει_1009">: see on <ref target="cl867">867</ref> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κατασκήπτω_λιταῖς_1011"> , <quote xml:lang="eng"> " <emph>enjoin on</emph> you with prayers,"</quote>is an unexampled use of this compound. On the other hand <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπισκήπτω</quote> was often used in entreaty, as <cit> <bibl n="Aeschin. 3.157" ref="Aeschin. 3.157">Aeschin. or. 3 § 157</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">κλαίοντας, ἱκετεύοντας,...ἐπισκήπτοντας μηδενὶ τρόπῳ τὸν... ἀλιτήριον στεφανοῦν</quote> </cit>. Wecklein supports his attractive conjecture <foreign xml:lang="grc"> κἀξεπισκήπτω</foreign> ( <ref> <title>Ars Soph. em.</title> p. 99</ref>) by <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Phil. 668" ref="Soph. Phil. 668"> <title>Ph.</title> 668 </bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">κἀξεπεύξασθαι</quote> </cit> . </gloss>

data.quote:
κλαίοντας,
									ἱκετεύοντας,...ἐπισκήπτοντας μηδενὶ τρόπῳ τὸν...
									ἀλιτήριον στεφανοῦν

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ξεῖνος_1014">: cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 33">33</ref> . Elsewhere, with the exception of <ref>fr. 726. 4</ref> , Sophocles uses in dialogue only the vocative of the Ionic form.— </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αἱ_ς_1014">: while <emph>he</emph> is innocent, his <emph>fortunes</emph> have been appalling,</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄξιαι_ἀμ_1014"> , <emph>but</emph> (all the more) deserve sympathy. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄξιαι_ἀμυναθεῖν_1015">, worthy that one should succour them. The forms in <quote xml:lang="grc"> ‐θον</quote> have not always an aoristic force, <foreign xml:lang="lat"> e.g.</foreign> in <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 1014" ref="Soph. El. 1014"> <title>El.</title> 1014</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰκαθεῖν</quote> </cit>has no such force (cp. on <ref n="Soph. OT 651"> <title>O. T.</title> 651</ref>): but here, at least, as 461 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπάξιος...κατοικτίσαι</quote> shows, an aorist inf. is not less fitting than a present. For the <emph>act.</emph> inf., see on <ref target="cl461">461</ref> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="παθόντες_1016"> , and the impossibility (as I think) of justifying <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἐξηρπασμένοι</foreign> , confirm F. W. Schmidt's <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἐξειργασμένοι</foreign> . Since E also represented H in the older Ionic alphabet, the origin of the vulgate is at once explained if it is supposed that in <quote xml:lang="grc">ΕΞΕΡΓΑΣΜΕΝΟΙ</quote> the <quote xml:lang="grc">Γ</quote> became <quote xml:lang="grc">Π</quote> ,—one of the slightest and easiest of all errors in uncial writing. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐξηρπασμένοι_1016">must be explained in one of two ways. 1. <quote xml:lang="eng"> " <title>The captured ones are speeding.</title> "</quote>Here ( <emph>a</emph> ) <foreign xml:lang="grc">σπεύδουσιν</foreign> is most strange as= <quote xml:lang="eng">"are being carried off"</quote> : it should imply eagerness. ( <emph>b</emph> ) The masc. plur. is strangely used when <emph>two girls</emph> are definitely meant. It is different when a woman, speaking of <emph>herself</emph> in the <emph>plur.</emph> , uses the masc. ( <ref n="Soph. El. 399"> <title>El.</title> 399</ref>),— when the masc. sing. is used by the leader of a female Chorus ( <ref n="Eur. Hipp. 1105"> Eur. <title>Hipp.</title> 1105</ref>),—or when the masc. sing. is used in an abstract statement, though with allusion to a woman ( <ref n="Soph. El. 145"> <title>El.</title> 145</ref>). 2. <quote xml:lang="eng"> " <title>The captors are hurrying away.</title> "</quote>There is no other instance of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἥρπασμαι</quote> , simple or in comp., as a perf. middle, while the pass. use is common. This may be an accident, for there are several instances of perfect forms which can be either passive or middle,— <foreign xml:lang="lat">e.g.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἴργασμαι, τετιμώρημαι</quote> (pass. in <ref n="Thuc. 7.77">Thuc. 7.77</ref> , midd. in <ref n="Antiph. 4.2.8"> Antiph. <title>Tetr.</title> 3. <foreign xml:lang="grc">β.</foreign> § 8</ref> ), <quote xml:lang="grc">μεμίμημαι</quote> (pass. in <ref n="Hdt. 2.78">Her. 2.78</ref> , midd. <ref n="Hdt. 2.169"> <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 169</ref>etc.). But it is a fact which increases the difficulty of assuming a middle sense here. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἕσταμεν_1017">: the same form in <ref n="Soph. OT 1442"> <title>O. T.</title> 1442</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Trach. 1145"> <title>Tr.</title> 1145</ref>. In <ref n="Soph. El. 21"> <title>El.</title> 21</ref>, too, I would restore <quote xml:lang="grc">ἵν’ ἕσταμεν</quote> for the corrupt <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐνταῦθ’ ἐμέν</quote> . </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀμ_φωτὶ_1018"> , <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> Creon himself. The tone is half sulky, half whining. He has given up the game.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀμαυρῷ_1018"> here <quote xml:lang="eng">"feeble"</quote> (cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 880">880</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">βραχύς</quote> , 958 <quote xml:lang="grc">σμικρόν</quote> ), but in 182 <quote xml:lang="eng">"dim"</quote> (where see n.). Cp. 391 <quote xml:lang="grc">τοιοῦδ’ ὑπ’ ἀνδρός</quote> , said by Oed. of himself; and so 1109 <quote xml:lang="grc">φωτός</quote> .—Others render: <quote xml:lang="eng"> " <emph>for</emph> the blind man"</quote>(Oed.), a dat. of interest with <foreign xml:lang="grc">ποεῖν</foreign> . This seems harsh. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τῆς_ἐκεῖ_1019"> = <quote xml:lang="grc">τῆς ἐκεῖσε</quote> : <cit> <bibl n="Hdt. 9.108" ref="Hdt. 9.108">Her. 9.108</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκεῖ...ἀπίκετο</quote> </cit> : <cit> <bibl n="Thuc. 3.71" ref="Thuc. 3.71">Thuc. 3.71</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τοὺς ἐκεῖ καταπεφευγότας</quote> </cit>. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 1099" ref="Soph. El. 1099"> <title>El.</title> 1099</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁδοιποροῦμεν ἔνθα</quote> </cit> (= <quote xml:lang="grc">οἶ</quote> ) <quote xml:lang="grc"> χρῄζομεν</quote> : <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Phil. 256" ref="Soph. Phil. 256"> <title>Ph.</title> 256</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">μηδαμοῦ διῆλθε</quote> </cit> .</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἡμῖν_1021">, <quote xml:lang="eng">"for us,"</quote> <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> so that we may find them: ethic dat. (cp. 81). Campbell defends the MS. <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἡμῶν</foreign> as if <quote xml:lang="grc"> ἔχεις...ἡμῶν</quote> = <quote xml:lang="eng"> "hast taken <emph>from</emph> us,"</quote>comparing 821 <quote xml:lang="grc">τὴν παῖδ’ ἔχεις μου</quote> , where, however, the gen. is possessive, and <ref n="Soph. OT 1522"> <title>O. T.</title> 1522</ref>, where <quote xml:lang="grc">μου</quote> depends on <quote xml:lang="grc">ἕλῃ. τὰς παῖδας ἡμῶν</quote> could mean only <quote xml:lang="eng"> " <emph>our</emph> maidens,"</quote>—which is hardly to be justified as the language of a paternal government.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐγκρατεῖς_1022"> , <foreign xml:lang="lat">sc.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">τῶν παίδων</quote> :</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φεύγουσιν_1022"> , <foreign xml:lang="lat">sc.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">οἱ ἐξειργασμένοι</quote> (1016), Creon's guards. Theseus is not sure whether these guards have merely carried the sisters to some spot in Attica, at which they are to await Creon himself, or are already in full flight with them to Boeotia. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄλλοι_1023">: the horsemen who at 900 were told <quote xml:lang="grc">σπεύδειν ἀπὸ ῥυτῆρος</quote> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὓς_θεοῖς_1023">, <quote xml:lang="eng"> "from whom having escaped out of this land, never shall they make grateful ( <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπ‐"</quote> ) vows to the gods.</quote> <quote xml:lang="grc">φεύγω</quote>can take a gen. of separation, denoting the thing, or the region, <emph>from which</emph> one escapes: <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Od. 1.18" ref="Hom. Od. 1.18"> <title>Od.</title> 1.18</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδ’ ἔνθα πεφυγμένος ἦεν ἀέθλων</quote> </cit>. This gen. is here combined with an acc., as in <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Supp. 148" ref="Eur. Supp. 148"> Eur. <title>Suppl.</title> 148</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">Τυδεὺς μὲν αἷμα συγγενὲς φεύγων χθονός</quote> </cit>, flying from the land, from (the penalties of) a brother's murder: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Orest. 1506" ref="Eur. Orest. 1506"> <title>Or.</title> 1506</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ποῦ )στιν οὗτος ὃς πέφευγε τοὐμὸν ἐκ δόμων ξίφος</quote> </cit>:</gloss>

data.quote:
Τυδεὺς μὲν αἷμα συγγενὲς φεύγων
									χθονός

ποῦ )στιν οὗτος ὃς πέφευγε τοὐμὸν ἐκ
									δόμων ξίφος

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐπεύξωνται_1023">implies a vow of thank-offerings for safety: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Xen. Anab. 3.2.9" ref="Xen. Anab. 3.2.9"> Xen. <title>Anab.</title> 3.2.9</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">εὔξασθαι τῷ θεῷ τούτῳ θύσειν σωτήρια ὅπου ἂν πρῶτον εἰς φιλίαν χώραν ἀφικώμεθα</quote> </cit> . <ref n="Aesch. Seven 276"> Aesch. <title>Theb.</title> 276</ref> ( <quote xml:lang="grc">θεοῖς</quote> ) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπεύχομαι | θήσειν τρόπαια</quote> . The partic. <quote xml:lang="grc">φυγόντες</quote> expresses the cause to which <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπί</quote> in the compound refers: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Ant. 483" ref="Soph. Ant. 483"> <title>Ant.</title> 483</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">δεδρακυῖαν γελᾶν</quote> </cit>. (Distinguish the different sense of the verb in <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Soph. 235C" ref="Plat. Soph. 235C"> Plat. <title>Soph.</title> 235C</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὔτε ἄλλο γένος οὐδὲν μήποτε ἐκφυγὸν ἐπεύξηται τὴν...μέθοδον</quote> </cit> , <emph>glory in</emph> having eluded.) </gloss>

data.quote:
εὔξασθαι τῷ θεῷ τούτῳ θύσειν σωτήρια
									ὅπου ἂν πρῶτον εἰς φιλίαν χώραν ἀφικώμεθα

οὔτε ἄλλο γένος οὐδὲν μήποτε ἐκφυγὸν
									ἐπεύξηται τὴν...μέθοδον

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀλλ_1025">, <quote xml:lang="eng">"nay"</quote> ; cp. 237. In <foreign xml:lang="grc"> ἐξυφηγοῦ</foreign> (only here), <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκ</quote> refers to the moment of starting, while <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑπό</quote> = <quote xml:lang="eng">"onward,"</quote> as in <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑπάγω</quote> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔχων_ἔχει_1025">, cp. our phrase, <quote xml:lang="eng">"the biter bitten."</quote> <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. Ag. 340" ref="Aesch. Ag. 340"> Aesch. <title>Ag.</title> 340</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὔ τἂν ἑλόντες αὖθις ἀνθαλοῖεν ἄν</quote> </cit>. <cit> <bibl n="Hor. Ep. 2.1.156" ref="Hor. Ep. 2.1.156"> Hor. <title>Ep.</title> 2. 1. 156</bibl> <quote xml:lang="lat">capta ferum victorem cepit.</quote> </cit> <cit> <bibl n="Isaiah 14.2" ref="Isaiah 14.2">Isaiah xiv. 2</bibl> <quote xml:lang="eng">and they shall take them captives, whose captives they were.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

data.quote:
οὔ τἂν ἑλόντες αὖθις ἀνθαλοῖεν
									ἄν

and they shall take them captives,
									whose captives they were.

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θηρῶνθ_1026">recalls the metaphor used by Creon himself, <quote xml:lang="grc">τήνδ’ ἐχειρούμην ἄγραν</quote> (950).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἡ_τύχη_1026">=Destiny: see on <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 977" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg004:977"> <title>O. T.</title> 977</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τί δ’ ἂν φοβοῖτ’ ἄνθρωπος, ᾧ τὰ τῆς τύχης | κρατεῖ</quote> </cit>. The <quote xml:lang="eng">"irony of fate"</quote> is better denoted by <quote xml:lang="grc">τύχη</quote> than by the proposed substitute <quote xml:lang="grc">Δίκη</quote> ( <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 528" ref="Soph. El. 528"> <title>El.</title> 528</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἡ γὰρ Δίκη νιν εἷλεν, οὐκ ἐγὼ μόνη</quote> </cit>). </gloss>

data.quote:
τί δ’ ἂν φοβοῖτ’ ἄνθρωπος, ᾧ τὰ τῆς
									τύχης | κρατεῖ

ἡ γὰρ Δίκη νιν εἷλεν, οὐκ ἐγὼ
									μόνη

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὰ_κτήματα_1026"> = <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰ δόλῳ κατακτηθέντα</quote> , the instrum. dat. with the noun as with the cognate partic.: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Trach. 668" ref="Soph. Trach. 668"> <title>Tr.</title> 668</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τῶν σῶν Ἡρακλεῖ δωρημάτων</quote> </cit> : <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Laws 631C" ref="Plat. Laws 631C"> Plat. <title>Legg.</title> 631C</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἴς τε δρόμον καὶ εἰς τὰς ἄλλας πάσας κινήσεις τῷ σώματι</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Soph. 261e" ref="Plat. Soph. 261e"> <title>Soph.</title> 261 E</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τῶν τῇ φωνῇ περὶ τὴν οὐσίαν δηλωμάτων.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

data.quote:
εἴς τε δρόμον καὶ εἰς τὰς ἄλλας πάσας
									κινήσεις τῷ σώματι

τῶν τῇ φωνῇ περὶ τὴν οὐσίαν
									δηλωμάτων.

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τῷ_δικαίῳ_1026">: cp. 73. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κοὐκ_τόδ_1028">, and you will not have another (to aid you) with a view to this ( <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> to the removal of the captives). For this use of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔχειν</quote> cp. <cit> <bibl n="Andoc. 1.63" ref="Andoc. 1.63">Andoc. or. 1 § 63</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἕξεις ἡμᾶς ἐπιτηδείους</quote> </cit>: for <foreign xml:lang="grc">εἰς τόδ’</foreign> cp. 507.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὡς_ἔξοιδα_1028"> , <quote xml:lang="eng"> "(I speak of <quote xml:lang="eng"> another,"</quote> ) <emph>for</emph> I know,</quote>etc.: <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς</quote> causal; cp. 45. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐ_ψιλὸν_1028">: see on <ref target="cl866">866</ref> .</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄσκευον_1028">: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 36" ref="Soph. El. 36"> <title>El.</title> 36</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄσκευον αὐτὸν ἀσπίδων τε καὶ στρατοῦ</quote> </cit>. The allusion is not, of course, to Creon's guards, but to some Attic accomplices, whose secret aid had emboldened him to make the attempt (1031). The ancient Greek was quick to explain disaster by treason; thus it instantly occurs to Oedipus that some Theban must have been concerned in the murder of Laïus ( <ref n="Soph. OT 124"> <title>O. T.</title> 124</ref>). After Aegospotami, <quote xml:lang="eng">"the general belief...held that the Athenian fleet had been sold to perdition by some of its own commanders"</quote> (Grote VIII. 300). Theseus had no definite ground for his suspicion, but its utterance serves to place him (for a Greek audience) on the proper level of wary sagacity. </gloss>

data.quote:
ἄσκευον αὐτὸν ἀσπίδων τε καὶ
									στρατοῦ

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐς_τόλμης_1029">. The <quote xml:lang="grc">τόλμα</quote> is the audacious spirit manifested in the <quote xml:lang="grc">ὕβρις</quote> , or outrageous action. The gen. <foreign xml:lang="grc">τόλμης</foreign> seems best taken as partitive, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐς τοσήνδ’ ὕβριν ἥκοντα</quote> being equiv. to <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐς τοσοῦτον ἥκοντα</quote> : cp. <cit> <bibl n="Isoc. 8.31" ref="Isoc. 8.31">Isocr. 8 § 31</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰς τοῦτο γάρ τινες ἀνοίας ἐληλύθασιν</quote> </cit>(and n. on <ref n="Soph. OT 771"> <title>O. T.</title> 771</ref>): <quote xml:lang="eng"> "you have come to such a point of violence <emph>in</emph> the daring which now possesses you."</quote>If the gen. is taken as possessive, <quote xml:lang="grc">ὕβρις τόλμης</quote> nearly = <quote xml:lang="grc">ὕβρις τολμηρά</quote> : but the addition of <quote xml:lang="grc">τῆς παρεστώσης τανῦν</quote> makes this awkward. </gloss>

data.quote:
εἰς τοῦτο γάρ τινες ἀνοίας
									ἐληλύθασιν

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀλλ_ὅτῳ_1031">. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Aristoph. Cl. 1347" ref="Aristoph. Cl. 1347"> Aristoph. <title>Nub.</title> 1347</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="lyric"> <l>ὡς οὗτος, εἰ μή τῳ )πεποίθειν, οὐκ ἂν ἦν</l> <l>οὕτως ἀκόλαστος·</l> <l>ἀλλ’ ἔσθ’ ὅτῳ θρασύνεται.</l> </lg> </quote> </cit> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πιστὸς_1031">, active: <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. PB 916" ref="Aesch. PB 916"> Aesch. <title>PV</title> 916</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">θαρσῶν καθήσθω τοῖς πεδαρσίοις κτύποις | πιστός</quote> </cit>. So <quote xml:lang="grc">μεμπτός</quote> , <quote xml:lang="eng">"blaming"</quote> ( <ref n="Soph. Trach. 446"> <title>Tr.</title> 446</ref>); <quote xml:lang="grc">ὕποπτος</quote> , <quote xml:lang="eng">"suspecting"</quote> ( <ref n="Eur. Hec. 1135"> Eur. <title>Hec.</title> 1135</ref>); <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀφόβητος</quote> , <quote xml:lang="eng">"not fearing"</quote> ( <ref n="Soph. OT 885"> <title>O. T.</title> 885</ref>); <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄψαυστος</quote> , <quote xml:lang="eng">"not having touched"</quote> ( <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 969); <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀμφίπληκτος</quote> , <quote xml:lang="eng">"beating around"</quote> ( <ref n="Soph. Phil. 688"> <title>Ph.</title> 688</ref>). </gloss>

data.quote:
θαρσῶν καθήσθω τοῖς πεδαρσίοις κτύποις
									| πιστός

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τι_τούτων_1034">, ironical for <quote xml:lang="grc">ταῦτα</quote> : <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 1140" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg004:1140"> <title>O. T.</title> 1140</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">λέγω τι τούτων, ἢ οὐ λέγω πεπραγμένον</quote> </cit>; </gloss>

data.quote:
λέγω τι τούτων, ἢ οὐ λέγω
									πεπραγμένον

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὰ_νῦν_1034">is slightly better than <foreign xml:lang="grc">τανῦν</foreign> . (1) With <foreign xml:lang="grc">τὰ νῦν</foreign> the sense is:— <quote xml:lang="eng"> "Or do the things <emph>said just now</emph> seem to you no less vain than ( <emph>the things said</emph> ) at the time when you were plotting these deeds?"</quote>alluding to the remonstrances and menaces of the Chorus, 829 ff. <foreign xml:lang="grc">τὰ νῦν τε χὥτε</foreign> is then like <quote xml:lang="grc">τἀμὰ κἀκείνων</quote> (606), one article doing double duty. (2) With <foreign xml:lang="grc">τανῦν</foreign> : <quote xml:lang="eng"> "Or do these things ( <quote xml:lang="grc">ταῦτα"</quote> , supplied from <quote xml:lang="grc">τούτων</quote> ) seem to you to have been said in vain, <emph>both now</emph> , <emph>and when</emph> you were plotting these deeds?</quote>But it is natural that Theseus should refer to his own <emph>words</emph> —by <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰ νῦν</quote> —rather than to thoughts which the Chorus had suggested before him. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐνθάδ_ὢν_1036">has been generally suspected, because the qualification, <quote xml:lang="eng">"while here,"</quote> seems to suit Creon better than Theseus. But, though <quote xml:lang="grc"> ἐνθάδ’ <emph>ὄντ’</emph> ἐρεῖς ἐμέ</quote>lies near, the vulgate is right. <quote xml:lang="eng"> " <title>While here</title> ,"</quote>said of Theseus, means, <quote xml:lang="eng">"since this is your own realm, in which you have force at command."</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μεμπτὸν_ἐμοί_1036">, predicate; <quote xml:lang="eng">"you will say nothing to my dissatisfaction"</quote> : <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="eng">"you can say what you please,—I shall not dispute it."</quote> It is vain to argue with a master of legions. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="χωρῶν_νῦν_1038">, "threaten (if you will) now—only set out." The enclitic <foreign xml:lang="grc">νυν</foreign> ("well then") would be weak here: <foreign xml:lang="grc">νῦν</foreign> takes point from <ref n="Soph. OC 1037">1037</ref> . For the partic. expressing <emph>the leading idea of the sentence</emph> , cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Trach. 592" ref="Soph. Trach. 592"> <title>Tr.</title> 592</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλ’ εἰδέναι χρὴ δρῶσαν</quote> </cit> : <cit> <bibl n="Andoc. 3.34" ref="Andoc. 3.34">Andoc. or. 3 § 34</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">φημὶ...στρατηγὸν...λανθάνοντα δεῖν τοὺς πολλοὺς τῶν ἀνθρώπων καὶ ἐξαπατῶντα ἄγειν ἐπὶ τοὺς κινδύνους</quote> </cit>, "he must elude their notice, and beguile them, if he is to lead them," etc.: <cit> <bibl n="Thuc. 1.20" ref="Thuc. 1.20">Thuc. 1.20</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἵππαρχον οἴονται τύραννον ὄντα ἀποθανεῖν</quote> </cit>, "was <emph> reigning</emph> when he was killed": <cit> <bibl n="Thuc. 4.11" ref="Thuc. 4.11">4. 11</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰς σφετέρας ναῦς, βιαζομένους τὴν ἀπόβασιν, καταγνύναι ἐκέλευε</quote> </cit>: "he cried, 'Wreck your ships, if you must—but force your way ashore.'" </gloss>

data.quote:
φημὶ...στρατηγὸν...λανθάνοντα δεῖν
									τοὺς πολλοὺς τῶν ἀνθρώπων καὶ ἐξαπατῶντα ἄγειν ἐπὶ τοὺς
									κινδύνους

τὰς σφετέρας ναῦς, βιαζομένους τὴν
									ἀπόβασιν, καταγνύναι ἐκέλευε

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πιστωθεὶς_1039">, as <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Od. 21.218" ref="Hom. Od. 21.218"> <title>Od.</title> 21.218</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὄφρα μ’ ἐῢ γνῶτον πιστωθῆτόν γ’ ἐνὶ θυμῷ</quote> </cit>, that ye twain may be <emph>assured</emph> in your minds: but elsewhere <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπιστώθην</quote> is said of him who <emph>gives</emph> the pledge ( <ref n="Eur. IA 66"> Eur. <title>I.A.</title> 66</ref>etc.): cp. on <ref target="cl650">650</ref> . </gloss>

data.quote:
ὄφρα μ’ ἐῢ γνῶτον πιστωθῆτόν γ’ ἐνὶ
									θυμῷ

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὄναιο_1042">, a blessing, usu. with simple gen., as <cit> <bibl n="Eur. IA 1359" ref="Eur. IA 1359"> Eur. <title>I. A.</title> 1359</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὄναιο τῶν φρενῶν</quote> </cit> , <quote xml:lang="eng">"bless thee for thy kindness,"</quote> or a defining partic., as <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Orest. 1677" ref="Eur. Orest. 1677"> <title>Or.</title> 1677</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">γήμας ὄναιο</quote> </cit>: but there is no reason to suspect </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="χάριν_1042"> , for which Blaydes suggests <quote xml:lang="grc">τρόπου</quote> . Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 569" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg007:569">569</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ σὸν γενναῖον</quote> </cit> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἴην_ὅθι_1044">: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 1218" ref="Soph. Aj. 1218"> <title>Ai.</title> 1218</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <note anchored="true" place="inline" resp="RCJ" xml:lang="eng"> (chorus)</note>γενοίμαν ἵν’ ὑλᾶεν ἔπεστι πόντου | πρόβλημ’</quote> </cit>, etc.: <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Hipp. 732" ref="Eur. Hipp. 732"> Eur. <title>Hipp.</title> 732</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <note anchored="true" place="inline" resp="RCJ" xml:lang="eng"> (chorus)</note>ἀλιβάτοις ὑπὸ κευθμῶσι γενοίμαν, | ἵνα</quote> </cit> etc. </gloss>

data.quote: γενοίμαν ἵν’ ὑλᾶεν ἔπεστι πόντου | πρόβλημ’

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐπιστροφαὶ_1045">, the wheeling-about of Creon's guards, carrying off their captives, when overtaken by the Attic pursuers. For the military use of the word see on <ref target="cl536">536</ref> . <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνδρῶν ἐπιστροφαί</quote> = <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄνδρες ἐπιστρεφθέντες</quote> : cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 417" ref="Soph. El. 417"> <title>El.</title> 417</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰσιδεῖν πατρὸς...δευτέραν ὁμιλίαν</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Her. 581" ref="Eur. Her. 581"> Eur. <title>Her.</title> 581</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑμεῖς δ’, ἀδελφῶν ἡ παροῦσ’ ὁμιλία</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Alc. 606" ref="Eur. Alc. 606"> <title>Alc.</title> 606</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνδρῶν Φεραίων εὐμενὴς παρουσία</quote> </cit>. </gloss>

data.quote:
ὑμεῖς δ’, ἀδελφῶν ἡ παροῦσ’
									ὁμιλία

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="χαλκοβόαν_1046">cannot be resolved into two separate epithets,— <quote xml:lang="eng"> "brassclad,"</quote> and <quote xml:lang="eng">"clamorous"</quote> : rather it seems to mean, <quote xml:lang="eng">"with noise of brass,"</quote> —the clatter of shields and swords in battle. Cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 190"> <title>O. T.</title> 190</ref>, where the Death-god (the plague) is an Ares who is <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄχαλκος ἀσπίδων</quote> , yet <quote xml:lang="grc">περιβόατος. χαλκεόφωνος</quote> , <quote xml:lang="eng"> "with <emph>voice as</emph> of brass,"</quote>is not really similar: it is the epithet of Stentor ( <ref n="Hom. Il. 5.785"> <title>Il.</title> 5.785</ref>) and of Cerberus ( <ref n="Hes. Th. 311"> Hes. <title>Theog.</title> 311</ref>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μείξουσιν_1046">: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Il. 15.510" ref="Hom. Il. 15.510"> <title>Il.</title> 15.510</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἢ αὐτοσχεδίῃ μῖξαι χεῖράς τε μένος τε</quote> </cit>. The Attic spelling in the age of Sophocles was <quote xml:lang="grc">μείξω</quote> (not <quote xml:lang="grc">μίξω</quote> ), <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔμειξα</quote> , verb. adj. <quote xml:lang="grc">μεικτός</quote> : and so in the proper names <quote xml:lang="grc">Μειξίας, Μείξιππος</quote> , etc.: see Meisterhans pp. 25, 87. There is no epigraphic evidence for the pres.; but, as Curtius remarks ( <title>Gr. Verb</title> p. 111 Eng. tr.), <quote xml:lang="grc">μείγνυμι</quote> : <quote xml:lang="grc">μιγ</quote> :: <quote xml:lang="grc">δείκνυμι</quote> : <quote xml:lang="grc">δικ</quote> . </gloss>

data.quote:
ἢ αὐτοσχεδίῃ μῖξαι χεῖράς τε μένος
									τε

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἢ_ἀκταῖς_1046">. The Chorus here imagine the Athenians as pursuing the Thebans through the pass of Daphne, over Mount Aegaleos, towards Eleusis. Two points are mentioned as possible scenes for a fight.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πότνιαι_1050">, Demeter and Persephone (Cora), who in <ref n="Aristoph. Thes. 1156"> Ar. <title>Th.</title> 1156</ref>are called <quote xml:lang="grc">Θεσμοφόρω πολυποτνία</quote> . Cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 683">683.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τιθηνοῦνται_1050">, as the spiritual nurturers of their faithful votaries. Simonides, too, has this word in a fig. sense, <cit> <bibl n="Simon. 150.7" ref="Simon. 150.7">fr. 150. 7</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">εὖ δ’ ἐτιθηνεῖτο γλυκερὰν ὄπα Δωρίοις Ἀρίστων | Ἀργεῖος</quote> </cit> ("cultivated"). </gloss>

data.quote:
εὖ δ’ ἐτιθηνεῖτο γλυκερὰν ὄπα Δωρίοις
									Ἀρίστων | Ἀργεῖος

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τέλη_1050">: <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Rep. 560E" ref="Plat. Rep. 560E"> Plat. <title>Rep.</title> 560E</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τελουμένου ψυχὴν μεγάλοισι τέλεσι</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Hipp. 25" ref="Eur. Hipp. 25"> Eur. <title>Hipp.</title> 25</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">σεμνῶν ἐς ὄψιν καὶ τέλη μυστηρίων</quote> </cit>: Aesch. fr. 377 <quote xml:lang="grc">μυστικοῦ τέλους</quote> : in prose usu. <quote xml:lang="grc">τελεταί</quote> . </gloss>

data.quote:
σεμνῶν ἐς ὄψιν καὶ τέλη
									μυστηρίων

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θνατοῖσιν_1051">, esp. fitting here, since the highest value of the Eleusinia consisted in opening a prospect of bliss after death. <cit> <bibl>Soph. fr. 753</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="trimter"> <l>ὡς τρὶς ὄλβιοι</l> <l>κεῖνοι βροτῶν, οἳ ταῦτα δερχθέντες τέλη</l> <l>μόλωσ’ ἐς Ἅιδου· τοῖσδε γὰρ μόνοις ἐκεῖ</l> <l>ζῆν ἔστι, τοῖς δ’ ἄλλοισι πάντ’ ἐκεῖ κακά</l> </lg> </quote> </cit> . <cit> <bibl>Pindar fr. 114</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὄλβιος ὅστις ἰδὼν κεῖν’ εἶσ’ ὑπὸ χθόν)· οἶδε μὲν βίου τελευτάν, οἶδεν δὲ διόσδοτον ἀρχάν</quote> </cit>. <cit> <bibl n="Isoc. 4.28" ref="Isoc. 4.28">Isocr. or. 4 § 28</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἧς ( τελετῆς ) οἱ μετασχόντες περί τε τῆς τοῦ βίου τελευτῆς καὶ τοῦ σύμπαντος αἰῶνος ἡδίους τὰς ἐλπίδας ἔχουσιν</quote> </cit>. </gloss>

data.quote:
ὄλβιος ὅστις ἰδὼν κεῖν’ εἶσ’ ὑπὸ
									χθόν)· οἶδε μὲν βίου τελευτάν, οἶδεν δὲ διόσδοτον
									ἀρχάν

ἧς ( τελετῆς ) οἱ μετασχόντες περί τε
									τῆς τοῦ βίου τελευτῆς καὶ τοῦ σύμπαντος αἰῶνος ἡδίους
									τὰς ἐλπίδας ἔχουσιν

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὧν_χρυσέα_1051"> <foreign xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</foreign>: <foreign xml:lang="grc">ὧν</foreign> refers to <quote xml:lang="grc">θνατοῖσιν</quote> : <foreign xml:lang="grc">καὶ</foreign> ("also") has the effect of limiting the reference to those persons on whom the pledge of secrecy has been imposed; — " <emph>those mortals on whose lips has been set the divine seal of the ministrant Eumolpidae</emph> ": <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> those who have been duly initiated by the Eumolpid Hierophant at Eleusis, and have been bound by him to secrecy. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κλὴς_Εὐμολπιδᾶν_1051">(possessive gen.), the silence which they impose. Perhaps we should read <foreign xml:lang="grc">βέβακ’ ἐκ</foreign> . The Eumolpidae figure here as interpreters between the Two Goddesses and mortals, not as guardians of a secret which they may not communicate. Hence the above version is better than either of the following:—(1) <foreign xml:lang="grc">ὦν</foreign> referring to <foreign xml:lang="grc">πότνιαι</foreign> : "whose seal has been set on the lips of the Eumolpidae": (2) <foreign xml:lang="grc">ὧν</foreign> referring to <foreign xml:lang="grc">τέλη</foreign> : "the seal whereof has been set on the lips of the E." </gloss>
