<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κλὴς_1052">, "that which closes," cannot well be rendered " <emph>key</emph> " here, any more than in <cit> <bibl>Aesch. fr. 309</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλ’ ἔστι κἀμοὶ κλὴ|ς ἐπὶ γλώσσῃ φύλαξ</quote> </cit>. The apparent boldness of a Greek metaphor is sometimes thus mitigated by the poet's consciousness of the literal sense; as when Pindar calls an inspiring thought an <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀκόνη</quote> ,—literally, "sharpener," conventionally "whetstone"); or when he calls the master, who tempers a chorus into harmony, a <quote xml:lang="grc">κρατήρ</quote> ( <ref n="Pind. O. 6"> <title>Ol.</title> 6. 82</ref>, <ref n="Pind. O. 6">91</ref> : cp. my paper in <ref> <title>Journ. Hellen. Stud.</title> III. 171</ref>). — Cp. the <cit> <quote xml:lang="grc">βοῦς ἐπὶ γλώσσῃ</quote> <bibl n="Aesch. Ag. 36" ref="Aesch. Ag. 36"> (Aesch. <title>Ag.</title> 36)</bibl> </cit>, perh. a mere metaphor from a heavy weight,—parodied by <cit> <bibl> Menander <title xml:lang="grc">Ἁλιεῖς</title> fr. 1</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">παχὺς γὰρ ὗς ἔκειτ’ ἐπὶ στόμα</quote> </cit> . <cit> <bibl> <title>Anthol. Pal.</title> 10. 42</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀρρήτων ἐπέων γλώσσῃ σφραγὶς ἐπικείσθω</quote> </cit>. <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Med. 660" ref="Eur. Med. 660"> Eur. <title>Med.</title> 660</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">καθαρὰν ἀνοίξ | αντα κλῇδα φρενῶν</quote> </cit>, "having unlocked his heart in sincerity." <quote xml:lang="grc">κλῃδοῦχος</quote> was said either of a tutelar deity or of a priestess, and on the vases the symbolic key, adorned with woollen threads, is sometimes borne by the priestess ( <ref>Passeri III. 294</ref> , <ref> Welcker <title>Alte Denkm.</title> III. 450 ff. </ref>etc.): but there is no evidence for the Eleusinian Hierophant actually <emph>putting a key</emph> to the lips of the initiated. </gloss>

data.quote:
ἀλλ’ ἔστι κἀμοὶ κλὴ|ς ἐπὶ γλώσσῃ
									φύλαξ
παχὺς γὰρ ὗς ἔκειτ’ ἐπὶ στόμα

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="χρυσέα_1052">, divine, precious,—because of the truths revealed: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 157" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg004:157"> <title>O. T.</title> 157</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">χρυσέας τέκνον Ἐλπίδος</quote> </cit> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="προσπόλων_Εὐμολπιδᾶν_1053">. The Eleusinia had four chief ministrants. 1. The <quote xml:lang="grc"> ἱεροφάντης</quote> . This office was hereditary in the Eumolpid gens; <cit> <bibl> Plut. <title>De Exil.</title> 17</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">Εὔμολπος ἐμύησε καὶ μυεῖ τοὺς Ἕλληνας</quote> </cit>(as the earliest hierophant, and the ancestor of his successors). 2. The <quote xml:lang="grc">δᾳδοῦχος</quote> : hereditary in the gens of Callias and Hipponicus, which traced itself from Triptolemus. 3. The <quote xml:lang="grc">ἱεροκήρυξ</quote> : hereditary in the gens of the <quote xml:lang="grc">Κηρυκίδαι</quote> (or <quote xml:lang="grc">Κήρυκες</quote> ). 4. The altar-priest, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἱερεὺς ὁ ἐπὶ βωμῷ</quote> , or <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπιβώμιος</quote> , who offered the sacrifice. It is not known whether this office was hereditary. As some relationship seems to have existed between the Eumolpidae and the two other gentes, <foreign xml:lang="grc">προσπόλων</foreign> here possibly includes (2) and (3), but is more naturally taken of the <quote xml:lang="grc">ἱεροφάντης</quote> only. A hydria found at Cumae, and belonging to a Campanian collection now at St Petersburg, exhibits an Eleusinian group of deities and priests, among whom the <quote xml:lang="grc">ἱεροφάντης</quote> is distinguished by a long white stole, partly embroidered with gold, a myrtle wreath, and the thyrsus. (It is reproduced by Baumeister, <title>Denkmäler des kl. Alt</title> .., p. 474, pl. 520.)</gloss>

data.quote:
Εὔμολπος ἐμύησε καὶ μυεῖ τοὺς
									Ἕλληνας

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐγρεμάχαν_1054"> , <quote xml:lang="eng">"rousing the fight,"</quote> is a fit epithet for the champion who overtakes the captors, and forces them to a contest. Elsewhere we find only the fem. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐγρεμάχη</quote> , as epithet of Pallas, <ref n="HH 5.424"> <title>Hom. Hymn.</title> 5.424</ref>. Cp. <ref n="Bacchyl. Ep. 12.100">Bacchyl. 12. 100</ref> [=13.67 of Kenyon's ed.] <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀερσιμάχους</quote> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Θησέα_1055">has the final <quote xml:lang="grc">α</quote> long in 1458, but short here: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Hec. 882" ref="Eur. Hec. 882"> Eur. <title>Hec.</title> 882</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ξὺν ταῖσδε τὸν ἐμὸν φονέα τιμωρήσομαι</quote> </cit>, =870 ed. Porson, who adds Philemon <foreign xml:lang="lat">ap.</foreign> Athen. 7. 307 E <quote xml:lang="grc">κεστρἔ ὀπτόν</quote> . Is the MS. <foreign xml:lang="grc">καὶ</foreign> after <quote xml:lang="grc">Θησέα</quote> genuine? If so, <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἐμμείξειν</foreign> is here intrans., like <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπι‐, προς‐, συμμιγνύναι</quote> : and the sense is, <quote xml:lang="eng"> "Theseus and the two maidens will soon <emph>meet</emph> amid a battle-cry of confident prowess."</quote>Thus with <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἐμμείξειν</foreign> we are to understand <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλήλοις</quote> . The verb is fitting, because the maidens, though their sympathies are with Theseus, are <emph>in the midst of the hostile force.</emph> </gloss>

data.quote:
ξὺν ταῖσδε τὸν ἐμὸν φονέα
									τιμωρήσομαι

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αὐτάρκει_βοᾷ_1055">is dat. of circumstance. This I believe to be the right view. <title>Not</title> , <quote xml:lang="eng"> "Th. and the maidens will join battle <emph>with the foe</emph> ,"</quote> <foreign xml:lang="lat">sc.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῖς πολεμίοις</quote>: for the maidens are in the hands of the foe. Such a phrase is not defensible merely because <emph>in spirit</emph> they are with Theseus.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="διστόλους_1055"> = <quote xml:lang="eng">"two journeying"</quote> sisters, —as borne off by their captors: see on <ref target="cl17">17</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πυκνόπτεροι</quote> . Not, <quote xml:lang="eng">"separately carried off,"</quote> with ref. to two bands of Thebans (cp. 818). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αὐτάρκει_1055"> , <quote xml:lang="eng">"self-sufficing,"</quote> and so <quote xml:lang="eng">"self-reliant,"</quote> giving confident promise of victorious rescue.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τούσδ_χ_1055"> : <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> in Attica, before the border can be passed.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="νομοῦ_1059">, being always masc., could not agree with <foreign xml:lang="grc"> Οἰάτιδος</foreign> , and the latter, without art., could not stand for <quote xml:lang="grc">Οἰάτιδος γῆς</quote> : while <quote xml:lang="grc"> Οἰατέος</quote> is most improbable.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πελῶς_1059">, if sound, must be <emph>fut.</emph> of <quote xml:lang="grc">πελάζω</quote> , as <quote xml:lang="grc">πελᾶν</quote> clearly is in <ref n="Soph. El. 497"> <title>El.</title> 497</ref>. The evidence for a pres. <quote xml:lang="grc">πελάω</quote> is scanty ( <cit> <bibl n="HH 7.44" ref="HH 7.44"> <title>Hom. Hymn.</title> 7.44</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">πελάαν</quote> </cit> : <ref> poet. <foreign xml:lang="lat">ap.</foreign> Plut. <title> Mor.</title> 457 C</ref>imperat. <quote xml:lang="grc">πέλα</quote> : <ref n="Opp. C. 1.514"> Oppian <title>Cyn.</title> 1. 514</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πελάει</quote>: cp. Veitch <title>Irreg. Verbs</title> ). The fut. seems defensible here, as= <quote xml:lang="eng">"they will (presently) approach"</quote> : though Hartung's <foreign xml:lang="grc">περῶσ’</foreign> may be right. Construe, then: — <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἤ που πελῶσ’ εἰς ἐφέσπερον πέτρ. νιφ. Οἰάτιδος νομόν</foreign> : <quote xml:lang="eng"> " <emph>or perchance they will presently approach the pastures to the west of the snowy rock of Oea.</emph> "</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ῥιμφαρμάτοις_ἁμίλλαις_1062"> = <quote xml:lang="grc">ἁμίλλαις ῥίμφα φερομένων ἁρμάτων</quote> (see on <ref target="cl711">710</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc"> αὔχημα...εὔιππον</quote> ), emulous careers of swift chariots, as <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 861" ref="Soph. El. 861"> <title>El.</title> 861</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">χαλαργοῖς ἐν ἁμίλλαις</quote> </cit>, races of swift steeds: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Ant. 1065" ref="Soph. Ant. 1065"> <title>Ant.</title> 1065</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τροχοὺς ἁμιλλητῆρας ἡλίου</quote> </cit> , <emph>rapid</emph> courses of the sun. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἁλώσεται_1065"> , <foreign xml:lang="lat">sc.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁ Κρέων</quote> , <quote xml:lang="eng">"he will be worsted"</quote> (not, <quote xml:lang="eng">"captured,"</quote> since he was already in the hands of Theseus): cp. <cit> <bibl n="Thuc. 1.121" ref="Thuc. 1.121">Thuc. 1.121</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">μιᾷ...νικῇ...ἁλίσκονται</quote> </cit>, they are sure to be <emph>overthrown</emph> by one victory of ours. For the ellipse of the subject, where the mind could readily supply it, cp. <cit> <bibl n="Xen. Cyrop. 2.4.24" ref="Xen. Cyrop. 2.4.24"> Xen. <title>Cyr.</title> 2.4.24</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">πορεύσομαι διὰ τοῦ πεδίου εὐθὺς πρὸς τὰ βασίλεια. καὶ ἢν μὲν ἀνθιστῆται</quote> </cit> , <quote xml:lang="eng">"and if the enemy (the king) resist,"</quote> etc.—This is better than (1) <quote xml:lang="eng">"the fugitive will be captured,"</quote> supplying <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁ φεύγων</quote> from <quote xml:lang="grc">φεύγοντες</quote> : (2) <quote xml:lang="eng">"a capture will be made,"</quote> — taking the verb as impers.: or (3) <quote xml:lang="eng">"the battle will be won,"</quote> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἁλώσεται ὁ ἀγών</quote> , as Elms. takes it, comparing 1148 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἁγὼν ᾑρέθη</quote> . </gloss>

data.quote:
πορεύσομαι διὰ τοῦ πεδίου εὐθὺς πρὸς
									τὰ βασίλεια. καὶ ἢν μὲν ἀνθιστῆται

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="προσχώρων_1065">, the neighbours of the grove, the Coloniates (cp. 493); not, <quote xml:lang="eng">"our neighbours the Thebans,"</quote> for the Chorus are predicting an easy victory, not a tough fight. Colonus and its neighbourhood had furnished a contingent to the party of rescue (897).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Θησειδᾶν_1065">, schol. <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἀθηναίων</quote> : cp. <quote xml:lang="grc">Κεκροπίδαι, Ἐρεχθεῖδαι</quote> , Aeneadae, etc.: here, followers of Theseus from <title>Athens</title> , as distinct from the Coloniates. We could not well understand, with Ellendt, <quote xml:lang="eng"> "the Coloniates, and the followers of Theseus <emph> generally</emph> ,"</quote>as if <quote xml:lang="grc">Θησειδᾶν</quote> <emph> included</emph> <quote xml:lang="grc">προσχώρων.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀκμά_1065">, vigour, might: <cit> <bibl n="Pind. I. 3" ref="Pind. I. 3"> Pind. <title>Isthm.</title> 3. 68</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="lyric"> <l>ἀλλ’ ὀνοτὸς μὲν ἰδέσθαι,</l> <l>συμπεσεῖν δ’ ἀκμᾷ βαρύς</l> </lg> </quote> </cit>, "dread to grapple with in his strength." </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀμπυκτήρια_πώλων_1069">is the MS. reading. Hesychius <foreign xml:lang="lat">s.v.</foreign> has: <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀμπυκτήρια· τὰ φάλαρα. Σοφοκλῆς Οἰδίποδι ἐν Κολωνῷ</quote> . This proves what the metre (on any view of 1054) already hinted,— that <foreign xml:lang="grc">φάλαρα</foreign> is a gloss. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀμπυκτήριον</quote> here= <quote xml:lang="eng">"bridle,"</quote> as <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀμπυκτήρ</quote> in <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. Seven 461" ref="Aesch. Seven 461"> Aesch. <title>Theb.</title> 461</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἵππους δ’ ἐν ἀμπυκτῆρσιν ἐμβριμωμένας</quote> </cit>: where the schol. (minor) expressly says that <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄμπυξ</quote> (properly the <emph>head-band</emph> ) was similarly used: <quote xml:lang="grc">κυρίως οἱ περὶ τὴν κεφαλὴν ἱμάντες τοῦ χαλινοῦ ἄμπυξ καλοῦνται</quote> : and so Quintus Smyrnaeus uses <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄμπυξ</quote> , 4. 511. It is but a slight poetical extension of meaning to use <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἀμπυκτήρια</foreign> as including the <emph>bridle-reins.</emph> The MS. <foreign xml:lang="grc">πώλων</foreign> is against the metre, unless 1054 f. are very boldly altered (see Metrical Analysis). When the gloss <foreign xml:lang="grc">φάλαρα</foreign> had crept into the text, <foreign xml:lang="grc">πώλων</foreign> (suggested by 1062) may have been tacked on to it. Wecklein's conjecture, <quote xml:lang="grc"> ἀμπυκτήρια <emph>στομίων</emph> </quote>( <quote xml:lang="eng"> "the <emph>reins</emph> of the <emph>bits</emph> "</quote>) gives an exact correspondence with 1054 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔνθ’ οἶμαι τὸν ἐγρεμάχαν</quote> . Nothing better has been suggested. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄμβασις_1070">, in such a context, needs not to be defined by <quote xml:lang="grc"> πώλων</quote> . For the apocopè, cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Ant. 1275" ref="Soph. Ant. 1275"> <title>Ant.</title> 1275</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀντρέπων</quote> </cit>, n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οἳ_1070">, as if <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀναβάται</quote> had gone before: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 235" ref="Soph. Aj. 235"> <title>Ai.</title> 235</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὧν</quote> </cit> after <quote xml:lang="grc">ποίμνην</quote> : <cit> <bibl n="Hdt. 8.128" ref="Hdt. 8.128">Her. 8.128</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> περιέδραμε ὅμιλος,... <emph>οἳ</emph> </quote> </cit>etc. Cp. 942 n. ( <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐτούς</quote> after <quote xml:lang="grc">πόλιν</quote> ).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὰν_ἱππίαν_1070">: see on <ref target="cl55">55</ref> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γαιάοχον_1072">, in the Homeric use, is most simply explained as "earth-embracer," with ref. to the Homeric idea of <quote xml:lang="grc">Ὠκεανός</quote> flowing round the earth: though some understand "reigning on earth" (as Zeus in heaven, and Hades in the nether world <ref n="Hom. Il. 15.190"> <title>Il.</title> 15.190</ref>). Some take it here as = " <emph>guarding our land,</emph> " like <quote xml:lang="grc">γ. Ἄρτεμιν</quote> in <ref n="Soph. OT 160"> <title>O. T.</title> 160</ref>: and this certainly has more special point here. But would the constant Homeric epithet of Poseidon be applied to <emph>him</emph> in a sense different from the Homeric? All Greek hearers would think of the <quote xml:lang="grc">γαιήοχος Ἐννοσίγαιος.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Ῥέας_1072">, here a monosyllable, as in <ref n="Hom. Il. 15.187"> <title>Il.</title> 15.187</ref>,—the only instance of this form in <title> Il.</title> or <title>Od.</title> , but a dissyllable in <cit> <bibl n="HH 5.459" ref="HH 5.459"> <title>Hom. Hymn.</title> 5.459,</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὴν δ’ ὧδε προσέειπε Ῥέη λιπαροκρήδεμνος</quote> </cit>. Elsewhere in the <title>Hymns</title> the form is always <quote xml:lang="grc">Ῥείη</quote> , as in <ref n="Hom. Il. 14.203"> <title>Il.</title> 14.203</ref>. In <cit> <bibl n="Hes. Th. 634" ref="Hes. Th. 634"> Hes. <title>Th.</title> 634</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">Ῥεῖα</quote> </cit>is read. </gloss>

data.quote:
τὴν δ’ ὧδε προσέειπε Ῥέη
									λιπαροκρήδεμνος

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔρδους_1074">: <quote xml:lang="eng"> "are they (the pursuers) in action, or on the point of being so? <emph>for</emph> ( <foreign xml:lang="grc">ὡς</foreign> ) I have a foreboding, etc."</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μέλλουσιν_1074"> , <foreign xml:lang="lat">sc.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc"> ἔρξειν</quote> : cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Trach. 74" ref="Soph. Trach. 74"> <title>Tr.</title> 74</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="trimeter"> <l>Εὐβοῖδα χώραν φασίν, Εὐρύτου πόλιν,</l> <l>ἐπιστρατεύειν αὐτὸν ἢ μέλλειν ἔτι</l> </lg> </quote> </cit> : <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Phil. 567" ref="Soph. Phil. 567"> <title>Ph.</title> 567</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς ταῦτ’ ἐπίστω δρώμεν’, οὐ μέλλοντ’ ἔτι</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Phil. 1255" ref="Soph. Phil. 1255"> <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 1255</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλὰ κἀμέ τοι | ταὐτὸν τόδ’ ὄψει δρῶντα κοὐ μέλλοντ’ ἔτι</quote> </cit> . </gloss>

data.quote:
ὡς ταῦτ’ ἐπίστω δρώμεν’, οὐ μέλλοντ’
									ἔτι

ἀλλὰ κἀμέ τοι | ταὐτὸν τόδ’ ὄψει
									δρῶντα κοὐ μέλλοντ’ ἔτι

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γνώμα_μοι_1075">my mind </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="προμνᾶταί_τι_1075"> (adv.) somehow pleads for the belief, presages. <quote xml:lang="grc"> προμνᾶσθαι</quote> means (1) <emph>to woo</emph> for another, <quote xml:lang="grc">κόρην τινί</quote> : (2) fig., to seek to obtain anything for another, <foreign xml:lang="lat">e.g.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">δῶρά τινι</quote> . The bold use here comes through the notion of <emph>pleading</emph> , or <emph>speaking persuasively</emph> , as the <quote xml:lang="grc">προμνήστρια</quote> to the maiden on behalf of the lover. This use is bolder than the fig. use in <ref n="Plat. Menex. 239c"> Plat. <title>Menex.</title> 239 C</ref>, where the question is of themes which have already been <quote xml:lang="eng">"married to immortal verse,"</quote> as distinguished from others in respect of which Poetry <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔτι ἐστιν ἐν μνηστείᾳ</quote> , is still in the stage of courtship. Of these latter, says the orator, I will speak— <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπαινοῦντά τε καὶ προμνώμενον ἄλλοις ἐς ᾠδὰς...αὐτὰ θεῖναι</quote> , commending them, and <emph>wooing them for others</emph> ( <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> for the poets), with a view to their putting them into verse. (A passage which has often been misunderstood as if <quote xml:lang="grc">προμν. ἄλλοις</quote> meant <quote xml:lang="eng"> "pleading <emph>with</emph> others"</quote>:—so Lidd. and Scott, with Ast.) </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀντάσειν_1076">(Buecheler),—a conjecture which had occurred independently to myself,—seems the most probable correction of <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἂν δώσειν</foreign> . The Chorus express a presentiment that they will soon again be brought face to face with the maidens who were dragged away before their eyes; and this prepares for the approaching entrance of Antigone and Ismene, 1097 <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰς κόρας γὰρ εἰσορῶ.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀντάω_1076">usu. takes a dat. of <emph>meeting a person</emph> , but sometimes a gen., as <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Il. 16.423" ref="Hom. Il. 16.423"> <title>Il.</title> 16.423</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀντήσω γὰρ ἐγὼ τοῦδ’ ἀνέρος</quote> </cit>(in battle). With the gen., <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἀντάω</foreign> also= <quote xml:lang="grc">κυρεῖν, τυγχάνειν</quote> : <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Od. 3.97" ref="Hom. Od. 3.97"> <title>Od.</title> 3.97</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἤντησας ὀπωπῆς</quote> </cit> : <cit> <bibl n="Hdt. 2.119" ref="Hdt. 2.119">Her. 2.119</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ξεινίων ἤντησε μεγάλων</quote> </cit>. Cp. Soph. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Ant. 982" ref="Soph. Ant. 982"> <title>Ant.</title> 982</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄντασ’ Ἐρεχθειδᾶν</quote> </cit>, she <emph>attained unto</emph> them (traced her lineage back to them). Here the idea of <emph>obtaining back</emph> is blended with that of being brought <emph>face to face.</emph> It is not, then, a valid objection that the Chorus do not <emph>move to meet</emph> the maidens. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κατ_ἆμαρ_1079"> here= <quote xml:lang="grc">κατ’ ἦμαρ...τὸ νῦν</quote> ( <ref n="Soph. Aj. 753"> <title>Ai.</title> 753</ref>), as <quote xml:lang="grc">μοῖρα καθαμερία</quote> ( <ref n="Soph. El. 1414"> <title>El.</title> 1414</ref>) = <quote xml:lang="eng"> "the doom of <emph>to-day.</emph> "</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀελλαία_1081">: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 466" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg004:466"> <title>O. T.</title> 466</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀελλάδων | ἵππων.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ταχύρρωστος_1081">goes closely with it in sense, <quote xml:lang="eng">"with a swift, strong impetus, as of the storm,"</quote> <quote xml:lang="grc">ταχέως ῥωομένη, ὡς ἄελλα</quote> : cp. <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Il. 23.367" ref="Hom. Il. 23.367"> <title>Il.</title> 23.367</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐρρώοντο μετὰ πνοιῇς ἀνέμοιο</quote> </cit> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄνωθ_1083"> for the <foreign xml:lang="grc">αὐτῶν δ’</foreign> of the MSS., with <foreign xml:lang="grc">αἰωρήσασα</foreign> for <quote xml:lang="grc"> θεωρήσασα</quote> , gives the most probable correction of the passage. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄνωθε</quote> , for <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄνωθεν</quote> , though it does not occur elsewhere in trag., is once used by <ref n="Aristoph. Eccl. 698"> Aristoph. <title>Eccl.</title> 698</ref>( <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄνωθ’ ἐξ ὑπερῴου</quote> ), and we can hardly doubt that a tragic poet would have admitted it,—at least in lyrics,—when metre required. Note these points. (1) If we read <foreign xml:lang="grc">τῶνδ’</foreign> with Wunder, the gen. <foreign xml:lang="grc">τῶνδ’ ἀγώνων</foreign> must be governed in one of two ways: ( <emph>a</emph> ) by <foreign xml:lang="grc">κύρσαιμι</foreign> , when <foreign xml:lang="grc">αἰθερίας νεφέλας</foreign> must mean, <quote xml:lang="eng"> " <emph>from</emph> a cloud."</quote>This is possible (cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 808" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg004:808"> <title>O. T.</title> 808</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὄχου...καθίκετο</quote> </cit>n.): but it is awkward. It is much more natural to take <quote xml:lang="grc">αἰθερ. νεφέλας</quote> with <quote xml:lang="grc">κύρσαιμι</quote> . ( <emph>b</emph> ) By something substituted for <quote xml:lang="grc">θεωρήσασα</quote> . Wecklein makes the gen. depend on <foreign xml:lang="grc">αἰωρήσασα</foreign> , as= <quote xml:lang="eng"> "having lifted <emph>above</emph> "</quote> <emph> :</emph>but the gen. would mean <quote xml:lang="eng"> " <emph>from</emph> ,"</quote>as <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Ant. 417" ref="Soph. Ant. 417"> <title>Ant.</title> 417</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">χθονὸς</quote> </cit> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θεωρήσασα_1083">cannot be defended by Campbell's version, <quote xml:lang="eng"> " <emph>having gone as a spectator</emph> with mine eye."</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θεωρήσουσα_1083">is read by Blaydes, who renders, <quote xml:lang="eng">"to give my eye a sight."</quote> This, as Paley says, is not Greek. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αἰωρεῖν_1083">, not <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἐωρεῖν</foreign> , is the classical Attic form: cp. on <ref n="Soph. OT 1264"> <title>O. T.</title> 1264</ref>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δαμούχοις_1087">(cp. on <ref target="cl458">458</ref> ), the people of Attica. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σθένει_1088">: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Trach. 497" ref="Soph. Trach. 497"> <title>Tr.</title> 497</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">μέγα τι σθένος ἁ Κύπρις ἐκφέρεται νίκας ἀεί.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

data.quote:
μέγα τι σθένος ἁ Κύπρις ἐκφέρεται
									νίκας ἀεί.

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐπινικείῳ_1088"> for <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπινικίῳ</quote> : cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Ant. 358" ref="Soph. Ant. 358"> <title>Ant.</title> 358</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑπαίθρεια</quote> </cit>(Boeckh, for <quote xml:lang="grc">αἴθρια</quote> ).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸν_λόχον_1088">(grant to the Athenians) to accomplish the successful surprise,— the way-laying of Creon's guards, by which the Athenians will secure their quarry ( <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄγρα</quote> ), viz. the maidens.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸν_εὔαγρον_1088">, proleptic: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Trach. 477" ref="Soph. Trach. 477"> <title>Tr.</title> 477</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="trimeter"> <l>τῆσδ’ οὕνεχ’ ἡ πολύφθορος</l> <l>καθῃρέθη πατρῷος Οἰχαλία δόρει.</l> </lg> </quote> </cit> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λόχον_1088">, <quote xml:lang="eng">"ambuscade,"</quote> seems here to have the more general sense, <quote xml:lang="eng">"scheme of capture"</quote> (cp. <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Od. 4.395" ref="Hom. Od. 4.395"> <title>Od.</title> 4.395</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">φράζευ σὺ λόχον θείοιο γέροντος</quote> </cit>, a <emph>way to take</emph> him): though there is nothing in the scanty references to the pursuit which necessarily excludes the idea of a literal ambush. Taking <foreign xml:lang="grc">λόχον</foreign> as= <quote xml:lang="eng"> "company,"</quote> we could render, <quote xml:lang="eng"> "grant this to our folk,—that thou shouldst <emph>crown</emph> the successful band <emph>with victory</emph> "</quote>( <quote xml:lang="grc">τελειῶσαι</quote> ): cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 1508" ref="Soph. El. 1508"> <title>El.</title> 1508</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὦ σπέρμ’ Ἀτρέως … | τῇ νῦν ὁρμῇ τελεωθέν</quote> </cit>, <quote xml:lang="eng">"crowned with peace by this day's effort"</quote> : but the construction thus supposed is less simple, while the frequent poetical association of <quote xml:lang="grc">λόχος</quote> with capture points to the other sense. </gloss>

data.quote:
ὦ σπέρμ’ Ἀτρέως … | τῇ νῦν ὁρμῇ
									τελεωθέν

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σεμνά_παῖς_1090"> , <foreign xml:lang="lat">sc.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc"> πόροι</quote> (from <quote xml:lang="grc">πόροις</quote> , 1086). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸν_ἀγρευτὰν_1091">, the hunter. Cp. Aesch. fr. 195 (Heracles, in the <quote xml:lang="grc">Προμηθεὺς Λυόμενος</quote> , when aiming his shaft at the eagle) <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἀγρεὺς δ’ Ἀπόλλων ὀρθὸν ἰθύνοι βέλος</quote> . Paus. (1. 41. 3) saw at Megara a temple dedicated to <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἀγροτέραν Ἄρτεμιν καὶ Ἀπόλλωνα Ἀγραῖον</quote> . Xenophon, in his treatise on hunting, bids the hunter pray <quote xml:lang="grc">τῷ Ἀπόλλωνι καὶ τῇ Ἀρτέμιδι τῇ Ἀγροτέρᾳ μεταδοῦναι τῆς θήρας</quote> ( <title>Cyneg.</title> 6. 13).—Note the change from vocative ( <quote xml:lang="grc">Ζεῦ</quote> ), and 3rd pers. ( <quote xml:lang="grc">παῖς</quote> ) with optat., to the constr. of acc. and infin. with <foreign xml:lang="grc">στέργω</foreign> . Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 204" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg004:204"> <title>O. T.</title> 204</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">Λύκεῑ ἄναξ</quote> </cit> ...209 <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸν χρυσομίτραν τε κικλήσκω</quote> : <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. PB 88" ref="Aesch. PB 88"> Aesch. <title>PV</title> 88</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὦ δῖος αἰθήρ</quote> </cit> etc.... <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ τὸν πανόπτην κύκλον ἡλίου καλῶ</quote> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὀπαδὸν_ἐλάφων_1092">, as follow ing them in the chase. Artemis <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἀγροτέρα</quote> had a temple at Athens in the suburb <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἄγραι</quote> , on an eminence by the Ilissus; and to her, as <quote xml:lang="eng">"smiter of deer,"</quote> the festival of the <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἐλαφηβόλια</quote> was held in the month thence named (Mar.—Apr.). <cit> <bibl n="HH 27" ref="HH 27"> <title>Hom. Hymn.</title> 27.2</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="hexameter"> <l>ἐλαφηβόλον, ἰοχέαιραν, …</l> <l>ἣ κατ’ ὄρη σκιόεντα καὶ ἄκριας ἠνεμοέσσας</l> <l>ἄγρῃ τερπομένη παγχρύσεα τόξα τιταίνει</l> </lg> </quote> </cit>. She is also <quote xml:lang="grc">ἑλλοφόνος</quote> , <title>Corp. Inscr.</title> 5943 ( <quote xml:lang="grc">ἑλλός</quote> , a faun), <quote xml:lang="grc">θηροκτόνος, θηροφόνος</quote> , etc. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πυκνοστίκτων_1092">: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Hipp. 215" ref="Eur. Hipp. 215"> Eur. <title>Hipp.</title> 215</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="lyric"> <l>εἶμι πρὸς ὕλαν</l> <l>καὶ παρὰ πεύκας, ἵνα θηροφόνοι</l> <l>στείβουσι κύνες,</l> <l>βαλιαῖς ἐλάφοις ἐγχριμπτομένα</l> </lg> </quote> </cit> : <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Alc. 584" ref="Eur. Alc. 584"> <title>Alc.</title> 584</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ποικιλόθριξ | νεβρός</quote> </cit> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="στέργω_1094">, <quote xml:lang="eng">"I desire."</quote> Schol. <quote xml:lang="grc">σημαίνει μὲν οἶον προσίεμαι· τελευτᾷ δὲ εἰς ἴσον τῷ προκαλοῦμαι</quote> , “the (literal) sense is nearly, <quote xml:lang="eng">"I approve"</quote> (or <quote xml:lang="eng">"consent"</quote> ); but the ultimate (or virtual) sense is, <quote xml:lang="eng">"I invoke"</quote> .” The scholiast saw the impropriety of rendering, <quote xml:lang="eng"> "I <emph>am content</emph> that the gods should come to help us,"</quote>and so imagined this transition of meaning. His only fault lay in starting from the special and derivative sense of <quote xml:lang="grc">στέργειν</quote> , <quote xml:lang="eng">"to be content,"</quote> and not from its primary sense, <quote xml:lang="eng"> "to <emph>love</emph> ,"</quote>whence poetry could easily draw the neighbouring sense, <quote xml:lang="eng"> "to <emph>desire.</emph> "</quote>So in <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 11" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg004:11"> <title>O. T.</title> 11</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">στέρξαντες</quote> </cit> = <quote xml:lang="eng">"having formed a desire."</quote> Hermann and others take <quote xml:lang="grc">στέργω</quote> here as= <quote xml:lang="eng"> "I <emph>entreat</emph> ,"</quote>—getting the idea of <quote xml:lang="eng">"praying"</quote> through that of <quote xml:lang="eng">"revering"</quote> (as implied in the <quote xml:lang="grc">στοργή</quote> of children for parents, etc.). Hermann so takes the word in the Orphic <title>Argonautica</title> 772 <quote xml:lang="grc">μειλιχίοις στέρξοι τε παραιφάμενος ἐπέεσσιν</quote> ( <quote xml:lang="eng">"entreat him"</quote> ), where Ruhnken conjectured <quote xml:lang="grc">θέλξοι</quote> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="διπλᾶς_ἀρωγὰς_1094">, two aids (abstract for concrete), Apollo and Artemis. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 164" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg004:164"> <title>O. T.</title> 164</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τρισσοὶ ἀλεξίμοροι προφάνητέ μοι</quote> </cit>(Zeus, Apollo, Artemis). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τῷ_μὲν_1096"> , <quote xml:lang="eng">"to thy watcher at least"</quote> (cp. 802 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐμοὶ μέν</quote> ). The Chorus, left alone with the blind man, has acted as his watchman.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μέν_1096"> implies, <quote xml:lang="eng"> "if my mere <emph>presage</emph> (1075) did not persuade, my eyes, at least, may be trusted."</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="προσπολουμένας_1098">has been much suspected (see cr. n.). The verb <quote xml:lang="grc"> προσπολεῖν</quote> elsewhere occurs only in the act. as=to be a <quote xml:lang="grc">πρόσπολος</quote> (with dat., Eur.). So <quote xml:lang="grc">δορυφορεῖν</quote> =to be a body-guard, <quote xml:lang="grc">ῥαβδουχεῖν</quote> to be a lictor. And if the passives <quote xml:lang="grc">δορυφορεῖσθαι</quote> (Plat., etc.) and <quote xml:lang="grc">ῥαβδουχεῖσθαι</quote> ( <ref n="Plut. Num. 10"> Plut. <title>Num.</title> 10</ref>) can mean to be escorted by <quote xml:lang="grc">δορυφόροι</quote> or <quote xml:lang="grc">ῥαβδοῦχοι</quote> , it is not plain why the pass. <quote xml:lang="grc">προσπολεῖσθαι</quote> should not mean to be escorted by <quote xml:lang="grc">πρόσπολοι</quote> . The attendants are the <quote xml:lang="grc">ὀπάονες</quote> (1103) of Theseus. The version <quote xml:lang="eng"> " <emph>moving</emph> hither"</quote>(Schaefer) is wrong. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τίς_δοίη_1100"> , <quote xml:lang="eng">"who would give?"</quote> = <quote xml:lang="eng">"oh that some one would give!"</quote> <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. Ag. 1448" ref="Aesch. Ag. 1448"> Aesch. <title>Ag.</title> 1448</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="lyric"> <l>τίς ἂν …</l> <l>μόλοι φέρουσ’ ἐν ἡμῖν</l> <l>μοῖρ’ ἀτέλευτον ὕπνον</l> </lg> </quote> </cit>. So more often <quote xml:lang="grc">πῶς ἄν.</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δοίη_1100">, by a sudden gift of sight to the blind eyes. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φιλτάτων_τ_1103">. The omission of <foreign xml:lang="grc">τ’</foreign> by the first hand in L was clearly a mere slip. From 1117, and from the words of Theseus himself (1148), it is manifest that he is supposed to have aided personally in the rescue. Cp. on <ref target="cl1054">1054</ref> ff. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μηδαμά_1104">, <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδαμά</quote> are used by the poets when the final must be short; <quote xml:lang="grc">μηδαμῇ, οὐδαμῇ</quote> , when it must be long. Where, as here, either form is possible, L is not a safe guide in choosing between them. The <quote xml:lang="grc">μη</quote> -adverb occurs 5 times in Soph.: here L has <quote xml:lang="grc">μηδαμᾶ</quote> : in <ref n="Soph. Phil. 789"> <title>Ph.</title> 789</ref>(a like case) <quote xml:lang="grc"> μηδαμῆι</quote> . Above, 517, where <quote xml:lang="grc">μηδαμά</quote> is necessary, L has <quote xml:lang="grc">μηδαμᾶ</quote> : in 1698 (a like case), <quote xml:lang="grc">μηδαμῆι</quote> . The <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ</quote> -adv. occurs 4 times in Soph., and L has always <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδαμᾶι</quote> , which is necessary only in <ref n="Soph. Ant. 874"> <title>Ant.</title> 874</ref>, while <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδαμά</quote> is necessary <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 830: either could stand <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 763, <ref n="Soph. Trach. 323"> <title>Tr.</title> 323</ref>. Thus L's perispomenon form has displaced a <emph>necessary</emph> <quote xml:lang="grc">‐ά</quote> in 3 places, while only one place of all 9 requires the long form.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἃ_τεύξει_1106">need not be explained as an attraction for <quote xml:lang="grc">ὧν τεύξει</quote> , since the neut. plur. acc. of pronouns and adjectives can stand after <quote xml:lang="grc">τυγχάνειν</quote> and <quote xml:lang="grc">κυρεῖν</quote> , rather as a cognate or adverbial acc. than as directly governed by the verb: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. Lib. 711" ref="Aesch. Lib. 711"> Aesch. <title>Cho.</title> 711</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τυγχάνειν τὰ πρός‐</quote> </cit> <quote xml:lang="grc">φορα</quote>, and see on <ref n="Soph. OT 1298"> <title>O. T.</title> 1298</ref>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σὺν_χάρις_1106">, the grace shown (by granting thy wish) is combined with a desire (on our own part). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔρνη_1108">, like <quote xml:lang="grc">θάλος</quote> (which, however, was used only in nom. and acc. sing.).—</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τῷ_τεκόντι_1108"> as <ref n="Aesch. Lib. 690"> Aesch. <title>Cho.</title> 690</ref>: so the allusive <emph>plur.</emph> , <ref n="Soph. OT 1176"> <title>O. T.</title> 1176</ref>.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πᾶν_1108">, <foreign xml:lang="lat">sc.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">τεχθέν</quote> . </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σκῆπτρα_1109">: see on <ref target="cl848">848</ref> .</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φωτός_1109">: cp. 1018. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θανὼν_1111">can mean only, <quote xml:lang="eng">"having died,"</quote> — <quote xml:lang="eng">"after my death"</quote> : but the reading, which has been suspected, seems sound. The sense is:— <quote xml:lang="eng">"were I to die now, I could not after my death be said to have been altogether unhappy, when my last hours had been thus cheered."</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐρείσατε_ἀμφιδέξιον_1112"> , <quote xml:lang="eng">"press each her side (to mine) on right and left"</quote> —Antigone on his one hand, Ismene on the other. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 1243" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg004:1243"> <title>O. T.</title> 1243</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀμφιδεξίοις ἀκμαῖς</quote> </cit>, with the fingers of both hands, where see n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐμφύντε_1113">, clinging close, like the Homeric <cit> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν δ’ ἄρα οἱ φῦ χειρί</quote> <bibl n="Hom. Il. 6.253" ref="Hom. Il. 6.253"> ( <title>Il.</title> 6.253)</bibl> </cit> , <cit> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔφυν ἐν χερσὶν ἕκαστος</quote> <bibl n="Hom. Od. 10.397" ref="Hom. Od. 10.397"> <title>Od.</title> 10.397</bibl> </cit> , <emph>clasped</emph> my hands, each and all. For the paronomasia with <foreign xml:lang="grc">φύσαντι</foreign> cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 878" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg004:878"> <title>O. T.</title> 878</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">χρησίμῳ χρῆται</quote> </cit>n.: for the masc. ending, see on <ref target="cl1676">1676</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἰδόντε</quote> . </gloss>

data.quote: ἐν δ’ ἄρα οἱ φῦ χειρί

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πλάνου_1113">, then, must mean, "wanderer's doom," for we cannot explain it merely of restless movements on the scene since his daughter's departure. But this seems forced. Wecklein explains it <emph>figuratively</emph> , of the insecurity felt by a blind man who has no guide ( <quote xml:lang="deu">"Haltlosigkeit und Unsicherheit, wie sie der Blinde ohne Führer fühlt"</quote> ). But how could <foreign xml:lang="grc">πλάνου</foreign> <emph>alone</emph> denote this mental state? Neither <quote xml:lang="grc"> <emph>τὸν</emph> πρόσθ’ ἔρη <emph>μον</emph> τοῦδε δυστήνου πλάνου</quote> nor <quote xml:lang="grc"> τοῦ πρ. ἐρήμου <emph>τόν</emph> δε δύστη <emph>νον</emph> πλάνου</quote>mends matters. Schneidewin (rightly, I think) referred <foreign xml:lang="grc">πλάνου</foreign> to the carrying away of the maidens by Creon's guards, rendering, " <emph>repose from</emph> your late forlorn and hapless wandering." But <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀναπαύσατον</quote> could not thus stand for the midd.: when the act. seems to do so, there is an acc. to be mentally supplied, as <cit> <bibl n="Thuc. 4.11" ref="Thuc. 4.11">Thuc. 4.11</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀναπαύοντες ἐν τῷ μέρει</quote> </cit>, (not "resting," but) "relieving (their comrades) in turn": <cit> <bibl n="Xen. Hell. 5.1.21" ref="Xen. Hell. 5.1.21"> Xen. <title>Hellen.</title> 5.1.21</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπειδὴ δὲ ἀπεῖχε πέντε ἢ ἓξ στάδια τοῦ λιμένος ἡσυχίαν εἶχε καὶ ἀνέπαυε</quote> </cit> ( <foreign xml:lang="lat">sc.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰς ναῦς</quote> ). I would read <foreign xml:lang="grc">κἀναπνεύσατον</foreign> : for the gen. cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 274" ref="Soph. Aj. 274"> <title>Ai.</title> 274</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔληξε κἀνέπνευσε τῆς νόσου</quote> </cit> : <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Il. 11.382" ref="Hom. Il. 11.382"> <title>Il.</title> 11.382</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνέπνευσαν κακότητος</quote> </cit> : <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Il. 15.235" ref="Hom. Il. 15.235">15. 235</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀναπνεύσωσι πόνοιο</quote> </cit>. At such a moment it is surely natural that the father should have a word of sympathy for the late terror and distress of his helpless daughters, instead of dwelling solely on the pain to <emph>himself</emph> of being left without their support. The <foreign xml:lang="grc">ε</foreign> in L is a trace of the truth. </gloss>

data.quote:
ἐπειδὴ δὲ ἀπεῖχε πέντε ἢ ἓξ στάδια τοῦ
									λιμένος ἡσυχίαν εἶχε καὶ ἀνέπαυε

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ταῖς_τηλικαῖσδε_1116"> : <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> it is not fitting for young maidens to make long speeches in such a presence. The epithet need not be pressed as implying extreme youthfulness (cp. 751). We seem to hear a covert criticism on some drama in which this maxim had been neglected. (A similar allusion has been supposed in <ref n="Soph. El. 1289"> <title>El.</title> 1289</ref>ff., but is doubtful: see n. there.) So Eur., in <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Phil. 751" ref="Soph. Phil. 751"> <title>Ph.</title> 751,</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὄνομα δ’ ἑκάστου διατριβὴ πολλὴ λέγειν</quote> </cit>, glances at <ref n="Aesch. Seven 375"> Aesch. <title>Theb.</title> 375</ref>—652, and in fr. 165 at Soph. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 563"> <title>Ant.</title> 563</ref>f.—Cp. 1148. </gloss>

data.quote:
ὄνομα δ’ ἑκάστου διατριβὴ πολλὴ
									λέγειν

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὅδ_τοῦδε_1117">: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 981" ref="Soph. El. 981"> <title>El.</title> 981</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="trimeter"> <l>τούτω φιλεῖν χρή, τώδε χρὴ πάντας σέβειν·</l> <l>τώδ’ ἔν θ’ ἑορταῖς</l> </lg> </quote> </cit> etc.: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Ant. 384" ref="Soph. Ant. 384"> <title>Ant.</title> 384</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἥδ’ ἔστ’ ἐκείνη … τήνδ’ εἵλομεν</quote> </cit>etc. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πρὸς_λ_1119"> = <quote xml:lang="grc">λιπαρῶς</quote> , as <quote xml:lang="grc">πρὸς βίαν</quote> = <quote xml:lang="grc">βιαίως, πρὸς ἡδονήν</quote> = <quote xml:lang="grc">ἡδέως</quote> : <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 38" ref="Soph. Aj. 38"> <title>Ai.</title> 38</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">πρὸς καιρόν</quote> </cit> = <quote xml:lang="grc">καιρίως</quote> : <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 464" ref="Soph. El. 464"> <title>El.</title> 464</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">πρὸς εὐσέβειαν</quote> </cit> ( <quote xml:lang="grc">λέγει</quote> )= <quote xml:lang="grc"> εὐσεβῶς</quote> . It is possible to join <foreign xml:lang="grc">πρὸς τὸ λ.</foreign> with <foreign xml:lang="grc">θαύμαζε</foreign> , as Schneidewin and others do, comparing <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Trach. 1211" ref="Soph. Trach. 1211"> <title>Tr.</title> 1211</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">φοβεῖ πρὸς τοῦτο</quote> </cit>: but such a constr. for <quote xml:lang="grc">θαυμάζειν</quote> is without example. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τέκνα_1120">, acc. governed by <foreign xml:lang="grc">μηκύνω λόγον</foreign> as= <quote xml:lang="grc">διὰ μακρῶν προσηγορῶ</quote> : see on <ref target="cl223">223</ref> : cp. 583, 1150. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄελπτα_1120">, adv.: cp. 319. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὴν_τάσδε_1121">, having reference to them, <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> caused by their return. Cp. <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰς</quote> in <quote xml:lang="grc">τό γ’ εἰς ἑαυτόν</quote> ( <ref n="Soph. OT 706"> <title>O. T.</title> 706</ref>n.), <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Orest. 542" ref="Eur. Orest. 542"> Eur. <title>Or.</title> 542</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ηὐτύχησεν ἐς τέκνα</quote> </cit> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μηδενὸς_1122">, instead of <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδενός</quote> , gives the emphasis of strong assurance: cp. on <ref target="cl797">797</ref> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὡς_1124">instead of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἃ</quote> or <quote xml:lang="grc">οἷα</quote> : cp. the phrase <quote xml:lang="grc">διδόναι εὖ</quote> (642). Schneidewin cp. <cit> <bibl n="HH 5.136" ref="HH 5.136"> <title>Hom. Hymn.</title> 5.136</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">δοῖεν...τέκνα τεκέσθαι | ὡς ἐθέλουσι τοκῆες</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Ant. 706" ref="Soph. Ant. 706"> <title>Ant.</title> 706</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς φὴς σύ, κοὐδὲν ἄλλο, τοῦτ’ ὀρθῶς ἔχειν</quote> </cit>. </gloss>

data.quote:
ὡς φὴς σύ, κοὐδὲν ἄλλο, τοῦτ’ ὀρθῶς
									ἔχειν

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αὐτῷ_τε_1125"> <quote xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</quote>: see 462 n., and cp. 308. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τό_εὐσεβές_1125">: see on <ref target="cl260">260</ref> .</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μόνοις_1125">: on 261. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοὐπιεικὲς_1127">: an equitable and humane disposition. <cit> <bibl n="Aristot. Nic. Eth. 1137a" ref="Aristot. Nic. Eth. 1137a"> Arist. <title>Eth. Nic.</title> 5.10</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ ἐπιεικὲς δίκαιον μέν ἐστιν, οὐ τὸ κατὰ νόμον δέ, ἀλλ’ ἐπανόρθωμα νομίμου δικαίου</quote> </cit> . <cit> <bibl n="Hdt. 3.53" ref="Hdt. 3.53">Her. 3.53</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τῶν δικαίων τὰ ἐπιεικέστερα προτιθεῖσι</quote> </cit>, <quote xml:lang="eng">"prefer the more equitable course to the letter of their right."</quote> Soph. fr. 699 <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="trimeter"> <l>ὃς οὔτε τοὐπιεικὲς οὔτε τὴν χάριν</l> <l>οἶδεν, μόνην δ’ ἔστερξε τὴν ἁπλῶς δίκην</l> </lg> </quote>(speaking of Hades). Cp. <ref n="Isoc. 15.300">Isocr. or. 15 § 300</ref> ; Athens might be called the <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄστυ τῆς Ἑλλαδος</quote> both for other reasons <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ μάλιστα διὰ τὸν τρόπον τῶν ἐνοικούντων</quote> (the Athenian character) <quote xml:lang="grc">· οὐδένας γὰρ εἶναι πραοτέρους οὐδὲ κοινο‐</quote> <quote xml:lang="grc">τέρους οὐδ’ οἷς οἰκειότερον ἄν τις τὸν ἅπαντα βίον συνδιατρίψειεν</quote> : <quote xml:lang="eng">"no people are gentler, or of larger sympathies, or more kindly associates throughout life."</quote> </gloss>

data.quote:
τὸ ἐπιεικὲς δίκαιον μέν ἐστιν, οὐ τὸ
									κατὰ νόμον δέ, ἀλλ’ ἐπανόρθωμα νομίμου δικαίου

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἰδὼς_ἀμύνω_1128"> <quote xml:lang="grc">κ.τ.λ.</quote>, <quote xml:lang="eng"> "and I have experienced these qualities which I <emph>requite</emph> (acknowledge) with these words"</quote>: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Phil. 602" ref="Soph. Phil. 602"> <title>Ph.</title> 602</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <note anchored="true" place="inline" resp="RCJ" xml:lang="eng">(the gods)</note>ἔργ’ ἀμύνουσιν κακά</quote> </cit>, requite evil deeds. The stress is on <foreign xml:lang="grc">εἰδώς</foreign> , which is interpreted by the next v., <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἔχω γάρ</foreign> etc. Better thus than, <quote xml:lang="eng"> "and I am conscious that I requite these merits ( <emph>merely</emph> ) with these (feeble) words."</quote>For that sense we should need something like <quote xml:lang="grc">φαύλοις δ’ ἀμύνων οἶδα τοῖς λόγοις τάδε</quote> .— Others render: <quote xml:lang="eng">"And as one who has had experience I thus support these sayings (about Athens),"</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τάδε_1128">being an adverbial cogn. acc., as <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 264" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg004:264"> <title>O. T.</title> 264</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τάδ’ ὡσπερεὶ τοὐμοῦ πατρὸς | ὑπερμαχοῦμαι</quote> </cit>. But <foreign xml:lang="grc">τοῖσδε τοῖς λόγοις</foreign> would then refer to what others say of Athens, whereas it plainly refers to what he himself has just said. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ψαύσω_1131">, <foreign xml:lang="lat">sc.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐτῆς.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἰ_θέμις_1131"> , <quote xml:lang="eng">"if it is lawful,"</quote> —a reverential or courteous formula usu. employed when the speaker believes that the act <emph>is</emph> lawful, as fr. 856. 14 <quote xml:lang="grc">εἴ μοι θέμις, θέμις δὲ τἀληθῆ λέγειν, | Διὸς τυραννεῖ πλευμόνων</quote> ,—if it is lawful to say so,—and it <emph>is</emph> lawful to say the truth,—she (Aphrodite) sways the heart of Zeus: so <ref n="Soph. Trach. 809"> <title>Tr.</title> 809</ref>f., etc. Here, however, the impulse of Oed. is abruptly checked by the thought that he is defiled:— <quote xml:lang="grc"> <emph>καίτοι</emph> τί φωνῶ</quote>; <quote xml:lang="eng">"but what am I saying?"</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πῶς_σ_1132">. Hermann's change of <foreign xml:lang="grc">δ’</foreign> to <foreign xml:lang="grc">σ’</foreign> is necessary, since otherwise the sense would be, <quote xml:lang="eng"> "and how could I wish to touch <emph>a man</emph> ,—I who,"</quote>etc.; when <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἀνδρὸς</foreign> would be unendurably weak. But the words <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἄθλιος γεγὼς</foreign> are clearly sound. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄθλιος</quote> being a euphemism like <quote xml:lang="grc">συμφορά</quote> said of a defilement or crime ( <ref n="Soph. OT 99"> <title>O. T.</title> 99</ref>). There is no justification for the bold change <quote xml:lang="grc">πῶς ἂν ἁγνὸν ὄντα σε</quote> (Dind.), or the still bolder <quote xml:lang="grc">πῶς δ’ ἂν ἔρνος Αἰγέως</quote> (Mekler). Cp. the words of Heracles (when stained with blood-guilt) to Theseus, Eur. <title>H. F.</title> 1233 <quote xml:lang="grc">φεῦγ’, ὦ ταλαίπωῤ, ἀνόσιον μίασμ’ ἐμόν</quote> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τίς_οὐκ_1132">= <quote xml:lang="grc">πᾶσα</quote> : cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 1526" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg004:1526"> <title>O. T.</title> 1526</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὖ τίς οὐ ζήλῳ πολιτῶν ταῖς τύχαις ἐπέβλεπεν</quote> </cit>; (n.): fr. 871 <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅπου τίς ὄρνις οὐχὶ κλαγγάνει;</quote> </gloss>

data.quote:
οὖ τίς οὐ ζήλῳ πολιτῶν ταῖς τύχαις
									ἐπέβλεπεν

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κηλὶς_κακῶν_1132"> , <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 833" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg004:833"> <title>O. T.</title> 833</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">κηλῖδ’ἐμαυτῷ συμφορᾶς ἀφιγμένην.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ξύνοικος_1132"> : <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Phileb. 63D" ref="Plat. Phileb. 63D"> Plat. <title>Phileb.</title> 63D</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἆρ’ ἔτι προσδεῖσθ’ ὑμῖν τὰς μεγίστας ἡδονὰς ξυνοίκους εἶναι</quote> </cit>...; cp. on <ref n="Soph. OT 337"> <title>O. T.</title> 337</ref>. </gloss>

data.quote:
ἆρ’ ἔτι προσδεῖσθ’ ὑμῖν τὰς μεγίστας
									ἡδονὰς ξυνοίκους εἶναι

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐκ_σε_1132"> , <foreign xml:lang="lat">sc.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">θέλω θιγεῖν</quote> :</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐδ_οὖν_1132">, nor <emph>indeed</emph> will I allow it ( <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ καὶ σὺ θέλεις</quote> ).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="βροτῶν_1135">is changed by Nauck to <quote xml:lang="grc">κακῶν</quote> , and by Dindorf to <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐμῶν</quote> ( <quote xml:lang="eng">"my affairs"</quote> ), on the ground that <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἐμπείροις</foreign> needs definition. But if the preceding words leave any need for such definition, it is supplied in the next v. by <foreign xml:lang="grc">συνταλαιπωρεῖν τάδε</foreign> . Only those who, like his daughters, are already involved in the family sorrows can show him the offices of affection without fear of a new stain from the contact. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αὐτόθεν_1137">, <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> , <quote xml:lang="eng">"from where thou now art,"</quote> —without drawing near to receive an embrace. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Il. 19.76" ref="Hom. Il. 19.76"> <title>Il.</title> 19.76</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="hexameter"> <l>τοῖσι δὲ καὶ μετέειπεν ἄναξ ἀνδρῶν Ἀγαμέμνων</l> <l>αὐτόθεν ἐξ ἕδρης, οὐδ’ ἐν μέσσοισιν ἀναστάς</l> </lg> </quote> </cit>,—from where he sat, without rising. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐς_ἡμέρας_1138">: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 14" ref="Soph. El. 14"> <title>El.</title> 14</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τοσόνδ’ ἐς ἥβης</quote> </cit> : <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 961 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐς τοσόνδε τοῦ χρόνου</quote> (to this time of thy life). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὔτ_κτλ_1139">: lit., <quote xml:lang="eng"> "if you have used somewhat great ( <foreign xml:lang="grc">πλέον</foreign> ) length of speech"</quote>: <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἔθου</foreign> = <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐποιήσω</quote> . Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Thuc. 5.89" ref="Thuc. 5.89">Thuc. 5.89</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὔτε μετ’ ὀνομάτων καλῶν...μῆκος λόγων ἄπιστον παρέξομεν</quote> </cit>. <foreign xml:lang="grc">τι</foreign> (adv., <ref n="Soph. OT 969"> <title>O.T.</title> 969</ref>) courteously softens the phrase. </gloss>

data.quote:
οὔτε μετ’ ὀνομάτων καλῶν...μῆκος λόγων
									ἄπιστον παρέξομεν

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θαυμάσας_ἔχω_1139"> = <quote xml:lang="grc">τεθαύμακα</quote> : cp. 817: <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Phaedrus 257c" ref="Plat. Phaedrus 257c"> Plat. <title>Phaedr.</title> 257 C</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸν λόγον δέ σου πάλαι θαυμάσας ἔχω</quote> </cit>, and <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Phaedrus 258b" ref="Plat. Phaedrus 258b"> <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 258 B</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐχ ὡς ὑπερφρονοῦντες,...ἀλλ’ ὡς τεθαυμακότες</quote> </cit>. For the perfect, see on <ref target="cl186">186</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc"> τέτροφεν</quote> . </gloss>

data.quote:
τὸν λόγον δέ σου πάλαι θαυμάσας
									ἔχω

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὔτ_1141">: see cr. n. and cp. on <ref target="cl450">450</ref> . <quote xml:lang="grc"> <emph>πρὸ τοὐμοῦ προὔλαβες</emph> κ.τ.λ.</quote>, received their words first, in preference to speech with me. We need not supply <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔπους</quote> with <quote xml:lang="grc">τοὐμοῦ</quote> , which= <quote xml:lang="eng">"my part,"</quote> <quote xml:lang="eng">"what I had to say"</quote> ; cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Trach. 1068" ref="Soph. Trach. 1068"> <title>Tr.</title> 1068</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ τοὐμὸν ἀλγεῖς μᾶλλον</quote> </cit>. The verb <quote xml:lang="grc">προλαμβάνειν</quote> nowhere = <quote xml:lang="grc">προαιρεῖσθαί τί τινος</quote> , to <emph>prefer</emph> one thing to another. It is <foreign xml:lang="grc">πρὸ τοὐμοῦ</foreign> which here suggests preference, while <foreign xml:lang="grc">προὔλαβες</foreign> merely expresses priority in time. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γὰρ_1142">= <quote xml:lang="eng">"indeed,"</quote> conveying an assurance. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δείκνυμι_δ_1145">: cp. on <ref target="cl146">146</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">δηλῶ δ’</quote> . </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὤμοσα_1145">: 1040. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ᾑρέθη_1148">: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Hdt. 9.35" ref="Hdt. 9.35">Her. 9.35</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὕτω δὴ πέντε σφι...ἀγῶνας τοὺς μεγίστους... συγκαταιρέει</quote> </cit>, helps them to <emph>conquer in</emph> five of the most important contests. Nauck formerly conjectured <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἅγων</foreign> = <quote xml:lang="eng">"the captor"</quote> (Creon).—Cp. on <ref target="cl1116">1116</ref> . </gloss>

data.quote:
οὕτω δὴ πέντε σφι...ἀγῶνας τοὺς
									μεγίστους... συγκαταιρέει

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λόγος_1150">, by inverse attraction, instead of an acc. <quote xml:lang="grc">λόγον</quote> governed by <foreign xml:lang="grc">συμβαλοῦ γνώμην</foreign> as= <quote xml:lang="grc">συνδιάσκεψαι</quote> (cp. on <ref target="cl223">223</ref> ). Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Phoen. 940" ref="Eur. Phoen. 940"> Eur. <title>Phoen.</title> 940</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="trimeter"> <l>ἐκ γένους δὲ δεῖ θανεῖν</l> <l>τοῦδ’, ὃς δράκοντος γένυος ἐκπέφυκε παῖς</l> </lg> </quote> </cit>. When the antecedent is thus drawn into the case of the relat., the case is more often the acc.: see on <ref target="cl56">56</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τόπον.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λόγος_1150_2">here=a subject for consideration (cp. our <quote xml:lang="eng">"argument"</quote> in the old sense of <quote xml:lang="eng">"theme"</quote> ).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐμπέπτωκεν_1150">, has presented itself to me: so <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Prot. 314C" ref="Plat. Prot. 314C"> Plat. <title>Prot.</title> 314C</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">περί τινος λόγου διελεγόμεθα ὃς ἡμῖν κατὰ τὴν ὁδὸν ἐνέπεσεν</quote> </cit> . </gloss>

data.quote:
περί τινος λόγου διελεγόμεθα ὃς ἡμῖν
									κατὰ τὴν ὁδὸν ἐνέπεσεν

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="συμβαλοῦ_γνώμην_1150">, not "collect your thoughts" (Blaydes), but " <emph>contribute your opinion</emph> ," <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> help me to decide what should be done. <cit> <bibl n="Hdt. 8.61" ref="Hdt. 8.61">Her. 8.61</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <note anchored="true" place="inline" resp="RCJ" xml:lang="eng">(adeimantus in the council of greek leaders)</note> πόλιν … τὸν Θεμιστοκλέα παρεχόμενον οὕτω ἐκέλευε γνώμας συμβάλλεσθαι</quote> </cit>, "he said that T. should have a city to represent before he contributed his views." <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Stat. 298C" ref="Plat. Stat. 298C"> Plat. <title>Polit.</title> 298C</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <note anchored="true" place="inline" resp="RCJ" xml:lang="eng">(if we should decide)</note>ξυλλέξαι...ἐκκλησίαν..., ἐξεῖναι δὲ καὶ τῶν ἰδιωτῶν καὶ τῶν ἄλλων δημιουργῶν περί τε πλοῦ καὶ περὶ νόσων γνώμην ξυμβαλέσθαι</quote> </cit>.</gloss>

data.quote:
πόλιν … τὸν Θεμιστοκλέα παρεχόμενον οὕτω
							ἐκέλευε γνώμας συμβάλλεσθαι

ξυλλέξαι...ἐκκλησίαν..., ἐξεῖναι δὲ καὶ τῶν
							ἰδιωτῶν καὶ τῶν ἄλλων δημιουργῶν περί τε πλοῦ καὶ περὶ νόσων γνώμην
							ξυμβαλέσθαι

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἰπεῖν_θαυμάσαι_1152">: for the inf. act., cp. on <ref target="cl37">37</ref> , <ref target="cl461">461</ref> . So <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 777" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg004:777"> <title>O. T.</title> 777</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="trimeter"> <l>( τύχη ) θαυμάσαι μὲν ἀξία,</l> <l>σπουδῆς γε μέντοι τῆς ἐμῆς οὐκ ἀξία</l> </lg> </quote> </cit> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄνθρωπον_1153">, emphatic (as <ref n="Soph. OT 977"> <title>O. T.</title> 977</ref>, cp. <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 1528 <quote xml:lang="grc">θνητὸν ὄντ’</quote> ). A mortal cannot read the future, and therefore can never be sure that an incident, seemingly trivial, will not prove momentous. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τί_ἔστι_1154">; cp. 311. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δίδασκέ_εἰδότ_1154">. The <foreign xml:lang="grc">μή</foreign> is due to the imperative: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Phil. 253" ref="Soph. Phil. 253"> <title>Ph.</title> 253</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς μηδὲν εἰδότ’ ἴσθι μ’ ὧν ἀνιστορεῖς</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Phil. 415" ref="Soph. Phil. 415"> <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 415</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς μηκέτ’ ὄντα κεῖνον ἐν φάει νόει</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Rep. 327C" ref="Plat. Rep. 327C"> Plat. <title>Rep.</title> 327C</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς τοίνυν μὴ ἀκουσομένων οὕτω διανοεῖσθε.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

data.quote:
ὡς μηδὲν εἰδότ’ ἴσθι μ’ ὧν
									ἀνιστορεῖς

ὡς μηκέτ’ ὄντα κεῖνον ἐν φάει
									νόει

ὡς τοίνυν μὴ ἀκουσομένων οὕτω
									διανοεῖσθε.

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὡς_οὐ_1154">, instead of <foreign xml:lang="grc">ὡς μή</foreign> , sometimes stands, however, with the partic. (esp. in gen. or acc. absol.), although the verb is imperative: <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Med. 1311" ref="Eur. Med. 1311"> Eur. <title>Med.</title> 1311</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς οὐκέτ’ ὄντων σῶν τέκνων, φρόντιζε δή</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Lys. 27.16" ref="Lys. 27.16">Lys. or. 27 § 16</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">μὴ...ἀζημίους ἀφίετε,...ὥσπερ τοῦ ὀνείδους ἀλλ’ οὐ τῆς ζημίας αὐτοῖς μέλον</quote> </cit>. And, when the verb is <emph>not</emph> imperative, <foreign xml:lang="grc">ὡς οὐ</foreign> in such cases is normal, as <cit> <bibl n="Xen. Mem. 2.3.3" ref="Xen. Mem. 2.3.3"> Xen. <title>Mem.</title> 2.3.3</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τῶν δ’ ἀδελφῶν ἀμελοῦσιν, ὥσπερ ἐκ πολιτῶν μὲν γιγνομένους φίλους, ἐξ ἀδελφῶν δὲ οὐ γιγνομένους</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Thuc. 4.5" ref="Thuc. 4.5">Thuc. 4.5</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν ὀλιγωρίᾳ ἐποιοῦντο, ὡς...οὐχ ὑπομενοῦντας</quote> </cit>: 6. 24 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔρως ἐνέπεσε τοῖς πᾶσιν...ἐκπλεῦσαι,...ὡς...οὐδὲν ἂν σφαλεῖσαν μεγάλην δύναμιν</quote> . This is against referring <foreign xml:lang="grc">μὴ εἰδότ’</foreign> here to a cause independent of the imperative, viz. to the mental conception implied by</gloss>

data.quote:
ὡς οὐκέτ’ ὄντων σῶν τέκνων, φρόντιζε
									δή

μὴ...ἀζημίους ἀφίετε,...ὥσπερ τοῦ
									ὀνείδους ἀλλ’ οὐ τῆς ζημίας αὐτοῖς μέλον

τῶν δ’ ἀδελφῶν ἀμελοῦσιν, ὥσπερ ἐκ
									πολιτῶν μὲν γιγνομένους φίλους, ἐξ ἀδελφῶν δὲ οὐ
									γιγνομένους

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὡς_1154">: for though ( <foreign xml:lang="lat">e.g.</foreign> ) <quote xml:lang="grc"> ἐδίδαξας ὡς <emph>μὴ</emph> ·εἰδότα</quote>could mean, <quote xml:lang="eng">"you instructed me on the supposition that I knew not,"</quote> usage indicates that <quote xml:lang="grc"> ὡς <emph>οὐκ</emph> εἰδότα</quote>would then have been preferred. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἡμῖν_1156">, ethic dat. (81). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔμπολιν_1156">: cp. 637. As Theseus was returning from the rescue, word had been brought him that a stranger had seated himself as a suppliant on the steps of the altar of Poseidon at Colonus (see on <ref target="cl55">55</ref> ). This man said merely that he was a kinsman of Oedipus; and that he wished to speak a few words to him (1162). The fact that he was not from Thebes, but from Argos (1167), seems to have been inferred from something in his dress, for Theseus says that he does not <emph>know</emph> whence the man had come (cp. 1161). Polyneices took this precaution of becoming a <quote xml:lang="grc">ἱκέτης</quote> because he did not know what power might now be at the command of the paternal anger which he foresaw (cp. 1165). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="προσπεσόντα_πως_1156">: lit., <quote xml:lang="eng">"having somehow rushed to"</quote> the altar: <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> he had come in the absence of those Coloniates who had hurried from the sacrifice to the rescue (899), and no one had witnessed his arrival. (Cp. 156 <quote xml:lang="grc">προπέσῃς</quote> , 915 <quote xml:lang="grc"> ἐπεισπεσων</quote> .)</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πως_1156">could not mean, <quote xml:lang="eng">"for an unknown reason."</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="βωμῷ_1158">with <quote xml:lang="grc">προσπεσόντα</quote> , rather than locative dat. with <quote xml:lang="grc">καθῆσθαι</quote> : with the latter cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 1160" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg007:1160">1160</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">θάκημα</quote> </cit> , <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 1163" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg007:1163">1163</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἕδρα</quote> </cit> ( <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 15" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg004:15"> <title>O. T.</title> 15</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">προσήμεθα</quote> </cit> , <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 20" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg004:20"> <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 20</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">θακεῖ</quote> </cit>, and <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 2 n.). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔκυρον_1158">. In <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Hipp. 746" ref="Eur. Hipp. 746"> Eur. <title>Hipp.</title> 746</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">κύρων</quote> </cit>was restored by Heath from MS. <quote xml:lang="grc">κυ^ρῶν</quote> ( <foreign xml:lang="lat">v.l.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">ναίων</quote> ): elsewhere Attic poets have only <quote xml:lang="grc">κυρέω</quote> . <ref n="Hom. Il. 23.821"> <title>Il.</title> 23.821</ref>has <quote xml:lang="grc">κῦρον</quote> : <cit> <bibl n="HH 5.189" ref="HH 5.189"> <title>Hom. Hymn.</title> 5.189</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">κῦρε</quote> </cit>: and the form was used by the Alexandrian poets. It seems unnecessary, then, to conjecture <quote xml:lang="grc">κυρῶν ἔθυον</quote> (Blaydes). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἡνίχ_ὡρμώμην_1158"> , <quote xml:lang="eng"> "when I <emph>first</emph> set out,"</quote> lit., <quote xml:lang="eng">"when I proceeded to set out"</quote> : <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> when he left the sacrifice, summoned by the cry of the Chorus, 887. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τῷ_θακήματι_1160">, instrum. dat.:</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="προς_1160">as in <quote xml:lang="grc">προσαιτεῖν</quote> (cp. on <ref target="cl122"> 122</ref> ).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σοῦ_1161">seems to be an objective gen. with <foreign xml:lang="grc">μῦθον</foreign> , a colloquy with thee (cp. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐμὰν λέσχαν</quote> , 167). We find <quote xml:lang="grc"> <emph>αἰτῶ</emph> τινα, παρά τινος, πρός τινος</quote>, etc., but never the simple gen. <quote xml:lang="grc">αἰτῶ τινος</quote> (like <quote xml:lang="grc">δέομαί τινος</quote> ). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐκ_πλέων_1162">, on a subject of no great pretensions,— <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> not so important as to demand any great exertion from the old man. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Phoen. 717" ref="Eur. Phoen. 717"> Eur. <title>Phoen.</title> 717</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔχει τιν’ ὄγκον τἄργος Ἑλλήνων πάρα</quote> </cit>. This seems better than to take <foreign xml:lang="grc">ὄγκου</foreign> here as= <quote xml:lang="eng"> " <emph>effort</emph> ,"</quote>a sense which it bears (in a different context) below, 1341 <quote xml:lang="grc">βραχεῖ σὺν ὄγκῳ</quote> ( <foreign xml:lang="lat">non magna mole</foreign> ). If we rendered, <quote xml:lang="eng"> "of no great <emph>compass</emph> "</quote>( <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> length), <foreign xml:lang="grc">οὐκ ὄγκου πλέων</foreign> would merely repeat <foreign xml:lang="grc">βραχύν</foreign> . </gloss>

data.quote:
ἔχει τιν’ ὄγκον τἄργος Ἑλλήνων
									πάρα

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κατ_Ἄργος_1167">. This brings the first flash of light to Oed.,—he remembers Ismene's words (378). Cp. on <ref target="cl1156">1156</ref> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοῦτο_1167">is best taken as acc. after <foreign xml:lang="grc">τυχεῖν</foreign> : cp. 1106 n., and <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 1155" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg004:1155"> <title>O. T.</title> 1155</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τί προσχρῄζων μαθεῖν</quote> </cit>; But it might, of course, be acc. after <quote xml:lang="grc"> <emph>προσχρῄζοι</emph> , τυχεῖν</quote>being epexegetic inf. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σχὲς_εἶ_1169"> , <quote xml:lang="eng">"stop where thou art,"</quote> <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="eng">"say no more"</quote> —do not go on to urge that I should receive this visitor. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Eur. IA 1467" ref="Eur. IA 1467"> Eur. <title>I. A.</title> 1467</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">σχές, μή με προλίπῃς</quote> </cit> : <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Hipp. 1354" ref="Eur. Hipp. 1354"> <title>Hipp.</title> 1354</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">σχές, ἀπειρηκὸς σῶμ’ ἀναπαύσω</quote> </cit>. This correction (Heath's) of the MS. <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἴσχες</foreign> is much better than Doederlein's <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἴσχε σ’</foreign> . While the intrans. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔχε</quote> is common as <quote xml:lang="eng">"hold!"</quote> ( <ref n="Plat. Prot. 349E"> Plat. <title> Prot.</title> 349E</ref>etc.), we never find <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔχε σε</quote> in that sense. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τίδ_σοι_1169"> ; <quote xml:lang="eng">"what is the matter with thee?"</quote> Cp. 311. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πράγματος_ποίου_1170">; The construction <quote xml:lang="grc">δέομαί σού τινος</quote> , though less freq. than <quote xml:lang="grc">δέομαί σού τι</quote> , occurs in good prose, as <cit> <bibl n="Xen. Cyrop. 8.3.19" ref="Xen. Cyrop. 8.3.19"> Xen. <title>Cyr.</title> 8.3.19</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">δεόμενοι Κύρου ἄλλος ἄλλης πράξεως</quote> </cit>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀκούων_τῶνδ_1171">, hearing these words (1167): cp. 418: for <quote xml:lang="grc">τῶνδ’</quote> referring to what precedes, 787. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὅς_1171">= <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅστις</quote> : <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 1068" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg004:1068"> <title>O. T.</title> 1068</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">μήποτε γνοίης ὃς εἶ</quote> </cit> : <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 1259" ref="Soph. Aj. 1259"> <title>Ai.</title> 1259</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">μαθὼν ὃς εἶ</quote> </cit> . <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Meno 80D" ref="Plat. Meno 80D"> Plat. <title>Meno.</title> 80D</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">περὶ ἀρετῆς, ὃ ἔστιν, ἐγὼ μὲν οὐκ οἶδα</quote> </cit>. <cit> <bibl n="Hdt. 9.71" ref="Hdt. 9.71">Her. 9.71</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">γενομένης λέσχης ὃς γένοιτο αὐτῶν ἄριστος</quote> </cit>. </gloss>

data.quote:
περὶ ἀρετῆς, ὃ ἔστιν, ἐγὼ μὲν οὐκ
									οἶδα

γενομένης λέσχης ὃς γένοιτο αὐτῶν
									ἄριστος

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="προστάτης_1171">, one who presents himself before a god as a suppliant: so 1278: schol. <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁ ἱκέτης, ὁ προσεστηκὼς τῷ βωμῷ</quote> . Elsewhere the word always= <quote xml:lang="eng">"protector"</quote> or <quote xml:lang="eng">"patron"</quote> (as <ref n="Soph. OT 303"> <title>O. T.</title> 303</ref>, <ref n="Soph. OT 411">411</ref> , <ref n="Soph. OT 882">882</ref> , <ref n="Soph. Trach. 209"> <title>Tr.</title> 209</ref>). Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 1377" ref="Soph. El. 1377"> <title>El.</title> 1377</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="trimeter"> <l>ἥ σε <note anchored="true" place="inline" resp="RCJ" xml:lang="eng"> ( <foreign xml:lang="lat">sc.</foreign> sc.<foreign xml:lang="grc">τὸν Ἀπόλλωνα</foreign>to\n *)apo/llwna</note>πολλὰ δὴ</l> <l>ἀφ’ ὧν ἔχοιμι λιπαρεῖ προὔστην χερί</l> </lg> </quote> </cit>, "have oft <emph>come before thee</emph> with offerings of my best in suppliant hand." </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὅν_ψέξαιμι_1172">, who is he, to whom I could possibly have any objection? See note in Appendix on 170; and cp. <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. PB 292" ref="Aesch. PB 292"> Aesch. <title>PV</title> 292</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐκ ἔστιν ὅτῳ | μείζονα μοῖραν νείμαιμ’ ᾓ σοί</quote> </cit>. Distinguish 561 <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁποίας ἐξαφισταίμην</quote> , which is not strictly similar (see n. there). </gloss>

data.quote:
οὐκ ἔστιν ὅτῳ | μείζονα μοῖραν
									νείμαιμ’ ᾓ σοί

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="στυγνός_1173">has greater force through its position: <quote xml:lang="eng">"my son, king—a son whom I hate"</quote> : cp. 1615 <quote xml:lang="grc">σκληράν.</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λόγων_1173">: for the gen. cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 418">418.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄλγιστα_ἀνδρῶν_1173"> ,= <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄλγιον ἢ παντὸς ἄλλου ἀνδρὸς</quote> ( <quote xml:lang="grc">λόγων</quote> ), more reluctantly than the words of any one else. The usage is similar to that by which a Greek could say, <quote xml:lang="grc">πυραμίδα ἀπελίπετο ἐλάσσω τοῦ πατρός</quote> ( <ref n="Hdt. 2.134">Her. 2.134</ref> ), instead of <quote xml:lang="grc">τῆς τ. π.</quote> , or <quote xml:lang="grc">ἣν ὁ πατήρ</quote> . Cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 467"> <title>O. T.</title> 467</ref>n. More often the words would mean, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄλγιον ἢ πᾶς ἄλλος ἀνήρ</quote> (so <quote xml:lang="grc">οἶμαι κάλλιστ’ ἀνθρώπων λέγειν</quote> , <ref n="Plat. Ion 530c"> Plat. <title>Ion</title> 530 C</ref>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἃ_μὴ_1175">: <quote xml:lang="eng"> " <emph>such</emph> things <emph>as</emph> thou dost not wish"</quote>( <foreign xml:lang="lat">quae non cupias</foreign> ): cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 1186">1186</ref> , <ref target="cl73">73 n</ref> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φθέγμα_τοῦθ_1177">(art. omitted, as 629), <quote xml:lang="eng">"that voice"</quote> —his son's. The blind man could not express loathing more vividly: cp. 863.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἥκει_1177">, has come to be: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 1519" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg004:1519"> <title>O. T.</title> 1519</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">θεοῖς γ’ ἔχθιστος ἥκω</quote> </cit>. (Not, <quote xml:lang="eng">"has come hither."</quote> )</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μή_προσβάλῃς_1178"> , <quote xml:lang="eng">"do not force me to the necessity"</quote> of yielding,—the <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνάγκη</quote> being, as it were, a rock on which his course is driven: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. Eum. 564" ref="Aesch. Eum. 564"> Aesch. <title>Eum.</title> 564</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸν πρὶν ὄλβον | ἕρματι προσβαλὼν … ὤλετ’</quote> </cit>. We cannot properly call this <quote xml:lang="eng">"an inverted expression"</quote> for <quote xml:lang="grc">μή μοι ἀνάγκην προσβάλῃς</quote> , which would suggest a wholly different image: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Trach. 255" ref="Soph. Trach. 255"> <title>Tr.</title> 255</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅρκον αὑτῷ προσβαλών</quote> </cit> : <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Trach. 41" ref="Soph. Trach. 41"> <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 41</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐμοὶ πικρὰς | ὠδῖνας … προσβαλών.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

data.quote:
τὸν πρὶν ὄλβον | ἕρματι προσβαλὼν …
									ὤλετ’

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἰκαθεῖν_1178">: cp. 862, 1015. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸ_θάκημ_1179">(1160), his suppliant <quote xml:lang="grc">ἕδρα</quote> at the altar of Poseidon, in whose name he implored the boon.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐξαναγκάζει_1179">: cp. 603. If we point at <foreign xml:lang="grc">σκόπει</foreign> , as is best, then <quote xml:lang="grc">μὴ … ᾖ</quote> is elliptical: <quote xml:lang="eng">"(beware, I say) lest."</quote> Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Gorg. 462E" ref="Plat. Gorg. 462E"> Plat. <title>Gorg.</title> 462E</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ΠΩΛ. τίνος λέγεις ταύτης</quote> </cit>; ( <quote xml:lang="eng">"what calling do you mean?"</quote> ) <quote xml:lang="grc">ΣΩ. μὴ ἀγροικότερον ᾖ τὸ ἀληθὲς εἰπεῖν</quote> , <quote xml:lang="eng">"I fear it may be scarcely courteous to say the truth."</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πρόνοια_θεοῦ_1179">, respect for the god: <cit> <bibl n="Andoc. 1.56" ref="Andoc. 1.56">Andoc. or. 1 § 56</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἶπον...ἃ ἤκουσα..., προνοίᾳ μὲν τῶν συγγενῶν καὶ τῶν φίλων, προνοίᾳ δὲ τῆς πόλεως ἁπάσης</quote> </cit>. Cp. on <ref n="Soph. OT 978"> <title>O. T.</title> 978</ref>.</gloss>

data.quote:
εἶπον...ἃ ἤκουσα..., προνοίᾳ μὲν τῶν
									συγγενῶν καὶ τῶν φίλων, προνοίᾳ δὲ τῆς πόλεως
									ἁπάσης

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φυλακτέα_1179">, must be <emph>observed</emph> , like <quote xml:lang="grc">φυλάσσειν νόμον, ὅρκια</quote> , etc. For slightly different, though kindred, uses of the verb, cp. 626, 1213. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πιθοῦ_μοι_1181"> , <quote xml:lang="eng">"comply with me,"</quote> grant this wish, as <ref n="Soph. El. 1207"> <title>El.</title> 1207</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Trach. 470"> <title>Tr.</title> 470</ref>(n.): while <quote xml:lang="grc"> πείθου</quote> is rather, <quote xml:lang="eng">"be persuaded,"</quote> as <ref n="Soph. El. 1015"> <title>El.</title> 1015</ref>, and above, 520.—</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κεἰ_1181">where <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ καί</quote> would be normal: cp. 661.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="vέα_1181">: see on <ref target="cl751">751</ref> : cp. 1116. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸν_τόνδε_1182">, Theseus (cp. 1100). <quote xml:lang="eng">"Allow him at once to gratify his own mind (his hinted desire that Polyneices should be heard, 1175), and to gratify Poseidon as he wishes to do,"</quote> <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> by granting the prayer made in Poseidon's name. The whole phrase <foreign xml:lang="grc">χάριν παρασχεῖν</foreign> belongs to both clauses; <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἃ</foreign> is acc. of respect. The subj. to <foreign xml:lang="grc">βούλεται</foreign> is Theseus, not <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁ θεός</quote> .—These two vv. mark two leading traits in the character of Theseus—his sense of justice ( <foreign xml:lang="grc">φρενί</foreign> ), and his piety ( <foreign xml:lang="grc">θεῷ</foreign> ). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὕπεικε_1184"> here= <quote xml:lang="grc">συγχώρει</quote> , <quote xml:lang="eng"> " <emph>concede to</emph> us that..."</quote>; so <quote xml:lang="grc"> παρείκειν</quote> in prose. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="παρασπάσει_1185"> , <foreign xml:lang="lat">sc.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁ κασίγνητος</quote> . Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Ant. 791" ref="Soph. Ant. 791"> <title>Ant.</title> 791</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">σὺ καὶ δικαίων ἀδίκους | φρένας παρασπᾷς ἐπὶ λώβᾳ</quote> </cit>, "thou wrenchest the minds e'en of the just unto injustice, for their bane." </gloss>

data.quote:
σὺ καὶ δικαίων ἀδίκους | φρένας
									παρασπᾷς ἐπὶ λώβᾳ

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἃ_μή_1185">=( <quote xml:lang="grc">ταῦτα</quote> ) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἂ μή</quote> (1175), <quote xml:lang="eng">"in respect of such words as shall not be spoken for thy good,"</quote> —a tribute, marked by feminine tact, to her father's judgment.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λέξεται_1185">is always pass. in trag.: cp. 581 <quote xml:lang="grc">δηλώσεται</quote> , and see on <ref n="Soph. OT 672"> <title>O. T.</title> 672</ref>. </gloss>
