<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κακῶς_1187">is Hermann's easy and certain correction of the MS. <quote xml:lang="grc"> καλῶς</quote> . <quote xml:lang="eng">"Evilly devised deeds are disclosed by speech"</quote> : <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> even supposing that Polyneices <emph>is</emph> harbouring ill designs, the best way to discover them is to converse with him. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Ant. 493" ref="Soph. Ant. 493"> <title>Ant.</title> 493</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="trimeter"> <l>φιλεῖ δ’ ὁ θυμὸς πρόσθεν ᾑρῆσθαι κλοπεύς,</l> <l>τῶν μηδὲν ὀρθῶς ἐν σκότῳ τεχνωμένων</l> </lg> </quote> </cit>,—where the bad consoience is supposed to bewray itself even before ( <quote xml:lang="grc">πρόσθεν</quote> ) investigation. With <foreign xml:lang="grc">καλῶς</foreign> , the words are merely <quote xml:lang="eng">"a rhetorical generality,"</quote> as Campbell (who retains it) says: <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> speech is a good thing, <quote xml:lang="eng">"for it is by speech that all man's best discoveries are revealed."</quote> But surely we need something more relevant to the matter in hand. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δυσσεβέστατ_ὦ_1190">(Dawes) seems right: it amends the MS. <foreign xml:lang="grc">τὰ τῶν κακίστων δυσσεβεστάτων</foreign> by simply striking off the final <foreign xml:lang="grc">ν.</foreign> "The most <emph>impious</emph> among the <emph> worst</emph> of deeds" is a vehement phrase suited to the passion of the appeal. Among evil deeds, <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰ κακά</quote> , those which outrage gods or kinsfolk form a class, <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰ δυσσεβῆ</quote> . If <foreign xml:lang="grc">κακίστων</foreign> were changed to <foreign xml:lang="grc">κάκιστα</foreign> , the latter must be an adv., and <foreign xml:lang="grc">τῶν δυσσεβεστάτων</foreign> must be masc.: "the deeds of men who in the worst way are most impious." <quote xml:lang="grc">κἀσεβεστάτων</quote> ("the deeds of the worst and most impious men") is less probable. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θέμις_εἶναι_1191">. The MSS. here agree in the nominative. Vauvilliers suggested that <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐστί</quote> might be supplied, taking <foreign xml:lang="grc">σέ γ’ εἶναι</foreign> in the sense of <quote xml:lang="grc">σέ γ’ ὄντα</quote> , and comparing <quote xml:lang="grc">ἑκὼν εἶναι</quote> , etc. This may be rejected, as may also Reisig's <quote xml:lang="grc">δυσσεβέστατ’ ἄν</quote> , with <foreign xml:lang="grc">εἴη</foreign> for <quote xml:lang="grc">εἶναι</quote> : for then we should require <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδὲ</quote> in <ref n="Soph. OC 1189">1189</ref> . Is <foreign xml:lang="grc">θέμις</foreign> , then, indeclinable in this phrase? That is now the received view. It rests, however, solely on the fact that our MSS. have <foreign xml:lang="grc">θέμις</foreign> , and not <foreign xml:lang="grc"> θέμιν</foreign> , here, and in four other places, <ref n="Plat. Gorg. 505D"> Plat. <title>Gorg.</title> 505D</ref>, <ref n="Xen. Ec. 11.11"> Xen. <title>Oec.</title> 11 § 11</ref>, Aelian <title>Nat. An.</title> 1. 60, <ref n="Aesch. Supp. 335"> Aesch. <title>Suppl.</title> 335</ref>. Porson believed that, with Dawes, we ought to read <foreign xml:lang="grc">θέμιν</foreign> . That is my own opinion; but, as the question must be considered doubtful, I have preferred to leave <foreign xml:lang="grc">θέμις</foreign> in the text, and to submit the evidence in the Appendix. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀλλ_εἰσὶ_1192">, etc., is the traditional reading, on which <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἀλλ’ αὑτόν</foreign> was a variant, adapted, seemingly, to the fatuous interpretation, <quote xml:lang="eng"> "Nay, you will hurt <emph> yourself</emph> "</quote>(see cr. n.). It is a robust faith which can accept <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἀλλ’ αὐτόν</foreign> as an aposiopesis. <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἀλλ’ ἔασον</foreign> , "Nay, allow (him to come)," is perhaps the best remedy, since we can suppose <foreign xml:lang="grc">αὐτόν</foreign> to have been an explanatory gloss which supplanted the verb. For the synizesis cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 1451" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg004:1451"> <title>O. T.</title> 1451</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλ’ ἔα με</quote> </cit>, n. <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἀλλ’ ἔα αὐτόν</foreign> as = <foreign xml:lang="meter">uu-</foreign> is surely impossible for tragedy. Musgrave's <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἀλλ’ εἶξον</foreign> is intrinsically preferable to either, but leaves the corruption unexplained. I had thought of <foreign xml:lang="grc">αἰδοῦ νιν</foreign> ("have compassion on him"). If <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐτόν</quote> had supplanted <quote xml:lang="grc">νιν, ΑΙΔ</quote> might have become <quote xml:lang="grc">ΑΛΛ</quote> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐξεπᾴδονται_φύσιν_1194"> , <quote xml:lang="eng">"are charmed out of their nature"</quote> : lit. <quote xml:lang="eng">"are subdued by the charm, in their nature"</quote> (acc. of respect). <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Phaedo 77E" ref="Plat. Phaedo 77E"> Plat. <title>Phaedo</title> 77E</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλ’ ἴσως ἔνι τις καὶ ἐν ἡμῖν παῖς, ὅστις τὰ τοιαῦτα φοβεῖται· τοῦτον οὖν πειρώμεθα πείθειν μὴ δεδιέναι τὸν θάνατον ὥσπερ τὰ μορμολύκεια. Ἀλλὰ χρή, ἔφη ὁ Σωκράτης, ἐπᾴδειν αὐτῷ ἑκάστης ἡμέρας, ἕως ἂν ἐξεπᾴσητε</quote> </cit>( <quote xml:lang="eng">"charm him out of us"</quote> ). <cit> <bibl> Plut. <title>De Iside et Os.</title> 384 A</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰ κρούματα τῆς λύρας, οἷς ἐχρῶντο πρὸ τῶν ὕπνων οἱ Πυθαγόρειοι, τὸ ἐμπαθὲς καὶ ἄλογον τῆς ψυχῆς ἐξεπᾴδοντες οὕτω καὶ θεραπεύοντες</quote> </cit> , <quote xml:lang="eng"> " <emph>subduing by the charm</emph> (of music) the passionate and unreasoning part of the soul."</quote> <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Phaedrus 267d" ref="Plat. Phaedrus 267d"> <title>Phaedr.</title>267 D</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὀργίσαι τε αὖ πολλοὺς ἅμα δεινὸς ἀνὴρ γέγονε, καὶ πάλιν ὠργισμένοις ἐπᾴδων κηλεῖν</quote> </cit> , <quote xml:lang="eng">"soothe them, when angered, by his charming."</quote> <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. PB 172" ref="Aesch. PB 172"> Aesch. <title>PV</title> 172</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">μελιγλώσσοις πειθοῦς | ἐπαοιδαῖσιν</quote> </cit>. The frequency of the metaphor is due to the regular use of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπῳδαί</quote> in the medical practice of the age: thus Pindar describes Cheiron as using (1) incantations, (2) draughts, (3) amulets, (4) surgery ( <title>Pyth.</title> 3. 51), and Plato's list of remedies is the same, with <quote xml:lang="grc">καύσεις</quote> added ( <ref n="Plat. Rep. 426b"> <title>Rep.</title> 426 B</ref>). In <ref n="Hom. Od. 19.457"> <title>Od.</title> 19.457</ref>an <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπῳδή</quote> stops hemorrhage, and in <ref n="Dem. 25.80">[Dem.] or. 25 § 80</ref> is applied to epilepsy. Sophocles <ref n="Soph. Trach. 1001"> <title>Tr.</title> 1001</ref>has <quote xml:lang="grc">τίς γὰρ ἀοιδός</quote> (= <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπῳδός</quote> ), <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="lyric"> <l>τίς ὁ χειροτέχνης</l> <l>ἰατορίας, ὃς τήνδ’ ἄτην</l> <l>...κατακηλήσει;</l> </lg> </quote> <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 582" ref="Soph. Aj. 582"> <title>Ai.</title>582</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">θρηνεῖν ἐπῳδὰς πρὸς τομῶντι πήματι</quote> </cit>. Lucian mocks the notion that a fever or a tumour can be scared by an <quote xml:lang="grc">ὄνομα θεσπέσιον ἢ ῥῆσιν βαρβαρικήν</quote> ( <ref n="Lucian Philops. 9"> <title>Philops.</title> 9</ref>). Cp. <ref n="Shak. Cym 1.6"> Shaksp. <title>Cymbeline</title> 1. 6. 115</ref> <quote xml:lang="eng"> <lg type="blank"> <l part="F">'tis your graces</l> <l>That from my mutest conscience to my tongue</l> <l part="I">Charms this report out.</l> </lg> </quote> </gloss>

data.quote:
ἀλλ’ ἴσως ἔνι τις καὶ ἐν ἡμῖν παῖς,
									ὅστις τὰ τοιαῦτα φοβεῖται· τοῦτον οὖν πειρώμεθα πείθειν
									μὴ δεδιέναι τὸν θάνατον ὥσπερ τὰ μορμολύκεια. Ἀλλὰ χρή,
									ἔφη ὁ Σωκράτης, ἐπᾴδειν αὐτῷ ἑκάστης ἡμέρας, ἕως ἂν
									ἐξεπᾴσητε

τὰ κρούματα τῆς λύρας, οἷς ἐχρῶντο πρὸ
									τῶν ὕπνων οἱ Πυθαγόρειοι, τὸ ἐμπαθὲς καὶ ἄλογον τῆς
									ψυχῆς ἐξεπᾴδοντες οὕτω καὶ θεραπεύοντες

ὀργίσαι τε αὖ πολλοὺς ἅμα δεινὸς ἀνὴρ
									γέγονε, καὶ πάλιν ὠργισμένοις ἐπᾴδων κηλεῖν

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐκεῖνα_1195">, away yonder, in the past.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πατρῷα_μ_1195">, connected with them: so <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Ant. 856" ref="Soph. Ant. 856"> <title>Ant.</title> 856</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">πατρῷον δ’ ἐκτίνεις τιν’ ἆθλον</quote> </cit>. He is to turn from his present causes for anger ( <foreign xml:lang="grc">τὰ νῦν</foreign> ) to the issues of his former anger—when he slew his sire.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μητρῷα_1195">, because the slaying prepared the marriage. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τελευτὴν_1198">, result: <cit> <bibl n="Hdt. 7.157" ref="Hdt. 7.157">Her. 7.157</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τῷ δὲ εὖ βουλευθέντι πρήγματι τελευτὴ ὡς τὸ ἐπίπαν χρηστὴ ἐθέλει ἐπιγίνεσθαι</quote> </cit>. For the constr. cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Ant. 1242" ref="Soph. Ant. 1242"> <title>Ant.</title> 1242</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="trimeter"> <l>δείξας ἐν ἀνθρώποισι τὴν ἀβουλίαν</l> <l>ὅσῳ μέγιστον ἀνδρὶ πρόσκειται κακόν</l> </lg> </quote> </cit> . </gloss>

data.quote:
τῷ δὲ εὖ βουλευθέντι πρήγματι τελευτὴ
									ὡς τὸ ἐπίπαν χρηστὴ ἐθέλει ἐπιγίνεσθαι

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τἀνθυμήματα_1199">(cp. 292), <quote xml:lang="eng">"the food for meditation"</quote> (on the evils of anger) which his <emph>blindness</emph> might furnish— itself due to an act of anger, the climax of acts traceable to the anger in which he slew Laïus. Cp. 855. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀδέρκτων_1200"> : <quote xml:lang="eng">"being deprived of thy sightless eyes,"</quote> = <quote xml:lang="eng">"being deprived of thine eyes, so that they shall see no more,"</quote> the adj. being proleptic: cp. 1088 <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸν εὔαγρον</quote> n.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τητώμενος_1200">: the pres. <quote xml:lang="grc">τητᾶσθαι</quote> denotes a state ( <quote xml:lang="eng">"to be without"</quote> ), not an act ( <quote xml:lang="eng">"to lose"</quote> ); cp. <cit> <bibl n="Hes. WD 408" ref="Hes. WD 408"> Hes. <title>Op.</title> 408</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">μὴ σὺ μὲν αἰτῇς ἄλλον, ὁ δ’ ἀρνῆται, σὺ δὲ τητᾷ</quote> </cit>, <quote xml:lang="eng"> "and thou <emph>remain in</emph> want."</quote> </gloss>

data.quote:
μὴ σὺ μὲν αἰτῇς ἄλλον, ὁ δ’ ἀρνῆται,
									σὺ δὲ τητᾷ

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐδ_1202">, <foreign xml:lang="lat">sc.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">καλόν ἐστι</quote> . Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Isoc. 4.175" ref="Isoc. 4.175">Isocr. or. 4 § 175</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄξιον ἐπισχεῖν, ἀλλ’ οὐκ ἐπειχθῆναι.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐκ_ἐπίστασθαι_1202">: with the inf. after <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ καλόν ἐστι</quote> the normal negative would be <quote xml:lang="grc">μή</quote> , or <quote xml:lang="grc">μὴ οὐ</quote> : but <foreign xml:lang="grc">οὐ</foreign> is treated as forming one word with the inf.: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Il. 24.296" ref="Hom. Il. 24.296"> <title>Il.</title> 24.296</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ δέ τοι οὐ‐δώσει.</quote> </cit> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τίνειν_1202">= <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀμείβεσθαι</quote> : see on <ref target="cl228">229</ref> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐδ_τίνειν_1202"> illustrates the Greek tendency to co-ordinate clauses: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Isoc. 6.54" ref="Isoc. 6.54">Isocr. or. 6 § 54</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">πῶς οὐκ αἰσχρόν,...τὴν μὲν Εὐρώπην καὶ τὴν Ἀσίαν μεστὴν πεποιηκέναι τροπαίων,... ὑπὲρ δὲ τῆς πατρίδος...μηδὲ μίαν μάχην φαίνεσθαι μεμαχημένους</quote> </cit>; We sometimes meet with the same construction in English: <foreign xml:lang="lat">e.g.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="eng"> "For one thing I am sorry, and that is <emph>that the English Government might have prevented the conflict with one single word</emph> , <emph>and</emph> yet has not thought it necessary to interfere."</quote> </gloss>

data.quote:
πῶς οὐκ αἰσχρόν,...τὴν μὲν Εὐρώπην καὶ
									τὴν Ἀσίαν μεστὴν πεποιηκέναι τροπαίων,... ὑπὲρ δὲ τῆς
							πατρίδος...μηδὲ μίαν μάχην φαίνεσθαι
									μεμαχημένους

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἡδονὴν_1204">is a bold acc. of respect with <quote xml:lang="grc">νικᾶτε</quote> , suggested by the constr. with a cognate acc., <quote xml:lang="grc">νίκην νικᾶτε</quote> , since the pleasure is secured by the victory. Cp. on <ref target="cl849">849</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">νικᾶν</quote> . We cannot well take <quote xml:lang="grc">ἡδ</quote> with <quote xml:lang="grc">λέγοντες</quote> , <quote xml:lang="eng">"ye prevail over me in"</quote> (or <quote xml:lang="eng">"by"</quote> ) <quote xml:lang="eng"> " <emph>speaking of</emph> a pleasure"</quote>etc. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δ_οὖν_1204">: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 115" ref="Soph. Aj. 115"> <title>Ai.</title> 115</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">σὺ δ’ οὖν … | χρῶ χειρί</quote> </cit>, "well, then, (if thou must)." </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐλεύσεται_1206">: this form occurs <ref n="Soph. Trach. 595"> <title>Tr.</title> 595</ref>, <ref n="Aesch. PB 854"> Aesch. <title> PV</title> 854</ref>, <title>Suppl.</title> 522: not in Eur., Comedy, or Attic prose, unless it be genuine in <ref n="Lys. 22.11">Lys. or. 22. 11</ref> . The Att. fut. is <quote xml:lang="grc">εἶμι</quote> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κρατείτω_ψυχῆς_1207"> , <quote xml:lang="eng">"become master of my life,"</quote> acquire the power to dispose of me,—alluding to the Thebans' plan for establishing him on their border (cp. 408).</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τῆς_ψ_1207">is merely a pathetic periphrasis for <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐμοῦ</quote> : see on <ref target="cl998">998</ref> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κλύειν_1208">is not perfectly courteous, as Wecklein says, who reads <foreign xml:lang="grc">λέγειν</foreign> ,—perhaps rightly. But for <foreign xml:lang="grc">κλύειν</foreign> it may be pleaded that, just after so signal a proof of good-faith and valour, Theseus might be excused if he showed a little impatience at the reiterated fears of Oedipus. Cp. their conversation at 648—656. Besides,</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὰ_τοιαῦτ_1208">, a phrase which implies some annoyance, must refer to the fears just uttered, rather than to pledges which should allay them. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὅστις_χρῄζει_1211">, whoever desires the ampler portion, </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ζώειν_1211"> (epexeg. inf.) that he should live (through it), </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="παρεὶς_1211"> , having neglected, <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> not being content, </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοῦ_μετρίου_1211"> ( <quote xml:lang="grc">χρῄζειν</quote> ), to desire a moderate portion: <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> , <quote xml:lang="eng">"whoever desires the larger part (of the extreme period allotted to human life), and is not satisfied with moderate length of days."</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="χρῄζ_1211">. with gen., as <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 473" ref="Soph. Aj. 473"> <title>Ai.</title> 473</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῦ μακροῦ χρῄζειν βίου</quote> </cit>, which also illustrates the art. with <quote xml:lang="grc">πλέονος</quote> : cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 518" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg004:518"> <title>O. T.</title> 518</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὔτοι βίου μοι τοῦ μακραίωνος πόθος</quote> </cit>. For <foreign xml:lang="grc">χρῄζ. τοῦ πλ. μ., ζώειν</foreign> , instead of <foreign xml:lang="grc">χρῄζ. ζώειν τὸ πλέον μέρος</foreign> , cp. 1755: <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Crito 52B" ref="Plat. Crito 52B"> Plat. <title>Crito</title> 52B</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδ’ ἐπιθυμία σε ἄλλης πόλεως οὐδ’ ἄλλων νόμων ἔλαβεν εἰδέναι</quote> </cit> . </gloss>

data.quote:
οὔτοι βίου μοι τοῦ μακραίωνος
									πόθος

οὐδ’ ἐπιθυμία σε ἄλλης πόλεως οὐδ’
									ἄλλων νόμων ἔλαβεν εἰδέναι

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="παρείς_1211">, if sound, must be construed in one of two ways: (1) as above, which is best: or (2) in Hermann's way, <quote xml:lang="grc">παρεὶς τοῦ μετρίου</quote> ( <quote xml:lang="grc">χρῄζων</quote> ) <quote xml:lang="grc">ζώειν</quote> , <quote xml:lang="eng">"negligens vivere modicam partem expetens,"</quote> scorning to live with desire of a modest span only. Others make it govern</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μετρίου_1211">, <quote xml:lang="eng">"neglecting the moderate portion,"</quote> and for the gen. Campbell quotes <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Phaedrus 235e" ref="Plat. Phaedrus 235e"> Plat. <title>Phaedr.</title> 235 E</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">παρέντα τοῦ ... ἐγκωμιάζειν</quote> </cit>. Liddell and Scott give the same citation along with this passage, which they render, <quote xml:lang="eng">"letting go one's hold of moderation,"</quote> <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> giving it up. But the active <quote xml:lang="grc">παριέναι</quote> never governs a gen. (in the nautical <quote xml:lang="grc">παριέναι τοῦ ποδός</quote> , <quote xml:lang="eng">"to slack away the sheet,"</quote> the gen. is partitive): and a reference to <ref n="Plat. Phaedrus 235e"> Plat. <title>Phaedr.</title> 235 E</ref>will show that <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῦ</quote> has nothing to do with the inf., but is masc. The passage runs:— <quote xml:lang="grc">τίνα οἴει λέγοντα ὡς χρὴ μὴ ἐρῶντι μᾶλλον ἢ ἐρῶντι χαρίζεσθαι, παρέντα τοῦ μὲν τὸ φρόνιμον ἐγκωμιάζειν, τοῦ δὲ τὸ ἄφρον ψέγειν, ἀναγκαῖα γοῦν ὄντα, εἴτ’ ἄλλ’ ἄττα ἕξειν λέγειν</quote> ; <foreign xml:lang="lat"> i.e.</foreign> , <quote xml:lang="eng"> "if he omitted to praise the sense <emph>of the one</emph> ( <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῦ μέν"</quote> , the non-lover), and to blame the folly of <emph>the other</emph> ( <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῦ δέ</quote> , the lover).</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σκαιοσύναν_1211">, perversity, folly: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Ant. 1028" ref="Soph. Ant. 1028"> <title>Ant.</title> 1028</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐθαδία τοι σκαιότητ’ ὀφλισκάνει.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φυλάσσων_1211">, cleaving to: <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Ion 735" ref="Eur. Ion 735"> Eur. <title>Ion</title> 735</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄξῑ ἀξίων γεννητόρων | ἤθη φυλάσσεις</quote> </cit>. Cp. 626, 1180. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐν_ἐμοὶ_1211">, <foreign xml:lang="lat">me iudice</foreign> , <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἐν</foreign> denoting the tribunal, as <ref n="Soph. OT 677"> <title>O. T.</title> 677</ref>(n.) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν...τοῖσδ’ ἴσος</quote> , "just in their sight": <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Laws 916B" ref="Plat. Laws 916B"> Plat. <title>Legg.</title> 916B</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">διαδικαζέσθω δὲ ἔν τισι τῶν ἰατρῶν</quote> </cit>. </gloss>

data.quote:
διαδικαζέσθω δὲ ἔν τισι τῶν
									ἰατρῶν

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αἱ_ἁμέραι_1214">, the long days (of any given long life), </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πολλὰ_κατέθεντο_1214">are wont (gnomic aor.) to lay up full many things, </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λύπας_1214">somewhat near to grief: <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> advancing years are apt to accumulate around men a store of cares, regrets, sorrows,—in brief, a store of things which are nearer to pain than to joy; while in the mean time the joys of earlier days have vanished.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λύπας_ἐγγυτέρω_1214">is a sort of euphemism: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Ant. 933" ref="Soph. Ant. 933"> <title>Ant.</title> 933</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οἴμοι, θανάτου τοῦτ’ ἐγγυτάτω | τοὔπος ἀφῖκται</quote> </cit>, <quote xml:lang="eng">"this word hath come very nigh unto death"</quote> — <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> threatens imminent death.</gloss>

data.quote:
οἴμοι, θανάτου τοῦτ’ ἐγγυτάτω | τοὔπος
									ἀφῖκται

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐκ_ὅπου_1214"> ( <foreign xml:lang="lat">sc.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc"> ἐστί</quote> , as <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 890" ref="Soph. Aj. 890"> <title>Ai.</title> 890</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄνδρα μὴ λεύσσειν ὅπου</quote> </cit>): cp. <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. Eum. 301" ref="Aesch. Eum. 301"> Aesch. <title>Eum.</title> 301</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ χαίρειν μὴ μαθόνθ’ ὅπου φρενῶν</quote> </cit>, <quote xml:lang="eng">"knowing not where to find joy in thy soul."</quote> </gloss>

data.quote:
τὸ χαίρειν μὴ μαθόνθ’ ὅπου
									φρενῶν

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοῦ_δέοντος_1220">(Reiske) is indicated by the schol. in L, <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῦ μετρίου, τοῦ ἱκανοῦ</quote> , and is, I think, true. The phrase, <foreign xml:lang="grc">ὅταν πέσῃ τις ἐς πλέον τοῦ δέοντος</foreign> , means, <quote xml:lang="eng"> " <emph>when one has lapsed into excess of due limit</emph> "</quote>in respect of prolonged life, <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> when one has outlived those years which alone are enjoy able, and at which the line of the <quote xml:lang="grc">μέτριον μέρος</quote> (1212) is drawn.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πέσῃ_1220">(cp. <quote xml:lang="grc">πίπτειν εἰς κακά</quote> , etc.) suggests a joyless decline of life, with decay of the faculties.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὁ_ἰσοτέλεστος_1220"> , <quote xml:lang="eng"> "and the succourer ( <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> , the deliverer from life's troubles) comes at the last to all alike,"</quote>— when the doom of Hades has appeared, — <quote xml:lang="eng">"namely, Death at the end."</quote> The man who is to attain <emph>long</emph> life has the same <emph>end</emph> before him as the man of shorter span,— viz. death; the only difference is that the long-lived man has to go through years of suffering which the other escapes, until death comes to him as a welcome <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπίκουρος</quote> . Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 475" ref="Soph. Aj. 475"> <title>Ai.</title> 475</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="trimeter"> <l>τί γὰρ παρ’ ἦμαρ ἡμέρα τέρπειν ἔχει,</l> <l>προσθεῖσα κἀναθεῖσα τοῦ γε κατθανεῖν;</l> </lg> </quote> </cit> <quote xml:lang="eng">"what joy is there in the sequence of the days,—now threatening, now delaying— <emph>death?</emph> "</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἰσοτέλεστος_1220">might be defended as act., <quote xml:lang="eng"> " <emph>making an end for</emph> all alike,"</quote>(see examples on 1031,) but is better taken as pass., lit., <quote xml:lang="eng"> " <emph>accomplished for</emph> all alike,"</quote> <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>forming the <quote xml:lang="grc">τέλος</quote> for them. The phrase <quote xml:lang="grc">τέλος θανάτοιο</quote> was in the poet's mind, and has blended itself with the image of a personal deliverer. (Cp. on <ref n="Soph. OT 866"> <title>O. T.</title> 866</ref>, 1300.)—Whitelaw takes <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἰσοτέλεστος</foreign> (as pass.) with <foreign xml:lang="grc">μοῖρα</foreign> , a doom paid alike by all; <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> all are <quote xml:lang="grc"> ἰσοτελεῖς</quote> in paying the tribute of their lives to Pluto. This may be right; but the accumulation of epithets on <quote xml:lang="grc"> μοῖρα</quote> becomes somewhat heavy, while <quote xml:lang="grc"> ἐπίκουρος</quote> is left in a long suspense. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀνυμέναιος_1222">: to death belongs the <quote xml:lang="grc">θρῆνος</quote> , not the joyous song of the marriage procession, or the music of the lyre, with dancing: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Eur. IT 144" ref="Eur. IT 144"> Eur. <title>I. T.</title> 144</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="lyric"> <l>θρήνοις ἔγκειμαι,</l> <l>τᾶς οὐκ εὐμούσου μολπᾶς</l> <l>ἀλύροις ἐλέγοις</l> </lg> </quote> </cit>. So Aesch. ( <title>Suppl.</title> 681) calls war <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄχορον ἀκίθαριν δακρυογόνον Ἄρη</quote> : cp. <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Tro. 121" ref="Eur. Tro. 121"> Eur. <title>Tro.</title> 121</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄτας κελαδεῖν ἀχορεύτους</quote> </cit> : <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. Eum. 331" ref="Aesch. Eum. 331"> Aesch. <title>Eum.</title> 331</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὕμνος ἐξ Ἐρινύων</quote> </cit> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀναπέφηνε_1222">, hath <emph>suddenly</emph> appeared: <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Il. 11.173" ref="Hom. Il. 11.173"> <title>Il.</title> 11.173</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="hexameter"> <l> <note anchored="true" place="inline" resp="RCJ" xml:lang="eng">(oxen) </note>ἅς τε λέων ἐφόβησε μολὼν ἐν νυκτὸς ἀμολγῷ</l> <l>πάσας· τῇ δέ τ’ ἰῇ ἀναφαίνεται αἰπὺς ὄλεθρος</l> </lg> </quote> </cit> : <quote xml:lang="eng"> "he turns all to flight, and to one of them sheer death appeareth <emph>instantly.</emph> "</quote>Cp. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνακύπτω</quote> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μὴ_λόγον_1225">, lit., <quote xml:lang="eng"> "Not to be born <emph>exceeds every possible estimate</emph> ,"</quote>—of the gain, as compared with the loss, of being born.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὁ_λόγος_1225">is strictly, <emph>the whole</emph> range of possible appreciation: for the art. with <quote xml:lang="grc">ἅπας</quote> cp. <cit> <bibl n="Thuc. 6.16" ref="Thuc. 6.16">Thuc. 6.16</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">περὶ τῶν ἁπάντων ἀγωνίζεσθαι</quote> </cit>, for <emph>the sum</emph> of their fortunes: <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 6 <quote xml:lang="grc">τὴν</quote> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἅπασαν δύναμιν τῆς Σικελίας</quote> , <emph>the total</emph> power. Rate the gain of being born as high as you please; the gain of <emph>not</emph> being born is higher. Two other ways are possible:—(1) <quote xml:lang="eng"> "Not to be born <emph>excels the whole account</emph> ,"</quote>— <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> excels all the other things (joys, sorrows, of life) that come into account. The drawback to this is the somewhat strained sense of <foreign xml:lang="grc">λόγον</foreign> . (2) <quote xml:lang="eng"> " <title>Stands first on the whole reckoning</title> ,"</quote>( <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸν ἅ. λόγον</quote> being cogn. acc., or acc. of respect)— <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> when a balance is struck between the good and the evil of being born. This seems too cold and cautious for the context.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐπεὶ_φανῇ_1225">, when he has been born, cp. 974: for subj., 395. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πολὺ_δεύτερον_1226">: easily the second-best thing: <cit> <bibl n="Thuc. 2.97" ref="Thuc. 2.97">Thuc. 2.97</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἡ βασιλεία</quote> </cit> ( <quote xml:lang="grc">ἡ τῶν Ὀδρυσῶν</quote> )... <quote xml:lang="grc">τῶν...ἐν τῇ Εὐρώπῃ μεγίστη ἐγένετο χρημάτων προσόδῳ,...ἰσχύϊ δὲ μά‐</quote> <quote xml:lang="grc">χης καὶ στρατοῦ πλήθει πολὺ δευτέρα μετὰ τὴν τῶν Σκυθῶν</quote> (where <quote xml:lang="eng">"easily second"</quote> suits the context better than <quote xml:lang="eng">"decidedly inferior"</quote> ).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πολὺ_1226">with compar., as <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Il. 6.158" ref="Hom. Il. 6.158"> <title>Il.</title> 6.158</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">πολὺ φέρτερος</quote> </cit> , <cit> <bibl n="Thuc. 1.35" ref="Thuc. 1.35">Thuc. 1.35</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">πολὺ...ἐν πλείονι αἰτίᾳ</quote> </cit>, etc. (but <cit> <quote xml:lang="grc">πολλῷ...πρῶτον</quote> <bibl n="Soph. Ant. 1347" ref="Soph. Ant. 1347"> <title>Ant.</title> 1347</bibl> </cit> ). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὡς_ἔνι_1229">; The first point to decide in this vexed passage is:—Does Sophocles here speak of <foreign xml:lang="grc">τὸ νέον</foreign> as a brief space of <emph> joy</emph> before the troubles of life begin? Or is <foreign xml:lang="grc">τὸ νέον</foreign> itself the period of fierce passions and troubles? The former, I think. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 552" ref="Soph. Aj. 552"> <title>Ai.</title> 552 ff.</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="trimeter"> <l> <note anchored="true" place="inline" resp="RCJ" xml:lang="eng">(ajax speaking to his young son)</note>καίτοι σε καὶ νῦν τοῦτό γε ζηλοῦν ἔχω,</l> <l>ὁθούνεκ’ οὐδὲν τῶνδ’ ἐπαισθάνει κακῶν.</l> <l>ἐν τῷ φρονεῖν γὰρ μηδὲν ἥδιστος βίος,</l> <l>ἕως τὸ χαίρειν καὶ τὸ λυπεῖσθαι μάθῃς.</l> <l>… τέως δὲ κούφοις πνεύμασιν βόσκου, νέαν</l> <l>ψυχὴν ἀτάλλων</l> </lg> </quote> </cit> . <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Trach. 144" ref="Soph. Trach. 144"> <title>Tr.</title> 144</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="trimeter"> <l>τὸ γὰρ νεάζον ἐν τοιοῖσδε βόσκεται</l> <l>χώροισιν αὑτοῦ, καί νιν οὐ θάλπος θεοῦ,</l> <l>οὐδ’ ὄμβρος, οὐδὲ πνευμάτων οὐδὲν κλονεῖ,</l> <l>ἀλλ’ ἡδοναῖς ἄμοχθον ἐξαίρει βίον</l> </lg> </quote> </cit>, etc. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="παρῇ_1229">, then, must be taken from <quote xml:lang="grc">παρίημι</quote> , not from <quote xml:lang="grc">πάρειμι</quote> , unless we are prepared to write <quote xml:lang="grc">φέρει</quote> , and boldly to alter <foreign xml:lang="grc">τις πλάγχθη</foreign> , etc. For <foreign xml:lang="grc">παρῇ</foreign> ("remit,""give up"), cp. <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Tro. 645" ref="Eur. Tro. 645"> Eur. <title>Tro.</title> 645</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">παρεῖσα πόθον</quote> </cit> : <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Rep. 460E" ref="Plat. Rep. 460E"> Plat. <title>Rep.</title> 460E</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπειδὰν τὴν ὀξυτάτην δρόμου ἀκμὴν παρῇ</quote> </cit>. </gloss>

data.quote:
ἐπειδὰν τὴν ὀξυτάτην δρόμου ἀκμὴν
									παρῇ

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τίς_πλαγὰ_1231">(Herwerden) is the best correction yet proposed for the MS. <foreign xml:lang="grc">τις πλάγχθη</foreign> . Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. Pers. 251" ref="Aesch. Pers. 251"> Aesch. <title>Pers.</title> 251</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς ἐν μιᾷ πληγῇ κατέφθαρται πολὺς | ὄλβος</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. Eum. 933" ref="Aesch. Eum. 933"> <title>Eum.</title> 933</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">πληγαὶ βιότου</quote> </cit>. For other interpretations and conjectures see Appendix.</gloss>

data.quote:
ὡς ἐν μιᾷ πληγῇ κατέφθαρται πολὺς |
									ὄλβος

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φθόνος_1233">(see cr. n.), the root of so much evil, is more naturally placed before <foreign xml:lang="grc">στάσεις</foreign> , while <foreign xml:lang="grc"> φόνοι</foreign> is more fitting as a climax than at the beginning of the list. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κατάμεμπτον_1235"> , <quote xml:lang="eng">"disparaged,"</quote> because often spoken of as dreary (cp. <quote xml:lang="grc">ὀλοῷ ἐπὶ γήραος οὐδῷ, γήραϊ λυγρῷ</quote> , etc.). <cit> <bibl n="Shak. AYL 2.3" ref="Shak. AYL 2.3"> Shaksp. <title>As You Like It</title> 2. 3. 41</bibl> <quote xml:lang="eng">"When service should in my old limbs lie lame, And unregarded age, in corners thrown."</quote> </cit> </gloss>

data.quote:
"When service should in my old limbs
									lie lame, And unregarded age, in corners
									thrown."

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐπιλέλογχε_1235"> , <quote xml:lang="eng"> "next ( <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπι‐"</quote> ) falls to his lot.</quote>Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Pind. O. 1" ref="Pind. O. 1"> Pind. <title>O.</title> 1. 53</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀκέρδεια λέλογχεν θαμινὰ κακαγόρος</quote> </cit>(Dor. acc. pl.), <quote xml:lang="eng">"sore loss hath oft come on evil-speakers,"</quote> a <emph>gnomic</emph> perf., as here. Here, too, we might understand <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸν ἄνθρωπον</quote> : but the verb seems rather to be intrans., as oft. <quote xml:lang="grc">λαγχάνω</quote> : <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Hel. 213" ref="Eur. Hel. 213"> Eur. <title>Hel.</title> 213</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">αἰὼν δυσαίων τις ἔλαχεν, ἔλαχεν</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Od. 9.159" ref="Hom. Od. 9.159"> <title>Od.</title> 9.159</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐς δὲ ἑκάστην | ἐννέα λάγχανον αἶγες</quote> </cit>, <quote xml:lang="eng">"fell to the portion of each ship"</quote> : <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Laws 745D" ref="Plat. Laws 745D"> Plat. <title>Legg.</title> 745D</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">καθιερῶσαι τὸ λαχὸν μέρος ἑκάστῳ τῷ θεῷ</quote> </cit>. The ellipse of the object here is made easier by the notion which the verb conveys, <quote xml:lang="eng">"'tis the turn of old age next."</quote> —Not: <quote xml:lang="eng">"he obtains old age next."</quote> </gloss>

data.quote:
ἐς δὲ ἑκάστην | ἐννέα λάγχανον
									αἶγες

καθιερῶσαι τὸ λαχὸν μέρος ἑκάστῳ τῷ
									θεῷ

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀκρατές_1235">, <quote xml:lang="eng">"weak"</quote> : Eustath. 790. 92 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀκρατὲς ἐκεῖνός φησιν, οὐ τὸ ἀκόλαστον, ἀλλα τὸ ποιοῦν πάρεσιν, ὡς μὴ ἔχοντα τὸν γέροντα κρατεῖν ἑαυτοῦ</quote> . So Hesych. <foreign xml:lang="lat">s.v.</foreign> , quoting Eur. in the lost <title>Aeolus.</title> Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Phil. 486" ref="Soph. Phil. 486"> <title>Ph.</title> 486</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">καίπερ ὢν ἀκράτωρ ὁ τλήμων, χωλός</quote> </cit>. Perhaps an Ionic use of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀκρατής</quote> , for Hippocr. has it in this sense ( <ref n="Hp. Aph. 1247"> <title>Aph.</title> 1247</ref>): in Attic <emph>prose</emph> it always means <quote xml:lang="eng">"without control"</quote> over passion or desire ( <foreign xml:lang="lat">impotens</foreign> ). For <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἄφιλον</foreign> placed after <quote xml:lang="grc"> γῆρας</quote> , cp. <ref n="Soph. Phil. 392"> <title>Ph.</title> 392 </ref>n. </gloss>

data.quote:
καίπερ ὢν ἀκράτωρ ὁ τλήμων,
									χωλός

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κακὰ_κακῶν_1238"> , <quote xml:lang="eng">"ills of ills,"</quote> = <quote xml:lang="eng">"worst of ills"</quote> : <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 465" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg004:465"> <title>O. T.</title> 465</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄρρητ’ ἀρρήτων</quote> </cit> (n.). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ξυνοικεῖ_1238">: cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 1134">1134.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="βόρειος_ἀκτὰ_1240">, a shore exposed to the north wind, and so lashed by the waves ( <foreign xml:lang="grc">κυματοπλὴξ</foreign> ) which that wind raises <foreign xml:lang="grc">χειμερία</foreign> , in the stormy season. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Ant. 592" ref="Soph. Ant. 592"> <title>Ant.</title> 592</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">στόνῳ βρέμουσιν ἀντιπλῆγες ἀκταί</quote> </cit>(in a like comparison). So <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Trach. 112" ref="Soph. Trach. 112"> <title>Tr.</title> 112</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">πολλὰ γὰρ ὥστ’ ἀκάμαντος ἢ νότου ἢ βορέα τις | κύματ’ … ἴδοι</quote> </cit>(of the troubles of Heracles). </gloss>

data.quote:
πολλὰ γὰρ ὥστ’ ἀκάμαντος ἢ νότου ἢ
									βορέα τις | κύματ’ … ἴδοι

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κατ_ἄκρας_1241">, "utterly," in the sense of "violently": perh. with a reminiscence of <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Od. 5.313" ref="Hom. Od. 5.313"> <title>Od.</title> 5.313</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <note anchored="true" place="inline" resp="RCJ" xml:lang="eng">(quoted by campbell)</note>ὡς ἄρα μιν εἰπόντ’ ἔλασεν μέγα κῦμα κατ’ ἄκρης</quote> </cit>, "the great wave smote down on him" (Odysseus on his raft): in <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Ant. 201" ref="Soph. Ant. 201"> <title>Ant.</title> 201</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">πρῆσαι κατ’ ἄκρας</quote> </cit>(of destroying a city). </gloss>

data.quote: ὡς ἄρα μιν εἰπόντ’ ἔλασεν μέγα κῦμα κατ’ ἄκρης

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κυματοαγεῖς_1241">, breaking like billows. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀνὰ_ἀκτῖν_1245"> = <quote xml:lang="eng">"in the region of the noon-tide ray,"</quote> <emph>i.e. these</emph> waves of trouble are supposed to be driven by a south wind (cp. <ref n="Soph. Trach. 112"> <title>Tr.</title> 112</ref>, n. on 1240). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πιπᾶν_1248">. <ref n="Aristot. Meteo. 1.13"> Arist. <title>Meteor.</title> 1.13</ref>(Berl. ed. 350 <emph>b</emph> 6) <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑπ’ αὐτὴν δὲ τὴν ἄρκτον ὑπὲρ τῆς ἐσχάτης Σκυθίας αἱ καλούμεναι *(πῖπαι, περὶ ὧν τοῦ μεγέθους λίαν εἰσὶν οἱ λεγόμενοι λόγοι μυθώδεις</quote> . It is fortunate that this passage is extant, showing, as I think it does beyond all reasonable doubt, that Soph. here named the Rhipaean mountains, <quote xml:lang="eng">"beyond utmost Scythia,"</quote> as representing the <title>North.</title> Aristotle's words prove that the name <quote xml:lang="grc">*(πῖπαι</quote> for these mountains was thoroughly familiar. Cp. Alcman of Sparta (660 B.C.) fr. 51 (Bergk), <quote xml:lang="grc">*(πίπας, ὄρος ἔνθεον</quote> ( <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνθέον</quote> Lobeck) <quote xml:lang="grc">ὕλᾳ, | Νυκτὸς μελαίνας στέρνον</quote> . Hellanicus (circ. 450 B.C.) fr. 96 (Müller) <quote xml:lang="grc">τοὺς δὲ Ὑπερβορέους ὑπὲρτὰ Ῥίπαια ὄρη οἰκεῖν ἱστορεῖ</quote> . Damastes of Sigeum (his younger contemporary) fr. 1 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄνω δ’ Ἀριμασπῶν τὰ *(πίπαια ὄρη, ἐξ ὦν τὸν βορέαν πνεῖν, χιόνα δ’ αὐτὰ μήποτε ἐλλείπειν· ὑπὲρ δὲ τὰ ὄρη ταῦτα Ὑπερβορέους καθήκειν εἰς τὴν ἑτέραν θάλασσαν</quote> . For the age of Sophocles, these mountains belonged wholly to the region of myth, and so were all the more suitable for his purpose here. The Roman poets, too, used the <quote xml:lang="eng">"Rhipaei montes"</quote> to denote the uttermost North ( <ref n="Verg. G. 1.240"> Verg. <title>Geo.</title> 1. 240</ref>, etc.). The name <quote xml:lang="grc">Ῥῖπαι</quote> was only <quote xml:lang="grc">ῥιπαί</quote> ,—the <quote xml:lang="eng"> "blasts"</quote> of Boreas coming thence.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐννυχιᾶν_1248">, wrapped in gloom and storm: cp. 1558.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καὶ_μὴν_1249">, introducing the new comer (549):</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἡμῖν_1249">ethic dat. (81). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀνδρῶν_μοῦνος_1250"> (cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 875">875</ref> ), "with no escort at least," in contrast to Creon, <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 722" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg007:722">722</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἆσσον ἔρχεται | Κρέων ὅδ’ ἡμῖν οὐκ ἄνευ πομπῶν, πάτερ</quote> </cit>. Oedipus dreaded that his son, like Creon, would make an attempt to carry him off by violence: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 1206" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg007:1206">1206</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἴπερ κεῖνος ὧδ’ ἐλεύσεται, | μηδεὶς κρατείτω</quote> </cit>etc.: and Antigone hastens to assure him at once that Polyneices comes otherwise than as Creon came. He is <emph>alone</emph> , and in tears. For the gen. cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 511" ref="Soph. Aj. 511"> <title>Ai.</title> 511</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">σοῦ...μόνος</quote> </cit>.—Others: —(1) <quote xml:lang="eng">"he, and no one else"</quote> : this seems somewhat weak. (2) <quote xml:lang="eng"> "weeping as no <emph>man</emph> weeps"</quote>(but only women):—a modern view of weeping: it is enough to remember Achilles and Aeneas. </gloss>

data.quote:
ἆσσον ἔρχεται | Κρέων ὅδ’ ἡμῖν οὐκ
									ἄνευ πομπῶν, πάτερ

εἴπερ κεῖνος ὧδ’ ἐλεύσεται, | μηδεὶς
									κρατείτω

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀστακτὶ_1251"> has <quote xml:lang="grc">ι—</quote> in <ref n="Soph. OC 1646"> 1646</ref> . The general rule (Blomfield <emph>glossar.</emph> <ref n="Aesch. PB 216"> Aesch. <title>PV</title> 216</ref>) is that such adverbs, when from nouns in <quote xml:lang="grc">η</quote> or <quote xml:lang="grc">α</quote> , end in <foreign xml:lang="grc">ει</foreign> (as <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐτοβοεί</quote> ): when from nouns in <quote xml:lang="grc">ος</quote> , in <quote xml:lang="grc">ι</quote> , which is more often short, but sometimes long. For <foreign xml:lang="grc">ι^</foreign> cp. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐγερτί</quote> ( <ref n="Soph. Ant. 413"> <title>Ant.</title> 413</ref>), <quote xml:lang="grc">νεωστί</quote> ( <ref n="Soph. El. 1049"> <title>El.</title> 1049</ref>), <quote xml:lang="grc">σκυθιστί</quote> (fr. 429), <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀωρί</quote> ( <ref n="Aristoph. Eccl. 741"> Aristoph. <title>Eccl.</title> 741</ref>), <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνδριστί</quote> ( <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 149), <quote xml:lang="grc">δωριστί</quote> ( <ref n="Aristoph. Kn. 989"> <title>Eq.</title> 989</ref>), the Homeric <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀμογητί, μεγαλωστί</quote> , etc. For <quote xml:lang="grc"> <emph>ι—</emph> , ἀνοιμωκτί</quote>( <ref n="Soph. Aj. 1227"> <title>Ai.</title> 1227</ref>), <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνιδρωτί</quote> ( <ref n="Hom. Il. 15.228"> <title>Il.</title> 15.228</ref>), <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀσπουδί</quote> ( <ref n="Hom. Il. 8.512">8. 512</ref> ), <quote xml:lang="grc"> ἀνωιστί</quote> ( <ref n="Hom. Od. 4.92"> <title>Od.</title> 4.92</ref>), etc. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀστακτὶ_1251_2">, not <quote xml:lang="grc">στάγδην</quote> ( <foreign xml:lang="lat"> stillatim</foreign> ): <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Phaedo 117C" ref="Plat. Phaedo 117C"> Plat. <title>Phaedo</title> 117C</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐμοῦ γε...ἀστακτὶ ἐχώρει τὰ δάκρυα</quote> </cit>. So <cit> <bibl n="Eur. IT 1242" ref="Eur. IT 1242"> Eur. <title>I. T.</title> 1242</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀστάκτων...ὑδάτων</quote> </cit>, and <cit> <bibl n="Apollon. 3.804" ref="Apollon. 3.804">Apoll. Rh. 3. 804</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀσταγές.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὧδε_1251">= <quote xml:lang="grc">δεῦρο</quote> : cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 1286">1286</ref> , <ref n="Soph. OT 7"> <title>O. T.</title> 7</ref>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κατείχομεν_γνώμῃ_1252">, apprehended: <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Meno 72d" ref="Plat. Meno 72d"> Plat. <title>Men.</title> 72 D</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ μέντοι ὡς βούλομαί γέ πω κατέχω τὸ ἐρωτώμενον</quote> </cit>. </gloss>

data.quote:
οὐ μέντοι ὡς βούλομαί γέ πω κατέχω τὸ
							ἐρωτώμενον

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δράσω_1254">, probably aor. subj. (cp. 478), though it might be fut.: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Trach. 973" ref="Soph. Trach. 973"> <title>Tr.</title> 973</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τί πάθω; τί δὲ μήσομαι; οἴμοι</quote> </cit>. So <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Phoen. 1310" ref="Eur. Phoen. 1310"> Eur. <title>Phoen.</title> 1310</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οἴμοι, τί δράσω; πότερ’ ἐμαυτὸν ἢ πόλιν | στένω δακρύσας</quote> </cit>, etc. The <title>Phoenissae</title> being the earlier play, it is possible that Soph. had it in mind, but it is quite as likely that the coincidence is accidental: it is at any rate trivial. </gloss>

data.quote: τί πάθω; τί δὲ μήσομαι; οἴμοι

οἴμοι, τί δράσω; πότερ’ ἐμαυτὸν ἢ
									πόλιν | στένω δακρύσας

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐνθάδ_ἐκβεβλημένον_1257">, in exile here: <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Gorg. 468D" ref="Plat. Gorg. 468D"> Plat. <title>Gorg.</title> 468D</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἴ τις ἀποκτείνει τινὰ ἢ ἐκβάλλει ἐκ πόλεως ἢ ἀφαιρεῖται χρήματα</quote> </cit> (cp. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκπίπτειν</quote> , of being exiled). We might understand, <quote xml:lang="eng">"shipwrecked here,"</quote> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκβάλλω</quote> being regularly used of casting ashore; but I prefer the simpler version. </gloss>

data.quote:
εἴ τις ἀποκτείνει τινὰ ἢ ἐκβάλλει ἐκ
									πόλεως ἢ ἀφαιρεῖται χρήματα

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σὺν_1258">: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 191" ref="Soph. El. 191"> <title>El.</title> 191</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀεικεῖ σὺν στολᾷ.</quote> </cit> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τῆς_1258">: see on <ref target="cl747">747</ref> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γέρων_πίνος_1258"> : <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Od. 22.184" ref="Hom. Od. 22.184"> <title>Od.</title> 22.184</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">σάκος εὐρὺ γέρον, πεπαλαγμένον ἄζῃ</quote> </cit>(stained with rust): <cit> <bibl n="Theoc. 7" ref="Theoc. 7">Theocr. 7. 17</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀμφὶ δέ οἱ στήθεσσι γέρων ἐσφίγγετο πέπλος</quote> </cit>(cp. <cit> <quote xml:lang="lat">anus charta,</quote> <bibl n="Catul. 68" ref="Catul. 68">Catull. 68. 46</bibl> </cit>). So <cit> <bibl n="Aristoph. Lys. 1207" ref="Aristoph. Lys. 1207"> Aristoph. <title>Lys.</title> 1207</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄρτος...νεανίας</quote> </cit> </gloss>

data.quote:
ἀμφὶ δέ οἱ στήθεσσι γέρων ἐσφίγγετο
									πέπλος

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="συγκατῴκηκεν_1258">, has made an <emph>abiding</emph> home, emphatic perf., cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 186" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg007:186">186</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τέτροφεν</quote> </cit> <ref target="cl186">(n.)</ref>, <ref n="Soph. OC 1004">1004</ref> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πλευρὰν_μαραίνων_1260">can mean only that the squalor of the raiment is unwholesome for the body to which it clings. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. PB 596" ref="Aesch. PB 596"> Aesch. <title>PV</title> 596</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">νόσον</quote> </cit> ..</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κρατὶ_ὀμματοστερεῖ_1260">, locative dat.: cp. on <ref target="cl313">313</ref> .</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀκτένιστος_1260"> : <ref n="Hdt. 7.208">Her. 7.208</ref> (the Lacedaemonians before Thermopylae) <quote xml:lang="grc">τοὺς μὲν δὴ ὥρα γυμναζομένους τῶν ἀνδρῶν, τοὺς δὲ τὰς κόμας κτενιζομένους</quote> . The <quote xml:lang="grc">κτείς</quote> was usu. of boxwood, ivory, or metal.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ᾄσσεται_1260">: <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Il. 6.510" ref="Hom. Il. 6.510"> <title>Il.</title> 6.510</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀμφὶ δὲ χαῖται | ὤμοις ἀΐσσονται</quote> </cit>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀδελφὰ_τούτοισιν_1262">: but <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Ant. 192" ref="Soph. Ant. 192"> <title>Ant.</title> 192</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀδελφὰ τῶνδε</quote> </cit>. The dat. occurs elsewhere (as <ref n="Plat. Tim. 67E"> Plat. <title> Tim.</title> 67E</ref>), but the gen. is much commoner. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φορεῖ_1262">is taken by some as <quote xml:lang="eng">"obtains by begging"</quote> ; but a <emph>conjecture</emph> to that effect would be hardly in place. Obviously it means simply <quote xml:lang="eng"> " <emph>carries</emph> ,"</quote>and alludes to a wallet ( <quote xml:lang="grc">πήρα</quote> ) carried by Oed., for the reception of the <quote xml:lang="grc">σπανιστὰ δωρήματα</quote> (4). This was a part of the conventional outfit for the wandering beggar ; so, when Athena turned Odysseus into that guise, she gave him <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="hexameter"> <l>σκῆπτρον καὶ ἀεικέα πήρην,</l> <l>πυκνὰ ῥωγαλέην· ἐν δὲ στρόφος ἦεν ἀορτήρ</l> </lg> </quote> : <quote xml:lang="eng">"a staff, and a mean, much-tattered wallet; and therewith was a cord to hang it"</quote> ( <ref n="Hom. Od. 13.437"> <title>Od.</title> 13.437</ref>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἥκειν_1265">as 1177 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔχθιστον...ἥκει</quote> (n.). (Better thus than, <quote xml:lang="eng">"I, who have come hither, am,"</quote> etc.) </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τροφ_σαῖσιν_1265">, dat. of respect. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μὴ_ἄλλων_1265"> : <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 1225" ref="Soph. El. 1225"> <title>El.</title> 1225</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ΗΛ. ὦ φθέγμ’, ἀφίκου; ΟΡ. μηκέτ’ ἄλλοθεν πύθῃ</quote> </cit>. </gloss>

data.quote:
ΗΛ. ὦ φθέγμ’, ἀφίκου; ΟΡ. μηκέτ’
									ἄλλοθεν πύθῃ

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀλλὰ_γὰρ_1267"> , <quote xml:lang="eng"> "but <emph>since</emph> "</quote>: see on <ref target="cl988">988</ref> .</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Ζηνὶ_θρόνων_1267">, a sharer with Zeus on his throne: cp. on <ref target="cl1382">1382</ref> . Where we should say, <quote xml:lang="eng"> "an <emph>attribute</emph> "</quote>of godhead, the Greeks often use the image of assessor.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Αἰδὼς_1267">, here compassion; see on <ref target="cl237">237</ref> . <quote xml:lang="grc">Αἰδώς</quote> , as well as <quote xml:lang="grc"> Ἔλεος</quote> , had an altar at Athens (see <ref n="Paus. 1.17.1">Paus. 1.17.1</ref> , cited on 260). <ref n="Shak. MV 4.1"> Shaksp. <title> Merch.</title> 4. 1. 193</ref>(mercy): <quote xml:lang="eng">"It is enthroned in the hearts of kings, It is an attribute to God Himself; And earthly power doth then show likest God's, When mercy seasons justice."</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐπ_πᾶσι_1267"> , <emph>in</emph> all deeds: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Il. 4.178" ref="Hom. Il. 4.178"> <title>Il.</title> 4.178</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">αἴθ’ οὕτως ἐπὶ πᾶσι χόλον τελέσεῑ Ἀγαμέμνων</quote> </cit>, <quote xml:lang="eng">"in all cases"</quote> (as in this). </gloss>

data.quote:
αἴθ’ οὕτως ἐπὶ πᾶσι χόλον τελέσεῑ
									Ἀγαμέμνων

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καὶ_σοί_1267"> , <quote xml:lang="eng"> " <emph>nigh to</emph> thee also."</quote>In this sense <foreign xml:lang="grc">πρός</foreign> is usu. said of places (see 10), very seldom of persons (except in such phrases as <quote xml:lang="grc">ἃ πρὸς τοῖς θεσμοθέταις ἔλεγε</quote> , <emph>before</emph> their tribunal, <ref n="Dem. 20.98">Dem. or. 20 § 98</ref> ). In <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Ant. 1188" ref="Soph. Ant. 1188"> <title>Ant.</title> 1188</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">κλίνομαι</quote> </cit> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τῶν_ἡμαρτημένων_1269"> : <quote xml:lang="eng"> "there are remedies for the faults committed ( <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> if Oed. will return to Thebes with Polyneices), while there is no possibility of adding to them."</quote>In this appeal for pardon, the <quote xml:lang="eng"> "faults"</quote> most naturally mean those committed by the speaker; but the vague phrase which he has chosen permits the thought that there have been errors on both sides.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="προσφορὰ_1269">implies at once a confession and an assurance: the son has behaved as ill as possible; he could not, even if he would, add to his offence. Hartung's <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἀναφορά</foreign> could not mean what he intends, <quote xml:lang="eng"> "there is no possibility of <emph>recalling</emph> the past,"</quote>but only, <quote xml:lang="eng">"there is no possibility of referring the blame elsewhere,"</quote> —of putting it on other shoulders. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τί_σιγᾷς_1271">; An anxious pause, while Oed. remains silent: cp. 315, 318. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μή_ἀποστραφῇς_1272"> : <cit> <bibl n="Xen. Cyrop. 5.5.36" ref="Xen. Cyrop. 5.5.36"> Xen. <title>Cyr.</title> 5.5.36</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἦ καὶ φιλήσω σε; Εἰ σὺ βούλει, ἔφη. Καὶ οὐκ ἀποστρέψει με ὥσπερ ἄρτι</quote> </cit>; But the <emph>place</emph> from which one turns is put in the gen., as <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 431" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg004:431"> <title>O. T.</title> 431</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οἴκων τῶνδ’ ἀποστραφείς.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

data.quote:
ἦ καὶ φιλήσω σε; Εἰ σὺ βούλει, ἔφη.
									Καὶ οὐκ ἀποστρέψει με ὥσπερ ἄρτι

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀτιμάσας_1272">, of rejecting a suppliant, cp. 49, 286. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὦ_σπέρματ_1275">: for the plur. cp. 600. The <foreign xml:lang="lat">v.l.</foreign> <foreign xml:lang="grc">σπέρμα τἀνδρὸς</foreign> might be defended by <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Trach. 1147" ref="Soph. Trach. 1147"> <title>Tr.</title> 1147</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">κάλει τὸ πᾶν μοι σπέρμα σῶν ὁμαιμόνων</quote> </cit>(cp. <ref n="Soph. Trach. 304"> <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 304</ref>); but the sing., when it refers to more than one person, is usu. rather "race," like <cit> <quote xml:lang="grc">σπέρμα Πελοπιδῶν</quote> <bibl n="Aesch. Lib. 503" ref="Aesch. Lib. 503"> Aesch. <title>Cho.</title> 503</bibl> </cit>. Cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 330">330.</ref> </gloss>

data.quote:
κάλει τὸ πᾶν μοι σπέρμα σῶν
									ὁμαιμόνων

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐμαὶ_δ_1275">. When different relationships of the same person are expressed, the second is introduced by <foreign xml:lang="grc">δέ</foreign> , without a preceding <foreign xml:lang="grc">μέν</foreign> : <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. Pers. 151" ref="Aesch. Pers. 151"> Aesch. <title>Pers.</title> 151</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">μήτηρ βασιλέως, | βασίλεια δ’ ἐμή</quote> </cit> <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Med. 970" ref="Eur. Med. 970">Eur. <title>Med.</title> 970</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">πατρὸς νέαν γυναῖκα, δεσπότιν δ’ ἐμήν</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Hdt. 7.10" ref="Hdt. 7.10">Her. 7.10</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">πατρὶ τῷ σῷ, ἀδελφεῷ δὲ ἐμῷ</quote> </cit> : <cit> <bibl n="Hdt. 8.54" ref="Hdt. 8.54">8. 54</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἀθηναίων τοὺς φυγάδας, ἑωυτῷ δὲ ἑπομένους</quote> </cit>. </gloss>

data.quote:
πατρὸς νέαν γυναῖκα, δεσπότιν δ’
									ἐμήν
πατρὶ τῷ σῷ, ἀδελφεῷ δὲ ἐμῷ

Ἀθηναίων τοὺς φυγάδας, ἑωυτῷ δὲ
									ἑπομένους

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀλλ_γε_1276"> , <quote xml:lang="eng"> " <title>Ye</title> at least"</quote>(since I have failed): cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 411" ref="Soph. El. 411"> <title>El.</title> 411</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">συγγένεσθέ γ’ ἀλλὰ νῦν</quote> </cit> ( <emph>now</emph> , at least): <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 415 <quote xml:lang="grc">λέγ’ ἀλλὰ τοῦτο</quote> ( <emph>this</emph> , at least): <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 1013 <quote xml:lang="grc">νοῦν σχὲς ἀλλὰ τῷ χρόνῳ ποτέ</quote> : <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Trach. 320" ref="Soph. Trach. 320"> <title>Tr.</title> 320</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἴπ’, ὦ τάλαιν’, ἀλλ’ ἡμίν</quote> </cit> : <cit> <bibl n="Dem. 3.33" ref="Dem. 3.33">Dem. or. 3 § 33</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐὰν οὖν ἀλλὰ νῦν γ’ ἔτι...ἐθελήσητε</quote> </cit>. </gloss>

data.quote:
ἐὰν οὖν ἀλλὰ νῦν γ’
									ἔτι...ἐθελήσητε

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δυσπρόσοιστον_1277"> = <quote xml:lang="grc">χαλεπὸν προσφέρεσθαι</quote> (midd.), hard for one to hold intercourse with. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Lysis 223b" ref="Plat. Lysis 223b"> Plat. <title>Lys.</title> 223 B</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐδόκουν ἡμῖν...ἄποροι εἶναι προσφέρεσθαι</quote> </cit>, they <quote xml:lang="eng">"seemed to us hard to deal with."</quote> The epithet refers to his <emph>sullen silence</emph> , and is defined by <foreign xml:lang="grc"> ἀπροσήγορον</foreign> . In <cit> <bibl n="Eur. IA 345" ref="Eur. IA 345"> Eur. <title>I. A.</title> 345</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">δυσπρόσιτος ἔσω τε κλῄθρων σπάνιος</quote> </cit>, <cit> <bibl n="Thuc. 1.130" ref="Thuc. 1.130">Thuc. 1.130</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">δυσπρόσοδον... αὑτὸν παρεῖχε</quote> </cit>, the sense is <quote xml:lang="eng"> "hard of <emph>access</emph> ,"</quote> <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>living in a haughty seclusion. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Trach. 1093" ref="Soph. Trach. 1093"> <title>Tr.</title> 1093</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">λέοντ’, ἄπλατον θρέμμα κἀπροσήγορον</quote> </cit>(the Nemean lion). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="στόμα_1277">: for the periphrasis cp. 603. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὡς_με_1278">. The objection to <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἀφῇ γε</foreign> is that a second <foreign xml:lang="grc">γε</foreign> (though possible, see on <ref target="cl387">387</ref> ) is here weak after <foreign xml:lang="grc">θεοῦ γε</foreign> . As to its place after <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἀφῇ</foreign> , that is paralleled by 1409. On the other hand a repeated <foreign xml:lang="grc">με</foreign> , in the utterance of impassioned entreaty, may be defended by 1407 ff. <quote xml:lang="grc">μή τοί με... μή μ’ ἀτιμάσητέ γε</quote> : cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Trach. 218" ref="Soph. Trach. 218"> <title>Tr.</title> 218</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἰδού μ’ ἀναταράσσει | εὐοῖ μ’ ὁ κισσός</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Phoen. 497" ref="Eur. Phoen. 497"> Eur. <title>Phoen.</title> 497</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="trimeter"> <l>ἐμοὶ μέν, εἰ καὶ μὴ καθ’ Ἑλλήνων χθόνα</l> <l>τεθράμμεθ’, ἀλλ’ οὖν ξυνετά μοι δοκεῖς λέγειν</l> </lg> </quote> </cit> . </gloss>

data.quote:
ἰδού μ’ ἀναταράσσει | εὐοῖ μ’ ὁ
									κισσός

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀφῆται_1278">(Blaydes) would mean <quote xml:lang="eng"> " <emph>let go hold of</emph> "</quote>(with gen., <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 1521" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg004:1521"> <title>O. T.</title> 1521</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τέκνων δ’ ἀφοῦ</quote> </cit>), not <quote xml:lang="eng"> " <emph>dismiss.</emph> "</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοῦ_γε_1278">, Poseidon (1158): <foreign xml:lang="grc">γε</foreign> emphasises the whole phrase, to which <quote xml:lang="grc">ὄντα</quote> would usu. be added (cp. 83): cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 929" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg004:929"> <title>O. T.</title> 929</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὀλβία … | γένοιτ;, ἐκείνου γ’ οὖσα παντελὴς δάμαρ.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

data.quote:
ὀλβία … | γένοιτ;, ἐκείνου γ’ οὖσα
									παντελὴς δάμαρ.

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="προστάτην_1278">: cp. on <ref target="cl1171">1171</ref> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὕτως_1278">, so contemptuously: cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 256"> <title>O. T.</title> 256</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Ant. 315"> <title>Ant.</title> 315</ref>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="χρείᾳ_1280">, a causal (rather than modal) dat., cp. 333 <quote xml:lang="grc">πόθοισι</quote> : <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Phil. 162" ref="Soph. Phil. 162"> <title>Ph.</title> 162</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">φορβῆς χρείᾳ | στίβον ὀγμεύει</quote> </cit> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὰ_ῥήματα_1281"> , <quote xml:lang="eng">"the many words"</quote> (of any given long speech), with gnomic aor., as <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 1214" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg007:1214">1214</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">αἱ μακραὶ | ἁμέραι κατέθεντο</quote> </cit>. Distinguish 87 <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰ πολλ’ ἐκεῖνα κακά</quote> , <quote xml:lang="eng"> " <emph>those</emph> many,"</quote>in a definite allusion. ( <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰ πολλὰ</quote> must not be taken separately as adv., <quote xml:lang="eng">"oft."</quote> ) </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἢ_τι_1281"> etc.: <quote xml:lang="eng">"by giving some pleasure,—or by some utterance of indignation, or of pity."</quote> Not, <quote xml:lang="eng"> "by <emph>exciting</emph> some indignation or some pity."</quote>Neither <foreign xml:lang="grc">δυσχεραίνειν</foreign> nor <foreign xml:lang="grc">κατοικτίζειν</foreign> is ever causative in classical Greek. In <cit> <bibl n="Eur. IA 686" ref="Eur. IA 686"> Eur. <title>I. A.</title> 686</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">κατῳκτίσθην</quote> </cit>is not, <quote xml:lang="eng">"I was moved to pity,"</quote> but <quote xml:lang="eng">"I bewailed myself,"</quote> the pass. aor. in midd. sense, as often. The emotion of the speaker will awaken a response in the hearer.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀφωνήτοις_1283">in act. sense: so <cit> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀναύδατος</quote> <bibl n="Soph. Trach. 968" ref="Soph. Trach. 968"> ( <title>Tr.</title> 968)</bibl> </cit> , <cit> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄφθεγκτος</quote> <bibl n="Aesch. Eum. 245" ref="Aesch. Eum. 245"> (Aesch. <title>Eum.</title> 245)</bibl> </cit>; cp. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀφόβητος</quote> , "fearless," <ref n="Soph. OT 885"> <title>O. T.</title> 885</ref>: and n. above <ref target="cl1031">on 1031</ref> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐξηγεῖ_1284">, <foreign xml:lang="lat">praecipis</foreign> (but otherwise in 1520). Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 320" ref="Soph. Aj. 320"> <title>Ai.</title> 320</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐξηγεῖτ’</quote> </cit> , <quote xml:lang="eng">"he ever taught"</quote> (Tecmessa recalling the utterances of Ajax). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ποιούμενος_ᾀρωγόν_1285"> , <quote xml:lang="eng">"making my helper,"</quote> <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> appealing to his name: cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 240"> <title>O. T.</title> 240</ref>( <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸν ἄνδρα</quote> ) <quote xml:lang="grc">κοινὸν ποεῖσθαι</quote> , <quote xml:lang="eng">"make him partner"</quote> : Theognis 113 <quote xml:lang="grc">μήποτε τὸν κακὸν ἄνδρα φίλον ποιεῖσθαι ἑταῖρον.</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὧδε_1285">with <foreign xml:lang="grc">μολεῖν</foreign> (epex. inf.), <quote xml:lang="eng">"that I should come hither"</quote> : cp. 1251.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀνέστησεν_1285">: cp. 276. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λέξαι_τ_1288">: see on <ref target="cl189">190</ref> . </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐξόδῳ_1288">: see 1165. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="βουλήσομαι_1289"> , <quote xml:lang="eng">"I shall wish"</quote> ( <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> until the hoped-for fulfilment of the wish has been attained). So <ref n="Soph. OT 1077"> <title>O. T.</title> 1077</ref>(where see n.), <ref n="Soph. Aj. 681"> <title>Ai.</title> 681</ref>, etc. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θέλω_λέξαι_1291">, those things <emph>for which</emph> I came; cognate acc. of errand, as <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 1005" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg004:1005"> <title>O. T.</title> 1005</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῦτ’ ἀφικόμην</quote> </cit> : <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Prot. 310E" ref="Plat. Prot. 310E"> Plat. <title>Prot.</title> 310E</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐτὰ ταῦτα καὶ νῦν ἥκω</quote> </cit>. See n. on <ref n="Soph. OT 788"> <title>O. T.</title> 788</ref>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πανάρχοις_1293">is fitting, since each brother claimed the sole power (373). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γεραίτερος_1293">, (Jacobs and Nauck,) for <foreign xml:lang="grc">γεραιτέρᾳ</foreign> , has been received by several edd., including Dindorf and Wecklein. The common idiom doubtless favours it; yet the phrase, <quote xml:lang="eng"> "brought into being by the <emph>elder birth</emph> ,"</quote>is surely intelligible as a poetical fusion of <quote xml:lang="grc">γονῇ προτέρᾳ πεφυκώς</quote> with <quote xml:lang="grc">γεραίτερος πεφυκώς</quote> .</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀνθ_ὧν_1295">, <quote xml:lang="eng">"wherefore"</quote> : cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 264"> <title>O. T.</title> 264</ref>n.—In <foreign xml:lang="grc"> Ἐτεοκλῆς</foreign> the <foreign xml:lang="grc">ο</foreign> might be either long or short (cp. on <ref target="cl1">1</ref> ): elsewhere Soph. has the name only in <ref n="Soph. Ant. 23"> <title>Ant.</title> 23</ref>, 194 ( <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἐτεοκλέα</quote> beginning both verses). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λόγῳ_1296">, in an argument upon the claim, before a competent tribunal. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἰς_ἔλεγχον_1296">: cp. 835 <quote xml:lang="grc">τάχ’ εἰς βάσανον εἶ χερῶν.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="χειρὸς_ἔργου_1296">is a species of hendiadys,—the practical test of single combat (cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 814" ref="Soph. Aj. 814"> <title>Ai.</title> 814</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τάχος γὰρ ἔργου καὶ ποδῶν ἅμ’ ἕψεται</quote> </cit>). We cannot distinguish <foreign xml:lang="grc">χειρός</foreign> , as the duel, from <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἔργου</foreign> , as a trial of strength between adherents, since Thebes was with Eteocles. Hermann's <foreign xml:lang="grc">οὐδ’</foreign> (for the MS. <foreign xml:lang="grc"> οὔτ’</foreign> ), before <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἔργου</foreign> , is necessary, unless we suppose an <quote xml:lang="grc">οὔτε</quote> <emph> understood</emph> before <foreign xml:lang="grc">χειρός</foreign> : cp. on <ref n="Soph. OT 236"> <title>O. T.</title> 236</ref>ff. </gloss>

data.quote:
τάχος γὰρ ἔργου καὶ ποδῶν ἅμ’
									ἕψεται

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μάλιστα_μὲν_1298"> with <foreign xml:lang="grc">λέγω</foreign> , not with <foreign xml:lang="grc">τὴν σὴν Ἐρ</foreign> .: "and of these things I hold (as the most probable account) that the curse on thy race is the cause;— then from seers also I hear in this sense." Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 932" ref="Soph. El. 932"> <title>El.</title> 932</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="trimeter"> <l>οἶμαι μάλιστ’ ἔγωγε τοῦ τεθνηκότος</l> <l>μνημεἶ Ὀρέστου ταῦτα προσθεῖναί τινα</l> </lg> </quote> </cit> , <quote xml:lang="eng"> "I think it <emph>most likely</emph> that..."</quote>: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Phil. 617" ref="Soph. Phil. 617"> <title>Ph.</title> 617 </bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οἴοιτο μὲν μάλισθ’ ἑκούσιον λαβών</quote> </cit>, <quote xml:lang="eng">"he thought it most likely that he (could bring him) without compulsion."</quote> The <foreign xml:lang="grc">μέν</foreign> after <foreign xml:lang="grc">μάλιστα</foreign> opposes <emph>this</emph> view, the most likely, to <emph>other</emph> views (not stated) which are possible, though less probable: <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἔπειτα</foreign> is not opposed to <foreign xml:lang="grc">μέν</foreign> , but introduces the fact which confirms his conjecture. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὴν_Ἐρινὺν_1298">, the Fury who pursues thee and thy race, the family curse, 369 <quote xml:lang="grc">τὴν πάλαι γένους φθοράν</quote> (cp. 965), as Oed. himself called his sons' strife <quote xml:lang="grc">πεπρωμένην</quote> (421). Not, <quote xml:lang="eng"> " <emph>thy curse</emph> on thy sons"</quote>: Polyneices knows nothing of the imprecation uttered at 421 ff. It is a distinctive point in the Sophoclean treatment of the story that the curse of Oed. on his sons comes <emph>after</emph> the outbreak of war between them, not <emph>before</emph> it, as with Aesch. and Eur.: see <ref target="cis6">Introd.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μάντεων_1298">, at Argos, probably alluding to Amphiaraus (1313). This Argive utterance as to the <emph>cause</emph> of the brothers' strife may be conceived as a part of the oracles noticed at 1331, which also concerned the <emph> issue.</emph> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸ_Δωρικόν_1301">, simply as being in the <quote xml:lang="grc">Δωρίδι νάσῳ Πέλοπος</quote> (see on <ref target="cl695">695</ref> ); cp. on <ref target="cl378">378</ref> ( <quote xml:lang="grc">προσλαμβάνει</quote> ).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γῆς_Ἀπίας_1303">, a name for the Peloponnesus ( <ref n="Aesch. Ag. 256"> Aesch. <title> Ag.</title> 256</ref>), from the mythical king <quote xml:lang="grc"> Ἆπις</quote> , who crossed over from Naupactus, <quote xml:lang="eng">"before Pelops had come to Olympia,"</quote> as Paus. says, and purged the land of monsters. The Sicyon myth made him son of Telchin ( <ref n="Paus. 2.5.7">Paus. 2.5.7</ref> ); Aesch. calls him <quote xml:lang="grc">ἰατρόμαντις παῖς Ἀπόλλωνος</quote> ( <title>Suppl.</title> 263). Distinguish 1685 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπίαν γᾶν</quote> , <quote xml:lang="eng">"a far land"</quote> ( <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπό</quote> ). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τετίμηνται_1304">: for the pf., expressing <emph>fixed</emph> repute, cp. on <ref target="cl186">186</ref> , 1004: <cit> <bibl n="Thuc. 2.45" ref="Thuc. 2.45">Thuc. 2.45</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">φθόνος γὰρ τοῖς ζῶσι πρὸς τὸ ἀντίπαλον, τὸ δὲ μὴ ἐμποδὼν ἀνανταγωνίστῳ εὐνοίᾳ τετίμηται</quote> </cit>, is in permanent honour. </gloss>

data.quote:
φθόνος γὰρ τοῖς ζῶσι πρὸς τὸ
									ἀντίπαλον, τὸ δὲ μὴ ἐμποδὼν ἀνανταγωνίστῳ εὐνοίᾳ
									τετίμηται

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δορί_1304">: see on <ref target="cl620">620</ref> . This was the ordinary form, <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> the form used in prose, as by Thuc. In the iambic verse of tragedy it is only once necessary ( <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Hec. 5" ref="Eur. Hec. 5"> Eur. <title>Hec.</title> 5</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">κίνδυνος ἔσχε δορὶ πεσεῖν Ἑλληνικῷ</quote> </cit>). In lyrics it was freely used by Aesch. and Eur. But neither the iambics nor the lyrics of Soph. anywhere require it, while they thrice require <foreign xml:lang="grc">δόρει</foreign> . The question, then, is: Are we to assume that Soph. never used <foreign xml:lang="grc">δορί</foreign> ? As the MSS. give that form even where <foreign xml:lang="grc">δόρει</foreign> is necessary, their evidence is indecisive. On general grounds it is more probable that Soph. should have admitted both forms. This was Hermann's view; among recent editors, Bellermann supports it. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸν_στόλον_1305"> , <quote xml:lang="eng">"the expedition with seven bodies of spearmen"</quote> ; <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> the compound adj. is equivalent to two separate epithets, <quote xml:lang="eng"> "sevenfold,"</quote> and <quote xml:lang="eng">"armed with spears"</quote> : cp. on <ref target="cl17">17</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πυκνόπτεροι</quote> . The boldness of the phrase consists in the collective sing. <quote xml:lang="grc">στόλον</quote> being used instead of a plur. like <quote xml:lang="grc">τάξεις</quote> (1311). Not, <quote xml:lang="eng">"under the seven spears of seven leaders,"</quote> as if the <quote xml:lang="grc">λόγχη</quote> of each leader was an ensign. Cp. on <ref target="cl1311">1311</ref> . The art. <foreign xml:lang="grc">τόν</foreign> , because the expedition is no longer a project, but a fact (1312). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πανδίκως_1306">, as asserting just claims in fair fight. The device on the shield of the Aeschylean Polyneices is <quote xml:lang="grc">Δίκη</quote> leading a man in golden armour, with the words, <cit> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="trimeter"> <l>κατάξω δ’ ἄνδρα τόνδε, καὶ πόλιν</l> <l>ἕξει πατρῴων δωμάτων τ’ ἐπιστροφάς</l> </lg> </quote> <bibl n="Aesch. Seven 647" ref="Aesch. Seven 647">( <title>Theb.</title> 647).</bibl> </cit> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοὺς_ἐκπρ_1306">., Eteocles: for pl., cp. 148. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἶεν_1308">marks a pause after a statement, before the speaker proceeds to comment or argument: so <ref n="Soph. El. 534"> <title>El.</title> 534</ref>: <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Med. 386" ref="Eur. Med. 386"> Eur. <title>Med.</title> 386</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="trimeter"> <l>εἶεν·</l> <l>καὶ δὴ τεθνᾶσι· τίς με δέξεται πόλις;</l> </lg> </quote> </cit> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αὐτός_τ_1310">: cp. 462. The genitives are simply subjective, <quote xml:lang="eng">"prayers of mine and of theirs,"</quote> <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> made by us (cp. 1326), rather than gen. of connection, <quote xml:lang="eng">"about myself,"</quote> etc. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τάξεσιν_λόγχαις_1311">. The <quote xml:lang="eng">"allies"</quote> are the chieftains. They have marched <quote xml:lang="eng">"with their seven hosts and their seven spears,"</quote> because each, carrying his spear, rides at the head of his own body of spearmen. Polyneices, who is one of the seven, thinks of himself for the moment as present with his comrades in arms. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δορυσσοῦς_1313"> = <quote xml:lang="grc">δορυσσόος</quote> , a word used also by Hes. and Aesch. (not Hom.), and usu. rendered <quote xml:lang="eng"> "spear- <emph>brandishing.</emph> "</quote>But this seems to confuse <quote xml:lang="grc">σεύω</quote> with <quote xml:lang="grc">σείω</quote> . On the analogy of the Homeric <quote xml:lang="grc">λαοσσόος</quote> , <quote xml:lang="eng">"urging on the host"</quote> (epith. of Ares etc.), and the Pindaric <quote xml:lang="grc">ἱπποσόας</quote> , <quote xml:lang="eng">"steed-urging,"</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δορυσσόος_1313">should mean rather <quote xml:lang="eng"> "spear <emph>hurling</emph> "</quote> (cp. <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Il. 11.147" ref="Hom. Il. 11.147"> <title>Il.</title> 11.147</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅλμον δ’ ὣς ἔσσευε κυλίνδεσθαι</quote> </cit> , <emph>sent</emph> him rolling like a ball of stone). <quote xml:lang="eng"> " <title>Charging with</title> the spear"</quote>is less suitable, since the epic <quote xml:lang="grc">δόρυ</quote> is rather a missile than a cavalry-lance. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Ἀμφιάρεως_1313"> ( <foreign xml:lang="meter">-uu-</foreign> , cp. on <ref target="cl1">1</ref> ), son of Oecles, <quote xml:lang="eng">"at once the Achilles and the Calchas of the war"</quote> (as Schneidewin says), is the most pathetic figure of the legend. He foresees the issue; but his wife Eriphylè, the sister of Adrastus, persuades him to go (having been bribed by Polyneices with Harmonia's necklace); and when all the chiefs save Adrastus have fallen, the Theban soil opens, and swallows up Amphiaraus and his chariot: <ref n="Soph. El. 837"> <title>El.</title> 837</ref>: <ref n="Pind. N. 9"> Pind. <title> Nem.</title> 9.24</ref>: <ref n="Pind. N. 10">10. 8.</ref> Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Pind. O. 6" ref="Pind. O. 6"> <title>Ol.</title> 6. 15</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="lyric"> <l> <note anchored="true" place="inline" resp="RCJ" xml:lang="eng">(adrastus speaking) </note>ποθέω στρατιᾶς ὀφθαλμὸν ἐμᾶς,</l> <l>ἀμφότερον μάντιν τ’ ἀγαθὸν καὶ δουρὶ μάρνασθαι</l> </lg> </quote> </cit>. Aesch. makes him the type of ill-fated virtue ( <ref n="Aesch. Seven 597"> <title>Theb.</title> 597</ref>). In contrast with the <quote xml:lang="grc">ὕβρις</quote> of the other chiefs, his <quote xml:lang="grc">σωφροσύνη</quote> is marked by the absence of any device ( <quote xml:lang="grc">σῆμα</quote> ) on his shield ( <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 591, <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Phoen. 1112" ref="Eur. Phoen. 1112"> Eur. <title>Phoen.</title> 1112</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄσημ’ ὅπλα</quote> </cit>). The same Greek feeling for a tragic prescience is seen in the story so beautifully told by Herod. (9. 16) of the Persian guest at the banquet of Attagīnus. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὰ_δέ_1313">: the art. is to be repeated with the second clause. For the epanaphora cp. 5: <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Il. 1.258" ref="Hom. Il. 1.258"> <title>Il.</title> 1.258</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οἳ περὶ μὲν βουλὴν Δαναῶν, περὶ δ’ ἐστὲ μάχεσθαι</quote> </cit>. </gloss>

data.quote:
οἳ περὶ μὲν βουλὴν Δαναῶν, περὶ δ’
									ἐστὲ μάχεσθαι

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οἰωνῶν_ὁδοῖς_1313">, in respect to the paths of birds of omen, <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> in applying the rules of augury to their flights. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Il. 12.237" ref="Hom. Il. 12.237"> <title>Il.</title> 12.237</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="hexameter"> <l>τύνη δ’ οἰωνοῖσι τανυπτερύγεσσι κελεύεις</l> <l>πείθεσθαι· τῶν οὔ τι μετατρέπω οὐδ’ ἀλεγίζω,</l> <l>εἴτ’ ἐπὶ δεξἴ ἴωσι</l> </lg> </quote> </cit>, etc. Quite different is <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 311" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg004:311"> <title>O. T.</title> 311</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄλλην μαντικῆς...ὁδόν</quote> </cit>, some other <emph>way</emph> of divination (as distinct from augury). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Τυδεύς_1316">: cp. <ref n="Aesch. Seven 377"> Aesch. <title>Th.</title> 377 ff.</ref> : <ref n="Eur. Phoen. 1120"> Eur. <title>Phoen.</title> 1120</ref> ff., <ref n="Eur. Supp. 901"> <title>Suppl.</title> 901 ff.</ref> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Ἐτέοκλος_1316"> : <ref n="Aesch. Seven 457"> Aesch. <title>Th.</title> 457 ff.</ref> : <ref n="Eur. Supp. 872"> Eur. <title>Suppl.</title> 872</ref> ff.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Ἱππομέδοντ_1316"> : <ref n="Aesch. Seven 486"> Aesch. <title>Th.</title> 486 ff.</ref> : <ref n="Eur. Phoen. 1113"> Eur. <title>Phoen.</title> 1113</ref> ff. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κατασκαφῇ_πυρί_1318"> = <quote xml:lang="eng">"to destroy it with fire, in such a manner as to raze it to the ground"</quote> : </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πυρί_1318"> is instrum. dat., and coheres closely with the verb; </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κατασκαφῇ_1318"> is dat. of manner, but with proleptic force, like <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 51" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg004:51"> <title>O. T.</title> 51</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλ’ ἀσφαλείᾳ τήνδ’ ἀνόρθωσον πόλιν</quote> </cit>, = <quote xml:lang="grc">ὥστε ἀσφαλῆ εἶναι.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Καπανεὺς_1318">is the giant in whom the <quote xml:lang="grc">ὕβρις</quote> of the assailants takes its most daring and impious form, the Goliath or Mezentius of the story: cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 133"> <title>Ant.</title> 133</ref>, <ref n="Aesch. Seven 422"> Aesch. <title> Th.</title> 422 ff</ref>. In <ref n="Eur. Phoen. 1228"> <title>Ph.</title> 1128</ref>Eur. follows this conception; but in <title> Suppl.</title> 861 ff. he presents Capaneus in a totally new light, as no less modest than trusty. That whole passage of the <title>Supplices</title> ,—in which Eur. seeks to individualise some of these champions more closely,—is curious and characteristic. </gloss>
