data.quote:
μἴ ἡμέρα | τὰ μὲν καθεῖλεν ὑψόθεν, τὰ
									δ’ ἦρ’ ἄνω.

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὁρᾷ_1453_2">, as <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Phil. 843" ref="Soph. Phil. 843"> <title>Ph.</title> 843</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τάδε μὲν θεὸς ὄψεται</quote> </cit> , <quote xml:lang="eng">"will look to"</quote> this. Time is the vigilant minister of Fate. The mighty are humbled (as the Labdacidae have been); the lowly, again, are exalted. The last words contain an unconscious hint that the sufferings of Oedipus are well-nigh finished, and that honour is coming to him. At that instant, the thunder is heard.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔκτυπεν_1456">, the epic aor., only here in Attic: elsewhere <quote xml:lang="grc"> ἐκτύπησα</quote> . </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πῶς_ἄν_1457">: cp. on <ref target="cl1100">1100</ref> . </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἴ_ἔντοπος_1457">,—other, that is, than the Chorus: some one who could be sent on the errand. Cp. 70, 297. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δεῦρο_πόροι_1458">, cause him to come hither. <quote xml:lang="grc">πορεῖν</quote> , to give, is never found as= <quote xml:lang="grc">πορεύειν</quote> , to make to come (1476): and here the phrase is strictly a compressed one, <quote xml:lang="eng">"enable me to speak to him, (by bringing him) hither."</quote> But the associations of <quote xml:lang="grc">πόρος</quote> and <quote xml:lang="grc">πορεύειν</quote> have doubtless influenced it. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 1267" ref="Soph. El. 1267"> <title>El.</title> 1267</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="lyric"> <l>εἴ σε θεὸς ἐπόρισεν</l> <l>ἁμέτερα πρὸς μέλαθρα</l> </lg> </quote> </cit> .—Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Pind. P. 3" ref="Pind. P. 3"> Pind. <title>Pyth.</title> 3.45</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">καί ῥά νιν Μάγνητι φέρων πόρε Κενταύρῳ διδάξαι</quote> </cit>( <quote xml:lang="eng">"gave,"</quote> with the like notion as here of bringing to). </gloss>

data.quote:
καί ῥά νιν Μάγνητι φέρων πόρε Κενταύρῳ
									διδάξαι

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πάντ_1458">, adv.: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 911" ref="Soph. Aj. 911"> <title>Ai.</title> 911</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁ πάντα κωφός</quote> </cit> : <ref n="Soph. OT 475"> <title>O. T.</title> 475</ref>n. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τί_δ_1459">, after the voc.: cp. 507. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τἀξίωμ_1459">: see on <ref target="cl1451">1451</ref> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πτερωτὸς_1460"> : <cit> <bibl n="Verg. A. 5.319" ref="Verg. A. 5.319"> Verg. <title>Aen.</title> 5. 319</bibl> <quote xml:lang="lat">et ventis et fulminis ocior alis.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

data.quote:
et ventis et fulminis ocior
									alis.

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄξεται_1460">: the fut. midd. here merely= <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄξει</quote> , for <quote xml:lang="eng">"cause me to be led"</quote> would be strained. In <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Od. 21.322" ref="Hom. Od. 21.322"> <title>Od.</title> 21.322</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὔ τί σε τόνδ’ ἄξεσθαι</quote> </cit> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὀϊόμεθ’</quote>( <quote xml:lang="eng">"wed thee"</quote> ), the midd. has its proper special force: cp. <foreign xml:lang="lat"> ib.</foreign> 214. In <ref n="Eur. Hipp. 625"> Eur. <title>Hipp.</title> 625</ref>it is doubtful. In <ref n="Aesch. Ag. 1632"> Aesch. <title> Ag.</title> 1632</ref>etc. it is passive. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐρείπεται_1462"> , <foreign xml:lang="lat">ruit</foreign> (cp. <ref n="Soph. Aj. 309"> <title>Ai.</title> 309</ref>n.); the very sky seems to come down with the crash: so Valerius Flaccus 8. 334 <quote xml:lang="lat">ruina poli</quote> of thunder. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κτύπος_διόβολος_1462">, the noise of a bolt hurled by Zeus: cp. on <ref target="cl711">710</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">αὔχημα ...εὔιππον.</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄκραν_1462">, the tips, not the roots: cp. 1624. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔπτηξα_1466">, aor. referring to a moment just past, where we should ordinarily use the pres.: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 693" ref="Soph. Aj. 693"> <title>Ai.</title> 693</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔφριξ’ ἔρωτι</quote> </cit>. Cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 337"> <title>O. T.</title> 337</ref>n. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θυμόν_1466">, acc. of part affected. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐρανία_1466">: schol. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀντὶ τοῦ ταχεῖα</quote> . This seems to be merely a marginal note by the <quote xml:lang="eng">"diorthotes"</quote> of L, not one of the ancient scholia which he copied into the MS.: and I doubt whether it points to a different reading. Rather, probably, it means that the writer took <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐρανία</quote> as= <quote xml:lang="eng">"rushing from the sky."</quote> Heinrich Schmidt defends <foreign xml:lang="grc">οὐρανία</foreign> as <foreign xml:lang="meter">-u-</foreign> : others deny that such a synizesis is possible. But in <ref n="Aesch. Seven 288"> Aesch. <title>Th.</title> 288</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">καρδίας</quote>answers metrically to <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐχθροῖς</quote> ( <ref n="Aesch. Seven 305">305</ref> ); in his <ref n="Aesch. Supp. 71"> <title>Suppl.</title> 71</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">καρδίαν</quote>=the last two syllables of <quote xml:lang="grc">στυγοῦντες</quote> ( <ref n="Aesch. Supp. 80">80</ref> ); and <ref n="Aesch. Supp. 799"> <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 799</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">καρδίας</quote>=the first two of <quote xml:lang="grc">γαιάοχε</quote> ( <ref n="Aesch. Supp. 816">816</ref> ). Dindorf cuts the knot in all these places by adopting <quote xml:lang="grc">κάρζα</quote> , an Aeolic form mentioned in <title>Etym. M.</title> 407. 21,—surely a most improbable remedy. It is more reasonable to infer that so easy a synizesis as that of <foreign xml:lang="grc">ια</foreign> was sometimes allowed in the lyrics of Attic drama. Elmsley's <foreign xml:lang="grc">οὐρία</foreign> (suggested by the schol.'s <quote xml:lang="grc">ταχεῖα</quote> ) is unsuitable here. From Hesych. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄργιος· λευκός, ταχύς</quote> , Wecklein suggests <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἀργία</foreign> , comparing the Homeric <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀργῆτα κεραυνόν</quote> . If any change were needed, I should prefer <foreign xml:lang="grc"> οὐρανῷ</foreign> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τί_τέλος_1468"> ; <quote xml:lang="eng">"what end (event) will (the lightning) bring forth?"</quote> For <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀφιέναι</quote> as= <quote xml:lang="eng">"to emit,"</quote> <quote xml:lang="eng">"produce from one's self,"</quote> cp. Arist. <title>Hist. An.</title> 6. 14 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀφιᾶσι τὸ κύημα,...τῆς θηλείας ἀφιείσης τὸ ᾠόν</quote> . This use, which was common, suggests how the word might be figuratively said of the storm <emph>giving birth</emph> to some disastrous issue. We need not, then, seek a correction (as <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐφήσει</quote> or <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐφήξει</quote> ).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μὰν_1468">, <quote xml:lang="eng">"verily,"</quote> here nearly=an exclamation, such as <quote xml:lang="eng">"ah!"</quote> Cp. on <ref target="cl182">182</ref> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀφορμᾷ_1470">, <foreign xml:lang="lat">sc.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἡ ἀστραπή</quote> , <quote xml:lang="eng">"rushes forth"</quote> (from the sky),—better here than the <foreign xml:lang="lat">v.l.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐφορμᾷ.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ξυμφορᾶς_1470">, not definitely <quote xml:lang="eng">"misfortune,"</quote> but rather, more generally, <quote xml:lang="eng">"grave issue."</quote> The thought is merely that something momentous always follows such a storm. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 44" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg004:44"> <title>O. T.</title> 44</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰς ξυμφορὰς ...τῶν βουλευμάτων</quote> </cit>, the issues or effects of counsels. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὦ_αἰθήρ_1471">is a cry, rather than an address like <foreign xml:lang="grc">ὦ Ζεῦ</foreign> : yet in <ref n="Aesch. PB 88"> Aesch. <title>PV</title> 88</ref>, in a direct address, we have <quote xml:lang="grc">ὦ δῖος αἰθήρ</quote> , followed by the voc. <quote xml:lang="grc">παμμῆτόρ τε γῆ</quote> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἥκει_ἀνδρί_1472">. We may render the prep. <quote xml:lang="eng">"upon"</quote> me, but properly it is rather <quote xml:lang="eng">"against"</quote> me;—the doom, from which there is no <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀποστροφή</quote> , advances to take him. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 509" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg004:509"> <title>O. T.</title> 509</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπ’ αὐτῷ πτερόεσσ’ ἦλθε κόρα</quote> </cit>. (Not, <quote xml:lang="eng">"in my case,"</quote> as <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 829.) The conjecture <foreign xml:lang="grc">τῷδε τἀνδρὶ</foreign> is needless, and impairs the solemnity of the words. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="συμβαλὼν_ἔχεις_1474">(cp. 817, 1140), hast inferred, a freq. sense of the act. in Attic: Her. in this sense prefers the midd. As <foreign xml:lang="grc">ὦ παῖδες</foreign> (1472) evidently means the daughters, this v. is rightly given to Antigone; but her question reminds us that she, and she only, had heard Oed. speak of the signs which should announce his end (95). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μοι_1475">, ethic dat., <quote xml:lang="eng">"I pray you"</quote> : cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 1512" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg004:1512"> <title>O. T.</title> 1512</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῦτ’ εὔχεσθέ μοι</quote> </cit> , <quote xml:lang="eng">"I would have this to be your prayer."</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔα_1477">is the cry of one startled by a sight or sound ( <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. PB 298" ref="Aesch. PB 298"> Aesch. <title>PV</title> 298</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔα· τί χρῆμα λεύσσω</quote> </cit>;): only here in Soph. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μάλ_αὖθις_1477"> , <quote xml:lang="eng">"again, and loudly"</quote> : <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 1410" ref="Soph. El. 1410"> <title>El.</title> 1410</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἰδοὺ μάλ’ αὖ θροεῖ τις.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀμφίσταται_1477">, because the peals of thunder, now at their loudest, seem to be around them on every side. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Od. 6.122" ref="Hom. Od. 6.122"> <title>Od.</title> 6.122</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὥστε με κουράων ἀμφήλυθε θῆλυς ἀϋτή</quote> </cit>: so <cit> <quote xml:lang="grc">περὶ...ἤλυθ’ ἰωὴ | φόρμιγγος</quote> <bibl n="Hom. Od. 17.261" ref="Hom. Od. 17.261">(17. 261)</bibl> </cit> , <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄσημα περιβαίνει βοῆς</quote> ( <ref n="Soph. Ant. 1209"> <title>Ant.</title> 1209</ref>): but the phrase here is more vigorous, suggesting the image of a threatening foe. </gloss>

data.quote:
ὥστε με κουράων ἀμφήλυθε θῆλυς
									ἀϋτή

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="διαπρύσιος_1477">, as with <quote xml:lang="grc">κέλαδος</quote> , <ref n="Eur. Hel. 1308"> Eur. <title>Hel.</title> 1308</ref>; <quote xml:lang="grc">ὀλολυγαί</quote> , <ref n="HH 4.19"> <title>Hom. Hymn.</title> 4.19</ref>: in Homer only as adv., <quote xml:lang="grc">ἤϋσεν δὲ διαπρύσιον</quote> ( <ref n="Hom. Il. 8.227"> <title>Il.</title> 8.227</ref>): properly, <quote xml:lang="eng">"going through"</quote> the ear, <quote xml:lang="eng">"piercing,"</quote> like <quote xml:lang="grc">τορός, διατόρος</quote> . </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὦ_δαίμων_1480"> cp. on <ref target="cl185">185</ref> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἵλαος_1480">( <foreign xml:lang="lat">sc.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἴσθι</quote> ), as usually in Homer, etc., though <quote xml:lang="grc">ἵλα—ος</quote> also occurs (as <ref n="Hom. Il. 1.583"> <title>Il.</title> 1.583</ref>, <title>Hymn.</title> 5. 204, <ref n="Hes. WD 340"> Hes. <title>Op.</title> 340</ref>, <ref n="Aesch. Eum. 1040"> Aesch. <title>Eum.</title> 1040</ref>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γᾷ_ματέρι_1481">, Attica: cp. 707 <quote xml:lang="grc">ματροπόλει τᾷδε</quote> . <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Rep. 414E" ref="Plat. Rep. 414E"> Plat. <title>Rep.</title> 414E</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">δεῖ ὡς περὶ μητρὸς καὶ τροφοῦ τῆς χώρας ἐν ᾗ εἰσὶ βουλεύεσθαι.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

data.quote:
δεῖ ὡς περὶ μητρὸς καὶ τροφοῦ τῆς
									χώρας ἐν ᾗ εἰσὶ βουλεύεσθαι.

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀφεγγὲς_1481">, gloomy as the thunder-cloud. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σοῦ_τύχοιμι_1482">is a certain correction. With <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐναισίῳ</quote> (or <quote xml:lang="grc">‐ου</quote> )... <foreign xml:lang="grc">συντύχοιμι</foreign> we must still <emph>understand</emph> <quote xml:lang="grc">σοί</quote> (or <quote xml:lang="grc">σοῦ</quote> ); for the version, <quote xml:lang="eng"> "may I meet with a righteous <emph>man</emph> ,"</quote>gives a sense which is intolerably weak here. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄλαστον_ἄνδρ_1482">, Oedipus. With Homer, this adj. is always the epithet of <quote xml:lang="grc">πένθος</quote> or <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄχος</quote> , except in <ref n="Hom. Il. 22.261"> <title>Il.</title> 22.261</ref>(Achilles), <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἕκτορ, μή μοι, ἄλαστε, συνημοσύνας ἀγόρευε</quote> , <quote xml:lang="eng"> " <title>Wretch</title> , prate not to me of covenants,"</quote>—usu. taken as= <quote xml:lang="eng">"thou whom I cannot forget (or forgive),"</quote> though others render <quote xml:lang="eng">"madman"</quote> (as if connecting the word with the rt of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλύω</quote> ). It is simplest to suppose that the epithet of the <emph>act</emph> (537, 1672) is transferred to the <emph>agent</emph> ,— the doer of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄλαστα</quote> being called <quote xml:lang="grc"> ἄλαστος</quote> in the general sense of <quote xml:lang="eng">"wretch,"</quote> <quote xml:lang="eng">"accursed one."</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἰδὼν_1482">, since, in the old Greek belief, even casual association with a polluted man was perilous: <cit> <bibl n="Antiph. 5.82" ref="Antiph. 5.82">Antiph. or. 5 § 82</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">πολλοὶ ἤδη ἄνθρωποι μὴ καθαροὶ χεῖρας ἢ ἄλλο τι μίασμα ἔχοντες συνεισβάντες εἰς τὸ πλοῖον συναπώλεσαν μετὰ τῆς αὑτῶν ψυχῆς τοὺς ὁσίως διακειμένους τὰ πρὸς τοὺς θεούς</quote> </cit>. Cp. <ref n="Aesch. Seven 597"> Aesch. <title>Th.</title> 597 ff.</ref> , <ref n="Eur. El. 1354"> Eur. <title>El.</title> 1354</ref>, <ref n="Xen. Cyrop. 8.1.25"> Xen. <title>Cyr.</title> 8.1.25</ref>, <ref n="Hor. Carm. 3.2.26"> Hor. <title>Carm.</title> 3. 2. 26.</ref> </gloss>

data.quote:
πολλοὶ ἤδη ἄνθρωποι μὴ καθαροὶ χεῖρας
									ἢ ἄλλο τι μίασμα ἔχοντες συνεισβάντες εἰς τὸ πλοῖον
									συναπώλεσαν μετὰ τῆς αὑτῶν ψυχῆς τοὺς ὁσίως διακειμένους
									τὰ πρὸς τοὺς θεούς

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀκερδῆ_μετάσχ_1484">, have for my portion an unprofitable recompense (in return for the sympathy shown to Oed.); cp. <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. PB 544" ref="Aesch. PB 544"> Aesch. <title>PV</title> 544</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄχαρις χάρις</quote> </cit> ( <quote xml:lang="eng">"a thankless favour"</quote> ), Soph. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 665" ref="Soph. Aj. 665"> <title>Ai.</title> 665</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄδωρα δῶρα</quote> </cit> . <cit> <bibl n="Pind. O. 1" ref="Pind. O. 1"> Pind. <title>Ol.</title> 1.54</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀκέρδεια</quote> </cit>=disaster (with a similar euphemism). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μετέχω_1484">takes (1) gen. of thing shared, (2) acc. of share, (3) dat. of partner: but when (3) is present, (2) is usu. absent, unless equality is affirmed or denied, as <cit> <bibl n="Xen. Cyrop. 7.2.28" ref="Xen. Cyrop. 7.2.28"> Xen. <title>Cyr.</title> 7.2.28</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">εὐφροσυνῶν πασῶν ἐμοὶ τὸ ἴσον μετεῖχε</quote> </cit>. <ref n="Xen. Hiero 2.7"> <title>Hiero</title> 2. 7</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τούτου</quote> ( <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῦ κακοῦ</quote> ) <quote xml:lang="grc">πλεῖστον μέρος οἱ τύραννοι μετέχουσιν</quote> . In <cit> <bibl n="Aristoph. Pl. 1144" ref="Aristoph. Pl. 1144"> Aristoph. <title>Pl.</title> 1144</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ γὰρ μετεῖχες τὰς ἴσας πληγὰς ἐμοί</quote> </cit>, the dat. depends on <quote xml:lang="grc">ἴσας</quote> , not on the verb: <quote xml:lang="eng">"you did not get for your share the same number of blows as I."</quote> (Cp. Dein. or. 1 § 54 <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ πέμπτον μέρος οὐ μετείληφε τῶν ψήφων</quote> .) The peculiarity here is only in the use of the acc. <emph> alone</emph> , without a gen. (as <quote xml:lang="grc">τῆς ἀρᾶς</quote> ). </gloss>

data.quote:
εὐφροσυνῶν πασῶν ἐμοὶ τὸ ἴσον
									μετεῖχε

οὐ γὰρ μετεῖχες τὰς ἴσας πληγὰς
									ἐμοί

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κιχήσεται_1487">with gen., on the analogy of <quote xml:lang="grc">τυγχάνειν</quote> . Elsewhere <quote xml:lang="grc">κιχάνω</quote> always governs acc. We might take <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐμψύχου … μου</quote> ( <foreign xml:lang="lat">sc.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὄντος</quote> , cp. 83) <foreign xml:lang="grc">καὶ κατορθοῦντος</foreign> as gen. absol., but this is less probable. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κατορθοῦντος_1487"> intrans., <quote xml:lang="grc">φρένα</quote> acc. of respect: cp. <cit> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐξ ὀρθῆς φρενός,</quote> <bibl n="Soph. OT 528" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg004:528"> <title>O. T.</title> 528</bibl> </cit>. The <emph>intrans.</emph> <quote xml:lang="grc">κατορθόω</quote> usu. = <quote xml:lang="eng">"to succeed"</quote> ( <cit> <bibl n="Thuc. 6.12" ref="Thuc. 6.12">Thuc. 6.12</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἢ κατορθώσαντας,...ἢ πταίσαντας</quote> </cit>), but also <quote xml:lang="eng">"to be right or correct,"</quote> as <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Laws 654C" ref="Plat. Laws 654C"> Plat. <title>Legg.</title> 654C</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὃς ἂν τῇ μὲν φωνῇ καὶ τῷ σώματι μὴ πάνυ δυνατὸς ᾖ κατορθοῦν</quote> </cit>(in song and dance). The <emph>transitive</emph> <quote xml:lang="grc"> κατορθόω</quote> = <quote xml:lang="eng">"to bring anything to a successful end"</quote> (though, in such cases, the acc. might often be one of respect, and the verb intrans.), or <quote xml:lang="eng">"to make one successful"</quote> ( <ref n="Soph. El. 416"> <title>El.</title> 416</ref>). </gloss>

data.quote:
ὃς ἂν τῇ μὲν φωνῇ καὶ τῷ σώματι μὴ
									πάνυ δυνατὸς ᾖ κατορθοῦν

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐμφῦναι_φρενί_1488">. Schol.: <quote xml:lang="grc">τί τὸ πιστὸν θέλεις ἐμβαλεῖν τῇ φρενὶ ἐκείνου, τῇ τοῦ Θησέως δηλονότι; ἀντὶ τοῦ, τί βούλει πιθανὸν ἀνακοινώσασθαι τῷ Θησεῖ</quote> ; This proves that <foreign xml:lang="grc">φρενί</foreign> is at least as old as the ancient scholia in L, and also that the schol. had either <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἐμφῦναι</foreign> , or, as Herm. infers from <quote xml:lang="grc"> ἐμβαλεῖν, <emph>ἐμφῦσαι</emph> </quote>. Many recent critics have held that <foreign xml:lang="grc">φρενί</foreign> has come in from 1487. So far as the mere <emph>repetition</emph> is an argument, we must be cautious in applying it: cp. 70 f., and n. on 554. The sense must be either:— <quote xml:lang="eng"> "And what is <emph>the pledge</emph> which thou wouldst have fixed (1) in <emph>his</emph> mind?"</quote>— <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="eng">"What is it that thou wouldst tell him in confidence, under his pledge of secrecy?"</quote> —or else (2) <quote xml:lang="eng"> "in <emph>thy</emph> mind?"</quote>— <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="eng">"what promise wouldst thou obtain from him before death?"</quote> Here (2) is recommended by the fact that the <quote xml:lang="grc">φρήν</quote> is then the same in both vv. Nor is the reply of Oed. (1489) inconsistent with it; since the fulfilment of his promise (580) to Theseus involves a pledge from Theseus to keep the secret (1530). It is, of course, possible that Soph. wrote <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἐμφῆναι ξένῳ</foreign> , or the like: but the vulgate is at least defensible. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τελεσφόρον_χάριν_1489">, a requital (1484) fraught with fulfilment (of my promise). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σφιν_1489">is most naturally taken here, with the schol., as= <quote xml:lang="grc"> αὐτῷ</quote> , seeing that vv. 1486 f. refer to Theseus alone; though it is tenable as= <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐτοῖς</quote> , <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> Theseus and his people. The evidence for <foreign xml:lang="grc">σφιν</foreign> as dat. sing. is slender; but in <cit> <bibl n="HH 19.19" ref="HH 19.19"> <title>Hom. Hymn.</title> 19.19</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">σὺν δέ σφιν</quote> </cit>ought to mean <quote xml:lang="grc">σὺν Πανί</quote> , and in <ref n="HH 30"> <title>Hymn.</title> 30. 9</ref>we have <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="hexameter"> <l>βρίθει μέν σφιν ἄρουρα φερέσβιος, ἠδὲ κατ’ ἀγροὺς</l> <l>κτήνεσιν εὐθηνεῖ, οἶκος δ’ ἐμπίπλαται ἐσθλῶν</l> </lg> </quote>, where <foreign xml:lang="grc">σφιν</foreign> should refer to <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁ δ’ ὄλβιος</quote> shortly before, and the subject to <quote xml:lang="grc">εὐθηνεῖ</quote> seems clearly to be <emph>the man</emph> , not <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄρουρα</quote> . As to <ref n="Aesch. Pers. 759"> Aesch. <title>Pers.</title> 759</ref>, it is a case exactly parallel with ours here: <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> <foreign xml:lang="grc">σφιν</foreign> would most naturally refer to Xerxes alone, but <emph>might</emph> refer to Xerxes and his advisers ( <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῖς προτρεψαμένοις</quote> schol.). In <ref n="Pind. P. 9"> Pind. <title> Pyth.</title> 9.116</ref>, again, <quote xml:lang="grc">σφιν</quote> might mean Antaeus and his family. Lycophron 1142 seems to have meant <quote xml:lang="grc">σφι</quote> for <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐτῷ</quote> , as the schol. thought. On the whole, it appears unsafe to deny that poetry sometimes admitted the use. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τυγχάνων_1489"> = <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅτε ἐτύγχανον</quote> ( <quote xml:lang="grc">ὧν ᾔτησα</quote> ), cp. 579 ff. The absol. use is made easier by <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνθ’ ὦν ἔπασχον εὖ</quote> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἴτ_ἱκοῦ_1491">. On this corrupt passage, see Appendix. Reading <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἄκρα | περὶ γύαλ’</foreign> for <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἄκραν | ἐπὶ γύαλον</foreign> , I take the sense to be: <quote xml:lang="eng"> " <emph>or if</emph> ( <foreign xml:lang="grc">εἴτ’</foreign> ), in the furthest recesses of the glade, for the honour of the Poseidonian sea-god, thou art hallowing his altar with sacrifice, (yet) come."</quote>The precinct of Poseidon at Colonus was large enough for an ecclesia to be held within it ( <ref n="Thuc. 8.67">Thuc. 8.67</ref> ). It included the <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄλσος</quote> and <quote xml:lang="grc">ναός</quote> mentioned by <ref n="Paus. 1.30.4">Paus. I. 30 § 4</ref> . (See Introd.) The word <quote xml:lang="grc">γύαλον</quote> , <quote xml:lang="eng">"a hollow,"</quote> was oft. used in the plur. of hollow <emph>ground</emph> , valleys, or dells: cp. <ref n="Aesch. Supp. 550"> Aesch. <title>Supp.</title> 550</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="lyric"> <l>Λύδιά τ’ ἄγ γύαλα</l> <l>καὶ δῑ ὀρῶν Κιλίκων</l> </lg> </quote>. It would apply to the depressions between the gentle eminences of this <quote xml:lang="grc">στερνούχου χθονός</quote> (691),—as <foreign xml:lang="lat">e.g.</foreign> between the two neighbouring knolls at Colonus (cp. 1600). <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἄκρα περὶ γύαλα</foreign> means that the altar of Poseidon is in the part of the large <quote xml:lang="grc">τέμενος</quote> furthest from the Chorus. When Theseus left the scene (1210), his purpose was to send the suppliant Polyneices from this same altar to Oedipus (cp. 1349). The Chorus surmise that Theseus may have stayed at the altar to complete his interrupted sacrifice (888). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="βούθυτον_1491">proleptic with <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἁγίζων</foreign> ; to sacrifice on the altar <emph>is</emph> to <quote xml:lang="eng">"hallow"</quote> it. Cp. <ref n="Aristoph. Birds 1232"> Aristoph. <title>Av.</title> 1232</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="trimeter"> <l>μηλοσφαγεῖν τε βουθύτοις ἐπ’ ἐσχάραις</l> <l>κνισᾶν τ’ ἀγυιάς.</l> </lg> </quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἑστίαν_1491">= <quote xml:lang="grc">βωμόν</quote> (888, 1158): <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. Seven 275" ref="Aesch. Seven 275"> Aesch. <title>Th.</title> 275</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">μήλοισιν αἱμάσσοντας ἑστίας θεῶν.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Ποσειδωνίῳ_θεῷ_1491"> = <quote xml:lang="grc">Ποσειδῶνι</quote> , not really like <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁ Βακχεῖος θεός</quote> ( <ref n="Soph. OT 1105"> <title>O. T.</title> 1105</ref>), <quote xml:lang="eng"> "the god of <quote xml:lang="grc">Βάκχοι"</quote> </quote>(cp. 678), but somewhat similar to the Homeric <quote xml:lang="grc">βίη Ἡρακληείη</quote> , etc. Perhaps <foreign xml:lang="grc">Ποσειδωνίαν</foreign> (with <quote xml:lang="grc">ἑστίαν</quote> ): cp. <cit> <bibl n="Pind. N. 6" ref="Pind. N. 6"> Pind. <title>N.</title> 6. 46</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">Ποσειδάνιον ἂν τέμενος</quote> </cit> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐπαξιοῖ_1496">: lit. <quote xml:lang="eng"> "he <emph>deems</emph> thee, thy city, and thy friends <emph>worthy</emph> (of a recompense),— <emph>that he should make a due return</emph> , after receiving benefits."</quote>The constr. is of the same class as <quote xml:lang="grc">χρῄζω στόματος</quote> (instead of <quote xml:lang="grc">στόμα</quote> ) <quote xml:lang="grc">προσπτύξασθαι</quote> ( <ref n="Eur. Med. 1399"> Eur. <title>Med.</title> 1399</ref>), <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> the inf. is added epexegetically, outside of the construction with the principal verb (cp. 752 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἁρπάσαι</quote> , 1212 <quote xml:lang="grc">ζώειν</quote> ). This is, however, a peculiarly bold example, since we should have expected <quote xml:lang="grc">δικαίας χάριτος</quote> . Against the conject. <quote xml:lang="grc">σοι καὶ πολίταις καὶ φίλοις</quote> , remark that the strophic v. (1482) has no spondee. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πόλισμα_1496">in Attic prose usu. implies a town of the <emph>smaller</emph> kind, as <ref n="Thuc. 4.109">Thuc. 4.109</ref> (of Thracian tribes) <quote xml:lang="grc">κατὰ δὲ μικρὰ πολίσματα οἰκοῦσι</quote> . But <ref n="Eur. Med. 771"> Eur. <title>Med.</title> 771</ref>has <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄστυ καὶ πόλισμα Παλλάδος</quote> , <quote xml:lang="eng"> "the <emph>town</emph> and <emph>stronghold</emph> of Pallas"</quote>(Athens), <ref n="Eur. Ba. 919"> <title>Bacch.</title> 919</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πόλισμ’ ἑπτάστομον</quote>(Thebes): so it is used of the grand Cloud-city ( <ref n="Aristoph. Birds 553"> Aristoph. <title>Av.</title> 553</ref>, <ref n="Aristoph. Birds 1565">1565</ref> ): and Her. applies it to Ecbatana (1. 98). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="παθών_1496">does not require us to <emph>supply</emph> anything: it is strictly, <quote xml:lang="eng">"for treatment received,"</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="χάριν_1496">sufficing to mark that this treatment was good. Cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 1203">1203.</ref> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αὖ_introduction">: cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 887">887</ref> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἠχεῖται_introduction">is probably pass., as we find <quote xml:lang="grc">ἠχῶ γόους, ὕμνον</quote> , etc. (The midd. occurs in Pind. fr. 53. 18 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀχεῖται Σεμέλαν...χοροί</quote> .) </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σαφὴς_introduction">would ordinarily have been repeated in the second clause (cp. 5); but the equivalent <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἐμφανὴς</foreign> takes its place: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 54" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg004:54"> <title>O. T.</title> 54</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄρξεις...κρατεῖς</quote> </cit> : <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Ant. 669" ref="Soph. Ant. 669"> <title>Ant.</title> 669</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">καλῶς...εὖ</quote> </cit>: also <ref n="Soph. El. 986"> <title>El.</title> 986</ref>f.: <ref n="Soph. Aj. 647"> <title>Ai.</title> 647</ref>, 1323. The two adjectives could not be <emph> contrasted.</emph> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀστῶν_introduction">is a certain correction of <foreign xml:lang="grc">αὐτῶν</foreign> , which, as= <quote xml:lang="eng"> "you <emph>yourselves</emph> ,"</quote>would be very awkward after <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑμῶν</quote> and <quote xml:lang="grc">κοινός</quote> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μή_τις_1502">: <quote xml:lang="eng">"Can it be some thunderbolt of Zeus, or the rushing onset of some hail-storm (that has scared you)?"</quote> — <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐξέπληξεν ὑμᾶς</quote> , or the like. Theseus must, of course, be supposed to have heard the thunder which was pealing a few moments before; the doubt implied by <foreign xml:lang="grc">μή</foreign> is merely as to whether the blunder is the cause of the summons. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὀμβρία_χάλαζα_1502">, hail falling in a <emph>shower:</emph> cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 1279" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg004:1279"> <title>O. T.</title> 1279</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὄμβρος χαλάζης</quote> </cit> (n.). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐπιρράξασα_1502">, from <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπιρράσσω</quote> , which is either (1) trans., <quote xml:lang="eng">"to dash one thing against another,"</quote> as <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 1244" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg004:1244"> <title>O. T.</title> 1244</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">πύλας...ἐπιρράξασ’</quote> </cit> , <quote xml:lang="eng">"having dashed the doors together"</quote> at her back: or (2) intrans., as here, <quote xml:lang="eng">"to dash or burst on one"</quote> : so with dat. <ref n="Diod. 15.84">Diod. 15. 84</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῖς Μαντινεῦσιν...ἐπέρραξεν</quote> , <quote xml:lang="eng">"he dashed upon"</quote> them. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοιαῦτα_1504">: <quote xml:lang="eng">"for one might forebode anything when the god sends such a storm as this"</quote> (on <quote xml:lang="grc">διοσημίαι</quote> see n. to 95):—a courteous way of hinting that their alarm was not unnatural. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ποθοῦντι_προὐφάνης_1505">: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 1356" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg004:1356"> <title>O. T.</title> 1356</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">θέλοντι κἀμοὶ τοῦτ’ ἂν ἦν</quote> </cit>, n.: <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Il. 12.374" ref="Hom. Il. 12.374"> <title>Il.</title> 12.374</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπειγομένοισι δ’ ἵκοντο.</quote> </cit> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καί_θεῶν_1505"> : <quote xml:lang="eng">"and some god (cp. 1100) hath ordained for thee the good fortune of this coming"</quote> : </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τύχην_ὁδοῦ_1505"> , a fortune belonging to (connected with) it.—The MS. <foreign xml:lang="grc">θῆκε</foreign> was a mere blunder caused by transposition. As to the occasional omission of the syllabic augment in tragic <quote xml:lang="grc">ῥήσεις</quote> , see on <ref n="Soph. OT 1249"> <title>O. T.</title> 1249</ref>. Cp. above, 974. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ῥοπὴ_μοι_1508">, the turn of the scale ( <foreign xml:lang="lat">momentum</foreign> ) for my life,—the moment which is to bring it down to death. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 961" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg004:961"> <title>O. T.</title> 961</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">σμικρὰ παλαιὰ σώματ’ εὐνάζει ῥοπή</quote> </cit>(where see n.): <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Hipp. 1162" ref="Eur. Hipp. 1162"> Eur. <title>Hipp.</title> 1162</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="trimeter"> <l>Ἱππόλυτος οὐκέτ’ ἔστιν, ὡς εἰπεῖν ἔπος·</l> <l>δέδορκε μέντοι φῶς ἐπὶ σμικρᾶς ῥοπῆς</l> </lg> </quote> </cit> , <quote xml:lang="eng">"but his life still hangs in the trembling scale."</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καὶ_τετήνδε_1508">( <quote xml:lang="grc">τούτων</quote> )</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἅπερ_ξυνῄνεσα_1508"> , <quote xml:lang="eng">"and I wish to die without having defrauded thee and this city of the things on which I agreed."</quote> For the constr. of <foreign xml:lang="grc">ψεύσας</foreign> cp. on <ref target="cl1145">1145</ref> , and for the chief stress on the partic., 1038: for <foreign xml:lang="grc">ξυνῄν.</foreign> , <cit> <bibl n="Xen. Cyrop. 4.2.47" ref="Xen. Cyrop. 4.2.47"> Xen. <title>Cyr.</title> 4.2.47</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ταῦτα συνῄνουν</quote> </cit>, they agreed to these terms. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐν_κεῖσαι_1510">: usu. explained, <quote xml:lang="eng"> "And on what sign of thine end <emph>dost thou rely?</emph> "</quote>But <quote xml:lang="grc">κεῖμαι ἔν τινι</quote> (see on <ref target="cl247">247</ref> )= <quote xml:lang="eng">"to be situated in a person's power"</quote> : an analogous use of <quote xml:lang="grc">κεῖμαι</quote> here would give us, <quote xml:lang="eng"> "on what sign <emph>doth thy fate depend?</emph> "</quote>In <ref n="Soph. Trach. 82"> <title>Tr.</title> 82</ref>, however, we have <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν οὖν ῥοπῇ τοιᾷδε κειμένῳ</quote> : and, if the text be sound, <foreign xml:lang="grc">κεῖσαι</foreign> has (I think) a like sense here: lit., <quote xml:lang="eng">"at what sign of thy fate art thou in suspense?"</quote> The phrase is thus <emph>virtually</emph> equivalent to <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν τίνι ῥοπῇ κεῖσαι</quote> ;—the <foreign xml:lang="grc">τεκμήριον</foreign> itself standing for the crisis which it marks. The phrase seems to me possible (for our poet), but slightly suspicious. We might conjecture <foreign xml:lang="grc">καὶ τῷ πέπεισαι</foreign> : cp. <ref n="Eur. Hel. 1190"> Eur. <title>Hel.</title> 1190</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="trimeter"> <l>ἐννύχοις πεπεισμένη</l> <l>στένεις ὀνείροις</l> </lg> </quote>. (To the obvious <quote xml:lang="grc">κεῖται σοῦ, σόν</quote> , or <quote xml:lang="grc">σοί...τεκμήριον</quote> , the objection is the phrase <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν τῷ κεῖται</quote> .) </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αὐτοὶ_1511"> with </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κήρυκες_1511"> : the gods herald their own interposition in his fate. No <quote xml:lang="grc">μάντις</quote> , but Heaven itself, gi gives the warning. Cp. Bekker <title>Anecd.</title> 5. 14 <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐτοκῆρυξ· ὁ μὴ δῑ ἑτέρων ἀλλὰ δῑ ἑαυτοῦ κηρυκεύων</quote> . <ref n="Eur. Supp. 589"> Eur. <title> Suppl.</title> 589</ref>(Theseus says that he will march on Thebes) <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐτὸς σίδηρον ὀξὺν ἐν χεροῖν ἔχων, | αὐτός τε κῆρυξ</quote> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ψεύδοντες_προκ_1511"> , <quote xml:lang="eng"> "disappointing me in no way ( <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδὲν"</quote> adv., cp. 1145) of the signs appointed beforehand</quote>(94): as <ref n="Hdt. 2.38">Her. 2.38</ref> (of the Apis) <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ καθαρὴ</quote> ( <quote xml:lang="grc">ἡ γλῶσσα</quote> ) <quote xml:lang="grc">τῶν προκειμένων σημηίων</quote> , the marks <emph>appointed</emph> by sacred law. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πολλὰ_1514">= <quote xml:lang="eng">"very,"</quote> with the adj.: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Ant. 1046" ref="Soph. Ant. 1046"> <title>Ant.</title> 1046</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">χοἱ πολλὰ δεινοί</quote> </cit> : <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Phil. 254" ref="Soph. Phil. 254"> <title>Ph.</title> 254</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὦ πόλλ’ ἐγὼ μοχθηρός</quote> </cit> : <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 1326" ref="Soph. El. 1326"> <title>El.</title> 1326</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὦ πλεῖστα μῶροι</quote> </cit> : <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Il. 11.557" ref="Hom. Il. 11.557"> <title>Il.</title> 11.557</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">πόλλ’ ἀέκων</quote> </cit>.—The answer is framed as if Theseus had said, <quote xml:lang="grc">ποῖα δὲ σημεῖα ἐφάνη τῶνδε</quote> ; If Reiske's <foreign xml:lang="grc">δηλοῦσι</foreign> (which Wecklein receives) is to be admitted, we must view L's <foreign xml:lang="grc">αἱ πολλαὶ</foreign> as a mere gloss suggested by <foreign xml:lang="grc">διατελεῖς</foreign> and conformed to <foreign xml:lang="grc">τὰ πολλά τε</foreign> . This, however, seems very improbable, since ( <emph>a</emph> ) the article <foreign xml:lang="grc"> τὰ</foreign> with <foreign xml:lang="grc">βέλη</foreign> recommends the art. with <foreign xml:lang="grc">βρονταὶ</foreign> , and ( <emph>b</emph> ) the reiterated <foreign xml:lang="grc">πολλὰ</foreign> is effective. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="στράψαντα_1515"> . <quote xml:lang="grc">στράπτω</quote> is not extant in classical Attic, but occurs in Apollonius Rhodius ( <date from="-0200" to="-0100">2nd cent. B.C.</date> ) and Oppian ( <date from="0100" to="0200">2nd cent. A.D.</date> ), also in an Orphic hymn of uncertain date, and in the Anthology. In cases of this kind we should always recollect how incomplete is our knowledge of the classical Attic vocabulary, and allow for the likelihood that the learned Alexandrian poets had earlier warrant for this or that word which, as it happens, we cannot trace above them. (Cp. on <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀκορέστατος</quote> , 120.) With <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀστράπτω</quote> and <quote xml:lang="grc">στράπτω</quote> , cp. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀστεροπή</quote> and <quote xml:lang="grc">στεροπή, ἀσπαίρω</quote> and <quote xml:lang="grc">σπαίρω, ἀσταφίς</quote> and <quote xml:lang="grc">σταφίς, ἄσταχυς</quote> and <quote xml:lang="grc">στάχυς</quote> , and many other instances in which the longer form and the shorter both belong to the classical age. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σκήψαντα_1515">(Forster) is much less forcible: the thought is of the lightningflash breaking forth as a sign in the sky ( <quote xml:lang="grc">φλέγει</quote> , 1466), rather than of its descent on earth: and this word would hardly have passed into the MS. <quote xml:lang="grc">στρέψαντα.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="χειρὸς_ἀνικήτου_1515">, gen. of point <emph>whence</emph> with <foreign xml:lang="grc">στρ</foreign> . ( <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 152" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg004:152"> <title>O. T.</title> 152</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">Πυθῶνος...ἔβας</quote> </cit>) rather than possess. gen. with <foreign xml:lang="grc">βέλη</foreign> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θεσπίζονθ_1516">: as Oed. had predicted trouble from Thebes at a time when Theseus thought it impossible (606 ff.); Creon had fulfilled the prediction, and had even hinted at future war (1037). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ψευδόφημα_1516">: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 723" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg004:723"> <title>O. T.</title> 723</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">φῆμαι μαντικαί</quote> </cit> : <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 43 <quote xml:lang="grc">φήμην</quote> =a message from a god (n.). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σοι_1518">ethic dat., </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τῇδε_πόλει_1518"> dat. of interest; which thou shalt have stored up for Athens. The ethic dat. is often combined with another, as <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Rh. 644" ref="Eur. Rh. 644"> [Eur.] <title>Rhes.</title> 644</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐχθρῶν τις ἡμῖν χρίμπτεται στρατεύματι</quote> </cit>, we have some foeman approaching our camp. The <foreign xml:lang="lat">v.l.</foreign> <foreign xml:lang="grc">σῇ τε</foreign> came of not seeing this. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γήρως_ἄλυπα_1518">: see on <ref target="cl677">677</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc"> ἀνήνεμον...χειμώνων</quote> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="χῶρον_ἐξηγήσομαι_1520">, show the way to the place: the literal notion being blended with that of expounding (as the <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐξηγηταί</quote> expounded the sacred law). Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Hdt. 3.4" ref="Hdt. 3.4">Her. 3.4</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐξηγέεται...τὴν ἔλασιν</quote> </cit>, expounds the route for the march. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄθικτος_1520">, pass., as always in Attic (though <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 969" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg004:969"> <title>O. T.</title> 969</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄψαυστος ἔγχους</quote> </cit> = <quote xml:lang="eng">"not touching"</quote> ): <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Trach. 685" ref="Soph. Trach. 685"> <title>Tr.</title> 685</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀκτῖνός τ’ ἀεὶ | θερμῆς ἄθικτον</quote> </cit>. The act. sense, <quote xml:lang="eng">"not touching,"</quote> occurs later ( <ref n="Call. Dian. 201"> Callim. <title>Hymn. Dian.</title> 201</ref>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοῦτον_1522">refers to <quote xml:lang="grc">χῶρον</quote> , the place where he was to <quote xml:lang="eng">"die,"</quote> <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> disappear. This place is accurately described at 1590. It was the <emph> grave</emph> (1545) that was to remain secret. But here, by a slip, the poet identifies them (see Introd.). We should not change <foreign xml:lang="grc">τοῦτον</foreign> to <foreign xml:lang="grc">τύμβον</foreign> . — Note how Soph. uses the vagueness of the local legend as to the <emph> grave.</emph> Secrecy was imposed by the dying breath of Oed. himself. The descent of the secret in the line of the Attic kings would serve to explain any esoteric knowledge on the subject which, in the poet's time, may have been claimed by a gens of hereditary priests. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μήθ_κέκευθε_1522">: neither where (precisely) it is concealed, nor (even) whereabouts it is situated. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὥς_τιθῇ_1524">. Like <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῦτον</quote> in 1522, <foreign xml:lang="grc">ὅδε</foreign> refers to <quote xml:lang="grc">χῶρον</quote> (1520), <quote xml:lang="eng">"this spot"</quote> ; it is not for <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνὴρ ὅδε</quote> (450). For <foreign xml:lang="grc">πρὸ</foreign> cp. <cit> <bibl n="Thuc. 1.33" ref="Thuc. 1.33">Thuc. 1.33</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἣν ὑμεῖς ἂν πρὸ πολλῶν χρημάτων καὶ χάριτος ἐτιμήσασθε δύναμιν ὑμῖν προσγενέσθαι, αὕτη πάρεστιν αὐτεπάγγελτος</quote> </cit>. (Not, <quote xml:lang="eng"> " <emph>against</emph> many shields etc.,"</quote> as <cit> <bibl n="Xen. Anab. 7.8.18" ref="Xen. Anab. 7.8.18"> Xen. <title>Anab.</title> 7.8.18</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅπως τὰ ὅπλα ἔχοιεν πρὸ τῶν τοξευμάτων</quote> </cit>, <quote xml:lang="eng"> "that they might have their shields <emph>to screen them from</emph> the arrows."</quote>) Cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 218"> <title>O. T.</title> 218</ref>n. </gloss>

data.quote:
ἣν ὑμεῖς ἂν πρὸ πολλῶν χρημάτων καὶ
									χάριτος ἐτιμήσασθε δύναμιν ὑμῖν προσγενέσθαι, αὕτη
									πάρεστιν αὐτεπάγγελτος

ὅπως τὰ ὅπλα ἔχοιεν πρὸ τῶν
									τοξευμάτων

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δορός_ἐπακτοῦ_1524">. As the hoplite was armed with a <quote xml:lang="grc">δόρυ</quote> no less than with a shield, there is no contrast here between infantry and cavalry, but only between citizens and foreign allies. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Isoc. 10.37" ref="Isoc. 10.37">Isocr. or. 10 § 37</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδ’ ἐπακτῷ δυνάμει</quote> </cit>(foreign mercenaries) <quote xml:lang="grc">τὴν ἀρχὴν διαφυλάττων, ἀλλὰ τῇ τῶν πολιτῶν εὐνοίᾳ δορυφορούμενος</quote> .—The old <foreign xml:lang="lat">v.l.</foreign> <foreign xml:lang="grc">γειτονῶν</foreign> , <quote xml:lang="eng">"being near you,"</quote> would be weak: as to the form, <quote xml:lang="grc"> γειτονέω</quote> is classical, though Attic prose preferred <quote xml:lang="grc">γειτνιάω</quote> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀλκὴν_γειτόνων_1524"> , <quote xml:lang="eng">"a defence against neighbours"</quote> (the Thebans, 1534), but, though the objective gen. is quite correct (see on <ref n="Soph. OT 218"> <title>O. T.</title> 218</ref>), the order of the words makes it hardly possible to disjoin <foreign xml:lang="grc">γειτόνων</foreign> from <foreign xml:lang="grc">δορός τ’ ἐπακτοῦ</foreign> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἃ_ἐξάγιστα_1526"> , <quote xml:lang="eng"> "but as to things which are <emph> banned</emph> "</quote>(which cannot be uttered without impiety). Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Aeschin. 3.113" ref="Aeschin. 3.113">Aeschin. or. 3 § 113</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οἱ Λοκροὶ οἱ Ἀμφισσεῖς...τὸν λιμένα τὸν ἐξάγιστον καὶ ἐπάρατον πάλιν ἐτείχισαν</quote> </cit> : <quote xml:lang="eng"> "the harbour which was <emph>banned</emph> and accursed,"</quote>—the Amphictyons having pronounced an <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀρά</quote> , which said of the transgressor, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐναγὴς ἔστω</quote> ( <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> § 110). The verb occurs <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. Ag. 641" ref="Aesch. Ag. 641"> Aesch. <title>Ag.</title> 641</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">πολλοὺς δὲ πολλῶν ἐξαγισθέντας δόμων</quote> </cit>, many <quote xml:lang="eng">"devoted to death"</quote> out of many houses. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἁγίζω</quote> =to make <quote xml:lang="grc">ἅγιος</quote> (1495): <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐξαγίζω</quote> = to devote to avenging gods (cp. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐξοσιόω</quote> , to dedicate), rather than (as some explain it) <quote xml:lang="eng"> "to <emph>de</emph> -consecrate."</quote> </gloss>

data.quote:
οἱ Λοκροὶ οἱ Ἀμφισσεῖς...τὸν λιμένα
									τὸν ἐξάγιστον καὶ ἐπάρατον πάλιν ἐτείχισαν

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μηδὲ_λόγῳ_1526"> , <quote xml:lang="eng"> "and such things as ( <quote xml:lang="grc">μηδέ"</quote> of the class, cp. 73) are not to be touched upon in speech</quote>(see on <ref target="cl624">624</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τἀκίνητ’ ἔπη</quote> ). Nauck proposed <quote xml:lang="grc">δεῖ κινεῖν</quote> , but the pres. <quote xml:lang="grc">κινεῖται</quote> expresses what fate has decreed ( <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Phil. 113" ref="Soph. Phil. 113"> <title>Ph.</title> 113</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">αἱρεῖ</quote> </cit> ). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μαθήσει_1526">, by sight as well as by hearing: see 1641, 1650. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σῷζε_1530">, "guard them," — not merely, "remember" them, a sense peculiar to the midd. <quote xml:lang="grc">σῴζομαι</quote> ( <ref n="Plat. Theaet. 153b"> Plat. <title>Theaet.</title> 153 B</ref>, etc., n. on <ref n="Soph. OT 318"> <title>O. T.</title> 318</ref>). Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Ant. 1113" ref="Soph. Ant. 1113"> <title>Ant.</title> 1113</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">νόμους | … σῴζοντα</quote> </cit> ( <quote xml:lang="eng">"observing"</quote> ). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀφικνῇ_1530">: L's <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀφίκῃ</quote> is of course impossible, the <foreign xml:lang="grc">ι</foreign> of the aor. being long only in the indic. (cp. 1495). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τῷ_μόνῳ_1530"> : <quote xml:lang="eng"> <lg type="english"> <l>but to one,</l> <l> <emph>Thy chiefest</emph> </l> </lg> </quote>(Whitelaw), which well gives the vagueness of the phrase. While the hereditary monarchy lasted, the <foreign xml:lang="grc">προφέρτατος</foreign> would, in fact, be the king's eldest son: afterwards, it would be the man whose place in the State made him the proper guardian of the secret. The poet chose a phrase which would cover priestly tradition. I would not, then, change <foreign xml:lang="grc">μόνῳ</foreign> , with Nauck, to <foreign xml:lang="grc">γόνῳ</foreign> . In fr. 401 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἦ γὰρ φίλη )γὼ τῶνδε τοῦ προφερτέρου</quote> , the sense <quote xml:lang="eng">"elder"</quote> is possible, but not certain. <ref n="Hes. Sh. 260"> Hes. <title>Scut.</title> 260</ref>has <quote xml:lang="grc">τῶν γε μὲν ἀλλάων προφερής τ’ ἦν πρεσβυτάτη τε</quote> , where the second adj. helps the first; <ref n="Plat. Euthyd. 271b"> Plat. <title>Euthyd.</title> 271 B</ref>has <quote xml:lang="grc">προφερής</quote> , <quote xml:lang="eng">"wellgrown,"</quote> of a youth, as opp. to <quote xml:lang="grc">σκληφρός</quote> , <quote xml:lang="eng">"slight."</quote> Hom. has only <quote xml:lang="grc"> προφερέστερος</quote> , never of age. The nearest parallel to our passage is <cit> <bibl n="Hes. Th. 361" ref="Hes. Th. 361"> Hes. <title>Th.</title> 361</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">προφερεστάτη ἐστὶν ἁπασέων</quote> </cit> , <emph>foremost</emph> among the daughters of Oceanus is Styx; and at <ref n="Hes. Th. 777">777</ref> she is called <quote xml:lang="grc">πρεσβυτάτη</quote> . So, here, the word <emph> suggests</emph> seniority, but without excluding pre-eminence of other kinds. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀδῇον_1533">contr. for <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀδήϊον</quote> , <quote xml:lang="eng"> "unravaged,"</quote> from <quote xml:lang="grc">δήϊος</quote> ( <quote xml:lang="grc">δῇος</quote> Theogn. 552, always Dor. <quote xml:lang="grc">δάϊος</quote> in trag.), <quote xml:lang="eng"> "ravaging"</quote> ( <quote xml:lang="grc">√ΔΑv</quote> , whence <quote xml:lang="grc">δαίω</quote> , to kindle):</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σπαρτῶν_ἀνδρῶν_1533"> , <quote xml:lang="eng">"on the part of,"</quote> <quote xml:lang="eng">"from the quarter of"</quote> the Thebans. For <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἀπό</foreign> , cp. <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Phaedo 83B" ref="Plat. Phaedo 83B"> Plat. <title>Phaedo</title> 83B</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδὲν τοσοῦτον κακὸν ἔπαθεν ἀπ’ αὐτῶν</quote> </cit>. Schaefer's <foreign xml:lang="grc">ὑπό</foreign> is admissible ( <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Rep. 366A" ref="Plat. Rep. 366A"> Plat. <title>Rep.</title> 366A</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀζήμιοι...ὑπὸ θεῶν</quote> </cit>): but <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπό</quote> is fitter here as including all peril from that <emph>region.</emph> When Cadmus was founding Thebes, he required water from a well guarded by a dragon, the offspring of Ares. He killed the dragon, and sowed its teeth in the ground. Armed men sprang up, who slew each other, all save five. These five, of whom Echion was chief, became the ancestors of the Cadmeans. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Ant. 1123" ref="Soph. Ant. 1123"> <title>Ant.</title> 1123</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="lyric"> <l>παρ’ ὑγρῶν</l> <l>Ἰσμηνοῦ ῥείθρων, ἀγρίου τ’</l> <l>ἐπὶ σπορᾷ δράκοντος</l> </lg> </quote> </cit> . <cit> <bibl>Pind. fr. 6</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἢ Κάδμον, ἢ σπαρτῶν ἱερὸν γένος ἀνδρῶν</quote> </cit>. </gloss>

data.quote:
οὐδὲν τοσοῦτον κακὸν ἔπαθεν ἀπ’
									αὐτῶν

ἢ Κάδμον, ἢ σπαρτῶν ἱερὸν γένος
									ἀνδρῶν

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αἱ_πόλεις_1533">, justifying his hint of possible danger from Thebes. <quote xml:lang="eng"> "Most cities are apt (gnomic aor.) to enter on aggression with a light heart ( <foreign xml:lang="grc">ῥᾳδίως</foreign> ), even though their neighbour is wellbehaved."</quote>Cp. what he said of the Thebans in 619 f., where <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκ σμικροῦ λόγου</quote> answers to <quote xml:lang="grc">ῥᾳδίως</quote> here. So the schol.:— <quote xml:lang="grc">κἂν δικαίως τις πολιτεύηται, πολλαὶ πόλεις ἀδίκως ἐπέρχονται</quote> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κἂν_οἰκῇ_1533">: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Rep. 423A" ref="Plat. Rep. 423A"> Plat. <title>Rep.</title> 423A</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἕως ἂν ἡ πόλις σοι οἰκῇ σωφρόνως</quote> </cit>. (It might also be transitive, <quote xml:lang="eng">"governs,"</quote> <foreign xml:lang="lat">sc.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὴν πόλιν</quote> .) A compliment to Theseus and to Athens is implied: cp. 1125.</gloss>

data.quote:
ἕως ἂν ἡ πόλις σοι οἰκῇ
									σωφρόνως

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γὰρ_1536">refers to <quote xml:lang="grc">ῥᾳδίως</quote> . <quote xml:lang="eng"> "(Outrage is lightly committed), <emph>for</emph> the gods are late, though they are sure, in visiting sin,"</quote>and so the hope of <emph> present</emph> impunity emboldens the wicked. See 1370. The remark is general, but Oed. is thinking especially of his unnatural sons; the divine vengeance has long been delayed, but is now at hand. Cp. <title>Orac. Sibyll.</title> 8. 14 <quote xml:lang="grc">ὀψὲ θεῶν ἀλέουσι μύλοι, ἀλέουσι δὲ λεπτά</quote> . Longfellow, <quote xml:lang="eng"> "Retribution"</quote> : <quote xml:lang="eng"> " <title>Though the mills of God grind slowly</title> , <emph>yet they grind exceeding small</emph> "</quote>(from the German of F. von Logau, <title>Sinngedichte</title> 3. 2. 24). <cit> <bibl n="Hor. Carm. 3.2.32" ref="Hor. Carm. 3.2.32"> Hor. <title>Carm.</title> 3. 2. 32</bibl> <quote xml:lang="lat">pede Poena claudo.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εὖ_δ_1536">. When two clauses are co-ordinated by <foreign xml:lang="grc">μέν</foreign> and <foreign xml:lang="grc">δέ</foreign> , if we wish to subordinate one to the other we must take care that the subordinated clause is that which has <foreign xml:lang="grc">μέν</foreign> . Thus here:— <quote xml:lang="eng"> "late, <emph>though</emph> surely."</quote> <quote xml:lang="eng">"Surely, <emph>though</emph> late,"</quote>would be <foreign xml:lang="grc">ὀψὲ μὲν εὖ δέ</foreign> . So <ref n="Soph. OT 419"> <title>O. T.</title> 419</ref>(n.) <quote xml:lang="grc">βλέποντα νῦν μὲν ὄρθ’, ἔπειτα δὲ σκότον</quote> ,=sightless then, though seeing now. It is the necessity of giving the chief emphasis to <foreign xml:lang="grc">ὀψέ</foreign> , not to <foreign xml:lang="grc">εὖ</foreign> , that decides the true relation of this verse to the preceding. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὰ_ἀφείς_1537">, having set religion at nought: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 910" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg004:910"> <title>O. T.</title> 910</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔρρει δὲ τὰ θεῖα.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μαίνεσθαι_1537">, the madness of passions which are no longer controlled by religion,—as the frenzy of ambition (371), and of hatred (1392).—I do not suppose that the poet directly <emph>alludes</emph> to any contemporary event; but we may surmise that, when he wrote these lines, he had in his thoughts the daring outrage on religion at Athens in 415 B.C.,—the partial impunity of its authors (including Alcibiades),—and the tremendous disasters of the city two years later. Cp. <ref n="Soph. OT"> <title>O. T.</title> </ref>p. xxx, 886 n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὃ_παθεῖν_1538">, referring to <foreign xml:lang="grc">τὰ θεἶ ἀφείς</foreign> etc. To divulge the <cit> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐξάγιστα</quote> <bibl n="Soph. OC 1526" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg007:1526">(1526)</bibl> </cit>would be <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἀφεῖναι τὰ θεῖα</foreign> . The next verse turns off this light reminder by adding that Theseus does not require it. "Well ( <foreign xml:lang="grc"> οὖν</foreign> ), thou knowest such things, without my precepts." <cit> <bibl n="Thuc. 2.43" ref="Thuc. 2.43">Thuc. 2.43</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὠφελίαν, ἣν ἄν τις πρὸς οὐδὲν χεῖρον αὐτοὺς ὑμᾶς εἰδότας μηκύνοι</quote> </cit> : <cit> <bibl n="Thuc. 2.36" ref="Thuc. 2.36"> <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 36</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">μακρηγορεῖν ἐν εἰδόσιν οὐ βουλόμενος</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Il. 10.250" ref="Hom. Il. 10.250"> <title>Il.</title> 10.250</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰδόσι γάρ τοι ταῦτα μετ’ Ἀργείοις ἀγορεύεις</quote> </cit>. Cp. on <ref target="cl1038">1038</ref> . </gloss>

data.quote:
ὠφελίαν, ἣν ἄν τις πρὸς οὐδὲν χεῖρον
									αὐτοὺς ὑμᾶς εἰδότας μηκύνοι

εἰδόσι γάρ τοι ταῦτα μετ’ Ἀργείοις
									ἀγορεύεις

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="χῶρον_1540">: cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 644">644</ref> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοὐκ_παρόν_1540"> : <quote xml:lang="eng">"that which has come from the god,"</quote> (cp. 1694 <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ φέρον ἐκ θεοῦ</quote> ,)—the summons as conveyed both by the storm and by an inward prompting. Matthiae's <foreign xml:lang="grc">πτερόν</foreign> (97) would be less mysterious, and therefore, in this context, less solemn. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μηδ_ἐντρεπώμεθα_1540"> , <quote xml:lang="eng"> "nor longer <emph>hesitate</emph> ,"</quote> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὀκνῶμεν, μέλλωμεν. ἐντρέπεσθαι</quote>(1) <quote xml:lang="eng">"to turn about"</quote> : (2) <quote xml:lang="eng">"to give heed to,"</quote> with gen., as <ref n="Soph. OT 724"> <title>O. T.</title> 724</ref>: (3) then, absol., <quote xml:lang="eng"> " <emph>to feel a scruple or misgiving</emph> ,"</quote>to hesitate, as here. Intelligible as the third use is, this is perh. the only clear example of it in classical Attic: but cp. Polyb. 31. 12 (with ref. to Demetrius son of Seleucus urging his claims before the Roman Senate), <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐνετρέποντο μὲν ἅπαντες ἀκούοντες ἐν ἑαυτοῖς, κοινῇ γε μὴν ἔδοξε τῇ συγκλήτῳ τὸν μὲν Δημήτριον κατασχεῖν</quote> , <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="eng"> "they all <emph>felt some compunction</emph> in their own minds,"</quote>—some <emph> misgiving</emph> as to the fairness of their conduct. The hesitation which Oed. deprecates is that which the <emph>others</emph> might feel in acknowledging that the hour of his end had come.—The schol. wrongly took <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἐντρεπ</foreign> . in its first sense, <quote xml:lang="eng">"look behind us"</quote> : <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀντὶ τοῦ ἐπιστρεφώμεθα</quote> : <quote xml:lang="grc">Ὅμηρος· ἐντροπαλιζόμενος</quote> . </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὧδ_1542">: see 182. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καινός_1542">, of a novel <emph>kind</emph> , <quote xml:lang="eng">"in strange wise"</quote> : cp. <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Euthyd. 271b" ref="Plat. Euthyd. 271b"> Plat. <title>Euthyd.</title> 271 B</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">καινοί τινες...σοφισταί... καὶ τίς ἡ σοφία;</quote> </cit> </gloss>

data.quote:
καινοί τινες...σοφισταί... καὶ τίς ἡ
									σοφία;

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τῇδ_τῇδε_1547">, lit., <quote xml:lang="eng">"this way,— hither,—this way"</quote> ;—marking that he is already sure of his path. The number of forms from <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅδε</quote> in this v. and 1546 is curious, but it is evident that the natural distribution of emphasis in speaking would avoid an unpleasing effect. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὁ_πομπὸς_1548"> : <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 831" ref="Soph. Aj. 831"> <title>Ai.</title> 831</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">καλῶ δ’ ἅμα | πομπαῖον Ἑρμῆν χθόνιον εὖ με κοιμίσαι</quote> </cit>: hence <quote xml:lang="grc">ψυχοπομπός</quote> ( <ref n="Diod. 1.96">Diod. I. 96</ref> ): <cit> <bibl n="Hor. Carm. 1.10.17" ref="Hor. Carm. 1.10.17"> Hor. <title>Carm.</title> 1. 10. 17</bibl> <quote xml:lang="lat">Tu pias laetis animas reponis Sedibus.</quote> </cit>He was also the guide of the living on errands of danger or guile ( <ref n="Soph. El. 1395"> <title>El.</title> 1395</ref>, <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Phil. 133" ref="Soph. Phil. 133"> <title>Ph.</title> 133</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἑ. ὁ πέμπων δόλιος</quote> </cit> ). </gloss>

data.quote:
καλῶ δ’ ἅμα | πομπαῖον Ἑρμῆν χθόνιον
									εὖ με κοιμίσαι

Tu pias laetis animas reponis
									Sedibus.

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἥ_θεός_1548">: Persephone: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Ant. 893" ref="Soph. Ant. 893"> <title>Ant.</title> 893</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="trimeter"> <l>ὧν ἀριθμὸν ἐν νεκροῖς</l> <l>πλεῖστον δέδεκται Περσέφασσ’ ὀλωλότων</l> </lg> </quote> </cit> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φῶς_ἀφεγγές_1549">, light which, for the blind, is no light: cp. <quote xml:lang="eng">"darkness of life"</quote> in Tennyson's lines quoted on 33: <cit> <quote xml:lang="grc">σκότον βλέπειν, ἐν σκότῳ ὁρᾶν</quote> <bibl n="Soph. OT 419" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg004:419"> ( <title>O. T.</title> 419)</bibl> </cit>, 1273). The dying bid farewell to the sunlight, as <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 856" ref="Soph. Aj. 856"> <title>Ai.</title> 856</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="trimeter"> <l>σὲ δ’, ὦ φαεννῆς ἡμέρας τὸ νῦν σέλας</l> <l>… προσεννέπω</l> <l>πανύστατον δή</l> </lg> </quote> </cit>. So here the blind man, for whom light has long been changed to darkness, bids farewell to his <emph>memory</emph> of it. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πρόσθε_1549">, before he blinded himself (cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 1183"> <title>O. T.</title> 1183</ref>). The full thought is,— <quote xml:lang="eng"> "Once I <emph>saw</emph> thee, but for long I have only <emph>felt</emph> thee, and now I <emph>feel</emph> thee for the last time."</quote>Whitelaw cp. <cit> <bibl> <title>Par. Lost</title> 3. 21,</bibl> <quote> <lg type="verse" subtype="iambic-pentameter"> <l>Thee I revisit safe,</l> <l> <emph>And</emph>feel <emph>thy sovran vital</emph> lamp; <emph>but thou</emph> </l> <l> <emph>Revisit'st not these eyes.</emph> </l> </lg> </quote> </cit> And <cit> <bibl> <title>Lear</title> 4. 1. 23</bibl> <quote> <lg type="verse" subtype="iambic-pentameter"> <l>Might I but live to see thee in my touch,</l> <l>I'd say I had eyes again.</l> </lg> </quote> </cit> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸν_βίον_1551">is most simply taken (1) as= <quote xml:lang="eng">"the last part of my life,"</quote> its close, as ( <foreign xml:lang="lat">e.g.</foreign> ) <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Il. 6.40" ref="Hom. Il. 6.40"> <title>Il.</title> 6.40</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν πρώτῳ ῥυμῷ</quote> </cit>=at the end of the pole. He is going <quote xml:lang="eng"> " <emph>to hide the close of his life with Hades</emph> "</quote>( <quote xml:lang="grc"> παρ’ <emph>Ἅιδην</emph> </quote>since motion is implied), not merely because he is about to quit life, but because he is destined to quit it by a strange passing not beheld of men.—(2) We might also take <foreign xml:lang="grc">τελευταῖον</foreign> as proleptic adj. <emph>with art.</emph> (see on <ref target="cl1088"> 1089</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸν εὔαγρον</quote> ): <quote xml:lang="eng">"to hide my life, so that it shall be ended."</quote> I prefer (1). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αὐτός_τε_1553">: cp. on <ref target="cl488">488</ref> . Theseus and his realm are identified, as 308 f., 1125, 1496. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πρόσπολοι_1553">, like <quote xml:lang="grc">ὀπάονες</quote> (1103). Here his Attic lieges generally seem meant, rather than his followers from <title>Athens</title> as opposed to the Coloniates (1066). So 1496 <quote xml:lang="grc">σε καὶ πόλισμα καὶ φίλους</quote> .</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κἀπ_εὐπραξίᾳ_1554"> : <quote xml:lang="eng"> "and <emph>in</emph> your prosperous <emph> state</emph> ,"</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐπί_1554">expressing the attendant condition (as it denotes the terms of a treaty): cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 108" ref="Soph. El. 108"> <title>El.</title> 108</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπὶ κωκυτῷ</quote> </cit> ...</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μέμνησθε_1554">, imper., not <quote xml:lang="grc">μεμνῇσθε</quote> , optat.: for this depends on them, but their weal ( <quote xml:lang="grc">εὐδ. γένοισθε</quote> ) on the gods. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εὐτυχεῖς_ἀεί_1554">: (remember me), for your lasting welfare. If they duly revere his memory, their good-fortune will abide. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἰ_ἐστι_1556">: a propitiatory address, since Pluto and the other <quote xml:lang="grc">χθόνιοι θεοί</quote> are stern to human prayers. So Hades is <quote xml:lang="grc">δίχα παιάνων</quote> ( <ref n="Eur. IT 185"> Eur. <title>I. T.</title> 185</ref>), <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀμείλιχος ἠδ’ ἀδάμαστος</quote> ( <ref n="Hom. Il. 9.158"> <title>Il.</title> 9.158</ref>). <cit> <bibl n="Hor. Carm. 2.14.5" ref="Hor. Carm. 2.14.5"> Hor. <title>Carm.</title> 2. 14. 5</bibl> <quote xml:lang="lat">Non si tricenis, quotquot eunt dies, Amice, places illacrimabilem Plutona tauris.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

data.quote:
Non si tricenis, quotquot eunt dies,
									Amice, places illacrimabilem Plutona tauris.

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὰν_θεὸν_1556">, Persephone ( <ref n="Soph. OC 1548">1548</ref> ), an unusual title, perhaps suggested by the literal sense of <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἅιδης</quote> : cp. Pind. fr. 207 <quote xml:lang="grc">Ταρτάρου πυθμὴν πιέζει σ’ ἀφανοῦς</quote> : <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. Seven 859" ref="Aesch. Seven 859"> Aesch. <title>Th.</title> 859</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰν ἀνάλιον | πάνδοκον εἰς ἀφανῆ τε χέρσον</quote> </cit>(the nether-world). </gloss>

data.quote:
τὰν ἀνάλιον | πάνδοκον εἰς ἀφανῆ τε
									χέρσον

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐννυχίων_ἄναξ_1558"> ( <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Trach. 501" ref="Soph. Trach. 501"> <title>Tr.</title> 501</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸν ἔννυχον Ἅιδαν</quote> </cit>), suggested by <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Il. 20.61" ref="Hom. Il. 20.61"> <title>Il.</title> 20.61</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄναξ ἐνέρων Ἀϊδωνεύς</quote> </cit>. This poetically lengthened form of <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἅιδης</quote> (trisyllabic only here) occurs also <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Il. 5.190" ref="Hom. Il. 5.190"> <title>Il.</title> 5.190</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἀϊδωνῆϊ προϊάψειν</quote> </cit> , <ref n="Hes. Th. 913"> Hes. <title>Theog.</title> 913</ref>, and oft. in later poets. A stream of the Troad on Mt Ida was called <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἀϊδωνεύς</quote> from its disappearing into the ground, <ref n="Paus. 10.12.3">Paus. 10.12.3</ref> f. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λίσσομαι_1558"> = <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐξ ἄντρων</quote> in the antistrophe (1571): but, since the first syll. of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄντρων</quote> is <quote xml:lang="eng"> "irrational,"</quote> <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> a long syllable doing duty for a short, the normal choree <quote xml:lang="grc"> λισσο</quote> is defensible. (See Metr. Analysis.) Dindorf's <foreign xml:lang="grc">λίσσωμαι</foreign> , which he calls a <quote xml:lang="eng">"modestior subiunctivi usus"</quote> ( <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="eng">"permit me to pray"</quote> ), will certainly not stand. The schol. had in his text <foreign xml:lang="grc">δίδου μοι</foreign> ,—not instead of <foreign xml:lang="grc">λίσσομαι</foreign> , but (as his words show) in addition to it. As the construction of <quote xml:lang="grc">λίσσομαι</quote> was clear enough, <quote xml:lang="grc">δίδου μοι</quote> would rather seem to have been a gloss on some other imperat. with <quote xml:lang="grc">μοι</quote> . Possibly <foreign xml:lang="grc">νεῦσόν μοι</foreign> (cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Phil. 484" ref="Soph. Phil. 484"> <title>Ph.</title> 484</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">νεῦσον,... πείσθητι</quote> </cit> : <ref n="Pind. P. 1"> Pind. <title>P.</title> 1. 71</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">νεῦσον, Κρονίων</quote>) which may have been current as a <foreign xml:lang="lat">v.l.</foreign> for <foreign xml:lang="grc"> λίσσομαι</foreign> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐπὶ_μόρῳ_1561">: for the prep. (= <quote xml:lang="eng">"with"</quote> ) see on <ref target="cl1554">1554</ref> . This prayer to Pluto needed the preface <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ θέμις</quote> (1556), since he <quote xml:lang="grc">στεναγμοῖς καὶ γόοις πλουτίζεται</quote> ( <ref n="Soph. OT 30"> <title>O. T.</title> 30</ref>). Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. Seven 915" ref="Aesch. Seven 915"> Aesch. <title>Th.</title> 915</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="trimeter"> <l>δόμων μάλ’ ἀχὰν ἐς οὖς προπέμπει</l> <l>δαϊκτὴρ γόος</l> </lg> </quote> </cit> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐξανύσαι_1562">, reach: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 607" ref="Soph. Aj. 607"> <title>Ai.</title> 607</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνύσειν... Αιδαν</quote> </cit> : <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Ant. 804" ref="Soph. Ant. 804"> <title>Ant.</title> 804</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸν παγκοίτην...θάλαμον</quote> </cit> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="παγκευθῆ_1563">, as Hades is <quote xml:lang="grc">πάνδοκος</quote> (n. 1556), <quote xml:lang="grc">πολυδέγμων</quote> ( <title>H. Hymn.</title> 5. 31), <quote xml:lang="grc">παγκοίτας</quote> ( <ref n="Soph. Ant. 810"> <title>Ant.</title> 810</ref>), <quote xml:lang="grc">πολύκοινος</quote> ( <ref n="Soph. Aj. 1193"> <title>Ai.</title> 1193</ref>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πλάκα_1564">(1577, 1681), a plain: cp. the <foreign xml:lang="lat">lugentes campi</foreign> of Vergil's Inferno ( <ref n="Verg. A. 6.441"> <title>Aen.</title> 6. 441</ref>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καὶ_μάταν_1565">. Buecheler's <quote xml:lang="grc"> πολλῶν γὰρ <emph>ἀνταλλαγὰν</emph> πημάτων</quote>is brilliant, though the word occurs only in glossaries, and is slightly prosaic, as properly meaning <quote xml:lang="eng"> "barter."</quote> But it leaves the blot <foreign xml:lang="grc"> ἱκνουμένων</foreign> . I would suggest <foreign xml:lang="grc"> ἱκνούμενον</foreign> , and, for <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἂν καὶ μάταν</foreign> , <foreign xml:lang="grc"> <emph>αὖ</emph> <note anchored="true" place="inline" resp="RCJ" xml:lang="eng"> (or <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἂν</foreign>a)\n</note> <emph>τέρματ’ ἂν</emph> </foreign>: <quote xml:lang="eng"> " <emph>now that he is coming to the goal of many sorrows.</emph> "</quote>So the pl. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 686" ref="Soph. El. 686"> <title>El.</title> 686</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">δρόμου...τὰ τέρματα</quote> </cit>. A doubled <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἄν</foreign> would not be unsuitable here, as expressing earnest hope; but <foreign xml:lang="grc">αὖ</foreign> , which MSS. often confuse with <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄν</quote> , would well mark the turning-point: and for its combination with <foreign xml:lang="grc">πάλιν</foreign> cp. 1418.—Another possibility would be <quote xml:lang="grc">πολλῶν γὰρ ἂν ἀλλαγὰν πημάτων ἱκνούμενον</quote> (cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 1206" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg004:1206"> <title>O. T.</title> 1206</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλαγᾷ βίου</quote> </cit>), or <quote xml:lang="grc">ἱκνουμέναν</quote> as= <quote xml:lang="eng"> "due"</quote> : so oft. <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁ ἱκνούμενος χρόνος, ἡλικία</quote> , etc. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αὔξοι_1567">, <quote xml:lang="eng">"uplift,"</quote> raise to honour: cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 1092"> <title>O. T.</title> 1092</ref>(n.), <ref n="Soph. Trach. 116"> <title>Tr.</title> 116</ref>(n. on 1453 f.). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="χθόνιαι_θεαί_1568">: schol. <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἐρινύες</quote> . Hardly Demeter and Persephone (683), who would not be thus associated with the fell Cerberus. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σῶμά_τ_1568">: the periphrasis suggests a more vivid image of the dread monster: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Trach. 508" ref="Soph. Trach. 508"> <title>Tr.</title> 508</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">φάσμα ταύρου</quote> </cit> : <cit> <bibl n="Verg. A. 6.289" ref="Verg. A. 6.289"> Verg. <title>Aen.</title> 6. 289</bibl> <quote xml:lang="lat">et forma tricorporis umbrae</quote> </cit> (Geryon). <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Phoen. 1508" ref="Eur. Phoen. 1508"> Eur. <title>Phoen.</title> 1508</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">Σφιγγὸς ἀοιδοῦ σῶμα</quote> </cit> : <title>Her. Fur.</title> 24 <quote xml:lang="grc">τρισώματον κύνα.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀνικάτου_1568">is sound, since the long penult. (= <quote xml:lang="grc">θε</quote> of <quote xml:lang="grc">θεόν</quote> 1556) is an <quote xml:lang="eng"> "irrational"</quote> syllable. Meineke's <foreign xml:lang="grc"> ἀμαιμάκου</foreign> is an unexampled form of <quote xml:lang="grc"> ἀμαιμάκετος</quote> . Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Trach. 1097" ref="Soph. Trach. 1097"> <title>Tr.</title> 1097</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="trimeter"> <l>τόν θ’ ὑπὸ χθονὸς</l> <l>Ἅιδου τρίκρανον σκύλακ’, ἀπρόσμαχον τέρας</l> </lg> </quote> </cit>. Homer mentions <quote xml:lang="eng">"the dog of Hades"</quote> only in reference to Eurystheus sending Heracles <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐξ Ἐρέβευς ἄξοντα κύνα στυγεροῦ Ἀΐδαο</quote> ( <ref n="Hom. Il. 8.368"> <title>Il.</title> 8.368</ref>, <ref n="Hom. Od. 11.625"> <title>Od.</title> 11.625</ref>). The name Cerberus occurs first in <ref n="Hes. Th. 311"> Hes. <title>Th.</title> 311</ref>, where he is the offspring of Typhaon and Echidna, and has fifty heads: Horace makes him <cit> <quote xml:lang="lat">centiceps,</quote> <bibl n="Hor. Carm. 2.13.34" ref="Hor. Carm. 2.13.34"> <title>Carm.</title> 2. 13. 34</bibl> </cit> . <quote xml:lang="grc">Κερβέριοι</quote> was used (at least in comedy) as= <quote xml:lang="grc">Κιμμέριοι</quote> , but the connection with <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔρεβος</quote> is doubtful. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πολυξένοις_1569">appears certain: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. Supp. 157" ref="Aesch. Supp. 157"> Aesch. <title>Suppl.</title> 157</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="lyric"> <l>τὸν πολυξενώτατον</l> <l>Ζῆνα τῶν κεκμηκότων</l> <l>ἱξόμεσθα σὺν κλάδοις</l> <l>ἀρτάναις θανοῦσαι</l> </lg> </quote> </cit>. See above on <quote xml:lang="grc">παγκευθῆ</quote> (n. 1561 ff.), and cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 893"> <title>Ant.</title> 893</ref>in n. on 1548. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐξ_ἄντρων_1571"> : <cit> <bibl n="Verg. A. 6.417" ref="Verg. A. 6.417"> Verg. <title>Aen.</title> 6. 417</bibl> <quote xml:lang="lat">Cerberus haec ingens latratu regna trifauci Personat, adverso recubans immanis in antro. — Immania terga resolvit Fusus humi, totoque ingens extenditur antro.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

data.quote:
Cerberus haec ingens latratu regna
									trifauci Personat, adverso recubans immanis in antro. —
									Immania terga resolvit Fusus humi, totoque ingens
									extenditur antro.

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φύλακα_1572">. <cit> <bibl n="Hes. Th. 767" ref="Hes. Th. 767"> Hes. <title>Th.</title> 767</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="hexameter"> <l>ἔνθα θεοῦ χθονίου πρόσθεν δόμοι ἠχήεντες …</l> <l>ἑστᾶσιν· δεινὸς δὲ κύων προπάροιθε φυλάσσει</l> </lg> </quote> </cit>. He fawns on those who enter: <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="hexameter"> <l>ἐξελθεῖν δ’ οὐκ αὖτις ἐᾷ πάλιν, ἀλλὰ δοκεύων</l> <l>ἐσθίει ὅν κε λάβῃσι πυλέων ἔκτοσθεν ἰόντα.</l> </lg> </quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λόγος_ἔχει_1572">, transitive, like <cit> <bibl n="Pind. P. 1" ref="Pind. P. 1"> Pind. <title>P.</title> 1. 96</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐχθρὰ Φάλαριν κατέχει...φάτις</quote> </cit>, rather than intransitive like <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁ λόγος κατέχει</quote> ( <quote xml:lang="eng"> "the report <emph> prevails</emph> that..."</quote>) <ref n="Thuc. 1.10.2">Thuc. 1.10 § 2</ref> . Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Paus. 9.3.9" ref="Paus. 9.3.9">Paus. 9. 3 § 9</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">μαντεύεσθαι δὲ τὰς νύμφας τὸ ἀρχαῖον αὐτόθι ἔχει λόγος</quote> </cit>. </gloss>

data.quote:
μαντεύεσθαι δὲ τὰς νύμφας τὸ ἀρχαῖον
									αὐτόθι ἔχει λόγος

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τόν_1574">(as relat.) is more probable than <foreign xml:lang="grc">ὅν</foreign> after the vowel: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 199" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg004:199"> <title>O. T.</title> 199</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔρχεται· | τόν, ὦ τᾶν πυρφόρων.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Γᾶς_παῖ_1574">. This cannot mean Pluto, who was the son of Cronus and Rhea; nor Cerberus (usu. called son of Typhaon and Echidna), unless with Nauck we change <foreign xml:lang="grc">τόν</foreign> to <foreign xml:lang="grc">δός</foreign> . Thanatos is not elsewhere thus described, (in <ref n="Hes. Th. 211"> Hes. <title>Th.</title> 211</ref>he is the son of <quote xml:lang="grc"> Νύξ</quote> , no father being named,)—but is probably meant here. The invocation in 1578 is certainly addressed to him. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πλάκας_1575">: see on <ref target="cl1564">1564</ref> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸν_αἰένυπνον_1578">, Death, the giver of the <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀτέρμονα νήγρετον ὕπνον</quote> (Moschus 3. 105): in contrast with his brother who <quote xml:lang="grc">λύει πεδήσας</quote> ( <ref n="Soph. Aj. 676"> <title>Ai.</title> 676</ref>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ξυντομώτατον_1579">(neut. as adv.) is the best correction of the MS. <quote xml:lang="grc"> ξυντομωτάτως</quote> . A few such forms in <quote xml:lang="grc">‐ως</quote> have MS. authority in good writers, though they are mostly comparatives, as <quote xml:lang="grc">βεβαιοτέρως, ἐρρωμενεστέρως</quote> (Isocr.), <quote xml:lang="grc">καλλιόνως, σαφεστέρως</quote> , etc. In <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Supp. 967" ref="Eur. Supp. 967"> Eur. <title>Suppl.</title> 967</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">γηράσκω <emph>δυστηνότατος</emph> | οὔτ’</quote> </cit>is corrected by Reiske to <quote xml:lang="grc">δυστηνοτάτως</quote> , which metre commends: but this is an almost isolated example.
