There is thus a strong presump tion in favour of the ordinary form where, as here, it can easily be restored.—Wecklein reads <quote xml:lang="grc"> ξυντομωτάτων</quote> : <quote xml:lang="eng">"I would hit on the briefest mode of speech."</quote> —Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 1234" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg004:1234"> <title>O. T.</title> 1234</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="trimeter"> <l>ὁ μὲν τάχιστος τῶν λόγων εἰπεῖν τε καὶ</l> <l>μαθεῖν, τέθνηκε θεῖον Ἰοκάστης κάρα.</l> </lg> </quote> </cit> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λέξας_ὀλωλότα_1579">: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 463" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg004:463"> <title>O. T.</title> 463</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἶπε... τελέσαντα</quote> </cit>, n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἃ_πραχθέντ_1581"> . <quote xml:lang="grc">ἃ</quote> = <quote xml:lang="grc">ἅτινα</quote> : see on <ref target="cl1171">1171</ref> . <quote xml:lang="eng"> "But as to what the occurrences were, <emph>neither</emph> is the tale possible for me to tell in brief compass, <emph>nor</emph> (were) the events (brief) which happened there"</quote>: <foreign xml:lang="lat"> sc.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὔτε</quote> ( <quote xml:lang="grc">βραχέα ἦν</quote> ) <quote xml:lang="grc">τἄργ’</quote> . That is, resolving the parataxis with <quote xml:lang="grc">οὔτε — οὔτε</quote> :— <quote xml:lang="eng"> "But as to what occurred, the tale cannot be briefly told, <emph>as neither</emph> were the occurrences themselves brief."</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὁ_πάρεστιν_1581">would have sufficed: <foreign xml:lang="grc">φράσαι</foreign> (epexeg. inf.) further defines <quote xml:lang="grc">πάρεστιν</quote> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀπόνῳ_1585">, as they themselves had just prayed for him. Cp. the prayer of Ajax to Hermes Chthonios that he may die <cit> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀσφάδαστος</quote> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 833" ref="Soph. Aj. 833"> ( <title>Ai.</title> 833)</bibl> </cit> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοῦτ_ἤδη_1585"> means, <quote xml:lang="eng"> "here we come to the point which is indeed ( <foreign xml:lang="grc">καὶ</foreign> ) worthy of wonder"</quote>: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Sym. 204B" ref="Plat. Sym. 204B"> Plat. <title>Symp.</title> 204B</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">δῆλον δὴ...τοῦτό γε ἤδη καὶ παιδί, ὅτι</quote> </cit>, etc. </gloss>

data.quote:
δῆλον δὴ...τοῦτό γε ἤδη καὶ παιδί,
									ὅτι

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὑφηγητῆρος_1588">is supported against <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑφ’ ἡγητῆρος</quote> ( <emph>a</emph> ) by such examples as 83, <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς ἐμοῦ μόνης πέλας</quote> , ( <emph>b</emph> ) by the fact that the compound with <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑπό</quote> is suitable where, as here, the sense is that no one so much as hinted or indicated the way. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Crito 54E" ref="Plat. Crito 54E"> Plat. <title>Crito</title> 54E</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">πράττωμεν ταύτῃ, ἐπειδὴ ταύτῃ ὁ θεὸς ὑφηγεῖται</quote> </cit>. So <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 966" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg004:966"> <title>O. T.</title> 966</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὧν ὑφηγητῶν</quote> </cit> ( <quote xml:lang="eng">"on whose showing"</quote> ): <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 1260 <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς ὑφηγητοῦ τινος</quote> . </gloss>

data.quote:
πράττωμεν ταύτῃ, ἐπειδὴ ταύτῃ ὁ θεὸς
									ὑφηγεῖται

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καταρράκτην_1590"> (from <quote xml:lang="grc">ῥάσσω</quote> to strike hard, or dash, cp. 1503), lit. <quote xml:lang="eng">"dashing or rushing down"</quote> ; Strabo 10. 640 <quote xml:lang="grc">τηρήσας καταρράκτην ὄμβρον</quote> : here, of a cleft <emph>descending abruptly</emph> into the ground. Strabo applies the word to the cataracts of the Nile (called <quote xml:lang="grc">κατάδουποι</quote> by Her.): in this sense it is usu. spelt with one <quote xml:lang="grc">ρ</quote> , as if from <quote xml:lang="grc">καταράσσω</quote> : so Lucan 10. 317 <quote xml:lang="lat">praecipites cataractae.</quote> Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Plut. Ad Princ. 781e" ref="Plut. Ad Princ. 781e"> Plut. <title>Mor.</title> 781 E</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οἴκημα θύραν ἔχον ἐπιρρακτήν</quote> </cit>, a room with a trap-door in it: so too <title>Arat.</title> 26 <quote xml:lang="grc">θύρᾳ καταρρακτῇ</quote> . The <foreign xml:lang="lat">v.l.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">καταφράκτην</quote> (Suidas) is worthless.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="χαλκοῖς_βάθροισι_1591"> . <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Il. 8.13" ref="Hom. Il. 8.13"> <title>Il.</title> 8.13</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="hexameter"> <l>ἐς Τάρταρον ἠερόεντα,</l> <l>τῆλε μάλ’, ἧχι βάθιστον ὑπὸ χθονός ἐστι βέρεθρον,</l> <l>ἔνθα σιδήρειαί τε πύλαι καὶ χάλκεος οὐδός</l> </lg> </quote> </cit> . <cit> <bibl n="Hes. Th. 811" ref="Hes. Th. 811"> Hes. <title>Theog.</title> 811</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="hexameter"> <l> <note anchored="true" place="inline" resp="RCJ" xml:lang="eng">(of tartarus)</note>ἔνθα δὲ μαρμάρεαί τε πύλαι καὶ χάλκεος οὐδός,</l> <l>ἀστεμφής, ῥίζῃσι διηνεκέεσσιν ἀρηρώς,</l> <l>αὐτοφυής</l> </lg> </quote> </cit> : <quote xml:lang="eng"> "a brazen threshold, immoveable, <emph>fixed in the earth by roots without a break</emph> , of natural growth,"</quote> <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>not wrought by human hands. The rift or cavern at Colonus, from which the adjoining region took the name of the <quote xml:lang="grc">χαλκοῦς ὀδός</quote> (see on <ref target="cl57">57</ref> ), was locally supposed to be connected with the <quote xml:lang="eng">"brazen threshold"</quote> below by brazen steps reaching down into the under-world. The stress laid on the <foreign xml:lang="grc">χαλκοῖς βάθροισι</foreign> here, and the name <quote xml:lang="eng">"Brazen Threshold"</quote> itself, rather suggest that the myth was visibly symbolised by some artificial steps made at the top of the steep rift. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γῆθεν_1591">, as <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Od. 13.163" ref="Hom. Od. 13.163"> <title>Od.</title> 13.163</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">λᾶαν ἔθηκε καὶ ἐρρίζωσεν ἔνερθεν</quote> </cit>(Poseidon petrifying the Phaeacian ship). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πολυσχίστων_1592">. Several paths converged at the <quote xml:lang="grc">καταρράκτης ὀδός</quote> . Oedipus halted ( <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἔστη</foreign> ) near the <quote xml:lang="grc">ὀδός</quote> , <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> just before the path which he was following met the others. We are reminded, perhaps designedly, of that <quote xml:lang="grc">σχιστὴ ὁδός</quote> in Phocis at which the misfortunes of his early manhood began ( <ref n="Soph. OT 733"> <title>O. T.</title> 733</ref>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κοίλου_κρατῆρος_1593">. (1) Schneidewin takes this to mean <emph>a large brazen vessel</emph> set in a rift of the ground, over which Theseus and Peirithous slew the victims when they made their pact ( <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅρκια</quote> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔταμον</quote> ). He cites <ref n="Eur. Supp. 1201"> Eur. <title>Suppl.</title> 1201</ref>, where Theseus is directed thus to make a covenant with Adrastus; the throats of nine sheep are to be cut over a bronze <quote xml:lang="grc">τρίπους</quote> , and the terms of the pact ( <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅρκοι</quote> ) are then to be graven in its basin ( <quote xml:lang="grc">τρίποδος ἐν κοίλῳ κύτει</quote> ). (2) The schol., whose view is more likely, understands a <emph>basin or hollow in the rock:</emph> <quote xml:lang="grc">κοίλου πέλας κρατῆρος· τοῦ μυχοῦ· τὰ γὰρ κοῖλα οὕτως ἐκάλουν ἐκ μεταφορᾶς· ὅθεν καὶ τὰ ἐν τῇ Αἴτνῃ κοιλώματα κρατῆρες καλοῦνται</quote> . Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Aristot. Mu. 400a.33" ref="Aristot. Mu. 400a.33"> Arist. <title>De Mundo</title> 6</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τῶν ἐν Αἴτνῃ κρατήρων ἀναρραγέντων</quote> </cit>. <ref n="Plat. Phaedo 111D"> Plat. <title>Phaedo</title> 111D</ref>says of the subterranean cavities, <quote xml:lang="grc">συντετρῆσθαί τε πολλαχῇ...καὶ διεξόδους ἔχειν, ᾗ πολὺ μὲν ὕδωρ ῥεῖν ἐξ ἀλλήλων εἰς ἀλλήλους ὥσπερ εἰς κρατῆρας</quote> . The scholiast adds:— <quote xml:lang="grc">λέγει δῑ οὗ</quote> ( <foreign xml:lang="lat">sc.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">μυχοῦ</quote> ) <quote xml:lang="grc">καταβῆναι φασὶ τὴν Κόρην ἁρπαγεῖσαν</quote> . That is, the schol. took this <quote xml:lang="grc">κρατήρ</quote> or <quote xml:lang="grc">μυχός</quote> in the rock to be the actual cavity in which the <quote xml:lang="grc">καταρράκτης ὀδός</quote> began. In each case the <quote xml:lang="grc">κρατήρ</quote> was close to the <quote xml:lang="grc">ὀδός</quote> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Θησέως_1593">. Theseus went down to Hades with Peirithous, king of the Thessalian Lapithae, to help him in carrying off Persephone. Both heroes were made prisoners by Pluto. Theseus was afterwards delivered by Heracles, when sent by Eurystheus to capture Cerberus. According to another version, adopted by Eur. in his <quote xml:lang="grc">Πειρίθους</quote> , Heracles delivered Peirithous also. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Περίθου_1594">. Elsewhere in extant classical literature the form is <quote xml:lang="grc">Πειρίθοος</quote> or (Attic) <quote xml:lang="grc"> Πειρίθους</quote> . But a form <quote xml:lang="grc">Περίθους</quote> is sufficiently attested by the name of the Attic deme of which this hero was eponymus. Harpocr. <quote xml:lang="grc">Περιθοῖδαι· δῆμός ἐστι τῆς Οἰνηΐδος</quote> . <cit> <bibl n="Aeschin. 1.156" ref="Aeschin. 1.156">Aeschin. or. 1 § 156</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">Περικλείδην τὸν Περιθοίδην</quote> </cit>: and so <ref n="Dem. 50.41">[Dem.] or. 50 § 41</ref> . Eustathius (101. 3) notices both forms, and Dindorf ascribes <quote xml:lang="grc"> Περίθουν</quote> to the Paris MSS. of Georgius Syncellus (the Byzantine chronographer, 800 A.D.), p. 299. 11: vases and inscriptions also give it. There is no need, then, to write <quote xml:lang="grc">Πειρίθου Θησέως τε</quote> , as Blaydes does. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κεῖται_ξυνθήματα_1594">: schol. <quote xml:lang="grc">οἷον ὑπομνήματα τῆς πίστεως ἧς ἔθεντο πρὸς ἀλλήλους</quote> : <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> he understood by <foreign xml:lang="grc">κεῖται</foreign> some visible memorial. This seems clearly right. The local belief probably pointed to characters or marks on the rock. See the schol. on <cit> <bibl n="Aristoph. Kn. 785" ref="Aristoph. Kn. 785"> Aristoph. <title>Eq.</title> 785</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔστι δὲ καὶ ἀγέλαστος πέτρα καλουμένη παρὰ τοῖς Ἀθηναίοις, ὅπου καθίσαι φασὶ Θησέα μέλλοντα καταβαίνειν εἰς ᾍδου</quote> </cit>. Leake ( <title>Demi</title> I. 635) conjectured that this <quote xml:lang="grc">πέτρα</quote> may have marked the place ( <quote xml:lang="grc">χωρίον</quote> ) mentioned by <ref n="Paus. 1.18.4">Paus. 1.18.4</ref> as near the temple of Sarapis, S.E. of the acropolis, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔνθα Πειρίθουν καὶ Θησέα συνθεμένους ἐς Λακεδαίμονα καὶ ὕστερον ἐς Θεσπρωτοὺς σταλῆναι λέγουσιν</quote> . But the expeditions named there are distinct from the descent to Hades with which the schol. connects the <quote xml:lang="grc">πέτρα</quote> . And, wherever this <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀγέλαστος πέτρα</quote> was, there must have been a cavern suggestive of the descent to Hades. The schol.'s phrase, <quote xml:lang="grc">παρὰ τοῖς Ἀθηναίοις</quote> , would cover Colonus.</gloss>

data.quote:
ἔστι δὲ καὶ ἀγέλαστος πέτρα καλουμένη
									παρὰ τοῖς Ἀθηναίοις, ὅπου καθίσαι φασὶ Θησέα μέλλοντα
									καταβαίνειν εἰς ᾍδου

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μέσος_1595">usu. takes a simple gen. of the extremes, and is not elsewhere found with <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἀπό</foreign> , but the latter is natural ( <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Parm. 145b" ref="Plat. Parm. 145b"> Plat. <title>Parm.</title> 145 B</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τό γε μέσον ἴσον τῶν ἐσχάτων ἀπέχει</quote> </cit>). </gloss>

data.quote:
τό γε μέσον ἴσον τῶν ἐσχάτων
									ἀπέχει

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοῦ_πέτρου_1595">. It was from Thoricus ( <ref n="Apollod. 2.4.7">Apollod. 2. 4. 7</ref> ) that <quote xml:lang="eng">"radiant Eos caught up Cephalus to the gods"</quote> ( <ref n="Eur. Hipp. 455"> Eur. <title>Hipp.</title> 455</ref>). Hence the name of that place may have been associated in the Athenian mind with the idea of removal to another world. <quote xml:lang="grc">Θορικός</quote> (so Her., Xen., etc.: <quote xml:lang="grc">Θόρικος</quote> schol.) was a town and deme of Attica, belonging to the tribe <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἀκαμαντίς</quote> , on the S.E. coast, about 6 miles N. of Sunium, and 42 S.E. of Colonus. It was reckoned among the twelve towns of the old Attic dodecapolis, and, to judge by the ruins, was a considerable place down to late times (Leake, <title>Demi</title> II. 17—22). If <quote xml:lang="grc"> Θορικίου</quote> is unsound, the familiarity of <quote xml:lang="grc"> Θορίκιοι</quote> as a deme-name may have suggested it. Schneidewin's <foreign xml:lang="grc">τρικορύφου</foreign> rests on the schol. to 57: <quote xml:lang="grc"> καί τις τῶν χρησμοποιῶν φησί· <quote> <lg type="hexameter"> <l>Βοιωτοὶ δ’ ἵπποιο ποτιστείχουσι Κολωνόν,</l> <l>ἔνθα λίθος τρικάρανος ἔχει καὶ χάλκεος οὐδός</l> </lg> </quote> </quote>. But, if <foreign xml:lang="grc">Θορικίου</foreign> came from <quote xml:lang="grc">τρικορύφου</quote> , the genuine word must have been well-nigh obliterated. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κοίλης_ἀχέρδου_1596">: schol. <quote xml:lang="grc">τῆς τὸν πυθμένα ἐχούσης ὑπόκενον, σαπέντα</quote> . The wild pear gave its name to the Attic deme <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἀχερδοῦς</quote> ( <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἀχερδούσιοι</quote> ); as in its other form, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀχράς</quote> , to <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἀχραδίνη</quote> , the E. quarter of Syracuse. If, as the schol. states (n. 1593), the local myth placed the rape of Persephone here, this old tree may have been pointed out as the spot whence she was snatched. An <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐρινεός</quote> (wild figtree) by the Cephisus was connected with a like legend ( <ref n="Paus. 1.38.5">Paus. 1.38.5</ref> ). A wild olive-tree ( <quote xml:lang="grc">κότινος</quote> ) at Troezen was associated with the disaster of Hippolytus (2. 32. 10), as the <quote xml:lang="grc">στρεπτὴ ἐλαία</quote> at Epidaurus (see on <ref target="cl694">694</ref> ) with Heracles. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κἀπὸ_τάφου_1596">. Dobree's <foreign xml:lang="grc">καὐτολαΐνου</foreign> ( <quote xml:lang="eng">"of natural rock,"</quote> cp. on <ref target="cl192"> 192</ref> ) is more ingenious than probable. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Hel. 962" ref="Eur. Hel. 962"> Eur. <title>Helen</title> 962</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τόνδε λάϊνον τάφον</quote> </cit> : <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 328" ref="Soph. El. 328"> <title>El.</title> 328</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">μνῆμα λάϊνον πατρός</quote> </cit>. The <quote xml:lang="grc">λάϊνος τάφος</quote> is opposed to a <quote xml:lang="grc">τύμβος</quote> of earth or a <quote xml:lang="grc"> λάρναξ</quote> of wood ( <ref n="Thuc. 2.34">Thuc. 2.34</ref> ): it would commonly denote an oblong monument with a flat slab ( <quote xml:lang="grc">τράπεζα</quote> ) on top, the sides being sometimes sculptured.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔλυσε_1597">, as <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Trach. 924" ref="Soph. Trach. 924"> <title>Tr.</title> 924</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">λύει τὸν αὑτῆς πέπλον</quote> </cit>: while the midd. in <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Il. 17.318" ref="Hom. Il. 17.318"> <title>Il.</title> 17.318</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">λύοντο δὲ τεύχεα</quote> </cit>refers to Greeks stripping Trojans. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δυσπινεῖς_1597">: cp. 1258. He prepares to put on the garb of the dead. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ῥυτῶν_1598">( <quote xml:lang="grc">ῥέω</quote> ), flowing, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐξ ἀειρύτου κρήνης</quote> (469). Cp. Theophr. <title>Causs. Plantt.</title> 2. 6. 3 (with ref. to correcting the properties of water by mixing different kinds), <quote xml:lang="grc">δῑ ὃ πολλάκις ἂν ἁρμόσειε</quote> <quote xml:lang="grc">λεπτὸν καὶ καθαρὸν μὴ καθαρῷ καὶ παχεῖ, καὶ φρεατιαῖον ναματιαίῳ</quote> (well water with river water), <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ ῥυτὸν καὶ ὄμβριον</quote> (spring or rain water) <quote xml:lang="grc">λιμναίῳ καὶ ἁπλῶς στασίμῳ</quote> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εὐχλόου_1600">, as protecting the young green corn and other young vegetation ( <quote xml:lang="grc">χλόη</quote> ), <cit> <bibl n="Paus. 1.22.3" ref="Paus. 1.22.3">Paus. 1.22.3</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔστι δὲ</quote> </cit>(at Athens) <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ Γῆς κουροτρόφου καὶ Δήμητρος ἱερὸν Χλόης</quote> . She was associated with <quote xml:lang="grc">Γῆ κουροτρόφος</quote> and with Apollo in the <quote xml:lang="grc">Χλόεια</quote> held on 6th Thargelion (latter part of May). Other names given to Demeter as protectress of crops, etc., expressed the ills which she averted, as <quote xml:lang="grc">καῦστις, ἀζησία</quote> (parcher), <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐρυσι—/βη</quote> (mildew), <quote xml:lang="grc">ἑλήγηρις</quote> (popularly referred to <quote xml:lang="grc">εἵλη</quote> , sunshine, but doubtful). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="προσόψιον_1600">, not found elsewhere, is read by L and Suidas. <quote xml:lang="eng">"The hill of Demeter, in full view"</quote> : rather than, <quote xml:lang="eng">"the hill looking on Demeter."</quote> The act. sense is possible ( <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Phil. 1040" ref="Soph. Phil. 1040"> <title>Ph.</title> 1040</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">θεοί τ’ ἐπόψιοι</quote> </cit>), but the other seems better here: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Ant. 1110" ref="Soph. Ant. 1110"> <title>Ant.</title> 1110</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁρμᾶσθ’...εἰς ἐπόψιον τόπον</quote> </cit> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πόρευσαν_1602"> and <foreign xml:lang="grc">πόρευσαν</foreign> are alike admissible in this <quote xml:lang="grc">ῥῆσις</quote> (cp. 1606 ff.), but the former seems preferable on the general principle of not multiplying omissions of augment without necessity. (Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Trach. 560" ref="Soph. Trach. 560"> <title>Tr.</title> 560</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">μισθοῦ )πόρευε</quote> </cit> .) <quote xml:lang="eng"> " <title>Brought</title> this behest,"</quote> <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>the water for which he had asked. <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Phoen. 984" ref="Eur. Phoen. 984"> Eur. <title>Phoen.</title> 984</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ΜΕΝ. χρημάτων δὲ τίς πόρος; | ΚΠ. ἐγὼ πορεύσω χρυσόν</quote> </cit>. Cp. on <ref target="cl1458">1458</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">πόροι.</quote> </gloss>

data.quote:
ΜΕΝ. χρημάτων δὲ τίς πόρος; | ΚΠ. ἐγὼ
									πορεύσω χρυσόν

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ταχεῖ_χρ_1602">.: cp. 885: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Trach. 395" ref="Soph. Trach. 395"> <title>Tr.</title> 395</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">σὺν χρόνῳ βραδεῖ μολών.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λουτροῖς_1602">, as the dead were washed: Lucian <title>De Luctu</title> 11 <quote xml:lang="grc">μετὰ ταῦτα δὲ λούσαντες αὐτοὺς...προτίθενται</quote> . So <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 1405" ref="Soph. Aj. 1405"> <title>Ai.</title> 1405</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">λουτρῶν ὁσίων</quote> </cit>(for the dead Ajax). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ᾗ_νομίζεται_1603">, as the dead were usually dressed for burial, <foreign xml:lang="lat"> i.e.</foreign> in white. Artemidorus <title>Oneir.</title> 2. 3 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνδρὶ δὲ νοσοῦντι λευκὰ ἔχειν ἱμάτια θάνατον προαγορεύει, διὰ τὸ τοὺς ἀποθανόντας ἐν λευκοῖς ἐκφέρεσθαι</quote> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="παντὸς_δρῶντος_1604">. (1) Usu. explained:— <quote xml:lang="eng"> "when he had content of all <emph> service</emph> ,"</quote> <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>when his daughters had done for him all that he wished. Then <quote xml:lang="grc">πᾶν δρῶν</quote> will be <quote xml:lang="eng">"every activity"</quote> of attendants: cp. the Homeric <quote xml:lang="grc">δρηστῆρες, δρήστειραι</quote> , of servants, <ref n="Hom. Od. 10.349"> <title>Od.</title> 10.349</ref>etc. (2) A better view is: <quote xml:lang="eng"> "when <emph>of doing all</emph> he had content"</quote>(as Whitelaw), when <quote xml:lang="grc">πᾶν δρῶν</quote> is his <emph>own</emph> activity. Cp. <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ βουλόμενον τῆς γνώμης</quote> and similar phrases (see on <ref target="cl267">267</ref> ): also <cit> <bibl n="Thuc. 1.142" ref="Thuc. 1.142">Thuc. 1.142</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν τῷ μὴ μελετῶντι</quote> </cit> , <quote xml:lang="eng">"in the absence of practice."</quote> As to <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Trach. 196" ref="Soph. Trach. 196"> <title>Tr.</title> 196</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ γὰρ ποθοῦν ἕκαστος ἐκμαθεῖν θέλων</quote> </cit>,—where <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ ποθοῦν</quote> used to be explained as <quote xml:lang="eng">"the desire within him,"</quote> —it is now generally held to be corrupt (E. Thomas conjectures <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰ γὰρ ποθείν’</quote> ). But the absence of the art. makes <quote xml:lang="grc">πᾶν δρῶν</quote> a bolder expression than any of these; nor can the adverbial <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν ἀμείβοντι</quote> , <quote xml:lang="eng">"alternately,"</quote> ( <ref n="Pind. N. 11"> Pind. <title>N.</title> 11. 42</ref>) be properly compared. I suspect, then, that the text is corrupt. To Mekler's conjecture (see cr. n.) the obstacle is <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἔδρων</foreign> instead of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔδρασαν</quote> . The obvious <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἔρωτος</foreign> ( <quote xml:lang="eng">"desire"</quote> ) should not be too lightly rejected: cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 436">436.</ref> </gloss>

data.quote:
τὸ γὰρ ποθοῦν ἕκαστος ἐκμαθεῖν
									θέλων

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀργὸν_1605">, neglected: see on <ref n="Soph. OT 287"> <title>O. T.</title> 287</ref>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κτύπησε_1606">: for the omission of the augment, see on <ref n="Soph. OT 1249"> <title>O. T.</title> 1249</ref>.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Ζεὺς_χθ_1606"> .: <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Il. 9.457" ref="Hom. Il. 9.457"> <title>Il.</title> 9.457</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">Ζεύς τε καταχθόνιος καὶ ἐπαινὴ Περσεφόνεια</quote> </cit>. At Corinth Pausanias saw three images of Zeus, one being <quote xml:lang="grc">Χθόνιος</quote> , another <quote xml:lang="grc"> Ὕψιστος</quote> , the third nameless (2. 2. 8). The Zeus Chthonios was a benevolent Pluto, associated with Demeter in the prayers of the husbandman ( <ref n="Hes. WD 465"> Hes. <title>Op.</title> 465</ref>). </gloss>

data.quote:
Ζεύς τε καταχθόνιος καὶ ἐπαινὴ
									Περσεφόνεια

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐδ_ἀνίεσαν_1608"> , <quote xml:lang="eng">"did not remit"</quote> (cp. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνιέναι φυλακήν, ἄσκησιν, ἔχθραν</quote> , etc.); <emph> not</emph> , <quote xml:lang="eng">"did not send up"</quote> (as in <ref n="Soph. OT 1277"> <title>O. T.</title> 1277</ref>, a different context). <quote xml:lang="grc">κλαυθμός</quote> was commonly associated with <quote xml:lang="grc">κομμός</quote> ( <foreign xml:lang="lat">planctus</foreign> ) and <quote xml:lang="grc">γόος</quote> . If Soph. had meant otherwise, he would have added another verse with <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἀλλά</foreign> . </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="pammhkeis_1608">, very loud: see on <ref target="cl489">489</ref> . </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐξαίφνης_1610">, because they burst into their wail when the sudden peal of thunder was heard. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πάντα_τἀμά_1613">, all that concerns my earthly life. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὴν_δυσπόν_1614">.: cp. 509: <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. Pers. 515" ref="Aesch. Pers. 515"> Aesch. <title>Pers.</title> 515</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὦ δυσπόνητε δαῖμον.</quote> </cit> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀμφ_ἐμοὶ_1614">: cp. <ref n="Soph. El. 1143"> <title>El.</title> 1143</ref>quoted on 345;</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τροφήν_1614">, <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> and 352. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σκληρὰν_1614">, in appos.; cp. 1173. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀλλὰ_γὰρ_1614"> , <quote xml:lang="eng"> "but (I need not speak of hardship), <emph> for</emph> "</quote>:= <quote xml:lang="eng">"but indeed"</quote> : cp. on <ref target="cl988">988</ref> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἕν_ἔπος_1614"> , <quote xml:lang="eng">"one word,"</quote> viz. <quote xml:lang="grc">φιλεῖν</quote> . Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Ant. 53" ref="Soph. Ant. 53"> <title>Ant.</title> 53</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">μήτηρ καὶ γυνή, διπλοῦν ἔπος</quote> </cit>. (This is better than <quote xml:lang="eng"> "one <emph>saying</emph> ,"</quote> <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign>reflection.) </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λύει_1614">, cancels. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τητώμεναι_1618">: cp. on <ref target="cl1200">1200</ref> .— The simplest view of the MS. <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ λοιπὸν ἤδη βίοτον διάξετον</quote> is Elmsley's, that <quote xml:lang="grc">βίοτον</quote> was written by a mistake for <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸν βίον</quote> . (The error here affords no ground for suspecting <quote xml:lang="grc">βίοτον</quote> in 1584.) But <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῦ βίου</quote> (Suidas) is equally possible: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 1487" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg004:1487"> <title>O. T.</title> 1487</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">νοούμενος τὰ λοιπὰ τοῦ πικροῦ βίου</quote> </cit>. The constr. <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸν λοιπὸν...τοῦ βίου</quote> would be a rare one: <cit> <bibl n="Dem. 15.16" ref="Dem. 15.16">Dem. or. 15 § 16</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">πρὸς τὸν λοιπὸν τοῦ χρόνου</quote> </cit> , <cit> <bibl n="Xen. Cyrop. 4.5.1" ref="Xen. Cyrop. 4.5.1"> Xen. <title>Cyr.</title> 4.5.1</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῦ σίτου...τὸν ἥμισυν</quote> </cit>: so <quote xml:lang="grc">ἡ πολλὴ τῆς γῆς</quote> , etc.</gloss>

data.quote:
νοούμενος τὰ λοιπὰ τοῦ πικροῦ
									βίου

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐπ_ἀμφικ_1620"> : <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> each of the daughters had twined her arms about her father, while he had also embraced them. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Od. 8.523" ref="Hom. Od. 8.523"> <title>Od.</title> 8.523</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς δὲ γυνὴ κλαίῃσι φίλον πόσιν ἀμφιπεσοῦσα.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

data.quote:
ὡς δὲ γυνὴ κλαίῃσι φίλον πόσιν
									ἀμφιπεσοῦσα.

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λύγδην_1620"> from <quote xml:lang="grc">λύζω</quote> , <foreign xml:lang="lat">singultare</foreign> . <cit> <bibl> <title>Anthol. Pal.</title> 15. 28. 3</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">λιγέως ὀλοφύρετο μήτηρ, | λύγδην, ἱσταμένη</quote> </cit>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σιωπή_1623">, a moment of absolute stillness, after the wails had subsided. Job iv. 15 <quote xml:lang="eng">"Then a spirit passed before my face; the hair of my flesh stood up. It stood still, but I could not discern the appearance thereof; a form was before mine eyes: there was silence, and I heard a voice."</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τινὸς_1623">: <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Andr. 1147" ref="Eur. Andr. 1147"> Eur. <title>Andr.</title> 1147</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="trimeter"> <l>πρὶν δή τις ἀδύτων ἐκ μέσων ἐφθέγξατο</l> <l>δεινόν τι καὶ φρικῶδες</l> </lg> </quote> </cit> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θώϋξεν_αὐτόν_1624">. Porson on <ref n="Eur. Phoen. 5"> Eur. <title>Phoen.</title> 5</ref>wished to read <quote xml:lang="grc">θεῶν ἐθώϋξ’</quote> (omitting <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐτόν</quote> ). But the change is unnecessary, if occasional omission of the augment is conceded to such <quote xml:lang="grc">ῥήσεις</quote> as this: cp. 1606. <quote xml:lang="grc">θωΰσσω</quote> denotes a loud, urgent cry (cp. <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Hipp. 219" ref="Eur. Hipp. 219"> Eur. <title>Hipp.</title> 219</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">κυσὶ θωΰξαι</quote> </cit>): here with acc. of the person called. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πάντας_1624">, subject to <foreign xml:lang="grc">στῆσαι</foreign> . For this phrase, instead of <quote xml:lang="grc">πᾶσι στῆναι τρίχας</quote> , see on <ref target="cl150">150</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">φυτάλμιος</quote> . Cp. 1464. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φόβῳ_1624">is causal dat. with <foreign xml:lang="grc">στῆσαι</foreign> , rather than modal dat. with <foreign xml:lang="grc">δείσαντας</foreign> , so that we should not compare <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Trach. 176" ref="Soph. Trach. 176"> <title>Tr.</title> 176</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">φόβῳ... ταρβοῦσαν</quote> </cit> : <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 65" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg004:65"> <title>O. T.</title> 65</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὕπνῳ γ’ εὕδοντα.</quote> </cit> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐξαίφνης_1624">, though it has come in 1623 (and 1610): see on <ref target="cl554">554</ref> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πολλὰ_πολλαχῇ_1626"> , <quote xml:lang="eng">"with repeated and manifold calling."</quote> There seems to be no genuine instance of <quote xml:lang="grc">πολλαχῇ</quote> meaning simply <quote xml:lang="grc">πολλάκις</quote> . It is always <quote xml:lang="eng">"by many routes"</quote> (as <ref n="Xen. Anab. 7.3.12"> Xen. <title> Anab.</title> 7.3.12</ref>), <quote xml:lang="eng">"in many ways"</quote> ( <ref n="Hdt. 6.21">Her. 6.21</ref> ), or <quote xml:lang="eng">"on many grounds"</quote> (id. 1. 42). The phrase here, then, cannot mean " <emph> loudly</emph> ( <quote xml:lang="grc">πολλά</quote> ) and <emph>often</emph> ": nor can it be merely, <quote xml:lang="eng">"again and again."</quote> But <quote xml:lang="grc">πολλαχῇ</quote> need not refer to different <emph>forms of words.</emph> It is enough to understand it of varying tones in which the name was sounded, or of the voice seeming to come from different points at successive moments.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὦ_οὗτος_1627">. So the goddess Athena, calling Ajax to come forth from his tent; <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 71" ref="Soph. Aj. 71"> <title>Ai.</title> 71</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὗτος, σὲ τὸν τὰς</quote> </cit>etc.: 89 <quote xml:lang="grc">ὦ οὗτος, Αἴας, δεύτερόν σε προσκαλῶ</quote> , where <quote xml:lang="grc">Αἴας</quote> is voc. ( <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 482), as <foreign xml:lang="grc"> Οἰδίπους</foreign> here (cp. 461). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὗτος_1627">( <quote xml:lang="eng">"Ho there!"</quote> ), thus used, implies that the person addressed is not duly heeding the speaker; here it helps to express impatience. So, when Medea turns her face away from Jason's smooth words, he cries to her, <quote xml:lang="grc">αὕτη, τί χλωροῖς δακρύοις τέγγεις κόρας</quote> ; etc. ( <ref n="Eur. Med. 922"> <title>Med.</title> 922</ref>). There is nothing of <emph>roughness</emph> in the phrase, except in the particular combination <quote xml:lang="grc">οὗτος σύ</quote> ( <ref n="Soph. OT 532"> <title>O. T.</title> 532</ref>, 1121: <ref n="Eur. Hec. 1280"> Eur. <title>Hec.</title> 1280</ref>).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="χωρεῖν_1628">: cp. the emphatic place of <quote xml:lang="grc">δεῖξαι</quote> , <ref n="Soph. OT 278"> <title>O. T.</title> 278</ref>. Nauck's <quote xml:lang="grc">μέλλομεν; | χώρει·</quote> by the change to the singular number, breaks the companionship of Oedipus with the Unseen. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τἀπὸ_σοῦ_1628"> adv., </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="βραδύνεται_1628"> pass. impers.: delay is made on thy part. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Tro. 74" ref="Eur. Tro. 74"> Eur. <title>Tro.</title> 74</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἕτοιμ’ ἃ βούλει τἀπ’ ἐμοῦ</quote> </cit> : <cit> <bibl n="Aristoph. Pl. 100" ref="Aristoph. Pl. 100"> Ar. <title>Plut.</title> 100</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄφετον με νῦν· ἴστον γὰρ ἤδη τἀπ’ ἐμοῦ</quote> </cit>(for in both places it is <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπό</quote> rather than <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπί</quote> ). Cp. 293. </gloss>

data.quote:
ἄφετον με νῦν· ἴστον γὰρ ἤδη τἀπ’
									ἐμοῦ

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οἱ_1630">, ethic dat., <quote xml:lang="eng">"for him,"</quote> as a grace to him: cp. 81. The enclitic almost adheres to <foreign xml:lang="grc">μολεῖν</foreign> , while <foreign xml:lang="grc">γῆς</foreign> is naturally drawn to <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἄνακτα</foreign> : thus the two monosyllables in the 3rd foot do not hurt the rhythm.—Theseus, with attendants, had followed Oed. to the <quote xml:lang="grc">ὀδός</quote> (see 1589), but had remained apart while the daughters ministered to their father (1598—1603). He is now summoned to approach them.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὁρκίαν_1632">, the conjecture of P. N. Papageorgius, is the best emendation of the certainly corrupt <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἀρχαίαν</foreign> . It gives exactly what we need, viz. such an epithet for <foreign xml:lang="grc"> πίστιν</foreign> as marks the special solemnity of the pledge. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Laws 843A" ref="Plat. Laws 843A"> Plat. <title>Legg.</title> 843A</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">φιλίαν τε καὶ ἔχθραν ἔνορκον</quote> </cit>. The occurrence of <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅρκιος</quote> in <ref n="Soph. OC 1637">1637</ref> cannot be made an objection (cp. <ref target="cl544">544 n.</ref> ); on the contrary, it rather confirms <quote xml:lang="grc">ὁρκίαν</quote> here. Theseus did just what Oedipus asked. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀρχαίαν_1632">has been explained as follows: —(1) <quote xml:lang="eng"> "Thy right hand, <emph>that time-honoured pledge.</emph> "</quote>I agree with Campbell and Bellermann that this is the best version: indeed, I should be disposed to say, the <emph>only</emph> sound one. But in such a context we surely want something more than so general an epithet. (2) Thy pledge, <quote xml:lang="eng"> " <emph>which some day will be old</emph> ,"</quote> <foreign xml:lang="lat"> i.e.</foreign>which you are sure to observe permanently. Herm. supports this extraordinary <quote xml:lang="eng">"prolepsis"</quote> by <ref n="Aesch. Ag. 579"> Aesch. <title>Ag.</title> 579</ref>, where, however, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀρχαῖον γάνος</quote> is rather, <quote xml:lang="eng">"the traditional ornament"</quote> of temples (spoils): unless we should read ( <quote xml:lang="grc">δόμοις</quote> ) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀρχαίοις</quote> . (3) <quote xml:lang="eng">"A pledge of such good faith as you have always observed"</quote> ( <foreign xml:lang="lat">fides perpetuo apud te usu sacrata</foreign> , Ellendt). (4) A modification of the last view refers <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀρχαίαν</quote> to v. 631, as= <quote xml:lang="eng">"the pledge given at the beginning (of our intercourse)."</quote> It is impossible to accept any one of these interpretations.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἑκών_1634">, <quote xml:lang="eng">"if thou canst help it"</quote> : cp. <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Prot. 345D" ref="Plat. Prot. 345D"> Plat. <title>Prot.</title> 345D</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὃς ἂν ἑκὼν μηδὲν κακὸν ποιῇ</quote> </cit>: in prose more often with <quote xml:lang="grc">εἶναι</quote> added, as <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Sym. 214e" ref="Plat. Sym. 214e"> <title>Symp.</title> 214 E</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἑκὼν γὰρ εἶναι οὐδὲν ψεύσομαι</quote> </cit>: almost always in sentences which contain or imply a negative: but <ref n="Hdt. 7.164">Her. 7.164</ref> has <quote xml:lang="grc">ἑκών τε εἶναι καὶ δεινοῦ ἐπιόντος οὐδενὸς...καταθεὶς τὴν ἀρχήν</quote> . </gloss>

data.quote: ὃς ἂν ἑκὼν μηδὲν κακὸν ποιῇ

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μέλλῃς_1635">, <foreign xml:lang="lat">sc.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">τελεῖν</quote> :</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φρονῶν_εὖ_1635"> , <quote xml:lang="eng">"wishing them well."</quote> Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 1066" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg004:1066"> <title>O. T.</title> 1066</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ μὴν φρονοῦσά γ’ εὖ τὰ λῷστά σοι λέγω</quote> </cit>. <quote xml:lang="eng"> "To do all that, <emph>as their well-wisher</emph> , thou seemest likely (to do) with advantage to them."</quote>As a well-wisher will do <emph>his best</emph> ,</gloss>

data.quote:
καὶ μὴν φρονοῦσά γ’ εὖ τὰ λῷστά σοι
									λέγω

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εὖ_φρονῶν_1635">thus practically means, <quote xml:lang="eng">"to the best of thy judgment"</quote> ; but that is not the first sense of the words. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐκ_μέτα_1636">, without making lamentation,—controlling his feelings in presence of the afflicted girls. Vauvilliers: <quote xml:lang="lat"> " <foreign xml:lang="grc">οἶκτος"</foreign> hic est quod nos Galli dicimus <foreign xml:lang="fra">foiblesse.</foreign> </quote> Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Phaedo 117C" ref="Plat. Phaedo 117C"> Plat. <title>Phaedo</title> 117C</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ ἡμῶν οἱ πολλοὶ τέως μὲν ἐπιεικῶς οἷοί τε ἦσαν κατέχειν τὸ μὴ δακρύειν, ὡς δὲ εἴδομεν πίνοντά τε καὶ πεπωκότα</quote> </cit>, (that Socrates had drunk the hemlock,) <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐκέτι, ἀλλ’ ἐμοῦ γε βίᾳ καὶ αὐτοῦ</quote> ( <emph>in spite of myself</emph> ) <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀστακτὶ ἐχώρει τὰ δάκρυα</quote> . If the men of the old Greek world were more easily moved to tears than modern men, at least they knew very well when a man is bound to repress his emotion, if he can. Why, then, obliterate a noble touch by changing <foreign xml:lang="grc">οἴκτου</foreign> —as Wecklein does with Wex and Bothe — to the wretchedly feeble <foreign xml:lang="grc">ὄκνου</foreign> ? </gloss>

data.quote:
καὶ ἡμῶν οἱ πολλοὶ τέως μὲν ἐπιεικῶς
									οἷοί τε ἦσαν κατέχειν τὸ μὴ δακρύειν, ὡς δὲ εἴδομεν
									πίνοντά τε καὶ πεπωκότα

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὅρκιος_1637">: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Ant. 305" ref="Soph. Ant. 305"> <title>Ant.</title> 305</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅρκιος δέ σοι λέγω</quote> </cit> : <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Phil. 811" ref="Soph. Phil. 811"> <title>Ph.</title> 811</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ μήν σ’ ἔνορκον ἀξιῶ θέσθαι</quote> </cit> . </gloss>

data.quote: οὐ μήν σ’ ἔνορκον ἀξιῶ θέσθαι

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀμαυραῖς_1639"> , <quote xml:lang="eng">"dark,"</quote> not guided by eyes: cp. 182 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀμαυρῷ | κώλῳ</quote> . Not <quote xml:lang="eng">"feeble"</quote> (1018), for no increase of physical weakness is among the signs that his end is near: rather is he lifted above his former helplessness (1587). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τλάσας_φρενὶ_1640"> , <quote xml:lang="eng">"ye must make a brave effort of the mind, and depart"</quote> : <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ γενναῖον</quote> , acc. governed by <quote xml:lang="grc">τλάσας</quote> . It might also be adv., like <cit> <quote xml:lang="grc">κάμνοντι τὸ καρτερόν</quote> <bibl n="Theoc. 1" ref="Theoc. 1">Theocr. 1. 41</bibl> </cit>, but an absolute use of <quote xml:lang="grc">τλάσας</quote> seems slightly less probable here. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φρενὶ_1640">, <emph> in</emph> or <emph>with</emph> it. L's <foreign xml:lang="grc">φέρειν</foreign> is conceivably genuine, but in that case <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ γενναῖον</quote> can hardly be so. Bellermann combines them by rendering, <quote xml:lang="eng">"that which it is noble to bear."</quote> This will not serve: nor yet this, <quote xml:lang="eng">"to bear what is noble."</quote> Wecklein suggests <foreign xml:lang="grc">τρέφειν</foreign> : <quote xml:lang="eng">"having brought yourselves to cherish fortitude."</quote> But since the effort demanded is one of <emph>moral</emph> courage, I do not see why the addition of <foreign xml:lang="grc">φρενί</foreign> to <foreign xml:lang="grc">τλάσας</foreign> should offend. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἃ_μὴ_1641">: <quote xml:lang="eng"> " <emph>such things as</emph> 'tis not lawful,"</quote>etc.: cp. 73. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φωνούντων_1641">, masc. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὁ_κύριος_1643">, the master, he who has control of all; since to him alone the <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐξάγιστα</quote> (1526) are to be confided. The word has a further fitness here, since the maidens had been committed to the care of Theseus (cp. n. on <ref n="Soph. OT 1506"> <title>O. T.</title> 1506</ref>). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἰσηκούσαμεν_1645">, simply <quote xml:lang="eng">"heard"</quote> (rather than <quote xml:lang="eng">"obeyed"</quote> ), as <ref n="Soph. Ant. 9"> <title>Ant.</title> 9</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Aj. 318"> <title>Ai.</title> 318</ref>, <ref n="Soph. Trach. 351"> <title>Tr.</title> 351</ref>, 424.—Wecklein suggests <quote xml:lang="grc">φωνήσανθ’ ὅτ’</quote> , with omission of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀστακτὶ... στένοντες.</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ξύμπαντες_1645">, the attendants of Theseus, who had remained apart when their master was summoned to approach Oedipus (1630). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀστακτὶ_1645">: see on <ref target="cl1251">1251</ref> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐξαπείδομεν_1648">. This compound occurs only here, but is not intrinsically more questionable than the Homeric <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐξαποβαίνω, ἐξαποδύνω</quote> , etc. While <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐξορᾶν</quote> = <quote xml:lang="eng"> "to see <emph>at a distance</emph> "</quote>(used in pass. by <ref n="Eur. Her. 675"> Eur. <title>Her.</title> 675</ref> etc.), <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀφορᾶν</quote> <emph>alone</emph> usu. =merely <quote xml:lang="eng">"to regard"</quote> : hence the double compound is really less pleonastic than those just mentioned. So <quote xml:lang="grc"> ἐκπροτιμᾶν</quote> occurs only in <ref n="Soph. Ant. 913"> <title>Ant.</title> 913</ref>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸν_μὲν_1648"> : <quote xml:lang="eng"> "we saw Oedipus,— <emph>him</emph> , I say,— no longer present anywhere, but <title>Theseus</title> , etc."</quote> The <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸν μὲν</quote> comes in, by an afterthought, to prepare the distinction: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Od. 1.115" ref="Hom. Od. 1.115"> <title>Od.</title> 1.115</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="hexameter"> <l>εἰ ποθεν ἐλθὼν</l> <l>μνηστήρων τῶν μὲν σκέδασιν κατὰ δώματα θείη,</l> <l>τιμὴν δ’ αὐτὸς ἔχοι</l> </lg> </quote> </cit> : <quote xml:lang="eng"> "make a scattering of the wooers,— <emph>those men there</emph> ,—in the house, but <emph>himself</emph> have honour,"</quote> etc. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αὐτὸν_1650">, <quote xml:lang="eng">"alone"</quote> : <cit> <bibl n="Aristoph. Ach. 504" ref="Aristoph. Ach. 504"> Aristoph. <title>Ach.</title> 504</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐτοὶ γάρ ἐσμεν οὑπὶ Ληναίῳ τ’ ἀγών</quote> </cit>(citizens without foreigners): cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 221"> <title>O. T.</title> 221</ref>n. </gloss>

data.quote:
αὐτοὶ γάρ ἐσμεν οὑπὶ Ληναίῳ τ’
									ἀγών

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὀμμ_1650">. (object. gen.)</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐπίσκιον_1650">, predicative, <quote xml:lang="grc">ὥστε ἐπισκιάζειν τὰ ὄμματα</quote> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀντέχοντα_1651">, holding over against, from the primary sense of <quote xml:lang="grc"> ἀντί</quote> : so with dat. ( <quote xml:lang="grc">ὄμμασι</quote> ) <ref n="Soph. Phil. 830"> <title>Ph.</title> 830</ref>. —Perhaps nothing else in Greek literature leaves on the mind an impression so nearly akin to that of the awful vision in Job <ref n="Job 4.15">(iv. 15, 16)</ref> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γῆν_Ὄλυμπον_1654">. Theseus bows down and kisses the earth, then suddenly rises, and with upturned face stretches forth his hands towards the sky. The vision which he had just seen moved him to adore both the <quote xml:lang="grc"> χθόνιοι</quote> and the <quote xml:lang="grc">ὕπατοι</quote> . This touch is finely conceived so as to leave the mystery unbroken. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Phil. 1408" ref="Soph. Phil. 1408"> <title>Ph.</title> 1408</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">στεῖχε προσκύσας χθόνα</quote> </cit> : <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Ant. 758" ref="Soph. Ant. 758"> <title>Ant.</title> 758</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τόνδ’ Ὄλυμπον</quote> </cit>(the heaven above us). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐν_λόγῳ_1654"> , <quote xml:lang="eng">"in the same address (or prayer),"</quote> <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> one immediately after the other: not, <quote xml:lang="eng">"on the same account."</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐξέπραξεν_1659">, like <quote xml:lang="grc">διειργάσατο, διεχρήσατο</quote> , <foreign xml:lang="lat">confecit</foreign> , <quote xml:lang="eng">"took his life"</quote> ; cp. <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Hec. 515" ref="Eur. Hec. 515"> Eur. <title>Hec.</title> 515</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">πῶς καί νιν ἐξεπράξατ’</quote> </cit> ; <quote xml:lang="eng">"how indeed did ye take her life?"</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ποντία_κινηθεῖσα_1659"> , <quote xml:lang="eng">"a whirlwind from the sea, suddenly aroused,"</quote> —so as to sweep inland on Colonus, and snatch him out of men's sight. For the locative force of <foreign xml:lang="grc">ποντία</foreign> as= <quote xml:lang="grc">ποντόθεν</quote> , cp. on <ref target="cl119"> 119</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐκτόπιος</quote> . Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Il. 6.345" ref="Hom. Il. 6.345"> <title>Il.</title> 6.345</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="hexameter"> <l> <note anchored="true" place="inline" resp="RCJ" xml:lang="eng">(helen's wish) </note>ὥς μ’ ὄφελ’ ἤματι τῷ ὅτε με πρῶτον τέκε μήτηρ</l> <l>οἴχεσθαι προφέρουσα κακὴ ἀνέμοιο θύελλα</l> <l>εἰς ὄρος ἢ εἰς κῦμα πολυφλοίσβοιο θαλάσσης</l> </lg> </quote> </cit> . </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πομπός_1661">: cp. 1548. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἢ_βάθρον_1661">, the nether world on which the upper world rests. <quote xml:lang="grc">γῆς βάθρον</quote> , earth's firm floor, rocky base: cp. Milton, <quote xml:lang="eng">"Hymn on the Nativity,"</quote> <title>And cast the dark foundations deep.</title> So <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 860" ref="Soph. Aj. 860"> <title>Ai.</title> 860</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἑστίας βάθρον</quote> </cit>is the ground on which the home stands. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀλύπητον_1661">, the MS. reading, is incomparably better than the variant <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἀλάμπετον</foreign> , which I believe to have been merely one of those conjectures in which the old transcribers and commentators sometimes indulged. By <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἀλύπητον</foreign> the poet meant, <quote xml:lang="eng"> " <emph>without pain</emph> "</quote>(to Oed.); though it does not follow that he used the word with definite consciousness of an active sense. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Phil. 687" ref="Soph. Phil. 687"> <title>Ph.</title> 687</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀμφιπλήκτων ῥοθίων</quote> </cit>, the billows that beat around him: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 969" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg004:969"> <title>O. T.</title> 969</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄψαυστος</quote> </cit> , <quote xml:lang="eng">"not touching,"</quote> etc. ( <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 885" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg004:885"> <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 885</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀφόβητος</quote> </cit> , <quote xml:lang="eng">"not fearing,"</quote> is not properly similar, since <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐφοβήθην</quote> was deponent). <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Laws 958E" ref="Plat. Laws 958E"> Plat. <title>Legg.</title> 958E</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰ τῶν τετελευτηκότων σώματα μάλιστα ἀλυπήτως τοῖς ζῶσι... κρύπτειν</quote> </cit>, to bury the dead <emph>with least annoyance</emph> to the living. The passive sense, <quote xml:lang="eng">"not pained,"</quote> — <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> , where all earthly pain is over,—seems less suitable. Pollux 3. 98 says, <quote xml:lang="grc">Πλάτων δὲ καὶ ἀλύπητος ἔχει, ὥσπερ καὶ Σοφοκλῆς ἀλύπητον</quote> : where, since Plat. has the word only in the place just cited, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλύπητος</quote> should perh. be <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλυπήτως</quote> . The second ref. seems to indicate this passage, rather than <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Trach. 168" ref="Soph. Trach. 168"> <title>Tr.</title> 168</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ζῆν ἀλυπήτῳ βίῳ</quote> </cit>, and, if so, proves the existence of the reading as early at least as c. 160 A.D. </gloss>

data.quote:
τὰ τῶν τετελευτηκότων σώματα μάλιστα
									ἀλυπήτως τοῖς ζῶσι... κρύπτειν

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀλάμπετον_1661">(instead of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλαμπές</quote> ) is not attested for the classical age, though it occurs in later poetry ( <title>Anthol. P.</title> 9. 540, etc.), as does also a subst. <quote xml:lang="grc">λαμπέτης</quote> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐ_στενακτὸς_1663"> , <quote xml:lang="eng">"not with wailing."</quote> Some assume a definitely active sense, <quote xml:lang="eng"> " <emph>not wailing</emph> "</quote>; see last n., and add <quote xml:lang="grc">μεμπτός</quote> <quote xml:lang="eng">"blaming"</quote> ( <ref n="Soph. Trach. 446"> <title>Tr.</title> 446</ref>). Others make it definitely passive, <quote xml:lang="eng"> " <emph>not bewailed.</emph> "</quote>The thought is that his end was <quote xml:lang="eng"> "not accompanied by <quote xml:lang="grc">στεναγμοί"</quote> ,</quote>and the poet probably meant to suggest both ideas. Cp. on <quote xml:lang="grc">σωτήριον</quote> 487. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="σὺν_νόσοις_1663">: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 17" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg004:17"> <title>O. T.</title> 17</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">σὺν γήρᾳ βαρεῖς.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀλγεινὸς_1663">, associated with <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄλγος</quote> , here as <emph> feeling</emph> , not as <emph>causing</emph> , it: thus only here. Analogous is <cit> <bibl n="Pind. O. 1" ref="Pind. O. 1"> Pind. <title>Ol.</title> 1.26</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">καθαροῦ λέβητος</quote> </cit>, the cauldron of cleansing, where Fennell cp. <cit> <bibl n="Theoc. 24" ref="Theoc. 24">Theocr. 24. 95</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">καθαρῷ δὲ πυρώσατε δῶμα θεείῳ</quote> </cit> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἰ_δοκῶ_1665"> , <quote xml:lang="eng">"But if I seem not to speak with understanding"</quote> ( <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> if my narrative is thought incredible and foolish), <quote xml:lang="eng">"I would not crave belief from those to whom I seem not sane."</quote> — <quote xml:lang="grc"> <emph>οὐκ ἂν παρείμην</emph> . παρίεμαι</quote>= <quote xml:lang="eng">"to win over to one's own side,"</quote> and so either (1) with <emph>gen.</emph> of pers., <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Rep. 341B" ref="Plat. Rep. 341B"> Plat. <title>Rep.</title> 341B</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐδέν</quote> </cit> (adv.) <quote xml:lang="grc">σου παρίεμαι</quote> , I ask no favour, no mercy, <emph>from</emph> you: or (2) with <emph>acc.</emph> of pers., <title>Legg.</title> 742 B <quote xml:lang="grc">παρέμενος...τοὺς ἄρχοντας ἀποδημείτω</quote> , <quote xml:lang="eng">"when he has persuaded the rulers,"</quote> —obtained their permission: so again <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 951 A. Here it seems better to understand <quote xml:lang="grc">τούτων</quote> than <quote xml:lang="grc">τούτους</quote> . He scorns to deprecate their unbelief. <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Med. 892" ref="Eur. Med. 892"> Eur. <title>Med.</title> 892</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">παριέμεσθα</quote> </cit>(I crave pardon) <quote xml:lang="grc">καί φαμεν κακῶς φρονεῖν</quote> .—His closing words mark <emph>his own</emph> profound belief in the reality of what he has seen. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 550" ref="Soph. El. 550"> <title>El.</title> 550</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="trimeter"> <l>εἰ δὲ σοὶ δοκῶ φρονεῖν κακῶς</l> <l>γνώμην δικαίαν σχοῦσα, τοὺς πέλας ψέγε</l> </lg> </quote> </cit> . <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 1038" ref="Soph. Aj. 1038"> <title>Ai.</title> 1038</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="trimeter"> <l>ὅτῳ δὲ μὴ τάδ’ ἐστὶν ἐν γνώμῃ φίλα,</l> <l>κεῖνός τ’ ἐκεῖνα στεργέτω, κἀγὼ τάδε</l> </lg> </quote> </cit> . <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Ant. 469" ref="Soph. Ant. 469"> <title>Ant.</title> 469</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="trimeter"> <l>σοὶ δ’ εἰ δοκῶ νῦν μῶρα δρῶσα τυγχάνειν,</l> <l>σχεδόν τι μώρῳ μωρίαν ὀφλισκάνω</l> </lg> </quote> </cit>. To the ancient Greek, who enjoyed discussion, there was something peculiarly impressive in declining it. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="χοἱ_προπέμψ_1667">: meaning Theseus (295 n.), though the plur. might also be explained of Theseus <emph>with</emph> his attendants (1646). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀσήμονες_1667"> = <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄσημοι</quote> , only here. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αἰαῖ_φεῦ_1670">. To delete <foreign xml:lang="grc">φεῦ</foreign> here seems a less probable remedy than to supply <foreign xml:lang="grc">τοι</foreign> in 1697, where the neighbourhood of <foreign xml:lang="grc">καὶ</foreign> may have caused its loss. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔστιν_δή_1670">. The passage is simple if it is only remembered that <foreign xml:lang="grc">οὐ τὸ μέν, ἄλλο δὲ μή</foreign> is an adverbial phrase, equivalent to <quote xml:lang="grc">παντελῶς</quote> . <quote xml:lang="eng"> "It is indeed for us twain <emph>in no incomplete sense</emph> to bewail the accurst blood of our father which was born in us, hapless that we are."</quote>While he lived, they suffered with him. Now, his fate has snatched him from them in strange and terrible sort, leaving them destitute. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐ_μή_1670"> ( <quote xml:lang="grc">μή</quote> , instead of <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ</quote> , because it goes with the inf. <quote xml:lang="grc">στενάζειν</quote> ), <quote xml:lang="eng"> "not in <emph> one</emph> respect merely, with the exception of some other"</quote>; not merely <emph>partially.</emph> This phrase is frequent where the notion of <emph>completeness</emph> or <emph>universality</emph> is to be brought out with greater emphasis than would be given by the mere use of <quote xml:lang="grc">πᾶς</quote> or like words. <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. Pers. 802" ref="Aesch. Pers. 802"> Aesch. <title>Pers.</title> 802</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">συμβαίνει γὰρ οὐ τὰ μὲν τὰ δ’ οὔ</quote> </cit>, <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="eng">"for our disasters are complete."</quote> <cit> <bibl n="Hdt. 1.139" ref="Hdt. 1.139">Her. 1.139</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ τὰ μέν, τὰ δ’ οὔ, ἀλλὰ πάντα ὁμοίως</quote> </cit>: so <ref n="Hdt. 2.37">id. 2. 37</ref> : <cit> <bibl>Phocylides fr. 1</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">Λέριοι κακοί, οὐχ ὁ μέν, ὃς δ’ οὔ, | πάντες</quote> </cit>: <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Phoen. 1641" ref="Eur. Phoen. 1641"> Eur. <title>Phoen.</title> 1641</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="trimeter"> <l>οὐ γὰρ τὸ μέν σοι βαρὺ κακῶν, τὸ δ’ οὐ βαρύ,</l> <l>ἀλλ’ εἰς ἅπαντα δυστυχὴς ἔφυς, πάτερ</l> </lg> </quote> </cit> : <ref n="Plat. Rep. 475B"> Plat. <title>Rep.</title> 475B</ref>, etc. The idiom strikingly illustrates three tendencies of Greek; (1) love of antithesis, (2) love of parataxis, (3) the tendency to treat whole clauses as virtually adverbs (cp. <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐκ ἔσθ’ ὅπως οὐ, οἶδ’ ὅτι</quote> , etc.). </gloss>

data.quote:
συμβαίνει γὰρ οὐ τὰ μὲν τὰ δ’
									οὔ

οὐ τὰ μέν, τὰ δ’ οὔ, ἀλλὰ πάντα
									ὁμοίως

Λέριοι κακοί, οὐχ ὁ μέν, ὃς δ’ οὔ, |
									πάντες

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔμφυτον_1671">, <quote xml:lang="eng">"planted in us at our birth"</quote> : whereby they are sharers in the hereditary <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀρά</quote> on the Labdacid race. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄλαστον_1671">: cp. on <ref target="cl1482">1482</ref> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αἷμα_1671">, as kinsfolk are of the same <quote xml:lang="eng">"blood"</quote> : cp. <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Phoen. 246" ref="Eur. Phoen. 246"> Eur. <title>Phoen.</title> 246</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">κοινὸν αἷμα, κοινὰ τέκεα</quote> </cit> : <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 1406" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg004:1406"> <title>O. T.</title> 1406</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">αἷμ’ ἐμφύλιον</quote> </cit>, an incestuous kinship. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ᾧτινι_1673">, dat. of interest, for whom: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 508" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg007:508">508</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τοῖς τεκοῦσι γὰρ | οὐδ’ εἰ πονεῖ τις</quote> </cit>. As making the sense of <foreign xml:lang="grc">πόνον</foreign> clearer, the dat. is preferable to the nom. dual, <foreign xml:lang="grc">ὥτινε</foreign> (Badham). </gloss>

data.quote:
τοῖς τεκοῦσι γὰρ | οὐδ’ εἰ πονεῖ
									τις

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸν_πολὺν_1673">: for the art. cp. on <ref target="cl87">87</ref> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐν_πυμάτῳ_1675"> , <quote xml:lang="eng">"at the last,"</quote> <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="eng">"at his death,"</quote> as opp. to <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἄλλοτε μέν</foreign> , <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="eng">"during his life."</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀλόγιστα_1675">, things which baffle <quote xml:lang="grc">λογισμός</quote> , things which transcend human reason. As <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἰδόντε</foreign> shows, the reference is to the mysterious manner of their father's death, while <foreign xml:lang="grc">παθούσα</foreign> marks their loss by that death. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="παροίσομεν_1675">can only be explained with Hermann, as= <quote xml:lang="eng">"we shall bring forward,"</quote> <quote xml:lang="eng">"allege."</quote> <quote xml:lang="eng"> "And <emph>we shall have to tell</emph> of things baffling reason, as seen and suffered by us at the end."</quote>This will seem less strained, I think, if we observe that Antigone need not be supposed to know of the <title>Messenger's</title> narrative. She may believe that she is bringing the Chorus the first intelligence of the event; and, if so, <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἀλόγιστα παροίσομεν</foreign> would be no unsuitable preface. This view agrees with the next words of the Chorus, who ask <foreign xml:lang="grc">τί δ’ ἔστιν</foreign> ; as if uncertain what she means; and <foreign xml:lang="grc">βέβηκεν</foreign> ; as if they did not <emph>know</emph> that Oedipus was gone. They do not wish to check the flow of her sorrow, to which utterance will be a relief. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Eur. IA 981" ref="Eur. IA 981"> Eur. <title>I. A.</title> 981</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">αἰσχύνομαι δὲ παραφέρουσ’ οἰκτροὺς λόγους</quote> </cit>, <quote xml:lang="eng">"advancing a plea to pity"</quote> (unless <quote xml:lang="eng"> "bringing <emph>in</emph> "</quote>be preferable). <cit> <bibl n="Hdt. 9.26" ref="Hdt. 9.26">Her. 9.26</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ καινὰ καὶ παλαιὰ παραφέροντες ἔργα</quote> </cit>, <quote xml:lang="eng">"citing"</quote> (as claims).—We cannot render <foreign xml:lang="grc">παροίσομεν</foreign> <quote xml:lang="eng">"we shall suffer beside"</quote> (over and above our former sufferings), since the reference is to the fact of their bereavement, not to its prospective consequences.—Though the phrase is certainly strange, yet the defence indicated above may at least avail in arrest of judgment. If <foreign xml:lang="grc">παροίσομεν</foreign> were to be altered, I should be disposed to suggest <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἐπεράσαμεν</foreign> ( <quote xml:lang="eng">"we have gone through,"</quote> cp. <quote xml:lang="grc">περᾶν κίνδυνον</quote> etc.). The more obvious <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄπορ’ οἴσομεν</quote> and <quote xml:lang="grc"> ἀπορήσομεν</quote> are barred by the context.</gloss>

data.quote:
καὶ καινὰ καὶ παλαιὰ παραφέροντες
									ἔργα

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἰδόντε_παθούσα_1676">. The difficulty is to explain how, if <foreign xml:lang="grc">παθόντε</foreign> originally stood here, it was changed in the MSS. to <foreign xml:lang="grc">παθούσα</foreign> , when <foreign xml:lang="grc"> ἰδόντε</foreign> (which metre requires) was more likely to cause an opposite change. I therefore leave <foreign xml:lang="grc">παθούσα</foreign> in the text. And it is important to notice that a similar combination of forms (both attested by metre) occurs in an Attic inscription of about the second cent. B.C., edited by Kaibel, <title>Epigr.</title> 1110: <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="hexameter"> <l>λευκοῖσιν φάρεσσι καλυψαμένα χρόα καλὸν</l> <l>ἀθανάτων μετὰ φῦλον ἴτον προλιπόντ’ ἀνθρώπους</l> <l>Αἰδὼς Εὐνομίη τε</l> </lg> </quote>.— Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Andr. 1214" ref="Eur. Andr. 1214"> Eur. <title>Andr.</title> 1214</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὦ κακὰ παθὼν ἰδών τε</quote> </cit>.—See Appendix. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐκ_1677">requires us to omit <foreign xml:lang="grc">μέν</foreign> or else to alter v. 1704, where see n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὡς_λάβοις_1678">, as thou mightest most desire (that he should pass away). <quote xml:lang="grc">λαμβάνειν τι ἐν πόθῳ</quote> , to take a thing into one's desires, to conceive a wish for it; cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Ant. 897" ref="Soph. Ant. 897"> <title>Ant.</title> 897</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν ἐλπίσιν τρέφω</quote> </cit> : <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν ὀργῇ ἔχειν τινά</quote> ( <ref n="Thuc. 2.21">Thuc. 2.21</ref> ). For <quote xml:lang="grc"> λαμβάνειν</quote> of mental conception, cp. 729.—The MS. <foreign xml:lang="grc">εἰ</foreign> (for <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἐν</foreign> ) seems a mere mistake. The construction <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς μάλιστα ἂν πόθῳ λάβοις, εἰ</quote> ( <quote xml:lang="grc">λάβοις</quote> ) is intolerable here. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τί_ὅτῳ_1679"> : <quote xml:lang="eng">"How else, when he,"</quote> etc. For the causal use of the relat. see on <ref target="cl263">263</ref> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μήτ_πόντος_1679">. His death was <emph>sudden</emph> , yet not <emph>violent.</emph> Death in battle and death by drowning are taken as types of the death which is both sudden and violent. Schol.: <quote xml:lang="grc">ᾧτινι μήτε πόλεμος μήτε νόσος ἐπῆλθεν</quote> . This certainly looks as if he read something else than <foreign xml:lang="grc">πόντος</foreign> . Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Ant. 819" ref="Soph. Ant. 819"> <title>Ant.</title> 819</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="anapests"> <l>οὔτε φθινάσιν πληγεῖσα νόσοις</l> <l>οὔτε ξιφέων ἐπίχειρα λαχοῦσ’</l> </lg> </quote> </cit>. Hence the conjecture <foreign xml:lang="grc">νοῦσος</foreign> , a form which the Attic poets nowhere use. Wecklein's <foreign xml:lang="grc"> πυρετὸς</foreign> is too specific (as if one said, <quote xml:lang="eng">"neither the War-God, nor typhoid"</quote> ). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄσκοποι_πλάκες_1681">, the <quote xml:lang="eng">"viewless fields"</quote> of the nether world (cp. on <ref target="cl1564">1564</ref> ). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φερόμενον_1681">, pass., is clearly right, "borne away," helping <foreign xml:lang="grc"> ἔμαρψαν</foreign> to express sudden and swift disappearance. <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Phaedo 98B" ref="Plat. Phaedo 98B"> Plat. <title>Phaedo</title> 98B</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπὸ δὴ θαυμαστῆς ἐλπίδος... ᾠχόμην φερόμενος</quote> </cit>, <quote xml:lang="eng"> "from what a summit of hope was I hurled <emph> headlong</emph> "</quote>: <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Rep. 496d" ref="Plat. Rep. 496d"> <title>Rep.</title> 496 D</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐν χειμῶνι κονιορτοῦ καὶ ζάλης ὑπὸ πνεύματος φερομένου</quote> </cit>. The midd. <foreign xml:lang="grc">φερόμεναι</foreign> , as <quote xml:lang="eng">"carrying off to themselves,"</quote> would be somewhat strange, and also much less forcible. </gloss>

data.quote:
ἀπὸ δὴ θαυμαστῆς ἐλπίδος... ᾠχόμην
									φερόμενος

ἐν χειμῶνι κονιορτοῦ καὶ ζάλης ὑπὸ
									πνεύματος φερομένου

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὀλεθρία_νὺξ_1683">: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 1222" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg004:1222"> <title>O. T.</title> 1222</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">κατεκοίμησα τοὐμὸν ὄμμα</quote> </cit>, I have closed my eyes (as in death),—said, as here, in despairing grief. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀπίαν_γᾶν_1685">, some distant land, the Homeric <quote xml:lang="grc">)α^πίη γαίη</quote> ( <ref n="Hom. Il. 1.270"> <title>Il.</title> 1.270</ref>etc.). If the regular quantity, <foreign xml:lang="grc">)α^πίαν</foreign> , is to be kept here, we must read <quote xml:lang="grc">τόσον</quote> , with Arndt, for <quote xml:lang="grc"> τοσόνδ’</quote> , in 1712. But <quote xml:lang="grc">τοσόνδ’</quote> is there confirmed by metre (see Metrical Analysis). In this word <foreign xml:lang="grc">α—</foreign> is not found elsewhere. But, by a converse license, <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἀπία</quote> (see on <ref target="cl1303">1303</ref> ) had sometimes <quote xml:lang="grc">α^</quote> in later epos; and if, in poetical usage, the quantity of <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἀπία</quote> could thus be affected by association with <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπίη</quote> , it is conceivable that the influence should have been reciprocal. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀλώμεναι_1685">with acc. of space traversed, as <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 30" ref="Soph. Aj. 30"> <title>Ai.</title> 30</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">πηδῶντα πεδία.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δύσοιστον_1685">, not <quote xml:lang="grc">‐ου</quote> , since <quote xml:lang="grc">βίου — τροφάν</quote> form one notion; cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Ant. 793" ref="Soph. Ant. 793"> <title>Ant.</title> 793</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">νεῖκος— ἀνδρῶν ξύναιμον</quote> </cit> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κατὰ_ἕλοι_1689"> = <quote xml:lang="grc">καθέλοι</quote> : so 1709 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀνὰ...στένει</quote> (cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 199"> <title>O. T.</title> 199</ref>n.). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φόνιος_1689"> here= <quote xml:lang="eng">"deadly,"</quote> in a general sense, as <ref n="Soph. OT 24"> <title>O. T.</title> 24</ref>(n.) <quote xml:lang="grc">φοινίου σάλου</quote> (of the plague). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πατρὶ_γεραιῷ_1690"> are not suspicious in themselves (though Nauck demurs to calling a dead man <quote xml:lang="grc">γεραίος</quote> ); but they are in metrical excess of 1715 f. Now, if <foreign xml:lang="grc">ξυνθανεῖν γεραιῷ</foreign> is omitted, <foreign xml:lang="grc">πατρί</foreign> must go also, or else be altered. For <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἕλοι πατρί</foreign> could not mean "take <emph>for</emph> " ( <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> to join) <quote xml:lang="eng">"my father."</quote> I prefer to leave <foreign xml:lang="grc">πατρὶ ξυνθανεῖν γεραιῷ</foreign> , and to suppose a lacuna after 1715. The sense is: <quote xml:lang="eng"> "may deadly Hades lay me low ( <quote xml:lang="grc">καθέλοι"</quote> ), so that I may share the death of mine aged sire.</quote>Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 516" ref="Soph. Aj. 516"> <title>Ai.</title> 516</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="trimeter"> <l>καὶ μητέρ’ ἄλλη μοῖρα τὸν φύσαντά τε</l> <l>καθεῖλεν Ἅιδου θανασίμους οἰκήτορας</l> </lg> </quote> </cit> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="γ_μέλλων_1691">is struck out by some. But it suits the sense, and it also fits the antistrophic metre, if in 1718 we add <foreign xml:lang="grc">τὰς</foreign> (with Hermann) before <foreign xml:lang="grc">πατρός</foreign> , —an addition probable in itself. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸ_θεοῦ_1693">,=the fortune from the god.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸ_φέρον_1693">in this sense admits of two explanations. (1) <quote xml:lang="eng"> " <title>That which brings</title> "</quote>good or evil. This view seems confirmed by the analogy of <foreign xml:lang="lat">fors</foreign> , <foreign xml:lang="lat">fortuna</foreign> ( <foreign xml:lang="lat">ferre</foreign> ): <cit> <bibl n="Ter. Ph. 1.2" ref="Ter. Ph. 1.2"> Ter. <title>Ph.</title> 1. 2. 88</bibl> <quote xml:lang="lat">quod fors feret, feremus</quote> </cit> : <cit> <bibl n="Cic. Att. 7.14" ref="Cic. Att. 7.14"> Cic. <title>Att.</title> 7. 14</bibl> <quote xml:lang="lat">ut fors tulerit</quote> </cit>, etc. (2) <quote xml:lang="eng"> " <title>That which carries</title> "</quote>or <quote xml:lang="eng"> " <emph>leads</emph> "</quote>us forward, in a course which we cannot control (cp. <quote xml:lang="grc">ἡ ὁδὸς φέρει ἐκεῖσε</quote> , and like phrases). This view might seem to be supported by the epigram of Palladas (c. <date when="0400">400 A.D.</date> ) in <title>Anthol. P.</title> 10. 73 <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="elegiacs"> <l>ᾗ τὸ φέρον σε φέρει, φέρε καὶ φέρου· εἰ δ’ ἀγανακτεῖς,</l> <l>καὶ σαυτὸν λυπεῖς, καὶ τὸ φέρον σε φέρει</l> </lg> </quote> : <quote xml:lang="eng">"as Fortune bears thee on, bear, and be borne; but if thou chafest, thou vexest thine own soul, and (none the less) she bears thee on."</quote> There, however, <quote xml:lang="grc">σε φέρει</quote> is said for the sake of a play on the word, and hardly warrants an inference as to the way in which <foreign xml:lang="grc">τὸ φέρον</foreign> was usually understood.—The conjecture <foreign xml:lang="grc">τὸ παρὸν</foreign> (cp. 1540) would be plausible only if there were reasons for thinking that <foreign xml:lang="grc">τὸ φέρον</foreign> in this sense was a phrase of postclassical date. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὔτοι_ἔβητον_1695"> , <quote xml:lang="eng">"ye have fared not blameably"</quote> : ye cannot justly complain of the destiny which has removed your father, in old age, by a painless death (cp. 1678). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κατάμεμπτα_1695">, neut. pl. as adv.: cp. on <ref target="cl319">319</ref> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="βαίνειν_1695">does not occur elsewhere in a strictly similar use, for we cannot compare the <emph> perf.</emph> <cit> <quote xml:lang="grc">εὖ βεβηκώς</quote> <bibl n="Soph. El. 979" ref="Soph. El. 979"> ( <title>El.</title> 979)</bibl> </cit> as= <quote xml:lang="eng"> " <emph>placed</emph> well,"</quote> <quote xml:lang="eng">"prosperous."</quote>But there is at least some analogy in such figurative uses of it as <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Her. 625" ref="Eur. Her. 625"> Eur. <title>Her.</title> 625</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἁ δ’ ἀρετὰ βαίνει διὰ μόχθων</quote> </cit>, the path of virtue lies through troubles; <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Her. 630" ref="Eur. Her. 630"> <title>H. F.</title> 630</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὧδ’ ἔβητ’ ἐπὶ ξυροῦ;</quote> </cit> <quote xml:lang="eng">"had ye come into such peril?"</quote> <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Phil. 20" ref="Soph. Phil. 20"> <title>Ph.</title>20</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">σὸς οἶκος βήσεται δῑ αἵματος</quote> </cit> , <quote xml:lang="eng">"will pass through deeds of blood"</quote> :—where a certain course of <emph>fortune</emph> is expressed. Indeed, the metaphor is so easy and natural as hardly to demand special warrant in the case of <quote xml:lang="grc">βαίνω</quote> : <foreign xml:lang="lat">e.g.</foreign> <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 883" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg004:883"> <title>O. T.</title> 883</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ δέ τις ὑπέροπτα χερσὶν ἢ λόγῳ πορεύεται</quote> </cit>( <quote xml:lang="eng">"walks haughtily"</quote> ). I hold, then, that no suspicion of the text can fairly be founded on <foreign xml:lang="grc"> ἔβητον</foreign> . </gloss>

data.quote: ἁ δ’ ἀρετὰ βαίνει διὰ μόχθων

εἰ δέ τις ὑπέροπτα χερσὶν ἢ λόγῳ
									πορεύεται

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="_τοι_1697">: see on <ref target="cl1670">1670</ref> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄρ_ἦν_1697">. The impf. of <emph>new perception:</emph> <quote xml:lang="eng">"there was such a thing, then"</quote> (all the time), though I did not know it before: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Phil. 978" ref="Soph. Phil. 978"> <title>Ph.</title> 978</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅδ’ ἦν ἄρα | ὁ ξυλλαβών με</quote> </cit>: Eur. fr. 807 <quote xml:lang="grc">μέγιστον ἆρ’ ἦν ἡ φύσις</quote> : <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Gorg. 508C" ref="Plat. Gorg. 508C"> Plat. <title>Gorg.</title> 508C</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἃ Πῶλον αἰσχύνῃ ᾤου συγχωρεῖν, ἀληθῆ ἄρα ἦν</quote> </cit>, <quote xml:lang="eng">"were true all the time."</quote> (Distinguish the impf. of <emph>previous admission: ib.</emph> 478 C <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ...τοῦτ’ ἦν εὐδαιμονία</quote> , <quote xml:lang="eng">"happiness, we agreed, was not this."</quote> ) </gloss>

data.quote: ὅδ’ ἦν ἄρα | ὁ ξυλλαβών με

ἃ Πῶλον αἰσχύνῃ ᾤου συγχωρεῖν, ἀληθῆ
									ἄρα ἦν

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸν_1698">= <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐτόν</quote> : cp. 742. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὦ_φίλος_1700">: for the nom. cp. on <ref target="cl185">185</ref> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸν_σκότον_1700"> , the eternal darkness beneath the earth: there is no warrant for <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸν ἀεί</quote> with ellipse of <quote xml:lang="grc"> χρόνον</quote> as= <quote xml:lang="eng">"for ever"</quote> (cp. 1584). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εἱμένος_1700">: <cit> <bibl n="Pind. N. 11" ref="Pind. N. 11"> Pind. <title>N.</title> 11. 15</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="lyric"> <l>θνατὰ μεμνάσθω περιστέλλων μέλη,</l> <l>καὶ τελευτὰν ἁπάντων γᾶν ἐπιεσσόμενος</l> </lg> </quote> </cit> : <cit> <bibl n="Xen. Cyrop. 6.4.6" ref="Xen. Cyrop. 6.4.6"> Xen. <title>Cyr.</title> 6.4.6</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπομνύω...βούλεσθαι ἂν...γῆν ἐπιέσσασθαι μᾶλλον ἢ ζῆν</quote> </cit> . </gloss>

data.quote:
ἐπομνύω...βούλεσθαι ἂν...γῆν
									ἐπιέσσασθαι μᾶλλον ἢ ζῆν

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐδέ_ἔνερθ_1702">is Wecklein's correction of the corrupt <foreign xml:lang="grc">οὐδὲ γέρων</foreign> . In Linwood's <foreign xml:lang="grc">οὐδὲ γὰρ ὣς</foreign> (which Hartung and Blaydes adopt), <foreign xml:lang="grc">γάρ</foreign> will refer to her addressing him as <quote xml:lang="grc">ὦ φίλος</quote> (1700). We might also conjecture <foreign xml:lang="grc">οὐδ’ ἐκεῖ ὢν</foreign> , <quote xml:lang="eng">"not even in that other world"</quote> ( <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 1372" ref="Soph. Aj. 1372"> <title>Ai.</title> 1372</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">κἀκεῖ κἀνθάδ’ ὤν</quote> </cit>): for the hiatus cp. 1720 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀλλ’ ἐπεὶ ὀλβίως.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="οὐδὲ_γέρων_1702">yields no intelligible sense. (1) <quote xml:lang="eng"> "Even though thou <emph> wast</emph> old at the time of thy death."</quote>(2) <quote xml:lang="eng"> "Even though thou <emph>art</emph> old in Hades,"</quote>—the dead being supposed to remain such as they were at the time of death. (3) <quote xml:lang="eng">"Even when they memory is old"</quote> — <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> after the lapse of years. This last is untenable: while neither (1) nor (2),—which Campbell blends by rendering <quote xml:lang="eng">"Even old as thou wast (or art),"</quote> —seems appropriate. She could hardly say that they would still love him <emph>though</emph> he had been so long with them, and had died at a ripe age. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="εὐσκίαστον_1707">: cp. on <ref target="cl406">406</ref> . <cit> <bibl n="Pind. P. 11" ref="Pind. P. 11"> Pind. <title>P.</title> 11. 21</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">Ἀχέροντος ἀκτὰν παρ’ εὔσκιον.</quote> </cit> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πένθος_ἄκλαυτον_1707">: lit. <quote xml:lang="eng">"he did not leave behind him a mourning unhonoured by tears,"</quote> — <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> he is duly mourned by weeping friends, as the spirits of the dead desired. Solon fr. 21 <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="elegiacs"> <l>μηδέ μοι ἄκλαυτος θάνατος μόλοι, ἀλλὰ φίλοισιν</l> <l>ποιήσαιμι θανὼν ἄλγεα καὶ στοναχάς</l> </lg> </quote> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τόδ_ἀναστένει_1709">, it is truer to regard <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἐμὸν ὄμμα</foreign> as a periphrasis for <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐγώ</quote> than <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἀναστένει</foreign> as a mere synonym for <quote xml:lang="eng">"mourns."</quote> Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 139" ref="Soph. Aj. 139"> <title>Ai.</title> 139</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">πεφόβημαι | πτηνῆς ὡς ὄμμα πελείας</quote> </cit>, <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 977" ref="Soph. Aj. 977"> <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 977</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὦ φίλτατ’ Αἴας, ὦ ξύναιμον ὄμμ’ ἐμοί</quote> </cit>. </gloss>

data.quote:
ὦ φίλτατ’ Αἴας, ὦ ξύναιμον ὄμμ’
									ἐμοί

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸ_ἄχος_1711">, grief for thee: cp. 419 n. </gloss>
