<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀφανίσαι_1711">, do away with, overcome (not, <quote xml:lang="eng">"conceal"</quote> ). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τόσον_1711">, Arndt's correction of <foreign xml:lang="grc">τοσόνδ’</foreign> , would give us the normal <foreign xml:lang="grc">)α^πίαν</foreign> in <ref n="Soph. OC 1685">1685</ref> <ref target="cl1685">(n.)</ref> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὤμοι_1713">is Wecklein's correction of <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἰὼ μή</foreign> . That <foreign xml:lang="grc">μή</foreign> was an error for <foreign xml:lang="grc"> μοι</foreign> had already been surmised by some old corrector (see cr. n.). Hermann defended <foreign xml:lang="grc">μή</foreign> by taking it with <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἔχρῃζες</foreign> as= <quote xml:lang="eng">"would that thou hadst not wished"</quote> —an unheardof construction (cp. on <ref target="cl540">540</ref> ). He took <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔρημος ὧδέ … μοι</quote> as= <quote xml:lang="eng">"lonely, just as thou wast, for me,"</quote> — <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> in his wanderings before he had found Attic friends; since, if he had died while still alone with her, she could have given him burial herself.—With <foreign xml:lang="grc">ὤμοι</foreign> render:— <quote xml:lang="eng"> "Ah me, it was thy <emph>wish</emph> to die in a strange land (and so far thy death is well): but thus (by this manner of death) thou hast died <emph>forlorn in regard to me</emph> "</quote>( <foreign xml:lang="grc">μοι</foreign> ethic dat.). She means, <quote xml:lang="eng"> "I have had no opportunity of rendering thee the due rites, and now I do not know the place of thy grave, so as to make the <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐναγίσματα"</quote> at it.</quote>Hence her passionate desire to find his grave (1724 ff.), which Theseus with difficulty allays by reminding her of his solemn promise (1760). The preparatory offices rendered at 1602 f. could not be viewed as taking the place of a daughter's tribute to the dead. Like 1410, this trait serves to recall the special manifestation of her piety in the earlier play.—Not merely:— <quote xml:lang="eng"> "It was your wish; but it was sad for me to see you die <emph>forlorn</emph> ,"</quote> — <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> in exile. Though <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπὶ ξένης</quote> , he was <emph>not</emph> in this sense <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἔρημος</foreign> ,—he who, in his own words, had <quote xml:lang="eng">"Athens and all her people"</quote> for his friends (772).—Cp. 1705 <quote xml:lang="grc">ἆς ἔχρῃζε...ἔθανε</quote> . The repetition of one phrase in no way justifies Dindorf's rash hypothesis of interpolation here (see cr. n.). Here, the wish is connected with a painful thought; there, with a soothing one. Mention of the wish itself might most naturally recur in a lament. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔλυσε_βίου_1720">, lit., <quote xml:lang="eng">"closed the end of life,"</quote> a pleonasm which blends <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔλυσε βίον</quote> and <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀφίκετο τὸ τέλος βίου</quote> : so Eur. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 956" ref="Soph. El. 956"> <title>El.</title> 956</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τέλος κάμψῃ βίου</quote> </cit>instead of the simple <quote xml:lang="grc">κάμψῃς βίον</quote> ( <title>Helen.</title> 1666). The phrase <quote xml:lang="grc">λύειν βίον</quote> occurs <ref n="Eur. IT 692"> Eur. <title>I. T.</title> 692</ref>, <quote xml:lang="grc">καταλύειν βίοτον</quote> <title> Suppl.</title> 1004. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="λήγετε_1722">: cp. on <ref target="cl1694">1694</ref> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κακῶν_δυσάλωτος_1722">, hard for calamity to capture. Every mortal is an easy prey to misfortune. The gen. as 1519: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Aj. 910" ref="Soph. Aj. 910"> <title>Ai.</title> 910</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄφρακτος φίλων</quote> </cit> , <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Ant. 847" ref="Soph. Ant. 847"> <title>Ant.</title> 847</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">φίλων ἄκλαυτος</quote> </cit> , <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Ant. 1034" ref="Soph. Ant. 1034"> <foreign xml:lang="lat">ib.</foreign> 1034</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">μαντικῆς | ἄπρακτος</quote> </cit>. In prose a prep. would usu. be added, as <cit> <bibl n="Xen. Ages. 8.8.8" ref="Xen. Ages. 8.8.8"> Xen. <title>Ages.</title> 8. 8. 8</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τείχη ἀνάλωτα...ὑπὸ πολεμίων</quote> </cit>. — Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Shak. 3h6 1.4.115" ref="Shak. 3h6 1.4.115"> Shaksp. <title>Hen. VI.</title> Pt. iii. 1. 4. 115</bibl> <quote xml:lang="eng">"their woes, <emph>whom fortune captivates.</emph> "</quote> </cit> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πάλιν_συθῶμεν_1724">, hasten back (601) to the neighbourhood of the <quote xml:lang="grc">καταρράκτης ὀδός</quote> (1590).— <quote xml:lang="grc"> <emph>ὡς τί ῥέξομεν</emph> ; ὡς</quote>with the fut. indic., depending on <quote xml:lang="grc">συθῶμεν</quote> , is the object-clause after a verb implying effort: <cit> <bibl n="Xen. Cyrop. 3.2.13" ref="Xen. Cyrop. 3.2.13"> Xen. <title>Cyr.</title> 3.2.13</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς δὲ καλῶς ἕξει τὰ ὑμέτερα, ἐμοὶ μελήσει</quote> </cit>. With the fut. indic., however, <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅπως</quote> is much commoner than <quote xml:lang="grc">ὡς</quote> . </gloss>

data.quote:
ὡς δὲ καλῶς ἕξει τὰ ὑμέτερα, ἐμοὶ
									μελήσει

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὰν_ἑστίαν_1727">, the home, resting-place, in the ground (1763 <quote xml:lang="grc">θήκεν ἱεράν</quote> ). Oedipus had himself spoken in her hearing of the <quote xml:lang="grc">ἱερὸς τύμβος</quote> (1545) where he was to rest. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θέμις_τάδ_1729">: cp. 883: <ref n="Soph. OT 1329"> <title>O. T.</title> 1329</ref>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μῶν_ὁρᾷς_1729">; dost thou not see for thyself that it cannot be?—since Oedipus solemnly forbade it (1529, 1640). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μῶν_οὐ_1729">is a strong <foreign xml:lang="lat">nonne?</foreign> ( <ref n="Aesch. Supp. 417"> Aesch. <title>Suppl.</title> 417</ref>, <ref n="Eur. Med. 733"> Eur. <title>Med.</title> 733</ref>, <ref n="Plat. Stat. 291D"> Plat. <title>Polit.</title> 291D</ref>, etc.). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐπέπληξας_1729"> , <foreign xml:lang="lat">sc.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc"> μοι</quote> : <quote xml:lang="eng">"what is this reproof of thine to me?"</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καὶ_τόδ_1731">still depends on <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐχ ὁρᾷς;</quote> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μάλ_αὖθις_1731">: cp. 1477. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔπιτνε_1731">, impf., must be either (1) <quote xml:lang="eng">"was appointed to perish,"</quote> or (2) <quote xml:lang="eng">"was perishing"</quote> when we last saw him. (2) seems best. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δίχα_παντός_1731"> , <quote xml:lang="eng">"apart from all"</quote> : <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> without any eye-witness (save Theseus).—Better thus than, <quote xml:lang="eng">"in a manner different from all other men."</quote> —Ismene opposes her sister's desire as (1) unlawful, and (2) impossible. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄγε_με_1733">. <quote xml:lang="eng"> "Lead me (to the spot where we last saw our father), and then slay me <emph>also</emph> ."</quote>In <foreign xml:lang="grc"> ἐπενάριξον</foreign> the prep.= <quote xml:lang="eng">"in addition"</quote> ( <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> to my father). <title>Not</title> , <quote xml:lang="eng"> "slay me <emph>at his grave</emph> "</quote>( <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Hec. 505" ref="Eur. Hec. 505"> Eur. <title>Hec.</title> 505</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">κἄμ’ ἐπισφάξαι τάφῳ</quote> </cit>). She could not intend this after Ismene's words <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄταφος ἔπιτνε</quote> , to which she had been attentive. Cp. Ismene's wish, 1689. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αὖθις_1734">, lit., <quote xml:lang="eng">"now again,"</quote> <foreign xml:lang="lat"> i.e.</foreign> after this new turn in our unhappy fortunes. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φύγω_1738">: cp. on <ref target="cl170">170</ref> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τὸ_κακῶς_1739"> , <quote xml:lang="eng">"so that your affairs should not fall out ill"</quote> ( <cit> <bibl n="Xen. Anab. 1.3.2" ref="Xen. Anab. 1.3.2"> Xen. <title>Anab.</title> 1.3.2</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">Κλέαρχος μικρὸν ἀπέφυγε μὴ καταπετρωθῆναι</quote> </cit>). I therefore incline to Hermann's <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἀπέφυγε ΑΝ. τί</foreign> ; <quote xml:lang="eng"> "Long ago <emph>there was an escape</emph> "</quote>— <quote xml:lang="grc">ΑΝ.</quote> <quote xml:lang="eng">"For what?"</quote> [lit. <quote xml:lang="eng"> " <emph> what</emph> escaped?"</quote>]— <quote xml:lang="grc">ΞΗ.</quote> <quote xml:lang="eng"> " <title>For your fortunes</title> , from falling out ill."</quote>The merits of this reading are:—(1) it leaves v. 1725, which seems quite sound, unaltered: (2) by making <foreign xml:lang="grc">τὰ σφῷν</foreign> nom. to <foreign xml:lang="grc"> ἀπέφυγε</foreign> , it smooths v. 1740. It may be added that, with <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἀπεφύγετον</foreign> , v. 1740 is somewhat pointless, since the mere allusion in <foreign xml:lang="grc">τὰ σφῷν</foreign> to Creon's attempt is too vague to answer Antigone's <foreign xml:lang="grc">τί</foreign> ; (" <emph>what</emph> did we escape?"). Most <quote xml:lang="eng">"escapes"</quote> are escapes from <quote xml:lang="eng">"one's affairs falling out ill."</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φρονῶ_1741">, I am conscious of that, — <quote xml:lang="eng">"I know it well,"</quote> —in quick and grateful response to their allusion. Theseus and the men of Attica had indeed rescued her and her sister in their extremity. Not, <quote xml:lang="eng">"I am thinking...,"</quote> for ( <emph>a</emph> ) the question <foreign xml:lang="grc">τί δῆθ’</foreign> refers back to her <foreign xml:lang="grc">ποῖ φύγω</foreign> ; and ( <emph>b</emph> ) some acknowledgement was due to their reminder.—The MS. <foreign xml:lang="grc">ὑπερνοεῖς</foreign> , as Hermann saw, is corrupt. The compound, which occurs only here, could not mean (1) <quote xml:lang="eng">"why art thou too anxious?"</quote> (Wunder): nor (2) <quote xml:lang="eng"> "what <emph>further</emph> hast thou in thy thoughts?"</quote>—Hermann's <foreign xml:lang="grc"> ὑπεννοεῖς</foreign> is a compound used by Aelian <title>Var. Hist.</title> 4. 8 as= <quote xml:lang="eng">"to have a secret thought or purpose."</quote> But the word seems scarcely appropriate in regard to thoughts which, far from hiding, she is in the act of uttering. Graser's <foreign xml:lang="grc">ὅπερ νοεῖς</foreign> is so far closer to the MSS. that <foreign xml:lang="grc">υ</foreign> for <foreign xml:lang="grc">ο</foreign> would be an easier mistake than <foreign xml:lang="grc">ρ</foreign> for <foreign xml:lang="grc">ν.</foreign> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὅπως_μολούμεθ_1742"> : <quote xml:lang="eng">"how we are to return to Thebes, I know not"</quote> :—for Oedipus had predicted that both her brothers would soon fall in the war (1373), and Creon, the next heir to the throne, was no friend. This continues the thought <foreign xml:lang="grc">ποῖ φύγω;</foreign> (1737). The interposed words of the Chorus did not touch her difficulty.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μηδέ_μάτευε_1743"> : <quote xml:lang="eng">"No, (thou canst not return to Thebes,) nor seek to do so,"</quote> —but stay in Attica under the protection of Theseus. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μόγος_ἔχει_1744"> , <foreign xml:lang="lat">sc.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc"> ἡμᾶς.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐπεῖχε_1744">, <quote xml:lang="eng">"bore hardly on you,"</quote> <foreign xml:lang="lat"> sc.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑμῖν</quote> or <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐφ’ ὑμᾶς</quote> : for <quote xml:lang="grc">μόγος ἐπεῖχεν ὑμᾶς</quote> would mean, <quote xml:lang="eng">"restrained you."</quote> The MS. <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἐπεί</foreign> doubtless arose from a contraction of <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἐπεῖχε</foreign> . The sense of <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἐπῄει</foreign> , <quote xml:lang="eng">"was coming on you,"</quote> would be less apt; and the preceding <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἔχει</foreign> also confirms <foreign xml:lang="grc"> ἐπεῖχε</foreign> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοτὲ_ὕπερθεν_1745">. Whitelaw: <quote xml:lang="eng">"Oh then past cure, but worst is now grown worse."</quote> The neut. plur. is most simply taken as adverb (319), referring to <quote xml:lang="grc">μόγος ἐπεῖχε</quote> : though we might also construe, <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄπορα</quote> ( <quote xml:lang="grc">ἦν τὰ ἡμέτερα</quote> ). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τοτὲ_δέ_1745">, "at one time" ( <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> while Oed. lived)... "at another time" ( <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> now that he is dead).</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὕπερθεν_1745">, hyperbolic, since <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἄπορα</foreign> already= <quote xml:lang="eng">"hopeless"</quote> : cp. fr. 188 <quote xml:lang="grc">ὦ πᾶν σὺ τολμήσασα καὶ πέρα, γύναι</quote> .</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πέλαγος_1746">, without <quote xml:lang="grc">κακῶν</quote> or the like, is excused by the familiarity of this metaphor in Greek: cp. on <ref target="cl663"> 663</ref> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="φεῦ_φεῦ_1747">. Dindorf substitutes <foreign xml:lang="grc">αἰαῖ</foreign> , because he supposes the latter to have generated the <quote xml:lang="grc">ναὶ ναί</quote> which, with the words <quote xml:lang="grc">ξύμφημι καὐτός</quote> , he ejects (see cr. n.). But so common a form as <foreign xml:lang="grc">αἰαῖ</foreign> was not very likely to be thus corrupted. It is simpler to suppose that the ejected phrase was a mere interpolation, perhaps due to actors. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐλπίδων_τίν_1748">: lit., <quote xml:lang="eng"> "(we may well ask whither we are to go,) <emph> for</emph> towards what remaining ( <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἔτι</foreign> ) hope of (all possible) hopes is fate now urging us?"</quote>What hope now remains for us, in the course on which we are driven? For <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἔτι</foreign> , which here is virtually equiv. to an adj. <quote xml:lang="grc">λοιπήν</quote> , cp. 865 <quote xml:lang="grc">τῆσδε τῆς ἀρᾶς ἔτι.</quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἐλπίδων_1748">in its good sense, rather than neutral or sinister ( <quote xml:lang="eng"> "bodings"</quote> ): cp. <ref n="Soph. El. 958"> <title>El.</title> 958</ref> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="trimeter"> <l>ποῖ γὰρ μενεῖς ῥᾴθυμος, ἐς τίν’ ἐλπίδων</l> <l>βλέψασ’ ἔτ’ ὀρθήν</l> </lg> </quote> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θρῆνον_introduction">, not <foreign xml:lang="grc">θρήνων</foreign> , is clearly right. The 2nd per. <emph>sing.</emph> imper., <foreign xml:lang="grc">παῦε</foreign> , is the only part of <quote xml:lang="grc">παύω</quote> which is used intransitively by the classical Attic writers,—being, in fact, an exclamation (like our <quote xml:lang="eng"> " <emph>stop!</emph> "</quote>), though sometimes joined with a gen. ( <quote xml:lang="grc">παῦε τοῦ λόγου</quote> , <ref n="Aristoph. Frogs 580"> Aristoph. <title>Ran.</title> 580</ref>). No weight can be given to the fact that L has <foreign xml:lang="grc">θρήνων</foreign> here, since it has it also in 1778 (see cr. n.). !-- need upsilon with a breve --> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ξύν_ἀπόκειται_introduction">for the MS. <foreign xml:lang="grc">ξυ^ναπόκειται</foreign> is (I think) right. The literal sense is:— <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἐν οἷς γὰρ</foreign> <quote xml:lang="eng">"for in a case where"</quote> ( <emph>neut. pl.</emph> ), <foreign xml:lang="grc">χάρις ἡ χθονία</foreign> "the kindness shown by the <quote xml:lang="grc"> χθόνιοι,</quote> " <foreign xml:lang="grc">ξύν’ ἀπόκειται</foreign> "is stored up as a common benefit" ( <quote xml:lang="grc">ξυνά</quote> , neut. pl. as adv.),—common, namely, to Oedipus and the Athenians. That is:— <quote xml:lang="eng"> "By the death of Oedipus, the Powers below have given <emph>him</emph> the everlasting rest which he desired, and <emph> us</emph> the abiding safeguard which he promised"</quote>( <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> his grave). To mourn here would be to provoke the deities who have ordered all things well for him and for us.—</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀπόκειται_introduction">, is laid up in store: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Dem. 23.42" ref="Dem. 23.42">[Dem.] or. 23 § 42</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ τῆς συγγνώμης ὠφέλιμον...ὅτῳ ποτὲ τῶν πάντων ἀπόκειται ἄδηλον ὄν</quote> </cit>, it being uncertain for whom the benefit of compassion <emph>is laid up</emph> ,— <foreign xml:lang="lat">i.e.</foreign> , who may need to draw upon it. <cit> <bibl n="Dem. 18.198" ref="Dem. 18.198">Dem. or. 18 § 198</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅτῳ τὰ τῶν Ἑλλήνων ἀτυχήματα ἐνευδοκιμεῖν ἀπέκειτο</quote> </cit>, <quote xml:lang="eng">"a man to whom the disasters of his countrymen were a fund of material for self-glorification."</quote> In the literal sense, <cit> <bibl n="Xen. Anab. 2.3.15" ref="Xen. Anab. 2.3.15"> Xen. <title>Anab.</title> 2.3.15</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐταὶ δὲ αἱ βάλανοι τῶν φοινίκων, οἵας μὲν ἐν τοῖς Ἕλλησιν ἔστιν ἰδεῖν, τοῖς οἰκέταις ἀπέκειντο, αἱ δὲ τοῖς δεσπόταις ἀποκείμεναι ἦσαν</quote> </cit> . </gloss>

data.quote:
τὸ τῆς συγγνώμης ὠφέλιμον...ὅτῳ ποτὲ
									τῶν πάντων ἀπόκειται ἄδηλον ὄν

ὅτῳ τὰ τῶν Ἑλλήνων ἀτυχήματα
									ἐνευδοκιμεῖν ἀπέκειτο

αὐταὶ δὲ αἱ βάλανοι τῶν φοινίκων, οἵας
									μὲν ἐν τοῖς Ἕλλησιν ἔστιν ἰδεῖν, τοῖς οἰκέταις
									ἀπέκειντο, αἱ δὲ τοῖς δεσπόταις ἀποκείμεναι ἦσαν

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="νέμεσις_γάρ_1753"> , <foreign xml:lang="lat">sc.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">πενθεῖν ἐστί</quote> : it is provocative of divine anger to mourn, as if insensible of the divine beneficence. <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Il. 14.80" ref="Hom. Il. 14.80"> <title>Il.</title> 14.80</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐ γάρ τις νέμεσις φυγέειν κακόν</quote> </cit>, <quote xml:lang="eng">"'tis no matter for indignation that one should flee from ill"</quote> : <cit> <bibl n="Hom. Od. 1.350" ref="Hom. Od. 1.350"> <title>Od.</title> 1.350</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τούτῳ δ’ οὐ νέμεσις...ἀείδειν</quote> </cit> : <cit> <bibl n="Aristot. Rh. 2.9.11" ref="Aristot. Rh. 2.9.11"> Arist. <title>Rhet.</title> 2.9.11</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐὰν οὖν ἀγαθὸς ὢν μὴ τοῦ ἁρμόττοντος τυγχάνῃ, νεμεσητόν</quote> </cit>: where, however, the <quote xml:lang="grc">νέμεσις</quote> is human, not, as here, divine. Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 1467" ref="Soph. El. 1467"> <title>El.</title> 1467</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">εἰ δ’ ἔπεστι νέμεσις, οὐ λέγω</quote> </cit>(in revoking words which might offend <emph>the gods</emph> ). </gloss>

data.quote:
οὐ γάρ τις νέμεσις φυγέειν
									κακόν

ἐὰν οὖν ἀγαθὸς ὢν μὴ τοῦ ἁρμόττοντος
									τυγχάνῃ, νεμεσητόν
εἰ δ’ ἔπεστι νέμεσις, οὐ λέγω

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τίνος_χρείας_1755"> , <quote xml:lang="eng"> " <emph>for</emph> what request,"</quote>—depending on the idea of <quote xml:lang="grc">δεόμεθα, χρῄζομεν</quote> , implied in <quote xml:lang="grc">προσπίτνομεν</quote> :</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀνύσαι_1755">( <foreign xml:lang="lat">sc.</foreign> <quote xml:lang="grc">αὐτήν</quote> ), epexegetic inf., <quote xml:lang="eng">"so that ye should obtain it"</quote> : cp. 1211 (n.).—For the use of <foreign xml:lang="grc">χρείας</foreign> , cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 1435" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg004:1435"> <title>O. T.</title> 1435</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">καὶ τοῦ με χρείας ὧδε λιπαρεῖς τυχεῖν;</quote> </cit> </gloss>

data.quote:
καὶ τοῦ με χρείας ὧδε λιπαρεῖς
									τυχεῖν;

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="αὐταὶ_1755">, with our own eyes (instead of merely hearing that it exists). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀπεῖπεν_1760">, forbade, takes <quote xml:lang="grc">μή</quote> after it, as is usual (cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 236" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg004:236"> <title>O. T.</title> 236</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀπαυδῶ ...μή</quote> </cit> ); <cit> <bibl n="Aeschin. 1.138" ref="Aeschin. 1.138">Aeschin. or. 1 § 138</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ταῦτα τοῖς δούλοις ἀπεῖπον μὴ ποιεῖν</quote> </cit>. </gloss>

data.quote:
ταῦτα τοῖς δούλοις ἀπεῖπον μὴ
									ποιεῖν

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="μήτ_θήκην_1762">must be carefully distinguished from <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπιφωνεῖν θήκῃ</quote> . The former must mean strictly (not, <quote xml:lang="eng"> "to utter <emph>over</emph> the grave,"</quote>but) <quote xml:lang="eng"> " <emph>to approach</emph> the grave <emph>with utterance</emph> ,"</quote>—the notion being that of invading the secret silence around it. Invocations and prayers to the dead were often made aloud at a grave: <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Hel. 961" ref="Eur. Hel. 961"> Eur. <title>Helen</title> 961</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="trimeter"> <l>λέξω τάδ’ ἀμφὶ μνῆμα σοῦ πατρὸς πόθῳ·</l> <l>ὦ γέρον, ὃς οἰκεῖς τόνδε λάϊνον τάφον</l> </lg> </quote> </cit>, etc.—The alternative is to take <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπιφωνεῖν</quote> as= <quote xml:lang="eng">"mention to another"</quote> : but this is unfitting, since Theseus <emph>alone</emph> knows the place.</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="θήκην_ἱεράν_1762">: cp. 1545. <cit> <bibl n="Thuc. 1.8" ref="Thuc. 1.8">Thuc. 1.8</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τῶν θηκῶν ἀναιρεθεισῶν ὅσαι ἦσαν τῶν τεθνεώτων ἐν τῇ νήσῳ</quote> </cit>. </gloss>

data.quote:
τῶν θηκῶν ἀναιρεθεισῶν ὅσαι ἦσαν τῶν
									τεθνεώτων ἐν τῇ νήσῳ

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="καλῶς_1764"> with <foreign xml:lang="grc">πράσσοντα</foreign> (not with <quote xml:lang="grc">ἕξειν</quote> ), <quote xml:lang="eng">"in a seemly manner,"</quote> <quote xml:lang="eng">"duly"</quote> (Lat. <foreign xml:lang="lat">rite</foreign> ): cp. 617: <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 879" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg004:879"> <title>O. T.</title> 879</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὸ καλῶς δ’ ἔχον | πόλει πάλαισμα</quote> </cit>. The fact that <foreign xml:lang="grc">πράσσοντα καλῶς</foreign> usually meant <quote xml:lang="eng">"faring well"</quote> is no objection. The ancient Greek instinct for words was remarkably free from bondage to phrases. Cp. <ref n="Soph. Ant. 989"> <title>Ant.</title> 989</ref>n. </gloss>

data.quote:
τὸ καλῶς δ’ ἔχον | πόλει
									πάλαισμα

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἄλυπον_1764">: an echo of the expression used by Oed. (1519). Why change it to <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἀδῇον</foreign> (1533, Nauck), or <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἄσυλον</foreign> (Wecklein)? </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ταῦτ_οὖν_1766"> : <quote xml:lang="eng"> " <title>These things</title> , then, ( <foreign xml:lang="grc">οὖν</foreign> , according to the injunctions of Oedipus,) I was heard to promise by the god,"</quote>etc. <foreign xml:lang="grc">ταῦτ’</foreign> is short for <quote xml:lang="eng">"the promise to do these things,"</quote> as if <quote xml:lang="grc">ὑπισχνουμένων</quote> stood with <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἡμῶν</foreign> . For <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἔκλυεν</foreign> with both gen. and acc. cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 235"> <title>O.T.</title> 235</ref>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="δαίμων_1766">: the Divine Power that called Oedipus away (1626). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πάντ_ἀΐων_1767">: cp. 42. The <quote xml:lang="grc">α</quote> of <quote xml:lang="grc">ἀΐω</quote> <emph> short</emph> , as in 240 and <ref n="Soph. Phil. 1410"> <title>Ph.</title> 1410</ref>: whereas it is <emph>long</emph> in 181, 304, <ref n="Soph. Aj. 1263"> <title>Ai.</title> 1263</ref>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Διὸς_Ὅρκος_1767">, as the servant of Zeus. <cit> <bibl n="Hes. WD 803" ref="Hes. WD 803"> Hes. <title>Op.</title> 803</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="hexameter"> <l>ἐν πέμπτῃ γάρ φασιν Ἐρινύας ἀμφιπολεύειν</l> <l>Ὅρκον γεινόμενον, τὸν Ἔρις τέκε πῆμ’ ἐπιόρκοις</l> </lg> </quote> </cit>. This personified Horkis is a deity who witnesses an oath, and punishes perjury ( <ref n="Hes. Th. 231"> Hes. <title>Theog.</title> 231</ref>). He is the son of Eris, because strife gives birth to treaties; he is attended at his birth by the Erinyes, because they avenge broken faith. And he is the servant of Zeus, because <foreign xml:lang="grc">Ζεὺς Ὅρκιος</foreign> is the <emph>supreme</emph> guardian of good-faith — represented in the <foreign xml:lang="grc">βουλευτήριον</foreign> at Olympia by a Zeus with lightnings in both hands, —the most terrible, Pausanias says, that he knew: <cit> <quote xml:lang="grc">πάντων ὁπόσα ἀγάλματα Διὸς μάλιστα ἐς ἔκπληξιν ἀδίκων ἀνδρῶν</quote> <bibl n="Paus. 5.24.9" ref="Paus. 5.24.9">(5. 24. 9)</bibl> </cit> </gloss>

data.quote:
πάντων ὁπόσα ἀγάλματα Διὸς μάλιστα ἐς
									ἔκπληξιν ἀδίκων ἀνδρῶν

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="κατὰ_νοῦν_1768"> . <cit> <bibl n="Aristoph. Kn. 549" ref="Aristoph. Kn. 549"> Ar. <title>Eq.</title> 549</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">κατὰ νοῦν πράξας</quote> </cit>: so oft. <foreign xml:lang="grc">κατὰ γνώμην.</foreign> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τάδ_ταῦτ_1768">: cp. on 787. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="τᾶς_ὠγυγίους_1770">: a specially fit epithet, since the mythical <quote xml:lang="grc">Ὠγύγης</quote> was represented (in one legend at least) as son of Boeotus, and first ruler of Thebes ( <ref n="Paus. 9.5.1">Paus. 9.5.1</ref> ). Another legend connected him with Attica ( <ref n="Paus. 1.38.7">Paus. 1.38.7</ref> ). The trait common to the two legends is a great inundation which happened in his reign. The adj. is applied by Aesch. to Thebes ( <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. Seven 321" ref="Aesch. Seven 321"> <title>Th.</title> 321</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">πόλιν ὠγυγίαν</quote> </cit> , <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. Pers. 37" ref="Aesch. Pers. 37"> <title>Pers.</title> 37</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τάς τ’ ὠγυγίους Θήβας</quote> </cit>), and also to Athens ( <ref n="Aesch. Pers. 974"> <title>Pers.</title> 974</ref>). The Attic poets used it in the general sense of <quote xml:lang="eng">"very ancient,"</quote> as <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Phil. 142" ref="Soph. Phil. 142"> <title>Phil.</title> 142</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">κράτος ὠγύγιον</quote> </cit>, "royalty inherited from of old." </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἰόντα_1771">, a pres., not fut., partic. ( <ref n="Soph. OT 773"> <title>O. T.</title> 773</ref>n.), "coming on them": <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 374" ref="Soph. El. 374"> <title>El.</title> 374</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">κακὸν...εἰς αὐτὴν ἰόν</quote> </cit> : <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Laws 873E" ref="Plat. Laws 873E"> Plat. <title>Legg.</title> 873E</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">παρὰ θεοῦ...βέλος ἰόν</quote> </cit>. So <cit> <bibl n="Soph. Ant. 185" ref="Soph. Ant. 185"> <title>Ant.</title> 185</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὴν ἄτην ὁρῶν | στείχουσαν ἀστοῖς.</quote> </cit> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὁμαίμοις_1771">: see on <ref target="cl330">330</ref> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ὁπόσ_ἂν_1773">seems slightly preferable to <foreign xml:lang="grc">ὅσα γ’ ἂν</foreign> as a correction of the MS. <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅσ’ ἂν</quote> (or <quote xml:lang="grc">ὅσα ἂν</quote> ), because the qualification which <foreign xml:lang="grc">γ’</foreign> would imply is sufficiently provided for by <foreign xml:lang="grc">πρόσφορα</foreign> etc.: cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OC 1634" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg007:1634">1634</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="trimeter"> <l>τελεῖν δ’ ὅσ’ ἂν</l> <l>μέλλῃς φρονῶν εὖ ξυμφέροντ’ αὐταῖς ἀεί</l> </lg> </quote> </cit> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πράσσειν_1774"> , <emph>pres.</emph> inf. with <foreign xml:lang="grc">μέλλω</foreign> , as in eight other places of Sophocles. He has the <emph>fut.</emph> inf. with it ten times, including <ref n="Soph. OT 967"> <title>O. T.</title> 967</ref>, where the MS. <quote xml:lang="grc"> κτανεῖν</quote> , if sound, would be the only instance of the <emph>aor.</emph> inf. with <quote xml:lang="grc">μέλλω</quote> in Soph.; but there the fut. <quote xml:lang="grc">κτενεῖν</quote> is clearly right. Where <quote xml:lang="grc">μέλλω</quote> means "to delay," the pres. inf. is naturally preferred: cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 1627">1627</ref> : <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 678" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg004:678"> <title>O. T.</title> 678</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τί μέλλεις κομίζειν δόμων τόνδ’ ἔσω;</quote> </cit> </gloss>

data.quote:
τί μέλλεις κομίζειν δόμων τόνδ’
									ἔσω;

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πρόσφορά_κατὰγῆς_1774">: at once for your advantage, and to the gratification of the dead. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πρόσφορα_1774">, "suitable" for a given purpose, and so "useful,""profitable": so often in Attic prose, as <ref n="Thuc. 1.125">Thuc. 1.125</ref> ; <ref n="Thuc. 2.46">2. 46</ref> , <ref n="Thuc. 2.65">65</ref> ; <ref n="Thuc. 7.62">7. 62</ref> . </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="πρὸς_χάριν_1774">: cp. <ref n="Soph. OT 1152"> <title>O. T.</title> 1152</ref>n. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔρρει_1774">is justified by the <emph>sudden and swift</emph> removal of Oedipus, as <cit> <bibl n="Soph. OT 560" ref="urn:cts:greekLit:tlg0011.tlg004:560"> <title>O. T.</title> 560</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἄφαντος ἔρρει</quote> </cit>, he hath been <emph>swept</emph> from men's sight. In <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 57" ref="Soph. El. 57"> <title>El.</title> 57</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τοὐμὸν ὡς ἔρρει δέμας | φλογιστὸν ἤδη</quote> </cit>, it is little more than <quote xml:lang="grc">οἴχεται</quote> . More commonly <quote xml:lang="grc">ἔρρειν</quote> implies either an evil end, or at least some feeling of contempt on the speaker's part, as <cit> <bibl n="Eur. Supp. 1112" ref="Eur. Supp. 1112"> Eur. <title>Suppl.</title> 1112</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc"> <lg type="trimeter"> <l>οὓς χρῆν, ἐπειδὰν μηδὲν ὠφελῶσι γῆν,</l> <l>θανόντας ἔρρειν κἀκποδων εἶναι νέοις</l> </lg> </quote> </cit>. Wecklein regards the words <foreign xml:lang="grc">ὃς νέον ἔρρει</foreign> as a spurious addition ( <ref> <title>Ars Soph. em.</title> p. 81</ref>). </gloss>

data.quote:
τοὐμὸν ὡς ἔρρει δέμας | φλογιστὸν
									ἤδη

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀποκάμνειν_1776">, "to cease from labouring," can take an acc. of the labour avoided: hence <foreign xml:lang="grc">πάνθ’</foreign> in <ref n="Soph. OC 1773">1773</ref> need not be merely acc. of respect. <cit> <bibl n="Xen. Hell. 7.5.19" ref="Xen. Hell. 7.5.19"> Xen. <title>Hellen.</title> 7.5.19</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">πόνον...μηδένα ἀποκάμνειν</quote> </cit>, "to flinch from no toil." Also with inf., <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Crito 45B" ref="Plat. Crito 45B"> Plat. <title>Crito</title> 45B</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">μὴ ἀποκάμῃς σαυτὸν σῶσαι</quote> </cit>, "do not abandon the effort to save yourself." So <foreign xml:lang="grc"> ἐκκάμνω</foreign> , <cit> <bibl n="Thuc. 2.51" ref="Thuc. 2.51">Thuc. 2.51</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">τὰς ὀλοφύρσεις τῶν ἀπογιγνομένων...ἐξέκαμον</quote> </cit>, "were worn out by the lamentations of the dying." For the form of the sentence cp. <cit> <bibl n="Plat. Rep. 445B" ref="Plat. Rep. 445B"> Plat. <title>Rep.</title> 445B</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">ἐπειδὴ ἐνταῦθα ἐληλύθαμεν, ὅσον οἷόν τε σαφέστατα κατιδεῖν ὅτι ταῦτα οὕτως ἔχει, οὐ χρὴ ἀποκάμνειν</quote> </cit>. For this force of <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἀπό</foreign> cp. <foreign xml:lang="grc">ἀπαλγέω, ἀπανθέω, ἀποζέω, ἀποκηδεύω, ἀπολοφύρομαι</foreign> . </gloss>

data.quote:
ἐπειδὴ ἐνταῦθα ἐληλύθαμεν, ὅσον οἷόν
									τε σαφέστατα κατιδεῖν ὅτι ταῦτα οὕτως ἔχει, οὐ χρὴ
									ἀποκάμνειν

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀλλ_1777">introduces the final words of comfort which the elders of Colonus address to the Theban maidens; cp. <ref n="Soph. OC 101">101</ref> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἀποπαύετε_1777">, no less than the following verb, governs <foreign xml:lang="grc">θρῆνον</foreign> : cp. <ref target="cl1751">on 1751</ref> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ἔχει_κῦρος_1779">, lit., "have validity," = <foreign xml:lang="grc">κεκύρωται</foreign> , <foreign xml:lang="lat">sancta sunt.</foreign> Cp. <cit> <bibl n="Soph. El. 919" ref="Soph. El. 919"> <title>El.</title> 919</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">πολλῶν...κῦρος...καλῶν</quote> </cit>("sanction of"), <cit> <bibl n="Aesch. Supp. 391" ref="Aesch. Supp. 391"> Aesch. <title>Suppl.</title> 391</bibl> <quote xml:lang="grc">οὐκ ἔχουσι κῦρος...ἀμφὶ σοῦ</quote> </cit>, "authority over thee." — Two meanings are possible: (1) " <emph>These promises of Theseus</emph> are certain to hold good": or, more generally, (2) " <emph>These events</emph> have assuredly been ordained past recall" (by the gods). Most commentators prefer (1). But (2) seems more fitting at the conclusion. The last soothing words of the Chorus convey a precept of resignation to the divine will. </gloss>
